original_note:
JEFFREY TYE								11/09/2011

Jeff is 26 years old, birth date is 01/10/1985.  He was referred by Dr. Sunhee Hong, 12801 Midway Road #401, Dallas, TX 75244, phone #972-241-6862.  The orthodontist will be Dr. Clay Pickering, 1308 Crescent Street #B, Denton, TX 76201.

Jeff’s TMJ problems began when he was about 20 years old to the best of his recollection.  He developed an infected tooth that was infected for some time and was on antibiotics long term and ended up having root canals done twice on it.  He had a crown placed on it that at one point was too high and it was redone and then it was too low. 

Primary concerns that Jeff has at this time include the following:

TMJ pain.
Clicking and popping in the jaw joints.
Tension in the face. 
Pain down through the neck.
Uncomfortable chewing.
Jaws and bite do not fit together right. 
Difficulties chewing.
Daily pain.
Jaw, mouth and neck often inflamed.
Stress and anxiety created by the jaw problem.

Jeff gets headaches about 4 times a week, but only at about a level of 2 and they occur predominantly in the posterior head area.  He rates his TMJ pain at 7, average daily pain at 5, jaw function at 6, diet at 5, disability at 7.  The only medication he takes is Tylenol occasionally for his headaches.  He gets occasional earaches but no tinnitus, no vertigo and no hearing loss.  Other joints that bother him include his left shoulder, knees, and he is beginning to develop some pain in his feet which sounds more like plantar fasciitis.  He does have allergies to probably environmental things, but he is not aware of clenching or bruxing.  He usually feels best in the morning when he wakes up.  

Jeff was in splint therapy from 2008 through the first half of 2011.  With the splint in place, he could chew a little bit easier, but then when he would take it out his bite seemed even more unnatural.  

In frontal view, Jeff has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 23 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 2.5.  The maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline as are the mandibular incisors.  The chin may be just slightly off to the left side about 2 mm.  In profile, he is maybe just slightly AP deficient in the maxilla by about 3 or 4 mm.  The chin may be retruded about 8 to 10 mm.  Intraorally, Jeff has all of his lower teeth back through the second molars, but has some significant crowding in the lower anterior arch.  The left cuspid may be about 1 mm posterior to the right cuspid.  In the upper arch, Jeff has all of his teeth back through the second molars.  He has a Class III end-on occlusion being probably at least 5 mm Class III on the right side and 5 mm Class III and he has an end-on incisor relationship due to some lingual inclination of the lower incisors.  He has a zero over bite relationship.  He has an anterior cross bite relationship.  Oropharyngeal tissues look normal and healthy.  Intranasally, the septum is deviated off towards the left side.  The left turbinate appears somewhat enlarged and appears to almost touch against the septum.  On the right side, the septum appears more normal in size and there appears to be an adequate airway on the right side.  To palpation, he has mild to moderate pain over the masseters, TMJs, SCMs, shoulders, left retromandibular area and milder tenderness over the posterior neck and temporalis muscles.  On opening, he had a little bit of a glitch on wide opening in the left joint, but the right side seemed to function reasonably smoothly.  The maximal incisal opening is 53 mm and excursion movements to the right is 4 mm and to the left 7 mm.  He seems to be a little bit more full in the right ramus area by about 3 to 4 mm compared to the left side.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of mandibular third molars, but the presence of impacted third molars.  He has a root canal on the maxillary left first molar.  Sinuses appear elastic clear.  Condylar heads and necks appear somewhat elongated.  

Coronal view shows at least back in the bony septum it is reasonably straight and the turbinates appear enlarged, more so on the right side than the left side.  Sinuses appear clear.  

Axial view shows that the septum appears reasonably straight at this particular cut and turbinates are moderate in size.  The sinuses appear relatively clear.  Clinically, the nasal septum is deviated off towards the left side.  

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has a fairly good morphology and there appears to be relatively normal joint space, although somewhat decreased at the lateral aspect of the joint.  At the lateral aspect, there is some flattening on the head of the condyle.  The articular eminence is relatively shallow.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has reasonably good morphology and just slightly narrow anteroposteriorly.  The joint space is a little bit more constricted on this side than the opposite side.  The articular eminence is relatively shallow.  

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has reasonably good shape and there appears to be reasonably good joint space except there is somewhat of a decrease at the lateral aspect of the joint.  

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears to have pretty good size to it.  The joint space seems a little bit more confined than the opposite side.  
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that he has a skeletal Class III occlusion.  Oropharyngeal airway actually looks fairly good.  He has a slight high occlusal plane angulation.  He has some AP microgenia relative to the AP position of the lower incisors.  

Basic diagnoses would be as follows:

Left TMJ medial displacement of the articular disc.
Right TMJ medial and posterior displacement of the articular disc.
Maxillary AP hypoplasia, posterior vertical hypoplasia, but transverse hyperplasia.
Mandibular prognathism.
High occlusal plane angle of 16 degrees (normal 8 degrees).
Impacted maxillary third molars.
Deviated nasal septum off to the left side.
Class III occlusion.
Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of 2.4 mm.  

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Presurgical orthodontics.
	a.	Align and level maxillary arch.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and 			cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies.
	b.	Align and level mandibular arch.  Try to transversely expand the 				mandibular posterior teeth.  Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing 			approximately 2 mm of tooth structure to correct for a tooth size 				discrepancy and to keep from flaring the lower incisors too far forward.  
2.	Surgery.
	a.	Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek 			anchors.
	b.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to rotate the mandible in a 				counterclockwise direction.
	c.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance, down graft it posteriorly and 			correct the transverse discrepancy.
	d.	Surgical removal of impacted third molars x2.
	e.	Nasal septoplasty.
3.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
GEORGE SMARZ									07-27-04

George is 34 years old.

Patient History

He had a broken jaw about 7 or 8 years ago. He had a fractured right mandibular angle. George is in good health with no major medical problems. George is not aware of any jaw joint problems. He has no problems with headaches, TMJ pain, TMJ noises, earaches, tinnitus, or vertigo. 

Patient Concerns

Jaws are too far positioned posteriorly 
The upper jaw is up too high 

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, George has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry and balance. Upper lip length is 21 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about -4 mm. Maxillary, mandibular, and chin midlines are all pretty much aligned on the facial midline. 

In frontal view, George has some slight AP maxillary deficiency of probably about 3 to 4 mm. Chin appears to be AP deficient by probably around 12 mm or so. Chin-neckline is short, and submental area is full. Chin-neck angle is relatively well-defined. Upper lip is retruded by about 5 mm posterior to the upper lip. George has a slight obtuse nasolabial angle and a slight C-shaped curve to the upper lip. Chin appears retruded relative to the lower lip, but the lower lip may be everted some because of the occlusal relationship.

Intraorally, George has all of his lower teeth back through the second molars. Teeth are reasonably well aligned and leveled. A little bit of crowding, lower anterior arch. Has adequate attached gingiva. Occlusal plane is relatively flat. In the upper arch, George has all of his teeth back through the second molars. Has basically a Class I cuspid-molar relationship. There is an anterior deep bite of probably about 2.5 mm. Uvula looks a little bit enlarged in width and maybe just slightly in length but not too bad. Soft palate looks relatively normal. Oropharynx appears relatively normal. 

Nasal septum is deviated a little bit off toward the right side. Turbinates are normal in size. Airway itself looks fairly good. Some minimal constricture of the luminal valves, but not too significant. 

In the left TMJ on opening, he does have a little bit of a click/crepitus kind of noise at about 30 mm of opening. On closing, he has the same kind of noise but more of a click kind of sound. He has no tenderness around the head and neck area nor does he have any tenderness over the TMJ areas. Incisal opening is probably about 57 mm. Excursive movements are about 12 mm in each direction.

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows that the four third molars are absent. Sinuses appear relatively clear. Nasal airway appears clear. Has a wire in the right ramus area where the previous fracture was. He has had a previous procedure to lengthen the vertical height of the chin, and the chin is rotated downward. He has two bone plates anteriorly with each bone plate having it appears four screws below the horizontal osteotomy site and on one plate one screw above in the midline and along the side another plate in the cuspid/first bicuspid area adjacent to/fairly close to the mental nerve where there are two screws. A bone cut is just barely beneath the inferior alveolar nerve canal. There is also a screw at the posterior aspect of the mandible on each side unknown but apparently holding an implant in place along each side posteriorly. On the horizontal osteotomy, it appears that there is a bony union on the left side posteriorly but does not appear to be any bone that has grown between the inferiorly repositioned segment and the superior segment. Bone gap probably measures about 8 mm in vertical discrepancy. Left condyle appears a little flattened on the anterior aspect. Right condyle appears to have a little bit better morphology. 

Waters view shows that the mandible appears fairly symmetric. Bone screws can be seen in the posterior body area toward the inferior border supporting an implant back in that area. Nasal airway appears good. Sinuses appear clear. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle with a little bit of a hockey stick shape to it. A little bit of flattening on the anterior head. Articular eminence is fairly steep. Some increased superior and posterior joint space. 

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that actually has a little bit better shape than the opposite side. May be a little sclerotic on the anterior head but not too bad. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Appears to be good almost excessive joint space vertically and superoposteriorly.

On opening, there is almost hypertranslation of the condyles bilaterally. On the open view, the right condyle actually looks a little bit more arthritic than the left side. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that there is vertical maxillary deficiency and AP maxillary and mandibular deficiency. The chin is rotated downward and probably backward from its original position. A big bone gap is seen between the inferior and superior segments. Two bone plates are stabilized in place. Oropharyngeal airway at the soft palate level measures about 5 mm and at the base of the tongue measures about 12 mm. 

Tooth-size analysis shows that there is a Bolton’s difference of about 2.5 mm. 

Basic Diagnoses

AP maxillary deficiency
Vertical maxillary deficiency
AP mandibular deficiency
Anterior mandibular vertical excess
Essentially a Class I cuspid-molar relationship with just a slight Class II tendency of probably 2 mm
Tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2.5 mm
Mild crowding, lower anterior arch
Previous chin procedure with the chin rotated downward and backward from its original position
Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocations
AP microgenia
Previous implants to angles of the mandible although positioned significantly medially
Clicking and crepitation in the left TMJ

Recommended Treatment

MRI of left and right TMJs

Presurgical orthodontics 
Align and level maxillary arch 
Align and level mandibular arch. Strip lower anterior teeth removing 2 mm of tooth structure and retract

Surgery
Maxillary osteotomies to advance and downgraft the maxilla approximately 7 to 8 mm anteriorly and 8 to 9 mm posteriorly
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction
Vertical reduction AP augmentation genioplasty. The other option for treatment for George would be to not include the chin procedure. He would actually prefer to leave the chin where it is. He was worked up both by repositioning the chin upward and forward and leaving the chin where it is, and he prefers to leave the chin where it is
Possible bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors if it is confirmed that the TMJ discs are dislocated
Removal of bilateral mandibular ramus implants
Possible repositioning of mandibular ramus implants by placing a little bit higher on the ramus as well as to provide more lateral projection

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion 

George also wanted to take all of his x-rays with him so we have no x-rays.

original_note:
XIMENA QUIROS									08-29-03

Ximena is 13 years old and referred by Dr. Ronald de la Cruz from Sabana Norte, 50 W. Interfin Bank, P.O. Box 3537-1000, San Jose, Costa Rica.

Patient History

Ximena is in good health with no major medical problems. 

She gives no history of headaches, TMJ pain, earaches, vertigo, etc. She states that she doesn’t have any noises in her joints. 


Patient Concerns

Her teeth don’t fit together
She has a significantly gummy smile
Makes noises when she sleeps
Mouth breathing particularly when sleeping
Cannot chew with her lips together

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Ximena has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Sometimes when she smiles she has a little bit of hyperfunction on the left side of her upper lip as compared to the right side. Upper lip length is about 19 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is 9 mm. Maxillary, mandibular, and chin midline are all pretty much on the facial midline. She has some slight AP maxillary deficiency. She has significant lip incompetence of probably about 10 mm. Chin is retruded, and chin-neckline is short, although the chin-neck angle is fairly well-defined. Chin is probably a good 10 to 12 mm AP deficient. 

Intraorally, Ximena has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. Teeth are nicely aligned and leveled. In the upper arch, she also has all of her teeth back through the second molars present and are well-aligned and leveled. She has some hyper-inflammation of the gingiva in the upper arch probably due to excessive exposure. She has almost a full class II cuspid relationship being probably at least 5 mm Class II. She has an overjet of probably about 5 mm and vertical overbite of probably about 4 mm. She has had her lower third molars removed, and this was done by Dr. Oscar Reiche. Transverse occlusal plane appears fairly level with the eyes, but the right side appears to be about a millimeter lower than the left side compared to the ears. I feel the symmetry is better with the eyes. 

To palpation, she had only some very minimal tenderness over the TMJ area of maybe 1 to 2. TMJs, however, seem to function very smoothy and no other signs or symptoms of having any TMJ problems. 

Intranasally, septum is deviated a little bit off to the left side, and there is a spur at the base of the septum on the left side. Turbinates are quite large, particularly on the left side, that seem to cause some significant obstruction of the airway and the right side at least anteriorly although the turbinate is quite large, but with the deviated septum, the airway appears better, although difficult to assess the posterior aspect. Oropharynx appears clear. She has her tonsils and appears to have some adenoid tissue posteriorly but no inflammation present.

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows that there may be a little mucous cyst in the right sinus. Turbinates appear fairly large. A spur is seen coming off the left side of the septum. Impacted maxillary third molars are present. Good space between the upper lateral incisor and cuspids. Absent mandibular third molars. Condyles look a little bit different sizes on this particular radiograph. 

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle with fairly good shape. Could be a little bit of anterior beaking present. Articular eminence is moderately steep but not very long vertically. Joint space appears fairly good superiorly and posteriorly.

Right TMJ tomogram appears that she is postured forward a little bit here. There is an increased posterior joint space. Condyle looks similar in shape to the opposite side. Articular eminence is perhaps even more shallow on this side than the opposite side. 

On opening, there is good translation of both condyles. 

Waters view shows that the sinuses all appear fairly clear. Turbinates appear a little bit large, although there may be some airway passage on the right side. Septum is deviated a little bit off toward the left side. Left side appears pretty much obstructed. About one month ago, she did have sinusitis and took an antibiotic for that. 

Cephalometrically, she has a skeletal Class II jaw relationship. She has a huge adenoid mass in the nasopharynx area which is probably causing most of her nasal airway obstruction. Tonsils are seen on the radiograph. Significant vertical maxillary excess is noted. Oropharyngeal airway dimensions are fairly good, and again the major obstruction is at the nasopharynx area where she has a huge adenoid mass.

Bolton’s analysis shows a Bolton’s difference of 2 mm in the anterior six teeth. Also, in a Class I position there is a transverse maxillary deficiency. Deficiency is probably about 5 mm. 

Basic Diagnoses

Anterior vertical maxillary excess
Transverse maxillary deficiency
AP mandibular deficiency
Class II occlusion
Hypertrophied turbinates
Severely hypertrophied nasopharyngeal adenoid tissue
Severe nasal airway obstruction
Impacted maxillary third molars
Severe lip incompetence (9 to 10 mm)
High occlusal plane angulation
Tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of approximately 2 mm

Recommended Treatment

Presurgical orthodontics
Continue to align and level maxillary arch keeping the maxillary lateral incisor roots tipped mesially and cuspid roots tipped distally as they currently are
Continue to align and level mandibular arch. Strip lower anterior teeth removing approximately no more than 2 mm of tooth structure to help compensate for the tooth-size discrepancy 

Surgery
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the maxilla superiorly approximately 7 to 8 mm, expand it, and decrease the occlusal plane angulation
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction
Bilateral partial turbinectomies
Nasopharyngeal adenoidectomy
Surgical removal of impacted maxillary third molars x 2

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

original_note:
EMMA KINDSCHY									04/30/2008

Emma is 7 years old, birth date 12/05/2000.  She is in good health with no major medical problems. She currently taking the following medications:

Motrin 260 mg b.i.d. 
Klonopin 0.5 mg at bedtime.

Emma apparently has had some problems with headaches over the past 2 years, but in the past year they have gotten worse.  Particularly during the past 4 to 6 weeks, her pain levels have increased significantly.  She does have a history of clenching and bruxism at night time.  She has worn an Aqualizer splint for about 2 to 3 weeks and this has helped some, but she chews them up fairly quickly.  Her mother has given her some 0.5 mg of Klonopin at night and this seems to help have helped the situation, both to keep her from chewing the Aqualizer splints as well as decreasing her headache and pain problems.  She did have some physical therapy last week as well.  She has been to see an orthodontist who wants to use some type of mandible growth appliances plus headgear to correct her bite problems.

Emma gets earaches, but no tinnitus and she does have some problems with vertigo. 

On clinical examination, upper lip length is probably about 18 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 3 mm.  Midlines all appear to be fairly well aligned.  She has fairly good profile balance.  Chin may be just slightly AP deficient.  Intraorally, she has her lower four incisors, but has her primary C, D, and E’s, but her first molars are also in in the lower arch.  In the upper arch, likewise she has four incisors and first molars, but has her primary C, D, and E’s.  She basically has Class II end-on cuspid relationship.  She has an anterior deep bite of about 5 mm.  She is a little bit more Class II on the right side as compared to the left side.  She is almost Class I on the left side, but a little bit Class II on the right side by probably 2 mm.  Mandibular dental midline may be just about 1 mm off towards the right side.  It is hard for Emma to bite on her back teeth as it is not comfortable for her.  Oropharyngeal tissues looks relatively normal.  Tonsils look normal for her age.  She does have a cold right now, but still tonsils look relatively normal.  She normally can breathe through her nose without any significant problems.  To palpation, she has mild tenderness over the left TMJ.  Maximum opening about 52 mm.  Excursion movements were about 9 mm in each direction.  On opening, joints seem to function relatively smoothly, although she does dislocate the condyle out beyond the articular eminence, but I do not feel any sounds or indications of an articular disc displacement.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the development of permanent cuspids and all the bicuspids.  The mandibular right second bicuspid is tipped mesially somewhat, but the others appear to be fairly aligned.  The second molars are all developing.  The condyles look relatively normal.  The sinuses look relatively clear.  
Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears fairly well seated within the fossa.  It appears to have adequate space around it.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  

Right TMJ tomogram is a little bit more difficult to read.  It is difficult to identify the condyle and joint spaces.  On opening, only the left side was taken with the jaw open.  The right side was not open all the way.  On the left side, the condyle comes out and anterior significantly to the articular eminence and may account for the popping noise that she has in her joints.  
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Emma has slight Class II skeletal relationship and Class II end-on occlusion.  Mandible appears slightly retruded.  Oropharyngeal airway, however, looks good.  Incisors appear to have good angulation in both the maxillary and mandibular arches.  
Diagnoses for Emma would be as follows:
AP mandibular deficiency.
Chronic subluxation of the mandibular condyle.
Chronic nocturnal clenching and bruxism.
TMJ pain. 
Headaches. 
In the mixed dentition. 

Emma does have chronic clenching and bruxism.  The mother has given her some Klonopin 0.5 mg that appears to work well for her, and Emma has felt better during the past few days that she has taken it and her headaches have pretty much stopped.  I believe her headaches and TMJ pain are caused by the inflammatory process created by the chronic clenching and bruxism as well as the subluxation of the mandibular condyles.  The orthodontist back home has recommended headgear and growth appliances on her mandible.  At this time, I think we should hold off on those and see if we can get her jaw situation under control.  I am recommending we continue on the Klonopin since that seems to be effective for her.  I gave her a prescription for Klonopin 0.5 mg, dispensing 30 tablets, 1 tablet q.h.s., refill x5.  Emma’s mother is to keep me informed of Emma’s progress.

original_note:
BRIGITTE OLSON									04-11-03

Brigitte is 39 years old and referred by Dr. Scott Clayhold from Seattle, Washington. 

Patient History

She has had problems in the past with postpartum depression and with hypertension. Brigitte has had previous jaw surgery. The previous surgery she had involved a mandibular advancement procedure with sagittal split osteotomies, and she had a submental lipectomy at the same time. Dr. Stephen Rayburn in Plano performed the orthodontics here, although Brigitte began her orthodontics in New Zealand. She does have iritis in her eyes that she says she has been informed is associated with arthritis. She has been told that it may be an autoimmune disease process. Brigitte apparently originally started out with probably a Class II division II malocclusion with an anterior deep-bite where her upper teeth are biting into her lower gums. She had orthodontics performed and then had the mandibular advancement performed. Prior to her surgery, she had longstanding TMJ problems with clicking and popping and episodes of closed lock where she could not open. She first became aware of having TMJ problems back in the ‘80s. She had orthodontics for four years and then had the surgery on October 22, 1998. Initially she did quite well after the surgery and was able to open fairly wide and was not aware of pain in the joints. It was approximately two years later than the pain redeveloped and the limited jaw opening recurred again. The onset was around the time that her second child was born. Her pain now is a little bit more agonizing for her. She feels this may be related some to stress and the fact that she is not getting as much sleep because she is in graduate school and yawning more. Yawning appears to really significantly increase the pain. 

She is unaware of any nocturnal clenching or bruxism. She is more aware of her pain when she is driving her car. Afternoons and evenings are usually when her pain is at its worse. She gets headaches about three to four times per week at a level of 5, and they occur in the frontal, temporal, and posterior head area. She thinks the headaches are related to her iritis. She does get pain and headaches behind her eyes. She rates her TMJ pain at 7, overall pain on an average day at 5, jaw function and diet at 6, and disability at 1. 

Current Medications

Celexa 2.5 mg 
Prednisone eye drops
Enalapril 2.5 mg (blood pressure medicine)			
Aleve for pain and discomfort

Patient Concerns

Primary concerns that Brigitte has now include the following:

TMJ pain
Headaches 
Pain through the jaws and neck
Difficulties eating and chewing
Limited jaw opening
Occasional earaches 

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Brigitte has fairly good facial symmetry in the middle and upper third face. Some asymmetry in the lower third face. Upper lip length is about 19 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship measures about 3.5 mm. Maxillary dental midline is pretty much on the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline is shifted to the right 2 mm. Chin is about 3 mm to the right. 

In profile view, Brigitte has some deficiency in AP projection of the maxilla about 3 to 4 mm. Chin is retruded by probably at least 10 to 12 mm. Chin-neckline is extremely short, and chin-neck angle is not well-defined. 

Intraorally, she has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. Teeth seem to be relatively well-aligned and leveled. Has good attached gingival around all of her teeth. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars present. Slight reverse curve of occlusion in the upper arch. Fairly close to Class I cuspid bilaterally. Has an anterior deep-bite of probably 5 mm. Transversely, the occlusal planes are fairly level. Oropharynx appears clear and looks relatively normal. Intranasally, there may be a slight deviation of the nasal septum off toward the right side. Right turbinate is moderately large. The left one is moderately large, but the airway on the left side appears much greater than the airway on the right side. Has somewhat of a wide columella base. Opening up the nostrils though did not seem to improve her breathing significantly. 

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows evidence of previous sagittal split osteotomies as there are bone plates present. She is missing the mandibular left first molar and has a bridge replacing that tooth. Sinuses appear a little bit cloudy, particularly the right one. Nasal airway looks better. Left TMJ condyle appears somewhat arthritis. Right condyle is quite small in size and appears to be arthritic and has undergone at some point in time some condylar resorption. She is missing all four third molars but has the rest of her teeth except the mandibular left first molar.

Waters view shows that the sinuses are clear. Shows the mandible is shifted a little bit off toward the right side. Nasal airways may be okay, although the right side is somewhat constricted. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows the presence of significant osteoarthritis in the joint with significant loss of volume of the condyle vertically, anteroposteriorly, and probably also in the third dimension. The articular eminence is moderately steep. May be some degenerative changes on the posterior aspect of the articular eminence but difficult to tell.

Left TMJ tomogram shows the presence of osteoarthritic changes as well with an osteophytic formation extending superiorly probably indicative of a perforation on that side. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Flattening of the posterior head of the condyle is present. 

Open views show restricted translation in both joints. 

Cephalometrically, the head is tipped way superiorly so I can’t really evaluate the airway dimensions. Appears to be basically a skeletal Class I, although both the maxilla and mandible may be retruded. Looking at a Panorex dated 10/29/98 shows that there apparently have been further degenerative changes in the right condyle today as compared to then. 

Basic Diagnoses

Bilateral  TMJ severe arthritis and articular disc dislocation with severe degenerative changes with adhesions to the articular eminence with evidence of significant condylar resorption on the right side
AP maxillary deficiency
AP mandibular deficiency
Slight Class II cuspid relationship (1 to 2 mm)
High occlusal plane angle
Decreased oropharyngeal airway
Anterior deep-bite
TMJ pain
Headaches 
Myofascial pain
Earaches 
Occasional vertigo

Recommended Treatment

Presurgical orthodontics
Align and level maxillary arch
Align and level mandibular arch. May require a bite splint to open up the bite in order to place lower appliances

Surgery, Treatment Option 1
Bilateral TMJ condylectomies, debridement, reconstruction, and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla and downgraft it posteriorly
Bilateral coronoidectomies
Fat grafts, bilateral TMJs (harvested from the abdomen)

Surgery, Treatment Option 2
Bilateral TMJ condylectomies, debridement, and reconstruction with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses
Fat grafts, bilaterally TMJs (harvested from the abdomen)

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

B217
B217 is 52 years old, referred by Dr. XXXX. B217 was apparently asymptomatic until about 10 years ago. She was involved in a motor vehicle accident where she was struck from behind and struck her head on the headrest. Apparently, something got dislocated in her jaw and she underwent a manipulation to try to reset the mandible. This was about 10 years ago. She was referred to a pain clinic where this manipulation was probably done. The pain clinic also did injections into the back of her head and her neck area and jaw area which helped some. In fact, she still gets injections periodically. She also has been through acupressure and she has had several years of physical therapy without resolution of the problems. Ten years ago, she also underwent dental work to try to alleviate her pain and problems. Last year, orthodontics was initiated and this has improved her bite some, but she still continues to have significant functional and pain problems. She takes the following medications:
1. Celebrex 100 mg p.r.n.
2. Premarin.
3. TriCor.
4. Ambien.

B217 does not have any problems with headaches, but her TMJ pain frequently gets to a level of 9 and average daily pain is at 7. She rates her jaw function at 8, diet at 8, and disability at 8. Primary concerns that B217 has includes the following:
1. Severe pain.
2. Muscle spasms.
3. Bite continues to change.
4. Difficulties eating and chewing.
5. Difficulties sleeping.
6. Teeth and jaws do not align properly.
7. Diet is significantly affected with ability to eat soft foods.
8. Clenching and grinding of the teeth at night and during the day.
9. Unstable jaw relationship.
10. Speech problems at times.

Treatment goals that B217 has would be:
1. Reduce or eliminate the pain.
2. Stabilize the jaws.
3. Reduce pain in the neck and shoulder area.
4. Align the teeth and bite.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
: In frontal view, B217 has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 22 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is about 3 mm. The maxillary dental midline is about 1 mm off to the right is as the mandibular dental midline, but the chin is about 2 mm to the left. In profile view, she has AP deficiency in the point A area. The chin appears to be retruded about 15 mm or so. Chin neckline is extremely short and there is an abscess at the chin neck angle. Intraorally, B217 is missing the left first bicuspid and she has the rest of her teeth back through the second molars. The lower right cuspid is about 1 mm posterior to the left cuspid. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. The lateral incisors appear a little bit small. In centric relation, B217 occludes only on her left second molar. All other teeth are out of occlusion. She has a 1 mm open bite at the incisors and about at least 2 mm open bite in the left cuspid area. In her centric relation, the maxillary midline is off to the right side about 2 mm, and the mandibular dental midline and chin are off towards to the left side about 2 mm. She has a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right side being 2 mm higher than the left side. The incisor teeth are tipped slightly towards the left side at the incisal edges. The lower dental midline and chin midline are fairly well aligned with each other. The nasal tip may be deviated just off to the right side about 1 to 2 mm. The uvula is excessively enlarged and elongated, but the rest of the structures appear relatively normal. The turbinates appear moderate in size, but the septum appears relatively normal. It is difficult to tell if there is any significant airway obstruction. To palpation, B217 has moderate to severe pain over the TMJs and retromandibular area, and mild pain over the masseters, medial pterygoids, and shoulders. Maximal incisal opening is 42 mm, opening without severe pain 30 mm. Excursion movement to the right 10 mm and to the left 7 mm, but very painful for her to do those manipulations.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of the maxillary third molars, mandibular right third molar, but the retainment of the mandibular left third molar. She is also missing the mandibular left second bicuspid with the posterior teeth brought forward. Sinuses appear relatively clear. She has significant arthritis in both TMJs, but being worse on the right side. She has crowns on the mandibular left first and second molars and right first molar. Waters view shows that the sinuses appear relatively clear. There may be a deviated septum off towards the left side that appears to block a good part of the airway. Turbinates are moderately enlarged. Not good airway evidenced on the left side and the right side is relatively narrow. Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has a decreased posterior and superior joint space with an increased anterior joint space. Articular eminence is moderately steep. There are some degenerative changes of the condyle. Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears smaller than the opposite side with a lot of sclerosis on the head on the condyle and some anterior beaking. Joint space appears actually a little bit larger than the opposite, at least with the two cuts performed here. Articular eminence is not as steep in this side as it is in the other side. On opening, there is normal translation of the left condyle, but there is restriction of the translation of the right condyle. Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that B217 has a retruded mandible. She has a skeletal and occlusal Class II bite relationship. She has a high occlusal plane angle. She has a severely decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of about 2 mm at the soft palate level and about 3.5 mm at the base of the tongue. She does toss and turn a lot at night. There is a tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2.5 mm and is even accentuated beyond that because the lower incisors are over angulated.

Basic diagnoses would be as follows:
1. Bilateral TMJ severe arthritis (right side worse than left side).
2. AP mandibular hypoplasia.
3. Posterior vertical maxillary hypoplasia.
4. Class II occlusion.
5. Occludes only on the mandibular left second molars with all other teeth out of occlusion.
6. Anterior and bilateral posterior open bites except for the left second molars.
7. High occlusal plane angle.
8. Severely reduced oropharyngeal airway of 2 to 3 mm (normal dimension 11 mm).
9. Severe TMJ pain.
10. Severe myofascial pain.
11. Sleep apnea syndrome.
12. Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2.5 mm.
13. Transverse cant in the occlusion with the right side 2 mm higher than the left side.
14. Hypertrophied turbinates with partial nasal airway obstruction.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1. Presurgical orthodontics. a. Continue to align and level maxillary arch. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. b. Align and level mandibular arch. Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing at least 2.5 mm of tooth structure and retract to correct for tooth size discrepancy as well as to decrease the over angulated lower incisors.
2. CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol).
3. Surgery. a. Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. b. Multiple maxillary osteotomies to down graft the posterior aspect and level transversely. c. Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen). d. Bilateral coronoidotomies. e. Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies.
4. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B217
MRI
AGE: DOB:
Date of Acquisition: Date of Interpretation:
Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has undergone significant arthritic changes with what appears to be loss of vertical height. There is significant decreased joint space present. Lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 mm. It is difficult to identify discal tissue.

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has a little bit better morphology. There appears to be decreased vertical joint space. Lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly probably just a couple of millimeters. There appears to be distal tissue anteriorly and laterally displaced.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle appears arthritic. There is significant anterior beaking present. There appears to be decreased superior and posterior joint space. Articular eminence is actually quite steep. The articular disc is anteriorly displaced and appears fairly degenerative. The discal tissue appears to be sitting beneath the articular eminence. The disc appears to be shorter in anteroposterior dimension. The disc is extremely foreshortened and quite thickened, particularly towards the lateral aspect of the joint. There appears to be some sclerosis on the head of the condyle. The disc significantly deformed and may be difficult to salvage. Open view shows that there is some restricted translation of the condyle with the condyle not quite coming down beneath the articular eminence. The articular eminence remains anteriorly displaced. The disc is severely deformed and questionable salvageability remains.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is somewhat arthritic. There is some superior peaking on the condylar head. There is sclerosis around the head. Articular eminence is quite steep. The articular discs are in much better condition as in the vertical orientation within the fossa. It could be in a pre-click displaced disc position. The disc does appear salvageable on this side. On opening, the left disc reduces and there is fairly good translation of the condyle and the disc.

IMPRESSION:
1. Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly dislocated, but with reduction.
2. Right TMJ advanced arthritis and articular disc dislocation with severe degeneration and deformation with questionable salvageability.

original_note:
SANDRA SEAL									12-29-03

Sandy is 21 years old.

Patient History

She had her tonsils removed at the age of 8 years and has had no other surgery. She is in good health with no major medical problems. She takes Nordette 1 per day. She has had previous orthodonics with Dr. Pat Brady.
 
She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, but does get occasional vertigo. The only other joints that give her any problem at all are her knees, and that is not of any great significance. She does give a history of nocturnal bruxism. She has undergone orthodontic treatment and finished when she was about 14 years old. The occlusion was fairly tight at that time, but over the years it has changed to its current situation. Sandy gets headaches only about once every two weeks that she relates to tension. In reference to her headaches, currently they occur about once every two weeks, but earlier in the semester they were more significant, and, in fact, she called her dad thinking she had a brain tumor. Currently she rates her headaches at about a level of 4 when they do occur in the frontal, temporal, and sometimes posterior head area. She rates her TMJ pain at about a level of 6, daily pain at a level of 2, jaw function at 5, diet at 4, and disability at 1. 

Sandy is not really sure when her jaw problems began, but she became aware of the clicking and popping about three years ago because she used to show off to people her clicking and popping joints. There is no known etiology for the onset of the joint problem.

Patient Concerns

TMJ pain
Joint occasionally locks
Bruxism
Bite changing
Difficulty biting particularly with the front teeth

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Sandy has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 20 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 3 mm. Maxillary dental midline is pretty much on the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline is just a little bit to the right side about 0.5 mm Mandibular dental midline and chin are fairly well aligned with each other.

In profile view, upper lip supralabrale is posterior to subnasale by probable about 3 to 4 mm. Maxilla appears to be probably about 6 mm AP deficient. Chin is approximately 15 mm AP deficient. Chin-neck angle is not well-defined. Chin-neck length appears short. 

Intraorally, Sandy has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars present. She has a 3 x 3 retainer. She has adequate attached gingiva around the lower anterior teeth, although the full crowns are exposed, and the roots may be just slightly exposed on the anterior four teeth. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. Has about a 2-mm open bite. She occludes only on her first and second molars with the rest of the teeth being out of occlusion. She has a Class II end-on occlusion, being Class II by about 4 to 5 mm. Transversely, the occlusal plane appears fairly level. Right cuspid could be about 0.5 mm lower than the left cuspid. 

Intranasally, turbinates appear somewhat enlarged and a little bit boggy. She says she has been having some allergies lately. 

Intraorally, oropharynx appears clear and without any evidence of pathology. Maximal incisal opening is about 51 mm. She can open to 33 mm without any significant discomfort. Excursive movement to the right is 9 mm and to the left 10 mm. In opening, she has some definite clicking and popping in her joints and fairly close to sounding almost like crepitation, but she feels it is more like clicking or popping. Occurs a little bit earlier on the left side as compared to the right side, and occurs at about 30 mm on the left, 35 on the right, and on closing, 20 mm on the left and 25 mm on the right. 

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. The rest of the teeth are present. Roots on the central incisors appear a little bit short in both the upper and lower arch. Sinuses appear relatively clear. Turbinates appear relatively moderate in size. Left TMJ is not on the radiograph, but the right one looks relatively normal. She has a 3 x 3 retainer in the lower arch.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has a little sclerosis on the top of it. Decreased posterior joint space. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Could be a little bit of anterior beaking on the condyle. 

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle again that looks a little bit sclerotic with some anterior beaking. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Posterior joint space may be slightly decreased but difficult to tell. 

On opening, there is good translation of both condyles with the right side actually translating a little bit further forward than the left side. In the open view, the condyles appear to have a little bit better morphology, although both show flattening on the anterior head of the condyle. 

Waters view shows that the sinuses are relatively clear. Turbinates appear fairly large and seem to fill most of the nasal airway. Mandibular angle on the right side appears a little bit longer vertically than the left side, but it could be possibly just due to some slight rotation of the head. 

Cephalometrically, Sandy appears to have some AP maxillary and mandibular hypoplasia. She does have the anterior open bite and slight Class II skeletal relationship. Appears to occlude only on her molars with the other teeth being out of occlusion. A little bit of vertical discrepancy at the inferior border of the mandible, although at the occlusal surface the level seems fairly good. 

Sandy has a tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of about 2 mm in the anterior six teeth. She also has about a 0.5-mm tooth-size discrepancy in the second bicuspid area with the upper second bicuspids being quite a bit smaller than the lower second bicuspids. Lining up the teeth in a good Class I cuspid relationship leaves Sandy in almost an end-on incisor relationship which goes along with the tooth-size discrepancy. 

Basic Diagnoses

Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with late reduction and significant deformation of the articular discs
AP maxillary deficiency
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency
AP mandibular deficiency
Class II occlusion
Anterior open bite of 2 mm
Headaches 
TMJ pain
Occluding only on the first and second molars bilaterally 
Nocturnal bruxism
Occasional vertigo
Slight overangulation of the lower incisors
Tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2 mm
High occlusal plane angulation of 16 degrees (normal 8 degrees)

Recommended Treatment

Presurgical orthodontics 
Align and level maxillary arch. Keep lateral incisor roots tipped mesially and cuspid roots tipped distally to provide room for interdental osteotomies
Align and level mandibular arch. Remove the 3 x 3 retainer. Strip lower anterior teeth, removing approximately 2 mm of tooth structure and retract to help correct for the tooth-size discrepancy 

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors 
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to expand the maxilla and level the occlusal plane
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion 

I did discuss in detail with Dr. Seals and his daughter that her TMJ problems have been present for much longer than four years, so the prognosis is much more guarded in reference to performing the surgery. We did discuss at length the possibilities that if the disc repositioning procedures do not hold up that total joint prostheses could be indicated down the line. If surgery is not done, and she develops symptoms later on, total joint prostheses may also be required. They fully understand these factors. I answered all the questions that Dr. Seal and Sandy had today. I will refer her to Dr. Michael Arvystas in New York to do the presurgical orthodontics.

original_note:
B134
B134 is 47 years old. She had a skiing accident about 20 years ago where she fell, striking her chest and subluxated her mandible. It went back in place on its own. She developed clicking and popping in her jaw joints in her 30s, however, she did not develop pain problems until 4 years ago. Since then, she has had extensive treatments including the following in no specific order:

Lavage of the right TMJ x2 with probable steroid injections. Steroid injections into the right joint approximately 30 times.
Splint therapy x4.
Physical therapy.
Lidocaine infusion. Attempt at electro-rhizotomy to kill the nerve in right TMJ.

B134 is currently on the following medications:
Clonidine 0.1 mg 2 tabs b.i.d. Gabapentin 1200 mg q.h.s. and 600 mg b.i.d. Seroquel 50 mg t.i.d., 300 mg q.h.s.
Effexor XR 150 mg q.d.
Prilosec 40 mg q.d.
Zofran 4 mg b.i.d. Visteril 50 mg t.i.d.
Zantac 150 mg b.i.d. Doxepin 100 mg q.h.s.
Advair inhaler p.r.n.

B134's pain is predominantly confined to the right side, but occasionally the left side will be symptomatic, but the majority of problems are on the right side. She rates her headaches at 7 in the right temporal area, right TMJ pain at 10, average daily pain at 10, jaw function at 7, diet at 8, disability at 10. With the current medication regimen that she is on, B134 has gained 50 pounds in the past 6 months. She gets frequent right-sided earaches and moderate vertigo, but no tinnitus or hearing loss. She has had her shoulders and hands reconstructed. She is aware of clenching and bruxism at night as well as during the day, and she feels this is getting worse for her. In addition, B134 has sleep apnea symptoms which have been present for approximately 2 years. B134 states that she has severe snoring and moderate daytime somnolence. She was diagnosed with sleep apnea 2 years ago of a moderate degree. She has moderate to severe difficulties sleeping at night. She smokes about a half a pack of cigarettes per day and has done this for about 30 years. She has had previous sinus surgery and a septoplasty.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
In frontal view, B134 has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. She has some slight increased fullness in the right TMJ area and perhaps the right angle area. Upper lip length is about 22 mm and upper tooth lip relationship is probably about -2 to 3 mm. The maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline. The mandibular dental midline is shifted 2 mm to the left. The chin is shifted about 3 to 4 mm to the left. In profile, she has some AP maxillary deficiency of probably 4 to 5 mm and the chin about 4 to 5 mm as well. Intraorally, she is missing mandibular right first and second bicuspids and on the left side she is missing probably a first bicuspid. In the upper arch, she is missing the left bicuspids and molars, and on the right side she is missing the molars. She is basically a Class I cuspid on the left side, but Class III cuspid on the right side. She has an end-on incisor relationship and cross bite tendency probably on the left side. She has a cant in the occlusal plane with the left side being about 2 mm higher than the right side. Oropharyngeal tissues look healthy. Soft palate may be somewhat elongated. Fascial pillars and tonsils appear relatively normal. Nasal septum is deviated a little bit off towards the right side. Turbinates appear moderate in size, but there does not appear at least clinically to be any significant nasal airway obstruction. B134 is currently on a CPAP machine. To palpation, she has moderate pain over the right temporalis, right medial pterygoid, and high moderate over the right masseter, and severe pain over the right TMJ. Maximal incisal opening is 27 mm, but that is with significant pain. Excursion movement to the right about 3 mm, to the left only 2 to 2.5 mm. B134 does have a partial denture that she can wear in the upper arch. B134 appears to have a component of macroglossia.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
The septum appears reasonably straight. Turbinates are moderate in size and there appears to be good airway. Sinuses appear relatively clear. Panorex shows she is missing mandibular left first bicuspid, mandibular right first bicuspid, second bicuspid, and all the molars, but the first molar has been replaced with an implant. In the upper arch, she is missing the left bicuspids and molars, and on the right side she is missing bicuspids and molars, but she has 2 implants replacing the bicuspids on that right side. Sinuses appear relatively clear. There is a little flattening on the anterior condyle on the left joint. Right joint shows significant arthritic changes and difficult to evaluate any joint space. It appears there may be a little bit of reactive bone around the joint. Left TMJ coronal tomograms shows that the condyle appears slightly arthritic, but not too significant. Joint space looks almost excessive. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly probably at least 4 mm. Right TMJ coronal view shows significant degenerative changes with erosions of the fossa and of the condyle. Some areas appear to have bone-on-bone contact. There is heterotopic bone growing down around the lateral pole of the condyle. There is significantly decreased joint space. Left TMJ sagittal views show a condyle with increased vertical joint space. Articular eminence is moderate in inclination and not very long in length. The condyle appears to be somewhat posteriorly positioned in the fossa. The condyle appears to have reasonably good shape. There is a little bit of flattening and degenerative changes at the lateral aspect of the joint. Right TMJ sagittal view shows a joint that is severely arthritic. There is reactive bone in the fossa and significant degenerative changes at the condyle. Out laterally, there appears to be heterotopic bone that has developed around the condyle, particularly around the posterior aspect. There is decreased joint space and just severe degenerative changes. The articular eminence is shallow and eroded. Lateral cephalogram shows that B134 has a significantly decreased oropharyngeal airway and at the soft palate level, the AP dimension is only about 3 mm. At the base of the tongue, it is about 7 mm. She has a slight skeletal Class III jaw relationship.

Basic diagnoses for B134 would be as follows:
Severe degenerative arthritis with heterotopic bone formation and anklyosis of the right TMJ.
Left TMJ mild arthritis, deformation of the articular disc with medial dislocation.
AP maxillary hypoplasia.
AP mandibular hypoplasia. Class I cuspid relationship on the left side, Class III cuspid relationship on the right side.
Missing maxillary bicuspids and molars with two dental implants in the bicuspid areas in the right maxilla. Missing mandibular left first bicuspid and mandibular right bicuspids and molars with the first molar replaced with an implant. Severely decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of 3 mm at the soft palate level and 7 mm at the base of the tongue (normal dimensions in those two areas are 11 mm).
Severe TMJ pain.
Severe headaches. Severe myofascial pain. Hyperplastic soft palate.
Sleep apnea syndrome with requirement of CPAP management.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
Medications: Pain management.
Reevaluate and eliminate unnecessary medications that she is currently on. CT scan of jaw and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol).
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. Maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla. Bilateral coronoidotomies. Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen).
Uvulopalatopharyngoplasty. Application of maxillary and mandibular arch bars. Dental reconstruction.

She apparently tried to commit suicide at one time. She was on numerous pain medications and states she was not aware of it, but did attempt suicide. She is going to be seeing a new psychiatrist very soon and hopefully we will be able to eliminate many of the medications she is currently on. I did give her a prescription for the following medications:

Norco 10, dispensing 120, 1 tablet q.4 to 6 hours p.r.n. pain. Klonopin, dispensing 60 tablets, 1 tablet q.h.s., ½ tablet b.i.d.
She is to keep me informed of her progress.

B134 MRI AGE: 47/F
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with good mediolateral width and, in fact, almost excessively wide. The articular disc is in position over the top of the condyle, but is medially displaced. There is decreased joint space at the lateral rim of the fossa. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 4 mm. The disc itself appears thickened.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle appears arthritic and appears to have lost some vertical volume. There appears to be reactive bone in the fossa and some other degenerative material within the joint space. Joint space appears narrow in some areas. The condyle appears sclerotic in some areas.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology. Out laterally, the condyle is a little flattened anteriorly and there may be just some slight anterior beaking towards the lateral aspect, but not too significant. Articular eminence is moderately steep. The articular disc is excessively thick, but in fairly good position. It may be just slightly forward at the lateral aspect of the joint, but more medially it appears to be in a little bit better position. The joint space is increased because of the thickness of the articular disc. On opening, there is reasonably good translation of the condyle and the disc, and the disc remains in proper position.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a joint that is severely arthritic. The condyle appears to have lost some vertical height. There is some anterior beaking. There appears to be a bone deposition in the fossa and in the condylar head and perhaps some heterotopic bone formation present. There is no identifiable disc present. On opening, there is some translation, but there appears to be reactive kind of tissue in the joint space. There is flattening of the articular eminence evident, and this could possibly be due to previous surgical intervention.

IMPRESSION:
Left TMJ relatively normal anatomy, but with an increased thickness of the articular disc and perhaps slight anterior positioning at the lateral aspect of the joint. Right TMJ severe arthritis affecting both the condyle and the fossa with a possible free bony fragment in the joint.
Right TMJ appears to be indicated for a total joint prosthesis.

original_note:
JORDAN PATTON									04/16/2008

Jordan is 13 years old.  She is referred by her mother, Blaire McDonald Patton.  Blaire is a former patient from 21 years ago.  Jordan has had tubes in her ears, but no other surgery.  Primary concerns are as follows:  
Jaws popping.
Gummy smile.
Difficulties breathing through the nose.  
Headaches.

Her problems began in reference to the TMJ area about 2 years ago.  In 2006, she had a Herbst appliance and about a year-and-a-half ago she began full orthodontics.  She takes no medications.  She has no earaches, tinnitus, nor vertigo.  The only other joints that bother her some are her knees.  She does have difficulties breathing through her nose.  She gets headaches sometimes 3 or 4 days per week at about a level of 5 in the frontal and temporal area.  She has some TMJ pain at about a level of 2, but states she has relatively normal jaw function at zero, diet at zero, and disability at zero. 

CLINICAL EVALUATION.  In frontal view, upper lip length is about 19 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is 5 mm.  Mandibular dental midline and chin are fairly well aligned on the facial midline.  Maxillary midline is about 1 mm off to the left side.  In profile view, upper lip is retruded about 3 mm and chin is protruded about 10 mm.  Chin neckline is a little bit short, but the chin neckline is reasonably well-defined.  The lower third face appears elongated.  Lip incompetence is about 7 mm.  Intraorally, she has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars.  Teeth are nicely aligned and leveled.  She has good attached gingiva around the lower arch.  There is a little hyperplasia of the gingival tissues.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back to the second molars present.  She has significant gingival hyperplasia in the upper ache.  She is very close to being a Class I cuspid-molar relationship.  She has some spacing on the right side between the lateral incisor and cuspid.  It appears that she probably has a tooth size discrepancy.  Transversely, occlusal plane is fairly level.  She has less than 0.5 mm of vertical overbite.  Oropharyngeal tissues look healthy.  Uvula is slightly elongated, but soft palate fascial pillars are normal and tonsils may be just mildly enlarged.  Intranasally, the septum may be deviated slightly off to the left side at the inferior aspect anteriorly.  Turbinates are fairly large in size.  She has difficulties breathing through her nose.  On opening, she has a pop on the left side at about 30 mm and closing at about 20 mm.  Right side seems to function reasonably smoothly. To palpation, she has moderate discomfort over the masseters, TMJs, and shoulders and milder tenderness over the temporalis muscles.  Maximal incisal opening is 46 mm.  Excursion movement in each direction is only about 4 mm.
RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the presence of developing unerupted third molars.  Sinuses look clear.  Condyles look relatively normal.  
Waters view shows that the bony septum at least is reasonably straight.  Turbinates appear quite large though.  Sinuses appear clear. 
Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology.  Joint space looks fairly well balanced.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  
Left TMJ tomogram shows there may be a little bit of flattening on the top of the condyle.  Articular eminence is fairly steep.  Joint space looks reasonable.  On opening, there is good translation of both joints.  
Lateral cephalogram shows that she has an anterior vertical maxillary excess and some AP macrogenia.  Oropharyngeal airway measures 8 mm at the soft palate level and about 5 mm at the narrowest part of the base of tongue.  Occlusal plane angle may be slightly increased.  She does have some lip incompetence.  
Basic diagnoses for Jordan would be as follows:
Left TMJ articular disc dislocation with reduction.
Anterior vertical maxillary excess.
AP maxillary deficiency. 
AP mandibular deficiency.
AP macrogenia.  
High occlusal plane angle.  
Over-angulation of lower incisors.  
Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of probably 2 to 3 mm (models were not available at the time of analysis).    
Hypertrophied turbinates.
Nasal airway obstruction.
Impacted third molars x4.
TMJ pain. 
Myofascial pain.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:	

MRI of left and right TMJs. 
Presurgical orthodontics.
Continue to align and level maxillary arch.  Place about 1 mm of space between each upper lateral and incisor and cuspid.
Continue to align and level mandibular arch.  Slenderize the low anterior teeth, removing at about 2 mm of tooth structure to correct for the tooth size discrepancy.
Surgery. 
Unilateral or bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors (dependent on MRI findings).
Multiple maxilla osteoarthritis to move the maxilla superiorly and decrease occlusal plane angle.  
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to set the mandible in proper position.
Anterior augmentation genioplasty with slight vertical reduction.
Bilateral inferior turbinectomies.
Surgical removal of impacted third molars x4.

original_note:
MICHELLE WEBER									03/23/2007

Michelle is 44 years old, referred by Dr. Al Bishop, 3001 Brown Trail #101, Bedford, TX 76021.  She had previous surgery for a hysterectomy, bladder suspension, breast augmentation, implant removal and lift.  

PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Headaches.
Jaw pain.
Clicking and popping when talking or eating.

She has had no treatment for the joint problems except she is under orthodontic treatment.  She began orthodontic treatment in March of 2006.  She has actually had nightguards to help to try and control her clenching and bruxism since the age of 28.  She has worn them almost continuously since then and she has been through 4 splints.  She gets earaches that are getting worse and tinnitus is getting worse.  She gets vertigo sometimes but has no hearing loss.  Other joints that bother her include her hips, fingers, neck and feet.  She rates her TMJ daily pain at a level of 7, average daily facial pain at level of 7, jaw function and diet at 0 and disability at 3.  She rates her headaches at level of 8 and she gets these in the frontal, temporal and posterior head area.  

CURRENT MEDICATIONS:
Norvasc 10 mg.
Triamp/HCTZ 37.5 mg.
Zyrtec 10 mg.
Acyclovir 200 mg. 
Multiple vitamins.
Calcium citrate.
Advil.
Excedrin Migraine for her severe headaches.

In frontal view, Michelle has fairly good left to right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 20 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 3 mm.  Maxillary dental midline is about 2 mm off to the left side.  Mandibular dental midline may be 1 mm off to the left.  Chin is pretty much on the facial midline.  In profile, Michelle has fairly good facial balance.  Intraorally, she is missing mandibular right second bicuspid.  She has the rest of her teeth back through the second molars.  She has a crown on the left second molar.  Significant lower arch asymmetry near the left cuspid that is probably 5 mm anterior to the right cuspid.  In fact, the right cuspid aligns better with the first bicuspid.  In the upper arch Michelle as all the teeth back through the second molars.  She has a bridge in the upper arch placed in the second bicuspid and the first molar.  She has a slight Class III end-on cuspid relationship on the left side being about 2 mm Class III.  On the right side she has a full Class II cuspid relationship.  She has an anterior deep bite of about 1 mm.  She has about a 1 mm overjet.  Oropharyngeal tissues look healthy.  Uvula, soft palate and faucial pillars all appear normal.  Intranasally, the septum is deviated off to the left side.  Turbinates appear normal to moderate in size and there appears to be adequate airway.  She does report some moderate snoring and some mild to moderate daytime somnolence. 
MICHELLE WEBER				03/23/2007				Page 2

She is not aware of having any sleep apnea type symptoms.  To palpation she has some moderate discomfort of masseters, TMJs and mild tenderness over the SCMs, styloid areas and temporalis muscles.  On opening, she has definite consistent pop in the right joint about 15 mm of opening and about 10 mm of closing.  On the left side it was a little bit more inconsistent and a little harder to pick up but she seemed to get a click at times at about 10 mm of opening and 5 mm of closing.  Maximal incisal opening is 50 mm.  Opening without discomfort 30 mm.  Excursive movements were 10 mm to the right and 9 mm to the left.  If Michelle lays on her back, she cannot sleep in that position at night as she blocks off her nasal airway.  She says she does not sleep much on her back.  Michelle thinks that her snoring mostly occurs when she is breathing through her mouth.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION:
Panorex shows the absence of all 4 third molars and the mandibular right second bicuspid.  She is also missing the maxillary left second bicuspid and first molar replaced by a 4-unit bridge.  She has root canals on the maxillary left first bicuspid and second molar.  She also has a root canal of mandibular left second molar. 

Waters view shows that she has some deviation of the bony septum.  Turbinates appear moderate in size.  Sinuses appear relatively clear.  

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is a little bit flattened on the anterior leading edge.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  Joint space looks reasonably good.  

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears to have fairly good morphology.  Joint space actually is normal and articular eminence is moderately steep.  On opening, there is good translation of both condyles. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has a slight skeletal Class II occlusal relationship.  She appears to have a high occlusal plane angle.  Oropharyngeal airway looks reasonably good with it being almost 10 mm at the soft palate level and probably 8 or 9 mm at the far base of the tongue.  There seems to be good space between the superior aspect of the tongue in the inferior aspect of the palate.

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation at the lateral aspect of the joint with reduction on opening. 
TMJ pain.
Headaches. 
Myofascial pain. 





MICHELLE WEBER				03/23/2007				Page 3

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Medications.
a.  Klonopin 1 mg tablets, 1 tablet q.h.s. ½ tablet b.i.d. if necessary for daytime clenching and bruxism.
Presurgical orthodontics.  
Continue to align and level the arches.
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor.
Bilateral mandibular ramus sagittal split osteotomies to maintain occlusion and allow the condyles to drop down enough to make room for the articular discs.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
CHARLES “CHARLIE” MILLER						04/22/2011

Charlie is 16 years old, birth date 01/18/1995.  Referred by Dr. Michael Naboroski, 1201 W. Army Trail Blvd., Addison, IL 60101-3105.  

Charlie has some significant difficulties breathing through his nose due to allergies.  He broke his collar bone last year and had a ganglion cyst removed from the heel of his left foot.  He has had tubes in his ears, tonsillectomy, and wisdom teeth removed.  Charlie’s basic concerns are as follows:

His jaws lock up every day when he is chewing.
Headaches.
TMJ pain. 
Difficulty chewing.

Charlie has had two phases of orthodontics done.  The last phase began in August- September 2010.  He had his wisdom teeth removed in August 2010.

Charlie’s TMJ problems began be believes when he was about 6 years old of unknown etiology.  He had clicking and popping in his jaw joints even back then.  He has had no other treatment except for the orthodontics to date for his jaw problems.  Charlie gets headaches about five times per week at a level of 7 in the frontal, temporal, and top of his head.  He rates his TMJ pain at 7, average daily pain at 5, jaw function at 7, diet at 7, disability at 6.  The only medication he takes routinely is Zyrtec.  He gets some earaches, occasional tinnitus, but no vertigo or hearing loss.  No other joints both him at this time.  He does have a lot of allergies including foods such as most nuts, peaches, bananas, and seafood except for salmon.  He also has seasonal allergies and is allergic to latex.  He believes that he may have some hypersensitivities to the metals in his braces as he tends to break out around his mouth area and get a rash there as well.  He is not aware of clenching or bruxing at nighttime. 

In frontal view, Charlie has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 21 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is 0.  The maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline.  The mandibular dental midline is 1 mm off to the left and the chin is probably about 3 mm off to the left.  Charlie has just a slight increased fullness in his right side of his face as compared to the left side as far as transverse projection.  In profile view, Charlie has fairly good balance between the upper lip, cheeks, and nose.  The chin is retruded and he has a very prominent labial mental fold.  The chin is retruded about 15 mm.  The chin neckline and chin neck angle are not defined.  Intraorally, Charlie has all of his lower teeth back through the second molars.  The lower teeth appear to be tipped outwards.  In the upper arch, Charlie has all of his teeth back through the second molars.  Charlie has an end-on Class II cuspid relationship on the right side by about 4 mm and on the left side he is almost a full Class II.  Transversely, the maxillary arch appears fairly level.  The left eye may be about 2 mm lower than the right eye.  Oropharyngeal tissues look normal and healthy.  Intranasally, the turbinates are moderately enlarged.  The septum is deviated towards at the left side at the base about 1 cm back inside.  Charlie has a 3 to 4 mm over jet and about a 1 to 2 mm vertical over bite.  To palpation, Charlie has got moderate pain over the masseters, TMJs, styloids, shoulders, and milder pain over the temporalis, SCMs, posterior neck areas.  The maximal incisal opening is 46 mm.  excursion movements are bout 10 mm in each direction.  As he opens and closes, he can elicit popping noises in both joints.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars, but all the rest of the teeth are present.  Roots of the four maxillary incisors are foreshortened.  The turbinates appear moderate in size.  The sinuses appear relatively clear.  It is difficult to evaluate the joints.

Coronal view shows that the septum is deviated a little bit off towards the left side.  The turbinates are moderately enlarged, particularly on the left side and may cause some airway difficulties.  The sinuses appear relatively clear except for a small mucocele in the left sinus.

Axial view shows that the septum is straight at this particular cut.  The turbinates are moderately enlarged and appears to present with a decreased airway.  The sinuses appear relatively clear.  

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that appears to have undergone some resorption perhaps as there are some changes.  There is a little sclerosis on the top of the condyle and some anterior beaking.  The condyle is a little bit displaced posterior in the fossa.  The articular eminence has a moderate inclination. 

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is a little sclerotic and with a little bit of anterior beaking present.  The condyle appears centered.  The articular eminence has a moderate inclination. 

Left TMJ axial view shows a condyle that is a little bit narrow mediolaterally.  There appears to be decreased vertical joint space.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 to 4 mm.  

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is a little bit narrow mediolaterally.  There appears to be decreased vertical joint space.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 to 4 mm.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Charlie has got a retruded mandible and has got posterovertical maxillary hypoplasia.  The oropharyngeal airway measures about 4 mm at the base of the tongue and at the soft palate measures about 7 to 8 mm.  The lower teeth appear to be somewhat over angulated.  The tongue appears to fill the majority of the oral cavity.  
Basic diagnoses for Charlie would be as follows:

Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation without reduction,
Maxillary vertical hypoplasia. 
Mandibular AP hypoplasia.  
Hypertrophied turbinates.  
Decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of 4 mm (normal AP dimension is 11 mm).  
TMJ pain.
Headaches. 
Myofascial pain.  
Multiple hypersensitivities.  
Short roots involving primarily the maxillary incisors. 

Recommended treatments would be as follows:

1.	Testing at the Environmental Center for hypersensitivities to titanium and 	titanium alloy as well as polyester. 
2.	Presurgical orthodontics. 
	a.	Continue to align and level maxillary arch.  Keep lateral incisor roots 			tipped mesially and cuspid roots tipped distally to make room for 				interdental osteotomies. 
	b.	Continue to align and level mandibular arch. 
3.	Surgery.
	a.	Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek 			anchors.
	b.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla and down graft it.
	c.	Bilateral ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible forward 				approximately 10 to 12 mm at pogonion.
	d.	Graft to anterior mandible to decrease problems with labial mental fold.
	e.	Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies. 
4.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
