original_note:
KASEY MAYBERRY							03/14/2011

Kasey is 21 years old, birth date 11/23/1989.  She is a junior at the University of Oklahoma and she is a cheerleader.  Referred by Nancy Harlan, 4112 Hanover Street, Dallas, TX.  Kasey’s TMJ problems began when she was 20 years old when she was coming down off of a stunt and got hit in the jaw during cheerleading.  This created quite a bit of pain for her.  She did not receive any treatment, but iced her jaws down for about 2 weeks and then things felt better.  Then in January 2011, she was coming back from New York on an airplane and yawned and heard cracking noises in her joints and then after that it hurt for her to yawn or open her mouth wide.  She now has a constant hurting in her jaw predominantly on the left side and she points more to the masseter muscle area.  She did see Dr. Bob Talley in Norman and he made a splint for Kasey which she wore for about 2 or 3 days and it made her pain worse so she discontinued it.  The only other treatment she has had to date is she had her wisdom teeth removed at the age of 11.  She has never required orthodontic treatment.  

Kasey rates her headaches at a level of 8 on the left side in the frontal, temporal, and top of the head area.  On the right side, it is closer to a 4 in the frontal, temporal and top of the head area.  She rates her TMJ pain at 8, average daily pain at 6, jaw function at 6, diet at 4, disability at 6.  She takes ibuprofen but that is the only medication that she currently is on.  She gets earaches predominantly in the left side, no tinnitus, but occasional vertigo.  She has no other joints that bother her and she is not aware of any allergies.  She has no difficulties wearing cheap jewelry.  She is aware of clenching at nighttime and also during the day.  

On frontal view, Kasey has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 17 mm.  Upper tooth lip tooth relationship is about 4 mm.  The maxillary, mandibular, and chin midline are all pretty much on the facial midline.  In profile view, she has reasonable facial balance, although the maxilla and mandible may be moderately retruded perhaps 2 or 3 mm at the maxilla and probably 6 or 8 at the chin area.  The chin neckline is reasonably well-formed, although the angle has a soft contour to it.  Intraorally, Kasey has all of her teeth in both arches back through the second molars.  She has basically a Class I cuspid relationship and an anterior deep bite of about 3 to 4 mm.  Oropharyngeal tissues look normal and healthy.  To palpation, she has low severe pain in the left TMJ, high moderate left masseter, right TMJ, right styloid area and mild tenderness in the right masseter.  Joints seem to function relatively smoothly.  The maximal incisal opening is 47 mm, opening without discomfort 23 mm.  Excursion movements to the right is 7 mm, to the left 8 mm.  

RADIOGRAPH EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars.  The septum looks straight.  The turbinates appear moderately enlarged.  

Coronal view shows that the sinuses are clear.  The septum does have a spur shifted off towards the right side above the inferior turbinates, but below the middle turbinates.  In the right side, the right turbinate is hypertrophic and appears to block most of the functional airway on the right side.  The left turbinate is more normal in size.  She has more difficulties breathing through the right side of her nose than the left side of her nose.  Kasey has no history of snoring, but does have severe daytime somnolence.  She is not aware of having sleep apnea and has a mild sleep disorder and some mouth breathing at nighttime and daytime.  

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has reasonably good morphology.  There appears to be good joint space present.  The articular eminence is moderately steep.  The joint space is well-balanced.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle with reasonably good morphology.  There is good joint space.  I suspect the disc is in position on that side.  The articular eminence is moderately shallow. 

Left TMJ coronal view shows that the condyle has fairly good morphology.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly quite significantly and there is decreased lateral joint space.  The articular eminence is likely medially displaced to a certain extent.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with good mediolateral width.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly probably 5 to 6 mm.  There is decreased lateral joint space.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has retruded maxilla and mandible along with some AP microgenia.  Oropharyngeal airway measures about 8 mm to the soft palate and about 6 mm at the base of the tongue.  

Basic diagnoses would be as follows:

Trauma to mandible 1 year ago.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc with partial medial displacement being worse on the left side than the right side. 
Myofascial pain being predominantly in the masseters and temporalis muscles, but being worse in the left masseter muscle.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Medication:  Klonopin 1 mg tablet, dispensing 30 tablets, 1 tablet q.h.s., refill x3.
2.	If medication does not resolve problem consider surgery.
	a.	TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair.
	b.	Sagittal split osteotomy to the mandible to maintain occlusion. 
3.	Consider hyaluronic injection into masseter muscles.  

Kasey is to contact me in 1 week to let me know if the Klonopin has made an improvement for her.  She has tried splint therapy and this made her pain significantly worse probably because it stimulated increased clenching and bruxism.

original_note:
BRENDA MORALES VAZQUEZ							12/07/2007

Brenda is 33 years old and is referred by Dr. Joel Teig and Dr. Ivan Marrero at Santurce Medical Plaza, Santurce, Puerto Rico.  Brenda is in good health with no major medical problems except she has hypothyroidism and takes Synthroid 0.75 mg daily.  She has had an adverse reaction to penicillin.  

Brenda had orthodontics at the age of 12.  She had a retruded mandible and then had upper first bicuspids extracted and maxillary teeth retruded to meet the mandible.  This did not fully correct the bite problem, however.  Orthodontics was initiated again in 2003 and lower first bicuspids were extracted at that time and lower teeth retracted to create more of an overjet problem.  On January 27, 2006, she had bilateral mandibular ramus sagittal split osteotomies to advance the mandible.  She continued to have some facial swelling and so in June of 2006 the bilateral bone plates were removed.  She had an esthetic defect at the inferior border of the mandible so on 09/21/2007, bilateral silastic implants to the antegonial notch areas was performed.  However, the left one became infected so both implants were removed on October 4, 2007. 

It is unknown when her TMJ problems began.  She believes that she may have had some noises in her joints before her surgery.  She became more aware of the problems in the joints after the surgery.  She became aware of having some joint problems at about the age of 28 in 2002 where she had some pain and discomfort.  Apparently Brenda has had problems over the years with posturing the lower jaw forward.  A number of her doctors had talked to her about trying to get her to bite more on her back teeth.  At times she still catches herself posturing her lower jaw forward.

PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Bone contour in the mandible after her orthognathic surgery.
Bite concerns.
Pain and discomfort predominately in the masseter muscles.
She feels as though she does not have enough room in her mouth for her tongue.

In frontal view, there is fairly good balance in the middle and upper third face.  Upper lip length is about 18 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 4 mm.  There is a little bit of orbital dystopia with the right eye being about 2 mm higher than the left eye.  Maxillary and mandibular midlines are about 1 mm to the left and chin is probably 1 to 2 mm to the left.  In profile view, Brenda has some AP maxillary deficiency probably about 3 to 4 mm.  The chin appears somewhat retruded probably about 4 to 5 mm as well.  Chin-neckline is somewhat short although the chin-neck angle is well defined.  Intraorally, she is missing lower first bicuspids.  Spaces are all closed.  Teeth appear to be nicely aligned and level.  In the upper arch she is also missing the first bicuspids.  She has fairly good Class I cuspid molar relationship.  She has a transverse cant in the occlusion with the left side being about 3 mm higher than the right side.  Oropharyngeal area looks good with normal uvula, soft palate and faucial pillars.  Intranasally, the turbinates are moderate in size.  The septum is deviated significantly off towards the left side.  At the very anterior aspect of the nose the septum is off to the right side.  To palpation, she has no tenderness around the head and neck area.  On opening, however, she has audible crepitation in both her jaw joints.  Chewing bothers her muscles particular her masseters.  
BRENDA MORALES VAZQUEZ		12/07/2007				Page 2

Incisal opening is 40 mm and excursive movements are 8 mm in each direction.  She does have prominent notching in the gonial notch areas as result of the previous surgery.  She has some TADs placed in the lower right cuspid bicuspid area and in the upper left bicuspid cuspid area.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION:
Panorex shows the absence off all four third molars and all four first bicuspids.  The TAD on the mandibular right side is between the lateral incisor and cuspid.  On the left side it is between the second bicuspid and first molar.  Right sinus appears a little bit full.  Condyles appear arthritic with more bone loss on the left side than the right side.  Some prominence of the gonial notches.  Septum appears deviated with a septal spur present.  

Waters view shows significant deviation of the nasal septum.  Turbinates are moderate in size and the left airway appears somewhat blocked.  Transverse cant in the occlusion is obvious.  Maxillary sinuses look a little bit unusual particularly on the left side.  

Right TMJ tomogram shows significant arthritis present in the joint.  Very minimal joint space.  There appears to be loss of condylar volume.  Articular eminence is moderate in steepness. 

Left TMJ tomogram likewise shows a condyle with significant arthritic changes and loss of volume.  Condylar head and neck are shorter on the left side as compared to right side and probably is the reason for the transverse cant and facial asymmetry.  On opening, there is good translation of both condyles.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows both the maxilla and mandible are retruded.  There is prominence of the gonial notches.  Cant in the occlusal plane.  Oropharyngeal airway measures about 6 mm at the soft palate and about 8 mm at the base of the tongue. 

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation without reduction and severe degenerative changes. 
AP maxillary deficiency.
AP mandibular deficiency. 
Transverse cant in the occlusion with the left side being 3 mm vertically shorter compared to the right side. 
Missing all four first bicuspids and all four third molars.
Crossbite tendency on the left side in the molar area.
Periodic myofascial pain affecting predominately the masseter muscles.
Nasoseptal deviation. 
Hypertrophied turbinates.
 Nasal airway obstruction. 
 Depressions in the antegonial notch area secondary to previous surgery.
 Loss of transverse dimension of the angles of the mandible.
 Decreased oropharyngeal airway. 

BRENDA MORALES VAZQUEZ 			12/07/2007			Page 3

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
1.  Treatment option 1.
Surgery.
Bilateral mandibular reconstruction with Medpor implants in the angles and gonial notch areas.
      Treatment option 2 to address all the concerns and factors.
Presurgical orthodontics.
Continue to align and level arch.
Continue to align and level mandibular arch.
2.   CT scan of TMJs and jaws (TMJ Concepts protocol.)
3.   Surgery. 
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance and expand the maxilla.
Nasoseptoplasty.
Partial nasal turbinectomies. 
Consider augmentation of mandibular gonial notch areas bilaterally. 
4.   Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B297 
Age: 44-year-old female.
Date of Acquisition: 
Date of Interpretation: 
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with good mediolateral width. Lateral pole of the condyle is significantly inferiorly positioned compared to the medial pole of the condyle. Articular disc is medially displaced. There is decreased vertical joint space towards the lateral rim of the fossa. Cortical bone in the fossa on the condyle appears to be intact. There is moderate downward curvature at the lateral rim of the fossa probably about 2 to 3 mm.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that may be a little bit smaller than the opposite side. The lateral rim of the fossa appears downward about 2 to 3 mm. Cortical bone appears slightly more fuzzy in some of the views. The joint space appears a little bit decreased compared to the other side. It appears that the disc is likely anteriorly displaced.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology, but with some flattening on the anterior head and a little bit of sclerosis in that area. Bilaterally, the disc is anteriorly displaced, but at the medial extent of the joint the disc is in better position. There is some fluid in the superior and inferior joint spaces. On opening, there is good translation of the condyle and articular disc and the disc does reduce into position. The disc appears to have good morphology, although the posterior band appears somewhat thickened.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has a little bit more arthritic changes. There is sclerosis on the anterior head of the condyle and some anterior beaking present. Articular eminence is fairly steep. Disc is anteriorly positioned and sitting beneath the articular eminence. Medially, the disc is less distinguishable but much more obvious towards the lateral aspect of the joint indicative of perhaps lateral displacement. There is some fluid in the superior joint space and a little bit in the inferior joint space. On opening, there is good translation of the condyle and disc and the disc does reduce very early on. The disc in the wide open view appears to have fairly good morphology.

IMPRESSION:
Left TMJ mild arthritis and articular disc dislocation anteriorly and medially with early reduction.
Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly and possibly laterally displaced, but with reduction on opening.
Both discs are salvageable and probably amenable to repositioning with Mitek anchors.

B297 
B297 is 44 years old, referred by Dr. __
B297 has had previous viral meningitis. She had a herniated disc at L5-S1 and is currently being treated with epidural steroid injections. Previous surgeries include an abdominal hysterectomy 4 years ago. B297 is in good health.

MEDICATIONS
Mobic 15 mg daily for her back. Estratest 1 daily for hormone replacement. With the hysterectomy they did remove her ovaries. B297 gets some earaches and has tinnitus in the left ear, but she relates this to when she shot a deer with a gun close to a truck and reverberation of the noise caused some ear damage. She has some slight residual loss of hearing on the left side. She is not aware of having any problems with vertigo. The other joints that bother her include her neck, back, left shoulder, left thumb and left knee. She wore a collar for her neck for about 6 months in the past. She does have a herniated disc in her back that she is being treated for. She feels her left thumb problems are related to some stretched out ligaments and it may be due to occupation as she does carry a bag where she has to extend her thumb to hold it. Her left knee makes noises and she has had a previous arthroscopy on it at about the age of 22. She was able to ski for 10 years after that and apparently is doing fairly well except for the noises. B297 states that she cannot hyperextend her neck and has never been able to do that ever since she was a youngster. She is aware of clenching and bruxing at night and she does clench some during the day as well. She gets headaches now about once every 2 weeks that occur in the temporal and posterior head area at about a level of about 8 to 9. She used to get her headaches 2 to 3 times a week before she got her splint this past fall and began orthodontics. She rates her TMJ pain at 5, average daily pain at 2, jaw function at 5, diet at 4, disability at 3. B297 does have a twin sister who does not have any jaw or joint problems. B297's TMJ problems began probably back in her teenage years and have been present she says as long as she can remember. The etiology is unknown. About 10 years ago, she did wear a splint for awhile. She then was placed back into the splint by Dr. __ last year and she went until last year when she received her orthodontic appliances. B297 also has gone to a chiropractor for treatment and she feels this may have helped her some as well. She had the chiropractic treatment for about 6 months.

In frontal view, B297 has very good facial symmetry in the middle and upper third face. Upper lip length is about 24 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 2. Maxillary midline is fairly close to being on the facial midline. Upper dental midline is about 3 mm off to the right side. Lower dental midline and chin are fairly well aligned with each other. In profile view, B297 has very good balance between the upper lips, cheeks and nose. There is a bit of an acute nasolabial angle. Chin is retruded by about 15 mm. Chin-neckline is not well defined. Intraorally, B297 has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars present.

B297 
There is adequate attached gingiva along the lower anterior teeth; however, her lower central incisor roots are very prominent. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. She has almost a full Class II cuspid relationship on the right side and closer to a Class I cuspid relationship on the left side. She has a vertical overbite of probably less than half a millimeter. She has an overjet of about 2 to 3 mm. Intraorally, the uvula and soft palate appear relatively normal. Faucial pillars are mildly constricted. When sleeping, B297 believes she probably breathes through her mouth. Intranasally, B297 has some deviation of the nasal septum off towards the right side. Turbinates are sort of moderate in size with what appears to be decreased airway on the right side. The left side appears to have better airway. B297 states she has been told that she does snore some. She has some daytime somnolence. She is not aware of whether or not she has any sleep apnea problems. To palpation, B297 has some mild tenderness over the TMJs and styloid areas but no other pain. On opening, she had very slow opening clicks at about 5 mm on the left side and probably about 25 mm on the right side. On closing, she had a click on the right side about 10 mm and the left side about 5 mm. On opening, she did have some crepitus that occurred in the left joint with sort of a crackling sound that was actually audible. Incisal opening is about 50 mm. She opened about 35 mm without significant discomfort. Excursive movement to the right side about 10 mm and to the left side 12 mm. Slight transverse cant in the occlusal plane with left side being 1 to 2 mm lower than the right side. Intraorally, in centric relation the right molars are out of occlusion. Both uppers and lowers appear to be intruded.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all 4 third molars. Sinuses look relatively clear. Difficult to evaluate the condyles, but it looks like there may be some flattening on the anterior heads.

Waters view shows that the bony septum is deviated off towards the right side. Turbinates look as if they are moderate in size and it appears that there is airway around them. She has a mucous retention cyst in the left maxillary sinus. The rest of the sinuses look clear. She has large frontal sinuses with the left one being significantly larger than the right side.

On tomograms, unfortunately B297 did not have her teeth together and it shows that there is an increased joint space and flattening on the anterior head of the condyle. Articular eminence is fairly steep. Joint space is increased but again due to the fact that she was not in centric relation.

Left TMJ tomogram shows that she was close to being in centric relation on this side. Condyle has some flattening on the anterior head. Articular eminence is fairly steep. Condyle is posteriorly positioned in the fossa. On opening, there is good translation of both condyles.

Cephalometrically, she has a Class II skeletal and occlusal relationship. Occlusal plane looks fairly flat. Lower incisors are tipped out very significantly. Upper teeth appear to be in a little better angulation. Oropharyngeal airway appears fairly adequate with some significant narrowing down near the hyoid bone of probably 5 to 6 mm (normal 11 mm.) At the soft palate level, the airway measurement is about 7 mm and at the base of tongue about 8 mm. B297 

BASIC DIAGNOSES
Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with reduction.
AP mandibular deficiency and significant asymmetry.
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
Class II occlusion on the right side, Class I on the left. Anterior open bite and right posterior open bite.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway.
TMJ pain.
Headaches.
Myofascial pain.
Large mass, left maxillary sinus.
Decreased eruption of right maxillary and mandibular first and second molars. Transverse cant in the occlusion with the right side being 2 mm vertically shorter than the left side.
Yaw in mandible begin shifted 3 to 4 mm to the left.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch. Try to extrude maxillary first and second molars on the right side.
Continue to align and level mandibular arch. Try to extrude mandibular right first and second molars. May need to consider using interarch elastics to help in that effort as well as repositioning the brackets.

Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning with ligament repair of Mitek anchors. Multiple maxillary osteotomies to down graft, level it transversely and decrease the accentuated curve of occlusion.
Bilateral ramus osteotomies to advance it in a counterclockwise direction approximately 12 mm. Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy to advance the chin approximately 3 to 4 mm. Removal of cyst in the left maxillary sinus.
3. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B297  MRI Evaluation 
Age: 44-year-old female.
Date of Acquisition:
Date of Interpretation: 
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with good mediolateral width. Lateral pole of the condyle is significantly inferiorly positioned compared to the medial pole of the condyle. Articular disc is medially displaced. There is decreased vertical joint space towards the lateral rim of the fossa. Cortical bone in the fossa on the condyle appears to be intact. There is moderate downward curvature at the lateral rim of the fossa probably about 2 to 3 mm.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that may be a little bit smaller than the opposite side. The lateral rim of the fossa appears downward about 2 to 3 mm. Cortical bone appears slightly more fuzzy in some of the views. The joint space appears a little bit decreased compared to the other side. It appears that the disc is likely anteriorly displaced.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology, but with some flattening on the anterior head and a little bit of sclerosis in that area. Bilaterally, the disc is anteriorly displaced, but at the medial extent of the joint the disc is in better position. There is some fluid in the superior and inferior joint spaces. On opening, there is good translation of the condyle and articular disc and the disc does reduce into position. The disc appears to have good morphology, although the posterior band appears somewhat thickened.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has a little bit more arthritic changes. There is sclerosis on the anterior head of the condyle and some anterior beaking present. Articular eminence is fairly steep. Disc is anteriorly positioned and sitting beneath the articular eminence. Medially, the disc is less distinguishable but much more obvious towards the lateral aspect of the joint indicative of perhaps lateral displacement. There is some fluid in the superior joint space and a little bit in the inferior joint space. On opening, there is good translation of the condyle and disc and the disc does reduce very early on. The disc in the wide open view appears to have fairly good morphology.

IMPRESSION:
Left TMJ mild arthritis and articular disc dislocation anteriorly and medially with early reduction.
Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly and possibly laterally displaced, but with reduction on opening.
Both discs are salvageable and probably amenable to repositioning with Mitek anchors.

original_note:
SARAH SOULIER									01/05/2009

Sarah is 15 years old.  Referred by Dr. James Dyer, 617 Ball Street, Grapevine, TX 76051.  Sarah’s TMJ problems began when she was about 13 years old and she first noticed it while she was chewing gum.  She had previously had orthodontics done in the second and third grade and then orthodontics again in the sixth and seventh grade.  She would have been age 12 to 13 with the second round of orthodontics.  She was placed in a splint when she was about 13 years old because of the developing TMJ problems.  The only medication she currently takes is Aleve p.r.n.  

Sarah gets headaches about three times a week that she rates at a level of 9 that occur predominantly in the right frontal and temporal areas as well as on the top of the head.  She rates her TMJ pain at 7, average daily pain at 6, jaw function at 9, diet at 5, and disability at 7.  She gets earaches on her left side occasionally and vertigo occasionally, but no tinnitus.  The only other joints that bother her are her knees.  She does a lot of dance and drill team.  She previously did play sports such as volleyball and basketball in fifth through eighth grade.  She is aware of clenching and bruxing her teeth at night. 

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, she has fairly good symmetry in the middle and upper third face.  Upper lip length is about 20 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is 4 mm.  The maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline.  Nasal tip is off to the right side about 3 mm.  The mandibular dental midline is off towards the right side about 1 mm and the chin is probably about 2 to 3  mm off towards the right side.  In profile view, upper lip is retruded about 4 to 6 mm and chin is retruded about 20 mm.  Chin neckline is short and chin neck angle is not well defined.  She does have a head postured forward neck position.  Sarah may have occasional mild snoring.  She may have mild to moderate daytime somnolence.  She has no overt sleep apnea problems.  She has a mild sleep disorder and wakes up several times at time at night.  She is predominantly a mouth breather at night time.  Intraorally, Sarah has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars present.  The teeth are nicely aligned and level.  She has a 3 x 3 retainer.  She has very thin attached gingiva around the lower central incisor.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars present.  She has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship bilateral being about 3 mm Class II with about a 3-mm overjet.  She has an anterior open bite of about 2 to 3 mm.  She appears to occlude predominantly on her second molars on the right side and the left side perhaps on the second bicuspid, first and second molars.  Intranasally, the septum appears to be deviated a little bit off towards the left side at its base.  The turbinates appears moderately enlarged.  Columella is moderately wide and nostrils are moderately narrow.  She has a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right side being about 3 mm higher than the right side.  There is some AP asymmetry in the lower arch with the right cuspids in both upper and lower jaw being posteriorly positioned about 1 to 1.5 mm compared to the left side.  To palpation, she has some moderate pain over the TMJs and milder discomfort over the masseters and the right retromandibular area.  Maximal opening is 30 mm but only about 18 mm without discomfort.  Excursion movement to the right is 5 mm, the left 9 mm.     
RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the presence of all the teeth.  The four third molars are impacted.  There is significant deviation of the nasal septum off towards the left side, particularly back in the bony aspect of the septum.  Turbinates appear moderate in size.  Sinuses appear relatively clear.  She appears to have small condyles bilaterally with the right side being smaller than the left side.  
Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears to have lost some vertical volume.  The condyle appears moderately wide.  There is decreased superior joint space.  Lateral rim of the fossa is relatively shallow.  
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears arthritic and appears to have lost vertical volume.  Joint space is somewhat constricted. 
Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle is quite small in mediolateral width.  There appears to be a little bit of anterior beaking present.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  There is decreased posterior and superoposterior joint space.  There is some anterior beaking present.  
Left TMJ tomogram sagittal view shows a condyle that appears quite small anteroposteriorly and appears to have lost vertical volume.  Articular eminence is relatively steep.  Joint space may be slightly decreased posteriorly.  There is some significant flattening of the head of the condyle, particularly towards the lateral aspect of the joint.  
Coronal x-ray shows the sinuses are clear.  She has huge turbinates, particularly on the right side.  The nasal septum is deviated significantly off to the left side and appears to obliterate the major portion of the airway, particularly at the inferior aspect of the nasal cavity.  In the axial views, the turbinates appear quite large.
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Sarah has a posterior vertical maxillary deficiency and AP mandibular deficiency.  She has a high mandibular plane angle and Class II skeletal and occlusal relationship and anterior open bite.  Oropharyngeal airway has somewhat constricted with an AP dimension of only about 5 mm at the base of the soft palate and at the base of the tongue.  

Basic diagnoses for Sarah would be as follows:    

Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation without reduction compatible with adolescent internal condylar resorption (AICR). 
AP mandibular hypoplasia. 
AP maxillary hypoplasia.
Posterior vertical maxillary hypoplasia. 
Class II end-on occlusion. 
Anterior open bite. 
High occlusal plane angle. 
Decreased oropharyngeal airway of 5 mm (normal 11 mm).
Transverse cant in the maxillary arch with the right side being 3 mm higher than the left side.
TMJ pain.
Headaches.
Myofascial pain. 
Impacted third molars x4.
Hypertrophied turbinates. 
Deviated nasal septum.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Presurgical orthodontics.  
Align and level maxillary arch.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies.  Place brackets on the second molars, but do not extend the arch wire to the second molars at this time.  Can use bonded brackets on all the teeth.
Align and level mandibular arch.  Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing about 1 to 2 mm of tooth structure and retract.
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor. 
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance it forward and down graft it posteriorly. 
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance it forward approximately 20 mm.  
Surgical removal of impacted third molars x4.
Partial turbinectomies. 
Nasal septoplasty.
3.   Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
THERESA RUTLEDGE								10-22-02

Theresa is 33 years old and referred by Dr. Lee Kamelchuk, 1107 Gleneagles Drive, Huntsville, AL  35801. She has seen Dr. Mike Koslin in Birmingham.

Patient History

Theresa has had previous surgeries for sinus problems, endometriosis, gallbladder, and she also had an ERCP, which is an evaluation of the bile ducts through endoscopic type evaluation. She apparently had some significant water retention problems from one of her surgeries including pulmonary edema and gaining about 20 pounds in fluid retention. They thought she was going into ARDS but pulled out of it. Theresa was involved in a motor vehicle accident in 1989 and sustained multiple internal injuries, and this has required several operative procedures. 

Theresa feels her problems with her jaws may have begun around 1989 when she was involved in an MVA and sustained her internal injuries. She also hit her jaw in that accident, although she had no acknowledged fractures. She has developed significant headache problems, and she has had sinus surgery x 2 trying to correct those problems. She has a significant history of nocturnal clenching and bruxism. She gets headaches daily and frequently wakes up with headaches. On the average, her headaches run at about a level of 7 and occur in the frontal, temporal, and posterior head area. They, however, can escalate to a level of 10. 

Theresa rates her TMJ pain and discomfort about a level of 5 particularly on the job, overall pain at 8, jaw function at 2, diet and disability at 4.

Patient Concerns

Primary concerns that Theresa has include the following:

Headaches.
Neck and shoulder pain.
TMJ pain.
Teeth don’t fit together very well.
Difficulties eating and chewing.

Current Medications

Medications that Theresa currently takes include the following:

Ultrace MT-18.
Clarinex.

Clinical Evaluation

Frontal view shows that Theresa has some relatively good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 18 mm. Upper tooth to lip relationship is 3 mm. Maxillary dental midline is fairly close to being on the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline is shifted about 1 to 2 mm to the left. Chin is pretty much on the facial midline. 

In profile view, Theresa has a little bit of deficiency in the maxillary projection. Mandible is maybe just slightly retruded. Chin is relatively prominent compared to the AP position of the lower lip. Chin-neckline is slightly short. Chin-neck angle is not well-defined. 

Intraorally, she has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. Mandibular left first bicuspid is partially blocked out of the arch, and the second bicuspid is rotated probably about 80 degrees. Some mild crowding, lower anterior arch. She has adequate attached gingiva. She has significant abrasions of the lower anterior teeth. She has an accented curve of occlusion in the lower arch. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. She has a Class I cuspid relationship bilaterally but with a division II malocclusion anteriorly. She has an anterior deep-bite of probably a good 8 mm. She has a reverse curve of occlusion in the upper arch. She has a crossbite in the left mandibular first bicuspid area. Transversely, maxillary arch is fairly level, but on the left side, the bicuspids are down just a little bit, probably 1 to 1.5 mm. She has some noises in her joints bilaterally to palpation, but by placing the fingers in the ears, she has definite popping in both joints with an opening pop at probably around 15 mm and a closing pop at about 5 mm. Maximal incisal opening is 42 mm counting the overbite. Excursive movements are about 8 mm in each direction.

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows the sinuses are clear and nasal airway actually looks quite good. She is missing all four third molars. Accentuated curve of occlusion is noted in the lower arch. Condyles look fairly good with a little flattening posteriorly on the anterior head. Articular eminences are very steep, being a little bit greater steepness on the right side as compared to the left side. No other abnormalities noted.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is very flattened in the posterior head area. Superior joint space actually appears to be increased, although posterior joint space appears to be diminished some. Articular eminence is quite steep with probably about 85% inclination.

Right TMJ tomogram shows some flattening on the anterior head of the condyle. Superior anterior joint space appears slightly increased, although the posterior joint space may be slightly decreased. Also, there appears to be increased anterior joint space between the bottom part of the condylar head and the articular eminence. Articular eminence is quite steep on this side being probably 80% to 85% of inclination.

On opening, there is good translation of both TMJs. 

Waters view shows that the sinuses are clear. Nasal airway looks fairly good. Jaws are fairly symmetric, although the lower dental midline is off to the left. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows probably a skeletal Class I kind of position with a Class I occlusion but division II malocclusion anteriorly. She has a low occlusal plane angle facial morphology. Chin is prominent relative to the position of the lower incisors. Oropharyngeal airway looks to be within normal range. Oropharynx appears clear. Some mild crenations on the tongue. 

Intranasally, turbinates are of moderate size with the left one being larger than the right side. However, the nasal airway appears fairly good. Slightly wide columella base but probably not causing a significant problem. 

Basic Diagnoses

Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocations in a vertical orientation and presence of very steep articular eminences.
Relative mandibular hyperplasia.
Slight AP maxillary deficiency.
Class I division II occlusion.
Vertical inclination of maxillary incisors.
Significant vertical inclination of mandibular incisors.
Anterior deep-bite of approximately 7 to 8 mm. 
Crowding, lower anterior arch, particularly in the left bicuspid area.
Mandibular dental midline shifted 1 to 2 mm to the left of the facial and chin midline.
Headaches.
TMJ pain.
Earaches.
Vertigo.

Recommended Treatment
Presurgical orthodontics
Align and level maxillary arch. Correct reverse curve of occlusion. Tip upper incisors forward approximately 2 mm. Keep upper lateral incisor roots tipped mesially and cuspid roots tipped distally to allow for interdental osteotomies if they are necessary.
Align and level mandibular arch. Tip lower incisors forward at least 2 to 3 mm. Try to level the accentuated curve of occlusion in the lower arch.
Consider using a bite plate to open bite in order to place appliances on the lower arch and to allow orthodontic access to align and level the arches. Upper splint may work the best to allow better access to the lower arch alignment and leveling. Could leave the maxillary incisors out of the splint to enable forward and upward repositioning of those teeth.

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies with bone screw stabilization.
Consider possible maxillary osteotomies if necessary to achieve the best functional and aesthetic outcome.

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

I gave Theresa a prescription for Klonopin 1 mg tablets, disp. 30 tablets, 1 tablet q.h.s., refill x 4.

original_note:
NINA MALONE									05/12/2009

Nina is 47 years old.  Referred by Dr. Tim Mickiewitz, 3401 Folsom Blvd., Suite A, Sacramento, CA 95816. 

Nina has had approximately 29 previous surgeries.  Her TMJ problems began when she was about 11 years old in 1972 when she was hit in the right temple and jaw area with a baseball from a foul ball as she was sitting on the sidelines.  Her first jaw surgery was on the left joint in about 1981.  Subsequently, and this does not include all of her surgeries, she had an additional left TMJ surgery in 1982 with a Silastic implant.  In 05/1983, she had bilateral condylectomies, 8/1984 she had Vitek-Kent total joint prostheses.  In 11/1986 the Vitek implants were removed and rib grafts were placed with debridement and a coronoidectomy.  In 08/1990, she has bilateral gap arthroplasties with implants placed to treat ankylosis.  In 08/1996 through 10/1996, the grafts were removed, the Vitek implants removed, and the joint reconstructed with custom-made Christensen total joint prostheses.  This does not include all of the surgeries that she had on her joints, but the major ones at least.  In addition, in 1990, she had a right ulnar nerve transposition, in 2002 gastric bypass, in 2002 cholecystectomy, and in 2008 right knee arthroplasty. 

Nina takes the following medications:

Lyrica 100 mg b.i.d. 
Topamax 25 mg daily.
Tylenol p.r.n. 
Iron injections monthly.
B12 injections monthly.

Nina has additional joint problems including shoulders, right knee, lower back, and fingers.  She was involved in an MVA in January 2005 where she sustained injury to her back and the knees and shoulders were injured at that time as well as probably additional trauma to the jaw joints. Primary concerns that Nina has at this time include the following:

Limited jaw function. 
Significant pain.
Difficulty getting dental treatment because of the significantly decreased jaw opening. 
She is concerned as her functionability in the future and with the decreasing jaw function she is experiencing probably this is a significant concern. 
Neck pain. 
Stuffy ears. 
Headaches that are worsening. 

Nina rates her headaches at a level of 6 in the temporal areas and her headaches are constant.  She rates her TMJ pain at 6, average daily pain at 6, jaw function at 7, diet at 5, and disability at 5.  The right frontal branch of the facial nerve has been severely injured and does not function well.  She has total numbness in the lower left lip and chin area.  She gets earaches on the right side frequently, tinnitus occasional, and vertigo of moderate occurrence.  Other joints that bother her include her shoulders, right knee, lower back, and fingers.  
CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Nina has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 18 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is 1 mm on the left, 2 mm on the right.  The maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline.  The mandibular dental may be just slightly to the left and chin is probably about 3 mm to the left.  Nina has a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the left side being elevated 2 mm higher than the right side.  In profile, the maxilla may be retruded 2-3 mm and the chin appears to be retruded by probably about 8 mm.  Nina does have a Silastic chin implant that was placed by a plastic surgeon because her chin was significantly receded.  The chin implant was done within the last 2 to 3 years.  Nina has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars present.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth except for the right first molar.  She is very close to being a Class I cuspid bilaterally.  She has a slight anterior open bite of about 1 mm.  She did have her wisdom teeth removed when she was about 15.  Intraorally, the uvula is moderately elongated, but the soft palate looked normal and I could not really evaluate the fascial pillars very well because of the limited opening.  I cannot evaluate the presence or absence of the tonsils.  Intranasally, the turbinates appear to be relatively normal in size and are actually the right one looks better than it did on x-ray.  The nasal septal spur I believe is at the base of the septum and does not appear to interfere too significantly with the airway as it appears that would on the x-rays.  Nina does identify increased difficulties breathing through the nose on the right side and sometimes it can be a fairly significant obstruction.  The right turbinate may be mild to moderately enlarged.  There is definitely a septal spur that appears to be at the base of the septum.  To palpation, Nina has high moderate to low severe pain over the masseters, medial pterygoids, TMJs, retromandibular area, and shoulders.  She has moderate pain over the temporalis, SCMs, and posterior neck areas.  Maximal incisal opening is 20 mm.  She could open to about 15 mm without significant pain.  She has no excursion movements.  Nina states that she has frequent hoarseness and sore throats.  She has difficulties swallowing at times as well which could go along with the significantly decreased oropharyngeal airway.  

Basic diagnoses for Nina is as follows:    

Bilateral TMJ failed Christensen total joint prostheses. 
Heterotopic bone formation around bilateral TMJ failed Christensen prostheses.
Bilateral TMJ foreign body giant cell reaction secondary to previous Proplast Teflon devices, Silastic implants, and failed Christensen total joint prostheses. 
Maxillary AP and posterior vertical hypoplasia.
Mandibular status post hypoplasia.
Transverse cant in the maxilla and mandible in the occlusal plane with the left side being 2-3 mm higher than the right side. 
Slight anterior open bite.
Missing maxillary right first molar and all 4 third molars. 
Decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of approximately 4 mm at the soft palate level and 6 mm at the base of the tongue. 
Sleep apnea symptoms including daytime somnolence, recurrent sore throats, recurrent hoarseness, and difficulties swallowing. 
Hypertrophied right nasal inferior turbinate. 
Large septal spur into the right side of the nose causing airway obstruction. 
TMJ pain. 
Headaches. 
Myofascial pain. 

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Presurgical orthodontics.  The orthodontist can use bonded brackets on all the teeth.  The molars do not need to be banded. 
Align and level maxillary arch.  Tip lateral incisors roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. 
Align and level mandibular arch.  
CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol).
Surgery. 
Bilateral removal of failed Christensen total joint prostheses.
Extensive debridement of heterotopic bone and foreign body giant cell reactive tissue. 
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. 
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen).
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance it forward and level the transverse occlusal cant. 
Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy to augment the chin. 
Removal of previously placed Silastic chin implant. 
Nasal septoplasty. 
Right inferior turbinectomy.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
Consider dental implant and crown to replace the missing maxillary right first molar.

original_note:
GARY LESSER										04-25-03

Gary is 35 years old. Dr. Mark Piper, 111 Second Ave. NE, Ste. 1006, St. Petersburg, FL  33701, referred Gary to us.

Patient History

Gary originally had trauma at the age of 4 when he hit his chin on a coffee table. He had no problems at that time. It wasn’t until he was involved in an MVA in 1990. Gary is not aware of hitting his jaw at that time, but he did begin to develop jaw joint problems about three weeks afterwards. His jaws started to click and pop at that time. Gary has had previously bilateral arthroscopies in 1994. He then had orthognathic surgery performed in 1995. He had bilateral TMJ arthroscopies performed again in 1996. The dates may be a little bit different. One arthroscopy was done in approximately January of 1994. The orthognathic surgery involving maxillary osteotomies and a genioplasty was performed probably in December of 1994, and then the second arthroscopy to both TMJs was performed in late 1995. He does have some pain in his right shoulder joint but has never been scoped or treated. Dr. Andy Slavin did the arthroscopies and performed the orthognathic surgery in conjunction with Dr. Steve Reimer. 

The only other joint that bothers him is his right shoulder. He has some significant allergy problems, but they are not sure exactly what the allergies are to. He does get frequent sinus infections and just recently came off of some antibiotics. He says by the end of the day, the effect is so significant that his speech is affected, and he begins to slur. He has developed snoring problems. He has chronic fatigue as well. He is aware of having nocturnal clenching and bruxism. Gary gets his headaches predominantly in the temporal and posterior head area, and they occur daily. He registers them at about a level of 5, but they can escalate to 8. He has consistent and continuous pain in the TMJ areas at a level of 6 on the left side and 4 on the right side. On an average day, his pain levels are at about a 7. He rates his jaw function at 4, diet at 9, and disability at 5. 

Current Medications

The only medication that Gary really takes at this time is some Bufferin.

Patient Concerns

TMJ pain
Headaches 
Pain down through the neck and shoulders
Teeth don’t fit together very well
Snoring
Sleep disorder
Apneic episodes at night when sleeping
Earaches, being greater on the left side than the right side
Lower jaw is becoming more retruded
Difficulties breathing through the nose
Predominantly a mouth breather

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Gary has fairly good facial symmetry in the middle and upper third face. Could be just some slight asymmetry in the mandible. Upper lip length is about 24 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 2.5 mm. Maxillary dental midline is pretty much on the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline may be about a millimeter to the right. Chin is probably a couple of millimeters to the right. Placing Gary in a centric relation causes pain in the jaw joints with it being greater on the left side. Upper lip supralabrale is probably 4 to 5 mm posterior to subnasale. Maxilla is AP retruded probably at least a good 6 mm. Chin is retruded approximately 18 to 20 mm. Chin-neckline is extremely short, and chin-neck angle is essentially absent.
Intraorally, Gary has all of his lower teeth back through the second molars. Has good attached gingiva. Has some calculus around the necks of the teeth and a little bit of inflammation around the gums. Anteroposteriorly, the lower arch looks fairly symmetric. In the upper arch, he also has all of his teeth back through the second molars present. He occludes predominantly on his second molars on the right side and maybe first and second molars on the left side with the other teeth all being pretty much out of occlusion. Anteriorly, he has 0 open bite. He has a Class II end-on occlusion at about 3 mm. There is a slight cant in the transverse occlusal plane with the left side being about 1.5 to 2 mm lower than the right side. Oropharynx appears relatively clear. Uvula is hyperplastic and somewhat enlarged. Faucial pillars appear to be somewhat posteriorly positioned. 

Intranasally, septum appears pretty straight, but he has fairly large turbinates that appear to take up a good part of the potential functional nasal airway.

Maximal incisal opening is about 35 mm, and he can get to about 30 mm without significant pain. Excursive movements to the right were about 8 mm and to the left pretty close to 8 mm also, but it was painful to do that with a greater amount of pain on the left side as compared to the right side. To palpation, he has extremely loud audible crepitus in both TMJs, and interestingly, the crepitation was significantly louder on closing as compared to opening. To palpation, he has some mild tenderness over the temporarlis muscles, masseters, TMJs, and styloid areas. 

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows the presence of four bone plates in the maxilla from the previous surgery performed there. Third molars are absent. He has some significant foreshortening of the root on the mandibular right first bicuspid. Sinuses look relatively clear. Turbinates may be a little bit large, but they are partly out of the picture. Condyles show severe condylar resorption and arthritic changes. There are bone screws in the chin area where the previous genioplasty was performed. Gary has root canals on the right maxillary and mandibular second molars. Four bone plates in the maxilla are all H-shaped plates. 

Waters view shows that the sinuses look fairly clear. Perhaps could be a little something in the right maxillary sinus. Otherwise, things look fairly clear. Right turbinate looks larger than the left turbinate. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows that the articular eminence is quite shallow as though it has previously been removed. The condyle is severely arthritic and shows evidence of significant vertical loss of volume. Virtually no joint space between the condyle and what is left of the articular eminence. Increased posterior joint space with the mandible postured forward approximately 4 to 5 mm from a normal position. 

Left TMJ tomogram likewise shows a condyle that is postured forward probably about 4 mm from a more normal position. The articular eminence is somewhat flattened. Virtually no joint space between the condyle and the articular eminence. The condyle also has lost significant volume, particularly in the vertical direction. 

Open views show that the condyles do translate some with a little bit greater translation on the left as compared to the right side. Forward posturing shows the significant loss of condylar volume.

Cephalometrically, evaluation shows that Gary has AP maxillary deficiency, AP mandibular deficiency, and high occlusal plane angle. Oropharyngeal airway actually looks better than anticipated with an airway dimension between the soft palate and posterior pharyngeal wall of about 8 mm and at the tongue level is almost 10 mm. 

Basic Diagnoses

Bilateral TMJ severe reactive arthritis with almost complete loss of the condylar heads
AP mandibular deficiency
AP maxillary deficiency
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency
Transverse maxillary deficiency
High occlusal plane angle
Class II end-on occlusion
Occluding primarily on the second molars with the rest of the teeth out of occlusion
Anterior open bite
Hypertrophied turbinates
Partial nasal airway obstruction
TMJ pain
Headaches 
Myofascial pain
Earaches 

Recommended Treatment

CT scan of TMJs and jaws (TMJ Concepts protocol)

Presurgical orthodontics 
Align and level maxillary arch. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies
Align and level mandibular arch. Strip lower anterior teeth, removing about a millimeter of tooth structure to compensate for the slight tooth-size discrepancy of 1.5 mm

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ condylectomy, debridement, reconstruction, and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses
Bilateral coronoidectomies
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla, decrease the occlusal plane angle, and expand
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvested from abdomen)
Bilateral inferior turbinectomies

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

original_note:
KYLE PARSONS									05-02-03

Kyle is 13 years old and referred by Dr. Hilton Goldreich, Legacy Drive, Plano, TX.

Patient History

Kyle had a hernia repair at the age of 2 years. Kyle has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, headaches, TMJ pain, etc. He states that occasionally in the morning when he starts to open up he will have a pop in his joints. 

Patient Concerns

Kyle’s primary concerns are that his teeth and jaws don’t fit together well.

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Kyle has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 20 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship would be probably about 3 mm or so. If the teeth were straightened up, it could be as much as 4 mm of tooth-to-lip relationship. Maxillary dental midline is shifted just about a millimeter to the left as is the mandibular dental midline and chin midline. Tip of the nose may be deviated just a little bit off to the right side 1 to 2 mm. 

In profile view, he has good balance between the upper lips, cheeks, and nose. Chin appears to be deficient probably by about 8 mm or so. Chin-neckline is short, although chin-neck angle is fairly well-defined.

Intraorally, Kyle has his teeth erupted from the first molars around. Second molar on the left side is starting to erupt through but not much on the right side. Some significant crowding in the lower anterior arch. Attached gingival is quite thin around the lower anterior teeth. Has a high attached labial frenulum. In the upper arch, he has some significant crowding; in fact, the right second bicuspid is blocked lingually. Has a Class I cuspid relationship on the right side but Class II end-on by about 5 mm or so on the left side. Has an anterior deep-bite of approximately 8 mm. Has a significant transverse cant in the occlusion with the left cuspid being about 3 mm higher than the right cuspid. Lower arch asymmetry with the left cuspid being about 2 mm posterior to the right cuspid. Right maxillary cuspid may be a couple of millimeters posterior to the left cuspid. Oropharynx appears clear. Tonsils are present but just of moderate size.

Intranasally, the septum is deviated a little bit toward the left side, but turbinates appear relatively small, and he appears that he has a good functional airway. 

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows that the sinuses appear relatively clear. Turbinates are moderate in size. Has a mesiodens between his two central incisors. Maxillary right second bicuspid is blocked out of the arch. Has developing maxillary third molars with really no room for them to erupt in. Lower arch shows that he has all of his teeth down there, and the second molars are developing and erupting in. Third molars are developing, and there will be no room for them to erupt in. It appears there is some crowding in the lower anterior arch. Right condyle looks a little bit flat on top, and the left one appears a little flat at the posterior aspect.

Left TMJ tomogram is a little hard to read, but articular eminence is moderately steep. Condyle appears a little bit flattened at the posterior aspect, but it appears there may be adequate joint space around the condyle. 

Right TMJ tomogram again is not a really good image, and it appears that it may not have gotten deep enough in the joint. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Difficult to evaluate the joint space from this radiograph. May need to repeat those with some deeper cuts. Translation shows fairly good movement of the condyles on both sides. 

Waters view shows that the sinuses are clear. Nasal septum looks relatively straight. Turbinates appear a little bit enlarged particularly on the right side. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Kyle has a skeletal Class II jaw relationship. Has a significant anterior deep-bite of about 8 mm. Has a Class II division II malocclusion. Has a mesiodens in the anterior part of the maxilla. Oropharyngeal airway is a little bit constricted but not too much. Airway measures about 8 mm at the base of the tongue and soft palate area. Does have a large adenoid mass in the nasopharynx that extends probably almost 15 mm beyond the posterior wall of the fossa and comes within 5 mm of the posterior aspect of the turbinates. He has a tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of about 3.5 mm in the anterior six teeth and probably a good 0.5 mm of tooth-size discrepancy with the lower bicuspids. 

Re-shoots of the tomograms show that there is good joint space present in both joints.

Basic Diagnoses

AP mandibular deficiency
Class II, division II malocclusion
Class I cuspid, Class II molar on the right side and Class II cuspid, Class II molar on the left side
Significant crowding, upper arch, with the right second bicuspid blocked out of the arch in a palatal direction
Moderate crowding, lower anterior arch
Accentuated curve of occlusion, lower arch
Anterior deep-bite of about 8 to 9 mm
Mesiodens between the roots of the incisors, but the predominant part of that tooth is on the palatal aspect
Impacted maxillary and mandibular third molars
Second molars not yet erupted completely in the lower arch
Lower arch asymmetry with the left cuspid being about 2 mm posterior to the right cuspid
Upper arch AP asymmetry with the right cuspid being about 2 mm posterior  to the left cuspid
Cant in the transverse occlusal plane with the right cuspid being about 3 mm lower than the left cuspid, but the first molars may be fairly level
Decreased angulation of the maxillary incisors
AP microgenia
Tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of 3.5 mm in the anterior teeth and at least 0.5-mm tooth-size discrepancy in the lower bicuspid area compared to the upper bicuspids

Recommended Treatment

Surgical removal of mesiodens from maxillary anterior palatal aspect

Presurgical orthodontics 
Align and level maxillary arch. Increase angulation of maxillary incisors. Try to distally position the right first molar 3 or 4 mm if possible to help make room for the right second bicuspid
Align and level lower arch. Strip lower anterior teeth removing up to 2.5 to 3 mm of tooth structure. Can also strip lower bicuspids removing about 0.5 mm of tooth structure from each tooth. This should prevent overangulation of the lower incisors and provide room for eliminating the crowding

Surgery
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible
Genioplasty to augment the chin
Surgical removal of four impacted third molars

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion 

If we can get Kyle setup orthodontically as designed, we may be able to avoid performing maxillary osteotomies and have to work with only the advancement of the mandible. This would make his surgery much easier for him as well as it would allow us to correct his problems perhaps next summer (summer of 2004) as compared to waiting four or five years if he requires maxillary surgery also.

original_note:
B247
B247 is 13 years old, referred by Dr. 
B247 has some asthma problems and takes Singulair, Flonase and Clarinex to treat the condition although it is primarily seasonal. She has had tonsils and adenoids removed 10 years ago. Otherwise, B247 is in good health. B247's jaw problems are of unknown occurrence and etiology. She says she thinks for at least a couple of years that she has had problems. She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo or hearing loss. No other joints bother her. She is aware of clenching and bruxism at nighttime but not during the day. She began her orthodontic treatment 2 years ago with Dr. . She has no problems with headaches, TMJ pain or pain around the head and neck area. She rates her jaw function at 4, diet at 3, disability at 1.

In frontal view, B247 has fairly good left to right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 18 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is 6 mm. Maxillary midline is about 1-1/2 to 2 mm off to the left side. Mandibular dental midline is fairly close to being on the facial midline as is the chin. In profile view, she has some AP maxillary deficiency. She has a C-shape to the upper lip. Maxilla is AP retruded in the point A area by about 2 to 3 mm. The chin is approximately 20 mg AP deficient. She has some lip incompetency of probably about 8 to 10 mm. Chin-neckline is short and chin-neck angle is well defined. Submental area is quite full. She has a head-postured forward neck position. She has a full Class II cuspid relationship fairly close to it. A little bit more Class II on the right side than the left side. She has an overjet of probably about 5 to 6 mm. She has vertical overbite about 3 to 4 mm. She is wearing Class II elastics. She has a transverse cant to the occlusion with the right side being 1 to 2 mm lower than the left side. There is some upper arch asymmetry with the left cuspid being 1 to 2 mm posterior to the right cuspid. To palpation, B247 has no tenderness around the head and neck area. Incisal opening is about 58 mm. Excursion movement is about 10 mm in each direction. Intranasally, right turbinate is a little bit larger than the left turbinate. The septum may be deviated a little bit off towards the right side but not too significant. On opening, she has some definite popping in her jaw joints, however, it is difficult to determine if it is due to a disc displacement or whether it is due to excessive condylar translation with the condyle coming out well beyond the articular eminence.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
:
Panorex shows the sinuses are difficult to evaluate. She has four developing but interrupted third molars. She has calcification a little bit below the apex of the mandibular right second bicuspid just slightly posterior to the mental foramen.

Waters view shows that the left sinus may have some thickening of the sinus membrane but the right maxillary sinus and the rest of the sinuses appear relatively clear.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears a little bit posteriorly positioned in the fossa. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Condyle might be a little bit flattened on the superior head but difficult to tell.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has some slight bony changes present. Articular eminence is fairly steep. Joint space may be slightly decreased posteriorly in one view but it is fairly well balanced. On opening, there is hypertranslation of condyles completely out of the fossa.

B247
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that incisors are a little bit over-angulated. Lateral incisors appear to be in better angulation with the lower jaw. Occlusal plain is increased in angulation. Oropharyngeal airway dimension is about 5 to 6 mm at the soft palate level and 8 mm at the base of the tongue. Occlusal plane appears to be increased in angulation. She has a C-shaped curve to the upper lip.

BASIS DIAGNOSES: Rule out bilateral TMJ articular disc dislocation. Anterior vertical maxillary excess.
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency. Transverse maxillary deficiency.
AP mandibular deficiency.
AP macrogenia.
Anterior mandibular vertical excess.
Class II occlusion.
Over-angulation of maxillary incisors and lower incisors.
Tooth size discrepancy of 's difference of approximately 2 mm. Decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Hypertrophied turbinates. Nasal airway obstruction.
Impacted third molars times 4.
Nocturnal clenching and bruxism.
Snoring.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Extract maxillary first bicuspids.
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch, retract the upper incisors approximately 4 mm.
Align and level mandibular arch. Slenderize the lower anteriors removing about 2 mm of tooth structures and retract. MRI of left and right TMJs.
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors if indicated based on further clinical and MRI evaluations. Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the anterior aspect of the maxilla superiorly, downgraft the posterior aspect, and advance slightly.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance to the mandible in a counterclockwise direction. Vertical reduction AP augmentation genioplasty. Partial reduction turbinectomies. Surgical removal of four impacted third molars times four. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion and retain.

B247 Evaluation
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has some arthritic changes associated with it. Vertical joint space appears adequate. Fairly minimal downward curvature at the lateral rim of the fossa. Discs may be medially and anteriorly displaced. Cortical bone appears to be intact. There appears to be decreased joint space and there appears to be medially displaced discal tissue.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has a little bit better shape than the opposite side. There appears to be medially displaced disc and some anterior displacement. Cortical bone appears to be intact.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has a little flattening on the anterior head and a little sclerosis on the posterior head. Articular eminence is moderately steep. The condyle appears postured forward in the fossa as one might expect with either forward posturing or splint therapy. The disc is anteriorly displaced. Out laterally, the disc is not seen indicative of medial displacement. There is not any significant fluid within the joint space. On opening, there is fairly good translation. On opening it appears that the disc does reduce and the disc translates fairly well.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows out laterally that the disc is not readily seen. There is some arthritis with flattening of the anterior head and a little bit of anterior beaking but not too significant. Articular eminence is moderately steep. The disc appears to be vertically oriented within the fossa and anteriorly displaced. There does not appear to be any significant inflammation present. Out laterally, the disc is not seen well indicative of medial displacement. On opening the disc appears to reduce and there is good translation of the condyle and disc.

IMPRESSION: Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteromedially displaced with reduction.

original_note:
ALISON RUSSO								11/14/2011

Alison is 37 years old, birth date 06/27/1974.  She was referred by Dr. Matthew Bruner, 17130 Avondale Way NE, Suite 109, Redmond, WA 98052, phone #425-883-3353, fax 425-885-3338.

Alison believes that her TMJ problems began as young as the age of 8 when she developed headaches.  It would hurt for her to sing.  She was in gymnastics and feels that she may have injured her jaw at that time with a whiplash injury coming off the bars.  She began chiropractic treatment for her head and neck area beginning at the age of 14.  She had orthodontic treatment from the age of 14 to 16 that included extraction of probably first bicuspids in all 4 quadrants.  She also had her wisdom teeth removed.  She began splint therapy as far back as 1992 through 1995 when she was in college where she had a night guard.  She started more formal splint therapy in 2003 and has had 3 splints since that time.  With the first splint, it apparently just included the anterior teeth and her molars erupted to a point that without the splint she would occlude only on 3 teeth.  The first splint was made by her dentist.  She then went to see Dr. Edmund Truelove at the University of Washington.  In 2009, she saw Dr. Matthew Bruner who reinitiated the splint therapy.  She wears a splint now only at night.  

She has been under chiropractic treatment since the age of 14 through all of these years and has also tried acupuncture.  She currently is receiving craniosacral therapy every 3 weeks.  In addition, she takes multiple supplements to help reduce inflammation.  There are approximately 15 supplements that she takes to aid her condition.  These are recorded on the back side of the questionnaire.   Besides those 15 supplements, she does currently take progesterone cream that she is weaning off of.  Alison gets headaches about 3 times per month at about a level of 7 in the frontal, temporal and posterior head area.  She rates her daily TMJ pain at 3, average daily pain at 2, jaw function at 4, diet at 5, and disability at 3.  She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, nor hearing loss but gets occasional vertigo.  Other joints that bother her include her fingers, hips (right greater than left), right ankle, and neck.  She is aware of clenching and bruxing at nighttime, but can control it during the day.  Her rheumatologist has diagnosed her with psoriatic arthritis and in addition she has been diagnosed with premenstrual disorder and low blood pressure.  Although Alison has no overt signs or symptoms of psoriasis, her rheumatologist has given her that diagnosis based on the CT findings of the TMJs and the history of several members of the family having psoriasis.  

Primary concerns that Alison has include the following:

She does not want the cartilage and bone around her jaw joints to degenerate any further and she would like to maintain what she has left.
TMJ pain.
Headaches.
Difficulties eating and chewing.
In frontal view, Alison has good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 21 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 2.5 mm.  The mandibular dental midline is close to being on the facial midline as is the chin.  The maxillary midline is just about 1 mm to the left.  In profile view, she has just slight AP maxillary deficiency of probably 2 to 3 mm.  The chin is retruded about 10 mm.  Chin neckline is short, although the chin neck angle is reasonably well defined.  Intraorally, she is missing probably the lower first bicuspids bilaterally, but she has the rest of her teeth back through the second molars.  She has some very minimal crowding in the lower anterior arch.  She has adequate attached gingiva.  In the upper arch, she is missing probably the first bicuspids as well and has the rest of her teeth back through the second molars.  She has palatal tori.  She has a Class I end-on cuspid relationship on the right side by probably 3 mm and on the left side she is probably just about 2 mm Class II.  She has an over jet of about 2 mm.  She has an anterior vertical over bite of 1.5 mm.  She has just a slight transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the left side being about 1 mm higher than the right side.  Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively normal.  To palpation, she has no tenderness around the head and neck area.  On jaw function, she has crepitus in both joints with the right side being worse than the left side.  The maximal jaw opening is 42 mm, but 22 mm without pain or discomfort.  The excursion movement to the right is 9 mm and to the left 8 mm.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of a bicuspid from each quadrant likely the first bicuspids.  Third molars are also absence.  Sinuses are clear.  Turbinates appear relatively normal in size.  There is significantly advanced arthritis in both temporomandibular joints.  Articular eminences are somewhat flattened. 

Coronal view shows that the septum is reasonably straight.  Right turbinate is somewhat enlarged.  The sinuses are clear.  

Axial view shows that the septum is straight, a turbinates are moderate in size with the right one being slightly larger than the left one, and the sinuses are clear.  

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle with advanced arthritis with areas of pitting and erosion.  There is anterior beaking present.  There appears to be degenerative changes on the surface of the condyle as well as on the fossa particularly on the articular eminence.  The joint is significantly narrowed and it appears there could be areas of bone-on-bone contact. 

Right TMJ sagittal view shows the arthritic condition is more progressive than the opposite side.  There is anterior beaking present.  There appears to be some free calcified bodies within the joint space.  The articular eminence has some irregularities in it as well as does the condylar head where there are areas of pitting or erosion present.  The joint space is narrowed.

Left TMJ coronal view shows an arthritic condyle with an area of erosion in the mid portion of the condyle.  There is decreased vertical joint space.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 mm.  

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with significant arthritic changes.  There is an osteophyte sticking up from the lateral aspect of the condyle that appears to have almost bone-on-bone contact with the fossa.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 4 to 5 mm.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that the maxilla and mandible are slightly retruded.  The oropharyngeal airway measures only about 3.5 mm at the soft palate level and also at the base of the tongue.  

Basic diagnoses for Alison would be as follows:

Bilateral TMJ psoriatic arthritis.
Maxillary AP and posterior vertical hypoplasia.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia.
Class II end-on occlusion.  
High occlusal plane angle facial morphology.
Missing all four first bicuspids and all four third molars.
TMJ pain. 
Headaches. 
Decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of 3.5 mm (normal AP dimension 11 mm).

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Evaluation at the Environmental Center in Dallas for metal hypersensitivities.
2.	Presurgical orthodontics.
	a.	Align and level maxillary arch.
	b.	Align and level mandibular arch.  Slenderize the lower anterior teeth 			removing about 2 mm of tooth structure and retract.
3.	CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol).
4.	Surgery.  
	a.	Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ 			Concepts total joint prostheses.
	b.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla in a 					counterclockwise direction.
	c.	Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen).
	d.	Bilateral coronoidotomies.
5.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion and retain.
