original_note:
B488 
Age 40. B488 was referred by XX.

B488's problems began last year, when she was 39 years old and was involved in a bus accident. During the accident, apparently she was sitting in the back row with no seat directly in front of her essentially sitting in the middle seat. When the accident occurred, she was thrown forward and she grabbed onto a seat to keep from rolling down the aisle and struck her jaw. She apparently struck it on her right side. Initially, she had quite significant pain in the back of her neck, shoulders, down into her arms and it was even black and blue in these areas. The pain was quite intense and she was not aware of the jaw pain until the first month after the accident she was aware of having tightness and pain in her jaw areas. When she finally was referred to chiropractic treatment, she brought that up to his attention that she had pain in the shoulder, neck and jaw areas. When she went to the chiropractic treatment, she had about 20 sessions about 2 to 3 per week. This made her dizzy and made her very sleepy but did provide no improvement in her pain levels. She then was sent to a pain management doctor who did epidural injections into the lower cervical vertebrae. The injections did not help her. She had the epidural injections done twice, but this did not improve her pain. She then was recommended to have aquatic therapy. She started the aquatic therapy earlier this year and still continues that treatment. Early this year, she tried a splint but this did not help her and, in fact, increased her pain and made the pain more frequent. It caused muscle cramps in the jaws.

B488 rates her headaches at a level of 8 that occur predominantly on the right side in the frontal and temporal area, but they occur bilaterally in the posterior head area and she gets these about three times per week. She rates her TMJ pain at 8, average daily pain at 8, jaw at 8, diet at 6, disability at 9. She gets occasionally right sided earaches, moderate tinnitus and moderate vertigo. She is aware of clenching her teeth severely at nighttime and she also clenches during the day. Other joints that bother B488  include her right shoulder, right elbow, right wrist, right hip, right ankle, neck and back. She has some neuropathies somewhat are affecting her right arm, but since the epidural injections in the cervical vertebrae area, this problem has improved. She still has the pain, but she had numbness through arm and hand that has cleared.

In frontal view, B488 has good facial balance. In profile, she has good facial balance as well. Occlusally, she has a Class II end-on cuspid-molar relationship and an anterior deep bite of about 9 mm. She has a reversed curve of Spee in the upper arch and an accentuated curve of Spee in the lower arch. She has all of her teeth back through the second molars present. Oropharyngeal tissues look healthy. She has moderately enlarged tonsils with the right one being larger than the left one. To palpation, she has low severe pain over the TMJs, retromandibular area, right shoulder, high moderate pain over the masseter muscles, right medial pterygoid and right SCM and moderate pain over the left medial pterygoid, left SCM and left shoulder as well as right temporalis area. She has significant clicking and popping in both joints with an opening clicking at about 15 mm on the right and 20 on the left and closing clicks at about 15 mm on the left and 10 mm on the right. The maximal incisal opening is 47 mm, opening without pain only about 20 mm. The excursion movements in each direction are about 7 mm with pain.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. The turbinates appear moderately enlarged. Sinuses appear relatively clear. She could have a periapical lesion around the mandibular right second molar.

Coronal view shows that the turbinates appear moderately enlarged. The sinuses appear clear.
Axial view shows that the turbinates are moderately enlarged and the sinuses appear clear.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has a little bit of anterior beaking. Out laterally, the condyle appears narrow. The posterior head of the condyle appears somewhat flattened particularly towards the medial side of the joint. The joint space appears narrow vertically and posteriorly. The articular eminence has a more shallow inclination.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is flattened on the anterior aspect with some anterior beaking present. There is decreased vertical joint space. The articular eminence has moderate inclination. The vertical joint space appears narrow.

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has an odd shape to it where the medial pole is significantly higher than the lateral pole. There is decreased vertical joint space. The lateral rim of the fossa curves probably about 4 to 5 mm. There is decreased lateral joint space.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has a little bit better morphology than the opposite side. There is decreased vertical joint space. The lateral rim curves inferiorly probably 4 to 5 mm.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has a Class II skeletal and occlusal relationship. Oropharyngeal airway measures about 8 mm at the base of the tongue and 5 mm at the soft palate level.

Basic diagnoses for B488 would be as follows:
Bilateral TMJ mild arthritis and articular disc dislocation with reduction.
Mandibular hyperplasia.
TMJ pain.
Headaches.
Myofascial pain. Class II end-on occlusion with anterior deep bite of 7 mm.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1.	Presurgical orthodontics. a.	Align and level maxillary arch. b.	Align and level mandibular arch.
2.	Surgery. a.	Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors. b.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to maximize the occlusal fit.
3.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

I will be referring her to Dr. XX. The other person to receive a letter is Mr. XX.

B488 MRI  
AGE: 40/F
Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology. The articular eminence is moderate steep. The joint space is narrow. It appears that the disc may be in position but very thin. At the lateral aspect of the joint, the disc might be anteriorly positioned, but very difficult to tell.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle with fairly good morphology. The disc tissue appears extremely thin and hard to identify. There is a little bit of fluid in the superior joint space.

Right TMJ coronal view shows it may have a laterally displaced articular disc. The condyle appears to have good width mediolaterally. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 to 3 mm.

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has a little bit more arthritic changes associated with it. The joint space is somewhat narrow particularly at the lateral aspect of the joint. It is very difficult to tell where the discal tissue is.

Left TMJ opening shows good translation of the condyle and the disc is in a reduced position on opening. In another view, the disc looks as thought it is of adequate size.

Right TMJ shows that the articular disc is in good positioning on opening and appropriately positioned between the condyle and the articular eminence.

IMPRESSION: Bilateral TMJ mild arthritis and articular discs that are quite thin and could be slightly medially displaced with possible disc displacements but with reduction on opening.

original_note:
KALINA KWONG									11/07/2005
DOB:  08/05/1992. 

Kalina is 13 years old.  She is referred by Dr. George Adams.  She is in good health with no major medical problems.  

PRIMARY CONCERN
Headaches.
Pain in the jaw joints. 
Difficulty breathing through the nose.

She takes no medications and has no known allergies.  Kalina did injure her ankle two days ago and currently has a boot on for support.  Kalina has had headaches since about the age of 10.  During the past year, she apparently has developed some clicking in her jaws and maybe some increased occurrence of headaches.  Etiology is unknown.  She gets earaches occasionally and vertigo occasionally but tenderness or hearing loss.  She does have weak ankles and she tends to sprain them frequently and also she has previously fractured her right ankle and it appears she fractured the right wrist.  She has had difficulties wearing pierced earrings and wore them for about a year but had problems with infections and stopped wearing them about 2 years ago.  Kalina does give a history of clenching at nighttime and also during the daytime.  She gets headaches about once a week that she wakes up with at about a level of 4 in the frontal and temple areas.  She gets pain in the jaw joints 2 to 3 times per week and is usually present when she wakes up in the morning at about a level of 5.  Average daily pain is 3, jaw function at 3, diet at 1, disability at 0.  She did have a traumatic injury where she got hit in the mouth with a bowling ball that fractured off part of a tooth and it was replaced.  Kalina is aware that she tends to breath through her mouth at night.  However, she is not aware of any problems with snoring, daytime somnolence or sleep apnea problems.

In frontal view, Kalina has fairly good left to right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 20 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 5 mm.  Maxillary midline is about 1 mm or 2 off to the left side.  Mandibular dental midline and chin midline are fairly close to being on the facial midline or maybe just about 1 mm off to the right side.  Chin appears to be off to the right side probably about 2 to 3 mm.  Maxillary midline maybe 1 mm off to the left and mandibular dental midline 1 mm off to the right.  Mandibular dental midline and chin midline are pretty much aligned with each other.  In profile view, she has fairly good balance in the upper lips, cheeks and nose.  The chin appears retruded by probably about 12 to 15 mm.  Intraorally, she has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars.  She has some mild crowding in the lower anterior arch.  She has adequate attached gingiva of lower anterior arch.  She has anterior deep bite of about 5 mm.  She has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship bilaterally.  Class II is a little bit greater on the right side and she is closer to a Class I on the left side with only about 2 mm of Class II on the left and probably 4 mm Class II on the right.  Upper incisor crown length is probably about 8.5 mm.  When Kalina smiles she exposes about 6 mm of gingiva.  Intraorally, the soft tissues look healthy.  The uvula, soft palate and faucial pillars all appear relatively normal.  Tonsils appear fairly minimal in size.  Intranasally, the septum looks fairly straight.  The right turbinate appears relatively normal in size.  The left turbinate appears enlarged and appears to block a fair portion of the airway.  
KALINA KWONG				11/8/2005				Page 2

To palpation, she has some moderate tenderness over the TMJs and styloid areas.  Joints seem to function reasonably smooth.  The maximal incisal opening is about 50 mm.  Excursive movement is about 9 mm in each direction.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the presence of all 4 third molars as being impacted.  The sinuses look relatively clear.  Some dilacerations on the lower bicuspids.  Left maxillary central incisor has an opacified root.  

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology.  There may be some slight decreased posterior joint space.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle with fairly good morphology.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  There may be some decreased posterior joint space.  On opening, there is good translation of both condyles forward and anterior to the articular eminence.  

Waters view shows the sinuses look relatively clear.  Nasal septum is deviated just slightly off the left side.  The turbinate on the right side actually looks a little larger than on the left side, which is opposite the clinical impression.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that there is some adenoid tissue still remaining in the oropharynx.  The turbinate extends back towards the posterior aspect of the hard palate.  However, oropharyngeal airway appears acceptable with the dimension of about 6 to 7 mm at the soft palate level and about 8 to 9 at the base of the tongue.  She appears to have a Class II skeletal and occlusal relationship.  Moderately high occlusal plane angle.  Lower incisors appear a little bit overangulated.

BASIC DIAGNOSES
Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocated anteriorly. 
Right TMJ arthritis and possible medial displacement of the articular disc. 
Vertical maxillary excess.
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency. 
AP mandibular deficiency. 
Class II end-on occlusion. 
High occlusal plane angle.
Anterior deep bite.
Impacted third molars x4.
Hypertrophied turbinates.
Partial nasal airway obstruction.
Overangulation of lower anteriors. 
Crowding of lower anteriors.
Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2.5.
Headaches.
TMJ pain.
KALINA KWONG				11/8/2005				Page 3


RECOMMENDED TREATMENT
MRI of left and right TMJs to include coronal views.
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch.
Align and level mandibular arch.  Slenderize lower anterior teeth removing approximately 2.5 to 3 mm of tooth structure and close spacing.
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors (dependent on MRI confirmation.)
Multiple maxillary osteotomies moving the anterior maxilla superiorly and posterior aspect inferiorly.  
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction.
Anterior augmentation genioplasty with sliding genioplasty.  
Surgical removal of impacted third molars x 4.
Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies. 
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B456 
B456 is 43 years old and referred by Dr. XX.
Patient History
She has had a previous hysterectomy. Other minor surgeries she has had include breast biopsy and a C-section. She is aware of having other areas of osteoarthritis that include her neck and her lower back.

The onset of B456's problems began a little over three years ago when she was involved in a motor vehicle accident and sustained a whiplash injury. Since that time, she has developed severe pain problems and headaches. She wore a splint for a year. She has been on numerous medications. She has had physical therapy, acupuncture, manipulations, water therapy, hands on treatment, etc. When she gets her severe headaches, they can lead to nausea and vomiting, difficulties walking, and difficulties with vision. She has earaches predominantly on the right side and tinnitus. She gets occasional vertigo. Other joints that bother her include her neck, back, shoulders, fingers, hips, knees, and feet. She has some environmental allergies. She cannot wear cheap earrings. She is not aware of any nocturnal clenching and bruxism habits.

B456 rates her headaches that occur predominantly in the right temporal area but can migrate to the frontal and posterior head area at a level of 8. She gets these two to three times per week, but these headaches can easily escalate to a 10. She rates her TMJ pain at 5, overall pain at 4, jaw function at 5, diet at 4, and disability at 4.

Current Medications
Estradiol Maxalt 10 mg for migraine
Clinical Examination
B456 has good facial symmetry and balance. In profile view, she has good facial balance. Chin-neckline is fairly well defined, and chin-neck angle is well defined.

Intraorally, she has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. She has some mild crowding, lower anterior arch. She has an accentuated curve of occlusion in the lower arch. In the upper arch, she is missing the right second and third molar and the left third molar. She essentially has a Class II cuspid-molar relationship. She has an overjet of probably about 4 mm and anterior deep-bite of probably about 3 to 4 mm. Oropharynx appears relatively clear.

Intranasally, turbinates don't appear too large but relatively normal in size. She has a significant nasal spur off the right side that does block the airway a little bit. On the left side, she has a narrow nostril secondary to a significant left lateral deviation of the nasal septum at the anterior aspect of the septum. Holding the left nostril open enabled B456 to breathe much easier through her nose. To palpation, she has mild to moderate discomfort over the temporalis, masseters, medial pterygoid, TMJ, styloid area, and SCM. No tenderness at all on the left side. In function, she has audible crepitation in the right TMJ. Left joint seems to function relatively smoothly. She is not aware of any significant clicking or popping in the left TMJ area. Maximal incisal opening is 35 mm but without significant pain only 23 mm. Excursive movement to the left is 5 mm and to the right 0. It is probably due to the fact she feels something is going to break on the right side if she shifts over to that side. B456's overjet in central relation is about 6 to 7 mm. She can protrude straight forward about 8 mm.

Radiographic Evaluation
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars and maxillary right second molar. Sinuses appear relatively clear. Left turbinate appears a little bit enlarged compared to the right turbinate. Airway, however, for the most part looks reasonably good in the bony areas. Has a root canal in the maxillary right central incisor. Demonstrates some significant osteoarthritic changes in the right condyle. Left condyle looks a little bit more normal.

Right TMJ Waters view shows that there is some significant deviation of the nasal septum. Turbinates appear just moderate in size. Sinuses appear clear.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle with a condyle with a little bit of hockey stick shape to it. A little sclerosis on the head of the condyle. Joint space appears a little bit decreased vertically. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Disc could be displaced or at least is extremely thin.

Right TMJ tomogram shows the condyle postured way forward, and I believe she probably wasn't in centric relation when this x-ray was taken. Posterior superior joint space is excessively increased and measures about 6 to 7 mm. We will retake this film in centric relation.

Opening views show that she has some restricted translation bilaterally, although the condyles do come forward onto the articular eminence, and both condyles seem to translate reasonably well. Decreased joint space on the left side between the condylar head and articular eminence. Right condyle looks significantly more arthritic than the left condyle.

Cephalometrically, she also is not in centric relation and actually has her mandible postured forward significantly to an end-on relationship.
Right TMJ tomogram, new one taken in centric relation, shows severely decreased vertical joint space with significant anterior beaking. Decreased anterior joint space as well indicative of total displacement of the articular disc and most likely a large perforation through the remaining tissues.

Cephalometrically, B456 has a Class II dental and skeletal relationship. Oropharyngeal airway appears reasonably good.
Basic Diagnoses
Right TMJ severe arthritis with articular disc dislocation and severe degenerative changes
Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with reduction
AP mandibular hypoplasia
Class II occlusion
Anterior deep-bite
Severe headaches TMJ pain
Myofascial pain
Earaches Tinnitus

Recommended Treatment
Presurgical orthodontics
Align and level maxillary arch. Strip lower anterior teeth and retract. Consider stripping bicuspids as well to prevent excessive flaring of the lower anteriors

Surgery
Option 1
Bilateral TMJ condylectomies, debridement, reconstruction, and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvested from abdomen)
Possible bilateral coronoidectomies if they will potentially cause any functional interferences

Option 2
Right TMJ total joint prostheses
Fat graft to right TMJ (harvested from abdomen)
Left TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor
Left mandibular sagittal split osteotomy to advance the mandible

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion
B456  MRI Evaluation 
Left TMJ, coronal view, shows a condyle with good mediolateral width. Appears to be an osteophyte sticking down from the fossa area toward the medial side of the condyle. Appears to be some compressed tissue in the joint space, but the disc appears to be more anteriorly positioned. Except for where the osteophyte is, the joint space seems to be pretty well balanced.

Right TMJ, coronal view, shows a condyle with fairly good mediolateral width. Decreased joint space vertically, however. Lateral rim of the fossa has a very minimal downward curvature. Discal tissues appear to perhaps be anteriorly positioned.

Left TMJ, sagittal view, shows a condyle with some flattening of the anterior head and some anterior beaking present. Some sclerosis on the head of the condyle. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Articular disc is anteriorly displaced and sitting beneath the articular eminence.

On opening, the condyle appears to reduce onto the disc, and there is fairly good translation of both the condyle and the disc. On the sagittal views, the osteophyte seen in the coronal views is not observed.

Right TMJ, sagittal view, shows anterior beaking of the condyle and significantly decreased joint space particularly vertically. Disc is anteriorly displaced. Significantly decreased anterior joint space actually. Anterior beaking is quite marked. Condyle appears somewhat enlarged.

On opening, the disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction.
Impression:
Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with reduction
Right TMJ with advanced arthritis and articular disc dislocation without reduction and severely deformed

original_note:
RICHARD NAGATOSHI								06/18/2007

Richard is 44 years old.  He is a policeman in California.  He has significant sleep apnea problems and has had a sleep study showing his severe apnea.  He cannot tolerate a CPAP machine.  He has chronic headaches.  He has some clicking in his jaw joints particularly on the right side.  It is unknown when this started and the etiology is unknown.  He says he has been hit around the jaws numerous times in the line of duty but never had anything broken.  He gets headaches 3 to 5 times per week predominately in the frontal areas where it is worse at about a level of 5 but can escalate to 10.  At times when it gets so bad, he cannot get out of bed.  The pain directly in the jaw joints he rates at 0, average daily pain at 3, jaw function 0, diet 0 and disability at 0.  On opening, he has a click in the right TMJ at about 20 mm of opening and about 15 mm of closing.  The left joint seems to function relatively smoothly.  To palpation, he has no tenderness around the head and neck area.  He has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo or hearing loss.  

CURRENT MEDICATIONS:
Tricor 145 mg daily.
Lipitor 20 mg daily. 
Tylenol.
Motrin.

Clinical evaluation shows the upper lip length of 27 mm and he has 0 tooth-to-lip relationship.  Maxillary midline is off to the right side 1 mm.  Mandibular dental midline and chin are off to the left 1 mm.  In profile view, the maxilla is 4 to 5 mm AP deficient and chin is about 10 to 15 mm AP deficient.  Intranasally, the turbinates are of moderate size.  The septum appears relatively normal.  Columella appears a little bit wide and he could benefit from having some narrowing of that.  He has severe snoring and moderate daytime somnolence.  He says he is not aware of having problems where he holds his breath at night when sleeping although he does have sleep apnea syndrome.  Intraoral examination shows that it is very difficult to evaluate the soft palate and uvula but the uvula is hyperplastic.  The soft palate is excessively elongated.  The faucial pillars are severely constricted. 

The MRI shows that he has bilaterally displaced articular discs.  Discussed with him my recommendation including all the previous surgical procedures that we already outlined, he would benefit from the TMJ surgery.  It is likely that the TMJ pathology is contributing to his headache situation.  He understands and would like to have the joint pathology corrected at the same time as the rest of the procedures are being done. 

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION:
Panorex shows that all 4 third molars are absent but the rest of the teeth are present and there are appliances on all the teeth.  Sinuses appear clear.  Right nasal airway appears somewhat blocked.  Condyles appear a little bit flat on the anterior surfaces.  Articular eminences appear steep.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology.  A little bit of flattening on the posterior head.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  There appears to be reasonably good joint space.
RICHARD NAGATOSHI			06/18/2007				Page 2

The left TMJ tomogram is a little bit more difficult to read.  There may be a decrease in the joint space superiorly and posteriorly.  Articular eminence appears a little bit steeper on this side than the opposite side.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that the maxilla and mandible are AP deficient.  There is a moderately high occlusal plane angle.  Oropharyngeal airway is only about 4 mm at the soft palate level and the base of the tongue is closer to about 10 mm.  

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
AP maxillary hypoplasia. 
AP mandibular hypoplasia.
Hyperplastic and elongated soft palate and uvula.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Severe snoring. 
Sleep apnea syndrome. 
Bilateral TMJ articular disc dislocations. 

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT: 
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction. 
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance it and downgraft it posteriorly. 
UPPP procedure. 
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors. 

His H&P was performed today.  Consent form was reviewed and signed.  I did my examination and answered all the questions that he had at this time.  The potential risks and complications were discussed with him and he understands.  He is anxious to proceed on with the surgery in 2 days.

original_note:
TIFFANY ZANCHA			Telephone Conversation 			01-06-04

TIFFANY AND HER MOTHER, CAROL:  Tiffany’s TMJ problems began about three or four years ago when she was in the eighth grade, and there is no known etiology for the onset of her symptoms. She is now a junior in high school. She had some soreness and achiness occasionally back at that time. In her freshman year, though, she began developing locking and increased symptoms and problems. She has worn a splint since January of 2002 but still continues to have her problems. She has had physical therapy, albeit one treatment, however, this made no improvement for her at all and made things worse and was discontinued. She currently takes the following medications:

Allegra
An inhaler
Zithromax (for recent sinus flareup)
Tylenol
Nitinol (?)

The primary concerns she has are as follows:

Headaches 
Severe TMJ pain
Pain through her jaws
Difficulty eating and chewing
Difficulty singing

She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, or vertigo but does have some hearing loss due to childhood recurrent ear problems. Other joints that bother her include her ankles, feet, and toes, but she has been tap dancing for 12 years. She says she has bad sinuses. She is aware of nocturnal clenching and bruxism. She gets headaches about two times a week that she rates at a level of 5 that occur in the frontal and temporal areas predominantly. She rates her TMJ pain on an average at about 6, but it can escalate to a 9. Her average daily pain is a level of 6. She rates her jaw function at 7, diet at 5, and disability at 3. She has opening clicking and popping in both joints and also closing clicks as well. She does occasionally get a closed lock problem. As she does self-palpation, she rates pain in her masseter muscles at a level of 5 and TMJs at a level of 3. She apparently has a lot of environmental allergies such as mold, dust, feathers, etc. She is 5’6” in height and weighs 210 lb. 

MRIs were not a very good quality, and the mother is aware of this. In my evaluation, I felt that the discs were dislocated. The fact that they click and pop is an indication that they are dislocated, and the fact that she has a periodic closed lock situation also supports this. The radiologist has misread the MRI, as he states:

“No evidence of dislocation of the temporomandibular joint meniscus.”
“Normal relationship of the temporomandibular joint.”

This is a complete misinterpretation of the MRIs in this patient. 

Based on the information at hand, which includes my telephone conversation, the basic diagnoses for Tiffany would be as follows:

Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocations with reductions
Mandibular hyperplasia
Severe TMJ pain
Headaches
Myofascial pain
TIFFANY ZANCHA				01-06-04				Page 2

Recommended treatment would be as follows: 

Presurgical orthodontics 
Align and level maxillary arch 
Align and level mandibular arch 

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair
Bilateral mandibular sagittal split osteotomies

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

original_note:
B033
B033 is 60 years old. She is referred by Dr. XXXX,. She has had orthodontic treatment by Dr. XXXX. Her physician is Dr. XXXX.
B033 is in good health with no major medical problems. Primary concerns she has include the following:

Severe overbite problems that are congenital in nature.
Headaches.
TMJ pain. Myofascial pain.
B033 first became aware of having jaw and headache problems about 10 to 12 years ago of unknown etiology. She wakes with headaches and usually goes to sleep with headaches. She did have orthodontics from the age of 55 for approximately 2 years. She gets headaches predominantly in the temporal areas at a level of 7. She rates her TMJ pain at 4, average daily pain at 7, jaw function at 3, diet at 5, disability at 4. She takes the following medications:

Tylenol.
Advil.
Ibuprofen, all for headache control.
She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, nor hearing loss. She does get occasional vertigo. No other joints bother her. She may clench particularly at night time. She may have some moderate snoring. She has moderate daytime somnolence. Occasionally, she will wake up with difficulties catching her breath.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
In frontal view, B033 has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 20 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is -2 mm. The mandibular dental midline is about 2 mm off to the left. The maxillary midline is maybe about 1 mm off to the left. The chin is fairly well on the facial midline. In profile, she has some AP maxillary deficiency of about 3 mm and AP mandibular deficiency of 5-6 mm. The chin neckline is just slightly short. She has prominent gonial angles with a brachiocephalic morphological profile. Intraorally, she is missing mandibular right first molar and has the rest of her teeth back around through the lower left second molar. The teeth appear a little bit smaller in size. Actually, her mandibular dental midline is shifted just slightly towards the right side about 1 mm and it is probably about 2 mm off to the right side. She has a Class II cuspid relationship on the right side and closer to Class I cuspid relationship on the left side. She has an anterior deep bite of probably about 5 to 6 mm. Oropharyngeal structures look healthy and the soft palate and uvula appear to be normal in length. There is mild to moderate discomfort over the temporalis muscles and the left posterior neck area. Joints seems to function fairly smoothly on opening, but on excursion movements she did have a pop in the right joint area. Intranasally, at least clinically, the turbinates in the front appear to be just mildly enlarged. The septum may have some slight deviation to it.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars and the mandibular right first molar with a maintained space. She has root canals on all the lower four incisors and the right cuspid. She has a crown on the mandibular left first molars. She also has crowns on her lower anterior six teeth. It is difficult to evaluate the joints in this view. The sinuses appear reasonably clear. The turbinates appear moderate in size with the left side perhaps giving a little bit more obstruction than the right side.

Water's view shows that she has moderately enlarged turbinates. The sinuses appear relatively clear.
Axial view shows that the turbinates are of moderate size. Sinuses appear relatively clear. The septum appears reasonably straight.

Left TMJ coronal tomogram shows that the medial pole is lower than the lateral pole. There appears to be some decreased joint space. There is decreased space at the lateral aspect of the joint.

Right TMJ shows an exaggerated inferior position of the mediolateral pole compared to the lateral pole. The joint spaces appear somewhat narrow. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 mm.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that appears fairly good size. There is some flattening on the anterior head. There could be some what looks like decreased vertical and posterior joint space.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that looks slightly larger. There is a small amount of anterior beaking present.
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has a slight Class II skeletal relationship. Functional oronasal airway is about 6 mm at the soft palate level and about 7 to 8 mm at the base of the tongue. She has a low mandibular plane, low occlusal plane angle facial morphology. She has some vertical maxillary deficiency. Oropharyngeal airway space measures 5 mm at the soft palate level and 6 mm at the base of the tongue. She appears to be short in the vertical anterior mandibular vertical height.

Basic diagnoses for B033 would be as follows:
Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly and laterally displaced. AP maxillary hypoplasia.
AP mandibular hypoplasia.
Class I occlusion on the left side, Class II occlusion on the right side.
Arch asymmetry with the maxillary left cuspid being 2-3 mm posterior to the right cuspid and in the mandibular arch the right cuspid is 2 mm posterior to the left cuspid.
Mandibular dental midline shifted 2 mm to the right of the facial midline and maxillary midline shifted 1-2 mm to the left of the facial midline.
Missing mandibular right first molar.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of 5-6 mm (normal is 11 mm).
TMJ pain. Headaches. Myofascial pain.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1. Medications: Klonopin 1-mg tablet, 1 tablet q.h.s. to decrease nocturnal clenching.
2. Presurgical orthodontics. a.	Align and level maxillary arch. Try to shift maxillary midline to the right about 1 mm and advance the left cuspid 1-2 mm and retract the right cuspid 1-2 mm. b.	Align and level mandibular arch. Shift mandibular dental midline to the left approximately 2 mm. Advance the maxillary right cuspid approximately 1-2 mm and retract the left cuspid 1-2 mm. c.	May need to use TADs to function as anchors in correcting the arch asymmetries.
3. Surgery. a.	Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors. b.	Bilateral ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible forward. c.	Maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla forward and correct any transverse problems that may be present.
4. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
5. Definitive restorative dentistry that may include an osteointegrated implant in the mandibular right first molar area and reconstruction of crowns.

B033 MRI
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width. There is some sclerosis on top of the condylar head. The lateral rim curves inferiorly a relatively minimal amount. It appears that the discal tissue may be anteriorly displaced. The medial pole is significantly lower than the lateral pole of the condyle. The disc is laterally displaced.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears somewhat broad compared to the opposite side. There is decreased lateral joint space. There appears to be discal tissue that is laterally displaced as well as anterior. The medial pole of the condyle is significantly lower than the lateral pole. The right condyle is significantly larger than the left condyle.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology, although there is some flattening and a little sclerosis on the anterior head of the condyle. The disc appears to be laterally displaced as well as anteriorly displaced at the lateral aspect of the joint. More medially, the disc appears to be in a little bit better position, although the disc appears a little bit more degenerated towards the medial side of the joint. The articular eminence is moderately steep. There is fluid at the lateral aspect of the joint in the superior compartment. On opening, there is good translation of the condyle and the articular disc with the disc in a reduced position.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has a little bit of flattening anterior but not too much. However, mesially the disc is not seen as readily as it is towards the lateral aspect. It actually appears fairly prominent at the lateral aspect of the joint. There is fluid at the lateral aspect of the joint with the disc displaced laterally. There is good translation of the condyle and articular disc with the disc in good position.

IMPRESSION:
Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly and laterally displaced. The anterior displacement of the disc is at the lateral aspect of the joint. Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly and laterally displaced. The anterior displacement of the disc is at the lateral aspect of the joint. Discs appear salvageable.

original_note:
B478 
B478 is 20 years old. Referred by Dr. X.

B478's jaw problems began when she was about 17 years old of unknown etiology. The joints first started to click and pop at that time. At age 18, she began developing pain associated with her jaw areas. When she was 19, she developed a close lock on the right side. B478 gets some headaches at about a level of 2 in the temporal and top of the head area. She rates her TMJ pain at 5, average daily pain at 4, jaw function at 2, diet at 3, disability at 1. She gets occasional earaches, but no tinnitus nor vertigo. She believes she may have some hearing loss. The only other joints that really bother her are her lower back. She does have a history of asthma. She is aware of bruxing at nighttime and clenching during the day.

She takes the following medications:
Dulara.
Singulair.
Albuterol.
Iron, vitamin C and vitamin D.
In frontal view, B478 has fairly good facial symmetry in the middle and upper third face. Upper lip length is about 21 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is 3.5 mm. The maxillary midline is about 0.5 mm to the left. The mandible dental midline is 1 mm to the left and chin is about almost 6 mm to the left. In profile, B478 has fairly good facial balance. Intraorally, B478 has all of her teeth back through the second molars present and her teeth are fairly well aligned and level in the lower arch. She has good attached gingiva. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. The mandibular dental midline is about 1 to 1.5 mm off towards the left side. She has a just a slight Class III cuspid relationship on the right side by about 2 mm and fairly close to Class I on the left side. She has a cross bite tendency on the left side. She is Class III on the right by about 2 mm and on the left she is pretty close to being Class I, but has a posterior cross bite tendency on the left side. She has an end-on incisor relationship. Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively normal and healthy. She has moderately enlarged tonsils. Intranasally, B478 has enlarged turbinates and appear a little bit inflamed on the right side and congested on the left side. She has had previous septal surgery and it appears fairly straight. She probably has moderate nasal airway obstruction. To palpation, she has moderate pain over the TMJs and milder pain over the retromandibular area and masseter muscles. On opening, she has some crepitus in the right joint but no popping and opening, but on closing she has a definite pop at about 15 mm in the closing mode. The maximal incisal opening is 47 mm and excursion movement to the right 7 mm and to the left 6 mm.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. The left condyle appears small in size. The right condyle appears larger than normal. The left condyle looks quite small anteroposteriorly compared to the right side.

Left TMJ sagittal views show a condyle that is quite smaller in size then the opposite condyle. There appears to be well balanced joint space.
Right TMJ sagittal view shows an elongated condylar head and neck. The joint space appears a little bit decreased. The articular eminence is moderately shallow.

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with fairly reduced mediolateral width. The joint space looks fairly well balanced.
Right TMJ coronal view shows a lateral osteophytic growth off the condyle. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 5 mm. There is a lateral extension of the condyle.

Coronal view shows that the septum is reasonably straight. Turbinates are moderately enlarged and sinuses appear relatively clear.
In the axial view, the septum appears reasonably straight, but the turbinates are quite large and appear to block the majority of the airway. Sinuses look relatively clear.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that B478 has a skeletal Class I jaw relationship. The inferior border of the mandible is down about 4 to 5 mm on the right side as compared to the left side.

Basic diagnoses for B478 would be as follows:
Right mandibular condylar osteochondroma.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc dislocations with reduction on the right side, but without reduction on the left side.
Mandibular asymmetry with the mandibular shifted off to the left side.
Maxillary asymmetry.
Hypertrophied turbinates (the patient is a predominant mouth breather).
TMJ pain.
Headaches.
Slight Class III occlusion on the right side and end-on incisor relationship.
Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2 to 3 mm.
Transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right side being 2 mm lower than the left side.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1.	Presurgical orthodontics. a.	Align and level maxillary arch. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. b.	Align and level mandibular arch. Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing 1 to 2 mm of tooth structure and retract to help correct the tooth size discrepancy.
2.	Surgery. a.	Right mandibular low condylectomy to remove the abnormal growth process. b.	Bilateral articular disc repositioning and repair with Mitek anchors. c.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to set the mandible back on the facial midline and level the occlusal plane transversely. d.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to align the maxilla. e.	Partial inferior turbinectomies.
3	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
4.	Restorative dentistry if it is required to place veneers on the anterior teeth.

B478 MRI  
DOB:	 
DATE OF ACQUISITION: 
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width. The disc appears to be medially displaced and perhaps somewhat anteriorly displaced. The lateral rim curves inferiorly about 3 mm. There is decreased lateral joint space.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is significantly larger than the left side and could be compatible with an osteochondroma. The disc appears that it may be laterally displaced. There is decreased superior joint space. There is an extension of the condyle out laterally.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has a little bit of anterior beaking present. The disc appears to be anteriorly displaced and sitting along the posterior slope of the articular eminence. The articular eminence has a moderate inclination. There is a little bit of fluid in the superior joint space. On opening, there is restricted translation and the disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is significantly elongated in the head and neck area. The disc is thin but appears to be in reasonable position, although not seen well medially. Out laterally, it does appear laterally displaced. The articular eminence has a moderate inclination. There appears to be a little bit of fluid in the anterior and superior joint space. On opening, there is significantly limited translation of the condyle. There appears to be some discal tissue present, although it is quite thinned at this particular cut.

IMPRESSION: Right TMJ condylar enlargement compatible with an osteochondroma versus condylar hyperplasia.
Left TMJ articular disc dislocation without reduction and mild arthritis

original_note:
RANDY REYNOLDS								03/26/2008

Randy is 54 years old.  Referred by Dr. Sanjay Reddi, 2453 E. 11th Street, Odessa, TX 79761.  Randy has high blood pressure.  He takes the following medications:
Lisinopril/HCTZ 20/12.5 x2.
Nifedical XL 60 mg x2.

Randy has only been aware of having a problem with his jaw during the last 2 to 3 years and only began paying much attention to it during the past year.  The etiology is unknown.  Primary concerns are as follows:

Lower jaw shifting off towards the right side.
Crossbite has developed. 
Teeth do not fit together very well.
Pain in the left jaw joint.

Randy has no problems with headaches, but he does get pain in the left TMJ at about a level of 7, average daily pain can reach a level of 7.  Jaw function is at 4, diet at 0, disability at 2.  He has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, or hearing loss.  His left knee will bother him occasionally, but otherwise his joints are in good condition.  He is aware of clenching and bruxing at night time and sometimes during the day as well.  

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  The upper lip length is about 26 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about minus 3 mm.  Maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline.  Mandibular dental midline is shifted about 3.5 mm to the right, and chin is about 8 to 9 mm to the right.  The left side of the face is somewhat elongated, probably about 4 to 5 mm.  In profile view, Randy has good facial balance.  Intraorally, Randy has all of his lower teeth back to the second molars.  A lot of wear is present from his bruxing.  He has good attached gingiva.  In the upper arch, he is missing the maxillary right second bicuspid replaced with a bridge.  He may be missing a bicuspid on the opposite side as well.  He has basically a Class I cuspid on the right side and a full Class III cuspid on the left side.  He has a total buccal crossbite on the right side and a non-buccal relationship on the left side.  He has about 0.5 mm of anterior open bite.  Oropharyngeal tissues look healthy.  The uvula is moderately elongated.  Intranasally, he appears to have a good airway, although the septum seems to be deviated a little bit off towards the right side with a spur off to the left side at the base of the nose.  Turbinates appear moderate in size.   He does have moderate snoring, but is not aware of having any nasal airway obstruction.  He has no sleep apnea issues.  Maximal opening is 60 mm, but it deviates off towards to the left side probably about 4 mm or so.  Excursion movement to the right was 2 mm, to the left about 6 mm.  The right TMJ functions smoothly.  Left TMJ has some fine crepitus noises.  No tenderness to palpation around the head and neck area. 






Based on clinical and radiographic analyses, Randy’s diagnoses are as follows:

Left mandibular condylar osteochondroma.
Left temporal bone probable osteochondroma, involving predominately the glenoid fossa area.
Class III occlusion on left side.
Mandible deviated to the right side 3.5 mm at the incisors, with the chin 8 to 9 mm to the right.
Cross bite on the right side.
Crepitus noises in left TMJ with function.
Left TMJ pain.
History of clenching and bruxism.	


Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Resection and removal of left mandibular condylar osteochondroma. 
Resection and removal of left temporal osteochondroma. 
Reconstruct left TMJ with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses.
Fat graft to left TMJ area (harvest from abdomen).
Right mandibular ramus sagittal split osteotomy.
Application of arch bars.

original_note:
MACKENSIE PEAK									10-11-02

MacKensie is 20 years old and referred by Dr. Moody Alexander.

Patient History

She has been diagnosed with hypothyroidism in the past, and MacKenzie has some allergy problems. She broke her wrist in 1992; that is doing well now.

SHE HAS BEEN TOLD THAT SHE IS ALLERGIC TO PENICILLIN.

Current Medications

She takes Armour 1 gr per day and Unithroid 75 mcg.

Patient Concerns

Primary concern that MacKensie has is a gummy smile. She has had an aesthetic gingivoplasty performed, but this has not corrected her problem. 

She has no problems with breathing through her nose, earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, headaches, TMJ pain, nor TMJ noises. 

She has had no previous orthodontic treatment.

Clinical Evaluation

In frontal view, MacKensie has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 21 mm. Upper tooth to lip relationship is 6 mm. Maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline. Mandibular midline is deviated about 1 to 1.5 mm to the right. Chin is pretty much on the facial midline. 

In profile view, she has sort of a C-shaped curve to the upper lip. A little prominence of the nasal dorsum. In frontal view, the nose also is a little bit broad. Upper lip supralabrale is just slightly posterior to the subnasale. Chin appears slightly retruded by probably about 5 to 8 mm. Upper lip is retruded by probably 3 to 4 mm. Chin-neckline appears slightly short, and chin-neck angle is not really well defined. As she postures her mandible forward a little bit, it improves the AP position of the mandible, although the lower third face is still somewhat long. She does have some lip incompetence of probably 5 to 6 mm. She does have a head postured forward neck position. Clinical crown length is approximately 10 mm. Gingiva still exposed is probably about 7 mm. 

The left side might be about a millimeter longer in vertical length as compared to the right side.

Intraorally, she has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars erupted in. Some mild crowding, lower anterior arch. Some slight lower arch asymmetry with the right cuspid being about a millimeter posterior to the left cuspid. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. She is essentially just slightly Class II bilaterally by about 2 mm. Has an anterior deep bite of probably about 6 to 7 mm. Covers almost the entire lower crowns with the upper teeth. Appears to have adequate attached gingiva, however, in all quadrants. 

She has no myofascial pain or discomfort. No TMJ symptoms. Joints function smoothly. Incisal opening is about 59 mm. Excursive movement to the left about 11 mm and to the right about 9 mm. 

Oropharynx appears clear. 

MacKensie has relatively moderate-size turbinates and appears to have good nasal airway with it being greater on the right side as compared to the left because of a slight deviation of the nasal septum to the left side. She has a wide columella base, which causes some constricture of her nose. Opening up the nostrils enabled her to breathe a little bit easier. 

Radiographic Evaluation

Radiographic evaluation shows the absence of all four third molars. Sinuses look relatively clear. Nasal airway looks clear. Condyles appears a little bit flattened on the anterior aspects but otherwise look fine. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows some significant flattening on the anterior head of the condyle. Slightly decreased joint space posteriorly. Significant depression at the base of the articular eminence where most likely the posterior band sits. Articular eminence is moderately steep and fairly long in length. 

Left TMJ tomogram likewise shows some significant flattening on the head of the condyle. Articular eminence has a moderate slope to it. There is significant depression at the base of the articular eminence and probably where the posterior band is sitting. There is slightly decreased posterior joint space. She does have a rudimentary impacted maxillary left third molar.

Opening views show good translation of the condyles well beyond the articular eminences bilaterally.

Waters view shows sinuses look relatively clear. Some deviation of the nasal septum is probably present. Turbinates appear moderate in size with probably better airway on the right side as compared to the left side.

Cephalometric evaluation shows that she does have her head tipped up slightly. Has vertical maxillary excess. Some difference of vertical height through the inferior borders of the mandible and vertical height difference at the occlusal surface area. Turbinates do not look particularly enlarged. 

Tooth-size analysis reveals that there is a 3-mm discrepancy beween the upper and lower anterior teeth. 

Basic Diagnoses

Vertical maxillary excess.
Slight AP maxillary deficiency.
AP mandibular deficiency.
Class II end-on occlusion (2 to 3 mm).
Anterior deep bites of 6 mm.
Mandibular anterior teeth occluding on the palate.
Reverse curve of occlusion, upper arch.
Slightly accentuated curve of occlusion, lower arch.
Tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of 3 mm.
Mandibular arch asymmetry with the right cuspid being 1 to 2 mm posterior to the left cuspid.
Mild crowding, lower anterior arch.
Mandibular dental midline shifted off to the right of the mandibular midline.
Vertical inclination of maxillary incisors.
Slight over-inclination of mandibular incisors.
Relatively hypertrophied turbinates.
Wide columella.
External nasal deformity.

Recommended Treatment

Presurgical orthodontics
Align and level maxillary arch. Open up 1 mm of space around each of the upper lateral incisors.
Align and level mandibular arch. Strip lower anterior teeth removing about 1.5 mm of tooth structure. 
To correct mandibular arch asymmetry, use some Class II elastics on the left side and Class III elastics on the right side to try to shift the right cuspid forward a millimeter and the right cuspid posteriorly the same amount. This should require probably just a couple of months of elastics. Objective would be to get the lower dental midline directly onto the chin midline and to align it with the maxillary midline.

Surgery
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the maxilla superiorly approximately 6 mm.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible forward about 5 or 6 mm.
Partial inferior turbinectomies.
Surgical removal of maxillary left impacted third molar.
External nasal reconstruction including columella reconstruction.

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

Restorative dentistry to place bonding or veneers on the maxillary lateral incisors

original_note:
CHANEL RAFIE							07/25/2007

Chanel is 17 years old, referred by Dr. Allen Gandy, 8756 Teel Parkway, Ste. 336, Frisco, TX 75034.  

PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Crossbites.
TMJ problems. 

She desires to have a straight bite and the underbite corrected and have her teeth fit together properly.  Chanel does have some facial asymmetry involving particularly the mandible.  This has gotten a little more prominent as she has gotten older. 

In frontal view she has fairly good symmetry in the middle and upper third face.  Upper lip length is 17 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 1 to 2 mm.  Maxillary midline is about 1 mm off to the right side.  Mandibular dental midline is probably about 4 to 5 mm off to the right and chin is probably about 6 to 7 mm off to the right side.  In profile view, Chanel has some deficiency in the maxillary projection probably about at least 5 to 6 mm.  The chin appears to be in fairly good AP balance.  The upper lip filled out and the chin actually appears to have fairly good AP balance.  Chin-neckline and chin-neck angle appear fairly well defined.  Intraorally, Chanel has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars.  Teeth appear fairly well aligned and level.  The right cuspid appears to be about 3 mm posterior to the left cuspid.  In the upper arch Chanel has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars.  In the upper arch the right cuspid is about 1 mm posterior to the left cuspids.  Intraorally, Chanel has a full Class III occlusion the left side of about 7 mm, on the right side it side it is about 5 mm.  She has a 1 mm vertical overbite anteriorly.  She has an anterior in the right posterior crossbite.  There appears to be a posterior yaw in both the maxilla and mandible of probably around 2 to 3 mm off towards the left side.  The uvula, soft palate, faucial pillars and tonsils appear relatively normal.  Intranasally, the turbinates appear mild to moderately enlarged.  The septum appears fairly normal though it may be slightly deviated to the left.  There may be just some mild nasal airway obstruction.  She may have some mild snoring.  No daytime somnolence except when she stays up until 4 in the morning studying.  No history of sleep apnea and no sleep disorder.  Of interest is that Chanel's mother had a similar kind of jaw deformity.  Her lower jaw was set backwards 13 mm on one side and 11 mm on the other side plus she apparently had a chin reduction performed as well.  To palpation, she has no tenderness around the head and neck area.  On opening, she has subluxation of the condyles particularly on the right side and upon closing there is a definite click in the left joint of probably about 25 to 30 mm.  On the right side there also could be a click but not as discernible as the left side.  Maximal incisal opening is about 55 mm.  Excursive movements are about 8 mm in each direction.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION: 
Panorex shows that she has 4 developing third molars with the uppers impacted and the lowers may be partially erupted or soon to erupt but not enough room for them to erupt completely.  The sinuses appear relatively clear.  The condylar heads and necks appear elongated. 







 CHANEL RAFIE		07/26/2007				Page 2
 

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears to be posteriorly positioned in the fossa.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  Some increased anterior joint space compatible with anteriorly dislocated disc.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears to be posteriorly positioned in the fossa.  The articular eminence is moderately steep and there is increased anterior joint space.  On opening there is good translation of both joints.

Waters view shows that the bony septum is at least relatively straight.  The turbinates appear fairly large and appear to obliterate most of the nasal airway.  Sinuses are clear.  Frontal sinuses are fairly large.   

BASIC DIAGNOSES: 
Bilateral condylar hyperplasia being worse on the left side.
Bilateral TMJ medial displacement of the articular disc. 
Deviated mandibular prognathism. 
AP maxillary deficiency. 
Class III occlusion being worse on the left side as compared to right side. 
Anterior and right posterior crossbite.
Impacted third molars x4.
Hypertrophied turbinates.
Nasal airway obstruction. 
 External nasal deformity.
 Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2 mm.  

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT: 
Presurgical orthodontics.
Continue to align and level maxillary arch.  Open a slight space between the upper lateral incisor and cuspid to help correct for the tooth size discrepancy.  The space does not need to be more than about ½ mm.
Align and level mandibular arch.  Continue to align and level the mandibular arch.
Assess to determine if active condylar hyperplasia is still present. 
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ high condylectomies (if condylar hyperplasia is confirmed) and articular disc repositioning with Mitek anchors.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to set the mandible back on the facial midline and level it transversely. 
Maxillary osteotomies to advance it about 5 to 6 mm. and level it transversely. 
Bilateral inferior turbinectomies.
Surgical removal of impacted third molars x4.
Consider vertical reduction genioplasty.
Consider external rhinoplasty. 
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B190
B190 is 20 years old and referred by Dr. XXXX and Dr. XXXX. She is currently seeing Dr. XXXX for orthodontic treatment. B190 began having TMJ problems at about the age of 12 of unknown etiology. She did have an MRI when she was 14 that showed she had an anteriorly displaced disc on the right side with some arthritis and nonreducing discs. Prior to the onset of her TMJ problems, she did have phase 1 orthodontics from about the age of 9 to 12 years. She was treated by Dr. XXXX, whom the family is very pleased with and he apparently did a palatal expansion and she was then put on hold for orthodontics because of her TMJ problems. She wore head gear for almost 2 years. She then basically was put on hold orthodontically because of her TMJ issues and did not finally get to wearing braces again until about 18 months ago. In the meantime, she has had some ongoing TMJ treatment. She had splints x2 from about the age of 13 to 15. Five years ago, she had arthrocentesis to the right joint only. She has had an electrophoresis and NSAIDS. With the arthrocentesis she had 5 years ago, she had steroids injected in the joint. She began her orthodontic treatment with Dr. XXXX 18 months ago and just this past month transferred to Dr. XXXX.

B190 takes the following medications:
Nexium 20 mg b.i.d. Synthroid 50 mcg q.d.
Singulair 10 mg q.h.s.
Allegra 180 mg q.d.
Celebrex 100 mg b.i.d. Nasonex nasal spray.
Vicodin 5 mg q.h.s.
Skelaxin q.h.s.

B190 rates her headaches that she gets in the temporal area only at a level of 1. She rates her TMJ pain at 6, average daily pain at 4, jaw function, diet, and disability all at 4. She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, nor hearing loss. The only joint that bothers her is her right shoulder. She does have the following medical conditions:

GERD.
Chronic sinusitis.
Hypothyroidism.
TMJ problems.
She is aware of clenching and bruxing her teeth at night.
Primary concerns that B190 has include the following:

TMJ pain.
Bite position is off.
Difficulties eating and chewing.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
In frontal view, B190 has fairly good symmetry in the middle and upper third face. Upper lip length is 21 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is about 3 mm. The maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline. The mandibular dental midline is about 1 mm off to the right and chin may be off 2-3 mm off to the right. In profile, B190 has about 4 mm of AP maxillary deficiency and probably about 6 mm of AP mandibular deficiency, although her jaw structures are in reasonably good balance. Chin necklines and chin neck angle appear relatively normal. Intraorally, she has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. She has lingual braces in the upper and lower arch. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. In centric relation, her lower dental midline is 2 mm off towards the right side and the chin is probably 3 to 4 mm off to the right side. She has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship on the right side being probably close to 4 mm Class II and on the left side she is probably 1-2 mm Class II. She has about a 1 mm overbite relationship and probably 2 mm overjet. She has an almost 3 mm transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right side being 3 mm higher than the left side. She has lingual and buccal tads in and around the first molar area. Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively normal in architecture and texture. Tonsils are mildly enlarged. She has had previous nasal surgery to treat her chronic sinus problems and she has had a partial reduction of the turbinates, but it looks like it was just the anterior part that was addressed and not the middle or posterior part. Apparently, she had some work done on her septum as well. B190 has had her wisdom teeth removed. To palpation, B190 has high moderate pain over the TMJs and moderate pain over the masseters, retromandibular area, mild tenderness over the temporalis and shoulders. Incisal opening is 51 mm and she can open 29 mm reasonably comfortably. Excursion movements are 8 mm in each direction.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. Turbinates appear moderate in size. The right joint looks somewhat arthritic. The septum appears reasonably straight. Coronal view shows the presence of tads in the maxilla. Sinuses look clear. Turbinates are quite large and appear to block a good portion of the airway. Axial view shows that the turbinates are quite large. Sinuses are clear. Left TMJ sagittal tomograms show some arthritic changes associated with the condyle. The articular eminence is moderately steep. Joint space looks reasonably good, just some slight decreased posterior joint space. Right TMJ sagittal tomograms show that there is some slight arthritic changes that have occurred in the condyle with some loss of vertical volume as well as some anterior beaking. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width. Joint space appears fairly equal and balance, although could be slightly decreased vertically. Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is smaller in size than the left one with decreased vertical height and mediolateral width. Joint space actually looks fairly good. Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has over-angulation of the lower incisors but appears to have a Class III skeletal and fairly close to a Class I occlusal relationship. Occlusal plan may be must moderately high. Oropharyngeal airway measures about 7 mm at the soft palate level and probably 7-8 mm at the base of the tongue. There is a tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2 mm.

Basic diagnoses for B190 would be as follows:
Right TMJ reactive arthritis with articular disc dislocation without reduction and with significant degenerative changes.
Left TMJ mild arthritis and articular disc anteriorly dislocated with early reduction.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia.
Maxillary AP and posterior vertical hypoplasia. Transverse cant in the occlusal plan with the right side being 3 mm higher than the left side. Class II end-on occlusion being worse on the right side as compared to the left side. Mandibular dental midline shifted to the right 2 mm. Hypertrophied turbinates. Nasal airway obstruction. Decreased oropharyngeal airway. TMJ pain. Myofascial pain. Tooth size discrepancy with 2+ mm Bolton's difference.
Over angulation of lower incisors.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
Presurgical orthodontics. Continue to align and level maxillary arch. Continue to align and level mandibular arch. Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing at least 2 mm of tooth structure to correct for a tooth size discrepancy as well as to decrease the over angulated lower anterior teeth. Surgery Stage 1: Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Presurgical orthodontics: Continue with the alignment and leveling of the arches. Surge Stage II:
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla, down graft it slightly posterior, and correct for the asymmetry. Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to correct the facial asymmetry and advance the mandible.
Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B190 MRI AGE: 20/F
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is moderate in width. It may have lost some vertical height. Lateral rim of the fossa is fairly flat with only about a 2-mm lip. There appears to be decreased vertical joint space. The disc may be anteriorly and medially displaced.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is quite small in size. It appears to have lost vertical volume. There is decreased vertical joint space and some areas that appear relatively normal. Lateral rim of the fossa is fairly flat, curving inferiorly only about 1-2 mm.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that actually relatively normal in height. There is flattening on the anterior head. There is perhaps the beginning of some slight anterior beaking. The articular disc actually looks as though it is in reasonably good position. Articular eminence is significantly shallow. The disc appears somewhat degenerated up in the fossa. On opening, there appears to be good translation of the condyle and the disc with the disc in relatively normal position. It is possible the disc could be anteriorly displaced at the lateral aspect of the joint.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that appears a little bit shorter in vertical height than the opposite side. There is definite flattening on the head of the condyle and a little bit of sclerosis. There is some anterior beaking present. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Articular disc is anteriorly displaced and sitting beneath the articular eminence and it is more evident at the lateral aspect of the joint than medial. On opening, the articular disc remains anteriorly displaced and nonreducing. The disc is foreshortened anteroposteriorly and is thicker than normal.

IMPRESSION:
Left TMJ relatively normal joint architecture with some mild arthritic changes of the condyle and possible anterior dislocation at the lateral aspect of the joint. Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly displaced without reduction.

original_note:
CHELSEA NUNEZ									05/19/2008

Chelsea is 13 years old.  Birth date is 09/15/1994.  Referred by Dr. Thomas Czarnecki, 754 N. Carroll Avenue, South Lake, TX 76092.  Chelsea’s parents became aware that her lower jaw was out in front of her upper jaw about 3 years ago.  She has been in orthodontic treatment for 2 years now.  There has been no real plan as to Chelsea’s management.  To date, she has had a palatal expander to the expand the maxilla and that expander is still in place.  She has orthodontic appliances on.  
She does get some TMJ pain at about a level of 3 and that discomfort usually occurs on the right side. She rates her jaw function at 8, diet and disability at 5.  She has no problems with headaches, tinnitus, vertigo, or hearing loss.  She gets an occasional earache on the left side.  She has no other joints that bother her.  She is not aware of clenching or bruxing.  
CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Chelsea has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 19 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 1 mm.  The maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline as is the mandibular dental midline and chin.  In profile, she has some maxillary deficiency of probably 3 mm or so.  Chin appears somewhat prominent by about 4 mm.  Chin neckline looks relatively normal in length.  The lower lip is a little bit prominent compared to the chin and particularly compared to the upper lip.  Intraorally, she has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars.  The lower second bicuspids are tipped a little bit lingually.  She has an accentuated curve of Wilson on the posterior aspect.  She also has an accentuated curve of Spee anteriorly.  She appears to have adequate attached gingiva.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth erupted back through the first molar on the right side and left side.  She has a palatal expander.  She has a significantly increased curve of Wilson in the upper arch in the posterior teeth area.  Left cuspid is down in position, but the right cuspid is still elevated about 5 mm.  She has a full Class III cuspid relationship on the left side and a crossbite tendency.  She has a huge anterior crossbite of probably close to 10 mm.  On the right side, she has a posterior crossbite and has probably a full Class III cuspid relationship there as well.  She has an anterior open bite of probably about 4 mm.  Her lower arch is fairly level.  In the maxillary arch on the right side, the right lateral incisor may be slightly elevated compared to the left lateral incisor.  She does have a reverse overjet of about 9 mm.  Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively normal.  Tonsils are mildly to moderately enlarged.  Intranasally, the turbinates appear moderately enlarged, although she claims no difficulties breathing through her nose.  Columella is somewhat wide and nostrils are somewhat narrow.  Chelsea says that when she sleeps she postures her tongue between her front teeth.  To palpation, she has some mild to moderate discomfort over the masseter muscles, TMJs, and styloid areas, and minimal tenderness over the posterior neck and temporalis area.  Joints seem to function relatively smoothly.  Maximal opening is 52 mm.  Excursion movements are about 7 mm in each direction.  
RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the presence of four impacted third molars and impacted maxillary second molars.  Maxillary right cuspid is not fully erupted.  The vertical height of the posterior mandible is quite short.  The condylar head and neck length are excessive.  Sinuses look relatively clear.  
Waters view shows that sinuses appear clear.  Turbinates appear fairly enlarged.  The bony septum appears reasonably straight.  
Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle and condylar neck that are excessively long.  Joint space is thin.  There appears to be a little bit of anterior beaking on the condyle.  Articular eminence is quite shallow.    
Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is a little bit small in size.  The condylar head and neck appear longer than normal.  Joint space is somewhat narrow.  Articular eminences are relatively shallow.  Condylar head and neck length is elongated.  On opening, there is fairly good translation of both condyles.  
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she occludes only on the maxillary first molars and mandible second molars.  The reverse overjet is almost 10 mm.  The oropharyngeal airway dimensions are normal.  Chelsea’s speech is affected quite considerably because of the severity of the jaw and dental malalignment. 
Basic diagnoses for Chelsea would be as follows:
Bilateral mandibular condylar hyperplasia.
Mandible prognathism.
AP maxillary hypoplasia. 
Transverse maxillary hyperplasia.
Class III occlusion. 
Anterior and bilateral posterior crossbites.
Decreased curve of Wilson in the lower arch including the bicuspids and molars.
Excessive curve of Wilson in the upper arch with significant buccal tipping of the teeth.
Retained maxillary palate expander appliance.
Anterior overbite. 
Occluding only on the maxillary first molars and mandibular second molars. 
Impacted third molars x4.
Bilateral maxillary unerupted second molars. 
Maxillary right cuspid positioned high and labially. 

Recommended treatment would be as follows:	
 
Presurgical orthodontics.
Removal of maxillary palatal expander and removal of other appliances if a different orthodontic system is preferred.
Align and level maxillary arch.  Try to decrease the transverse intramolar and bicuspid dimension by trying to tip the crowns of the teeth lingually to decrease the curve of Wilson.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies.
Align and level mandibular arch.  Try to tip the molars and bicuspids labially to expand the arch and decrease the excessive curve of Wilson.
Surgery.  
Bilateral mandibular high condylectomies to stop the accelerated growth process in the mandible.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to set the mandible posteriorly.  
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla and correct any transverse discrepancies.
Surgical removal of impacted third molars x4. 
3.   Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
