original_note:
PAMELA PETROFF									9/20/05

Pamela's problems may have begun back in 1986 when she was involved in an MVA and sustained a whiplash injury.  Two years ago, she fell at a Walgreens and injured her shoulder and apparently broke her arm and was not diagnosed for sometime and then she injured her shoulder.  Although she was involved in an auto accident in 1986, she really did not become aware of having significant head and neck problems until about 2000 when she developed a fairly significant earache.  She was treated for 9 months for her earache by ENT.  In the spring of 2001, she had severe earaches and pain in the right side of her jaw.  Since then the pain has progressed to include the left side of her jaw and now the left-sided pain is significantly worse than the right side.  In 2001, Dr. Ken Rotskoff injected botox into her on several occasions primarily into the muscles.  She went to another oral surgeon and had bilateral TMJ lavages done x2 and her problems continued to get worse.  She had repeated muscle injections done in different areas of her head and neck area without relief.  Dr. Duane Keller made her an orthotic in June of 2004 and she has worn that until more recently.  It has not worked and she currently cannot open wide enough to even consider putting the device in.  She had additional muscle injections by another doctor during that time.  She has developed an abscessed tooth, #10 and has a draining fistula associated with it.  She is to get root canal therapy done on that tooth sometime this week.  She originally had a root canal done on that tooth 37 years ago.  She has daytime somnolence that is very significant and she does not sleep very much but maybe an hour and a half at night.    

She now has a frozen left shoulder and she cannot straighten out her elbow.  She has the following concerns and problems:

Her jaws are frozen.
Headaches.
TMJ pain.
Myofascial pain.
Severe difficulty in eating and chewing.
Sleep disorder.
Significant weight loss.  

She rates her headaches at a level of 4 but gets headaches to a level of 10 and they occur about 2 or 3 days a week.  She rates her TMJ pain at 10, overall facial pain and headaches at 10, jaw function at 10, diet at 10 and disability at 10.  She has virtually no jaw movement at all.  To palpation she has no tenderness around the temporalis masseters, TMJs or posterior neck area.  She currently takes the following medications:

Imitrex.
Dalmane 30 mg q.h.s.
Serzone 200 mg q.h.s.
Demerol 50 mg for pain.
PAMELA PETROFF				9/20/05				Page 2


Other joints that bother her include her back at L4 and L5 and she has a bone spur at C5.  Her left shoulder is frozen, left elbow does not function well and her fingers on her left hand do not work well.  She is allergic to morphine, codeine, Ultram and Augmentin and she is lactose intolerant.   She does have Crohn's disease.  She has gone from 92 pounds down to 85 pounds.  

She had parakeets for about 25 years and a number of different ones as some would die and be replaced.  She had a cockatiel for 5 years.  She has had no birds for about the last 10 years.  She had cats and they did eat birds.  She has not had cats for the past 12 years.  She has had some dogs but none were interested in the birds.  No exposure to hoofed animals.  She does not give much of a history of urinary tract infections but has had 2 this past year.  She has had bronchitis x1.  She has had pneumonia x6 and was told that they were a viral pneumonia.  The last one required hospitalization.  Her Crohn's disease was diagnosed in 1994 and she says it is currently in remission.  She has had herpes that affected her esophagus and was treated for this and has had no recurrence.

In her photographs, she has a retruded maxilla and mandible but she reports no evidence of snoring or sleep apnea and has no interest at this time except to eliminate her severe pain and regain jaw function.  

BASIC DIAGNOSES 
Bilateral TMJ reactive arthritis and articular disc dislocations without reduction and with severe adhesions.  
Severe TMJ pain.
Headaches.
Severe masticatory dysfunction with only able to take a liquid diet.
Sleep disorder secondary to pain.
Crohn's disease.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT
Place orthodontic appliances on teeth.
CT scan TMJ Concepts protocol.
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses.
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from abdomen or buttock.)
Application of maxillary mandibular arch wires (only if orthodontic appliances cannot be done.)
      4.  Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B025
B025 is 13 years old. Referred by Dr. XXXX. B025 is here with the primary concerns of:
Misaligned teeth and jaw.
Overbite. Difficulties breathing through the nose.

B025 is unsure when any jaw joints problems began and etiology is unknown. She had previous orthodontics from about the second grade through the fifth grade (approximately age 7 to 10 years old) where she had expanders, head gear for 1-1/2 years, and braces by a Dr. XXXX. She moved back about 5 years ago and has been under the care of Dr. XXXX.

B025 gets an occasional headache in the frontal and temporal area at about a level of 3. She has no overt TMJ pain or pain around the head and neck area. She rates her jaw function, diet, and disability at 0. She has no problems with earaches, but has some moderate tinnitus and some vertigo, particularly when she stands up or gets up from lying down. No other joints bother her. She is not aware of clenching or bruxism.

In frontal view, B025 has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 20 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is 5 mm is pretty much on the facial midline. The nasal tip is deviated just slightly to the left side. The mandibular dental midline is about 2 mm to the left. The chin is slightly to the left. The mandibular dental midline and chin midline are fairly well aligned with each other. In profile view, upper lip supralabrale is posterior to subnasale. The maxilla is probably about 5 mm AP deficient. The lower third face is elongated vertically. She has some lip incompetence of probably close to 8 mm. The chin is retruded about 20 mm. The chin neckline is short, although the chin neck angle is reasonably well defined. Intraorally, B025 has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. She appears to have adequate attached gingiva. Teeth seem to be nicely aligned and leveled. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. She has an accentuated curve of Spee in the upper arch. She has about a 2 mm over bite relationship and about a 4 mm Class II elastic on the right side and 5 mm on the left side. Oropharyngeal tissues look healthy. Tonsils are mild to moderately enlarged. Intranasally, the turbinates are moderately enlarged at least anteriorly. She has a wide columella and narrow nostrils. To palpation, she has no tenderness around the head and neck area. On opening, she has a very soft click in the left joint about 30 mm and a closing click about 20 mm. The right side seemed to function a little bit more smoothly. The incisal opening is 51 mm, excursion movement to the right side about 8 mm, to the left side about 5 mm.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the presence of four developing but unerupted third molars. The rest of the teeth are all present. The right sinus looks clear, but the left sinus may have fluid mass in the sinus. Joints look somewhat arthritic and condyles appear small. When the x-ray was taken, apparently she was posturing her mandible forward because in the left joint she has a significantly increased superior and posterior joint space and the condyle is postured downward on the articular eminence. The articular eminence is fairly steep. The condyle is small in size and has a little bit of hockey stick shape to it. Right TMJ likewise is postured down and forward not quite as much as the opposite side. The articular eminence is steep, but not quite as steep as the opposite side. There is an increased superior and posterior joint space.

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has moderately good width and the condyle may be a little bit short in vertical dimension. The lateral rim curves inferiorly 3 to 4 mm. There is some decreased joint space at the lateral aspect of the joint.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that may have lost some vertical volume. The lateral rim curves inferiorly about 4 mm. There is decreased lateral joint space.

Coronal radiograph shows that the turbinates are moderate in size with the left one being excessively large. There is a significant fluid mass in the left sinus whereas the right sinus is reasonably clear with just some thickening of the sinus membrane lining at the floor of the sinus.

Axial view shows that the turbinates are moderately enlarged, particularly the left one is quite enlarged. The left sinus is pretty much filled with fluid.

Basic diagnoses for B025 would be as follows:
Bilateral TMJ adolescent internal condylar resorption (AICR).
Maxillary AP and posterior vertical hypoplasia, but anterior vertical hyperplasia.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia.
AP microgenia.
Class II occlusion. High occlusal plane angle of 21 degrees (normal 8 degrees).
Decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Hypertrophied turbinates. Wide columella and narrow nostrils. Nasal airway obstruction. Impacted third molars x4. Mucous retention cyst versus fluid in the left maxillary sinus.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1. MRI of left and right TMJs.
2. Presurgical orthodontics. a.	Align and level maxillary arch. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. b.	Align and level mandibular arch. Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing about 2 mm of tooth structure and retract.
3. Surgery. a.	Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors. b.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible forward in a counterclockwise direction. c.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance it, move the anterior aspect superior, and posterior aspect inferiorly as well as decrease the occlusal plane angle. d.	Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies. e.	Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy. f.	Surgical removal of impacted third molars. g.	Removal of mucous retention cyst left sinus. h.	Columella reconstruction.
4. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B025 MRI
Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that looks a little large for the fossa. The articular eminence is moderately steep. The disc is anteriorly displaced, more so at the lateral aspect of the joint.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is a little bit smaller in size. The articular disc appears anteriorly displaced at the lateral aspect of the joint, but more medially is in better position.

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good medial width. Vertical joint space appears narrow. It appears that there may be some discal tissue anterior to the condyle. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 mm. The left articular eminence is fairly steep and somewhat elongated. On opening, there is good translation of the condyle and articular disc and the disc is in a reduced position on wide opening.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears fairly large with good mediolateral width. The disc appears to be medially displaced. There appears to be some decreased lateral joint space. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 mm. The lateral pole appears to extend beyond the rim of the fossa. There is fairly good translation of the right disc and condyle with the disc going back into a reduced position fairly early on in the opening process.

IMPRESSION:
Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly displaced with reduction on opening. Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly displaced but with early reduction.

original_note:
PAUL MARROQUIN								01/03/2007

Paul is 23 years old and is referred by Dr. Mike Pipper via Dr. Robert Marroquin, his uncle, who is a dentist.  Paul had primary repair on the lip at roughly 6 months of age and then the palate was repaired roughly at a year.  Paul then had a bone graft to the maxillary alveolar cleft at around the age of 10.  These procedures were done by Dr. Ken Sayler.

In frontal view, Paul has repair in the upper left lip.  The vermillion on the left side is slightly short in vertical height by probably about 2 mm compared to the right side.  The cupids bow actually is fairly well formed and shaped.  The scar on the lip looks fairly nice with just a little bit of width to it.  Nasal tip is off to the right side by probably a couple of mm.  Mandibular dental midline is about 1 mm to the left and the chin is probably about 3 mm to the left.  Maxillary dental midline is 2 mm to the left of facial midline.  Reevaluation of the mandibular dental midline actually is fairly close to the facial midline and the chin is just slightly off to the left side maybe 2 mm.  Nasal tip is off to the right 2 mm.  He has differential heights of the alar domes with the left side being probably about 2 to 3 mm lower than the right side.  The alar base on the left side is wide compared to the right side and the nostril sill is much wider on the left side as compared to the right side by probably 6 mm.  Upper lip length is about 21 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 2 mm.  In profile view, Paul has fairly good prominence of the upper lip and lower lip.  Chin may be retruded just slightly.  He has a little bit of a high mandibular plane angle and perhaps may have a moderately high occlusal plane angle as well.  He has about a 90 to 100 degree nasolabial angle.  Chin-neckline may be just slightly short although the chin-neck angle is well defined.  Intraorally, Paul has all of his lower teeth back through the second molars.  He has some lower arch asymmetry with the left cuspid being about almost 2 mm posterior to the right cuspid.  He has good attached gingiva.  There is some very minor crowding in the lower arch.  In the upper arch he has all of his teeth on the right side but is missing the left lateral incisor but has the rest of the teeth back through the second molars.  He has an implant and crown replacing the maxillary left lateral incisor.  Paul has a slight Class III cuspid relationship bilaterally by about 2 mm.  He has an end-on incisor relationship.  He has a small upper lateral incisor and the upper left central incisor crown may be a little bit smaller than the right side.  He has a crossbite tendency in the bicuspid areas on the right side and on the left side a little tendency towards that along the entire left side.  He has a 0 overbite relationship.  His palate has been repaired.  There is no uvula.  The soft palate appears quite short in length.  Tonsils are relatively normal or small in size.  Intranasally, the septum as best I can see it appears reasonably straight.  The turbinates, however, are quite enlarged.  On the left side particularly, the turbinate is very boggy and appears to almost abut against the nasal septum.  On the right side, the turbinate is quite large but there may be some air passage.  Paul is not aware of having any problems with snoring.  Sometimes he is tired during the day but he is not aware of any sleep apnea problems.  To palpation, the joints function relatively smoothly.  Paul exhibits velopharyngeal incompetency on most all speech sounds.  He has nasal emission on all the speech sounds.  Transverse occlusal plane is actually fairly level except for the left central and lateral which are up a little bit higher than the bicuspid and the right incisors. 

Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars.  He is also missing maxillary left lateral incisor and has an osteointegrated implant with a crown to replace the missing tooth.  He has a 
3 x 3 retainer in the lower arch.  Sinuses look relatively clear.  
PAUL MARROQUIN			01/03/2007				Page 2


Waters view shows that the sinuses are clear.  He has a significantly deviated nasal septum with it going off towards the left side and he has a spur coming off the bony septum but it impinges into the turbinate.  This is probably causing the airway obstruction particularly on the left side of the nose.  Turbinates are hard to identify but clinically appear significant enlarged. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle with a little flattening on the posterior head but good joint space and articular eminences are moderately steep.  

Left TMJ shows a condyle with similar morphology and good space and moderately inclined articular eminences.  There is good translation in both joints.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that he has a skeletal Class III relationship.  Vertical height of the mandible appears elongated.  Soft palate is extremely short.  He has some AP maxillary deficiency.  Upper incisors appear somewhat over angulated.  There is a tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 3 mm.  

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
Repaired unilateral left cleft lip and palate. 
AP maxillary deficiency. 
Mandibular prognathism.
Maxillary asymmetry with the dental midline shifted 2 mm to the left. 
Maxillary arch asymmetry with the left cuspid being 2 to 3 mm posterior to the right cuspid.
Mandibular arch asymmetry with the left cuspid being 2 mm posterior to the right cuspid.
Crowding in the lower arch particularly on the right side. 
End-on incisor relationship.
Class III occlusal relationship by 2 to 3 mm. 
 Tendency towards posterior crossbites.
 Velopharyngeal incompetency.  
 Extremely short soft palate.
 Cleft lip deformity. 
 External nasal deformity. 
 Hypertrophied turbinates. 
 Nasal septal deviation with septal spurring off to the left side.
 Nasal airway obstruction particularly on the left side. 
 Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2.5 mm. 

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch.  Tip the right maxillary lateral incisor root mesially and cuspid root distally to make room for interdental osteotomies.  


PAUL MARROQUIN			01/03/2007				Page 3


Align and level mandibular arch.  Distalize mandibular left cuspid about 1 mm if possible.  Slenderize the posterior on the right side including the mesial of the second molar, mesial and distal aspect of the first molar and both bicuspids as well as the cuspid to improve the arch symmetry.  Consider using Class III elastics on that side during the orthodontics to maximize posterior anchorage so the right mandibular cuspid can be distalized 1 to 2 mm.  Slenderize the lower incisors removing about 1 mm of tooth structure to help compensate for the tooth size discrepancy. 
Surgery.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance and expand.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to set the mandible posteriorly slightly with a slight counterclockwise rotation.
Nasal septoplasty.
Partial turbinectomies.
Consider vertical reduction genioplasty.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
Restorative dentistry to build up the crown on the maxillary left lateral incisor implant. 
Surgery stage 2.
Lip revision. 
External rhinoplasty with extensive tip work.
Consider possible pharyngeal flap if speech worsens from surgery stage 1.

COURTNEY HUNT									11-24-04

Patient History

Courtney’s problems began when she was a young teenager, and she was aware of clicking and popping in her jaw joints at that time. The etiology is unknown. On her way home from school Thanksgiving of 2003, her right jaw joint locked up. The patient treatment she has had since then included physical therapy for four weeks with no improvement. She has worn a splint, but this has not improved her situation either. These are the treatments she has had so far for her current condition. She previously had orthodontics for about 10 months at the age of 15 to 16. She has had her third molars extracted. 

Courtney is unaware of any changes in her occlusion, although she is aware that when she opens her lower jaw shifts off toward the right side. Courtney has no problems with headaches. She rates her TMJ pain at a level of 7 particularly during function. Her average daily pain is at a level of 6, jaw function and diet at 7, and disability at 4. 

Current Medications

Naproxen 500 mg 1 to 2 q.d. 
Lexapro 10 mg q.d. 
Mircette (BCP)
Toprol XL 25 mg q.d. 

Patient Concerns (in no specific order)

Right joint locks
Right-sided earache that radiates down into the jaws
Jaw pain
Jaw joint pain
Sleeping problems related to the pain
Talking creates pain
Difficulties eating and chewing

Clinical Examination

To palpation, Courtney has some mild to moderate pain in the right masseter muscle and the right styloid areas. Doesn’t have any significant tenderness directly over the joints at this time. On opening, she has clicking in her left joint with an opening click at about 15 mm and closing click at about 10 mm. In the right joint, she has some crepitus type of sounds. 

Upper lip length is about 20 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 2 mm. She has good left-to-right facial symmetry. The dental midlines and chin midline appear fairly well with the facial midline. 

In profile, she has fairly good facial balance. Some slight retrusion of the maxilla and mandible. Chin-neckline is a little bit decreased in length.

Intraorally, Courtney has all of her teeth back through the second molars present in the lower arch. Has good attached gingiva. In the upper arch, she all of her teeth back through the second molars present. She has a Class I cuspid-molar relationship and about a 2-mm overbite relationship anteriorly. Maximal incisal opening is about 27 mm, and she can open about 23 mm without significant discomfort. Excursive movements to the right are about 8 mm and to the left 7 mm. She had crepitation in the right joint on excursive movements. 

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. The rest of the teeth are in place. A little calcification occurring between the mandibular right bicuspids. Sinuses appear relatively clear. She has a nasal stud present on the right side. Right condyle appears to be somewhat arthritic with some peaking of the condyle and some flattening on the anterior head. The left side shows some minor changes but appears more normal. Articular eminences are moderately steep.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows basically a skeletal and occlusal Class I relationship. Oropharyngeal airway looks good. Relatively normal occlusal plane angle. 

Waters view shows the sinuses are clear. Septum may have some deviation to it. Turbinate on the right side appears moderately large. 

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle with a hockey stick shape to it. Posterior joint space and superior joint space are decreased. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Anterior aspect of the condyle is flattened.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is a little bit smaller in size then the opposite side. The cortical bone is a little bit more difficult to identify. Condyle is posteriorly positioned in the fossa. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Increased anterior joint space.

On opening, there moderate restriction of translation in both joints, a little bit greater on the right side than the left side. 

Basic Diagnoses

Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation without reduction and a closed lock situation
Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with reduction
Right earaches
TMJ pain
Myofascial pain
Sleep disorder
Masticatory dysfunction

Recommended Treatment
Orthodontics
Apply appliances to maxillary teeth
Apply appliances to mandibular teeth. Can use bonded brackets

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors 
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to maintain occlusion and create space in the joints for the discs 

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

ANNE LANGHAM									12/29/2008

Anne is 41 years old, referred by Dr. Duane Keller.  Anne is in good health except for her jaw joint problems.  She takes Aleve and Advil during the day for pain.  

Her TMJ problems began at an unknown age, but by 25 she was aware of joint issues.  The etiology is unknown.  She has had splint therapy in the past from about 1995.  She wore a splint from about 1995 to 1997 and then had some improvement at that time.  This past year, however, her pain has gotten significantly worse.  She saw Dr. Keller in September 2008 because of severe pain.  He made a splint for her that has helped her some, but she still continues to have significant pain problems.  

Anne rates her TMJ pain at 8, average daily pain at 5, headaches at 5 that occur predominantly in the left temple area.  She rates her jaw function at 9, diet at 7, and disability at 7.  She gets occasional earaches and tinnitus but no vertigo.  No other joints bother her.  She does suffer from mild depression that in part is unrelated to the joint issue.  She finds that she does clench her teeth during the day, but she is not aware of that at night.  

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Anne has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 19 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 2  mm.  The maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline.  Nasal tip is deviated just a bit towards the left side 1 to 2 mm.  The mandibular dental midline is off to the right side about 2 mm.  The mandibular dental midline and chin are fairly well aligned with each other.  Therefore, the chin is also deviated a little bit off towards the right side 2 mm.  In profile view, Anne has fairly good facial balance.  Chin neckline appears relatively normal in length, and chin neck angle appears relatively normal.  Intraorally, Anne has all of her maxillary and mandibular teeth back through the second molars.  There is a slight Class III cuspid relationship on the left side by about 2 mm.  It is closer to Class I on the right side. In the lower arch, the right cuspid is about 1 mm posterior to the left cuspid.  As Anne shifts her mandible off towards the left side 2 mm, it does improve her facial symmetry, and articulating her dental models gets her into a pretty good Class I cuspid-molar relationship bilaterally with the lower dental midline being just 1 mm off towards the right side.  Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively normal, although the uvula is slightly elongated, but not too significant.  Intranasally, she appears to have good airway, but greater on the left side than the right side.  She has an anterior deep bite of about 1 mm.  Maximal opening is 25 mm, opening without severe pain 17 mm.  Excursion movement to the right 3 mm, to the left 4 mm.   
RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Axial views show that the sinuses are clear, but the right turbinate appears quite large.  
Coronal view shows that the left nasal airway appears quite good.  The turbinates are relatively normal in size.  The right turbinate appears enlarged.  
Panorex shows that all 4 third molars are absent, but the rest of the teeth are present.  Sinuses appear relatively clear.  She appears to have significant arthritis in both jaw joints.  
Right TMJ tomograms show that she has severe arthritis in her TMJ joint.  There appears to be significant loss of condylar volume and degenerative changes present.  There is anterior beaking.  There is decreased vertical joint space.  Articular eminence is moderately shallow.  
Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that likewise has undergone significant arthritic changes.  There is flattening on the head of the condyle and some sclerosis there.  The joint space actually appears increased on this side.  There is some anterior beaking present.  The condyle appears to have lost volume.  
Right TMJ coronal view shows significant degenerative changes in the condyle and significant decreased vertical joint space.  The condyle appears to have lost vertical volume.  

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has developed significant degenerative changes.  There is a significant erosion towards the lateral aspect of the condyle and also more over towards the medial side as well. There appears to be some degenerative changes in the fossa as well.  There is decreased vertical joint space particularly at the lateral aspect of the joint.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she is basically a skeletal and occlusal Class I jaw relationship.  There is some decreased oropharyngeal airway, but probably may be postural.  She appears to have her head pulled way back over her body, and her neck appears to be actually retro-inclined.  Airway dimension at the base of the tongue is about 4 mm.  

Basic diagnoses for Anne would be as follows:    

Bilateral TMJ severe arthritis.
Bilateral TMJ severe pain. 
Significant masticatory dysfunction.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Presurgical orthodontics.  
Align and level maxillary arch.  
Align and level mandibular arch.  Slenderize the lower anterior teeth to keep from flaring them too far forward.
CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol). 
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ reconstructions with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. 
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen).
4.   Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
ROXANNE YAHNER							11/11/2011

Roxanne is 59 years old, birth date 09/30/1952.  Referred by Dr. Bill Hang.  Primary concerns that Roxanne has include the following:

Headaches.
Asymmetric face.
Jaw pain.
Jaw malalignment.

I had previously talked with Roxanne over the phone on 08/13/2011.  She is now 3 months since that date.  She rates her headaches at a level of 8 throughout the head, although it varies from area to area.  She rates her TMJ pain at 8, average daily pain at 8, jaw function at 3, diet at 5, disability at 7.  She has no problems with earaches, gets occasional tinnitus, and a moderate occurrence of vertigo that has gotten a little bit worse since she has been wearing a palatal expander device.  She has no hearing loss.  She had 2 life stopping bouts of severe vertigo in the past.  Other joints that bother her include her right hip, lower right back, and right neck.  She is not aware of clenching or bruxing.  She has allergies to olives, grapes, and vinegar.  She really does not have any known allergies to jewelry.  She takes the following prescription medications:

Bio identical hormones.
Estradiol.
Testosterone.
Progesterone.  
Multivitamins.

In frontal view, Roxanne does demonstrate some facial asymmetry.  Upper lip length is about 20 mm on the right and 18 mm on the left.  Upper tooth lip is 1 mm on the left and 0 on the right.  Nasal tip is about 3 mm off to the left side.  The maxillary dental midline is 2 mm off to the left, mandibular dental midline 4 mm to the left, and chin approximately 8 mm off to the left.  The mandibular dental midline and chin midline are fairly well aligned with each other.  There is a transverse cant in the occlusal plan with the left side being 3 to 4 mm higher than the right.  There is a posterior yaw in the face with the right side being about 6 mm more prominent than the left side at the angle area.  There is a posterior yaw in the occlusion with the maxilla and mandible being rotated about 3 mm off towards the right side.  In profile, the upper lip is about 3 to 4 mm AP deficient.  The chin is probably about 4 to 5 mm AP deficient.  Intraorally, Roxanne has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars present.  She has good attached gingiva around the lower anterior arch.  In the upper arch, she is missing the right maxillary central incisor and has the rest of her teeth back through the second molars.  She has an expansion appliance currently in place expanding the upper arch.  She has had the device for about 3 months.  She is fairly close to being Class I cuspid relationship.  She has a 0 vertical overbite anteriorly.  She has a tendency towards a cross bite on the right side.  Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively healthy and normal.  Intranasally, she has some septal spurring anteriorly off to the left and right sides.  The turbinates appear relatively normal in size at least anteriorly.  To palpation, she has moderate pain over the masseters, right TMJ, and moderate pain over the right temporalis.  She states that her pain is worse in the mornings and she has difficulties getting to sleep at night.  The incisal opening is 47 mm.  The excursion movements are 6 mm in each direction.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars.  She has root canals on the mandibular right second molar, maxillary left first molar.  The sinuses look relatively clear.  Joints look reasonably good.  

Coronal view shows that the sinuses are clear.  Turbinates are relatively normal in size.  Slight curvature of the septum off towards the right side.  There is a little bit of increased sinus lining on the floor of the left maxillary sinus.

Axial view shows that the sinuses are clear.  The septum is reasonably straight, although the anterior portion does tip off towards the left side.  The turbinates appear relatively normal in size.  

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle with good morphology with just some slight anterior beaking.  The joint space looks well balanced.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle with some slight anterior beaking.  The joint space appears normal.  

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with fairly good mediolateral width.  There is some decreased lateral joint space particularly towards the posterior aspect of the fossa.

Right TMJ coronal view shows the decreased lateral joint space.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she is basically a skeletal and occlusal Class I, although the maxilla and mandible may be somewhat retruded.  Oropharyngeal airway measures about 5 to 6 mm at the soft palate level and about 6 mm at the base of the tongue.  Right commissure is about 3 mm lower than the left commissure.  

Basic diagnoses for Roxanne would be as follows:

Maxillary AP hypoplasia and transverse asymmetry.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia and transverse asymmetry.
Headaches.
External nasal asymmetry.
Posterior yaw with the maxilla and mandible rotated about 6 mm towards the right side.  

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Presurgical orthodontics.
	a.	Align and level maxillary arch.  Tip lateral incisors roots mesially and 			cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. 
	b.	Align and level mandibular arch.  
2.	Surgery.
	a.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance, transversely level and expand 			as well as correct posterior yaw.
	b.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible forward 			and transversely level as well as correct the posterior yaw.
	c.	External rhinoplasty to correct the nasal asymmetry.
3.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
BRANDI DICHELE PARSON						12/30/2011

Dichele is 23 years old and birth date is 05/10/1988.  She is referred by Dr. Christine Coke, 107 Suncreek Drive #200, Allen, TX 75013.  In October 2010, Dichele was involved in a motor vehicle accident and sustained a fracture of the left mandibular ramus.  Dr. Coke treated her and wired her jaws together for 13 weeks.  She subsequently had 9 root canals and some crown work done on her teeth.  Dichele is congenitally missing the maxillary second and third molars bilaterally and the mandibular first and second molars bilaterally.  She had orthodontics from the age of 12 to 13 and was put in retainers after that and she continues to wear those retainers at nighttime. 

Dichele gets headaches 2 to 3 times per weeks in the frontal and temple areas bilaterally at about a level of 7.  She complains of no pain in the TMJ joints, but her rates her overall jaw pain at about a level of 7.  She rates her jaw function at 3, diet at 3, and disability at 3.  She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, hearing loss and has no other joints that are involved.  She is aware of clenching her teeth at nighttime, but not during the day.  

In frontal view, Dichele has good facial balance and symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 21 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 2.5 mm.  The maxillary, mandibular, and chin midline are all pretty much on the facial midline.  She may have just a slight increased fullness in the left side of the mandible of about 2 and at the most 3 mm.  In profile, she has good facial balance.  There may be just some slight AP maxillary deficiency of maybe about 3 mm.  Intraorally, she is congenitally missing the mandibular left and right first and second molars, but has her anterior 8 teeth and these teeth appear to be fairly well aligned and leveled with the lower incisors in slightly more inclination.  In the upper arch, she is missing the second and third molars.  She has a slight Class III cuspid relationship on the left side being probably about 2 or 3 mm Class III and on the right side she is a little bit closer to Class I.  The maxilla transversely is narrow and she tends to go into a cross bite in the first bicuspid area on the left side and perhaps the second bicuspid on the right side.  Transversely, the arches are fairly level.  The right eye may be just slightly elevated compared to the left by probably 1 to 2 mm.  TMJ joints seem to function reasonably smoothly.  She has some moderate tenderness over the masseter muscles, but no other significant discomfort around the head and neck area to palpation.  The incisal opening is 51 mm and excursion movement to the right 10 mm and to the left 9 mm.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of the mandibular first and second molars bilaterally and the maxillary second and third molars bilaterally.  She has impacted mandibular third molars that are vertically oriented in the rami of the mandible.  The left side is elevated higher in the ramus than the right side.  She has root canals on the anterior 6 teeth and on the mandibular right central incisor, left central and lateral incisors.  The fracture in the left ramus area appears to be fairly well healed.  

Coronal view shows that the septum is reasonably straight and the turbinates are moderately enlarged.  Sinuses appear relatively clear with just some slight increased thickness of the sinus membrane along the floor, medial and lateral walls.  

Axial view shows that the septum is reasonably straight.  The turbinates are moderate in size.  There is generalized thickening of the mucosal lining throughout the maxillary sinuses, being a little bit thicker on the left side as compared to the right side.  

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology with some sclerosis on top and some anterior beaking more towards the medial aspect of the joint.  Towards the lateral aspect, the lateral condyle is more narrow anteroposteriorly and appears to be posteriorly positioned in the fossa.  The articular eminence has a moderate inclination.  It is possible that the disc could be displaced and oriented in a vertical position.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology with good joint space.  There is some slight anterior beaking tendencies.  The joint space seems fairly well balanced in this joint.  The articular eminence has a moderate inclination.

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width.  There is a decreased lateral joint space.  The lateral rim of the fossa is fairly flat and curves inferiorly only about 1 to 2 mm.  

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with good mediolateral width.  There appears to be good joint space throughout the joint, although slightly decreased towards the medially aspect of the joint.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that there is probably some AP maxillary hypoplasia and the mandible appears to be in a more normal position.  Oropharyngeal airway looks reasonably good.  Oropharyngeal airway measures 9 mm at the soft palate level as well as at the base of the tongue.  She appears to have an end-on incisor relationship.  

Basic diagnoses for Dichele would be as follows:

History of previous fracture of the mandible occurring in October 2010 where she sustained a vertical fracture through the left ramus of the mandible treated with closed reduction.
Rule out TMJ pathology.
Mandibular hypoplasia.
Congenitally missing bilateral mandibular first and second molars and maxillary second and third molars.
Vertically impacted third molars in the ramus of the mandible.
End-on incisor relationship.
Slight Class III cuspid relationship on the left side but basic Class I on the right side.
Posterior cross bite on the left side.
History of nocturnal clenching. 
Headaches.
Facial asymmetry with posterior maxillary and mandibular yaw of about 2 to 3 mm to the left.  

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Medications:   Klonopin 1 mg tablet, dispensing 30 tablets, 1 tablet q.h.s., refill 	x2.
2.	Consider MRI of TMJs if medication unsuccessful in eliminating the headaches.
3.	Presurgical orthodontics.
	a.	Align and level maxillary arch.
	b.	Align and level mandibular arch.
4.	Surgery.
	a.	Consider maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla forward			b.         Consider mandibular bilateral ramus osteotomies to correct the facial 			asymmetry.
	c.	TMJ surgery if indicated based on the MRI.
5.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
6.	Reconstructive dentistry to include:
	a.	Placement of dento-osseus implants x2 bilaterally in the mandible and one 		in each of the posterior maxillary dental alveolar areas bilaterally.  
	b.	Placement of crowns on implants.

original_note:
ALLISON MILSTEAD								 06/16/2008

Allie is 18 years old.  She was actually referred by Dr. Brian Stone when he was a resident at Baylor.  Allison had some orthodontic treatment from the age of 9 to 11.  On July 19, 2001, she was involved in a major motor vehicle accident in which three people died.  Her grandmother and Allie survived.  Allie had severe head injuries, leg fractures, left orbital fracture, and bilateral subcondylar fractures and a body fracture that I believe was on the left side.  She also ruptured her spleen and had a splenectomy performed.  She was comatose for almost 1-1/2 months.  She initially had a cervical collar on and the mandibular fractures were not identified until later.  It was approximately 2 weeks after the injury and the wearing of the cervical collar that the mandibular fractures were finally identified.  Later on, from about the age of 12 to 14, she placed back into orthodontics to try to correct the bite prior to having some surgery done.  She was followed by the plastic surgery department at Denver Children's Hospital, but after a couple of years they realized that they were unable to treat her injuries and suggested she see an oral maxillofacial surgeon.  At that time, Allie and her mother were living in Colorado Springs, and they saw some oral surgeons there, but nothing progressed relative to treatment. They then moved to Abilene, Texas, and were referred to the dental school where Dr. Stone saw her.  Currently, the medications that Allie takes are as follows:

Depo Provera injection q. 3 months.
Tylenol or ibuprofen p.r.n. pain. 

She apparently does not have too much difficulty breathing through her nose.  She may have a little bit at night when she sleeps.  She does have some mild snoring and some mild daytime somnolence.  She does smoke 1 to 5 cigarettes per day.  The only joints that bother her are primarily her left knee.  No earaches, occasional tinnitus, but no vertigo.  She may have a little bit of clenching at night.  She says when she wears the clear plastic retainers that cover her teeth at night that she wakes up with significant pain in the morning and, therefore, cannot wear those type retainers.  She gets headaches at about a level of 3 that occur on top of the head and in the right temporal area.  She rates her TMJ pain at 7, average daily pain at 1, jaw function at 7, diet at 5, disability at 4.  On an average day, her pain levels gets to about a level of 4, but she will have episodes that the pain gets quite severe for her.  

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  The upper lip length is 18 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is 6-7 mm.  The dental midlines and chin midline are all pretty much on the facial midline.  In profile,  the upper lip may be retruded a couple of millimeters, and the chin is retruded about 10-15 mm.  The chin neckline is extremely short.  There is no definition of the chin neck angle.  She has a head posture forward neck position. Intraorally, Allie has all of her lower teeth back to the second molars present.  In the upper arch, she also has all of her teeth back to the second molars.  She has almost a full Class II cuspid molar relationship bilaterally.  She has an overjet of about 5 to 6 mm.  She has an anterior deep bite of about 4 to 5 mm.  To palpation, she has moderate tenderness over the right TMJ, milder tenderness over the left TMJ, right medial pterygoid, right masseter, right retromandibular area.  Maximal incisal opening is 22 mm.  Excursion movement to the right about 4-5 mm, to the left 2 mm.  On opening, Allison feels crepitation in the joints with the right side being worse than the left side.  Intranasally, the turbinates are moderately enlarged.  The septum may be deviated just slightly off to the right side.  There is perhaps some mild to moderate obstruction there.
RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars.  Sinuses look relatively clear.  Turbinates, particularly the left side, look enlarged.  There is definite evidence of previously fractured condyles with severe malalignment of those condylar remnants.
Waters view shows that the septum is deviated a little bit off to the right side.  Turbinates are fairly large with the left nasal airway appearing to be pretty much obliterated.  There appears to be a slight airway on the right side.  The sinuses look relatively clear.   
Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is actually postured forward in the fossa almost beneath the articular eminence.  The condylar neck is extremely short.  The head is significantly displaced with malunion.  
Left TMJ tomogram shows that condyle is positioned almost beneath the articular eminence with increased joint space vertically.  The condyle is not as readily seen in this view.  On opening, there is restricted translation of the condyles.  
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Allison has a rather large adenoid mass present in the nasopharyngeal area.  She has a high occlusal plane angle and a significant overjet of probably about 4 mm.  She has a significantly decreased oropharyngeal airway particularly between the soft palate and the adenoid mass with an airway dimension of about 4 mm.  Relative to the turbinates, the airway dimension appears to be about 3 mm.  From the soft palate to the posterior pharyngeal wall, the airway dimension is about 12 mm, but the base of the tongue to the back of the throat is extremely constricted but may be due to tongue posture as there is an airway of only about 2 mm, but the tongue appears to be postured downwards and posterior. 
Basic diagnoses for Allie would be as follows:
Malalignment and malunion of bilateral TMJ subcondylar fractures.  
Mandibular hypoplasia.
Anterior vertical maxillary hyperplasia.
Hypertrophied turbinates.
Hypertrophied adenoid tissue.
Nasal airway obstruction.
Class II occlusion.
High occlusal plane angle.
TMJ pain. 
Headaches.

Recommended treatment would include the following:

Presurgical orthodontics.  
Align and level maxillary arch.  
Align and level mandibular arch.  
CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol).


Surgery. 
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses.  
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the anterior aspect superiorly and upright the incisors.   
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen).
Bilateral coronoidotomies. 
Bilateral nasal partial turbinectomies.  
Nasopharyngeal adenoidectomy.
Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B435 
B435 is 15 years and 7 months of age. Referred by Dr. XXXX. Primary concerns that B435 has includes the following:

Jaws are out of alignment.
Crowding of the teeth.
Overbite.
He currently takes no medications, but takes Concerta 54 mg daily through the school year. He does not have any significant airway problems. He did have a nasal fracture about a year-and-a-half ago, but it was reduced and he has had no subsequent problems. He gets occasional popping in his joint joints that started at about the age of 13. He gets headaches about ten per month. Headaches occur predominantly in the forehead and temporal areas. He is aware of clenching his teeth during the day. No earaches, but occasional tinnitus and moderate problems of vertigo primarily when he stands up quickly. No other joint problems are present.

B435 states he became aware of some TMJ problems at the age of 13 of unknown etiology where the jaws would occasionally pop. The popping occurs more on the right side than the left side. He rates his average headaches at a level of 4 in the temporal and frontal areas. He rates TMJ pain and discomfort at a level of 1, average daily pain around the head and neck area at a 4, jaw function at 1, diet at 0, and disability at 0. He does not feel that he has any airway problems.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
In frontal view, B435 has fairly good left-to-right facial asymmetry. Upper lip length is about 21 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is zero. The maxillary, mandibular, and chin midline are all pretty much on the facial midline. The nasal tip is about 3 mm off towards the left side. In profile view, he has just some slight AP maxillary deficiency of probably about 3 mm, but the chin may be retruded about 15-18 mm. The chin neckline is shorter, although the chin neck angle is fairly well defined. He has a head postured forward neck position. Intraorally, he has got all of his lower teeth back through the second molars. There is some moderate crowding in the lower anterior arch. He appears to have adequate attached gingiva. The second molars are slightly covered with soft tissue at the most posterior aspect. In the upper arch, he has all of his teeth back through the second molars present. There is just a slight Class II occlusal tendency by about 2 mm. He has about a 2 mm vertical overbite relationship. He has got about a 1 mm overjet. Oropharynx appears clear and normal in architecture. Turbinates appear moderate in size. The septum is deviated significantly off towards the right side. He has no sleep apnea or airway issues that are apparent. To palpation, he has some mild tenderness over the retromandibular areas bilaterally, but no other pain around the head and neck area. On opening and closing, his joints function smoothly most of the time, but he would occasionally get a popping kind of noise in the joints. Maximal incisal opening is 55 mm. Excursion movements to the right 10 mm, to the left 11 mm.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the presence of the maxillary impacted third molars and mandibular left impacted third molar. The right third molar is absent. Sinuses look relatively clear. The septum appears deviated off towards the right side. Turbinates appear moderate in size. Waters view shows that the sinuses appear relatively clear. The nasal septum is deviated severely off towards the right side, and, in fact, it appears that there is a large septal spur that may be even touching against the lateral wall of the nose. Left turbinate appears quite large. Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle and condylar neck that appear a little small in size. Articular eminence is fairly flat. It is difficult to evaluate joint spaces. Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle where the head and neck appear somewhat short in length. Articular eminence is moderately shallow. The joint space looks a little bit more appropriate on this side as compared to the opposite side. On opening, there is good translation in both condyles. The small size of the condylar head and neck can be observed.
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that unfortunately he was posturing his mandible forward when this was taken. On the ceph, he is biting end-to-end and the incisors are not in centric relationship so this x-ray would need to be redone. Oropharyngeal airway appears reasonably good with an airway of about 8 mm at the base of the tongue and also at the soft palate level. Basic diagnoses would be as follows:
Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation (awaiting MRI for confirmation). AP maxillary hyperplasia.
Posterior vertical maxillary hypoplasia.
AP mandibular hypoplasia.
High occlusal plane angle.
Impacted maxillary third molars bilaterally and impacted mandibular left third molar.
Nasal septum deviated severely off towards the right side creating nasal airway obstruction.
Left nasal turbinate hyperplasia.
Headaches.
Significant tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 3 mm.
Overangulation of lower anteriors.
Significant crowding of the lower anterior arch.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
MRI of left and right TMJs to determine health and position of the articular discs.
Extract maxillary second bicuspids and mandibular first bicuspids. Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch. Lose some posterior anchorage. Open up 1 to 1.5 mm of space between each maxillary lateral incisor and cuspid to correct for the tooth size discrepancy (3 mm). Align and level mandibular arch.
Surgery. Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors, if MRI confirms discs are displaced. Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible forward in a counterclockwise direction. Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla forward in a counterclockwise direction.
Surgical removal of three impacted third molars.
Nasal septoplasty. Left partial inferior turbinectomy. Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy to augment the chin.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
Restorative dentistry to place bonding or veneers on the maxillary lateral incisors.

B435 MRI
AGE: 15/M DOB:
Date of Acquisition:
Date of Interpretation:
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has undergone considerable condylar resorption with lots of vertical and mediolateral condylar volume loss. The articular disc is medially displaced and significantly deformed. There is a small osteophyte sticking up in the condyle. Lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 to 4 mm. There is almost no lateral space with bone coming down from the lateral rim of the fossa. There is severe degenerative changes of the condyle and disc.

Right TMJ coronal view shows decreased size vertically as well as mediolaterally. The disc is displaced medial and anteriorly about 3 to 4 mm.
Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is small in size vertically and anteroposteriorly particularly towards the medial side of the joint. There is anterior beaking present. The articular eminence is moderately steep. The articular disc is sitting anterior beneath the articular eminence. The disc is thickened and shortened anteroposteriorly. On opening, it appears that the disc may be non-reducing.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is very narrow anteroposteriorly. It appears to have lost vertical height as well. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Articular disc is anteriorly displaced and sitting down towards the superior aspect of the articular eminence. The disc is short and thickened. On opening, there is fairly good translation of the condyle, but the disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction.

IMPRESSION:
Left TMJ severe condylar absorption and articular disc anteromedially displaced without reduction. Right TMJ severe loss of condylar volume with articular disc anteromedially displaced without reduction.
Probable MRI changes compatible with AICR. The forward disc may still be salvageable.

original_note:
JENNIFER ROOSMAN								05-22-02

Jennifer is here for additional surgical evaluation. It appears that she is continuing to have some mandibular growth of probably 1.5 to 2 mm in the past year. She just got her braces on in January on the top and then in March on the bottom. There has not been much time to get too much accomplished.

From an orthodontic standpoint, recommend the following:

Continue to align and level the maxillary arch. Try to open up space between the upper left cuspid and central incisor to equal about 5 to 6 mm (the size of a normal lateral incisor). Upper incisors could stand to have some moderate increase in angulation but not too much. Try to open about 1 mm of space on either side of the right lateral incisor and close the spacing between the maxillary central incisors. 
Align and level the mandibular arch. Close the space on the lower left cuspid-first bicuspid area.
Consider use of Class II elastics on the left side to help distalize the maxillary left cuspid and move the mandibular left posterior teeth forward to lose some anchorage, and, thus, keep from retracting the lower anterior teeth any further posteriorly. Try to upright (buccally tip) the mandibular posterior teeth to decrease the excessive curve of Wilson. 

An easier treatment option for Jennifer may just be to extract the maxillary right lateral incisor, and this could be done at the time of surgery, but align and level the rest of the teeth without trying to open up space for the upper left lateral incisor. Then she could be treated with multiple maxillary osteotomies to remove any residual spacing and place the cuspids and molars in a Class II occlusal position. The occlusion actually fits together much better in that relationship as compared to a Class I position.

Surgery would then involve the following:
Extraction of the maxillary right lateral incisor.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies, placing the upper cuspids into the lateral incisor position.
Bilateral high condylectomies as there appears to be continuing to grow as a result of condylar hyperplasia.

original_note:
COLLIN PEEL								10/26/2011

Collin is 24 years old, birth date 05/01/1987.  Collin was referred by Jennifer Elbert, the mother of a former patient.  Primary concerns that Collin has are:

Under bite.
Difficulties eating.
Self conscious of the jaw and facial imbalances.

Collin has no trouble with headaches, no TMJ pain or myofascial pain problems.  He rates his jaw function at 3, diet at 7, disability at 3.  He has no earaches, tinnitus, vertigo or hearing loss.  The only other joint area that bothers him his is lower back.  He is not aware of clenching or bruxing.  

The onset of Collin’s problems was in his teenage years as he was going through puberty.  He had orthodontics from the age of 13 to 15 by Dr. Frank Stich.  At the end of treatment, his bite was pretty good.  He has 4 bicuspids extracted.  It was within about a year after the orthodontics was completed that his jaw began to grow.  Collin does not believe it has gotten much worse since he was about 18 or so.  

In frontal view, Collin has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 22 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 2.5 mm.  The maxillary, mandibular, and chin midline are pretty close to being on the facial midline.  In profile view, the maxilla is probably about 6 mm AP deficient.  The chin is actually in fairly good AP facial balance.  Intraorally, he is missing probably the lower second bicuspids.  The first bicuspid and first molars are in a little bit of lingual inclination.  He has good attached gingiva.  His lower incisors may be in just slight lingual inclination.  In the upper arch, he has had a bicuspid extracted on each side as well.  He has bilateral cross bites.  He is a super Class III cuspid relationship, being probably at least 10 mm Class III.  He has reverse anterior cross bite of approximately 10 to 12 mm.  He has a reverse deep bite of about 1 mm.  Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively normal except the uvula is moderately enlarged, but otherwise the structures look relatively normal.  Intranasally, the turbinates are moderately enlarged.  The septum may be deviated just a little bit off to the right side.  The columella is wide and the nostrils are narrow.  Holding open the nostrils with a nasal speculum enabled Collin to breathe easier through his nose.  Collin has no tenderness around the head and neck area to palpation.  Joints seem to function smoothly.  The maximal incisal opening is 56 mm and excursion movements are 8 mm in each direction.  

Basic diagnoses for Collin would be as follows:

Bilateral mandibular condylar hyperplasia (growth appears to be complete).
Mandibular prognathism.
Maxillary AP and transverse hypoplasia.
Hypertrophied turbinates with nasal airway obstruction.
Lingual inclination of mandibular bicuspids and first molars. 
Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of 4 mm.  
Super Class III occlusion (10 mm Class III).

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Presurgical orthodontics.
	a.	Align and level maxillary arch.  Open up at least 1 mm of space between 			the upper laterals and cuspids.  Keep lateral incisor roots tipped mesially 			and cuspid roots tipped distally to make room for interdental osteotomies.  
	b.	Align and level mandibular arch.  Tip the bicuspids and first molars 			labially.
2.	Surgery.
	a.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to set the mandible posteriorly 			approximately 5 to 6 mm.
	b.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla approximately 7 			mm and expand it approximately 6 mm.  
	c.	Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies.  
3.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
ALAN SPINDEL									04/12/2008

Alan is 14 years and 11 months old.  Referred by Dr. Yair Zymman.  He takes the following medications:

Symbicort.
Mometasone. 
Desloratadine. 

The primary concerns Alan has are as follows:

Sinusitis.
Difficulties breathing through his nose.
Difficulties getting the lips together.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Alan has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is 20 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is 10 mm.  Maxillary, mandibular, and chin midline are all aligned on the facial midline.  In profile view, the maxilla may be retruded about 4 to 5 mm.  Chin is retruded by probably about 10 to 15 mm.  Chin neckline is a little bit short, although the chin neck angle is fairly well defined.  Alan has a lip incompetence of  about 12 mm.  Lower third facial dimension is excessively long.  Alan has had orthodontic appliances on for approximately 5 years.  Intraorally, he has all of his lower teeth back to the second molars.  The teeth are nicely aligned and leveled.  He has some hyperplasia of the gingival tissues, but has good attached gingiva.  In the upper arch, he has all of his teeth back to the second molars.  He has some hyperplasia of the gingival tissues in the upper arch as well.  He has a good Class I cuspid-molar relationship and has almost 2 mm of vertical overbite.  He has no tenderness to palpation around the head and neck area.  Incisal opening is 47 mm.  Excursion movements are about 6 mm in each direction.
Initially, Alan had a slight anterior open bite of maybe about 2 mm.  With the orthodontics, that problem has been corrected.  Back in 2003, he had vertical maxillary excess at that time, and it has gotten somewhat worse for him.  Intranasally, he has got moderately hypertrophied turbinates.  Septum appears relatively straight.  There is moderate nasal airway obstruction.  Columella is somewhat wide and nostrils are narrow.  He does report moderate snoring, but  no daytime somnolence and no sleep apnea symptoms.  
RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows that he has got four impacted third molars.  Sinuses appear relatively clear.  
Waters view shows that the bony septum is relatively straight, but turbinates are enlarged.  He has significant cloudiness in the left maxillary sinus, but the rest of the sinuses appear relatively clear. He probably has ongoing sinusitis at this time.
Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle with fairly good morphology and fairly good joint space and moderate inclination of the articular eminence.  
Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle with fairly good morphology and fairly good joint space with moderate inclination of the articular eminence.  On opening, there is fairly good translation of both condyles.  
Basic diagnoses for Alan would be as follows:
Severe vertical maxillary excess.  
Mandibular prognathism (hidden by the excessive vertical growth of the maxilla).
Anterior vertical mandibular excess. 
Probable bilateral condylar hyperplasia.
High occlusal plane angle.
Hypertrophied turbinates.  
Nasal airway obstruction.
Wide columella.
Impacted third molars x4. 
Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of 2 to 3 mm.  
Over-angulation of lower incisors.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch. 
Align and level mandibular arch.  Slenderize the low anterior teeth, removing at least 2 mm of tooth structure to compensate for the tooth size discrepancy and to decrease the excessive angulation of the lower incisors.  
Continue to monitor growth until the patient is 16 years old.  
Surgery. 
Bilateral maxillary osteotomies to move the maxilla superiorly approximately 10 mm.  
Bilateral TMJ high condylectomies and articular disc repositioning with ligament repair with Mitek anchors if condylar hyperplasia confirmed.  
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to reposition it.
Surgical removal of impacted third molars x4.  
Anterior mandibular horizontal ostectomy to vertically reduce and AP augment the chin.
Bilateral turbinectomies.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B136
B136 is 43 years old. Referred by Dr. XXXX, and also XXXX.
To the best of B136's recollection, her problems began in about 27 years ago when her jaw would start to click and pop and sometimes lock. Twenty-seven years ago, she saw Dr. XXXX, who did bilateral disc repositioning procedures. The right side apparently did okay, but the left side became displaced again, and, 2 years later, the left side was done again and this time a Proplast Teflon interpositional implant placed. She then saw Dr. XXXX 23 years ago, and he removed the PTIPI and placed a temporal fascia graft. She continued to have severe pain. Two years later, she then came back to Dallas to see Dr. XXXX who debrided the left joint and did a right joint exploration. Twenty-one years ago, Dr. XXXX went back in and debrided the left joint once again. Eight years ago, she saw Dr. XXXX who placed a left Walter Lorenz total joint prosthesis. Her pain and swelling has continued since that time. She has been suffering pain and problems since then. She is currently seeing a pain doctor. She currently takes the following medications:

Lyrica 75 mg 3 times per day. Celebrex 200 mg q.d.
OxyContin 40-mg tablets, 1 to 2 tablets twice a day. Baclofen 10 mg t.i.d.
Axert 12.5 mg p.r.n. Amrix 15 mg q.d. as a muscle relaxant.

She gets headaches frequently at a level of 5 predominantly in the left temporal and posterior head area. Five to six times a week she gets rather severe migraines that escalate to a level of 8 to 9. Her average daily pain is around 7. She rates her jaw function at 5, diet at 5, disability at 8. She gets no earaches, tinnitus, or vertigo and has no hearing loss. Other joints that bother include her neck and her upper back area. She is not aware of clenching or bruxing. She does smoke half a pack of cigarettes per day.

B136 recently has undergone some dental implants and some dental reconstruction and this was completed about 2 weeks ago.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
In frontal view, B136 has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 22 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is about 3 mm. The facial midlines and dental midlines are all pretty much aligned, although the nasal tip may be just slightly off to the right side. In profile view, B136 has fairly good facial symmetry and balance. Intraorally, she has had lower restorative work done with replacing teeth back through the second bicuspids. The molars are missing. In the upper arch, she has had some extensive work done on the left side and second molar on the right side. Bite is fairly close to being Class I cuspid. In trying to place her into centric relation, she has significant pain in the left cheek area and the midlines do not line up. The mandibular dental midline appears about 1 mm off towards the left side. She does have some appreciable swelling in the left cheek area over by the malar eminence and likely detachment of the masseter muscle. She also has a little bit of swelling back more towards the ear along the zygomatic arch. Intranasally, the left turbinate appears a little large right now. She says at times she hears squeaking noises in the left jaw joint. Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively normal.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows that B136 is missing all of her permanent teeth. She has 5 implants in the mandibular arch to replace the lower teeth back to the second bicuspids. In the upper arch, she has 6 implants with 5 of them loaded to replace all the teeth from the second molar on the right side around to the first molar on the left side. There is an implant in the second molar area, but there has been no crown placed on that tooth yet. Right TMJ appears a little bit arthritic. The left joint has a Walter Lorenz total joint prosthesis in place with 6 screws holding the mandibular component and 4 screws holding the maxillary component. Axial view shows that the turbinates do not appear too large in size. There is a small spur off the right side of the septum. Coronal view shows that the turbinates are not too enlarged and the airway is reasonably good. The nasal septum is deviated off towards the right side slightly. Left TMJ sagittal view shows the presence of a Walter Lorenz total joint prosthesis. There may be some reactive heterotopic bone around the prosthesis. Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is a little bit arthritic with some flattening of the posterior head. There appears to be significantly decreased joint space between the condyle and the articular eminence. The condyle appears to be postured slightly forward in the fossa. Left TMJ coronal view shows the mandibular component present and also the upper component can be seen. It appears somewhat thin. There could be some heterotopic bone growing down around the inside of the fossa component. Right TMJ coronal view shows that in some areas there appears to be reasonably good joint space around the condyle. Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she probably has reasonably good facial balance and airway looks reasonably good.

Incisal opening is 30 mm, but only 23 mm without severe pain. Excursion movements are only about 1 mm in each direction. To palpation, she has severe pain over the left masseter; high moderate pain over the left TMJ, left posterior neck pain; moderate pain over the styloid areas, right TMJ, and right masseter area.

Basic diagnoses for B136 would be as follows:
Right TMJ failed Walter Lorenz total joint prosthesis. Heterotopic bone formation around the left TMJ prosthesis. Foreign body giant cell reaction around the left TMJ prosthesis.
Right TMJ arthritis and disc dislocation. Severe TMJ pain. Headaches. Myofascial pain. Masticatory dysfunction. Recommended treatment would be as follows:

MRI of right TMJ to evaluate degree of arthritis and articular disc position. CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol).
Surgery. Removal of left TMJ failed Walter Lorenz total joint prosthesis.
Extensive debridement of left TMJ including removal of heterotopic bone, foreign body giant cell reaction, and fibrosis.
Bilateral TMJ versus left TMJ (depending on MRI evaluation) reconstruction with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. Fat grafts to TMJs (harvest from the abdomen).
Application of maxillary and mandibular arch bars. Any dental reconstruction that may be necessary to finalize the occlusion.

B136 MRI AGE: DOB:
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears arthritic. There is a depression in the mid-part of the condyle. It is difficult to see discal tissue between the condyle and fossa. The left TMJ has a total joint prosthesis present. It is difficult to evaluate any translation in the joint prosthesis.

Right TMJ shows the presence of a total joint prosthesis with metal-on-metal occlusion likely a Christensen prosthesis. There is a little bit of fluid in the joint space. It is difficult to evaluate for any translation in the joint. There is some restricted translation in the condyle with it coming down just along the posterior slope of the articular eminence and not down beneath it.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has a little bit of arthritis with some anterior beaking. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Articular disc appears it may be in position, although does appear to be some degenerative changes associated with it. There is some slight amount of fluid in the superior joint space. There is flattening on the posterior head of the condyle. Articular eminence is moderately steep.

IMPRESSION:
Left TMJ total joint prosthesis with metal-on-metal articulation. Right TMJ arthritis, but articular disc appears to be in position, though with degenerative changes.

B136 MRI AGE: DOB:
Left TMJ coronal view has a total joint prostheses in position.
Right TMJ coronal view shows some advanced arthritis on the condylar head. There is an erosion and depression in the head of the condyle in the mid to lateral aspect of the joint. There appears to be a hot spot of inflammation within the joint. Lateral rim of the fossa is flat. There appears to be decreased joint space medially. There are some areas of sclerosis on the head of the condyle. It is difficult to identify discal tissue.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows the presence of a prosthesis with difficulty identifying the individual structures. There appears to be a lot of metal artifact present.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is arthritic. There is flattening on the posterior head of the condyle. The disc appears to be anteriorly displaced, although there is some scarred fibrotic tissue over the top of the condyle. There appear to be some bony degeneration on the posterior slope of the articular eminence. Medially, it appears that there is almost bone-on-bone contact.

In the T-2 films, the disc seems to be anteriorly displaced and sitting almost beneath the articular eminence. There appears to be compressed tissue between the condyle and the fossa, probably related to previous surgical intervention. The disc is extremely short anteroposteriorly and not sufficient to cover the head of the condyle. There is noted translation. In the open view, the discal tissue looks very degenerated. The articular disc appears to be adhesed to the articular eminence.

IMPRESSION:
Left TMJ total joint prostheses.
Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with significant degeneration and deformation with limited translation with the remnants of the disc adhesed to the articular eminence.
