B370 
B370 is 31 years old and referred by Dr. _

Patient History
She had an appendectomy done 27 years ago. 4 years ago, she had a head injury and had one seizure but none after that. She had a biking accident and struck the posterior left side of her head and was knocked unconscious and had a grand mal seizure at that time.

Current Medications
B370 takes no medications of any kind at this time.
Patient Concerns
Primary concerns are as follows:
Open bite.
Severe headaches (migraines).
Moderate to severe TMJ pain.
Pain through the muscles of the head, jaw, and neck area.
Significant noises in the jaw joints.
Difficulties eating and chewing.
Difficulties with her tongue as it is being displaced by the large mandibular tori.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway space of 5 mm (normal 11 mm).

B370 thinks that her TMJ problems may have begun back around the age of 10 or 11. She remembers one incident where someone hit her or bumped her in the jaw from the right side, and it did create pain on the left side. She did not develop clicking and popping right at that time but during about that same time frame she remembers having clicking in her jaw joints. She had orthodontics as a teenager. Prior to the orthodontics, she did have an open bite, and she recalls that was fairly closed up so that her front teeth actually did touch for a while but then the bite opened up. In the last six years, she feels that the bite has gotten worse. B370 feels that she pushes her tongue forward on her front teeth and feels that may be somewhat contributory to redeveloping her open bite. She does get frequent earaches and vertigo and gets occasional tinnitus. She does have a little bit of problem with her left shoulder, and her wrists do bother her, but she feels that is work-related.

Clinical Evaluation
In frontal view, B370 has fairly good facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 22 mm. Upper tooth to lip relationship is 0. Maxillary dental midline is pretty much on the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline is probably 1 to 2 mm off to the left side. Mandibular dental midline and chin midline are pretty much aligned with each other. Therefore, mandibular dental midline and chin midline may be just a little bit off toward the left side. Nasal tip is a little bit off to the right side probably a couple of millimeters. In profile view, B370 has fairly good balance between the upper lips, cheeks, and nose. Chin appears retruded, and chin-neckline is a little bit short, although the chin-neck angle is fairly well defined. Upper lip may be retruded 2 to 3 mm. Chin may be back 8 to 10 mm.

Intraorally, B370 has all of her lower teeth erupted in back through her third molars. There is adequate attached gingiva lower anterior arch. She has huge lingual tori that fill up all but about 8 mm transversely in the floor of the mouth. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the third molars, except she is missing the right lateral incisor replaced by a pontic. She does have a crown on the left lateral incisor. She essentially occludes only on her second and third molars with all other teeth being out of occlusion. She has an anterior open bite of 7 mm. She has a slight Class II cuspid-molar relationship.

Intranasally, the airway looks good with just moderate-size turbinates and no particular inflammatory response seen. Oropharynx also appears clear. Tonsils are present but relatively small in size.

B370 rates her headaches at a level of 8 that oftentimes begin in the temple area but can cover the entire head and neck area. Usually these occur about two times per week, but there are times when they occur daily. She rates her average TMJ pain at about 4, overall pain at 6, jaw function at 7, diet at 3, disability at 4. Maximal incisal opening is 48 mm excluding the open bite. Excursive movements are about 10 mm in each direction.

On opening, she has a distinctive pop in the right joint at probably around 40 mm of opening and a closing click at about 35. On the left side, she can manipulate her jaw and make some popping noises on that side at well at probably about 45 mm of opening and reciprocal one at 40 mm of closing. To palpation, she has some mild to moderate discomfort over the masseter muscles, TMJs, and styloid areas.

Radiographic Evaluation
Panorex shows the presence of all teeth except the maxillary right lateral incisor that has been replaced by a pontic that appears to be cantilevered off of the cuspid. Sinuses appear clear. Turbinates appear moderate in size. Left condyle appears a little bit more pointed with perhaps an osteophyte at the tip of it. Right joint appears a little bit flattened across the top. No other significant findings noted. Styloid processes are quite thickened and slightly elongated.

Left TMJ shows a condyle that is flattened on the posterior aspect and also on the anterior aspect. Some anterior beaking present. Vertical and posterior joint space actually looks pretty good. Articular eminence is moderately steep. There appear to be some significant degenerative changes associated with this condyle.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle with what appears to be loss of vertical height. Has anterior beaking present. Superior and posterior joint space appears almost excessive. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Translation is very good in both joints with the condyles coming anterior to the articular eminences.

Waters view shows that the sinuses are clear. Turbinate on the left side appears larger than the right side. Some mild asymmetry noted in the jaw structures.

Cephalometrically, she has a significant open bite and a reverse curve of occlusion in the lower arch and perhaps slightly accentuated curve of occlusion in the upper arch. Oropharyngeal airway dimension is about 6 mm at the most at the soft palate level and close to 7at the base of the tongue.

Basic Diagnoses
Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with severe deformation and degeneration.
Mandibular hypoplasia.
Maxillary hyperplasia.
Severe headaches.
Severe TMJ pain.
Myofascial pain.
Severely hyperplastic bilateral mandibular tori extending from cuspids all the way to the third molar area.
Anterior open bite of 7 mm.
Occludes only on second and third molars with all other teeth being out of occlusion.
Class II end-on cuspid-molar relationship.
Accentuated curve of occlusion, upper arch.
Reverse curve of occlusion, lower arch.
Moderate crowding, lower anterior arch.

Recommended Treatment
Presurgical orthodontics
Align and level maxillary arch in three segments: Segment 1: Left cuspid through second molar; Segment 2: Left lateral incisor through the right central incisor; Segment 3: Right cuspid through right second molar. Use a continuous arch wire, but place a step in the arch wire to align the four incisors at an elevated level and help slightly extrude the cuspid teeth. Tip cuspid roots distally and the left lateral incisor root mesially to make root for interdental osteotomies.
Align and level mandibular arch. Try to elevate lower incisors to help eliminate the slight reverse curve of occlusion in that arch.

CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol)
Surgery
Bilateral TMJ condylectomy, meniscectomy, reconstruction, and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to expand, level, and close the open bite.
Bilateral coronoidectomies.
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest graft from abdomen).
Surgical removal of bilateral mandibular tori.
Extract maxillary and mandibular third molars.

Postsurgical orthodontics to finish and retain
Dentistry to rebuild the maxillary lateral incisors
B370  MRI Evaluation
Left TMJ, coronal view, shows a condyle with a significant downward slope of the lateral pole of the condyle. A fairly significant downward turning of the lateral rim is also present. Vertical joint space actually looks fairly good. Disc is maybe anteriorly displaced.

Right TMJ, coronal view, shows a condyle with fairly good mediolateral width but appears to have been some bony changes associated with it. Significant downward rotation of the lateral pole of the condyle. Lateral rim of the fossa appears downward but not nearly to the extent as the opposite side. There appears to be some incongruency on the head of the condyle more so than the opposite side. Joint space looks quite good. Difficult to tell exactly where the disc is.

Left TMJ, sagittal view, shows a condyle that is a little bit narrow anteroposteriorly and has a significant hockey stick shape to it. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Some anterior beaking present. Out laterally, it appears that there may be some loss of cortical bone on the anterosuperior head of the condyle. Articular disc is anteriorly displaced and significantly deformed and quite short anteroposteriorly. Some significant amount of fluid in the superior joint space both above the condylar head and also in the anterior recess around the articular eminence.

On opening, there is reasonably good translation, and the articular disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction.
Right TMJ, sagittal view, shows a condyle that appears to have undergone significant arthritic changes. There appear to be osteophytic extensions to them off the superior head of the condyle. There is significant anterior beaking present. Some areas where the cortical bone may have some erosions. Articular eminence is moderately steep. It appears to be intact for the most part. Articular disc is anteriorly displaced and quite thickened and quite short anteroposteriorly. There is fluid in the superior joint space and in the anterior recess by the articular eminence.

On opening, there is moderately good translation but not quite as good as the opposite side, and articular disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction.

Impression:
Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with significant deformation and nonreducing.
Right TMJ arthritis (worse than the left side) with articular disc dislocation with significant deformation and nonreducing.

original_note:
CELIA HOLCOMB									06/12/2006

Celia is 23 years old, referred by Dr. James Phillips.  She is in good health.  She does have some problems with anemia but as of December of 2005, her hemoglobin and hematocrit were within normal range.  The only medication she takes is Advil.  She has had an adverse reaction to Ultracet.  Her orthodontist is Dr. Scott Allen, 911 Osler Dr., Jonesboro, AR 72401.

PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Headaches.
Jaw aches mostly after eating and while eating.
Difficulties gaining weight.
Underbite jaw relationship.

In frontal view, Celia has fairly good symmetry in the middle and upper third face.  Upper lip length is about 19 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 3.5 mm.  Maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline.  Nasal tip is a little bit off to the right side by about 2 mm.  Mandibular dental midline is off to the left about 1.5 mm.  Chin is probably 2 mm off to the left side.  Celia gives no history of trauma to her nose.  In profile, the upper lip appears to be deficient by 3 mm or so.  Chin is a little bit prominent by maybe 2 to 3 mm.  Chin-neckline may be just slightly long.  Chin-neck angle is very well defined.  Celia has a high occlusal mandibular plane angle and facial morphology.  She has some slight increased fullness in the left angle area maybe 1 to 2 mm at the most.  Also, some slight vertical elongation on the left side of maybe 1 to 2 mm.  Intraorally, Celia has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars present.  She has appliances on all the teeth including the second molars.  She appears to have adequate attached gingiva in the lower anterior arch.  In the upper arch she has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  She is almost a full Class III cuspid molar relationship.  She has an anterior and left posterior crossbite.  She has about ½ mm reverse overbite relationship.  She has a reverse overjet of probably approximately 7 mm.  Both arches are fairly symmetric.  As Celia shifts her mandible about 1.5 mm to the right to align her dental midlines, the posterior crossbites are a little bit better balanced and a little bit more equal.  Transverse occlusal plane in the maxilla is fairly level although the right side may be down about ½ mm compared to the left side.  Oropharyngeal area looks relatively normal.  The uvula is a little bit elongated.  The soft palate length looks relatively normal.  Faucial pillars may be just mildly to moderately constricted but not too significant.  Her tonsils are present and are normal in size.  Intranasally, the septum appears to be deviated off towards the left side but there is some septal type spur coming off towards the right side at the base of the floor.  Left turbinate appears large and boggy filling a good portion of the nasal airway.  The right side, at least anteriorly, the turbinate appears to be a little bit more normal in size.  The columella is wide and the nostrils are constricted.  The luminal valves look relatively normal.  She probably has at least moderate nasal airway obstruction.  Dental models show that there appears to be slight tooth size discrepancy probably secondary to small upper lateral incisors.  Tooth size analysis shows a Bolton’s difference of about 1.5 to 2 mm.  To palpation, Celia has some high moderate pain over the TMJs to palpation but no other pain around the head and neck area.  Maximal incisal opening is about 47 mm.  Excursive movement to the right is about 9 mm, to the left 5 mm.  


CELIA HOLCOMB				06/12/2006				Page 2


RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION:
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars.  All the other teeth are present.  Midline is fairly good except the left cuspid root has a mild dilaceration associated with it and it is actually tipped forward.  The sinuses look relatively clear.  Turbinates appear enlarged particularly on the left side.  Condylar heads and necks appear elongated particularly on the right side.  There does not appear to be a coronoid process on the right side.  The left side appears that there is still one present.  There appears to be a neurovascular canal that is fairly high on the ramus.  
Dr. Phillips did begin the surgery on the right ramus where he realized the anatomical difficulties.  

Waters view shows that the bony septum at least is reasonably straight.  The turbinates appear moderately enlarged and the nasal airway is constricted.  The sinuses appear relatively clear.  

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears to be posteriorly positioned in the fossa with decreased posterior joint space.  Articular eminence is relatively shallow.  Increased anterior joint space.  Condylar head and neck is long and the absence of the coronoid process is noted.  

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has decreased joint space vertically.  Articular eminence is relatively shallow.  The condylar head and neck are somewhat elongated.  On opening there is fairly good translation in both joints.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Celia has a Class III occlusal relationship.  Slight reverse curve of occlusion in the upper arch is present.  Oropharyngeal airway is larger than normal.  There is a Class III skeletal and occlusal relationship.

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
Bilateral TMJ condylar hyperplasia (arrested.)
Bilateral TMJ articular disc dislocations. 
Mandibular deviated prognathism. 
AP maxillary deficiency. 
Transverse maxillary deficiency.
Vertical maxillary excess.
Class III occlusion.
Anterior and left-sided crossbites. 
TMJ pain.
Headaches. 
Hypertrophied turbinates.
Nasal airway obstruction.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT: 
Presurgical orthodontics.
Continue to align and level maxillary arch. 
Continue to align and level mandibular arch.
CELIA HOLCOMB				06/12/2006				Page 3


Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Maxillary osteotomies to move it superiorly, advance and expand. 
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to shift the mandible posteriorly.
Bilateral partial turbinectomies.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

I did do an analysis comparing a lateral cephalogram taken by Dr. Allen in December of 2004.  Compared to today’s radiograph of which we had an exact magnification between both radiographs, it demonstrated no further mandibular growth.

original_note:
LESLIE ZOHRER								11/21/2011

Leslie is 35 years old, birth date 09/05/1976.  She is referred by Dr. David Adame, 210 W. Mahl, Edinburg, TX 78539 and Dr. Curt Mackie, 802 S. Loop 499, Suite 1, Harlingen, TX 78550.  

Leslie had orthodontic treatment from about the age of 10 to 15 years.  She is not sure exactly when her jaw joint problems began.  She may have been in high school, but she remembers in college having some clicking and popping predominantly in the right jaw joint.  The left side has never bothered her too much.  She has worn a mouth splint since 2000.  Her current situation escalated when in September 2011 she cracked her maxillary left first molar.  It got infected and they were unable to control it with antibiotics and the tooth was eventually extracted.  At the same time, a sinus graft was placed into the left maxillary sinus in order to provide adequate bone for subsequent implant placement.  Leslie has not had the implant placed as of yet.  

Leslie has no problems with headaches, TMJ pain or myofascial pain.  She rates her jaw function, diet, and disability at 1.  She has no trouble with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo or hearing loss.  The only other joint is her right big toe.  She is aware of clenching and bruxing at nighttime but not during the day.  

She takes the following medications:

Zyrtec.
Avianne (BCP).

In frontal view, Leslie has good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 23 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is 1 mm.  The maxillary midline is about 2.5 to 3 mm off to the left side.  The mandibular dental midline and chin are pretty much on the facial midline.  In profile, the maxilla is retruded about 5 to 6 mm.  The chin is retruded about 20 mm.  The chin neckline is short and chin neck angle is not well defined.  Intraorally, Leslie has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars.  She has some significant crowding in the lower anterior arch with probably about 5 mm of crowding.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars except she is missing the left first molar.  She has a slight Class II occlusion on the left side by probably 2 to 3 mm and on the right side she is Class III by probably 5 to 6 mm.  She has about a 2 mm anterior open bite.  She has about a 4 mm overjet relationship.  When Leslie occludes in centric relation, she occludes only on the second molars and all other teeth are out of occlusion.  Oropharyngeal tissues look normal and healthy.  Intranasally, the turbinates appear moderately enlarged.  The septum appears reasonably straight.  The columella appears moderately wide and nostrils are moderately narrow.  In the lower arch, she has relatively symmetric AP position of the cuspids.  In the upper arch, the left cuspid is about 2 mm posterior to the right cuspid.  To palpation, she has no tenderness around the head and neck area.  On opening, she has a pop in the right joint at about 40 mm and a closing pop at about 20.  The left side functions relatively smoothly.  The maximum incisal opening is 50 mm.  The excursion movement to the right is 10 mm and to the left 9 mm.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars.  She has had what appears to be an HA and bone graft done to the left maxillary sinus in preparation for a future implant to replace the missing left first molar.  She is missing the mandibular left first molar and all four third molars.  Turbinates appear moderate in size.  Sinuses appear relatively clear.  Both condyles appear arthritic.  

Coronal view shows that the septum is reasonably straight, but she has enlarged turbinates.  The sinuses appear relatively clear except for just a little bit of sinus membrane thickening on the left lateral nasal wall.  

Axial view shows that the turbinates are moderate in size and somewhat obstructive particularly on the left side.  The sinuses appear clear in this particular view.  

Left TMJ sagittal view shows that she may have not been biting all the way in centric relation at least on the left side.  There is evidence of arthritis in this joint with significant anterior beaking.  The articular eminence has a moderate inclination.  At the lateral aspect of the joint, the condyle is quite flattened.  There is some sclerosis on the top of the condyle.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is arthritic with anterior beaking present.  There is decreased superior and posterior joint space.  The articular eminence has a moderate inclination and is fairly long in length.  The anterior head of the condyle appears a little bit more degenerated than the opposite side.

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has undergone some arthritic changes.  There appears to be loss of vertical volume.  The joint space however appears reasonably normal.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 mm.  

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has arthritic changes associated with it.  There appears to be decreased vertical joint space in some of the views as compared to the opposite side.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 to 3 mm.  

Radiographically, the bone grafting into the maxillary sinus is evident.  It appears there were also some HA grafts put into the extraction socket position.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows there is a retruded maxilla and significantly retruded mandible.  There is a high occlusal plane angle.  The oropharyngeal airway measures about 2.5 mm at the soft palate level and also no more than 3 mm at the base of the tongue and shows that there is no significant discrepancy.  

Basic diagnoses for Leslie would be as follows:

Bilateral TMJ arthritic and articular discs anteriorly displaced without reduction on the left side and with late reduction on the right side.
Maxillary AP and posterior vertical hypoplasia.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia.
Class II occlusion.
Anterior open bite of 2 mm.
High occlusal plane angle.
Severely decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of 2 mm (normal dimension 11 mm).
Occludes only on the left second molars in centric occlusion. 
Significant crowding of the lower anterior arch.
Mild crowding in the upper anterior arch.
Slight increased curve of Spee in the upper arch.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Presurgical orthodontics.
	a.	Align and level maxillary arch.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and 			cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. 
	b.	Align and level mandibular arch.  Slenderize the lower anterior teeth 			removing 1 or 2 mm of tooth structure to keep from flaring the lower teeth 		too far forward.  
2.	Surgery Stage 1.  Consider bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament 	repair with Mitek anchors as a separate stage if done within the next couple of 	months.
3.	Surgery Stage 2.  
	a.	Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek 			anchors (if not separated out in a separate procedure).
	b.	Bilateral mandibular osteotomies to advance the mandible in a 				counterclockwise direction.
	c.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla in a 					counterclockwise direction.
	d.	Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy to augment the chin.
4.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.  
5.	Placement of osteointegrated implant to the maxillary left first molar area.  
6.	Restorative dentistry.  

We did discuss at length the possibility that the Mitek anchor technique may not work because of the amount of degenerative and dismorphological changes that have already occurred to the TMJ articular discs.  However, if they do hold up, it would eliminate the need for bilateral total joint prostheses.  Performing the Mitek anchor technique at a separate first surgery would allow us to see how the disc repositioning would hold up while the orthodontics are being done prior to the orthognathic portion of the treatment.  Another option would be to go straight to the total joint prostheses as that will give us a predictable outcome relative to the TMJ reconstruction. She understands the treatment options and will inform me as to her decision and contact me if she has further questions.

original_note:
JAMES OLIVER									09-20-02

Jim is 60 years old. 

Patient History

Nasal surgery composed of removing some nasal polyps and fixing a deviated nasal septum. He used to have asthma as a child but outgrew that when he was about 13 or 14 years old. He still has some hayfever problems.

Clinical Evaluation

In frontal view, Jim has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 21 mm. Upper tooth to lip relationship is about 2 to 3 mm. Maxillary dental midline is pretty much on the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline is shifted a little bit to the right side about a millimeter. Chin is pretty much on the facial midline.

In profile view, Jim appears to have some AP maxillary deficiency. Mandible also appears retruded, and he appears to have some AP microgenia. Chin-neckline is extremely short, although the chin-neck angle is fairly well defined.

Intraorally, Jim has all of his teeth back through the second molars. Has some crowns on the lower first molars and right second molar. Mandibular right first bicuspid is blocked almost totally out of the arch. Moderate crowding in the lower anterior arch. Has good attached gingiva around the lower anterior teeth. In the upper arch, he appears to have all of his teeth back through the second molars. Some mild crowding, upper anterior arch, particularly on the right side. Appears to have small upper lateral incisors. He basically has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship in the right side but Class I on the left side. Has a crossbite associated with the right mandibular first bicuspid. 

He has no history of headaches, TMJ pain, he has no vertigo, and no earaches. He gets occasional ringing in his ears. TMJs seem to function fairly smoothly, although there is a little bit of a rubbing noise there perhaps indicative of medial displacement or just thin tissues probably towards the lateral rim of the fossa. He has about a 2-mm overbite relationship. Incisal opening is about 55 mm and about 10 to 11 mm of excursive movements in each direction. Jim has moderate enlargement of the uvula. Faucial pillars actually appear to be fairly well anteriorly positioned and moderately wide. 

Intranasally, it appears that he has had a previous turbinectomy and also reports having a septoplasty. Columella base is a little bit wide causing some constricture of the nares. Luminal valves are somewhat restricted as well. Holding the luminal valves open made a significant improvement in his ability to breathe through his nose.




Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows the presence of all the teeth except for the third molars. Has crowns on most to the posterior teeth. Condyles appear to be broad and perhaps a little bit short vertically. Sinuses appear relatively clear.

Waters view shows that internally the septum appears fairly straight, and the turbinates appear to be small in size. The airway appears that it should be adequate.

Left TMJ tomogram shows what appears to be relatively normal architecture of the condyle, fossa, and the articular eminence with good balanced joint space.

Right TMJ x-rays are probably similar, although the images aren’t quite as clear as they were on the opposite side.

On opening, there is good translation of both condyles.

Cephalometrically, he appears to have retruded maxilla and mandible. Moderately high occlusal plane angle. Distance from soft palate to posterior pharyngeal wall at the uvula area measures about 5 mm. At the base of the tongue, it measures about 6 to 7 mm.

Basic Diagnoses

AP maxillary deficiency.
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
AP mandibular deficiency.
AP microgenia.
Class I occlusion on the left, Class II end-on occlusion on the right.
Blocked out mandibular right first bicuspid.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway, being about one third of the normal dimension.
Sleep apnea symptoms.
Constricture of luminal valves.
Nasal airway obstruction.
Slightly wide columella base.

Recommended Treatment

Recommended treatment for Jim would be as follows:

Consider sleep study

Surgery
Maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla forward and downgraft it posteriorly.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction.
AP augmentation genioplasty via osteotomy.
Reconstruct bilateral luminal valves.
Surgical narrowing of the columella base.

original_note:
EMMA MALONE									10-18-02

Emma is 34 years old and referred by Dr. Duane Keller.

Patient History

Emma’s problems began in 1988 when she had a swimming pool accident and hit her head on the bottom of the pool. This started her TMJ problems. In 1989, she saw Dr. Duane Keller, and he made splints for her. In 1992, Dr. Scott Nolen in St. Louis performed a left TMJ arthroplasty and disc repositioning procedure and a right TMJ arthroscopy. She did fairly well for 10 years. About six months ago, her problems began to worsen. About that same time, one of her kids accidently hit his head underneath Emma’s chin, and this really exacerbated her situation. Dr. Keller has since made two splints, and she has also gone through physical therapy, but the effects of that combination last only about a day. She currently takes some muscle relaxers that she doesn’t know the name of, and she takes those just p.r.n. She has earaches, no tinnitus, but experiences vertigo. Other joints that bother her include her left shoulder (she had surgery in 1995) and left elbow. She has pain in her neck, back, and shoulder area, but it appears that it may be more of a neuromuscular origin. She currently cannot eat or chew without experiencing severe pain, and, thus, has limited her diet to strictly a liquid diet.

She has no known allergies. She has no problems wearing jewelry. She experiences nocturnal clenching. She is in constant pain, and that includes waking up in the morning where there is no really significant difference in her pain levels at all during the day. Her pain is significantly exacerbated with any jaw function. She finds it extremely difficult to eat or chew and is on a liquid diet at this time. 

She rates her headaches in the temporal and posterior head area at a level of 6, and they are constant but can escalate with eating and chewing. She rates her TMJ pain at 6, although this can escalate significantly with jaw function. Overall pain is at about a level of 6 without jaw function and on a liquid diet, but any movement of the jaws can exacerbate it up to a level of 8. Jaw function rate is at 9, diet at 10, disability at 6. 

Primary Concerns

Primary concerns that Emma has include the following:

Severe TMJ pain.
Severe headaches.
Severe myofascial pain.
Total inability to eat and chew.
Significant sleep disorder related to the pain
Significant difficulties managing her kids because of the sleep deprivation and severe pain problems.
Back teeth do not touch.


Clinical Evaluation

Emma has good facial symmetry and balance in the frontal and profile views. 

Intraorally, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. May have a slight division II inclination of the maxillary incisors. She has basically a slight Class II cuspid relationship. Posterior teeth are slightly apart. Gap is greater on the left side as compared to the right side. She is missing all four third molars and maxillary left second molar. She has an anterior deep bite of about 2.5 mm. 

To palpation, she has moderate pain over the TMJ/SCM areas and moderate to severe pain over the posterior neck, right masseter areas, and the styloid areas. She has moderate pain over the temporalis muscles and medial pterygoids. To palpation, left joint seems to function reasonably smoothly, however, the right side has a very soft opening click at about 20 mm and more obvious closing click at 15 mm. Maximal incisal opening is 41 mm, and she can open to about 30 mm without too much pain. Excursive movements are about 5 mm in each direction.

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars and maxillary left second molar. She has root canals in bilateral maxillary first molars and mandibular right first molar. She has crowns on all the molar teeth. Sinuses appear relatively clear, and nasal airway appears clear. Condyles look relatively normal as best can be evaluated for this x-ray.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle with a hockey stick shape to it and decreased vertical dimension posteriorly. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Condyle appears to have fairly good morphology with a little flattening on the anterior head. If disc is present, it is probably anterior and could be in a vertical orientation.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle with fairly good morphology with a slight hockey stick shape to it. Decreased superior posterior joint space significantly. Anterior joint space may be slightly increased. Joint space would indicate that perhaps the disc is in a vertical orientation. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Translation is good in both joints with the right side being slightly better than the left side. 

Waters view shows the sinuses appear clear. Nasal airway appears reasonably good.

Cephalometrically, Emma has a slight skeletal Class II jaw relationship and slight Class II occlusion with a slight division II orientation of the maxillary incisors. 

Basic Diagnoses

Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocations.
Maxillary hyperplasia.
Class II division II end-on occlusion.
Severe TMJ pain.
Severe headaches.
Severe myofascial pain.
Inability to eat and chew.
Sleep disorder.

Recommended Treatment

Presurgical orthodontics
Align and level maxillary arch. Eliminate the division II relationship of the upper anteriors.
Align and level lower arch. Consider stripping lower anterior teeth to keep from tipping them any further forward. 

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies with bone screw stabilization.

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

Other Systems

In reviewing other systems, she has problems with the left shoulder and left elbow. No urinary tract problems. However, her periods are irregular, and she has excessive pain associated with them. She had fertility problems but does have two children, ages 3 and 1. She did have endometriosis that apparently was affecting her ability to get pregnant. She had surgery for her endometriosis and was able to get pregnant after that. She has some problems with dry eyes. She has had previous eye surgery. She smoked for four years during college about a half a pack per day but has not smoked in 10 years. She does have earaches and vertigo. She does have some fatigue problems but relates that to her sleep disorder caused by her TMJ problem. She has no thyroid problems herself, but there is a strong family history with a number of individuals in her family with thyroid disease. For about five years from the age of 19 through 24, she had two lovebirds and one cockatiel. One of the lovebirds died, and the fate of one of the cockatiels is unknown at this time. She had two cats for about five years, and one may have been able to eat birds, but they were primarily indoor cats. She has had a dog for 15 years that is primarily an inside dog. No exposure to hoofed animals. She has no implants in her body.

original_note:
KARLA RAMIREZ									08/11/2008

Karla is 18 years old and referred by Marco Navarro, DDS, 2501 Cornerstone, Edinburg, TX 78539, telephone #956-972-0013.  Karla is in good health with no major medical problems.  She takes Advil as her only medication.  She had previous orthodontics from the age of 12 to 13 and then she began orthodontics again on May 18, 2007.  She had maxillary second bicuspids and mandibular first bicuspids extracted as well as all four third molars removed.  She has been under orthodontic treatment since that time.  The spaces in the arches are getting close to being closed.  Karla's joint problems began about 2 or 3 years ago of unknown etiology.  The only treatment she has had to date has been orthodontic treatment.  Karla gets headaches 2 to 3 times a week in the temporal areas bilaterally at about a level of 7.  She gets pain in the left TMJ at a level of 3, average daily pain at 4, jaw function at 4, diet at 6, and disability at 4. 

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Karla has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 22 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is zero.  Maxillary midline is about 2 mm off to the left side.  The mandibular dental midline is a little bit closer to being on the facial midline as is the chin.  Karla has fairly good balance between her upper lips, cheeks, and nose.  Chin appears retruded and probably 10 to 12 AP deficient.  Chin neckline is a little bit short, although the chin neck angle is reasonably well defined.  Intraorally, she is missing the lower first bicuspids.  She has Class III mechanics on from lower bicuspid to upper first molars.  She has an anterior deep bite of at least 5 to 6 mm.  She has an accentuated curve of Spee in the lower arch and a slightly reversed curve of Spee in the upper arch.  She has a full Class II occlusal relationship.  Oropharyngeal tissues appear normal.  Tonsils are slightly enlarged particularly on the right side.  She does have some mild snoring.  Karla has a Bolton's difference of 2.75 mm.   

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars, the maxillary second bicuspids, and lower first bicuspids.   Sinuses look relatively clear.  
Waters view shows that the sinuses are clear.  The turbinates are fairly large and appear to obliterate most of the nasal airway.  Nasal septum is reasonably straight in the bony area.  
Right TMJ tomogram shows that there might be some decreased joint space vertically.  
Left TMJ tomogram shows that there appears to be reasonable joint space present.  The condyle has fairly good morphology and articular eminence is moderately steep.  On opening, there is good translation of both joints. 
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has a skeletal Class II and dental Class II occlusal relationship.  There is a slightly increased occlusal plane angle.  Oropharyngeal airway measures about 6 mm at the soft palate level and closer to 4 mm at the base on the tongue.  To palpation, she has no tenderness around the head and neck area.  On opening, she has some noise in her joints, but it may be the condyles coming out over the articular eminence more so than a disc displacement.  However, it is possible that she may have displaced discs, but make no noises.  Karla has an external nasal deformity with a dorsal hump present.  She would like to have this removed.  Therefore, a rhinoplasty would include:

Removal of the dorsal hump. 
Narrow the transverse width of the nose and the tip of the nose. 
Basic diagnoses would be as follows:    

Probable bilateral TMJ articular disc dislocations. 
AP mandibular hypoplasia.  
AP maxillary hypoplasia.  
Posterior vertical maxillary hypoplasia.
Class II occlusion.
Significantly decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of approximately 3..5 to 4 mm (normal 11 mm).
Snoring.
Daytime tiredness.
Missing maxillary second bicuspids, mandibular first bicuspids. 
Hypertrophied turbinates. 
Nasal airway obstruction. 
Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 3 mm.  
TMJ pain. 
Headaches.
External nasal deformity.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Presurgical orthodontics.  
Continue to align and level maxillary arch.  Open at least 1 mm of space between the maxillary laterals and cuspids to help compensate for the significant tooth size discrepancy of 3 mm.  
Align and level mandibular arch.  Continue to close up the posterior space, but try to lose posterior anchorage so the lower incisors are not overly retracted.  
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors, if  confirmed that the discs are displaced.    
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance it forward slightly and down graft it posterior as well as correct any transverse discrepancies that may be present.  
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction. 
External rhinoplasty.
3.   Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
DONNA MOORE									01-28-05

Donna is 39 years old and referred by Dr. Brenda Chockley, 8909 S. Yale, Ste. 210, Tulsa, OK  74137.

Patient History

Donna’s problems began in 11/2003 of unknown etiology. She began developing pain through the jaw muscles and down through the neck. In 01/2004, she had an NTI splint made by Dr. Aaron Harmon in Wagner, Oklahoma. This didn’t improve her situation. She then went and saw Dr. Terry Bennett and from 01/2004 through 03/2004 he constructed a splint for her and did some adjustments on it, but this actually made her pain a lot worse. Apparently it was an anterior jaw repositioning type of splint. Since then she has had chiropractic care that has not helped her. She had a consultation with a physical therapist, and he felt he could not help her. She went to an ENT specialist who could not find anything wrong with her either. She also has been to an acupuncturist, and this did not improve her situation. 

She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, or hearing loss. No other joints bother her. She has been told that she has neck problems by the chiropractic practitioner, but she is not sure if there are actual anatomical problems or not. She has seasonal allergies and is allergic to LINCOCIN. She has a little bit of problem wearing cheap earrings but not too significant. Her pain is worse in the afternoons. She teaches school and teaches Algebra II and geometry. She has to use a microphone and amplifying system for the students to hear her. She has limited jaw function. She rates her headaches at 0 and TMJ pain at 0. On her usual day at work where she has to lecture, her pain levels are about a level of 7. She rates her jaw function and diet at 7 and disability at 5. 

Donna has noted that her lower anterior teeth are becoming more crowded during the last three to four years. She said her teeth used to be fairly straight. This is the reason she went to see Dr. Chockley. 

Donna continues to wear the DTI device, and she wears it just at night, but she does find that she does bite into it. She feels that some of her bite changes and particularly the open bite factor may be related to that device. Donna has a tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 3.5 mm. 

Current Medications

Orthocycline
BuSpar 5 mg t.i.d. 
Flexeril 10 mg 2 tablets per day
Lortab 5 mg tablets, ½ tablet b.i.d. 

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Donna has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 20 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 2.5 mm. Maxillary, mandibular, and chin midlines are all pretty much on the facial midline.

In profile view, she has fairly good facial balance. Some mild weakness in the chin area but not too significant. Chin-neckline is fairly close to being normal, and chin-neckline is fairly well-defined. 	

Intraorally, Donna has all of her teeth back through the second molars present. She has some mild crowding, lower anterior arch. A little inflammation around the lower left central and lateral incisor at the gingival area. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars present. In centric occlusion, she appears to hit on the distal of her maxillary first molar and her lower first and upper and lower second molars. On the right side, she could possibly be hitting on her second molars, but the rest of her teeth are out of occlusion. Mandible is retruded back with about a 2-mm over jet relationship and about a 4 to 5-mm Class II cuspid relationship. If she postures forward to an end-on incisor relationship, she is a little bit closer to a Class I cuspid-molar relationship. Appears as though she probably has a tooth-size discrepancy. Maximal incisal opening is 23 mm and opening without significant pain only 18 mm. Excursive movements to the right are about 5 mm and to the left 3 mm. Joints seem to function relatively smoothly. To palpation, she has some mild tenderness over the TMJs but no other pain around the head and neck area.										

There is a slight transverse cant of the occlusal plane with the right side being 1 to 1.5 mm lower than the left side. 
						
Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows the sinuses appear relatively clear. Difficult to evaluate the nasal airway, but it does appear the left side is blocked compared to the right side. She is missing all four third molars but has the rest of her teeth. She has a crown on the maxillary right second molar. Left TMJ appears as though it has undergone some arthritic changes. Right joint appears a little bit longer vertically in length. 

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears postured forward in the fossa and appears to be approaching the articular eminence. It is possible that she may have been  holding her lower jaw forward when this radiograph was taken. Styloid process is calcified and quite thick and extends up and overlaps the posterior border of the mandible. Difficult to tell how far forward it comes. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows a joint that appears to have a decreased posterior joint space and maybe a little bit decreased vertical joint space. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Condyle may have a little pseudocyst in it but difficult to tell.

On opening, there is restricted translation of both TMJs. 

Waters view shows that the sinuses are clear, although the right side has a thickened sinus membrane. Right nasal airway appears blocked because of a large turbinate on that side. 

Cephalometrically, she appears to have a slight skeletal Class II relationship and a Class II occlusal relationship. There is asymmetry in the arch but don’t know if that is due to head posture. Oropharyngeal airway is good. She has an anterior open bite of probably a couple of millimeters and an anterior over jet of 2 mm. Transversely, the maxillary and mandibular occlusal planes are fairly level, but it appears that on the ceph radiograph that there is significant vertical discrepancy which would tend to make me believe that perhaps the head was tilted some when this radiograph was taken. 

Basic Diagnoses

Bilateral TMJ mild arthritis and articular disc dislocations predominantly in a medial direction with the right side being worse than the left side
AP mandibular deficiency 
Class II end-on cuspid relationship
Anterior open bite
Open bite extending from first molar to first molar
Significant tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 3.5 mm
Overangulation of lower incisors
Slight cant in the occlusal plane with the right side being about 1 mm to 1.5 mm lower than the left side
Myofascial pain

Recommended Treatment

Review MRIs with radiologist

Presurgical orthodontics 
Align and level maxillary arch 
Align and level mandibular arch. Strip lower anterior teeth removing approximately 3 mm of tooth structure to correct of the tooth-size discrepancy 

Surgery
Possible bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors depending on interpretation of the MRIs by the radiologist
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies versus maxillary osteotomies

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

original_note:
TERESA (TERRI) MAHANNA							11/04/2005

Terri is 36 years old and is referred by Dr. Tom Pitts.  

PRIMARY CONCERNS
Headaches.
Previous orthodontics with teeth extracted and the teeth pulled backwards.
Teeth becoming a little bit crooked. 

Terri does have some problems with headaches that she gets 5 to 7 days per week at times.  She rates them at about a level of 5 and these occur predominately in the frontal and temple area perhaps occurring a little bit more frequently on the right side than the left side.  She rates her average TMJ pain daily at 2, average daily pain at 2, jaw function at 0, diet at 2, disability at 0.  She does have some noises in the left jaw on opening with a slight pop at 5 to 10 mm of opening and closing pop at the same level.  The right joint seems to function reasonably smoothly.  No tenderness to palpation around the head and neck area.  

Terri's problems began around 10 years ago when she became aware of clicking and popping in her jaw joints.  The etiology is unknown.  She did have orthodontics at approximately the age of 20 to 24 and she had 4 bicuspids extracted.  

Approximately 5 weeks ago, Terri had a hysterectomy and an oophorectomy performed.  

CURRENT MEDICATIONS
Female hormones including estrogen and progesterone.
Vitamins.
Thyroid supplement. 
Advil or Aleve occasionally for headaches.

She thinks that she has some environmental allergies.  No earaches, tinnitus, vertigo or hearing loss.  No other joints bother her.  She does have some problems perhaps wearing cheap earrings.  She is aware of clenching of her teeth at night and also during the day.  To palpation she has no tenderness around the head and neck area.  

In frontal view, Terri has fairly good left to right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 21 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is 2 mm.  Maxillary midline is fairly close to being on the facial midline.  Mandibular dental midline is shifted about 1 mm to the left.  Mandibular dental midline and chin appear to be pretty much in line with each other.  In profile view, Terri has some AP maxillary deficiency of probably about 4 to 6 mm.  The upper lip is retruded by probably about 4 to 6 mm and the lower lip is retruded by probably about 6 mm as is the chin.  The lower lip is a little bit more retruded than the chin.  Chin neckline is just a little bit short but not too bad.  Chin-neck angle is very well defined.  Terri has a Class II end-on occlusal relationship.  She has about a 3.5 to 4 mm vertical overbite relationship.  The right joint functions smoothly.  The left one has a click at about 5 to 10 mm of opening and about 5 mm of closing.  No tenderness to palpation around the head and neck area.  

TERESA TERRI MAHANNAH  		11/04/2005				Page 2


Maximal incisal opening is about 41 mm.  Excursive movements to the right 9 mm, to the left 8 mm.  On reevaluation, on opening it appears she has a little bit of click in the right side also probably about 5 mm of opening and closing though the left side is more obvious and prominent than the right side.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION – Date of radiographs are 2/28/2005:
Panorex shows the absence of all mandibular second bicuspids, maxillary first bicuspids.  All 4 third molars are also absent.  The condyles appear large in size.  Lateral pole appears fairly prominent.  Right maxillary sinus appears more opacified than the left side.  Terri gives no history of chronic sinus problems.   She reports no problems breathing through her nose.  

Lateral cephalogram shows that the nasal septum is reasonably straight but some curvature to it and may be little spur off the left side.  Turbinates appear moderately large.  The right sinus appears opacified as compared to the left one.  

BASIC DIAGNOSES 
Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocations with early reduction. 
AP maxillary deficiency.
AP mandibular deficiency predominately in the alveolus.
Macrogenia. 
Class II end-on occlusion. 
Anterior deep bite. 
Absent maxillary first bicuspids and mandibular second bicuspids and all 4 third molars.
Vertical inclination of maxillary and mandibular incisors.
TMJ pain. 
Headaches.
Low occlusal plane angle. 

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT
Presurgical orthodontics. 
Continue to align and level maxillary arch.  Open up space around the maxillary lateral incisors and cuspids to help increase the axial inclination of the maxillary incisors and to correct the tooth size discrepancy.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies.
Continue to align and level mandibular arch.
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Maxillary osteotomies to advance it forward and increase the occlusal plane angle.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible forward in a clockwise direction.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
LAWRENCE (BURT) PIERCE						05/05/2011

Birth date 07/26/1963.  Age 47 years.  He is referred by Dr. Urszula Firlik, 6225 West River Drive NE, Belmont, MI 49306-9025.

Burt’s TMJ problems began in the late teens to early twenties in around 1983.  The etiology is unknown.  He had orthodontics at the age of 16 with extraction of four first bicuspids.  He developed significant symptoms in his early twenties and by age 25 he began his odyssey of splint therapy.  He has had five splints.  His TMJs did get somewhat better.  The most effective splint has been the current one that displaces his tongue inferiorly and forward.  In September 2008, he got very sick where he developed problems with his immune system and his protection from his immune system dropped way down.  He was hospitalized and ended up with shingles on his face and was sick for months.  He has been aware of sleep apnea for a number of years.  He has had a previous sleep study which showed he has severe sleep apnea.  He has had a total of five splints constructed.  He was placed on a CPAP machine in 2010 and has used it for the past year.  He continued to have significant problems so on 04/15/2011, he had bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies and a septoplasty.  He currently takes no medications.  He wakes up with headaches in the morning at about a level of 8 in the frontal and probably the anterior temple area.  He rates his TMJ pain at a level of 8, average daily pain at 5, jaw function at 6, diet at 4, disability at 7.  The incisal opening is 52 mm and excursion movements are 9 mm in each direction.  On opening and closing of his jaws, he has glitches in both TMJ joints.  To palpation, he has some mild tenderness over the left TMJ.  He has no problems with earaches, tinnitus or vertigo.  Other joints that bother him include his neck and his ankles.  He is allergic to pollens and cats.  He does have a history of shingles.  He is also aware that he clenches at nighttime.  He has a Class I occlusion with about a 4 mm anterior vertical over bite relationship.  

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  Upper lip length is 28 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is zero.  Midlines are well aligned.  In profile, the maxilla appears to be retruded 8 to 10 mm and the chin appears to be retruded 25 mm.  He is missing all four first bicuspids, but has erupted third molars.  He appears to occlude only on the second and third molars bilaterally with all other teeth out of occlusion.  He has just a slight Class II cuspid-molar relationship.  He has an anterior open bite of about 3 mm.  He has some mild crowding of the lower anterior arch.  

Primary concerns that Burt has at this time include the following:

Severe sleep apnea and the side effects of it. 
Bites only on his very back teeth and all other teeth do not meet.
Foggy thinking.
Difficulties eating and chewing. 
TMJ pain and headaches.  
He does have a high occlusal plane facial morphology.  
Oropharyngeal tissues look healthy, but uvula is moderately enlarged but the other structures look relatively normal.  Intranasally, the right turbinate appears a little bit large, yet the left one at least anteriorly appears a little bit normal in size.  The septum appears reasonably straight.  To palpation, he has some mild tenderness over the left TMJ.  On jaw function, he has some glitches in his joints.  The incisal opening is 52 mm and excursion movements are about 9 mm in each direction.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all four first bicuspids.  All four third molars are in place.  Sinuses look relatively clear.  He has elongated styloid processes bilaterally.  

Left TMJ coronal view shows a defect in the condylar head as a depression possibly related to the orientation of his articular disc within that joint.  He has a decreased lateral joint space.  The lateral rim curves inferiorly about 2 to 3 mm.  

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with fairly good mediolateral width.  The lateral rim curves inferiorly about 2 mm.  There is decreased lateral joint space.  

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has a significant depression in the middle of it.  There is some anterior beaking present.  The articular eminence has a moderate inclination.  The lateral joint space is decreased.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is somewhat posteriorly postured in the fossa.  The articular eminence is a little bit more shallow on this side.  The joint space looks reasonably good, although decreased at the lateral aspect of the joint.  

Coronal view shows that the septum is reasonably straight.  The turbinates are moderate in size on the right side, but the left side appears more normal.  The sinuses appear relatively clear with just a little bit of increased thickness of the sinus membrane on the floor of the sinus on the right side.  

Axial view shows the sinuses are clear and the right turbinate has a moderate enlargement, but the left one appears more normal in size.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that he has a retruded maxilla and mandible, although fairly close to a Class I cuspid molar relationship.  Oropharyngeal airway measures about 7 mm at the soft palate level and about 9 mm at the base of the tongue.  The head may be tipped up slightly which may increase the size of the airway.  He does have an anterior open bite of about 3 to 4 mm.  

Basic diagnoses for Burt would be as follows:

Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation.
Maxillary AP and posterior vertical hypoplasia.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia.
Apertognathia.  
Pain.
Sleep apnea.
High occlusal plane angle.
Occluding only on the maxillary and mandibular second and third molars. 

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Presurgical orthodontics. 
	a.	Align and level maxillary arch in two occlusal plane levels.  Align the 			cuspids through third molars at one occlusal plane level and place a 			vertical step in the arch wire to align the four incisors at an elevated plane.  		Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room 			for interdental osteotomies.  
	b.	Align and level mandibular arch.  Slenderize the lower anterior teeth 			removing 2 mm of tooth structure to keep from flaring the lower incisors 			any further forward.
2.	CT scan of jaws and jaw joints. 
3.	Surgery.
	a.	Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ 			Concepts total joint prostheses. 
	b.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies.
	c.	Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen).
	d.	Bilateral coronoidotomies. 
4.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B186
B186 is 16 years 11 months and 3 weeks old. Referred by Dr. XXXX. B186 is in good health with no major medical problems. B186's primary concerns are as follows:

Jaws lock.
Jaws click and pop.
Pain when eating.
B186 has had his wisdom teeth removed 7 months ago. B186's joint problems began when he was about 12 years old of unknown etiology. He has had no treatment to date. B186 rates his headaches at a level of 5 and they occur in the temporal area and are worse when he eats. He rates his TMJ pain at 5, average daily pain at 4, jaw function at 7, diet at 7, disability at 3. He gets earaches and tinnitus occasionally as well as vertigo. Other joints that bother him include his left wrist, knees, and ankles. Being a hockey player though may contribute to some of his joint problems. He has been diagnosed with ADHD. He is not aware of clenching or bruxism.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
In frontal view, B186 does have good facial symmetry and balance. He has got problems with his masseter muscles. In profile, he has good facial balance. Intraorally, B186 has all of his lower teeth back through the second molars. There is some mild crowding in the lower anterior arch. In the upper arch, he has all of his teeth back through the second molars. He has a good Class I cuspid-molar relationship bilaterally, and he has about a 2 mm vertical overbite. Intranasally, the septum is deviated a little bit off towards the left side and turbinates are moderate in size. To palpation, he has no tenderness around the head and neck area. On opening, he has clicking in both joints and actually multiple clicks on the right side and does not click quite as much as usual on his left side. The incisal opening is 55. The excursion movements are 11 mm in each direction. The opening clicks are somewhere around 15 mm and there are closing clicks around 10 mm.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. Sinuses look clear. Turbinates appears moderate in size. Bony septum at least appears straight. Difficult to evaluate the joints.

Coronal view shows that he has hypertrophied turbinates particularly on the right side. Sinuses look reasonably clear with just a little bit fluid with a little retention cyst in the right sinus along the medial wall. The septum is deviated with a spur off towards the left side and then deviates off a little bit towards the right side near the superior aspect of the septum.

Axial view shows that the right turbinate is moderately enlarged, and the left one appears a little bit normal. Sinuses appear relatively clear except for a little mucous retention area in the posterior medial recess of the maxillary sinus.

Left TMJ sagittal tomogram shows a condyle that is narrow anteroposteriorly. There are some degenerative changes present. There is a little bit of anterior beaking present. The articular eminence has a moderate inclination. The anterior joint space is increased.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is narrow anteroposteriorly, but not quite as much as the opposite side. The articular eminence is moderately steep. The joint space appears adequate with some slight increase anteriorly.

Left TMJ coronal view shows flattening on the condylar head. The joint space appears reasonably good, although slightly decreased at the lateral aspect of the joint.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has a little bit better shape than the opposite side and there appears to be an adequate joint space. There is some slight decrease at the lateral aspect of the joint.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that B186 has fairly good AP and vertical facial balance and appears to have a good Class I and skeletal relationship. Oropharyngeal airway measures about 8 mm at the base of the tongue and probably about 6 mm at the soft palate level.

Basic diagnoses would be as follows:
Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with reduction. Mandibular hyperplasia. TMJ pain.
Headaches.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1. Presurgical orthodontics. a.	Align and level maxillary arch. b.	Align and level mandibular arch.
2. Surgery. a.	Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor. b.	Bilateral ramus osteotomies to maintain the occlusion and allow the condyle to move down and forward to create space for the articular disc.
3. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B186 MRI AGE: 16/M
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears narrow mediolaterally. The lateral rim of the fossa is quite flat. There seems to be reasonable joint space present.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears somewhat small in size. The lateral rim of the fossa is quite flat. There is some joint space present.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has significant resorption vertically and anteroposteriorly. The articular eminence is moderately steep. The articular disc is anteriorly displaced and sitting down almost beneath the articular eminence. The disc must be medially displaced as well as it not as identifiable towards the lateral aspect of the joint as it is medially. The condyle is posteriorly positioned in the fossa. There appears to be a little bit of fluid in the superior joint space towards the lateral aspect of the joint. On opening, there is moderate translation of the condyle and reduction of the articular disc. However, the disc itself does not move much at all. There is some anterior beaking on the condyle at the lateral aspect of the joint.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has a little bit better morphology than the opposite side. The articular disc is anteriorly displaced sitting almost beneath the articular eminence. The articular eminence is moderately steep. The condyle is posteriorly positioned in the fossa. On opening, the disc reduces and there is some restriction of translation of the condyle. The disc itself does not appear to move significantly.

IMPRESSION: Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular discs anteriorly displaced with reduction on opening, but without translation of the discs indicative of adhesions to the fossa.

original_note:
ALLISON PARKS									07/12/2010

Allison is 28 years old.   Allison is basically a self-referral.  Allison’s TMJ problems began to the best of her knowledge in about 2005 shortly after she had a tonsillectomy performed.  She had a lot of pain in the joints at that time and clicking in the joints.  It was not until about 2007 that she began noticing a change in her occlusion and jaw alignment.  She feels she is still in a progressive state of her lower jaw and occlusion continue to change.  Most of the pain has subsided that she did have initially.  She has no problems with headaches, rates her TMJ pain at 2, but on the average day has no significant pain around the head and neck area.  She rates her jaw function at 4, diet at 3, disability at 4.  She has no earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, nor hearing loss.  The only other joint that bothers her is her right knee where she collects fluid and she has had it drained seven times.  The only treatment she has had to date is Invisaline from October 2009 through April 2010.  She has been to see seven doctors in reference to her current situation.  She has had a number of different opinions and recommendations.  

CLINICAL EVALUATION.  In frontal view, Allison has fairly good symmetry in the middle and upper third face, but has some asymmetry in the lower third face.  Upper lip length is 25 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is 5 mm.  The maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline and so is the mandibular dental midline and chin.  She appears to have a little  bit more fullness on the right side of her face than the left by probably about 3 or 4 mm.  In profile, the maxilla appears to be retruded about 4 to 5 mm.  The chin appears to be retruded about 20 mm.  The chin neckline is short and chin neck angle is not defined.  Intraorally, Allison has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars in place.  She has good attached gingiva.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  She has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship being about 4 to 5 mm Class II and she has an overjet of about 4 mm.  She has an anterior open bite of about 2 mm.  In centric relation, she occludes only her first and second molars bilaterally.  Allison has a posterior yaw with the maxilla and mandible being rotated off towards the right side about 2 mm.  Transversely, the occlusal plane is level.  Oropharyngeal tissues look reasonably healthy except the soft palate is a little bit shorter on the left side than the right side and it appears to be a little scar there probably related to the tonsillectomy.  Allison has a wide columella and narrow nostrils.  Holding the nostrils open enabled her to breathe much easier through her nose.  In profile, Allison tends to tip her head up significantly and posture her chin forward and has a slight head postured forward neck position.  To palpation, she has some very mild tenderness over the masseters and TMJ area.  Maximum opening is 43 mm, excursion movement to the right 7 mm, to the left 8 mm.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all third molars.  She has severe condylar resorption present bilaterally. Turbinates appear moderate in size.  The sinuses appear reasonably clear.  
Coronal radiograph shows that the sinuses are relatively clear with just some slight increased thickness of the mucosal on the floor of the left sinus.  Inferior turbinates are quite large.  

Axial view shows that the sinuses are clear in this perspective, but the turbinates are quite large and appear to block off a good portion of the airway. 

Left TMJ sagittal view shows severe destruction of the condyles with sclerosis on the head of the condyle.  There is increased joint space.  Articular eminences are fairly shallow.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a severe resorption of the condyle and condylar neck.  The articular eminence is relatively flat.  There is increased joint space.  

Left TMJ coronal view shows severe destruction of the condyles.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 mm.  There is increased vertical joint space present.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has undergone more loss of bone than it appears than the left side.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 to 4 mm.  There is increased joint space vertically.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that her mandible is postured forward and she was not in centric relation when the x-ray was taken.  She has a Class II skeletal and occlusal relationship.  Oropharyngeal airway is significantly constricted with an AP airway of only about 3 mm at the base of the tongue.  At the soft palate level, she has about 4 mm of airway space.  The lower incisors appear over angulated.  The upper incisors appear somewhat vertical in orientation.  She has a moderately high occlusal plane angle.  

Dental model analysis shows there is not any significant tooth size discrepancy, but there is a transverse deficiency in the maxilla.  The transverse deficiency is approximately 5 mm.  

Allison has had a rheumatology workup.  She has HLA-B27 antibodies that have been detected.  

Basic diagnoses for Allison would be as follows:

Bilateral TMJ idiopathic condylar resorption compatible with connective tissue autoimmune process.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia. 
Maxillary AP posterior vertical hypoplasia, but anterior maxillary vertical hyperplasia and transverse hypoplasia. 
Hypertrophied turbinates. 
Severely decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of 3 mm (normal AP dimension 11 mm).
Wide columella and narrow nostrils.
Class II occlusion.
Anterior open bite.
Occluding only on the first and second molars bilaterally with all other teeth out of occlusion. 

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Presurgical orthodontics.
	a.	Align and level maxillary arch.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots 			distally to make room for interdental osteotomies.  
	b.	Align and level mandibular arch.  
2.	CT scan of jaws and jaw joints. 
3.	Surgery.
	a.	Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts 			total joint prostheses.
	b.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla in a counterclockwise 			direction and expand.
	c.	Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen).
	d.	Bilateral mandibular coronoidotomies.
	e.	Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies. 
	f.	Columella reconstruction. 
4.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
