original_note:
MERIDITH LANE									03/28/2006

Meridith is 14 years old.  She has a partial complex seizure problem.  She had her wisdom teeth removed in June of 2005.  She takes Trileptal 300 mg 2 times a day and Tylenol for headaches.  Meridith had one generalized seizure in November of 2004 and prior to that she was aware of having some staring types of problems but since being on the medication she has had no reoccurrences.  When Meridith was born she was a twin and it is thought that maybe her position on her jaw and face created some noticeable asymmetry at birth.  Some of this has resolved but there is still a component of that remaining.  Meridith has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo and no other joint problems.  She is not aware of clenching or bruxism.  She gets headaches about 2 times per month in the temple areas about a level of 4.  She rates her TMJ pain at 0, average daily pain at 0, jaw function at 4, diet at 0 and disability at 3.  

In frontal view, Meridith has upper lip length of about 19 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 4 to 5 mm.  The nasal tip is off to the right about 2 to 3 mm.  Maxillary midline is off to the right side about 1.5 mm.  Mandibular dental midline is off to the right about 2 mm.  Meridith has a lip incompetence of about 8 mm.  The right side of the mandible in the lower jaw appears to be about 4 mm more prominent in transverse projections as compared to the left side of the face.  There is a transverse yaw in the maxilla and mandible where the posterior aspect of the occlusion is 2 to 3 mm to the right.  This accounts for the frontal transverse asymmetry.  Oropharyngeal tissues look healthy.  The uvula, soft palate and faucial pillars are all within normal range.  The tonsils are present and appear healthy.  In profile view she has a C-shaped curve to the upper lip.  Upper lip supralabrale is slightly posterior to the subnasale.  Upper lip appears to be retruded by about 4 to 5 mm.  Chin appears to be retruded by about 18 to 20 mm.  Chin-neckline is extremely short and chin-neck angle is not well-defined.  Lower third of the face appears long vertically.  Intraorally, Meridith has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars erupted in and they appear to be nicely aligned and leveled.  She has been wearing orthodontic appliances for approximately 3 years now.  In the upper arch she has all of her teeth also back through the second molars present.  Meridith has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship bilaterally being a little bit worse on the right side than the left side.  She is probably 4 to 5 mm Class II on the right side and probably a 3 mm Class II on the left side.  She has an anterior open bite.  She has an overjet of about 3 mm.  There is a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right side being 2 mm lower than the left side in both upper and lower back.  Intranasally, the septum may be deviated off towards the right side as the entire nose is deviated off in that direction but relative to the nasal alignment the septum appears reasonably straight.  The turbinates appear moderately enlarged but there appears at least anteriorly to be a passable airway.  The columella is somewhat wide.  The nostrils are somewhat constricted in width.  The luminal valves are mildly constricted.  She has mild snoring at night but no daytime somnolence and no sleep apnea symptoms.  She is predominately a mouth breather at night and also during the day.  She is fairly heavily involved in athletics including volleyball and tennis and she likes to run a lot.  To palpation, she has some mild tenderness over the styloid area.  In centric relation as she opens she has to manipulate her jaw in order to get it open as she has a displaced disc in the right TMJ.  It reduces at about 15 mm of opening.  On closing back in centric relation there is a closing click at about 5 mm.  When she normally opens and closes, her mandible is postured forward some so that disc appears that a lot of her normal jaw function may be reduced.  It is only when she goes into centric relation that the right side comes out.  
MERIDITH LANE				03/28/2006				Page 2


The left side seems to function relatively smoothly.  So, the TMJ problem is unknown and etiology is unknown.  She is unaware of having any particular tenderness or soreness in that right TMJ.  Maximal incisal opening is about 45 mm.  Excursive movement to the right side about 8 mm, left side 7 mm.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION 
Panorex shows the absence of all 4 third molars but there is an area of calcification in the right maxilla a little bit distal to where the third molar would normally be seen.  It could possibly be a 4th molar or possible a root tip but unlikely with the 3rd molars being removed at this age.  The sinuses look relatively healthy.  The right turbinate appears large compared to the left one.  The condyles look a little bit arthritic particularly on the left side.  

Waters view shows that there may be a mucocele in the sinuses bilaterally being on the floor on the left side and it could possibly be one on the lateral aspect of the sinus wall on the right side.  The septum is reasonably straight with a few little curves in it.  The right turbinate appears enlarged compared to the left turbinate but there appears to be constricted airway.  

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears a little bit small so there may have been a little bit of erosion associated with it.  It is posteriorly positioned in the fossa.  Articular eminence is relatively normal in steepness.  There appears to be decreased posterior joint space on this side.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears to have undergone some bony changes.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  There appears to be a slight decreased posterior joint space and little bit of increased anterior joint space.  On opening, there is good translation in both condyles but both condyles do look somewhat arthritic.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Meridith has an anterior vertical maxillary excess, AP mandibular deficiency and a high occlusal plane angle.  There is vertical asymmetry present.  She was not in centric relation on the initial x-ray.  Oropharyngeal airway dimension is about 6 mm at the soft palate level and about 8 mm at the base of the tongue.  She carries her tongue relatively low in her mouth.  The turbinate extends back to the posterior aspect of the hard palate.  A little bit of adenoid tissue is still remaining in the nasal pharynx.  It appears that there is perhaps a mucocele in one of the sinuses.  There is a tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of only about 1.5 mm.  

BASIC DIAGNOSES 
Bilateral TMJ idiopathic condylar resorption and articular disc dislocations. 
AP maxillary deficiency.
Anterior vertical maxillary excess, posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
AP mandibular deficiency. 
AP microgenia.
Anterior mandibular vertical excess.
MERIDITH LANE				03/28/2006				Page 3


Class II end-on occlusion.
Open bite tendency.
Lip incompetence. 
Transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right side being 2 mm lower than the left side. 
Frontal facial asymmetry with the right side of the face being considerably fuller than the left side.
Yaw in both the maxilla and mandible with the jaws being shifted about 3 mm off towards the right side. 
Hypertrophied turbinates. 
Retention cyst in the left maxillary sinus.
Moderately decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Snoring.
Nasal airway obstruction.
Predominate mouth breather. 
Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 1 to 2 mm.
Apertognathia with occlusion only on the second molars in centric relation and all other teeth are out of occlusion. 
External nasal asymmetry. 

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Presurgical orthodontics.
Continue to align and level maxillary arch.
Continue to align and level mandibular arch.  Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing about 1 mm to 1.5 mm of tooth structure and close the spacing.
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla forward approximately 5 mm and move it upwards about 3 to 4 mm as well as down graft the posterior aspect 2 mm.
Advance the mandible in counterclockwise direction approximately 14 mm.
Vertical reduction AP augmentation genioplasty shortening the chin about 5 mm and advancing it 4 mm.
Bilateral partial turbinectomies.
External rhinoplasty. 
Remove mucus retention cyst in left maxillary sinus. 
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
STEVE LINDLEY									05/23/2010

Steve is 61 years old and a self-referral.  On April 27, 2010, Steve went to Baylor College of Dentistry for a tooth extraction of the mandibular right second molar.  The tooth was removed.  During the process, he felt a pop in his left TMJ that radiated into his ear canal and through his face.  The next day his face swelled up and he came back in and had to have the area lanced as there was apparently an infection that occurred.  He was placed on antibiotics for 11 days.  The area appears well healed now without any residual evidence of infection; however, he continues to have problems with the left jaw joint.  He has popping the left jaw joint, limited opening, and the mandible deviates towards the left side on opening approximately 4 mm.  He has developed “migraine” headaches since the incident.  These headaches are daily and occur in the left frontal and temporal area at a level of 6.  He rates his normal TMJ pain at 3, but with jaw function it escalates to 7.  Average daily pain is at 5.  Jaw function, diet, and disability are all at 6.  Maximal incisal opening is 37 mm, but opening without pain 30 mm.  The excursion movement to the right 4.5 mm, to the left 9 mm.  On opening, the right joint functions smoothly, but the left joint has a soft click at about 15 mm of opening and a more prominent click at 10 mm of closing.  On excursion, however, there is a consistent click in the left TMJ area.  To palpation, he just has some mild tenderness over the left medial pterygoid area.  He takes no medications.  He has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, nor vertigo.  No other joints bother him.  He does get pain that goes down into his left neck and shoulder area.  He wakes up stiff and sore in the mornings, but the pain is worse when eating.  He is aware of clenching and bruxism at nighttime.  In profile, Steve has a retruded maxilla and mandible and AP microgenia.  He is absent the chin neckline and angle.  He has basically a Class II end-on cuspid relationship being about 3 or 4 mm Class I on the right side and just slight Class II by maybe 2 mm on the left side.  He has an anterior deep bite of about 2 mm.  He is missing his mandibular left second molar, mandibular right first molar with the second and third molars drifted forward and the missing the rest of the third molars.  He has crowns on a few of the maxillary posterior teeth.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows that he is missing the mandibular left second and third molar, maxillary left third molar, mandibular right first molar with the second and third molars drifted forward, and the maxillary right third molar.  Sinuses look relatively healthy.  He does have arthritis in both of his TMJ joint space.  

Coronal view shows that the sinuses are clear and the turbinates are relatively normal in size.  He does have a septal spur off to the left side of the nose. 

Axial view shows that the sinuses are clear.  The septum is deviated off towards the right side anteriorly and then towards the left side at the spur area.  The sinuses are clear.  Turbinates are moderate in size. 
Left TMJ sagittal view shows that he has anterior beaking present, but appears to have good vertical joint space.  There is some sclerosis on the head of the condyle and also on the articular eminence and posterior wall of the fossa.  Articular eminence is moderately steep. 

Right TMJ shows some anterior beaking present.  The articular eminence is moderately steep.  There is good superior and posterior joint space.  The joint space adjacent to the articular eminence appears somewhat narrowed.

Left TMJ coronal view shows that there is good joint space throughout the joint medially, superiorly, and at the lateral rim.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has a little bit of peaking of the condylar head, but appears to be good joint space through the joint.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Steve has a retruded maxilla and mandible.  oropharyngeal airway surprisingly is better than anticipated with about a 7 mm space at the soft palate level and almost 9 at the base of the gone. 

Basic diagnoses for Steve would be as follows:

Bilateral TMJ arthritis.
Left TMJ articular disc dislocation with reduction. 

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Surgery:
1.	Left TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor.
2.	Possible left mandibular ramus osteotomy with bone plate and screw stabilization. 
3.	Application of arch bars. 

I feel this treatment plan will get Steve the best treatment outcome.  At this time, he wants to wait and see how things progress for him.  He is to keep me informed of his progress.

original_note:
KATHERINE MCNERNEY								04-10-03

Katie is 20 years old and referred by Dr. Duane Keller, 3929 Bayless Ave., St. Louis, MO 63125-1437.

Patient History

Katie’s problems began probably when she was about 12 years old when her jaw got locked open. She was able to manipulate it and get it closed. She feels she has been aware of clicking and popping in her jaw joints since that time or even perhaps before. When she was 13, she had her third molars removed, and this severely aggravated the situation. By age 16, her problems began to worsen. She has been on numerous splints during this time, mainly night splints, and it has only been since August of 2002 that she has been in a splint full-time. She feels that the splint, however, may make her situation and discomfort worse. She has been on medications for her condition, primarily Celebrex, and she has had osteopathic treatment and physical therapy treatment. 

She was managing reasonably well until last summer, when she received a couple of concussions at a military camp. Since that time, her condition has further worsened. She gets earaches occasionally. She gets tinnitus, and this sometimes can get quite severe where she essentially can’t hear conversations. She gets vertigo, particularly when she stands up but doesn’t seem to have much of a problem with that if she is sitting down or if she is already up and moving about. She is aware of nocturnal clenching and bruxism. She gets shooting pains up through her neck that go up through the back of her head. She has fairly constant headaches that she registers at about a level of 4 in the posterior head and temporal area, but she gets severe ones that occur usually every Thursday, and she may have another one or two during the week if she is under a lot of stress. The headaches tend to start posteriorly and then work forward. She does have rather constant pain in her TMJ area. Katie rates her TMJ pain at about a level of 4 and overall facial pain at 4. Her mother registers it at about a level of 7, and Katie says it does escalate up to 8 at times. She rates her jaw function at 2, diet at 2, and disability at 4. Her mother feels that the disability is more at a level of 7 because oftentimes there are days that Katie cannot really do very much at all and has to spend a lot of time in bed.

Katie has to sleep on her side. She can’t sleep on her back because she just is unable to go to sleep in that position.

Current Medications

She currently takes Celebrex 200 to 600 mg per day and also BCP.

Patient Concerns

Primary concerns that Katie has, therefore, include the following:

Headaches 
TMJ pain
Pain down to the neck and back area
Severe ringing in the ears
Difficulties eating and chewing some foods
Difficulties breathing through the nose
Snoring

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Katie has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 18 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 3 mm. Maxillary, mandibular, and chin midlines are all pretty much on the facial midline. 

In profile view, Katie has probably about 4 to 6 mm of AP maxillary deficiency. Chin appears deficient by about 10 to 12 mm. Chin-neckline is short, and chin-neck angle is not well-defined. Katie and her family have noticed that the lower jaw has become more retruded than it was before these problems began.
Intraorally, she has all of her lower teeth actually back through the second molars. She seems to have adequate attached gingival around the lower anterior teeth although quite thin in some areas. There is some mild crowding in the lower anterior arch. In the upper arch, she is missing the first bicuspids, but the rest of the teeth are present back through the second molars. She has an end-on Class II cuspid relationship bilaterally of probably about 3 mm. Upper laterals appear small in size. She has about a 3-mm overjet relationship. She has a vertical overbite of about 1 mm. Transversely, the maxilla and mandible appear fairly level. Intraorally, the oropharynx appears clear. The uvula is about normal in shape and size.

Intranasally, the septum is deviated a little bit off toward the right side. Right turbinate is moderately large and seems to decrease the functional airway. In the left side of the nose, it appears she has a nasal polyp up superiorly and posteriorly along the septum. The inferior turbinate is moderately enlarged. The columella base is extremely wide due to lateral flaring of the alar feet. When the nostrils are physically opened with a speculum, she had significant improvement in her breathing through her nose indicative of the fact that the wide columella base is causing significant dysfunction of her nasal airway.

To palpation, she has high moderate to severe pain in the posterior neck area and moderate pain around the masseters, SCMs, shoulders, and mild to moderate in the temporalis areas. She had no pain to palpation directly over the joints in the resting position, but upon function, the pain level escalated to about a 5. Maximum incisal opening is 51 mm but without significant discomfort 28 mm. Excursive movement to the right is 9 mm and to the left 12 mm. 

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows the sinuses are relatively clear. The turbinates are extremely large and seem to block off a lot of the airway. It is interesting that in the nasal septum superiorly there is a significant lateral projection of the septum that seems to be occluding the airway. On the left side, the nasal polyp can be seen, and the turbinate on that side is moderately large. She is missing all four third molars and the maxillary first bicuspids. Condyles appear to have undergone significant resorption with the left side looking worse than the right side.  

Waters view shows that the sinuses look clear. Right nasal airway appears to have some significant obstruction. On the left side, it appears that there is some airway above the inferior turbinate. Condyles look quite small in size in the coronal type of view and probably maybe a third of normal width with significant loss of condylar volume vertically and transversely. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears to have undergone some significant degenerative changes. The condyle actually looks larger here than it does on the MRIs. Joint space appears a little better balanced; however, this may be due to the wearing of the bite plate. Articular eminence is moderately steep.

Left TMJ tomogram shows severe condylar resorption in this joint. The fossa and articular eminence appear to be intact, and articular eminence is moderately steep. There is increased joint space in all dimensions probably secondary to either the pathology occurring in the joint and/or the splint treatment that she is currently in. Small anterior beaking is present on the head of the condyle.

On opening, there is fairly good translation of both joints, with the right side being a little bit better than the left side. In the open view, a pseudocyst is seen in the right condyle.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows both maxilla and mandible are probably AP retruded. The occlusal plane may be somewhat high. Oropharyngeal airway is decreased some and is about 6 mm at the soft palate level and about 6 mm at the base of the tongue. She may not have been all the way back in centric relation in this film. 

She does have some problems with the bones and joints in her neck, back, elbows, fingers, left hip, and her knees.
Basic Diagnoses

Bilateral TMJ severe idiopathic condylar resorption with perforations and non-salvageable discs
AP mandibular deficiency
AP maxillary deficiency
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency
Class II end-on occlusion
High occlusal plane angle
Missing maxillary first bicuspids
Tooth-size discrepancy of 1.5 mm		CHECK THIS
Decreased oropharyngeal airway
Hypertrophied turbinates
Deviated nasal septum
Polyp, left nasal airway
Extremely wide columella base
Nasal airway obstruction due to above
Severe TMJ pan
Headaches 
Myofascial pain
Earaches 
Tinnitus (severe)

Recommended Treatment

Presurgical orthodontics
Align and level maxillary arch
Align and level mandibular arch. Strip lower anterior teeth removing approximately 1.5 to 2 mm of tooth structure to account for the tooth-size discrepancy 

CT scan of TMJs and jaw structures (TMJ Concepts protocol)

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ condylectomy, debridement, reconstruction, and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla and downgraft it posteriorly
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvested from the abdomen)
Bilateral coronoidectomies
Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies
Nasal septoplasty
Remove polyp from the left nasal cavity
Reconstruct columella

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

original_note:
FRANCES VALENE									02-20-04

Frances is 50 years old.

Patient History

Her problems began in 1982, either following third molar removal or following an MVA where she was rear ended. She developed severe headaches and decreased jaw function. She has had numerous surgeries, and we feel we have most of them listed here, which include the follow:
06/13/86:  Bilateral TMJ Proplast Teflon implants
07/11/86:  Replacement of left Proplast Teflon implant
02/25/87:  Right TMJ removal of PT implant and dermal graft
12/01/87:  Left TMJ removal of PT implant and dermal graft
Early ‘90s:  Bilateral TMJ reconstruction with rib grafts
10/10/94:  Recontouring of the rib grafts
’96 or ’97:  LeFort I osteotomy
2000:  Removal of screws and wires from the jaw area

Frances has continuous headaches at a level of 5, but they average around an 8, and they occur all throughout the head including the frontal, temporal, posterior head, and top of the head. The headaches can escalate to 10. When she is relaxed, she rates her TMJ pain at about a level of 6, but following jaw function, it can easily escalate to a 10. On an average day, her pain levels are about a level of 6. Jaw function is at 9, diet at 8, and disability at 7.

Patient Concerns

TMJ pain
Headaches 
Myofascial pain
Cannot chew
Decreased incisal opening
Severe pressure behind the right eye
Teeth and jaws don’t fit together
Fairly severe crepitus in the joints with jaw function
Earaches 
Ringing in the ears
Lightheadedness and dizziness
Hearing loss
Daytime clenching and inability to get a comfortable jaw position
Severe snoring
Daytime tiredness
Difficulty sleeping

Medications

Ambien 5 mg occasional at night
Zoloft
Zyrtec
Zocar
Tylenol PM
Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Frances has fairly good facial balance in the middle and upper third face. She has some increased fullness on the right side of her face particularly over the parotid area. Upper lip length is about 21 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 3.5 mm. Maxillary midline is fairly close to being on the facial midline. Nasal tip is a little bit off to the left side maybe a millimeter. Mandibular dental midline and chin midline are fairly well aligned with each other, but the lower dental midline is off to the left side about 2 mm. Actually, maxillary midline is off to the right side about a millimeter. Right eye might be elevated about a millimeter or so compared to the left side. 

In profile view, the maxilla is retruded probably I would say close to 8 mm. Mandible is retruded by about at least 15 mm or more. She has an obtuse nasolabial angle. Upper lip supralabrale is posterior to subnasale. There is no chin-neck angle or chin-neckline. She holds her breath when sleeping. Her husband, Larry, has observed the severe snoring problems and the apneic episodes. She wakes up periodically at night. She has difficulty sleeping. She states that she cannot sleep more than three or four hours at a time. 

Intraorally, Frances has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. She has an accentuated curve of occlusion, lower arch. Some mild crowding, lower anterior arch. Crown on the mandibular left first molar. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. Appears to have a little bit of perhaps an accentuated curve of occlusion. Appears that she probably has a tooth-size discrepancy secondary to small upper lateral incisors. In centric relation, she has a Class III cuspid relationship on the left side of probably 3 mm and a tendency toward a crossbite posteriorly. She has a crown on the maxillary left first molar. On the right side, she also tends into a crossbite tendency. She has almost a full Class III molar relationship on the right side and about a 4-mm Class III relationship in the cuspid area. When she bites together, she occludes on her right maxillary second bicuspid, first and second molar and mandibular first and second molar. On the left side, she occludes on the first and second molars, and she also occludes on her right maxillary central and mandibular central and lateral incisors. All other teeth are out of occlusion. She probably has about a 4-mm open bite in the cuspid areas. Oropharynx appears clear and tissues appear normal.

To palpation, she has severe pain over the masseter muscles, medial pterygoids, TMJs, styloid areas, moderate pain over the temporalis muscles, and mild to moderate pain over the SCMs. She gets muscle spasms in the left masseter muscle causing her face to quiver. Maximal incisal opening is 20 mm. She could open to about 14 mm without excruciating pain. Excursive movements are probably less than 1 mm. 

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows that she is missing all four third molars. She has root canals on the maxillary left first and second bicuspids, first molar, mandibular left first molar, and maxillary right second bicuspid. She has two screws in the mandible on each side. She has probably the remnants of rib grafts left in her joints with severe bone development and bony ankylosis around the joint area. She has a lot of heterotopic bone in the area as well. Difficult to make out any reference of the fossas. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows just massive bony formation around the joint area. It appears that any rib graft that may be in place was placed anterior to the articular eminence perhaps, and then she just has a massive amount of heteroptic bone formation posteriorly in the fossa area as well as coming down anterior and below the internal meatus of the ear. Styloid process appears quite calcified as well.
Left TMJ tomogram actually looks better than the right side. There appears to be a pseudoarthrosis between some heterotopic bone up in the fossa and the lower jaw component. However, there is probably bony ankylosis and maybe just a pseudoseparation. Bone screws can be seen in the mandibular ramus area. The styloid process is seen but not as evident on this side as the opposite side. 

Opening views show virtually no movement of the condyles. Could be just a slight increase in the pseudoankylosis area in the mandible on the left side in the open views compared to the closed view. 

Waters view shows that the sinuses are relatively clear. Turbinates are of moderate size, and it looks like the right one has been reduced. Nasal airway looks satisfactory. Maxilla looks fairly symmetrical. Mandibular teeth are a little bit off toward the left side. 

Cephalometrically, Frances has pretty close to a Class I skeletal relationship but has a high mandibular plane angle. Oropharyngeal airway measures about 4 mm at the soft palate level and 5 mm at the base of the tongue. Transverse occlusal plane appears fairly level. She does have a little bit of a tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2+ mm. 

Basic Diagnoses

Multiple previous TMJ surgeries including the placement of Proplast Teflon implants, dermal grafts, and rib grafts (approximately 6 to 7 per side)
Bilateral TMJ severe ankylosis 
AP mandibular deficiency
AP maxillary deficiency
Class III end-on occlusion 
Posterior crossbite tendency and end-on incisor relationship
Bilateral buccal open bites of 4 mm
Tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2+ mm
Mild crowding, upper and lower anterior arches
Accentuated curve of occlusion, lower arch
Severe TMJ pain
Severe headaches 
Severe myofascial pain
Decreased oropharyngeal airway
Sleep disorder
Sleep apnea symptoms 
Snoring
Decreased oropharyngeal airway
Severe masticatory dysfunction
Earaches 
Tinnitus
Vertigo

Recommended Treatment

Presurgical orthodontics 
Align and level maxillary arch. Open up at least 0.5 mm of space around each upper lateral incisor to help compensate for the tooth-size discrepancy
Align and level mandibular arch. Strip lower anterior teeth removing 1.5 to 2 mm of tooth structure to help correct for the tooth-size discrepancy and to help keep from flaring the incisors too far forward
Can use bonded brackets on all of the teeth but could possibly require a band on the mandibular left first molar and maxillary left first molar since those are crowns. Place appliances on the second molars but not necessary to incorporate them into the arch wire. This is to prevent untoward orthodontic movements

CT scan of TMJs and jaws (TMJ Concepts protocol)

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ extensive debridement 
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses
Bilateral coronoidectomies
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvested from the abdomen)

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion 

General dental treatment as necessary to preserve and maintain the teeth

original_note:
LISA PROULX									08/25/2009

Lisa was actually referred by Dr. Al Bishop.  Lisa is in good health with no major medical problems.  She takes the following medications:

Thyroid medications 30 mcg per day. 
Bio-identical hormones.
Allegra-D occasionally. 
Vitamin D. 
Fish oil.

Lisa is not aware of when her facial asymmetry began and the etiology is also unknown to her.  Her primary concerns at this time include the following:

Facial asymmetry.
Click in the right TMJ area. 
Bite and jaws are not aligned properly.

She has no problems with headaches.  She gets some mild discomfort in her TMJ joint at about a level of 2.  On an average day, she does have any significant pain around the head and neck area.  she rates her jaw function, diet, and disability at zero.  She does get some bilateral earaches, but she is a flight attendant and this could be contributory.  She gets some tinnitus and occasional vertigo.  No other joints bother her.  She is not aware of clenching or bruxing of her teeth. 
 
CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Lisa has fairly good symmetry in the middle and upper third face.  Lower third face demonstrates some asymmetry.  Upper lip length is about 22 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 0 on the left and 1 on the right.  The maxillary midline is just a little bit off to the left about 1 mm.  The mandibular dental midline is about 2 mm off to the left.  Chin is probably a good 6 mm off to the left .  In profile view, Lisa may have just some slight AP maxillary deficiency with the maxilla being retruded maybe 3 to 4 mm.  She has a C-shaped curve in the upper lip.  The mandible may be just slightly deficient also about 4 to 5 mm.  As she postures her mandible forward about 5 mm or so, it seems to improve her facial profile.  Intraorally, Lisa has all of her lower teeth back to the second molars present.  She has some significant on the left side between the cuspid and first bicuspid of 2 mm and some space between the rest of her anterior teeth.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars present.  She has a Class I cuspid-molar relationship on the right side and also on the left side, but she does have a crossbite on the left side extending from the cuspid through the second molar.  She has about almost a 3 mm anterior deep bite relationship.  The lower mandibular dental midline is about 2 mm to the right of the chin midline taking into account the transverse cant in the occlusion.  The cant in the occlusion shows that the left cuspid is 2 mm higher than the right cuspid in the upper arch and in the lower arch probably a 3 mm difference.  Oropharyngeal tissues look reasonably healthy with the uvula just mildly elongated, but other tissues look to be normal in structure.  Nasal airway looks fairly patent, although there is some deviation in the septum.  Turbinates at least anteriorly look relatively normal in size.  Lisa clinically appears to not have any significant nasal airway obstruction.  To palpation, she has some mild tenderness over the masseter muscle, but no other tenderness around the head and neck area.  The right side of the mandible in the frontal view appears to be a good 6-8 mm lower than the left side.  As Lisa shifts her mandible towards the left side about 3-4 mm, it improves her overall facial symmetry, although the angle on the right side of the mandible appears a little bit stronger in that position.  When she bites in her centric relation, she is more prominent in the anterior part of the mandible on the left side and not quite as prominent back in the angle area.  As Lisa opens her jaw, the mandible shifts about 5 mm to the right side.  Maximal opening is 67 mm.  Excursion movement to the right 12 mm, to the left 7 mm.   

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of the mandibular first molars with them being replaced with 3 inter-bridges on all each side.  All four third molars are absent.  Sinuses appear relatively clear.  She has a significantly enlarged condyle in the right joint.  
Axial view shows that the sinuses are clear.  Turbinates appear fairly enlarged and seemed to block a good portion of the airway.  
Coronal view shows that the sinuses are clear, but inferior turbinates appear quite large.  The vertical discrepancy at the inferior border of the mandible is noted with the right side being lower than left side. 
Left TMJ sagittal view shows condyle that has fairly good morphology.  Articular eminence is moderate in inclination.  There appears to be adequate joint space.  
Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is significantly enlarged with some significant anterior beaking.  The fossa is a little bit longer and a little bit more shallow than the opposite side.  Joint space actually looks reasonably good, except it is decreased posteriorly and perhaps at the anterior aspect as well.  
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology.  The joint space looks a little bit narrow at the lateral aspect of the joint.  Lateral rim curves inferiorly about 3 mm.   
Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is enlarged vertically and maybe laterally.  There appears to be an extension out lateral almost beyond the lateral rim of the fossa.  The joint space looks pretty good, but out laterally the joint space appears significantly decreased.  
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she may have had her head slightly tilted when this was taken.  The infraorbital rim seem to align, but orbit is in a different vertical position.  Oropharyngeal airway looks fairly good.  Lisa has a slight AP maxillary and mandibular hyperplasia. 

When Lisa was in high school, she was involved in a head-on accident and had a concussion.  She does not remember if she had any trauma to her jaws at that time.  When Lisa closes her jaws, she feels that the left side may hit before the right side. 

Basic diagnoses for Lisa would be as follows:    

Right TMJ osteochondroma. 
Left TMJ mild arthritis and minimally displaced articular disc. 
Mandible deviated to the left 6 mm and AP hypoplasia as well as transverse hypoplasia. 
Maxillary right vertical hyperplasia.
Transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right side being 2 mm lower than the left side. 
Class I cuspid relationship.
Bilateral hypertrophied turbinates with partial nasal airway obstruction. 
Bilateral infraorbital hypoplasia. 
Lower arch asymmetry with the right cuspid being 2 mm posterior to the left cuspid. 
Spacing between the lower anterior teeth, particularly the left cuspid and first bicuspid and there is gap of about 2.5 mm. 
Left central and lateral incisor are each at least 0.5 mm smaller in size than the right side.
Left posterior crossbite.
Increased curve of Wilson on the mandibular posterior arch.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Bone scan to determine activity of osteochondroma in the right TMJ. 
Presurgical orthodontics.  
Align and level maxillary arch.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. 
Align and level mandibular arch with the following adjustments:  
Place bonding on the lower left central and left lateral incisor to fill in the 0.5 mm gap between those teeth and the cuspid which will equalize their medial distal diameter to the teeth in the right ach. 
Retract left cuspid 1 mm and advance right cuspid 1 mm to achieve transverse arch symmetry at the cuspid level.  Consider advancing the posterior teeth to close residual gaps as this will improve the overall arch symmetry and keep from retracting the lower incisors too far.  May need to use TADs to accomplish that. These fees are current and subject to change without notice.  This letter is not considered a contract but an estimate of charges.  The diagnosis and treatment codes are also subject to change over the course of treatment and/or surgery.

Shift mandibular dental midline 1 mm to the left of its current position so that it will align better with the chin. 
Surgery. 
Right TMJ low condylectomy with articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor, if the osteochondroma in the right joint is found to be active. 
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to shift the mandible back onto the facial midline and transversely level occlusal plane and advancement.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to level it transversely, advance it about 4 mm, and expand transversely. 
Bilateral partial nasal turbinectomies to improve the airway. 
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
Surgery Stage 2:  Bilateral infraorbital osteotomies to advance and superiorly reposition in the infraorbital rims.

original_note:
ELISE LEGER									02/27/2009

Elise is 49 years old.  She was referred through Sleepnet.com, and in particularly Dr. Brian Rotskoff.  Elise has severe sleep apnea.  She has been tried on a CPAP machine, but cannot tolerate it.  The severity of the sleep apnea is significantly affecting her working abilities.  Primary concerns that Elise has include the following:

Severe sleep apnea. 
Extreme fatigue. 
Memory loss. 
Mental confusion.
Left TMJ popping.
Headaches.

Elise has recently had surgery on February 24, 2009, to include turbinectomies and a septoplasty.  She also had a sleep endoscopy procedure performed.  Elise currently takes the following medications:

Levoxyl 112 mg.
Prozac 40 mg.
Vytorin 10/40 mg.

Elise is unsure of when her left jaw joint began to click and pop, thus etiology is also unknown.  She rates her TMJ pain at a level of 2, average daily pain at 4, jaw function at 2, diet and disability at 0.  She gets headaches predominantly in the left frontal and temporal area at about a level of 6.  Usually, Elise will wake up with headaches in the morning, but she will also get headaches in the afternoon as well and this is a daily occurrence.  She gets occasional earaches, occasional tinnitus, and has moderate vertigo.  She has no hearing loss.  She has herniated disks in her neck and back, but these do not particularly bother her and she does not really participate in too many activities.  She is aware of clenching and bruxism at night time and sometimes during the day under stress she will also clench.  

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Elise has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 26 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is -3 mm.  The maxillary dental midline is pretty much on the facial midline.  The mandibular dental midline is shifted about 2 mm off to the left side.  The chin, however, is fairly close to being on the facial midline.  In profile view, upper lip supralabrale is just posterior to subnasale.  She has probably only about 4 mm of AP deficiency in the maxilla.  Chin is AP deficient by about 20 mm.  There is no definition to the chin neckline or angle.  Intraorally, Elise has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars and upper teeth back through the second molars.  She has a crown on the mandibular left first molar.  Teeth are fairly well aligned, although the lower incisors are over angulated.  Transverse width of the lower arch is somewhat constricted.  In the upper arch, she has crowns on the first molars as well and also on the left second molar.  She has a Class I cuspid relationship on the right side and Class II end-on cuspid relationship on the left side being 2 to 3 mm Class II.  She has an anterior deep bite of about 3 mm.  Intraorally, she has some moderate hyperplasia of the uvula and elongation of the soft palate.  Fascial pillars appear relatively normal.  Transverse width of the fascial pillars are quite restricted.  It is difficult to evaluate the tonsils, but they appear mildly enlarged perhaps.  Unable to evaluate turbinates and septum because of the recent surgery and discomfort.  She has severe snoring, severe daytime somnolence, and severe sleep apnea.  She has a moderate to severe sleep disorder.  She has frequent arousals at night.  She was predominantly a mouth breather prior to her recent surgery.  She has a moderate head postured forward neck position.  She was unable to tolerate the CPAP machine.  She had to use such a high setting that it creates significant problems for her.   Dental model analysis shows that she has some significant transverse deficiency in the widths of the maxillary and mandible arches.  She has an anterior deep bite of at least 3 mm.  Mandibular dental midline is shifted to the left 2 mm.  She has about a 2 to 3 mm Class II relationship on the left side, but Class I on the right side.  She has a posterior cross bite on the left side.  To palpation, she has some mild tenderness over the TMJs, left medial pterygoid area, and left retromandibular area.  Joints seem to function reasonably smoothly today.  

Basic diagnoses for Elise would be as follows:    

Left TMJ articular disc dislocation. 
AP and posterior vertical maxillary hypoplasia.
AP mandibular hypoplasia.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Hyperplastic soft palate and uvula.
Severe sleep apnea. 
Headaches predominantly on the left side.
Over angulation of maxillary and mandibular anterior teeth.
Transverse deficiency and arch width of both the maxilla and mandible.
Class I occlusion on the right side, Class II end-on occlusion on the left side.  
Left posterior cross bite. 

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Presurgical orthodontics.  
Align and level maxillary arch.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. 
Align and level mandibular arch.  Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing about 2 mm of tooth structure to help keep from flaring the lower incisors any further forward.  Try to expand the lower arch, if possible 2 to 3 mm.  Be sure to place brackets on the second molars to better align those teeth, particularly on the left side. 
Surgery. 
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla in a counterclockwise direction and expand it. 
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction.
Left TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor. 
Uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (UPPP procedure).
Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy to advance the genial tubercles further forward. 
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B019
B019 is 22 years old, referred by Dr. XXXX. B019's TMJ problems began to the best of her knowledge when she was 18 years old. The TMJ problems seemed to begin after her orthodontic treatment was completed. She had had functional complaints from about the age of 5 and then had full orthodontic treatment from the age of 12 to 15 including head gear. Occlusion came out very nice. When she was 18 was the first she became aware of having clicking and popping in her jaw joints. She gets no headaches, but does get TMJ pain at about a level of 4. On the average day, she does not have any real significant pain. She rates her jaw function at 5, diet at 0, disability at 0. B019 does feel that her bite has changed and she feels as though her back teeth are touching before any of the other teeth. She feels this is getting progressively worse for her. She currently takes no medications. The only other joint that bothers her is her foot where she has an abnormal bone formation. She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, nor vertigo. She is aware of clenching at nighttime and sometimes does wake up with discomfort in her jaws.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
: In frontal view, B019 has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. There may be just some slight increased fullness in the right angle area. Upper lip length is about 20 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is about 4.5 mm. The maxillary, mandibular, and chin midline are all pretty much on the facial midline. In profile view, upper lip supralabrale is about 2 to 3 mm posterior to subnasale and probably about 6 mm AP deficient. Chin may be approximately 15 mm AP deficient. Chin neckline is shorter, although the chin neck angle is reasonably well defined. She does have a high mandibular plane angle facial morphology. Intraorally, B019 has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. Teeth are in excellent condition, nicely aligned and leveled and attached gingiva looks normal. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. There is a slight increase curve of Wilson in the upper arch. She has a slight Class II end-on cuspid relationship being about maybe 1 to 2 mm Class II. She has an anterior deep bite of about 2 mm. Uvula, soft palate, and fascial pillars all look relatively normal as do the tonsils. Intranasally, the turbinates appear moderately enlarged and a little bit boggy looking. The septum appears reasonably normal. There is probably some mild to moderate airway obstruction perhaps particularly when she is sleeping. To palpation, she has no tenderness around the head and neck area. Incisal opening is 56 mm. Excursion movements are about 1 mm in each direction. On opening, she has a click in the left joint about 25 mm of opening and closing at 20 mm. On the right side, she has an opening click of 35 mm and a closing click of about 30 mm.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. Sinuses look reasonably clear. The condyles appear as though there has been some bone resorption that has occurred. Waters view shows that the sinuses are clear and the turbinates are moderately enlarged. Right TMJ tomogram shows that a condyle that appears a little bit smaller in size as well as loss of vertical dimension. Articular eminence is moderately steep. There appears to be a pseudocyst in the head of the condyle. There appears to be decreased superior joint space. Left TMJ tomogram shows the condyle appears smaller in size as though there has been some previous resorption that has occurred. There is a pseudocyst that is fairly large in the head of the condyle. There may be some decreased superior joint space. Articular eminence is relatively normal in inclination. On opening, there is good translation of both joints. Both joints look as though they have lost some bony volume in the open views. Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that B019 has a retruded maxilla and mandible with a high occlusal plane angle. The oropharyngeal airway is constricted with an airway dimension of about 5 mm at the base of the tongue and about 6 to 7 mm at the soft palate level.

Basic diagnoses would be as follows:
Bilateral TMJ resorptive arthritis and articular disc dislocation with reduction (possible AICR). AP mandibular hypoplasia. AP maxillary hypoplasia. Anterior vertical maxillary hyperplasia.
Posterior vertical maxillary hypoplasia.
High occlusal plane angle of 21 degrees (normal 8 degrees). Decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of 5 mm (normal 11 mm).
Hypertrophied turbinates. Partial nasal airway obstruction.
Sleep apnea symptoms including snoring and daytime somnolence.
TMJ pain.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
Presurgical orthodontics. Align and level maxillary arch. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. Align and level mandibular arch. Consider expanding the lower arch slightly if possible, but do not orthodontically expand the maxillary arch. Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance it forward approximately 6 mm, move the anterior aspect superiorly 2 mm and down graft the posterior aspect 5 mm to decrease the occlusal plane angle and also expand the arch if necessary.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction.
Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies.
3. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
KEREN LATZER									09/24/2010

Keren is 44 years old and referred by Dr. Bill Hang.  For the most part, she is in good health.  About 2 years ago she began developing some problems in her jaw joints of unknown etiology.  She is not aware of any trauma or other insightful factors that may have started the problem.  She has been in orthodontic treatment for the last 18 months.  Once Dr. Hang got her started in treatment, the headache and joint pains that she was having prior to then went away.  She did pretty well for quite some time, but has had 2 episodes since then of developing pain and headache problems associated with her jaw joints and back of her head.  She has had her previous sleep study that was normal.  

Keren rates her headaches at 0, TMJ pain at 3, average daily pain at 1, jaw function 1, diet at 4, disability at 2.  She has no earaches, tinnitus, vertigo nor hearing loss.  Other joints that bother her include her shoulders and hips sometimes.  She has had previous surgery on both knees, but they are doing very well.  She is aware of clenching and bruxism at nighttime and she is aware of clenching during the day, too.  

In frontal view, she has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 18 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is 0.  The maxillary midline may be about 1 mm off to the left.  The mandibular dental midline is fairly close to being on the facial midline.  The chin is shifted about 3 mm to the left.  She does have almost a full Class II occlusion and she has an anterior open bite in centric relation.  In centric relation, she occludes only on the first bicuspids on the right side and all the rest of her teeth are out of occlusion.  She has about a 2 mm open bite anteriorly and probably 2 mm posterior on the left side, 3 mm on the right side.  In profile view, she has a little fullness in the upper lip by about 2 to 3 mm.  The chin is retruded about 10 to 12 mm.  The chin neckline is short, although the chin neck angle is reasonably well defined.  She has hyperfunctioning mentalis muscle.  She has a head postured forward neck position.  In centric relation, she actually has a fairly good Class I cuspid-molar relationship, although buccally she is a little bit wide on the left side where her maxillary lingual cuspids touch almost on top of the lower buccal cuspid.  She has good attached gingiva around the teeth.  Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively normal and healthy.  The uvula is slightly elongated.  Keren does not report any difficulties breathing through her nose.  Keren has a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the left side being 2 to 3 mm higher than the right side.  There is some upper arch asymmetry with the maxillary left cuspid being at least 2 mm posterior to the right cuspid and the mandibular left cuspid is about 1 mm posterior to the right cuspid.  The left inferior border is about 2 to 3 mm elevated compared to the right side.  There is tenderness over the masseter muscles.  The incisal opening is 56 mm but only 26 without pain and discomfort.  The excursion movements are 10 mm to the right and 11 mm to the left.  It does appear on clinical photos that Karen does have a slight yaw towards the right side of probably a couple of millimeters.  

Basic diagnoses for Keren would be as follows:

Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly dislocated without reduction.
Right TMJ medial displacement of the articular disc.
AP mandibular hypoplasia.
Slight AP maxillary hyperplasia and posterior vertical hypoplasia. 
AP microgenia.
Class II end-on occlusion, in centric relation occluding only on the right first bicuspids.
Anterior and bilateral posterior open bites with occlusion only on the right first bicuspids. 
Missing mandibular right first molar, maxillary 1st molars and left 2nd biculpid. 
Decreased oropharyngeal airway of 5 mm (normal 11 mm).
Moderate increased occlusal plane angle.
TMJ pain.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Presurgical orthodontics.
	a.	Continue to align and level maxillary arch. 
	b.	Continue to align and level mandibular arch. 
2.	Surgery.
	a.	Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning with ligament repair with Mitek 				anchors.
	b.	Bilateral mandibular ramus sagittal split osteotomies to advance the mandible into 		a centric occlusion position.
3.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
4.	Restorative dentistry to reconstruct the occlusion.

original_note:
DARBY OWENS									08/12/2010

Darby is 14 years old.  Darby is referred by Dr. Tom Lowder, 2709 W. Huntsville Avenue, Springdale, Arkansas 72762.  Darby had a liver transplant in 1999 when she was 3 years old.  She currently takes ProGraf 1 mg twice daily.  The primary concerns that Darby has include the following:

Upper jaw is growing too fast. 
Excessive gum showing particularly when smiling.
Difficult to play her clarinet.
Difficulties eating and chewing because she cannot get her lips together.

Darby has no problems with headaches, TMJ problems or issues, no signs or symptoms.  She has no earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, hearing loss or other joint problems.  

In frontal view, Darby has some slight facial asymmetry.  Upper lip length is about 20 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 9 mm.  The nasal tip is off to the right side about 3 mm.  The maxillary midline is off to the right side about 3 to 4 mm.  The mandibular dental midline is fairly close to being on the facial midline, but the chin is off to the right side about 2 or 3 mm.  In profile view, Darby has an elongated lower third face.  She has a C-shaped curve to the upper lip.  The maxilla is retruded about 5 to 6 mm and the mandible is retruded about 15 to 18 mm.  The chin neckline is short, although the chin neck angle is reasonably well defined.  She has some hyperfunction of the mentalis muscle.  Intraorally, Darby has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars.  She has some crowding particularly in the posterior left side with a little bit of a lingual inclination of the second bicuspid and first molar.  There is mild crowding in the upper anterior arch, but she has good attached gingiva.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  She has an extra tooth in the upper left side with two lateral incisors.  She is basically Class I bilaterally.  She has a buccal cross bite of the left second bicuspids.  She has an anterior deep bite of about 7 mm.  Oropharyngeal tissues look normal and healthy except she does have a moderately enlarged right tonsil.  Intranasally, she probably has moderately enlarged turbinates, at least as best can be seen from the front.  She has a wide columella and narrow nostrils.  She has no pain to palpation around the head and neck area.  Joints function smoothly.  The incisal opening is 49 mm and excursion movements are 8 mm in each direction.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the presence of developing third molars on the right side but not on the left side.  She has a root canal in the mandibular left first molar.  She may have a mucous retention cyst in the left sinus perhaps.  
Left TMJ sagittal tomogram shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology to it with a little flattening towards the outside of the joint, but otherwise it looks pretty good.  The joint space looks balanced and articular eminence is moderately shallow.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that appears a little bit more flat on the anterior surface.  There may be slight increased posterior joint space.  The articular eminence has a moderate inclination.  

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with fairly good mediolateral width.  There is decreased lateral joint space which means the disc could be somewhat medially displaced.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 mm.  

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with good mediolateral width.  There is decreased lateral joint space indicative of perhaps medial displacement of the articular disc.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 to 4 mm. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Darby has anterior vertical maxillary excess, AP mandibular deficiency and a moderate high occlusal plane angle.  Oropharyngeal airway however looks very good.  She has some adenoid tissue remaining in the cranial base area.  The maxilla also appears retruded anteroposteriorly as well as does the mandible and there is AP microgenia as well.  

The coronal view shows that there is deviated nasal septum off towards the left side.  The left inferior turbinate has a moderate hyperplasia and the right side has more significant hyperplasia.  There is a little mucocele on the anterior aspect of the left maxillary sinus.  

Basic diagnoses for Darby would be as follows:

Maxillary anterior vertical hyperplasia.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia.
Maxillary midline shifted about 3 to 4 mm to the right of the facial midline.
Supernumerary maxillary left lateral incisor.
Anterior deep bite of about 7 mm.
Excessive tooth to lip exposure of 9 mm.
Hypertrophied turbinates.
Impacted right maxillary and mandibular third molars (x2).
Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of 3 mm.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Extract one of the maxillary supernumerary lateral incisors with probable removal of the 	more anterior one since the more posterior one has a slightly larger crown.
2.	Presurgical orthodontics. 
	a.	Align and level maxillary arch.  Close the extraction space on right side.  Keep 			about 1 mm of space between the cuspid, lateral incisor, lateral incisor, and 			central to compensate for the tooth size discrepancy of 3 mm.
	b.	Align and level mandibular arch.  Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing 			about 2 mm of tooth structure and retract to keep from flaring the lower incisors 			too far forward.
3.	Surgery.
	a.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the maxilla superiorly 7 mm, decrease     
                        the occlusal plane angle, and correct any other arch discrepancies.
	b.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance it forward approximately 15 			mm.
	c.	Augmentation genioplasty with a Medpor implant.
	d.	Surgical removal of right-sided impacted third molars x2.
	e.	Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies. 
4.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
JOHN NATHAN LANDRETH 							10/7/05

John is 18 years old, referred by Dr. George Adams, Plano, Texas.  Nat is in good health with no major medical problems.

PRIMARY CONCERNS
Teeth and jaws do not line up well and do not fit together properly.
Difficulty breathing through the nose.
Predominately a mouth breather.

In frontal view, Nat has fairly good left to right facial symmetry.  There may be a little bit of increased fullness at the angle in the area of the right side but not too significant.  Upper lip length is about 22 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is 0.  Maxillary midline and mandibular dental midline is pretty much on the facial midline.  Chin may be just a couple of mm off to the left side.  In profile view, Nat has some AP maxillary deficiency probably about 5 mm.  The chin also appears to be probably 4 to 6 mm AP deficient.  Chin-neckline and chin neck angles are not well defined.  Nat has all of his lower teeth back through the second molars.  Some very minimal crowding in the lower anterior arch.  The lower teeth appear to be in slight lingual inclination.  Slightly reverse curve of Spee at the 4 incisors.  In the upper arch, Nat has all of his teeth back through the second molars.  He has anterior and bilateral posterior crossbites.  He is about 2 mm Class III on the left side, 4 mm Class II on the right side.  His incisors hit just about end-on but a little anterior crossbite present.  He has zero overbite relationship.  Nat has a slight transverse cant to the occlusal plane with the left side being 2 mm higher than the right side in both the upper and lower arch.  Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively normal.  The uvula is mildly hyperplastic but not too significant.  The soft palpate and faucial pillars look normal.  Intranasally, Nat's septum is deviated off towards the right side.  The right turbinate looks somewhat inflamed and appears to almost touch against the septum.  On the left side, the turbinate looks healthier and is smaller in size and there appears to be an adequate airway at least in the front part of the nose.  The incisal opening is 53 mm.  Excursive movements are 7 mm in each direction.  No tenderness to palpation around the head and neck area.  TMJs function smoothly.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the presence of impacted maxillary third molars and an erupted right third molar but the left third molar apparently has not formed.  The sinuses look fairly clear.  The septum may be a little bit deviated.

Waters view show that the sinuses are clear including the frontal, ethmoid and maxillary sinuses.  The nasal septum appears to have a spur off of the superior side back in the bony area.  The right turbinate appears a little bit larger than the left turbinate though there may be some airway at least back in the bony area.  Anteriorly the septum deviated off to right side might cause some airway obstruction. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has fairly good form.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  There appears to be good joint space around the condyle.  

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology.  Articular eminence may be slightly steeper on this side as compared to the opposite side.  The joint space appears to be adequate but difficult to tell on this x-ray.  Open view shows good translation of both condyles.  


JOHN NATHAN LANDRETH 		10/7/05					Page 2


Lateral cephalometric radiographic shows that there is some AP maxillary deficiency and AP mandibular deficiency.  Probably a skeletal Class III occlusal relationship is present.  Oropharyngeal airway appears constricted with the airway at the soft palate level being only about 3 to 4 mm and at the base of the tongue it is about 7 mm.  He appears to have an increased occlusal plane angle.  There is a vertical difference at the inferior border of the mandible.  Nat has a tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 3 mm.

BASIC DIAGNOSES
AP maxillary deficiency.
Transverse maxillary deficiency.
AP mandibular deficiency.
High occlusal plane angle.
Class III end-on occlusion being worse on the right side as compared to the left side.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway with AP dimension of 3 to 4 mm at the soft palate level (normal 11 mm.)
Hypertrophied turbinates.
Nasoseptal deviation and spurs.
Nasal airway obstruction.
Predominately mouth breather.
Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 3 mm.
Impacted maxillary third molars and erupted maligned mandibular left third molar. 
Transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the left side being 2 mm lower than right side.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch.  Tip laterals incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies.  Open up space around the upper lateral incisors to equal 1 mm around each tooth.
Align and level mandibular arch.  Use Class III elastics on the right side and Class II elastics on the left side to try and improve the asymmetry in the lower arch where the left cuspid is about 2 mm posterior to the right cuspid.  He should only need these elastics for a couple of months to correct that situation.
Surgery.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla, level it transversely and correct any transverse discrepancies.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a slight counterclockwise direction.
Bilateral partial turbinectomies.
Nasoseptoplasty.
Removal of impacted maxillary third molars and malaligned erupted mandibular right third molar. 
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B424 
B424 is 22 years old, referred by her mother X. B424 is in good health with no major medical problems. She takes birth control pills but no other medications. B424's joint problems began she believes sometime around the age of 10 years old. Etiology is unknown. She had orthodontics from the age of 13 to 15 by Dr. XX. Following the completion of orthodontics she said she felt that something was not right with her jaws and her jaw joints and her bite ended up being retruded with excessive overjet relationship. B424 feels that her bite is continuing to shift and move. When she bites, her teeth do not bite in the same place everytime but there are different areas that touch or meet. She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus or hearing loss, but does have problems with vertigo. No other joints bother her. When she wakes up in the morning she has a weird sensation that involves some nausea sort of feelings, lightheadedness and dizziness. She is aware of clenching and bruxing of her teeth at night and she also does it sometimes during the day. She gets headaches about every couple of months that she rates at about a level of 5 and they occur predominately in the frontal and temple areas as well. She reports no TMJ pain and on an average day she does not have any significant pain around the head and neck area. She rates her jaw function at 3, diet 0, disability at 0. She has had no specific treatment for her TMJ and jaw problems.

PRIMARY CONCERNS
Teeth do not fit together very well.
Teeth and bite continue to shift.
Lightheadedness and dizziness. Weird feeling in the morning of nausea and dizziness. Front teeth do not touch.
Difficulties biting foods because the front teeth do not touch.
Frequent mouth breathing.

In frontal view, B424 has very good left to right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 21 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 4.5 to 5 mm. The nasal tip is deviated off to the right side probably about 3 mm. Maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline as is the lower teeth and chin. In profile view, B424  has fairly good balance of the upper lip, cheeks and nose. Upper lip could be retruded no more than a couple of millimeters at the most. The chin is retruded approximately 15 mm. Chin neck-line is not well-defined and chin-neck angle is not well-defined either. She has some fullness in the submental area. Intraorally, B424 has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars present. The teeth are fairly well aligned and leveled. She has adequate attached gingiva around the lower anterior teeth except it is a little bit thin between the central incisors. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars present. B424 has a Class II end-on occlusion with about a 4 mm overjet relationship anteriorly. She has about 4 to 5 mm Class II. She has a 1 mm anterior open bite. She has a little bit of tendency towards the crossbite in the bicuspid area. The other teeth are okay. Anteriorly, the oropharynx appears relatively normal tissue-wise and the uvula, soft palate and faucial pillars all appear relatively normal. She has moderately enlarged tonsils. Turbinates are moderately enlarged particularly the right side and it appears larger even on the left side. The septum has some tipping off to the left and right side but relatively straight. She has some moderate nasal airway obstruction.  

The columella and nostrils are relatively normal and the luminal valves are normal. Occlusion is in centric relation predominately on the second molars on the right side, and on the left side the second bicuspids, first and second molars might also touch. To palpation she has no tenderness around the head and neck area. Palpating the jaw joints during function reveals that she has inconsistent opening clicking and popping in the left joint that is much more obvious in excursive movements. In the right side, she would have an occasional click or pop on wide opening, but more common on excursive movements. It is indicative that she has bilateral displaced articular disc but are inconsistent at reduction on opening. Occasionally, the pops are audible. Maximal incisal opening is 54 mm. Excursive movement to the right 11 mm, to the left 12 mm.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. She has a root canal on the maxillary left first molar. Sinuses look relatively clear as best as they can be evaluated. Condyles are off the radiograph.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is small and has lost vertical dimension and overall condylar volume. The articular eminence is relatively shallow. Joint may be decreased superiorly but difficult to tell.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has lost vertical dimension and overall volume. Decreased superior and posterior joint space is present. Articular eminence is moderately steep, perhaps just a little bit steeper than the opposite side.

On opening, there is good translation of both condyles.
Waters view shows that the septum has some significant deviation to it as there are two images of the septum indicative of a significant deflection off to the right side. Turbinate on the right side appears enlarged and not much airway appears to be present on the right side. On the left side, there is some airway but normal. Sinuses all appear clear.

Cephalometrically, B424 has a Class II skeletal and occlusal relationship. It appears to occlude only on the second molars. She has an overjet of about 4 to 5 mm and slight anterior open bite of about 1 mm. Oropharyngeal airway is significantly reduced with dimension of the soft palate level about 4 to 5 mm and the base of the tongue about 5 mm. There is a Bolton's tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of about 1.5 mm.

BASIC DIAGNOSES Bilateral TMJ idiopathic condylar resorption with articular disc dislocation and significant deformation.
Anterior vertical maxillary excess.
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
AP mandibular deficiency.
 

Transverse maxillary deficiency. AP microgenia.
Class II occlusion. Anterior open bite.
Occludes predominately on the right second molars and left second bicuspid first and second molars.
Significantly decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Hypertrophied turbinates.
Decreased nasal airway.
Tooth side discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 1.5 mm.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies
Align and level mandibular arch. Strip lower anterior teeth removing about 2 mm of tooth structure to keep from moving the lateral incisor any further forward and to correct tooth size discrepancy Surgery
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning with Mitek anchors and artificial ligaments
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counter-clockwise direction with bone screw stabilization
Multiple maxillary osteotomies with bone plate stabilization and hydroxyapatite grafting
Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy to augment the chin
Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies 3. Post surgical orthodontics Finish and retain

B424 MRI Evaluation 
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has undergone significant resorption. It has lost transverse width. It has some lateral beaking of the condyle. A little bit of sclerosis on the top of the condyle and some unevenness in the shape. It appears there is discal tissue anterior to the condyle. Moderate downward curvature at the lateral rim of the fossa of about 2 to 3 mm. Joint space might be slightly narrow.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that similarly has lost transverse width and vertical height in the condyle. Some lateral beaking of the condyle is present. There is decreased joint space that appears vertically. There may be discal tissue anterior but difficult to tell on this side. Moderate downward curvature at the lateral rim of the fossa about 2 to 3 mm.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has undergone obvious changes with perhaps loss of vertical height and transverse width as the condyle is only seen in three views. There is moderately steep articular eminence. The articular disc is anteriorly displaced and sitting beneath the articular eminence. It is significantly shortened anteroposteriorly. The condylar head itself has areas where there appears to be some sclerosis present and areas where there may be some erosion that may have occurred with subsequent sclerosis. Joint space actually does not look too bad vertically. There is a little bit of fluid in the superior joint space. There is very good translation of the condyle and disc but the disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle with loss of vertical height. The condyle shows only about 3 of the views again indicative of perhaps significant narrowing of the condylar width. There is anterior beaking present. Articular eminence is moderately steep. The articular disc is anteriorly displaced and sitting beneath the articular eminence. This seems to have a little bit better morphology than the opposite side. On opening, there is fairly good translation of the condyle but the articular disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction.

IMPRESSION:
Left TMJ severe arthritis (ICR) with articular disc dislocation with significant deformation and nonreducing.
Right TMJ severe arthritis (ICR) with articular disc dislocation with significant deformation and nonreducing.
Discs may still be salvageable.
