original_note:
MARY COLLEEN WARD								01-31-05

Mary is 21 years old and referred by Terry Carpenter, DDS, 3300 Haynes, Midland, TX  79707. Her orthodontist in Midland would be Dr. James Compton, 4610 N. Garfield, Suite A-9, Midland, TX  79705. Also, Mary was referred by Kevin E. Porter, DDS, MD, 4610 N. Garfield, Suite A-6, The Colonnade, Midland, TX  79705.

Patient Concerns

Jaw pain
Right jaw catches and locks
Teeth don’t fit together
Painful to bite the teeth together
Difficulties sleeping
Headaches 
Jaw joint pain
Snoring
Tiredness during the day

Patient History

She has no problems with earaches, vertigo, or hearing loss. Gets occasional tinnitus. Is allergic to LODINE and over Christmas had a severe allergic reaction for which she had to be taken to the hospital, and it was felt it was either due to a muffin she ate or to her new Christmas pajamas. About two years ago, Mary did develop an autoimmune deficiency problem and took gamma globulin injection treatments for about six months. She apparently has no further problems with that. She is not aware of clenching or bruxing her teeth at night or during the day. She has had some orthodontics to straighten up her upper front teeth when she was about 8 or 9 years of age. 

She takes Zyrtec but no other medications.

Mary’s jaw problems began about 1-1/2 to 2 years ago when she began developing some tenderness and soreness on the right, and about a year-and-a-half ago she was aware of it clicking. The clicking is still there, although now it has a tendency to get locked. She feels that the lower jaw now catches on that right side, and so she has to manipulate and move her jaw in order to get her jaw to open up. 

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Mary has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 18 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 6.5 mm. Maxillary dental midline is just slightly to the left about 0.5 mm. Mandibular dental midline is about 2 to 3 mm off toward the right side. Chin is fairly close to being on the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline is probably about a millimeter to the right of the chin midline.

In profile view, Mary has some AP maxillary deficiency of probably about 4 to 5 mm. Chin appears retruded about 15 to 18 mm. Chin-neckline is a little bit short, and the chin-neck angle is moderately well-defined. 

Intraorally, Mary has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. She has some mild crowding lower anterior arch. Appears to have adequate attached gingiva. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. Upper lateral incisors appear small in size, and she has a tendency toward a division II occlusion. Has a narrow transverse width of the maxilla and almost slight reverse curve of Wilson in the upper arch. There is a Class II end-on cuspid relationship being about 1 to 2 mm Class II on the left side and probably about 4 to 5 mm Class II on the right side. She has an anterior deep bite of about 4 to 5 mm. She has maybe a millimeter overjet. Right cuspid is about 1.5 mm posterior to the left cuspid in the lower arch. In the upper arch, the left cuspid may be a millimeter posterior to the right cuspid. There is a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right side being about a millimeter to 2 mm higher than the left side. She does have some slight increased fullness in the left side of the face as compared to the right side. 

Intranasally, septum appears reasonably straight. Tonsils appear moderately enlarged but not too significant, and there appears to be adequate airway. The left turbinate was a little bit enlarged compared to the right side. 

Dental model analysis shows that there is some moderate crowding, lower anterior arch, and mild crowding in the upper arch. She does have a fairly flat curve of Wilson in the upper arch and an increased or accentuated curve of Wilson in the lower arch. There is an increased curve of Spee in the lower arch and slight reverse curve of Spee in the upper arch.

Mary has a tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2 mm. Maximal incisal opening is about 40 mm and excursive movement to the right of 8 mm and to the left of about 10 mm. 

On opening, left joint seems to function smoothly, but on the right side she has an opening click at about 10 mm and a closing click at about 5 to 10 mm. 

To palpation, she has some mild tenderness over the right temporalis, masseter, TMJ, and styloid area and no tenderness on the left side. 

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars, but the rest of the teeth are all present. A little calcification between the bicuspids on the mandibular left side. Sinuses appear reasonably clear, although the left one appears a little bit more cloudy than the right one. Turbinates appear moderate in size. Septum appears reasonably straight. Appears to be some flattening on the left head of the condyle. Right side appears to have a little bit of a vertical orientation associated with the anterior aspect of the condyle. 

Waters view shows the sinuses are all fairly clear. Bony septum appears reasonably straight. Turbinates appear somewhat enlarged particularly on the left side. Vertical height of the face on the right side appears a little bit shorter than the left side. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is posteriorly positioned in the fossa. A little flattening on the superoposterior aspect of the condylar head. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Decreased joint space could be indicative of anterior displacement of the articular disc. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears to be posteriorly positioned in the fossa and may be a slight decreased superior joint space but good anterior joint space. Articular eminence is moderately steep. A little bit of anterior beaking may be present on this condyle and a little flattening on the anterior head of the condyle.

On opening, there is good translation of both condyles.
Cephalometrically, Mary has anterior vertical maxillary excess. She has a fairly large adenoid mass in her nasopharynx and a decreased nasopharyngeal airway of about 4.5 mm. Tonsils are quite large and fill up a good part of the oropharynx and actually displace the tongue forward. She has an anterior deep bite and perhaps a mild high occlusal plane angulation. Lower incisors may be just slightly overangulated. Upper incisors are in a vertical inclination. Does note that when she sees Mary taking a nap on the couch that she oftentimes has her mouth open. 

Basic Diagnoses

Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocations with early reductions
Anterior vertical maxillary excess and posterior vertical excess
AP mandibular deficiency
Lip incompetence of about 7 mm
Class II end-on occlusion being worse on the right side than the left side
Maxillary midline shifted a millimeter to the left, mandibular dental midline shifted 2 mm to the right 
Mild crowding, lower anterior arch
Anterior deep bite of 4 to 5 mm
Transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right side being 1.5 to 2 mm higher than the left side
Tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of approximately 2 mm
Headaches
TMJ pain
Myofascial pain
Hypertrophied turbinates
Nasal airway obstruction 
Hypertrophied nasopharyngeal adenoid tissue
Hypertrophied tonsils
Recurrent sore throats
Decreased oropharyngeal airway
Snoring
Daytime tiredness

Recommended Treatment

Presurgical orthodontics 
Align and level maxillary arch. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies   
Align and level mandibular arch. Strip lower anterior teeth removing approximately 2 mm of tooth structure. Use Class II elastics on the right side and Class III elastics on the left side to try to get the mandibular dental midline shifted to the left and maxillary midline shifted slightly to the right to create a more even bilateral Class II end-on occlusion and create better symmetry in both the upper and lower arch

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors 
Maxillary osteotomies to move the maxilla superiorly, advance it, and transversely level the occlusal plane
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction
Nasopharyngeal adenoidectomy
Bilateral tonsillectomy
Bilateral turbinectomies

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

original_note:
B462 
B462 is 25 years old, referred by Dr. XX.
PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Headaches.
Pain in the jaws while awakening in the morning.
Mouth locks open.
Jaw pops frequently. Lips will not shut over her teeth.
Lots of gum tissue exposed when smiling. Teeth are flattening.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS: Polyethylene glycol 3350 POWD once daily. Rozerem 8 mg daily.
The only treatment she has had was orthodontics from 13 to 11 or 10 years ago. B462's TMJ problems began about 2 years ago of unknown etiology. She did have orthodontics when she was about 12 to 14 years old. She takes no medications currently. She gets no earaches, occasional tinnitus and moderate vertigo but no hearing loss. No other joints bother her. She does have an allergy to ragweed. She gets some problems when wearing cheap earrings. She is very much aware of clenching and bruxing her teeth at nighttime as well as during the day.

She gets headaches about two to three times a week that occur predominately in the temple about a level of 5. She rates her average TMJ pain at 5, average daily pain at 5, jaw function at 5, diet at 1, disability at 3.

In frontal view, B462 has fairly good left to right facial symmetry. She has prominent masseter muscles and mandibular angles. Upper lip length is about 21 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 7 mm. Maxillary dental midline is shifted off to the right about 1 mm. Mandibular dental midline is shifted about 1.5 mm off to the right side although the chin is fairly close to being on the facial midline. In profile view, B462 has excessive length in the lower third face. She has a lip incompetence of probably about 7 mm. She has a C-shaped curve in the upper lip. Some slight AP maxillary deficiency of probably about 4 to 5 mm. Chin is retruded about 15 mm. Chin-neckline is somewhat short though the chin-neck angle is fairly well defined. She does have a slight head postured forward neck position. Intraorally, B462 has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars present. There is some very mild crowding in the lower anterior arch and she has good attached gingiva. In the upper arch she has all of her teeth back through the second molars present. She has basically a Class I cupid molar relationship with about a 2 mm overbite relationship. Oropharyngeal tissues look healthy. The uvula, soft palate and faucial pillars appear normal. Intranasally, the turbinates appear hyperplastic and enlarged. The septum is relatively straight though anteriorly on the right side there is a little bit of spurring off to the right side. She is unaware but she may have some significant nasal airway obstruction. 

The columella and nostrils appear relatively normal and luminal valves appear normal. B462's husband does tell her that she does snore some. She is not aware of daytime somnolence. She says she does wake up sometimes at night unable to catch her breath. She does have a sleep disorder and has had this all of her life. She has difficulties getting to sleep. To palpation, she has some mild tenderness over the temporalis masseter muscles but no other tenderness around the head and neck area. She has an opening click about 10 mm bilaterally and a closing click around 5 mm bilaterally. Maximal incisal opening is 45 mm but it has gotten quite painful for her. She can open about 22 mm without significant pain. Excursive movement to right is about 8 mm and to the left 7 mm. She has consistent popping in her joints when she goes from side-to-side. There is a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right cuspid being 1 mm to 1.5 mm lower than the left cuspid. Upper and lower arch appears relatively symmetric anteroposteriorly in the cuspid areas.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
:
Panorex shows the absence of all 4 third molars but all the rest of her teeth are present. She has a root canal of mandibular left first molar. It is difficult to evaluate the sinuses on this view. The condyles appear a little bit arthritic.

Waters view shows that the sinuses appear relatively clear. Frontal sinus may be a little bit cloudy but difficult to tell. The bony septum is fairly straight. The right turbinate appears fairly large and the left one appears moderately enlarged.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has arthritic changes with flattening on the anterior head and some peaking as well as anterior beaking of the condyle. Decreased superior and posterior joint space is present.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is flattened on the anterior head. There is some superior peaking and anterior beaking of the condyle. Decreased superior and posterior joint space. Articular eminence is moderately steep. On opening, there is some restricted translation with condyles being a little bit greater on the right side than the left side.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that B462 has an anterior maxillary vertical excess, significant lip incompetence, high occlusal plane angle and retruded mandible. The oropharyngeal airway space appears fairly good at the soft palate level with a dimension of about 8 to 9 mm and at the base of the tongue it is about 5 to 6 mm. At the second vertebrae down to the third vertebrae is about 5 mm.

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocations with reduction.
Anterior vertical maxillary excess.
AP mandibular deficiency. AP microgenia. Anterior mandibular vertical excess.
 

High occlusal plane.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Hypertrophied turbinates. Nasal airway obstruction. TMJ pain. Myofascial pain.
RECOMMENDED TREATMENT: Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch.
Align and level mandibular arch. Strip the lower anterior teeth removing at least 2 mm of tooth structure to keep from flaring them any further forward. Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors. Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the maxilla superiorly approximately 7 mm below the occlusal plane angle.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction. Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy to reduce the vertical height of the mandible and augment the chin.
Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies. Postsurgical orthodontics to finish and retain the occlusion.

B462  MRI Evaluation  
Age: 25/F
Date of Birth:  
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with good mediolateral width. The lateral rim of the fossa appears inferiorly about 3 mm. Decreased superior joint space particularly laterally and medially. There appears to be discal tissue anteriorly positioned to the condyle. Cortical bone appears to be intact.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with good mediolateral width. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 mm. Decreased superior joint space particularly at the lateral rim of the fossa and at the medial aspect of the condyle. There appears to be discal tissue anterior to the condyle. Cortical bone appears to be intact.

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is fairly good size anteroposterior as well as vertically. Flattening of the anterior head is present. There is peaking at the superior aspect of the condyle and some anterior beaking present as well. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Articular eminence is anteriorly displaced particularly at the lateral aspect of the joint. Towards the medial aspect it is in a more vertical orientation within the joint space. The condylar head is posterior to the disc, however. There is a lot of fluid in the superior joint space. The disc appears to be in fairly good condition throughout most of the joint except at the lateral aspect of the joint it is quite thick. On opening, there is good translation of the condyle and the disc does reduce. There is actually good translation of both the condyle and the disc.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle with some flattening on the anterior head and an osteophyte extending superior just posteriorly to the anteriorly displaced disc. The disc seems to have reasonably good morphology. Articular eminence is moderately steep. There is a little bit of anterior beaking present but not too much. Decreased posterior joint space is present. There appears to be some fluid in the inferior joint space. On opening, there is reduction of the articular disc and good translation of both the condyle and articular disc.

IMPRESSION:
Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with reduction. Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with reduction. Both discs appear salvageable.

original_note:
B293  MRI Scan Evaluation 
Date of Acquisition:	
Date of Reading: 
Age: 57 years
Left TMJ, coronal views, show condyles that are extremely small in mediolateral width. Condyles may be rotated somewhat so that perhaps they have maybe an average width but may be narrow anteroposteriorly. Appears to be actually increased joint space vertically. Very flat lateral rim of the fossa with virtually no downward curvature. Condyle appears as though it has undergone resorption. Condyle appears arthritic. Fossa appears to be intact. On some views there appears there may be some discal tissue displaced medially but difficult to tell.

Right TMJ, coronal view, shows a condyle that appears to have a little bit better mediolateral width. However, there appears to be significantly decreased vertical joint space with almost no joint space present. Lateral rim of the fossa is very flat quite similar to the opposite side. The outline of the condyle is very difficult to identify in this view. It is possible that there could be some significant loss of cortical bone on the top of the condyle perhaps. It is possible that there may be an increased joint space instead of lack of joint space, but tissues are difficult to differentiate.

Left TMJ, sagittal view, shows a condyle that is extremely small anteroposteriorly. Condyle seems to have fairly good width but extremely narrow in the AP dimension. Articular eminence is fairly significantly steep. The condyle appears quite small in reference to the size of the fossa. Appears that there may be some discal tissue anterior to the condyle but with significant degenerative changes associated with it. There is an increased superior joint space, and the posterior joint space may be slightly increased as well. No particular inflammatory response seen and only a minor amount of fluid in the superior joint space.

On opening, there is limited translation of the condyle with it coming down toward the inferior aspect of the articular eminence but not beneath it. Any discal tissue that is present remains anteriorly displaced without reduction. Disc is severely deformed and degenerated.

Right TMJ, sagittal view, shows a condyle that is extremely narrow anteroposteriorly and even worse than the opposite side. Appears to be very little condylar substance present and remaining. However, there is good mediolateral width to the condylar head. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Condyle looks quite dwarfed relative to the size of the fossa. Appears that there may be some remnants of a degenerative articular disc anterior to the condyle. Definition of the disc is not very good. There is increased joint space vertically and posterior and particularly anteriorly within this joint. No particular inflammatory process seen with just a minimal amount of fluid in the superior joint space.

On opening, there is significant restriction of translation. Appears to be a little bit more restriction on this side as compared to the opposite side. Any discal remnants present remain anteroposteriorly displaced without reduction.

Impression:
Left TMJ severe arthritis and articular disc dislocation without reduction and severe degenerative changes
Right TMJ severe arthritis and articular disc dislocation with severe degenerative changes and severely deformed
Probable candidate for bilateral total joint prostheses

B293 
B293 is 57 years old and referred by Dr. __. Patient History She has had previous back surgery with effusion 4 years ago. Removal of hardware from the back was done 3 year ago. She had a partial facelift done last year by Dr. __.

B293 gives no history of problems with earaches, but she gets occasional tinnitus and vertigo but no hearing loss. The only other joint that really bothers her is her right hip. She has an allergy to IODINE but no known drug allergies or food allergies. No problems wearing jewelry that she is aware of. She is aware of clenching and bruxing of her teeth at night and sometimes during the day.

B293's TMJ problems began about two to three years ago of unknown etiology although it possibly could be related to her clenching and bruxism. The back surgery was 4 years a go and then a repeat 3 years ago. Stressed that this may have contributed and may have caused her to clench and brux perhaps. She began orthodontics on her lower teeth last year and in earlier this year the upper appliances were placed. She has had previous biofeedback treatments. She gets daily headaches in the temporal areas that she registers at a level of 5. She rates her average TMJ pain at 4 and overall average facial and jaw pain at 5, jaw function at 5, diet at 7, and disability at 5. She has some numbness in the left cheek area that has been there since the facelift procedure was performed. She gets pain in her neck and shoulder areas that she rates at about a level of 4.

Current Medications
Tylenol sometimes 2 to 6 per day Extra Strength for headaches
Ultra 50 mg as needed for pain
Clonazepam 0.5 mg as needed for sleep

Patient Concerns
Headaches
Jaw joint pain
Jaw joints click and pop
Jaws feel as though they are locked in the mornings
Neck and shoulder hurting
Teeth and jaws don't fit together and function well
Difficulties eating and chewing

Clinical Examination
In frontal view, B293 has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 21 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 1. Maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline as is the chin. Mandibular dental midline is shifted to the left about 3 mm.

In profile view, she has good balance between the upper lips, cheeks, and nose. Mandible appears short vertically and appears somewhat retruded. Chin appears to be probably about 8 to 10 mm AP deficient. Chin-neckline is short, although the chin-neck angle is moderately well-defined.

Intraorally, B293 has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars present. A slight accentuated curve of occlusion is present. Seems to have adequate attached gingiva. Lower incisors appear overangulated and a little bit crowded yet.

In the upper arch, B293 has all of her teeth back through the second molars present. B293  has a Class II end-on occlusion cuspid relationship on the right side and the left side with it being a little bit greater on the left side. It is almost a full Class II occlusion on the left side. Has an anterior deep bite of about 3 mm. The left cuspid in the lower arch is about 1.5 mm posterior to the right cuspid. Upper arch is fairly symmetric.

Incisal opening is 37 mm. Excursive movement to the right is 9 mm and to the left 10 mm. She has crepitus in both TMJs, and she has an opening pop in the left joint at about 20 mm and closing pop at about 15. To palpation, she has moderate pain over the temporalis, masseters, TMJs, posterior neck and shoulder areas, and mild to moderate pain to palpation over the styloid areas and SCMs.

Intraorally, oropharynx appears clear. May be a decreased dimension between the soft palate and the posterior pharyngeal wall area.
Intranasally, the turbinates are moderately enlarged. B293 does feel she has difficulty breathing through her nose. Septum has some deviation to it. Columella is extremely wide making the nostrils narrow. I could constrict the columella, and she could breathe easier through her nose. She has some mild constricture of the luminal valves intranasally but not too significantly.

Radiographic Evaluation
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. She has root canals on the left upper and lower first molars, right second bicuspid, and both central incisors. Sinuses appear clear. Turbinates appear moderate in size. Right condyle appears extremely arthritic. Left condyle appears arthritic but not nearly the degenerative changes as seen on the right side.

Waters view shows that the sinuses appear clear. Could have a little cyst on the right mesial sinus wall. Septum is deviated significantly off to the side, although turbinates appear moderate in size. Sinuses all appear clear.

For the left TMJ tomogram, she probably was not in centric relation as the mandible is postured forward into a Class I occlusion. She has an increased superior and posterior joint space. Will need to redo these x-rays. Condyle appears somewhat small in size. Appears quite small compared to the size of the fossa. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is significantly posteriorly positioned in the fossa in centric relation. Condyle is quite small anteroposteriorly. It is particularly small in reference to the size of the fossa. Articular eminence is quite long in length. Slope is sort of moderate. Appears to have significant arthritis in this joint as well as the opposite one.

For the right TMJ tomogram, she is in a centric relation position on this side. Condyle is posteriorly positioned in the fossa. Condyle is extremely small and probably perhaps a third to a quarter of the normal size. Articular eminence is extremely long vertically with sort of a moderate slope to it. Increased anterior joint space.

On opening, there appears to be restricted translation in both joints, but this is not compatible with my clinical impression.
Cephalometrically, B293 has a skeletal Class II relationship. She has a high occlusal plane angle an AP microgenia. Lower incisors appear overangulated. Oropharyngeal airway, however, looks quite good. Anterior vertical height of the mandible could be a little bit short.

B293 demonstrates a tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2 mm. In repositioning the models by hand, she has a tooth-size discrepancy with a Class II end-on cuspid relationship. On the right side, however, the teeth fit vertically together much better than they do on the left side where there is probably a 2 to 3-mm open bite present. This is in part due to the fact that the left first molar and second bicuspid are somewhat depressed, and the distal part of the second molar is elevated compared to the anterior part.

Basic Diagnoses
Bilateral TMJ severe arthritis and articular disc dislocations with perforations
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency
AP mandibular deficiency
Class II occlusion being a little bit worse on the left side than the right side
Lower arch asymmetry with the left cuspid being about 2 mm posterior to the right cuspid
Mandibular dental midline shifted 2 mm to the left of the chin
Significant overangulation of the lower incisors
Mild crowding, lower anterior arch
Headaches
TMJ pain
Myofascial pain
Tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of about 2 to 3 mm
Overangulation of lower incisors
Hypertrophied turbinates
Extremely wide columella constricting the nostrils Nasal airway obstruction

Recommended Treatment
Presurgical orthodontics Continue to align and level maxillary arch. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies
Continue to align and level mandibular arch. Strip lower anterior teeth removing at least 2 mm or perhaps 3 mm of tooth structure to keep from flaring them forward any further and actually hopefully retracting them some as well as to correct for the tooth-size discrepancy Consider using interarch elastics to try to help align up the mandibular dental midline back onto the midline of the chin. This could requite some Class III elastics on the right side and Class II elastics on the left side. Also the lower teeth on the right side could be stripped a little bit more as compared to the left side to further help shift the mandibular dental midline the 2 mm required to get it in the midline of the mandible

CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol)
Surgery
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvested from the abdomen)
Bilateral coronoidectomies
Multiple maxillary osteotomies with bone plate stabilization and hydroxyapatite grafting to advance it slightly, downgraft it posteriorly, as well as expand it
Partial turbinectomies
Reconstruction of the nasal columella

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion
Restorative dentistry as necessary to finalize the bite relationship

original_note:
JANET SCHERR									09/12/2008

Janet is 59 years old, referred by Dr. William Hang.  Janet is in good health except for her jaw problems.  Janet’s TMJ problems began she believes when she was about 27 years old when she had clicking and popping in her jaws of unknown etiology.  She developed a closed lock and it took about 6 weeks for that to release.  She was on some anti-inflammatory medicines and muscle relaxers to relieve that problem.  It was until about 2000, as best as Janet can recall, that her bite began to shift and change.  She then switched to Dr. Ross, who has managed her since that time.  Janet has undergone physical therapy for 10 years trying to control her situation.  She has worn a splint from about 2005 to 2008 and this has helped her significantly, although she still has some significant problems.  In the past, she has had equilibrations and also dental reconstruction performed.  The only medication she currently takes is HRT.  

Janet really does not get any headaches.  She rates her TMJ pain at 5, average daily pain at 4, jaw function at 8, diet at 6, and disability at 8.  She has no trouble with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, or hearing loss and no other joints bother her.  She is aware of clenching or bruxing of her teeth at nighttime.  Janet does wear a night guard at night.  Primary concerns that Janet has includes the following:

Extreme difficulties chewing. 
Her bite is off. 
Requires several visits to physical therapy per week.
Pain and discomfort in the jaws.

Janet states that her physical therapy is very painful for her, although she does find that it is helpful afterwards. 

CLINICAL EXAMINATION:  In frontal view, Janet has fairly good symmetry in the middle and upper third face.  Upper lip length is about 22 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is zero.  The maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline.  The nasal tip may be just slightly towards the right side.  The mandibular dental midline is 2 mm off to the left side.  Chin is probably about 5 mm off to the left side.  In profile view, Janet has fairly good balance between the upper lip, cheeks, and nose.  Chin appears retruded and chin neckline is short and chin neck angle is not defined.  Intraorally, Janet is missing the mandibular right first molar, has a hemisected tooth that is probably the second molar, and has a bridge that goes from the right first bicuspid to the hemisected remaining root of the second molar including four units.  On the left side, she is missing the left second molar, but the third molar has drifted forward.  She has crowns on the bicuspids and molars.  In the upper arch, she is missing the maxillary right second bicuspid and third molar.  On the left side, she is missing the first bicuspid replaced by a pontic.  She has crowns on all of the posterior teeth plus she has crowns on the maxillary cuspids and left lateral incisor.  She has a full Class II cuspid relationship on the left side and closer to Class I on the right side.  She has an overjet of about 2 mm.  She has about a 1 mm vertical over bite.  She has a tendency towards a buccal cross bite on the right side.  Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively healthy.  Uvula is moderately elongated.  The soft palate is maybe elongated.  Fascial pillars may be mildly constricted.  Tonsils look relatively normal.  Nasal airway at least anteriorly looks relatively clear.  There is some deviation of the nasal septum as it curves a little bit from side to side, but the airway appears to be open.  To palpation, she has mild to moderate pain over the temporalis, masseters, styloids, SCMs, shoulders, and particularly the TMJs.  She has crepitation in both joints on jaw function.  Maximal opening is 33 mm and she could open to about 23 without significant pain.  Excursion movement to the right about 7 mm, to the left 5 mm.  There is a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the left maxillary cuspid being about 2 mm higher than the right side. 
RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows Janet is missing the mandibular right first molar, has a hemisected tooth that is probably the second molar, and has a bridge that goes from the right first bicuspid to the hemisected remaining root of the second molar including four units.  On the left side, she is missing the left second molar, but the third molar has drifted forward.  She has crowns on the bicuspids and molars.  In the upper arch, she is missing the maxillary right second bicuspid and third molar.  On the left side, she is missing the first bicuspid replaced by a pontic.  She has crowns on all of the posterior teeth plus she has crowns on the maxillary cuspids and left lateral incisor.  She has bilateral TMJ severe arthritis, the left side being worse than the right side with greater loss of condylar volume on the left side as compared to the right side.  The articular eminences look fairly flat.  It is difficult to evaluate the sinuses.    
Waters view shows that the sinuses appear relatively clear.  The bony septum appears to be in two pieces with the bottom part rotated significantly off towards the left side and the upper part a little bit off towards the left side.  Turbinates appear moderate in size with the right being larger than the left one.  
Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is quite arthritic.  There is significant loss of volume vertically and anteroposteriorly.  There is decreased superior and posterior joint spaces.  Articular eminence is relatively shallow.  
Right TMJ tomogram likewise shows a condyle that has undergone significant arthritic changes.  The articular eminence on this side is even more shallow than the opposite side.  There is significantly decreased vertical joint space.  On opening, there is actually moderately good translation in both joints.  
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that the mandible appears to be slightly retruded.  There is a decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension from the soft palate to posterior pharyngeal wall of only about 4 mm.  At the base of the tongue, it measures about at the most 7 mm.  

Basic diagnoses for Janet would be as follows:    

Bilateral TMJ severe arthritis. 
AP mandibular hypoplasia and asymmetry. 
Maxillary posterior vertical hypoplasia and asymmetry.
Class II occlusion on the left side and closer to Class I on the right side.
Tendency towards buccal cross bite on the right side. 
Missing the mandibular right first molar, has a hemisected tooth that is probably the second molar, and has a bridge that goes from the right first bicuspid to the hemisected remaining root of the second molar including four units.  On the left side, she is missing the left second molar, but the third molar has drifted forward.  She has crowns on the bicuspids and molars.  In the upper arch, she is missing the maxillary right second bicuspid and third molar.  On the left side, she is missing the first bicuspid replaced by a pontic.  She has crowns on all of the posterior teeth plus she has crowns on the maxillary cuspids and left lateral incisor.  
Mild crowding   lower anterior arch. 
Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of 3 mm.
TMJ pain. 
Myofascial pain. 

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Presurgical orthodontics.  
Align and level maxillary arch.    
Align and level mandibular arch.  Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing 3 mm of tooth structure and retract.
CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol).
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction, mandibular advancement, and asymmetry correction with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to down graft the posterior aspect of the maxilla and correct the asymmetry.  
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen). 
Bilateral coronoidotomies.
4.   Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
5.   Restorative dentistry.

original_note:
SUSAN HOFFMAN									10/27/2007

Susan is 36 years old and is referred by Dr. Peter Crump, 24 S. Gore Ave., St. Louis, MO 63119.  Susan is 5 feet 1 inch and weighs 99 pounds.  On June 23, 2006, she was playing whiffleball and fell striking her chin.  She required sutures in her chin.  She sustained bilateral subcondylar fractures.  Her jaw was wired together for 6 weeks.  She then wore 2 weeks of training bands after that.  Arch bars were removed at that time and that is when her jaw malalignment problems were first identified.  She has had some physical therapy as well.  

In frontal view, Susan has more fullness on the left side of the face than the right side by probably 4 to 5 mm.  Upper lip length is about 23 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 1 mm on the left and 2 mm on the right.  Maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline.  Mandibular dental midline is shifted off to the right side probably about 1 to 2 mm.  Chin is fairly close to being on the facial midline.  In profile view, Susan may have 2 to 3 mm AP deficiency in the maxilla.  Chin is retruded approximately 22 mm.  Chin-neckline is extremely short.  Intraorally, Susan has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars present.  She has some crowns on the first and second molars.  She has a blocked-out right lateral incisor to the lingual side.  A little bit of crowding in the lower arch of probably about 4 to 5 mm.  She appears to have adequate attached gingiva.  In the upper arch she has all of her teeth back through the second molars present.  She has an accentuated curve of Spee with the 4 incisors elevated significantly above the cuspids.  She occludes only on her second molars with the rest of her teeth out of occlusion.  Susan has a 4 mm anterior open bite.  She has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship being about 4 to 5 mm Class II.  Oropharyngeal areas look relatively normal tissue-wise.  The uvula is moderately elongated.  The soft palate is mildly elongated.  Faucial pillars may be just mildly constricted.  Susan has found that her swallowing has been affected since her injury and it is much more difficult for her to swallow, particularly for instance, swallowing a pill.  Intranasally, the turbinates appear moderately enlarged.  She has a significant septal spur off the left side of the nose that seems to partially block the airway.  Turbinate appears small in that particular area but appears to be larger behind the spur.  She does not report any significant problems with snoring, daytime somnolence and sleep apnea problems. 

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION:
Panorex shows bilaterally fractured condyles.  The left condyle is vertically overlapped and anteriorly displaced out of the fossa.  The right condyle is fractured and displaced almost in a posterior direction.  The condyle and the condylar neck vertically overlap on both sides.  The sinuses look relatively clear.  The turbinates are moderately enlarged.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Susan has an anterior open bite and retruded mandible.  Vertical height of the angles of the mandible are differentiated with one side being significantly higher than the other side by probably 6 or 8 mm.  She does have an anterior open bite of probably 4 to 5 mm.  Oropharyngeal airway space is significantly reduced posteriorly being only probably 3 mm at the base of the tongue and maybe 4 to 5 mm at the soft palate level. 




SUSAN HOFFMAN				10/27/2006				Page 2


BASIC DIAGNOSES:
Bilateral mandibular subcondylar fractures.
AP mandibular retrusion. 
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
Slight AP maxillary deficiency.
High mandibular occlusal plane angle.
Anterior open bite of about 4 to 5 mm.
Class II end-on occlusion.
Occluding only on the second molars.
Significantly decreased oropharyngeal airway.
 Difficulties eating and chewing.
 Difficulties swallowing.
 Crowding of lower anterior arch.
 Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 3 mm. 
 Increased curve of Spee in maxillary arch.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT: 
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch.  Extrude maxillary incisors about 1 mm or so to correct the reverse curve of Spee.  
Align and level mandibular arch.  Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing approximately 3 mm of tooth structure to correct for the tooth size discrepancy. 
CT scans of TMJs and jaws (TMJ Concepts protocol.)
Surgical option 1.
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. 
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts.
Bilateral coronoidectomies.
Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy to advance the chin.
Surgical option 2.
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. 
Maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla in a counterclockwise direction.
Bilateral coronoidectomies.
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen.)
HTR implant to anterior mandible.
Surgical option 3.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible.
Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy to augment the chin.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
Restorative dentistry.

original_note:
B266  MRI Evaluation
Left TMJ, coronal view, the quality of the MRI is not very good. Condyle appears narrow mediolaterally but it is very difficult to identify the anatomy of the joint. Appears there must have been a lot of movement present. Appears to be roughness both on the condyle and in the fossa, but again this may be distorted because of movement. On one view, the condyle appears to have fairly good width. Difficult to define any disc tissue.

Right TMJ, coronal view, is similar in poor image quality probably due to movement and difficult to identify presence of the condyle and identify where the disc might be.

Left TMJ, sagittal view, shows a condyle with decreased posterior joint space. Articular eminence is moderately steep and fairly long. Articular disc is anteriorly displaced and positioned beneath the articular eminence. Disc appears to have reasonably good morphology, however, yet. Some anterior beaking is present. Cortical bone on top of the condyle appears somewhat thin.

On opening, she has fairly good translation. Difficult to tell if the disc reduces or not because the image of the disc is hard to identify. Condyle appears somewhat narrow anteroposteriorly. The disc may be nonreducing. Articular eminence itself lights up quite significantly possibly indicative of a large marrow space present. The shape of the condylar head is somewhat unusual.

Right TMJ, sagittal view, shows a condyle with significant distortion. Significant anterior beaking and sclerosis are present. Decreased joint space is present. Significantly decreased superior and posterior joint space. Disc is anteriorly positioned sitting beneath the articular eminence. Articular eminence is moderately steep but vertically not as long as the opposite side. No particular inflammatory process.

On opening, there is moderate translation of the condyle. Articular disc appears to be anteriorly displaced without reduction.
Impression:
Left TMJ articular disc dislocation and morphology deformation of the condylar head itself suggestive of either condylar hyperplasia or osteochondroma.
Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with significant deformation and possible salvage ability.

B266 
B266 is 17 years old and referred by Dr. __.
Patient History

She has had ear tubes x 3. B266 currently has a sinus infection that has just developed. She has a history of asthma and does use an inhaler for that. She has hypogamma globulinemia.

About two years ago, B266 and her family became aware that her jaw was becoming asymmetrical and growing off to the right side. The problem has been present for about two years.

B266 has some problems with her shoulders, but primarily she has problems with her ankles that are very weak, and her feet also bother her some. She has a history of torticollis, and she did wear a neck brace for a while and had massages for management of that.

She gets headaches about two to three times per week that occur primarily in the temporal and frontal area that register at about a level of 6. She rates her TMJ pain at about 8, although it is not there all the time but quite frequently, overall pain at 9, jaw function at 8, diet at 6, and disability at 6.

She had orthodontic treatment where she was in appliances for approximately six years and had been followed by an orthodontist from about 9 years ago until last year. She was in braces when the mother noticed the facial asymmetry and brought that to the attention of the orthodontist. She was then referred to Dr. __, an oral surgeon, who suggested the braces be removed and B266 just be monitored to evaluate facial growth. He was going to wait until growth was completed and then do some corrective surgery. Current Medications
She takes Omnicef, which she just started for the sinus infection. SULFA medications give her a rash.
Primary Concerns
Primary concerns that B266 has at this time include the following:

Facial asymmetry.
Clicking and popping in the jaw joints.
Inability to open wide.
Earaches.
Some lightheadedness and dizziness.
Headaches.
Pain in the jaw joints.
Difficulties eating and chewing.
Difficulties breathing through the nose.
Predominantly mouth breathing particularly when sleeping.
Snoring.
A lot of tiredness and low energy during the day.

Clinical Evaluation
In frontal view, B266 has fairly good facial symmetry in the middle and upper third face. Upper lip length is about 20 mm. Upper tooth to lip relationship is about 3.5 mm. Maxillary dental midline is pretty much on the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline may be just slightly to the left maybe 0.5 mm to a millimeter. Left side of the mandible is probably about 8 mm lower than the right side. Some slight increased fullness in the mandibular area on the left side and a little flattening on the right side, probably about a 3 or 4 mm transverse difference.

In profile, B266 is probably about 4 to 6 mm AP deficient in maxillary projection. She is probably about 10 to 12 mm AP deficient. Chin-neckline is short, although the chin-neck angle is fairly well defined. B266 does carry her head in a neck postured forward head position. She does have some nasal dorsal prominence. Cheeks have good projection. Lower third face appears a little bit elongated.

Intraorally, actually she has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. She has some moderate crowding, lower anterior arch. Lower teeth gingiva is very thin. In the upper arch, she is missing a right permanent maxillary second bicuspid but has a retained primary first molar in place. She also is missing the maxillary right cuspid tooth. These must be congenitally missing as well as her third molars as eh reports not having any teeth previously extracted. She has a slight Class II cuspid relationship on the left side and slight Class III on the right side. She has an anterior overbite of about 1.5 to 2 mm. She has a transverse occlusal cant with the right side being about 3 to 4 mm higher than the left side. Transversely, the mandibular right cuspid is about 2 mm posterior to the left cuspid. Likewise, the maxillary right cuspid is 2 mm posterior to the left cuspid. The yaw of the maxilla and mandible posteriorly is about 3 mm off towards the left side creating the arch asymmetry and probably accounting for the individual arch asymmetry with the right cuspids being more posterior compared to the left cuspids. Posterior faucial pillars are quite posteriorly positioned, and even when she is relaxed, the soft palate falls fairly close to the posterior pharyngeal wall area. Transverse width of the posterior faucial pillars is somewhat narrow.

Intranasally, the septum is deviated off towards the left side. The left turbinate is of moderate size, but it appears to be kissing against the septum, thus, probably causing significant airway blockage. On the right side, the turbinate is much larger than the left side, but there appears to be adequate airway because of the significant deviation of the septum off towards the left side. Columella is wide anteriorly, and the nostrils are somewhat constricted. Superior end of the lower lateral cartilages curves inwardly and causes some constricture of the nose in about the luminal valve area.

To palpation, she has moderate to very high moderate pain around the temporalis, masseters, TMJs, styloid areas, and milder tenderness to palpation around the medial pterygoids and shoulders. Maximal incisal opening is about 30 mm but without significant pain 24 mm. Excursive movements are less than 0.5 mm in each direction.

Radiographic Evaluation
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars and maxillary right second bicuspid with a retained primary cuspid. Left sinus appears a little bit cloudy compared to the right side. Difficult to evaluate the turbinates in this view. Right condyle appears a little bit small in size particularly in the condylar head and neck length. Difficult to see the top of the condyle. Left condyle appears enlarged and bigger than normal.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears to be posteriorly positioned in the fossa. Virtually no superoposterior joint space. Articular eminence is relatively shallow. Head may be tipped up quite a ways on this x-ray. This would be compatible with a displaced articular disc. May be a little bit of sclerosis on the head of the condyle.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has a little bit of unusual shape to it. Appears to have some unevenness across the surface in one of the views. Articular eminence is relatively shallow. Joint space appears decreased and posteriorly the posterior joint space in one of the views. This also could be compatible with articular disc dislocation. She demonstrates translation that is greater on the left side than the right side in her opening.

On the open view, there is anterior beaking present on the right condyle indicative of some degenerative changes occurring. Joint space is very narrow on the left side between the condyle and the articular eminence.

On the Waters view, she is in asymmetric posture. Fluid in the left sinus is present compatible with her sinus infection. Nasal septum is significantly deviated off towards the left side. Difficult to evaluate her turbinates because the x-ray is not straight on.

Cephalometrically, transverse occlusal plane looks fairly good. She may have had her head tilted a little bit when this x-ray was taken. Appears to have a retruded position of the maxilla and mandible. Head is tipped upwards. Oropharyngeal airway at the base of the tongue level is probably only about 3 or 4 mm and the same dimension at the soft palatal level.

Basic Diagnoses
Left TMJ osteochondroma versus condylar hyperplasia and articular disc dislocation.
Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation without reduction.
AP maxillary deficiency.
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
AP mandibular deficiency.
Significant transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right side being 3 to 4 mm higher than the left side.
Class II end-on occlusion on the left side, Class III end-on occlusion on the right side.
Missing maxillary right cuspid and second bicuspid as well as all four third molars.
Retained primary E in the maxillary right second bicuspid area but without any substantial root structure remaining.
High occlusal plane angulation.
Severe decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Nasal septum deviated off to the left side.
Hypertrophied turbinates.
Wide columella base with narrow nostrils.
Constricture of luminal valves primarily as a result of inward rotation of the superior aspect of the lower lateral cartilages.
Severe nasal airway obstruction.
Headaches. TMJ pain.
Myofascial pain.
Earaches.
Tinnitus.
Vertigo.
Daytime somnolence.
Sleep apnea symptoms.
Snoring.
Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2 mm

Recommended Treatment
Presurgical orthodontics
Align and level maxillary arch. Open about 1 mm of space around each upper lateral incisor to help correct for the tooth-size discrepancy. In the upper arch, the right first bicuspid will be used as the cuspid.
Align and level mandibular arch.

Evaluate for continued growth of the left condyle
Surgery
Left TMJ high condylectomy and articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor.
Right TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor and possible removal of anterior osteophytic development on the condyle.
Maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla, level it transversely, and decrease the AP occlusal plane level.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction as well as level the transverse occlusal plane.
Bilateral inferior turbinectomies.
Nasal septoplasty.
Reconstruct luminal valves.
Reconstruction of columella base.

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion
Extract retained maxillary right primary E
Osseointegrated implant to maxillary right second bicuspid area

Dental reconstruction
Dental model analysis demonstrates an anterior tooth-size discrepancy of 2 mm.
B266 
The patient and her parents came in for further consultation. I last saw B266 on this year. She has been diagnosed with Kallmann's syndrome that also has a Gn-RH deficiency. She has never had any menstrual periods. The mother said the facial asymmetry goes along with this syndrome, and they also have long, thin fingers, long arm span, and weak ligaments. They also have learning disabilities. I do have some information on that and will review that syndrome. She worked at Starbucks for a while but is so tired from her four to five hours of work that it is overwhelming for her. She had to quit her work because she was too tired to keep up the pace. The sleepiness could be coming from the sleep apnea problems. She rates her headaches at a level of 6 in the frontal and temporal areas, TMJ pain at 7, overall daily pain on the average at 5, jaw function at 8, diet and disability at 7. Her headaches can escalate to 10. Sometimes she wakes up with them, and other times they begin during the day or can even begin at night or get worse at that time. She has fairly constant headaches. Maximal incisal opening is 30 mm, and she could open about 30 mm without too significant pain. She could not voluntarily go into any excursions. Occlusion seems to be remaining about the same with basically a Class I occlusion. To palpation, she has high moderate to low severe pain around the temporalis, masseters, medial pterygoids, TMJs, and styloid areas. She has mild to moderate pain to palpation around the SCMs and posterior neck and shoulder areas. She takes Motrin and occasionally will take Prevacid for acid reflux. Other joints that bother her include her knees and her ankles. The knees just started to be a problem of significance since December. She does get earaches, and she gets occasionally vertigo but no tinnitus. She has an IgA and an IgG deficiency, and her endocrinologist wants to treat her with gamma globulin injections prior to the surgery. She remains with a slight Class III cuspid relationship on the right side and slight Class II on the left side. She has significant upper arch and lower arch asymmetries with the right cuspids being about 3 mm posterior to the left cuspids. Mandible and maxilla have about a 3-mm yaw off toward the left side. She continues to have her nasal airway obstruction problems. Her pain has gotten worse for her, and she now would like to proceed on and get her treatment completed. We need to check with Dr. __ to make sure that he is satisfied that we could proceed on with treatment. She could be done earlier than the tentatively scheduled time from my standpoint but will need to make sure that Dr. __ agrees.

Other Systems
Other joints that bother her include her knees and ankles. She has had a few urinary tract/bladder infections. She recently had some surgery to remove a membrane in her vagina. She had some bleeding problems after that surgery, but everything is fine now. That was done I believe in December. Reproductive system: She has never had a period, and this is secondary to her Kallmann's syndrome. She has been diagnosed with gingivitis in the past. She has a lot of problems with pink eye and gets an infection annually and periodically has had sties. She gets frequent sinusitis. She gets earaches and occasional vertigo. She gets frequent ear infections. Sometimes she will have problems with recurrent sore throats but feels this may be allergy related. She has had bronchitis x 4, and she had pneumonia once at the age of 13. She does have some problems with constipation and does have acid reflux. Did not question her about STDs. She does have a lot of problems with fatigue. No significant exposure to birds, except she had one dog I think when she was quite little that in the past had caught some birds. She has had minimal exposure to cats and dogs all of her life. She has had some minimal exposure to hoofed animals including cows and pigs. The family owns a ranch, but she really isn't out much with the animals. She does do some 4-wheeling through the pastures where the cattle are.

B266  CAT Scan Sinus Evaluation
Date of Acquisition: Date of Reading:
Sinus series shows that the frontal, ethmoid, and maxillary sinuses appear quite clear She has a significantly deviated nasal septum off toward the left side. Turbinates are moderately large, but it appears that she has a passable airway.

Left TMJ, coronal view, appears to have significantly decreased vertical joint space. The condyle has fairly good mediolateral width. Lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly 3 to 4 mm. Appears to be a significantly decreased vertical joint space.

Right TMJ, coronal view shows a condyle with an unusual shape to it. There seems to be lateral extension of the lateral pole of the condyle. It extends out underneath the lateral rim of the fossa. Significantly decreased vertical joint space. Lateral rim of the fossa appears to curve inferiorly about 4 mm. Appears to be some degenerative changes associated with this joint.

Impression:
Normal sinuses
Bilateral TMJ arthritis
Deviated nasal septum
Nasal airway looks clear

Dear :
I recently have evaluated B266. B266 is 17 years old and has been under orthodontic treatment since 9 years ago. She has been treated by a number of different orthodontists, and she has lived in different parts of the country. Two to three years ago, B266 and her family noticed that she was developing facial asymmetry. She also has had a lot of other TMJ-related symptoms as listed below in her concerns.

B266's primary concerns at this time include the following:
Facial asymmetry.

Inability to open wide.
Earaches.
Some lightheadedness and dizziness.
Headaches.
Pain in the jaw joints.
Difficulties eating and chewing.
Difficulties breathing through the nose.
Predominantly mouth breathing particularly when sleeping.
Snoring.
A lot of tiredness and low energy during the day.

Based on clinical, radiographic, dental model, and MRI analyses, B266's diagnoses are as follows:
Left TMJ osteochondroma versus condylar hyperplasia and articular disc dislocation.
Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation without reduction.
AP maxillary deficiency.
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
AP mandibular deficiency.
Significant transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right side being 3 to 4 mm higher than the left side.
Class II end-on occlusion on the left side, Class III end-on occlusion on the right side.

Retained primary E in the maxillary right second bicuspid area but without any substantial root structure remaining.
High occlusal plane angulation.
Severe decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Nasal septum deviated off to the left side.
Hypertrophied turbinates.
Wide columella base with narrow nostrils.
Constricture of luminal valves primarily as a result of inward rotation of the superior aspect of the lower lateral cartilages.
Severe nasal airway obstruction.

Myofascial pain.
Earaches.
Tinnitus.
Vertigo.
Daytime somnolence.
Sleep apnea symptoms.
Snoring.
Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2 mm.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
Presurgical orthodontics
Align and level maxillary arch. Open about 1 mm of space around each upper lateral incisor to help correct for the tooth-size discrepancy. In the upper arch, the right first bicuspid will be used as the cuspid.
Align and level mandibular arch.

Evaluate for continued growth of the left condyle
Surgery
Left TMJ high condylectomy and articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor.
Right TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor and possible removal of anterior osteophytic development on the condyle.
Maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla, level it transversely, and decrease the AP occlusal plane level.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction as well as level the transverse occlusal plane.

Extract retained maxillary right primary E
Osseointegrated implant to maxillary right second bicuspid area

Dental reconstruction
I do not believe that there are any major movements required orthodontically at this time, except for opening 1 mm of space around each of the maxillary lateral incisors and basically aligning and leveling the other teeth. Tip the upper lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies if they become necessary. I feel that this treatment plan would get B266 the best possible functional and aesthetic result and best chance to eliminate her ear symptoms and eliminate her headache, TMJ, and myofascial pain problems.

If you have any other thoughts or suggestions in reference to her treatment, please give me a call. I very much look forward to the opportunity of working with you in correcting B266's problems and concerns.

With best regards,

SHARON PERRY									10/12/2009

Sharon is 52 years old and referred by Dr. Robert Henderson, 723 N. Fielder, Suite E, Arlington, TX 76012.  Sharon had a mandibular right first molar that she had lost a crown with just basically root retention left.  On July 17, 2009, the tooth was extracted and an immediate dental implant was placed.  She had a lot of pain initially afterward and numbness to the right lower lip, chin, teeth, and gums.  On July 20, 2009, the original doctor changed out the implant and put a short implant in.  She continues to have problems with the sensitivity to the lower right lip, chin, teeth, and gums.  She currently is on Tegretol 100-mg tablets, 2 tablets b.i.d.  She also takes Femhrt 1/5 to 1 tablet daily.  Neurological testing for sensitivity to the lower lip and chin shows that with 2-point discrimination she has a normal response on the left side, but on the right side she could not distinguish two pressure points at 20 mm or less.  Von Fry hairs recorded 1.65 on the left side and 4.17 on the right side, but she felt it mainly as pressure.  With brush stroke, she could get some of the strokes on the right side, but had some difficulties with accuracy.  With sharp stimulus, she could feel sharp on both sides, but not with as good as sensibility on the right side as the left side.  With cold, there was delayed onset of cold sensation, although she could felt it.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows some difficulty in identifying the neurovascular canal beneath the present implant.  In one view, it appears it might be just lateral to the implant and then it looks like it may be medial and then it looks like it is very hard to distinguish.  Panorex shows she is missing all four third molars and has an implant in the mandibular right first molar area.  She has arthritis in both TMJs.  
TMJ tomograms show that she has arthritis in both of her joints, but her joints are asymptomatic.   
Lateral cephalogram shows that she has an adequate oropharyngeal airway.  She has a moderately high occlusal plane angle.  

Axial view shows that the sinuses are full of fluid.  Turbinates are relatively normal in size.

Coronal view shows that the left sinus is almost completely full of fluid and the right side is about half full.  Turbinates appear relatively normal in size and the septum appears relatively straight.  Occlusally, she appears to have a good Class I cuspid-molar relationship.  She has an implant present in the mandibular right first molar area. 

Basic diagnoses would be as follows:    

Right inferior alveolar nerve traumatic neuroma.
Painful dysthesia to right lip, chin, teeth, and gums. 

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Management of symptoms with Neurontin 300-mg tablets, dispensing 60 tablets, 1 tablet b.i.d., but may increased to t.i.d. if necessary, refill x4.
Surgery.  
Right mandibular body decortication.
Exploration and excision of traumatic neuroma right inferior alveolar nerve.
Microneurosurgical repair of the right inferior alveolar nerve with possible nerve grafting.

ARUN REDDY								10/22/2010

Arun is 32 years old and referred by Pushkar Mehra.  He is currently having orthodontics done by Joshika Beejesh.  He is at Walnut Central Orthodontics, 9669 N. Central Expressway, Suite 105, Dallas, Texas 75231.  

Primary concerns Arun has includes the following:

Recession of upper jaw.
Under bite problem.
Soreness in mouth most of the time. 
Difficulties breathing through the right side of his nose.

In frontal view, Arun has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 21 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is -3 mm.  The maxillary dental midline is probably about 2 mm to the right.  The mandibular dental midline and chin are pretty much on the facial midline.  In profile, Arun has some ptosis of the nasal tip.  This makes the nasolabial angle acute.  He actually has fairly reasonable projection of the upper lip to the cheeks.  When he opens his jaw probably about 5 to 6 mm, his profile is actually fairly good with just some slight increased fullness in the chin area.  Arun does have some bilateral infraorbital hypoplasia with the cheeks being probably about 6 to 7 mm AP deficient.  Intraorally, Arun has all of his lower teeth back through the third molars present.  He has spacing between the lower central and cuspid that equal to almost an incisor on each side.  He has probably a good 4 mm of space in the right side and 3 mm on the left side.  In the upper arch, he has braces on back to the second bicuspids, but has molars all the way back to the third molars present.  He has a full Class III cuspid relationship with anterior and bilateral posterior cross bites.  He has a large diastema between the central incisors of probably 4 mm and has a supernumerary tooth in place.  He has about a 1 mm anterior open bite at the incisor area.  Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively normal in architecture.  Tonsils are mild to moderately enlarged.  Intranasally, turbinates appear moderate in size and the septum at least anteriorly seems reasonably straight.  On holding the nostrils open, Arun could breath easier through his nose.  To palpation, Arun has no pain around the head and neck area.  On opening, he has a click in the left joint at 30 mm of opening and 30 mm of closing.  Right joint seemed to function relatively smoothly.  The maximal incisal opening is about 82 mm.  The excursion movements are about 7 mm in each direction.  Arun has no problems with headaches, TMJ pain, or myofascial pain.  He rates his jaw function and diet at 0 and disability at 1.  He has no earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, hearing loss, or other joint problems.  He is not aware of clenching or bruxing.  Arun is unaware of when his joint problems began or the etiology.


RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the presence of all the teeth.  There is spacing between the mandibular lateral incisors and cuspids.  There is a supernumerary tooth between the maxillary central incisors.  He has a root canal in the mandibular right first molar.  

Coronal view shows that the sinuses appear clear.  The septum is reasonably straight.  The turbinates are enlarged.  

Axial view shows that the sinuses are clear.  The septum appears reasonably straight.  The turbinates are enlarged and seem to block a good portion of the airway more towards the posterior aspect of the nose.  

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is a little sclerotic on top, particularly towards the lateral aspect of the joint.  The articular eminence has a moderate inclination.  The condyle is posteriorly positioned in the fossa.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is a little flattened across the top.  The articular eminence is more shallow on this side compared to the opposite side.  The joint space may be a little bit decreased posteriorly. 

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is fairly wide.  There is decreased lateral joint space and there appears to be a little extra outgrowth from the lateral pole of the condyle that is almost in contact with the lateral rim of the fossa.  

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is quite broad in width.  There may be some decreased joint space in the right side at the lateral aspect of the joint.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that he has some vertical hypoplasia as well as perhaps some mandibular mild prognathism.  Oropharyngeal airway looks very good.  He has a skeletal and occlusal Class III jaw relationship.  

Basic diagnoses for Arun would include the following:

Left TMJ articular disc dislocation with reduction.
Maxillary vertical and AP hypoplasia.
Mandibular prognathism.
Class III occlusion.
Over angulation of maxillary incisors.
Bilateral diastemas between the lateral incisors and cuspids in the mandible.
Large diastema of approximately 4 mm between the maxillary central incisors. 
Supernumerary tooth between the maxillary incisors. 
Hypertrophied turbinates.
External nasal deformity.
Malaligned third molars.
Bilateral infraorbital hypoplasia. 

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	MRI of left and right TMJ.
2.	Removal of supernumerary tooth from between the incisors.
3.	Presurgical orthodontics. 
	a.	Align and level maxillary arch.  Retract maxillary central incisors and close the 			diastema.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make 			room for interdental osteotomies.  Maintain about 1 mm of space between the 			crowns of the maxillary lateral incisors and cuspids.
	b.	Align and level mandibular arch.  Push cuspids forward and shift the anterior 			diastemas to between the cuspids and first bicuspids.  Keep cuspid roots tipped 			mesially and first bicuspid roots tipped distally.  Do not change the angulation of 			the mandibular incisors.  
	c.	Be sure to band all the teeth back through the second molars.  
2.	Surgery Stage 1.
	a. 	Left TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor. 
	b.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to set the mandible posteriorly.
	c.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to down graft it about 5 to 6 mm and advance it 			forward.
	d.	Bilateral mandibular anterior body ostectomies.
	e.	Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies.
	f.	Extraction of all four third molars.
	g.	External nasal reconstruction.
3.	Surgery Stage 2:  Bilateral infraorbital implants.  
4.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
COE PASSMORE									03/14/2007

Coe is 21 years old and is referred by Dr. Clint Barrett, 2420 Quaker Ave., Ste. 102, Lubbock, TX.   Coe previously went to Dr. Barrett because of some facial swelling on the right side.  A biopsy was taken and the diagnosis came back as dentigerous cyst of the right mandible.  This diagnosis was taken from a biopsy taken on February 2, 2007.  On February 21, 2007, Dr. Barrett went back in and removed a large portion of the cyst.  This time it came back as an ameloblastoma.  She is here for diagnosis and management of her tumor.  

PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Presence of an ameloblastoma in the right mandible.
Protecting her mouth and tongue and facial cosmetics.  
She is scheduled to graduate from Texas Tech in May and is concerned whether that would be jeopardized.

She has problems with vasovagal syncope and tachycardia.  

CURRENT MEDICATIONS:
Pindolol.
Clindamycin. 

Radiograph that was sent by Dr. Barrett dated January 31, 2007, prior to his surgery shows that she has a multiloculated lesion in the right ramus extending from the distal of the first molar up into the body of the ramus and within about 1 cm of the sigmoid notch.  There is a possibility of a lesion actually being up in the coronoid process but difficult to ascertain.  The condyle appears clear although there is an area just below the condylar neck that is a little bit suspicious.  In addition, she has a lesion in her right maxillary sinus that could be related to a mucous retention cyst or another type of tumor.  She has mandibular third molar that is severely displaced posteriorly away from the second molar.  The second molar appears to have only bony stability along the mesial root which has undergone significant resorption.  The distal root of the first molar also has undergone a little bit of resorption.  She has all 4 third molars present but the left ones appear to be erupted but the maxillary right ones are unerupted.  She has had previous orthodontics.  She is missing mandibular first bicuspids but has all of her maxillary teeth.  Her occlusion is a Class I cuspid on the right side and Class III molar.  On the left side, she has a Class II end-on cuspid by about 3 mm and Class III molar but to a lesser extent as compared to the opposite side.  

Coe is unaware when her jaw problem began but she did have a Panorex taken about 2003 or 2004.  It showed that the mandible was clear at that time.  She felt a bump in the right mandible in mid January of 2007.  An x-ray was taken on January 31 that showed the tumor in the right mandible.  It originally was biopsied and felt to be interpreted as a dentigerous cyst.  Then following Dr. Barrett's surgery, it came back as ameloblastoma.  She was on antibiotics initially after the surgery and then it was stopped.  She then began to develop some swelling again and she was placed back on clindamycin 150 mg 4 tablets per day.  She has no trouble with earaches.  She does get some tinnitus but no vertigo in the last few months.  She has no hearing loss.  

COE PASSMORE				03/14/2007				Page 2

She is not aware of clenching or bruxism.  She has no problems with headaches, TMJ pain or myofascial pain around the head or neck area.

In frontal view, Coe has a little bit of facial swelling on the right side with just a slight increase of probably about 3 to 4 mm.  Otherwise, she has fairly good facial symmetry.  In profile view, she has good balance.  Intraorally, she is missing what appears to be actually the second bicuspid.  She has a 3 x 3 retainer in the lower anterior arch.  She has good attached gingiva in the lower anterior teeth.  She is missing the right second and third molars but has the left second and third molars.  In the upper arch she has all of her teeth back through the second molars erupted in.  She has basically a Class I cuspid relationship on the right side and a Class II end-on cuspid relationship on the left side by about 3 mm.  Mandibular dental midline is just about 1 mm to the left of the maxillary midline.  Intraorally, she has some slight expansion of the mandible on the right side.  It is difficult to determine but there does not feel to be any real significant expansion that can be felt externally.  Radiographically, however, it does show some expansion.  She does have her maxillary left third molar that appears to have erupted in but the right third molar appears to be impacted.  She does have a mucous retention cyst in the maxillary right sinus.  She does have an anterior deep bite of about 3 to 4 mm.  Oropharyngeal tissues including the uvula, soft palate and faucial pillars all appear normal.  Intranasally, she has moderate hyperplasia of the turbinates.  They do appear somewhat inflamed.  The septum appears relatively normal.  To palpation, she has very minimal tenderness over the right masseter but no other tenderness.  Maximal incisal opening is 43 mm, excursive movement to the right 8 mm, left 7 mm.  The size of the lesion measures 5 cm AP to 4.5 cm vertical.  

TREATMENT PLAN:
Presurgical orthodontics. 
Place maxillary and mandibular orthodontic appliances and archwires.

Surgery Stage 1.
 Reset ameloblastoma tumor.
 Try to preserve integrity of the inferior alveolar nerve. 
 Reconstruct the mandible with bone grafting.
Surgery stage 2.
 Osseointegrated implants.
Dental reconstruction.
