BOBBIE VAN BOGAERT							10/19/2011

She is now 68 years old, birth date 01/07/1943.  We did her surgery on her joints one at a time apparently, one in 1990 and the other side in 1991 or so, but she could not remember the exact dates.  She medically has lots of problems ongoing.  She uses a CPAP at night.  She has glaucoma and cataracts.  She has congestive heart failure.  She has multiple joint problems including her knees, hips, shoulders, left big toe, right elbow, ankles, and sacroiliac area.  She gets headaches about 3 to 4 times per month at a level of 5 in the temporal area.  She has no TMJ pain and no myofascial pain or headaches in her usual day.  She rates her jaw function at 7, diet at 5, disability at 3.  The incisal opening is 32 mm, but excursion movements are less than 1 mm in each direction.  Occlusion is very good with a good Class I with a 2 mm overbite.  To palpation, she has moderate tenderness over the SCMs, posterior neck, left shoulder, but very high moderate to low severe pain over the left TMJ and mild tenderness over the temporalis area.  She gets earaches sometimes and she has constant tinnitus and occasional vertigo.  She has some hearing loss.  She is allergic to cats, dust, cigarette smoke, Albuterol and Patanol.  She is aware of clenching at nighttime and also in the daytime.  She wears a nasal CPAP at night, gets shooting pain in her eyes and she has narrow angle glaucoma.  She has been getting injections into her right knee.  She says she has osteoporosis and osteoarthritis and she particularly has osteoarthritis in her neck.  This may be the cause of some of her pain around the head and neck area.  She has fractured her shoulder and her toes in the past.  In late 2010, she fractured off the maxillary left crown.  She wanted to know if that was related to the surgery and I told her I did not feel it was related to the surgery, but could be related to her clenching.  She does wear a night guard, but she lost it and she is having a new one made now.  She was diagnosed with Chlamydia pneumoniae in 2000 and was told she had hypersensitivity pneumonitis.  She said she got the flu while she was in Dallas with one of her surgeries.  I would like to get an I-CAT of her TMJ joints.  I will try to locate someone in the San Diego area that could help us with that.  She is to keep in touch with me.  She does have a good sense of humor yet, but it is difficult for her to get around.  She uses a cane.  She had to quit nursing a number of years ago, in fact, shortly after our surgery as she had a nervous breakdown.  We need to try to find someone in the San Diego area to get an I-CAT on her.

original_note:
MELISSA WOOLFREY								05/22/2007

Melissa is 20 years old.  Melissa's doctors are Normand S. Boucher, DDS, 1509 McDaniel Dr., Westchester, PA, 19380, phone number is 610-430-8004; pain doctor is Daniel T. Rubino, MD, 176 E. Conestoga Rd, Devon, PA, 19333, phone number is 610-688-8580, fax is 610-687-2246 and Peter D. Quinn, DMD, 5-White 3400 Spruce St., Philadelphia, PA 19104-4283, phone number is 215-662-6176.   She has had longstanding TMJ problems.  TMJ problems began when she was about 13 years old of unknown etiology.  She has had numerous surgical procedures performed.  Her problem began initially on the left side and in 2001 she had a left eminectomy and disc repositioning procedure performed.  The disc apparently came back out of place so in approximately 2002, the left disc was repositioned once again.  In 2003, she had some bilateral TMJ debridement procedures done.  Also, around 2003 or 2004, she had bilateral meniscectomies and placement of Silastic implants.  She apparently had an adverse reaction to those and they were removed sometime in 2004 a few months after they were placed.  She also had orthodontics during the past year.  Her last surgery was in 2005 where she probably had some kind of bilateral TMJ debridement.  Melissa has no problems with earaches or tinnitus but does get vertigo.  She is aware of clenching and bruxing at nighttime and sometimes during the day.  She has a sleep disorder and is unable to sleep even with medication.  Pain is bad in the morning but then gets worse in the afternoons and evenings.  She gets headaches at a level of 8 throughout most of the head.  Her TMJ is 9, average daily pain 4, jaw function and diet at 7, disability at 10.  

PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Severe TMJ pain. 
Severe headaches.
Nausea associated with severe pain. 
Cannot eat and chew.
Cannot open very wide. 

CLINICAL EXAMINATION: 
Melissa has fairly good left to right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 22 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 3.  Maxillary dental midline, mandibular dental midline and chin are all shifted to the left about 1 to 2 mm.  In profile view, she has fairly good balance between the upper lip, cheeks and nose.  Chin does appear retruded.  Chin-neckline is a little bit short although the chin-neck angle is fairly well defined.  Upper lip may be retruded a couple of mm and the chin may be retruded around 10 mm.  Intraorally, Melissa has all of her teeth back through the second molars present.  Likewise in the upper arch she has all of her teeth back through second molars.  There is some mild crowding in the lower anterior arch and in the upper anterior arch.  She has basically a Class I cuspid relationship on the left side and just slightly a Class II on the right side by maybe 1 to 2 mm.  She has an anterior deep bite of about 1.5 to 2 mm.  Oropharynx is difficult to evaluate because of limited opening but the uvula appears mildly elongated.  The soft palate and faucial pillars are very difficult to see but seem to be relatively normal.  Intranasally, the turbinates may be mildly enlarged, but not too bad.  Septum is reasonably straight.  There is some mild airway obstruction.  She also has a history mild snoring and moderate daytime somnolence but she does not report any problems with sleep apnea.  

MELISSA WOOLFREY			05/22/2007				Page 2

Melissa has had her wisdom teeth removed.  To palpation, she has moderate to high moderate pain over the temporalis and masseter muscles and SCMs, moderate tenderness over the styloids, shoulders and severe pain to the TMJs.  Her left frontal nerve is nonfunctional.  Maximal incisal opening is 21 mm but opening without severe pain is 13 mm.  She has virtually no side-to-side movements that she can do without severe pain.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION:
Panorex shows the absence of all 4 third molars but the rest of the teeth are present.  Sinuses look clear.  Joints look arthritic as best as they can be seen.  Eminectomy evident on the left joint.

Waters view shows that the sinuses are clear.  Septum is relatively straight.  Turbinates are moderate in size but there does appear to be adequate airway.  

Right TMJ tomogram shows what appears to be an arthritic joint.  There may have been an eminectomy performed on this side as well.  

Left TMJ tomogram shows very arthritic joint so there appears to be bone deposition even up in the fossa area.  Eminectomy has previously been done.  There is anterior beaking and significant flattening of the condylar head.  On opening, there is very minimal translation of either joint and opening is quite constricted.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she appears to have retrusion of the maxilla and the mandible.  She has a high occlusal plane angle.  Oropharyngeal airway measures about 4 mm at the soft palate level and about 5 mm at the base of the tongue.  She has some slight vertical maxillary excess and retrusion of both the maxilla and mandible.  She has a tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 1 mm.  

BASIC DIAGNOSES: 
Bilateral TMJ severe arthritis.
AP mandibular deficiency.
Slight AP maxillary deficiency. 
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency. 
Slight anterior vertical maxillary excess.
High occlusal plane angle.
Slight Class II occlusion on the right side.
Severe TMJ pain. 
Severe headaches.
 Myofascial pain.
 Significantly decreased oropharyngeal of 4 mm (normal 11 mm.)
 Snoring.
 Sleep disorder secondary to pain. 
 Crowding in the upper arch.
 Crowding in the lower arch.

MELISSA WOOLFREY			05/22/2007				Page 3

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT: 
Medications.
a.  Klonopin 1 mg tablet q.h.s. to help control clenching and bruxism.
Presurgical orthodontics (can use bonded brackets on all the teeth.)
Maxillary arch: Place appliances on all the teeth including second molars.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally in case interdental osteotomies are required.  
Mandibular arch:  Place brackets on all the teeth including second molars and align and level the lower arch.  Slenderize the lower incisors removing about 1 mm of tooth structure. 
CT scans of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol.)
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses.
Maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla, downgraft it posteriorly to level the occlusal plane.
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen.)
Bilateral coronoidectomies.  
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
PAUL HISS										02/01/2008

Paul is 50 years old and an ophthalmologist.  He was referred here by Dr. Steve Nelson from Denver, Colorado.  He has had previous surgeries including bimaxillary osteotomies in November 2003, rhinoplasty in April 2004, and a revision rhinoplasty in February 2005.  He does have problems with cluster headaches that he treats prophylactically.  He takes Sansert 2 mg t.i.d.  Paul had orthognathic surgery in 2003 done by Dr. Guyuron.  Originally, the upper jaw was supposed to be advanced about 3 mm only, but the night before surgery Dr. Guyuron suggested that the maxilla be advanced further forward as well as the mandible.  Paul now feels that the maxilla is too far forward and is uncomfortable for him in talking, chewing and smiling.  He also has decreased incisor show.  He had his initial rhinoplasty done in 2004 in Las Vegas.  Surgeon apparently did a lot more to the nose than Paul had wanted.  He then had a revision by Dr. Dean Triniumi.  His office is in Chicago.  Dr. Triniumi took some rib cartilage and reconstructed the nose and put it in place a very prominent strut into the columella.   

Paul had orthodontic treatment in the 1970s with four first bicuspid extractions by Dr. Robert Ricketts in Los Angeles.  Paul’s profile was quite flattened with this treatment and was the reason that he sought treatment in 2003.  Since the 2003 orthognathic surgery, he has decreased sensation to his right lower lip, chin, teeth, and gums.  Initially after surgery, he had a total numbness, but some of it has returned.  Paul had orthodontic treatment again beginning in October 2002 and finishing in June 2004.  Paul rates his TMJ pain that occurs only on the left side at about a level of 3, average daily pain 1, jaw function at 4, diet at 3, disability at 1.  He gets headaches, but only on the right side.  They occur in the frontal and temporal area and feel as though they are behind the eye.  He has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, or vertigo.  Left elbow bothers him and he also has some lower back disk issues.  He is not aware of clenching or bruxism.  He does have a problem with cluster headaches that are fairly well controlled with the Sansert medication.  Paul does have some problems breathing through his nose and feels he probably has allergic rhinitis that causes swelling intranasally that causes more difficulties breathing the nose.  

CLINICAL EXAMINATION:  In frontal view, Paul has fairly good left-to-right facial asymmetry.  Upper lip length is about 25 mm.  Upper teeth lip relationship is about 3 mm.  dental midlines are on the facial midline.  In profile view, Paul appears to have a little fullness in the subnasale area.  The subnasale is anterior to upper lip superior labia.  Chin however appears a little bit retruded and the chin neckline is somewhat short.  Vertical facial dimension is reasonably good.  Intraorally, Paul is missing lower first bicuspids from his previous extractions.  Lower incisor has a little bit of lingual inclination.  He appears to have adequate gingiva.  The papilla are somewhat blunted interdentally.  In the upper arch, he has all his teeth back to the second molars except he is missing the first bicuspid as well.  He has basic Class I cuspid-molar relationship.  He has about 1.5 mm of vertical overbite.  Transversely, the occlusal plane is fairly level.  Maxillary mandible may be about a 1 mm off towards the left side.  Chin may be about 2 mm off to the right side.  The left side of the face is slightly more full than the right side.  Mandibular right cuspid is about 1 mm posterior to the left cuspid.  Oropharyngeal areas look relatively normal.  Uvula is mild to moderately elongated.  Tonsils are present, but relatively normal in size.  Turbinates appear moderately enlarged.  There appears to be a lot of inflammatory response intranasally.  Septum looks reasonably straight at least in the front.  Paul has no tenderness to palpation around the head and neck area.  On opening, the right joint functions smoothly, but the left side has an audible crepitation.  Incisor opening is 4-6 mm.  Excursion movements are 11 mm in each direction.  In side-to-side movements, there is come crepitus noises in the left joint as well.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all fourth and third molars and all four first bicuspids.  There is some significant root resorption on some of the teeth particularly the incisors, bicuspids, first molars in the upper arch, and in the lower arch the second molars and bicuspids as well. There is some bone loss around the teeth rather generalized.  He has root canal in maxillary left lateral incisor.  He has two bone screws remaining in the rings.  Condyles look relatively large and intact.  Sinuses look relatively clear.  Waters view shows that there appears to be some passable airway at least in the bony section of the septum.  The bony septum appears quite wide in width.  The turbinates appear moderate in size.  Sinuses look relatively clear.   Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has reasonably good morphology.  Joint space is thin.  Articular eminence has a relatively moderate inclination.  Left TMJ tomogram shows the condyles are very narrow anteroposteriorly and very pointed at the top.  Decreased superior and posterior joint space but increased anterior joint space.  Articular eminences are moderately steep.  There is good translation of both condyles.  Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that the incisors in both upper and lower arch are vertical in orientation.  Anterior nasal spine is almost anterior to the incisor tips.  There is a high occlusive plane angle and a prominence of pogonion.  Oropharyngeal airway is very good.  _____ in the upper tooth relationship is about zero.

The basic diagnoses for Paul would be as follows:

	1.  AP maxillary excess. 
	2.  Vertical maxillary deficiency particularly posterior.
	3.  AP mandibular deficiency.
4.  Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation without reduction and severe    deformation. 
5.  Vertical inclination of the maxillary incisors.
6.  Left TMJ pain.
7.  Headaches.
8.  Missing all four first bicuspids and all four third molars. 
9.  Periodontal problems with loss of papilla and generalized elevator bone loss.
10.  Short roots on a number of the teeth.
11.  High occlusive plane angle.
12.  Hypertrophied turbinates.
13.  Wide and deviated nasal septum.
14.  Nasal airway obstruction.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

	1.  Presurgical orthodontics.
a.  Place appliances on upper and lower arches and work up to a fairly stout arch wire.     No significant dental movements are required.
	2.  Surgery.
a.  Maxillary osteotomies to move the anterior nasal spine area posteriorly, the incisors forward, down graft the posterior aspect and decrease the occlusal plane angle.
b.  Left TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ concepts total joint prosthesis.
c.  Right mandibular ramus sagittal split osteotomy.
d.  Fat graft to left TMJ (harvest from the abdomen).
e.  Left coronoidotomy with fixation.
f.  Partial turbinectomies.
g.  Nasal septoplasty.
h.  Bone grafting to the maxillary osteotomy areas (harvest from the cranium).
	3.  Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

Paul wants to talk to Dr. Triniumi who did his second nasal reconstruction as he is concerned that Dr. Triniumi may try to discourage him from the surgery.  I told him I would be happy to talk to Dr. Triniumi.  He also would prefer autogenous bone grafts over synthetic bone grafts.  I told him we would have a choice between cranial bone grafts or harvesting bone from iliac crest.  He will think about it and let me know.  He will contact us if he has any other questions.  I reviewed the risks and complications.  He does have a significant deficit of the inferior alveolar nerve on the right side, and I do not feel this treatment plan will improve that situation for him.  He understands that and knows it could also get worse.

original_note:
KATE HOLLENBECK								04-08-03


Patient History

Kate’s problems apparently began when she was a young teenager. She fortunately has not had any significant pain problems with her condition but has undergone significant condylar resorption and retrusion of the mandible. She occludes now only on her second molars.

Kate has been under orthodontic treatment for some time, and apparently her occlusion is fairly well aligned to proceed on with surgery. 

Basic Diagnoses

Based on available records, which include the MRI, photographs, cephalometric tracing, as well as reports from other physicians, her diagnoses are as follows:

Bilateral TMJ idiopathic condylar resorption
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency
AP mandibular deficiency
Transverse maxillary deficiency
Anterior open bite
Occluding only on the second molars
Headaches 
Myofascial pain
Decreased oropharyngeal airway
Class II occlusion
High occlusal plane angulation

Recommended Treatment

Presurgical orthodontics
Continue to align and level maxillary arch
Continue to align and level mandibular arch

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ condylectomies, meniscectomies, debridement, and reconstruction with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to expand the maxilla, downgraft the posterior aspect, and level the occlusal plane
Bilateral mandibular coronoidectomies
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvested from abdomen)

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

This plan should get her the best possible functional and aesthetic result.

original_note:
ROBERT AARON RAND							07/14/2011

Aaron is 18 years old, birth date 08/05/1992.  He was referred by Dr. Farhad Moshiri, 1320-B East Kingsley, Springfield, MO 65804.

Aaron originally had orthodontics when he was about 12 to 13.  He thinks his bite was very good at the completion of treatment and the treatment was provided by Dr. Kirkpatrick in Tulsa, OK.  The family has since moved to Branson, MO.  At that time from about the age of 16 through 18, Aaron’s bite began to change and shift.  Aaron’s bite has opened anteriorly and the lower jaw has shifted backwards.  Current treatment includes a retainer type of device in the upper jaw now with TADs placed to try to intrude the posterior teeth.  

Aaron has no problems with headaches, TMJ pain, and no myofascial pain.  He rates his jaw function at 2, diet at 2, disability at 0.  He takes no medications.  He has no trouble with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo or hearing loss.  No other joints bother him.  He is allergic to sulfa drugs.  He is not aware of clenching or bruxing and he breathes primarily through his mouth at night when he sleeps.  However, he is not aware of having any problems with snoring, daytime somnolence, nor sleep apnea.

In frontal view, Aaron has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  The nose is deviated a little off towards the right side.  Upper lip length is about 22 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 4.5 mm.  The maxillary midline and mandibular dental midline and chin are all pretty much on the facial midline.  The nasal tip is off to the right side about 4 mm.  The maxilla is retruded probably 5 to 6 mm.  The chin is retruded about 25 to 28 mm.  The chin neckline is extremely short, although the chin neck angle is defined.  Intraorally, he has all of his lower teeth back through the second molars.  He has a 3 x 3 retainer in the lower arch.  The lower right bicuspids are tipped lingually.  In the upper arch, he has all of his teeth back through the second molars.  He has TADs inserted and a device to try to intrude the posterior teeth.  He has an open bite of probably about 5 mm.  Part of his bite has been increased because of the device that he has in place.  He has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship of probably 3 to 4 mm.  Aaron has been in the orthodontic device that covers his bicuspids and molar teeth with the springs attached to the TADs for over a year and the bite has yet to be corrected.  Oropharyngeal tissues look normal and healthy.  The uvula is somewhat thickened.  Intranasally, the nasal septum is deviated significantly off to the right side anteriorly and pretty much occludes the right nostril.  There is a curvature of the inferior part of the septum off to the left side.  The nasal airway is at least moderately obstructed.  The nostrils are narrow particularly on the right side.  To palpation, Aaron has no tenderness around the head and neck area.  On opening, he has a click in the right joint at probably about 40 mm of opening and about 30 mm of closing.  On the left side, he had some glitches on that side, but not a significant overt click.  The maximal incisal opening is 51 mm, excursion movement to the right 7 mm, to the left 7 mm.  He has no tenderness to palpation around the head and neck area.
RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the presence of impacted maxillary third molars and mandibular right third molar and the absence of a left third molar.  There is a 3 x 3 retainer in the mandibular arch.  He has the device fixed in place on the upper teeth extending from first bicuspid through second molar.  There are stabilizing transpalatal arch wires.  Although there are TADs in place, they are not readily seen on this particular radiograph.  The condyles appear small and arthritic.  

Coronal view shows that turbinates are enlarged.  The bony septum is reasonably straight.  There is a little increased sinus thickening in the floor of both left and right maxillary sinuses.  

Axial view shows that the sinuses are reasonably clear in this view.  The turbinates are moderately enlarged particularly on the left side.  Septal cartilage anteriorly is deviated very significantly off towards the right side and there is a little bone spur coming off the left side that appears to block a significant part of the airway.  

Left TMJ sagittal view shows that there has been significant condylar resorption that has occurred in this joint.  The condyle has lost vertical volume and is very narrow anteroposteriorly.  The articular eminence is moderately steep.  The joint space is actually fairly thick,  There may be a little bit of anterior beaking elements present.  The condyle is somewhat posteriorly positioned.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has small in size and has lost considerable volume, but not as significant as the left side.  There is significantly increased vertical joint space.  Some elements of anterior beaking present.  The articular eminence is moderately steep.

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has undergone significant resorption, but still has reasonably good mediolateral width.  The joint space may be somewhat increased vertically.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 mm.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has undergone significant resorption, but not as significant as the opposite side.  The vertical joint space is even a little bit greater on this side compared to the opposite side.  The condyle still has fairly good mediolateral width.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 mm.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Aaron has a retruded maxilla and severely retruded mandible.  He has a high occlusal plane angle.  He has an anterior open bite of about 5 mm.  He has a Class II skeletal and occlusal relationship.  He has a decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of about 5 mm at the base of the tongue and about 7.5 mm at the soft palate level.



Basic diagnoses for Aaron would be as follows:

Bilateral TMJ adolescent internal condylar resorption (AICR).
Maxillary AP and posterior vertical hypoplasia but anterior vertical hyperplasia.
AP mandibular hypoplasia.
Anterior open bite of about 5 mm.
Class II end-on occlusion.
High occlusal plane angle.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of 4 mm (normal AP dimension 11 mm).
Impacted maxillary third molars and mandibular right third molar.
Hypertrophied turbinates.
Severely deviated nasal septum.  
Nose deviated off towards the right side approximately 4 m.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:   

1.	MRI of left and right TMJs.
2.	Surgery Stage 1:  Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair 	with Mitek anchors (depending on MRI results).
3.	Presurgical orthodontics.
	a.	Remove current device.
	b.	Align and level maxillary arch.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and 			cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies in case they 		are needed.
	c.	Align and level mandibular arch.  Consider slenderizing the lower anterior 		teeth removing about 2 mm of tooth structure and retracting.
4.	Surgery Stage 2.
	a.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a 			counterclockwise direction advancing pogonion approximately 22 mm.
	b.	Maxillary osteotomies to intrude the anterior aspect, but down graft it 			posterior and correct any transverse problems that may be present.
	c.	Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies.
	d.	Nasal septoplasty.
	e.	Surgical removal of impacted third molars x3.
	f.	External rhinoplasty.
5.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion. 


Depending on MRI findings, I may recommend that the TMJ surgery be separated out and be done as soon as possible and then the orthodontics be done and the major orthognathic surgical procedures performed next summer.  This would help preserve the discs and improve the prognosis as compared to waiting and doing it all next summer.

original_note:
CLAIRE SMITH									01/26/2007

Claire is 24 years old and is referred by Dr. Keys Townsend from Colorado Springs.  Claire's problems began when she was about 14 years old of unknown etiology.  She developed what appeared to sound like a closed lock situation.  She was on vacation in Colorado and her orthodontist sent an air pillow kind of retainer for her to bite on and this seemed to help her and then she felt better for a while.  In approximately August of 2003, her situation got worse while she was in Costa Rica.  She was unable to open her mouth more than about 7 mm.  She received some treatment down there including some therapy and some muscle relaxants and by the time she came home she could open again fairly well.  

She had initial orthodontics when she was about 11 to 12 years and then had a second stage when she was from about 13 to 15 years old.  She had retainers at the end of that treatment and she found that she would grind through those retainers.  She got orthodontic appliances again placed in April of 2005.  She had physical therapy in the summer of 2006 with some help but did not help substantially.  She did have previous splint therapy beginning in January 2004 for about 15 months up until she started orthodontic treatment but this did not seem to help her at all.  Claire has no problems with earaches but does get occasional tinnitus and occasional vertigo but no hearing loss.  Other joints that bother her include her hips and right knee.  She had surgery on her right knee in June of 2003.  She has allergies to dust, molds, animals, pollens and grasses.  She is allergic to penicillin.  She does get allergy shots.  She has no known allergies to jewelry.  She is aware of clenching and bruxism.  

Claire gets headaches about once a week at about a level of 8 that occur sometimes beginning in the back of the head then coming forward through the temples into the frontal area or other times it will start in the front and migrate posteriorly.  These do occur about once a week and it may be stress initiated.  They occur more often in the late afternoon or evening.  She has a constant pain in her jaw joints at a level of 2 but will have episodes during the week that will escalate to 9 and then once that episode occurs, it lasts only for probably less than 10 minutes then her pain levels remain at about a 6.  When she gets her headaches and she takes ibuprofen, it seems to help.  Her average daily pain when she does not get these severe TMJ pains is at about a level of 1.  However, with the heading occurring or when she gets these jolts her average daily pain may be at an 8.  Claire rates her jaw function at 5, diet at 6 and disability at 4. 

PRIMARY CONCERNS: 
Headaches.
Jaw pain with the right side being worse than the left side. 
Neck and shoulder pain.
Difficulties eating and chewing.

In frontal view, Claire has reasonably good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 19 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 2.5 mm.  Maxillary, mandibular and chin midlines are all pretty much on the facial midline.  Nasal tip is deviated off to the right side about 2 to 3 mm.  In profile view she has just some slight AP maxillary deficiency of maybe 2 to 3 mm.  

CLAIRE SMITH				01/26/2007				Page 2

The chin appears that it may be retruded about 8 to 10 mm.  Chin-neckline is a little bit short but the chin-neck angle is reasonably well defined.  Intraorally, Claire has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars.  Teeth appear to be nicely aligned and leveled.  She has adequate attached gingiva around the lower anterior arch.  In the upper arch Claire has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars.  She has a Class II end-on occlusion of probably about 4 mm.  She has an overjet of about 4 mm.  She has about a 0 vertical overbite relationship.  Intraorally, the uvula, soft palate and faucial pillars all appear normal.  Intranasally, the septum is deviated a little bit off towards the left side.  Left turbinate is moderately enlarged but the right one appears relatively normal.  Right airway looks very good, left appears somewhat constricted.  No history of snoring, daytime somnolence nor sleep apnea.  Claire has had her wisdom teeth previously removed.  The only other surgery she had is on her right knee where she had arthroscopic surgery performed in the summer of 2003.  To palpation she has some very minimal tenderness over the temporalis muscles, right masseter and shoulders.  No tenderness directly over the TMJs.  On opening, she has crepitations in both joints with the right side being significantly worse than the left side.  Maximal incisal opening is 57 mm and excursive movements are about 8 mm in each direction.  Claire has no history of having rashes except with amoxicillin that she had a reaction to.  She was told to tell subsequent treating doctors that she is allergic to penicillin.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION:
Panorex shows the absence of all 4 third molars.  The rest of the teeth are present.  They have good bone levels around them.  Both condyles appear arthritic.  Sinuses appear relatively clear.  

Waters view shows that the nasal septum is deviated a little bit off to the left side and the left turbinate appears somewhat large.  The right side appears to have a more open airway.  Sinuses do appear clear.

Left TMJ tomogram shows increased joint space and a relatively flat articular eminence although it is long in length and it is possible that she may not have been in centric relation when this x-ray was taken.  There appears to be a little bit of sclerosis on the head of the condyle and there appears to be a little bit of anterior beaking present.  There appears to be slight increased posterior joint space.  

Right TMJ appears to be more taken in centric relation as the molars are a little bit more Class II on this side and she is equally Class II on both sides.  This x-ray demonstrates significant degenerative changes and arthritic changes in the condylar head and decreased joint space posteriorly and superiorly.  Articular eminence is relatively shallow and similar to the opposite side.  On opening, there is good translation of both condyles. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has a skeletal Class II jaw relationship and a Class II occlusion.  She has 0 overbite relationship.  She does have significantly decreased oropharyngeal airway with the airway being only about 4 mm from the soft palate to the posterior pharyngeal only about 3 mm from the base of the tongue to the posterior pharyngeal wall.  
CLAIRE SMITH				01/26/2007				Page 3

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
Bilateral TMJ idiopathic condylar resorption with displacement and severe degeneration of the articular discs
AP mandibular deficiency.
AP maxillary deficiency.
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency. 
Class II occlusion.
Anterior open bite tendency.
High occlusal plane angle. 
Decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of 3 mm (normal 11 mm.)
TMJ pain.
 Headaches.
 Masticatory dysfunction.  

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Presurgical orthodontics.
Continue to align and level maxillary arch.
Continue to align and level mandibular arch.
CT scans of TMJs and jaws.
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance and down graft it posteriorly to decrease the occlusal plane angle.
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen.)
Bilateral coronoidectomies.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

ADDENDUM:  Claire tends to posture her mandible forward in normal jaw relationship.  She has difficulties going back into a centric relation.  This will probably increase her pain levels if she did.  However, from her relaxed profile view, mandibular advancement would not be much more than another 4 to 5 mm.  She postures herself forward almost into a Class I position probably because of comfort and better fit of the bite relationship although it is not in a stable position.

original_note:
SHARON REID									8/25/05

Sharon is 59 years old.  She has had previous surgery that includes:

Correction of deviated nasal septum.
Thyroid surgery.
Gallbladder surgery.
Hysterectomy.
Basal cell surgery.
Previous jaw surgery.

She does have a low blood pressure that runs about 100/68.  Otherwise, she is in pretty good health.  She takes the following medications:

Synthroid 0.1 mg.
Gynodiol 0.1 mg
Lipitor 10 mg.

She does have seasonal allergies.  She is not aware of any food allergies.  She does not tolerate codeine well.  Sharon has had previous jaw surgery and it was performed by Dr. Doug Sinn in 1986.  The surgery that Sharon had at that time, they apparently took out 4 first bicuspid teeth.  She did have orthodontic appliances on when the surgery was done.  She wore the braces for about 2 years.  Apparently, the upper front teeth were moved backwards and on the lower jaw she at least had a chin procedure done.  Apparently, she had to go back and have some touch up work on her chin by Dr. Sinn and he may have reshaped the chin bone a little bit because it was too pointed and he put some hydroxyapatite in.  However, this impinged upon her mental nerves and caused her some significant pain and numbness.  The second surgical procedure by Dr. Sinn was probably done in 1987.  She then went and saw Dr. Bruce Epker who did some repeat surgery and apparently put some synthetic implants along the lateral side of the mandible on both sides.  Dr. Epker apparently performed the surgery sometime in the early to mid 90s.  He also removed some of the HA granules that Dr. Sinn had previously placed.  

PRIMARY CONCERNS
Residual numbness and discomfort on the left lower lip and chin area.
Her tongue feels too big for her mouth.
She constantly bites her tongue and it gets swollen.
Her jaw is sore and it is quite sore to the touch along the back part of the lower jaw line.
She wakes up unable to catch her breath (this seems to have developed since her surgery.)
Difficulty eating and chewing.
Difficulty breathing through her nose particularly on the left side (Dr. Sinn apparently corrected her deviated septum.)
Sleep disorder with difficulties sleeping.
Moderate to severe daytime tiredness.
 Difficulties swallowing.
 Feeling of choking at times.
 Extreme mouth dryness particularly in the morning when she wakes up.


SHARON REID				8/25/05				Page 2


Sharon has had extreme mouth dryness particularly in the morning when she wakes up.

Sharon has had longstanding TMJ problems but does not recall when they started nor the etiology.  She feels fairly confident that the joints problems were present well before she had all of her jaw surgeries and orthodontics performed.  Her joints were not addressed at all back at that time.  Sharon does have a history of hypoglycemia.  She believes that she sometimes clenches and grinds her teeth at night.  She gets occasional earaches that are a sharp piercing kid of pain.  Occasional tinnitus occurs but no vertigo or hearing loss.  She gets headaches about 2 times a month and they average about a level of 4, probably occurring more in the temple areas.  She rates her TMJ pain at 6, average daily pain at 8, jaw function and diet at 5, disability at 7.  She has some residual dysesthesia in the mandibular left chin area.

In frontal view, Sharon has reasonably good symmetry from the left to right side.  Upper lip length is about 28 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 1.  Nasal tip is deviated off to the left side probably about 3 mm.  Maxillary midline is shifted off to the right side probably 1 mm.  Mandibular dental midline is off to the right 1 to 2 mm.  The chin is off to the right side probably about 3 to 4 mm.  If Sharon shifts her lower jaw 2 mm to the left, it does improve her facial symmetry.  The fact that she shifts about 3 mm to the left, it does make a nice improvement in the facial symmetry.  In the profile view, Sharon has an elongated upper lip.  The maxilla is probably retruded by about 6 mm and chin is retruded by probably about 20 mm.  Chin-neck line is extremely short and chin-neck angle is not present.  Intraorally, Sharon is missing lower first bicuspids.  The lower left cuspids tip significantly lingually.  She appears to have adequate attached gingiva.  She may have had subapical osteotomies to move the anterior 6 teeth posteriorly as the cuspids both are tipped lingual to the labial aspect of the bicuspids.  In the upper arch, she had first bicuspids extracted as well.  She has an end-on Class II occlusal relationship being probably about 3 mm Class II.  She has about a 3 mm deep bite relationship.  She has a slight reverse curve of occlusion in the upper arch.  Oropharynx appears clear.  The uvula, soft palate and faucial pillars are all relatively normal.  Intranasally, the turbinates are fairly significantly enlarged with some significant constriction of the functional airway.  Sometimes she will get a sharp pain and sometimes it appears to be in her jaw and at other times in her throat.  When she gets a real sharp pain she has to manipulate her jaw to get rid of it.  To palpation, she has high moderate pain in the masseter muscles, medial pterygoids, right styloid area and milder tenderness over the temporalis TMJs and the left styloid area.  Incisal opening is 48 mm maximum but about 40 without significant discomfort.  Excursive movement to the right 9 mm, to the left 10 mm.  Transversely, the occlusal plane is canted with the right side being 2 mm higher than the left side.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all 4 third molars and all 4 first bicuspids.  She has root canals on the maxillary left lateral incisor and cuspid and mandibular left cuspid.  Cannot see the joints and cannot evaluate the sinuses.  
SHARON REID				8/25/05				Page 3


Waters view shows that the sinuses appear relatively clear.  Significant deviation in the nasal septum.  Turbinates actually do not appear to be too large.  The sinuses appear relatively equal but there appears to be some thickening of the lining particularly on the lateral aspect of the sinus with the right side being a little bit greater than the left side. 

Tomogram quality is not too great but there appears to be arthritic changes in the TMJ with decreased superior and posterior joint space.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  

Left TMJ tomogram shows arthritis in the TMJ with decreased superior and posterior joint spaces.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  On opening, there is restricted translation.  The condyle has come down towards the posterior aspect of the articular eminence.  The condyles appear quite flattened and appear to have lost vertical dimension.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Sharon has significant retrusion of the maxilla and severe retrusion of the mandible.  She has a high occlusal plane angle.  She has about a 2 to 3 mm overjet relationship.  Oropharyngeal airway is significantly constricted with an airway dimension of about 4 mm at the base of the tongue and 5 mm at the soft palatal level.  The tongue fills the majority of the oral cavity but there is some space remaining between the palate and the tongue.

BASIC DIAGNOSES 
Bilateral TMJ severe arthritis. 
Severe AP maxillary hypoplasia. 
Severe posterior vertical maxillary hypoplasia.
Severe AP mandibular hypoplasia.
Severely decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Sleep apnea. 
TMJ pain.
Headaches.
Myofascial pain.
 Sleep disorder.
 Class II end-on occlusion.
 High occlusal plane angle of 29 degrees (normal 8 degrees.)
 Previously placed bilateral mandibular body and ramus alloplastic implants. 
 Severe lingual inclination of the maxillary incisors.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch.  
Align and level mandibular arch.

CAT scan of TMJs and jaws (TMJ concepts protocol.)
SHARON REID				8/25/05				Page 4


Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses.  
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla in a counterclockwise direction.  
Bilateral coronoidectomties.
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen.)
Remove previously placed alloplastic implants to mandible and stabilizing screws.
Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy to reduce chin prominence.
      4.  Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B486 
B486 is 37 years old, referred by Dr. XX. B486 has had a previous tonsillectomy and cholecystectomy. When B486  was three years old she was bitten by a dog around the face area and had fairly extensive injuries. She was not sure if this was contributory to her current situation.

PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Pain and inability to open the jaws.
Bite is off.
Lower jaw seems to be becoming more recessive.
Significant difficulties breathing through her nose.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS:
Vicodin ES.
Lodine.
Flexeril.
Allegra-D.
Relpax as required for severe headaches. Afrin. Ambien (she uses it only when she is traveling.)

PREVIOUS TREATMENT FOR HER JAWS AND TMJs: 20 years ago, orthodontic appliances were removed. 4 years ago, she received a bite plate. Last year she had arthrocentesis on the right side.

By history, B486's TMJ problems began prior to 20 years ago. She remembers having clicking and popping in her joints when she had her orthodontic appliances on and then it went away but the when the appliances were removed the clicking and popping began again. Her problems got worse about four years ago when the left side started to have clicking and pain associated with it. She was placed into a bite splint and the left side got better. However, last year during a job training session that was fairly stressful, she developed rather severe pain in the right TMJ and associated areas. She had a splint made but it was not effective and it may have been making it worse. Dr. XX at the XX worked with her bite splint. He eventually took it away from her as it was making her situation worse. Last year, Dr. X performed a right TMJ arthrocentesis and this helped for about two weeks and then her pain problems actually became worse. The splint was infected. Since the more recent onset of her TMJ problems during this past year, B486 feels that her bite has significantly shifted. B486 currently gets earaches on the right side at about a level of 8. She has tinnitus on the right side and she has developed fairly significant vertigo that has even required ER visits. She has no hearing loss. The only joints that she has problems include a herniated disc at L3 and L4. She does have seasonal allergies that are fairly significant. She is unaware of any food allergies and is unaware of any problems wearing jewelry.

 
She is very much aware of significant problems with the clenching at nighttime and also during the day. She rates her headaches at a level of 8 and they occur throughout the head area, though initiate at the right TMJ and temple area but involve the entire head. Headaches occur daily. The Relpax is able to interrupt the cycle. Otherwise, B486 believes that her headaches would be continuous. She rates her current TMJ pain at a level of 10, average daily facial pain and headache level at 8, jaw function at 9, diet at 10, and disability at 9. She is essentially on a liquid diet at this time and unable to chew.

In frontal view, B486 has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. The right side of the lower jaw may be just slightly longer vertically by may be a couple of mm compared to the left side. Upper lip length is 23 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is 4.5 mm. Maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline.

In profile view, upper lip supralabrale is probably about 3 to 4 mm posterior to subnasale. The maxilla is retruded by probably 5 mm or so. Chin is retruded by probably 12 to 15 mm. Intraorally, B486 is missing maxillary mandibular first bicuspids. She has the rest of her teeth back through the second molars. She has some moderate crowding in the lower anterior arch. There is good attached gingiva except around the left central incisor. She has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship being about 2 mm Class II on the left and probably 4 on the right. B486 does not feel her left teeth touching at this time and this is a change that she has noted in her bite. There is an anterior deep bite of about 3 to 4 mm. She has an overjet of about 2 mm. Oropharyngeal areas look healthy. Uvula is mildly enlarged. The soft palate and faucial pillars appear relatively normal. B486 does report difficulties breathing through her nose. She has to use a lot of Afrin and feels as though she is almost addicted to it and would like to decrease usage of it. However, without the Afrin she says that she cannot breathe at all through her nose. Intranasally, she has moderate to severe enlargement of the turbinates with the left side being larger than the right side. The septum is deviated off to the right side posteriorly. She has moderate to severe nasal airway obstruction. She has a wide columella and very narrow nostrils. Holding the nostrils open with a nasal scope did improve her airway. Luminal valves appear relatively normal. She is not aware of snoring but she does have daytime somnolence of a moderate degree but she is unaware of any sleep apnea symptoms. She does have a sleep disorder and wakes up frequently caused currently by the pain factors. She has not slept well for the past six years because of pain factors and airway obstruction problems. Maximal incisal opening is 30 mm but only about 15 mm without severe pain. Excursive movement to the right 3 mm, to the left 2 mm. On opening, she has crepitation in the right TMJ and on the left side she has a vague click of at about 25 mm of opening and about 20 mm of closing but it may be unrelated to the disc position since on the MRI is nonreducing.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
:
Panorex shows the absence of all four first bicuspids and all four third molars. She has crowns on all four first molars and on both mandibular second molars. She has root canals on the maxillary first molars, right central incisor, mandibular second molars and left first molar.
 
Sinuses appear relatively clear. Turbinates appear enlarged particularly on the left side. She appears to have arthritis in both jaw joints.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears arthritic. It is flattened on top and has a little bit of anterior beaking present. Decreased superior joint space. Bone in the fossa looks a little bit funny. Articular eminence is moderately steep and somewhat elongated.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears sclerotic and appears to have an osteophyte sticking up off the top of it. Decreased superior and posterior joint space. Articular eminence is moderately steep and appears to be deformed as well with a posterior slope at the tip of the articular eminence. On opening, there is restricted translation in both joints but with greater restriction in the right side. In the open view, the right side appears much more arthritic than the left joint.

Waters view shows that the sinuses are clear. The bony septum is reasonably straight so that the deviation seen in the septum is apparently in the cartilage. Turbinates appear enlarged and there does not appear to be much of a nasal airway. Transverse width of the nose appears somewhat constricted.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that B486 has an AP deficiency of the maxilla and AP deficiency of the mandible. Oropharyngeal airway although a little bit decreased appears adequate. She does have an obtuse nasal labial angle and upper lip supralabrale is posterior to subnasale by probably about 4 mm.

B486 had a bird that was a Nandaya Conure. She had Noah for five years. About six years ago she gave Noah away because it was attacking her husband and they were having a baby on the way. It was given to a veterinarian receptionist and the bird died about two years ago of unknown causes. Of significant interest is that her allergy problems began after she got the bird. Prior to that she really had no allergy symptoms. After she got the bird she went through three years of allergy shots. She also has four dogs and three birds. The bird would actually sleep with her at night. She raised him from when he was an age. He lived for about nine years. Life expectancy for that kind of bird apparently is only about 12 years. She apparently has developed some GI problems of unknown etiology to date.

OTHER SYSTEMS: B486 does have back problems with L3-4, but apparently at least currently no other joint problems. She had significant problems with urinary tract infection when she was pregnancy six years ago. She has nocturia x1. She has a history of an ovarian cyst and fibrocystic breast disease. She has one child age six years. She has had no miscarriages and no fertility problems. She has had a vaginal discharge problem for the last six months and has been treated with Diflucan and Flagyl but unsuccessfully. Her physician suspects a bacterial infection.

She has had pink eye x5 in the past and she gets frequent conjunctivitis particularly in the springtime. She has burning, itching and dryness of her eyes. She has eye allergies. She does wear contacts. She has chronic sinus problems. Smoked for about two years about ten years ago but quit. She smoked about ten cigarettes per day at that time. She does have problems with earaches, tinnitus, and vertigo. She gets frequent colds and viruses. She gets recurrent sore throats, hoarseness and laryngitis. She has had bronchitis x20 and pneumonia x1 which occurred last year. She had some bouts of bronchitis when she was younger but the frequency increased after she had the bird.

She had her gallbladder removed 7 years ago when she was six months pregnant. She has consistent problems with constipation and does have pain and cramping associated with it. She is not aware of having any STDs. She does have a lot of fatigue problems and she has some memory affects but she believes that it could be related to the medication she is currently on. She has been exposed to her parrot for four years beginning at the age of 24. She has three cats currently and they do eat birds. She has four dogs that do not eat birds. She has had no exposure to hoofed animals and she has no implants. Her husband had problems with knee and shoulder. She does have a history of having a high ANA titer but this was a few years ago.

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
Bilateral TMJ arthritis being worse on the right side as compared to the left side. Bilateral TMJ articular disc dislocations with severe degenerative changes and nonreducing.
Probably Chlamydia psittaci infection right TMJ.
AP maxillary deficiency.
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency. AP mandibular deficiency. Moderate high occlusal plane angle. Class II end-on occlusion being worse on the right side as compared to the left side. Slight open bite on the posterior left side. Hypertrophied turbinates. Narrowed nostrils.
Wide columella. Nasal airway obstruction.
Severe TMJ pain.
Severe headaches.
Myofascial pain. Earaches.
Tinnitus.
Vertigo.
 
RECOMMENDED TREATMENT: Periodontal evaluation of lower anterior arch, particularly the left central incisor area. Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch.
Align and level mandibular arch. Consider slenderizing the lower anterior teeth removing about 2 mm of tooth structure to keep from flaring them any further forward.
CT scan (TMJ Concepts protocol.)
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla and down graft it posteriorly. Bilateral coronoidectomies.
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen.)
Partial turbinectomies.
Reconstruct columella.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

MRI Evaluation
Age: 35/F
Date of Birth:  
Date of MRI:  
Date of Interpretation:  
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width. The lateral pole may extend slightly beyond the lateral rim of the fossa. There may be a little discal tissue towards the medial side and then discal tissue anterior to the condyle. Cortical bone appears to be intact. In one area, the bone structures are fairly close indicative of perhaps a possible perforation. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 mm.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has reasonably good mediolateral width. The condyle appears a little bit darker indicative of decreased flow and more sclerotic bone. Decreased superior joint space. A little osteophyte sticking off the lateral pole of the condyle. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 to 3 mm. Discal tissue appears to be displaced anteriorly. Cortical bone appears to be intact for the most part.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has some arthritic changes with flattening on the anterior head particularly at the lateral aspect of the joint. Articular eminence is moderately steep. There appears to be a little bony defect in the articular eminence area. The bone appears a little bit rough on the anterior aspect of the fossa and articular eminence. Articular eminence is fairly steep. There appears to be an anterior osteophyte present. Disc is anteriorly displaced and sitting beneath the articular eminence. A little inflammatory process is seen in the bilaminar tissues. On opening, the disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction. There is some restriction in translation. Articular disc is short anteroposteriorly.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that demonstrates significant osteoarthritic changes with anterior beaking with a rather sharp point as well as an osteophyte. There appears to be some degenerative changes up in the fossa. The articular eminence is quite steep and actually has a slight backward curvature at the apex. Decreased superior joint space and posterior joint space. The condyle appears more sclerotic than the opposite side. A little bit of fluid in the superior joint space. At the lateral aspect of the joint there is a large fluid collection extending almost lateral to the fossa area. On opening, there is restricted translation of the condyle and the articular disc is anteriorly displaced, nonreducing. The articular disc itself is obliterated. It actually appears that there may be a pannus.

IMPRESSION:
Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with significant deformation and nonreducing. Right TMJ severe arthritis affecting the condyle as well as the fossa and articular disc dislocation without reduction and significant degenerative changes.
Questionable salvageability of the articular discs particularly on the right side.

original_note:
LOIS WATKINS									12-10-04

Lois is 62 years old and referred by Dr. Robert Talley, 448 36th NW, #103, Norman, OK  73072.

Patient History

Lois has had previous cervical fusion in 1993 then had a repeat surgery in 2000 on her neck. She had a tonsillectomy in 2001 and a hysterectomy in 1986. For the most part, she is in good health except for her jaw problems. She takes the following:

Zocor tablets 40 mg q.d.
Clonazepam 0.5 mg h.s.
Amitriptyline 25 mg for pain and for sleep
Benadryl as needed for allergies

She gets occasional earaches, tinnitus, and more frequently has problems with vertigo. Other joints that bother her include her left shoulder, wrists, hands, fingers, and occasionally her hips. She is allergic to CODEINE AND MORPHINE, and she is allergic to a lot of chemicals, particularly those used in cleaning, etc. She has no known allergies to jewelry. She is aware of clenching and bruxing at night. 

Her TMJ problems began about 36 years ago of unknown etiology. She has had two previous neck surgeries to try to eliminate the neck pain problems. About one year ago she got some bite splints or devices, but these have not helped her significantly. She went to see Dr. Talley, and he referred her down here without doing any initial treatment. Lois gets daily headaches that occur in the frontal and temporal area that average about a level of 5. She has constant pain through the head, jaw, and neck area at a level of 9. TMJ pain is at 9, jaw function at 8, diet and disability at 6. 

There are times when Lois just will say that she hurts all over, and this means she has achiness throughout her entire body. When Lois had her tonsils removed, she said that the doctor told her that there was a bone spur growing out from her neck into her tonsil on the left side, and he had to chisel that bone spur out of there. 

Patient Concerns

Headaches
TMJ pain
Tight painful muscles through the jaw area
Muscles in the throat area get extremely tight particularly in the mornings when she gets up

Clinical Examination

Lois has fairly good facial symmetry and balance. Upper lip length is about 22 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is 0. Maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline is about 2 mm to the left. Chin is probably 4 to 5 mm to the left. 

In profile view, Lois has fairly good facial balance vertically and anteroposteriorly. Intraorally, she is missing the mandibular right second and third molar and mandibular left second and third molar. The rest of the teeth are present. There is a crown on the mandibular left second bicuspid. Some mild crowding, lower anterior arch. A lot of attrition on the teeth. Good attached gingiva present.

In the upper arch, she is missing her maxillary left and right second and third molars but has the rest of her teeth. Has a Class II end-on occlusion on the right side and a Class II end-on occlusion on the left side, but it is worse on the right side than the left side. She has an anterior deep-bite of about 3 mm. 

Intranasally, the turbinates are mildly enlarged but not too significant. It appears that she has a fairly good airway. Her snoring is described as moderate. She probably has some moderate daytime somnolence. She is not aware of any other sleep apnea symptoms at this time. 

To palpation, she has high moderate pain over the TMJs, styloid areas, moderate pain over the SCMs, and milder pain over the posterior neck, shoulders, masseters, and left temporalis muscle. Maximal incisal opening is about 45 mm but only about 33 mm without significant pain or discomfort. Excursive movement to the right is about 4 mm and to the left 3 mm.

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows the absence of all second and third molars. She has crowns on the maxillary four incisors, mandibular right first molar, and mandibular left second bicuspid. Root canal in the maxillary right lateral incisor and perhaps on the right central but difficult to tell. Sinuses look relatively clear. Turbinates look moderate in size. Vertical dimension of the right mandibular ramus appears larger than the left ramus. Some flattening on the anterior head of the condyle on the right side. Appears to be some erosive changes in the left condyle with loss of vertical height. 

Waters view shows that the sinuses are clear. There is a transverse cant in the occlusal plane. The mandible is shifted off to the left side probably 4 to 5 mm. Vertical height of the mandible on the left side is shorter than the right side. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has a little bit of flattening on the superior head in one of the views. Good space appears to be present. Articular eminence is moderately steep and fairly long in length. 

Left TMJ shows a joint that appears arthritic with flattening on the anterior head and some of what appears to be erosion in the midportion of the condylar head. Articular eminence is flatter than the opposite side and appears to have undergone some significant bone changes. 

On opening, there is good translation of the right TMJ where the condyle comes anterior to the fossa. In the left joint, the mandible comes forward underneath the articular eminence. It appears that there are some significant degenerative changes present in the condylar head. 

Cephalometrically, on the lateral ceph there is a fairly good Class I skeletal relationship. Significant difference in the vertical  height of the mandible at the occlusal plane level as well as at the inferior border with a vertical difference of 10 mm at the angle of the mandible with the left side being shorter vertically than the right side. Slight tip in the occlusal plane angle is present as well. There is a transverse cant in the occlusal plane, and the left side may be about 2 mm higher than the right side. Angle of the mandible is visibly lower on the right side as compared to the left side by probably at least 6 to 8 mm.

Basic Diagnoses

Left TMJ severe arthritis and articular disc dislocation with severe deformation and perforations
TMJ pain
Headaches
Myofascial pain
Masticatory dysfunction
Vertigo

Recommended Treatment

CT scan of TMJs and jaws (TMJ Concepts protocol)
Surgery
Left mandibular condylectomy, meniscectomy, debridement, and reconstruction with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses
Left TMJ fat graft (harvested from the abdomen)
Application of arch bars and removal of arch bars

original_note:
CODY NELSON									04/19/2010

Cody is 14 years 9 months of age.  Referred by Dr. Danny Dugan, Hurst, Texas.  Cody is in fairly good health.  He does have exercise induced asthma, but really has not had much difficulties with that recently.  His primary concerns are as follows:

Jaw growth.
Crookedness in the teeth in the lower jaw in a horizontal direction. 
Difficulties breathing through the nose at night.

Cody takes the following medications:

Allegra.
Singulair.

Cody reports no TMJ issues.  He has no TMJ pain or myofascial pain.  He gets headaches about one time a month in the temporal areas at about a level of 6.  He rates his jaw function, diet, and disability at 0.  He has no trouble with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo nor hearing loss and has no other joint involvement.  He is aware of clenching at night, although his parents say when they go into his room he is breathing through his mouth, snoring, and with his jaws open.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Cody has fairly good left-to-right symmetry except the nose is tipped off towards the left side.  Upper lip length is about 20 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 3 mm on the left and 4 mm on the right.  The nasal tip is probably about 4 to 5 mm off towards the left side.  The maxillary midline is about 1 mm off to the right side.  The mandibular dental midline is pretty close to being on the facial midline as is the chin.  In profile view, Cody has some maxillary AP deficiency of probably about 5 to 6 mm.  The chin is retruded about 15 mm.  Intraorally, Cody has all of his teeth in the upper and lower arch back through the first molars.  The second and third molars have been previously extracted.  He has a 3 x 3 retainer in the lower anterior arch.  He has good attached gingiva.  He has just a slight Class II cuspid relationship being about 1 to 2 mm Class II.  He has an anterior deep bite of about 3 mm.  He has a got 1-2 mm overjet.  He has a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the left side being 2 mm higher than the right side.  He has had a gum graft performed on the lower anterior arch.  Oropharyngeal tissues look healthy with the tonsils mild to moderately enlarged.  Intranasally, the septum anteriorly is displaced off towards the left side and almost lateral to the columella.  The turbinates appear moderately enlarged.  He has a wide columella and narrow nostrils.  Nasal airway obstruction is probably mild to moderate.  Cody has no history of TMJ issues.  He has been in orthodontic treatment since the age of 7 on and off and is currently in maxillary appliances.  To palpation, he has some very minimal tenderness over the temporalis muscles, but no where else around the head and neck area. Maximal incisal opening is 55 mm.  The excursion movements are about 9 mm in each direction.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.    Panorex shows the absence of the mandibular second and third molars.  He has orthodontic appliances on from first bicuspid through first bicuspid in the upper arch and a 3 x 3 retainer in the lower arch.  Turbinates appear moderately enlarged.  Sinuses appear relatively clear.  It is difficult to evaluate the joints in that view.

Coronal view shows that the sinuses are clear.  Turbinates are moderate in size.  

Axial view shows that the nasal septum is deviated off towards the left side anteriorly.  The left turbinate is quite large compared to the right inferior turbinate.  

Left TMJ sagittal tomograms show a condyle that appears a little small in size.  There may be a little bit of anterior beaking in a couple of views.  The joint space appears fairly well balanced.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has a little bit better morphological shape to it.  The joint space looks reasonable.  The articular eminences in both joints have a moderate inclination.

Left TMJ coronal view shows significant downward curvature of the lateral rim of the fossa.  There appears to be decreased lateral joint space.  

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good width to it.  The lateral rim curves inferiorly about 3-4 mm. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Cody has a retruded maxilla and mandible.  He has some adenoid tissue off the nasopharynx.  Airway space from the soft palate to the posterior pharyngeal wall is about 5 mm, and, from the base of the tongue, the posterior pharyngeal wall is 10 mm.  

Basic diagnoses would be as follows:

Maxillary AP and posterior vertical hypoplasia.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia.
High occlusal plane angle.
Slight Class II occlusion by 2 mm.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Hypertrophied left nasal turbinate.
Deviated nasal septum.
External nasal deformity. 

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Presurgical orthodontics.
	a.	Align and level maxillary arch. 
	b.	Align and level mandibular arch. 
2.	MRI of left and right TMJs to rule out TMJ pathology.
3.	Surgery (recommend performing at the age of 17).
	a.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla forward, down graft 			posteriorly, and level the occlusal plan anteroposteriorly as well as transversely.
	b.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a 				counterclockwise direction approximately 11 mm. 
	c.	Augmentation genioplasty of 5 mm.
	d.	Left turbinectomy.
	e.	Nasal septoplasty. 
	f.	External rhinoplasty.
	g.	Consider TMJ surgery if MRI confirms joint pathology.
4.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B446 is 27 years old, referred by Dr. XXXX. B446's jaw problems began back in probably her mid-teens, but was not initially identified that she was developing facial asymmetry until she was about 18 years old. When she was 16, she had orthodontics and had apparently a fairly good bite at that time. She did have some teeth extracted, but apparently a good bite. Therefore, the onset of the facial asymmetry began when probably sometime around the age of maybe 15 or 16. She began getting her headaches and noises in her joints when she was 18. She had orthodontics 2 years ago. She had splints from that time and now again she has a splint with Dr. XXXXX that began about 8 months ago. She has had a number of adjustments on her new splint. B446 feels that her jaw problem is getting slowly and progressively more out of alignment. She takes no medications except for discomfort she may take aspirin, acetaminophen, Tylenol, etc.

She rates her TMJ pain at a level of 8, average daily pain at 8, headaches at a level of 8 in her frontal, temporal, and posterior head area. She rates her jaw function at a 4, diet at 5, disability at 3. She gets occasional earaches, tinnitus, and vertigo. No other joints bother her except her knees if she runs a lot. She is aware of nocturnal clenching and bruxism.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
In frontal view, B446 has fair facial symmetry in the middle and upper third face. She has significant asymmetry in the lower third face. Upper lip length is about 22 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is about 2 mm. Maxillary midline is probably fairly close to being on the facial midline. The nasal tip may be just slightly off to the right side about 1 or 2 mm. Mandibular dental midline is off to the right side at least 4 mm. Chin is off to the right side about 6 to 7 mm. In profile view, she has fairly good balance with the upper lips on the cheeks and nose. Chin has fairly good AP balance, but the lower lip appears protrusive. Chin neckline may be slightly elongated. Chin might be just slightly forward. Intraorally, she is missing the mandibular left first molar, the mandibular right probably first and second molar and third molar and lingual and mesial inclination. She has space between the lower anterior teeth. The lower anterior teeth appear to be in a little bit of a lingual inclination. The right lateral incisors and bicuspids appear to be slightly elevated compared to the right of the teeth. She appears to have adequate attached gingiva. In the upper arch, she is missing a right first molar. She seems to have all of her teeth on the left side back to the second molars. She has a left-sided almost total buccal crossbite and a right-sided crossbite. Maxillary right central incisor occludes on the mandibular left central incisor. She has open bite on the right side in the bicuspid area. She has a slight Class II cuspid relationship by about 2 mm on the right side and on the left side she is probably closer to a Class I cuspid relationship. To palpation, she has moderate tenderness over the temporalis, masseter, styloid, and right TMJ area. She has moderate to severe tenderness over the SCMs, posterior neck, shoulders, left TMJ, left medial pterygoid area. When she opens, the right joint functions reasonably smooth, but the left side has an opening click at probably about 15 to 20 mm and closing palpable click at 15 mm. When she bites together, she occludes only on her right maxillary central and mandibular left central incisor. Maximal size opening is 53 mm. Excursion movement to the right is 4 mm, to the left 8 mm. Oropharyngeal tissues appear relatively normal. Intranasally, the septum is deviated towards the left side, but turbinates appear relatively normal in size so there appears to be an adequate airway in both sides. She does have a history of snoring at times and it has been increasing lately particularly when she is very tired and it usually occurs in the second half of the night as she has difficulties falling asleep. She is not aware of any daytime somnolence, nor any sleep apnea problems.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows elongated condylar heads and necks being greater on the left side than right side. Missing mandibular left first molar, third molar, mandibular right first and second molar, the third molar drifted forward and mesial inclination. There is space between the lower anterior teeth. In the upper arch, she is missing the right first molar with the second molar drifted forward and missing both third molars. Sinuses look relatively clear.

Waters view shows that sinuses appear clear. Nasal septum appears broad. Turbinates appears moderate in size. Airway appears somewhat constricted. Asymmetry of the mandible is noted.

Right TMJ tomogram is a little bit hard to read. There is some decreased joint space.
Left TMJ tomogram shows elongated head and neck of the condyle. The condyle appears a little bit over enlarged vertically. On opening views, there is fairly good translation of both condyles.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows prognathic mandible. Oropharyngeal airway looks relatively normal. Bolton's analysis reveals no significant tooth size discrepancy, but the mediolateral width of the maxillary anterior teeth would indicate that there is likely a tooth size problem.

Basic diagnoses would be as follows:
Left mandibular condylar osteochondroma. Left TMJ lateral and anterior dislocated disc. Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocated anteriorly and non-reducing.
Deviant mandibular prognathism with the mandible shifted about 6 to 7 mm off to the right side. Occluding only on the maxillary right central incisor and mandibular left central incisor.
Severe TMJ pain.
Headaches.
Myofascial pain.
Missing mandibular left first molar, right first and second molars, and missing maxillary right first molar and bilateral third molars.
Class I occlusion on the left side, Class II cuspid relationship on the right side.
Transverse cant on the maxilla with the left side being approximately 2 mm lower than the right side.
Maxillary right cuspid being about 1 to 2 mm posterior to the left cuspid.
Spacing between the lower anterior teeth.
Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of around 2 mm.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1.	Presurgical orthodontics.
a. Align and level maxillary arch. Open space (the size of a bicuspid) in the maxillary right first molar area by shifting the cuspids and bicuspids forward 1 mm and tipping the molar posteriorly.
b.	Align and level mandibular arch. Try to shift the bicuspids and cuspids forward slightly to close the anterior spacing and try to minimize lingual retraction of the incisors.
2.	Surgery.
Left mandibular condylectomy to remove the tumor (osteochondroma).
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to shift the mandible back onto the facial midline. d.	Consider possible maxillary osteotomies to level and correct transverse discrepancies.
3.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
Dental implants in the maxillary right first molar area and bilaterally to the mandibular first molar areas. Restorative dentistry to place crowns on the implants.

B446  MRI EVALUATION
AGE: 27/F
DOB:
Date of Acquisition:
Date of Interpretation:
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has a very unusual shape to it. Medial pole of the condyle is significantly higher than the lateral pole by probably close to about 7 to 8 mm. Lateral pole of the condyle extends lateral to the lateral rim of the fossa. Lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly probably about 3 to 4 mm. It is difficult to identify discal tissue. Condyle appears larger than normal and takes on the appearance of perhaps an osteochondroma.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is much smaller in size. Medial pole is still higher than the lateral pole, but not to the extent as the left side. It appears there may be some discal tissue anterior and perhaps medial. Lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 to 4 mm. The lateral aspect of the condyle extends out even with, or just slightly beyond, the lateral rim of the fossa. The joint space is decreased vertically. The disc is displaced out laterally and anterior to the condylar head.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle head and neck that is extremely long in length. Towards the lateral aspect of the condyle, the condyle seems to extend out lateral to the lateral rim of the fossa. Joint space vertically and posteriorly appears narrow. Condyle has a little bit better shaped medially than it does laterally. It is difficult to identify discal tissue in the closed view. On opening, however, the disc does come into view and appears to be in relatively normal position on opening. Articular eminence is relatively shallow.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that appears significantly smaller in size than the opposite side, although it still is fairly long in vertical length. Articular eminence is relatively shallow. Articular disc appears to be anteriorly displaced and appears significantly deformed. There is discal tissue on lateral, although it appears quite thickened vertically. No particular inflammation is seen around the joint. A little bit of anterior beaking is present towards the lateral aspect of the joint. On opening, there is good translation of the condyle, but the disc appears to be significantly deformed and remains anteriorly displaced, although translation of the condyle is fairly good.

IMPRESSION:
Left TMJ large deformed condyle and articular disc laterally and anteriorly displaced. Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly displaced with questionable reduction.
