original_note:
B348 

B348 is 16 years old and referred by Dr. __.
Patient Concerns
Pain in the jaws
Headaches Difficulties eating and chewing some foods
Lower jaw is becoming retruded
Teeth don't fit together properly
Predominantly a mouth breather

Patient History
B348's jaw joint problems began to the best of her knowledge about three years ago when she became aware of clicking and popping in her joints. The etiology is unknown. However, her mother does have TMJ problems also. She has had no treatment to date. She does take Motrin 1 tablet t.i.d., and she has been on this for about a week for costochondritis. She is doing much better in reference to that. She has no trouble with earaches, gets some tinnitus particularly when she goes to concerts, and she gets occasional vertigo. Other joints that bother her include her lower back and her hip joints. She says her mother tells her that she does grind her teeth at night.

She gets headaches about two times per week, and they occur predominantly in the posterior head area at about a level of 4. She rates her usual daily TMJ pain at about 5, but it can escalate up to 8. On an average day, her overall pain levels are about at a level of 3. She rates her jaw function at 6 and diet and disability at 4.

Clinical Examination
In frontal view, B348 has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 21 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 2 mm. Maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline is shifted about 2 mm to the left. Mandibular dental midline and chin midline are fairly well-aligned with each other.

In profile view, she has some slight AP maxillary deficiency of probably 3 to 4 mm. Chin is probably 15 mm or so AP deficient. Lower lip is protrusive compared to the chin. Chin-neckline is short, although the chin-neck angle is moderately well-defined.

Intraorally, B348 has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars present. She has some mild crowding, lower anterior arch. Has some thin attached gingiva around the lower incisors. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. She has a slight Class II cuspid relationship on the right side of probably about 2 mm and on the left side she is probably closer to 4 mm Class II cuspid. She has a slight accentuated curve of occlusion in the upper arch. She has an anterior open bite of about 2 to 3 mm. She has an overjet of about 3 mm. Transversely, the occlusal plane is relatively parallel to the papillary plane as well as to the ears. Oropharynx appears clear. Uvula is moderate size.

Nasal septum is deviated off toward the right side. She has a spur at the base of the septum on the right side. The right turbinate is moderately enlarged, and there appears to be some decreased airway on that side. On the left side, the turbinates are moderately enlarged, but the airway appears much better since the septum is deviated off toward the right side.

In her centric relation, she does have a tendency toward a crossbite on the left side.
To palpation, she has some mild tenderness over the left masseter, left TMJ, and left styloid area. Maximal incisal opening is about 40 mm, but she could open comfortably to about 18 mm. Excursive movement to the right is about 7 mm and to the left about 5 mm from her centric relation. In centric occlusion, it would be about 6 mm in each direction.

Radiographic Evaluation
Panorex shows the presence of four bony impacted third molars, and she has a supernumerary tooth between the left second molar and third molar. The rest of all the other teeth are present. Could be a mucous retention cyst possibly in the right maxillary sinus. Left sinus looks pretty clear. Turbinates don't look too large. Condyles appear extremely flattened, and they have lost significant vertical height, being a little bit greater loss on the left side as compared to the right side.

Left TMJ tomogram appears as though it wasn't taken in centric relation but postured forward a little bit. However, the condyles are extremely flat. Articular eminences are fairly shallow. There is increased superior and posterior joint space but a decreased space between the articular eminence and the anterior part of the condyle. Again, the condyle may be postured forward in this view.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears to have a large pseudocyst in it and is posteriorly positioned in the fossa with decreased superior and posterior joint space. Articular eminence is quite shallow on this side.

On opening, there is fairly good translation of the left TMJ, but in a forward position, it appears that there is a lot of eburnation of the condylar head. On the right joint, there is restricted translation of the condyle. A significant amount of arthritic changes is noted.

Waters view does show that there is a large mucous retention cyst in the right sinus. Turbinates appear that they are moderate in size. May be causing some airway obstruction.

Cephalometrically, I am not sure she is in complete centric relation but does show she has an anterior open bite and a high occlusal plane angle and mandibular plane angle. Oropharyngeal airway at the base of the tongue measures about 6.5 mm and at the soft palate measures about 7 mm.

Basic Diagnoses
Bilateral TMJ severe resorptive arthritis (possibly of connective tissue autoimmune origin)
Severe AP mandibular deficiency
AP maxillary deficiency
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency
Slight transverse deficiency
Mild crowding, lower anterior arch
Mild crowding, upper anterior arch
High occlusal plane angulation of 22 degrees (normal 8 degrees)
Hypertrophied turbinates
Deviated nasal septum
Nasal airway obstruction
Decreased oropharyngeal airway
Large lesion (2 x 2 x 2 cm) in the right maxillary sinus
Impacted bilateral maxillary and mandibular third molars x 4 plus an impacted supernumerary tooth in the maxillary left posterior quadrant located between the second and third molars

Recommended Treatment
Removal of maxillary and mandibular third molars and supernumerary molar on the left side
Presurgical orthodontics Align and level maxillary arch. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies in case they are necessary

CAT scan of the jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol)
Surgery
Bilateral TMJ condylectomy, debridement, reconstruction, and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses
Bilateral TMJ coronoidectomies
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvested from abdomen)
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance it and downgraft it posteriorly as well as correct any transverse discrepancies
Removal of lesion, right maxillary sinus
Turbinectomies

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion
B348  MRI Evaluation 
Date of Acquisition:	
Date of Reading: 

Left TMJ, coronal view, shows a condyle that appears to have lost considerable vertical height, at least 2/3s of the condyle appears to be missing. Fossa appears to be intact but is quite thin. Curvature of the lateral rim of the fossa with it coming down probably about 2 or 3 mm. Appears to be some type of dense tissue in the fossa area that could possibly be discal tissue. There is perhaps some reactive tissue between the disc and the head of the condyle. Cortical bone, although thin, appears to be intact over the head of the condyle.

Right TMJ, coronal view, shows a condyle where the lateral aspect of the condyle is significantly higher vertically than the medial side. Also appears to be significant loss of condylar bone although not quite as significant as the left side. Significant slope downward toward the medial side of the fossa as compared to the opposite side which was more of a minimal downward slope. There is tissue between the condylar head and the fossa but difficult to tell if it is discal tissue or not. Minimal downward curvature of the lateral rim of the fossa. Cortical bone appears to be intact in both the fossa and the condyle.

Left TMJ, sagittal view, shows a condyle that is severely deformed with what appears to be a large amount of condylar resorption associated with it. Condyle is a little bit larger toward the medial side, but toward the lateral side, it is severely deformed. Articular eminence is moderately steep. On one view, it appears that there is almost a discontinuity through the roof of the fossa toward the medial side of the fossa. The articular disc appears to be in position between the condylar head and the articular eminence. Condyle appears to be postured forward in the fossa. Appears that there may be a thin reactive pannus around the disc. Toward the medial aspect of the joint, the disc is not as clearly defined, but there appears to be increased vertical height of soft tissue. On T2 films, there appears to be a white shadow surrounding the disc in both the superior and inferior joint spaces.

On opening, there is moderate translation of the condylar head. The discal mass appears to move slightly with the condyle. There may be adhesions here as the disc has fairly minimal movement. As the condyle translates forward, the joint space appears to increase in thickness.

Right TMJ, sagittal view, shows a condyle that is larger and in better condition than the opposite side. Decreased superior and posterior joint spaces. Articular disc appears to be anteriorly displaced on this side. Disc appears fairly degenerated. There is the appearance of some reactive tissue perhaps around the disc. There is a fairly significant depression on the anterior head of the condyle toward the lateral aspect of the joint. Articular eminence toward the medial side is fairly shallow.

On opening, there is moderate translation of the condyle. Discal tissue seems to move with the condyle but difficult to tell if it reduces or not.
Impression:

Left TMJ resorptive arthritis but appears that the disc is in position, but there may be a reactive pannus around it and major evidence of condylar resorption
Right TMJ resorptive arthritis but appears that the disc is in position, but there may be a reactive pannus around it and major evidence of condylar resorption but not as severe as the left side

original_note:
B263 
B263 is 15 years old and referred by Dr. __.
Patient History

B263 has had her adenoids and tonsils removed 11 years ago. She had sleep apnea at the age of 4. This pretty much eradicated her sleep apnea problems. She has some problems with seasonal allergies. She takes no medications. She has no known drug reactions.

She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, hearing loss, or headaches. She recently has been diagnosed as having some mild scoliosis. Her toes pop a lot but cause no other problems. She has an allergic reaction to most fruits and seasonal allergy problems. No known problems with jewelry. She used to clench and grind her teeth a lot but doesn't do that much anymore because her teeth just don't fit together at all.

To the best of her knowledge, her problems began about 2-1/2 years ago while she was in orthodontics. She said when she was wearing elastics she first noticed having some clicking and popping in her jaw joints. She had orthodontic appliances from the age of 13 to 14-1/2. Upon completion of the orthodontics, her bite was very good. Most of the changes in her occlusion have occurred since her braces have been removed. She rates her TMJ pain at 4 and pain on an average day is somewhere around 3. She rates her jaw function at 6, diet at 5, and disability at 3.

Patient Concerns
Teeth and jaws don't fit together
Jaw malalignment has been getting progressively worse
Speech has been significantly affected
Difficulty swallowing
Can't chew
Mouth is open all the time
Difficult to close the lips
Chronic mouth breathing.

Clinical Examination
In frontal view, B263 has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 18 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 4 mm. In centric relation, maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline may be about 0.5 mm to the right, and chin is probably 1 to 2 mm to the right.

In profile, she has significant lip incompetence of about 12 mm. She has a slight C-shaped curve to the upper lip. Some slight AP maxillary deficiency of probably about 3 to 4 mm. Chin is retruded by approximately 18 mm. Chin-neckline is short, although the chin-neck angle is fairly well-defined. She has some lower lip evertion, and the lower lip is more prominent than the chin. In centric occlusion, she occludes only on the second molars bilaterally, and all other teeth are out of occlusion. She has about a 3-mm anterior open bite with a tendency toward crossbite on the right side and a tendency toward a buccal crossbite on the left side. She has an end-on Class II occlusion being probably close to 5 mm Class II. Oropharynx, soft palate, and uvula all look normal and healthy.

Intranasally, the nares are a little bit constricted. Transverse width of the nose is narrow. Turbinates are of moderate size but may be significantly contributory to some nasal airway obstruction.

To palpation, she has some mild tenderness over the masseters, TMJs, styloid areas, SCMs, posterior neck, and shoulder area. On jaw function, she has a fairly palpable opening click at about 40 mm and closing click at 15 mm on the left side and probably at about the same on the right side but a little bit softer and more difficult to pick up by palpation. Maximal incisal opening is 52 mm. Excursive movements are 9 mm in each direction.

Radiographic Evaluation
Panorex shows the presence of four impacted third molars with inadequate room for them to erupt in. Sinuses look relatively clear. Turbinates appear moderate in size, but septum appears fairly straight. Appears to be some degenerative changes of the condyles being a little bit greater on the left side as compared to the right side. Anterior open bite is quite evident.

Waters view shows the turbinates are moderate size with the left one being larger than the right and a little bit more airway obstruction on the left side compared to the right side. Maxillary sinuses appear clear as do the ethmoids. She doesn't have a frontal sinus of any significance on the right side and has a relatively small one on the left side, but it is clear. Jaws look fairly symmetric.

Left TMJ tomogram shows that the condyle is postured forward, so she may not have been in centric relation when the x-ray was taken, maybe postured forward slightly. She has a slightly increased posterior joint space. Anterior joint space appears somewhat decreased. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Anterior aspect of the condylar head appears flattened. It appears as though there has been significant loss of condylar volume.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears smaller than normal indicative of loss of condylar volume. Significant anterior beaking is present on this side. Superior joint space may be decreased slightly. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Condylar head and neck appear foreshortened.

On opening views, there is good translation of the condyles bilaterally. The smallness of both condyles is seen in the forward position. In this particular position, there seems to be a little bit more sclerosis on the left condyle as compared to the right side. Articular eminences relatively have the same amount of inclination.

Cephalometrically, she has a significant Class II skeletal relationship. Has an anterior open bite of at least 3 mm. She has a skeletal and occlusal Class II relationship. Has a high occlusal plane angulation. Oropharyngeal airway at the soft palate level measures about 4 mm and at the soft palate level measures about 3.5 mm at the base of the tongue (normal airway 11 mm). Appears to be plenty of room for the tongue intraorally as the tongue sits well below the height of the palate. Dental model analysis shows that she doesn't have any significant tooth-size discrepancy. She does, however, have a significant accentuate curve of Spee with the incisors being 3 to 4 mm above the flat plane. In the lower arch, she has an accentuated curve of occlusion as well with the lower incisors being at least a couple of millimeters higher than the bicuspids. She has an increased curve of Wilson in the molars in the upper arch.

Basic Diagnoses
Bilateral TMJ idiopathic condylar resorption with articular disc dislocations without reduction
Vertical maxillary hyperplasia
AP mandibular hypoplasia
Apertognathia
Class II end-on occlusion
Accentuated curve of occlusion, upper arch
Slight overangulation of maxillary and mandibular incisors
Hypertrophied nasal turbinates
Partial nasal airway obstruction
Significantly decreased oropharyngeal airway of 3 to 4 mm (normal 11 mm)
Significant speech disorder
Lip incompetence
TMJ pain
Myofascial pain
Impacted maxillary and mandibular third molars
High occlusal plane angulation

Recommended Treatment
Presurgical orthodontics Align and level maxillary arch in three segments:
Segment 1: Left cuspid through first molar Segment 2: Left lateral incisor through right lateral incisor
Segment 3: Right cuspid through right first molar Place a step in the arch wire so that the four incisors are aligned at an elevated plane compared to the posterior teeth. Keep the lateral incisor roots tipped mesially and cuspid roots tipped distally to make room for interdental osteotomies
Align and level mandibular arch. Strip lower incisors removing 2 mm of tooth structure and retract. Place an arch wire only back through the first molars. Place brackets on all four second molars but do not engage in the arch wire at this time

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the maxilla superiorly and level the accentuated curve of occlusion (Spee)
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction
Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies Surgical removal of impacted maxillary and mandibular third molars x 4

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion
B263  MRI Scan Evaluation
Date of Acquisition:	
Date of Reading: 

Left TMJ, coronal view, shows a condyle that appears to have lost significant vertical height. Appears somewhat narrow, but the head has a mushroom shape to it. On some views there is a decreased vertical joint space, and the bone quality on some of the views does not look really good. In fact, the condyle shows up well on only one view. Moderate downward curvature of the lateral rim of the fossa by about 3 mm. Discal tissue appears to be anteriorly positioned.

Right TMJ, coronal view, shows a condyle that likewise has lost significant vertical dimension. Has more of a mushroom shape to it. Lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 or 3 mm. Joint space is not too deficient and similar to the opposite side. Discal tissue is probably anteriorly displaced.

Left TMJ, sagittal view, shows a condyle that is quite small in size. Has definitely lost condylar volume both vertically and anteroposteriorly. Articular eminence is actually fairly steep. Particularly toward the lateral aspect of the joint it is quite steep and long. Disc is anteriorly dislocated and sitting beneath the articular eminence.

On opening, there is restricted translation of the condyle, and the articular disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction. The disc is short anteroposteriorly.

Right TMJ, sagittal view, demonstrates a condyle that has undergone significant loss of volume vertically and anteroposteriorly. Anterior beaking is present. Articular eminence is extremely steep very similar to the opposite side. Appears to be some decreased superior joint space. Articular disc is anteriorly positioned and sitting beneath the articular eminence very similar to the opposite side.

On opening, there is restricted translation of the condyle, and the condyle stops quite a bit short of the apex of the articular eminence. Articular disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction. Disc is short anteroposteriorly and appears somewhat degenerated.

Impression:
Left TMJ significant condylar resorption (probably ICR) and articular disc dislocation with deformation and nonreducing
Right TMJ significant condylar resorption (probably ICR) and articular disc dislocation with deformation and nonreducing
Probable salvageable articular discs


Dr. __

RE:	B263 
Dear __:
Thank you so much for referring B263  for consultation. She is certainly a lovely young lady, and I enjoyed visiting with, her mother, and her aunt. B263 is 15 years old and had previous orthodontics performed from the age of 13 to 14-1/2. At the completion of her orthodontics, she had a really good bite relationship. Since that time she has developed significant retrusion of the mandible, Class II occlusion, and anterior open bite. MRI evaluation demonstrates relatively classic idiopathic condylar resorption bilaterally with subsequent retrusion of the mandible and development of an anterior open bite.

Based on clinical, radiographic, dental model, and MRI analyses, B263's diagnoses are as follows:
Bilateral TMJ idiopathic condylar resorption with articular disc dislocations without reduction
Vertical maxillary hyperplasia
AP mandibular hypoplasia
Apertognathia
Class II end-on occlusion
Accentuated curve of occlusion, upper arch
Slight overangulation of maxillary and mandibular incisors
Hypertrophied nasal turbinates
Partial nasal airway obstruction
Significantly decreased oropharyngeal airway of 3 to 4 mm (normal 11 mm)
Significant speech disorder
Lip incompetence
TMJ pain
Myofascial pain
Impacted maxillary and mandibular third molars
High occlusal plane angulation
Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Presurgical orthodontics Align and level maxillary arch in three segments:
Segment 1: Left cuspid through first molar Segment 2: Left lateral incisor through right lateral incisor
Segment 3: Right cuspid through right first molar Place a step in the arch wire so that the four incisors are aligned at an elevated plane compared to the posterior teeth. Keep the lateral incisor roots tipped mesially and cuspid roots tipped distally to make room for interdental osteotomies
Align and level mandibular arch. Strip lower incisors removing 2 mm of tooth structure and retract. Place an arch wire only back through the first molars. Place brackets on all four second molars but do not engage in the arch wire at this time

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the maxilla superiorly and level the accentuated curve of occlusion (Spee)
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction
Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies Surgical removal of impacted maxillary and mandibular third molars x 4

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion
I feel this treatment plan would get B263 the best possible functional and aesthetic result.
B263 has a significant accentuated curve of occlusion in the upper arch. Therefore, I have recommended that we align the teeth in segments but use a continuous wire with a vertical step in that wire. Segment 1 would be left cuspid through first molar, segment 2 left lateral incisor through right lateral incisor, and segment 3 right cuspid through right first molar.

Although it would be very helpful to have brackets on the second molars particularly at the time of surgery, do not engage the second molars in the arch wire as they could cause some significant aberrant tooth movement at this time since those are the other teeth that are in a functional occlusion. They can be engaged in the orthodontic treatment following surgery. Also, I plan to do interdental osteotomies between the upper lateral incisors and cuspids. Please be sure that the lateral incisor roots remain tipped mesially and cuspids distally to make room for these interdental bone cuts.

In the lower arch, the lower teeth are slightly overangulated. In order for us to get the best interdigitation, it would be helpful to slenderize the lower anterior teeth by removing approximately 2 mm of tooth structure and retracting the lower anteriors.

If you have any other thoughts or suggestions in reference to her treatment, please contact me. Thank you again so much for referring B263 , and I look forward to the opportunity of working with you in correcting her concerns and problems.

With best regards,

B272  MRI 
AGE: 29/F
DOB:
Date of Acquisition: 
Date of Interpretation: 
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width, although it may be slightly narrow. There may be a lateral extension at the lateral pole in one area. There appears to be discal tissue displaced laterally and perhaps anteriorly. Lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 4 mm. The superior joint space may be just slightly decreased.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has a similar morphology than the other side. It is slightly narrow, but with a little lateral extension of the lateral pole. Joint space vertically appears it may be slightly narrow. There appears to be discal space out laterally and anteriorly.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle has anterior beaking present. There appears to be decreased vertical joint space. Articular eminence is fairly steep and elongated vertically. Articular disc appears to be anteriorly displaced and sitting beneath the articular eminence. The disc is somewhat short anteroposteriorly, although it still appears relatively thin. There may be discal tissue out laterally as well. On opening, there is restricted translation of the condyle. The articular disc remains anteriorly and laterally displaced and appears more deformed in the open views, being short anteroposteriorly and with loss of the intermediate zone. The disc remains sitting beneath the articular eminence. Translation is significantly limited.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a very similar kind of morphology except there is a larger anterior beaking on the condyle present, and the articular eminence is not quite as steep. The articular disc is anteriorly and laterally displaced. Joint space looks reasonably good. Right TMJ open view shows likewise a condyle with restricted translation, and the disc remains anteriorly and laterally displaced without reduction. The disc remains beneath the articular eminence. The disc appears short anteroposteriorly. Anterior beaking is quite evident on the condyle.

IMPRESSION: Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly and laterally dislocated without reduction with significant degenerative changes with probable adhesions to the articular eminences.

B272 
B272 is 29 years old. Referred by Dr. __ .
B272's TMJ problems began when she was about 12 years of age when she was kicked in the jaw by her brother who was 2 years old at the time. Her jaw began clicking and popping after that. However, it was not until this year that she began developing pain and discomfort.

B272 has had previous orthodontics from the age of 15 to 17. At the end of the treatment, her bite fit together well and her midlines were straight and she states she had a normal Class I occlusion. She has orthodontic retainers and she actually ground through 3 sets of those until her problems began to escalate this year. B272's bite began to open anteriorly after she had her baby which was this year . B272 believes her bite opened to its current state about 6 months to a year after the baby was born. She does not feel that the problem has gotten worse for the past year and she does not believe the open bite has gotten any worse.

B272 has no problems with headaches. She rates her TMJ pain at 3, average daily pain at 3, jaw function at 4, diet at 5, disability at 5. She takes no medications. She has no earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, or hearing loss. No other joints bother her. She is aware of clenching and bruxing at night time and also clenching during the day. She chews predominantly on the right side as this creates less pain for her when chewing. A year ago she had a feeling that her lower jaw was unattached from her face. She feels as though she has to hold her mouth closed to play with her child as she is afraid that any trauma to the jaw would be extremely painful for her. Primary concerns that B272 has include the following:
Teeth do not fit together.
Chewing causes pain.
Anterior open bite.
Since this year, she has had 2 splints made, but one has helped her be pretty comfortable. This splint opens the bite slightly, but most of the teeth touch during function.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
In frontal view, B272 has fairly good facial symmetry with just some slight increased fullness on the right side of the face as compared to the left side in the ramus area by probably 3 mm. Upper lip length is about 22 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is about 2 mm. The right eye may be about 2 mm higher than the left one. She has about 6 mm of lip incompetence. Maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline. The mandibular dental midline is shifted to the left about 1 mm and the chin is probably about 3 mm to the left. In profile view, B272 appears to have fairly good balance between the upper lip, cheeks, and nose. The lower jaw appears retruded and the chin neckline is short and not well defined. The mandible appears to be retruded by about 10-12 mm. The lower third face appears a little bit long likely due to the open bite and downward and backward rotation of the mandible. Intraorally, B272 has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. The teeth appear nicely aligned and leveled. Gingiva looks healthy. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. She has an anterior open bite of about 4 mm. She has a Class III end-on cuspid relationship bilaterally, being worse on the left side. In centric relation, she occludes only on the right second molars. Oropharyngeal tissues look normal. Intranasally, turbinates appear moderate in size. It be causing a little bit of nasal airway obstruction. Maximal incisal opening is 40 mm and that is without pain. Excursion movements are about 10 mm in each direction. On opening, she may have a click or pop in the right joint at about 20 mm of opening and at 3 mm of closing, but difficult to tell. The left joint seemed fairly smooth. To palpation, she has some mild tenderness over the left temporalis muscle and right retromandibular muscle.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. Joints appear arthritic. Turbinates appear moderate in size, but sinuses appear relatively clear. She appears to hit on the right second molars and all other teeth appear to be out of occlusion. Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears arthritic and appears to have lost vertical volume. There is anterior beaking present. A little bit of sclerosis and a little bit of roughness on the head of the condyle. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has lost more condylar volume and has some peaking at the posterior head of the condyle and anterior beaking with the in between areas quite flattened. Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears somewhat degenerated. There is a small pseudocyst in the medial portion on the head of the condyle. Joint space looks reasonably good. Left TMJ view shows good joint space, but decrease lateral joint space. The condyle is a little bit narrow mediolaterally. Right TMJ shows that there appears to be erosion across the mid portion of the condyle. There is definite loss of bony cortex. Lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 to 4 mm. Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has a fairly high occlusal plane angle in the mandible. She is AP mandibular deficient. Oropharyngeal airway measures about 5 mm at the base of the tongue and 6 mm at the soft palate area. Hand manipulation of her dental models puts her back into fairly good Class I cuspid-molar relationship with about 1 mm of vertical overbite and the midline is reasonably well aligned.

Diagnoses for B272 would be as follows:
Bilateral TMJ resorptive arthritis and articular disc dislocated without reduction and severe deformation. AP mandibular hypoplasia.
Anterior open bite. Class II occlusion. Occluding only on the right second molars with all other teeth out of occlusion. Apertognathia extending from and including the right first molars through the left second molars.
TMJ pain.
Decrease oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of 5 mm (normal dimension 11 mm).

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
Presurgical orthodontics. Align and level maxillary arch. Align and level mandibular arch. CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol).
Surgery. Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement in a counterclockwise direction with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen).
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B332 I-CAT AGE: DOB:
Date of Acquisition: Date of Interpretation:
The patient has had previous orthognathic surgery. She has four bone plates in the maxilla and three bone screws in each ramus area in the mandible. Panorex shows plates in the maxilla. Sinuses look clear. Right turbinate appears moderately enlarged. Right TMJ appears arthritic with loss of condylar volume while the left side looks somewhat larger. She has a 3 x 3 retainer in the lower arch. She is missing all four third molars.

Sagittal TMJ view of the left joint shows decreased vertical joint space. Condyle appears to have reasonably good morphology. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Sinuses again appear clear. Oropharyngeal airway does look normal. The right sinus appears clear. Right condyle appears narrow anteriorly posteriorly and seems to have lost vertical and AP volume. There is decreased superior joint space. Articular eminence is moderately shallow. Axial view shows that the turbinates are moderate in size. It may have an adequate airway. Right condyle appears small in size compared to the left condyle. Sinuses appear clear. Septum has a little curvature off towards the right side. Inferior turbinates are moderately enlarged.

In coronal view, the sinuses are clear and turbinates are moderate in size with the right side being larger than the left side. The right TMJ appears quite arthritic and flattened with some mushrooming of the head, although the unilateral width of the head is narrow and the superior joint space is very narrow. The lateral rim curves inferiorly about 3 mm.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is much larger, although there is decreased superior joint space, particularly at the lateral aspect of the joint indicative of perhaps a medial displacement of the disc. Lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 mm.

IMPRESSION:
Right TMJ arthritis. Possible left TMJ articular disc medially displaced. Sinuses are clear. Turbinates are moderately enlarged.
Oropharyngeal airway appears relatively normal.

B332 CHASE
B332 is 35 years old, referred by Dr. __. She has had previous orthognathic surgery with both upper and lower jaw procedures performed. The only medication she currently takes is Imitrex as needed. B332 gets headaches that begin on the right side in the frontal, temporal, and posterior head area that begin at about a level of 9. The left side is not quite as severe and probably at a level of 8. She rates her TMJ pain at 8, average daily pain at 7, jaw function and diet probably around 2, and disability around 6. She has some residual dysthesia affecting the right upper lip, lower lip, and chin area. She has no earaches, tinnitus, gets occasional vertigo, but no hearing loss. She does have a single foot joint that tends to bother her. She is aware of clenching her teeth at night.

B332 is aware of aware of clicking and popping when she was in high school, but it was not particularly bothersome at that time. It was not until around 9 years ago that she noticed that her bite was beginning to change significantly. She has had previous orthodontic treatment one time as a teenager then again in her 20s to realign the lower teeth. 5 years ago, she began orthodontics again in preparation for surgery. The etiology of her situation at this time is unknown. 5 years ago, she had maxillary and mandibular osteotomies by Dr. __. Although it was recognized that there may be a joint problem, the TMJ joints were ignored. B332 has had several splints, in fact, three to four of them to date. She has also had chiropractic treatment, craniofacial treatment, and physical therapy without relief of the symptoms.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
In frontal view, B332 has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry in the middle and upper third face. She has some asymmetry in the lower third face. Upper lip length is about 23 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is about 3 mm and 4 mm on the right. The maxillary midline is probably about 1 mm off towards the left side. Both maxillary and mandibular midlines are about 1 mm off to the left side. The chin is a little bit off towards the right side, probably about 2 to 3 mm. In profile, B332 has some slight AP maxillary deficiency, probably about 2 to 3 mm. The chin appears to be AP retruded by probably about 10 to 12 mm. The chin neckline is short, although the chin neck angle is fairly well defined. There is some fullness in the submental area. Intraorally, B332 has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. The teeth appear to be nicely aligned and leveled. She has a 3 x 3 retainer in. She has fairly good attached gingiva, although around the maxillary right central incisor the attachment is quite thin. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. She has a slight Class 2 cuspid relationship on the right side by probably 2 to 3 mm and likewise on the left side she has probably got at least a 3 mm Class II occlusal relationship. However, she only has an overjet of about 1 to 2 mm. She has an open bite on the left side of 1 to 2 mm extending from the left incisor all the way around, through, and including the second molar. In centric relation, she occludes predominantly in the molar area on the right side and the rest of the teeth on the right are slightly out of occlusion. B332 has a transverse cant in the occlusal plane. Left maxillary cuspid is down about 1 mm lower than the right side, but the left mandibular cuspid is about 3 mm lower than the right side. Oropharyngeal tissues look healthy. Intranasally, the right turbinate appears small, but the left one appears enlarged and somewhat engorged. Holding the luminal valves open did not make any difference in her abilities to breath through her nose. She says it alternates from side to side at time as to which side she can breathe through. Today, she has significant difficulties breathing through the left side. B332 has about a 1 mm overbite on the average, but it is greater on the right side and less on the left side. To palpation, B332 has high moderate pain at a level of 6 in the TMJ area, moderate pain in the masseters, shoulders, and right retromandibular area. She has milder tenderness over the temporalis muscles, posterior neck, and left retromandibular area. On opening, she has a little bit of a glitch in the right joint. On excursion movements, she will occasionally have an audible pop in the right joint. Maximal incisal opening is 46 mm, but about 28 mm without significant discomfort. Excursion movements are about 9 mm in each direction.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. She has a 3 x 3 retainer in the lower anterior arch. She has got bone screws x3 in each ramus. She has got bone plates x4 in the maxilla with 4 screws in each plate. The left turbinate appears somewhat enlarged. Sinuses appear clear. Axial view shows that the sinuses appear clear. Fairly long screws were used and they extrude significantly into the sinuses. Left turbinate appears enlarged. Coronal view shows that the sinuses appear clear, but bone screws can be seen extending into the sinus. Left turbinate appears quite large and appears to occlude most of the airway on that side. The lateral turbinate is also is quite large. The ethmoid bones are not quite as clear on the left side as they are on the right side. Left TMJ sagittal view shows condyle that has fairly good morphology. There is a little bit of peaking at the posterior head of the condyle. Joint space appears fairly good, although decreased towards the lateral aspect of the joint. Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is small in size anteroposteriorly and also has significant loss of condylar volume in the vertical dimension. Joint space appears actually slightly thickened. Articular eminence is moderately steep.
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with good morphology. Joint space is significantly decreased. Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is quite arthritic. There has been loss of bone structure vertically and mediolaterally. Joint space seems a little bit thicker as compared to the opposite side. Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that B332 has a skeletal Class II relationship, although a Class I occlusal relationship. Oropharyngeal airway appears that there is moderate airway with an AP dimension of about 8 mm at the base of the tongue and probably 6 to 7 mm at the soft palate level. Vertical asymmetry is not quite as clear cephalometrically.

B332 apparently has had problems with Candida infections and also with black mold. She has been treated with special oils to treat these infections. She and her family went on for quite some time in reference to these special oils. I told her I had no understanding of them and I did not know what effect that would have on her surgery. She wants to stop the antibiotics as soon as she can after the surgery and start taking her special oils. Again I tried to emphasize that we need to keep her on antibiotics for at least a week to 10 days afterwards and stopping the antibiotics early could increase the risk of infection. They feel strongly that these oils will prevent her from having infections. Basic diagnoses for B332 would be as follows:

Right TMJ severe arthritis and articular disc dislocation with significant deformation and nonreducing. Left TMJ medial displacement of the articular discs with reduction. AP and posterior vertical hypoplasia with asymmetry. AP mandibular hypoplasia with asymmetry. Class II end-on occlusion. Occludes only on the right second and third molar in centric relation and all other teeth are out of occlusion. Left-sided open bite.
Hypertrophied turbinates. Nasal airway obstruction. TMJ pain.
Headaches.
Myofascial pain. Lack of attached gingiva around the mandibular right central incisor.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
Periodontal evaluation and possible gingival grafting on the mandibular right incisor. Presurgical orthodontics. Align and level maxillary arch. Tip lateral incisors mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. Align and level mandibular arch. Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing approximately 2 mm of tooth structure to correct for the tooth size discrepancy. CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol).
Surgery. Right TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. Left TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor.
Left mandibular ramus sagittal split osteotomy to advance the mandible.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to transversely level the maxilla and advance it forward in a counterclockwise direction and to level it transversely. Right TMJ fat graft (harvest from the abdomen). Right mandibular coronoidotomy.
Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies.

B332  MRI AGE: 35/F
DOB:
Date of Acquisition: Date of Interpretation:
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with fairly good mediolateral width. There is decreased superior joint space. The disc is displaced medially. Lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 mm.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has undergone some significant resorption with loss of volume. There is decreased vertical joint space. Discal tissue may be anteriorly displaced.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology. There is some flattening of the anterior head. There is a little sclerosis and perhaps a small osteophyte sticking up off the head of the condyle. Articular eminence is moderate in inclination. Articular disc is anteriorly displaced. It is not seen out laterally which would correlate to the medial displacement. There is some fluid in the superior joint space. Left TMJ open view shows that the disc appears to reduce relatively early on, but there is fairly good translation of the condyle and the articular disc.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has lost significant vertical and anteroposterior dimension. The condyle is anteroposteriorly. There is sclerosis on the top of the condyle. There is just a little bit of anterior beaking present. There is an osteophytic kind of formation in the superior aspect of the condyle. Articular eminence is moderate in inclination. The articular disc is anteriorly displaced and sitting beneath the articular eminence. There is a little bit of fluid in the superior joint space at the posterior aspect of the joint. Although there is fairly good translation of the condyle, the articular disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction.

IMPRESSION:
Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly and medially displaced, but with reduction early on on opening. Right TMJ significant degenerative changes of the condyle with loss of condylar volume and with the articular disc anteriorly displaced without reduction.

original_note:
B094
B094 is 16 years old. She is referred by Dr. XXXX. B094 is in good health with no major medical problems. B094's problems began in about April 2010 of unknown etiology. She developed a close lock on the right side of unknown etiology. The treatment B094 has had so far included:

A minor equilibration.
An appointment with Dr. XXXX who was recommending splint therapy. Physical therapy which started 3 months ago and significantly reduced her symptoms.

B094 gets headaches maybe about once a week at about a level of 3 in the frontal and probably the temporal areas as well. She today has no TMJ pain. She rates her pain around the face and head area at a level of 1. She rates her jaw function, diet, and disability all at 0. She gets right-sided earaches, moderate tinnitus, and occasional vertigo but no hearing loss. She has no other joints that bother her. She is not aware of clenching or bruxism. She did have orthodontics four times with the first beginning about the age of 6 with a palatal expander. Her last orthodontic appliances were only for a few months, 2 years ago.

Primary concerns that B094 has include the following:
Lip incompetence. Joint problems. Open bite.
In frontal view, B094 has good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 16 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is 7 mm. The mandibular dental midline and chin appear to be pretty much on the facial midline. The nasal tip is deviated to the right side about 1 to 2 mm. The maxillary midline is about 1 mm off to the right side. The right eye is about 2 mm lower than the left one. She has a lip incompetence of 11 mm. In profile view, she has an AP deficiency of probably 4 to 5 mm and the chin is AP deficient by probably about 10 to 12 mm. She has a C shaped curve to the upper lip. The lower third face is somewhat elongated and she does have the significant lip incompetence. She does have a nasal dorsal prominence. Intraorally, B094 has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. In the upper arch, she also has all of her teeth back through the second molars. She has fairly good attached gingiva along the anterior teeth. She has just a slight Class II end-on cuspid relationship on the left side and right side by about 1 mm. She has about 1.5 mm vertical overbite. She has about a 1 mm over jet. To palpation, she has some moderate pain over the left TMJ and milder pain over the left retromandibular area. She appears to have a little bit of closing click on the right side at about 30, but cannot really detect much on opening. On the left side on opening, she had a very soft click at about 30 mm of opening and closing at probably about 25. Oropharyngeal tissues look normal and healthy. Intranasally, as best I could see, the turbinates look relatively normal in size and the septum looked reasonably straight. The maximum incisal opening is 53 mm, excursion movements are about 8 mm in each direction.

Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. The turbinates appear enlarged, although the sinuses appear reasonably clear. It is difficult to evaluate the TMJ joints. Bony septum looks reasonably straight.

Coronal view shows that the septum is deviated a little bit off towards the left side. The turbinates appear significantly enlarged particularly on the right side. The sinuses appear clear.

Axial view shows that the sinuses are clear. The septum appears reasonably straight at this particular cut level and the right turbinate appears somewhat enlarged. Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology. Joint space looks reasonably well balanced. The articular eminence is moderately inclined.

Right TMJ shows a condyle with fairly good morphology. The joint space is fairly well balanced. The articular eminence has a moderate inclination.
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears to have good mediolateral width. The joint space appears fairly well balanced, although a little bit narrow on medial side. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 to 3 mm.

The right joint shows fairly good joint space. The lateral rim curves inferiorly about 3 mm. The condyle has good mediolateral width.
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that B094 has anterior vertical maxillary excess. Oropharyngeal airway actually looks fairly reasonable. She has some slight over inclination of the maxillary incisors. The lower incisors are also inclined. She has probably a moderate occlusal plane inclination. Basic diagnoses would be as follows:

Bilateral TMJ mild arthritis and articular discs anteriorly and medially displaced.
Maxillary vertical hyperplasia and slight AP hypoplasia.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia.
Hypertrophied turbinates. External nasal deformity.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1. Presurgical orthodontics. a.	Align and level maxillary arch. Tip lateral incisors roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. Try to slightly expand the upper arch. b.	Align and level mandibular arch. Try to slightly expand the lower arch.
2. Surgery. a.	Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors. b.	Bilateral ramus sagittal osteotomies to advance the mandible and to move it superiorly. c.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move it superiorly approximately 4 to 5 mm. d.	Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies. e.	External rhinoplasty.
3. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B094 MRI
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has good mediolateral width. It appears there may be some discal tissue medially and anteriorly displaced. The lateral rim curves inferiorly about 2 mm. The joint space looks fairly well balanced.

Right TMJ shows a condyle with good mediolateral width. The disc may also be anteriorly and medially displaced. There may be decreased vertical joint space. The superior joint space appears narrow.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle with fairly good morphology. There is a little bit of anterior beaking present. The articular disc may be slightly anteriorly displaced at the lateral aspect of the joint. More medially, it may be in a little bit better position. Out lateral, the disc is not quite as evidently seen as it is more towards the medial side. The articular eminence is moderately steep.

Right TMJ has fairly good joint morphology with just some slight anterior beaking present. The disc appears it may be in a little bit vertical orientation within the joint, particularly toward the lateral aspect of the joint. On opening, she has good reduction and movement of the condylar heads.

IMPRESSION: Bilateral TMJ mild arthritis and articular discs anteriorly and medially displaced.

original_note:
ERIN WILSON 									08/09/2006

Erin is 21 years old, referred by Dan Clifford, DDS, 7520 Montgomery NE, Albuquerque, NM 87109.  Phone number is 505-883-4865.  She had a previous tonsillectomy.  She has had MRSA real bad two years ago but also had it again last year from the knees downward.  They were skin abscesses that she had.  She smokes about six cigarettes a day and has done that for about four years.  She has had an adverse reaction to Demerol.  The medication she takes is Advil four tablets q.8h. for headaches.

PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Unable to open mouth. 
Painful headaches.
Ear ringing.
Teeth do not match up.
Unable to eat and chew.
Pain in the jaw joints.   

TREATMENTS:
Splints, both soft and hard.
Physical therapy.
Steroid therapy.
Injections into the TMJs times two. 

Erin's problems began on March 8, 2006, when she had surgery to take her tonsils out.  Prior to that, she was unaware of any TMJ problems.  Prior to this problem, the only problem she was aware of was clenching and grinding her teeth at night some but that was only periodic.  In June of 2006 she began splint therapy.  She initially had a soft splint on the lower teeth that created no improvement for her.  She then had an arch splint made on the lower arch but this increased her pain and headaches.  She had physical therapy beginning in July and this did help her neck and back but provided no relief of her TMJs, jaws, and headaches.  She also was tried on a Therabite but this increased her pain and headaches.  She saw Dr. Rabi Gorav, an ENT specialist in Las Cruses, who did steroid injections into both TMJs with no improvement.  She saw Dr. Clifford who did repeat steroid injections into the joints and also did ionophoresis.  None of these treatments have helped her to date.  The Advil medication does help her pain some.  She has been offered narcotics but she has refused those.

She gets frequent earaches, severe tinnitus and occasional vertigo.  No other joints bother her at this time.  Erin rates her headaches at a level of 9 in the frontal and temple area with them being worse on the right side as compared to the left side.  She rates her TMJ pain at 9, right side worse than the left side.  Average daily pain at 10, jaw function at 10, diet at 8, disability at 8.  She is not aware currently of clenching and bruxism.  Interestingly she has numbness of her tongue bilaterally since the tonsils were removed.  This has affected her speech.  




ERIN WILSON				08/09/2006				Page 2


In frontal view, Erin has good facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 18 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 3 mm.  Maxillary midline and chin midline are pretty much on the facial midline.  Mandibular dental midline is shifted about 1 mm to the right.  In profile, she has good AP and vertical facial balance.  She has a low mandibular plane angle facial morphology.  Intraorally, she has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars.  She has moderate crowding in the lower anterior arch.  In the upper arch she has all of her teeth back to the second molars.  She is slight Class II cuspid relationship bilaterally by maybe 1 to 2 mm.  Erin has significant difficulties even trying to bite her teeth together.  If she tries to close down she has about 1 or 2 mm of open bite bilaterally.  Trying to push her teeth, though they do go together, creates significant pain for her particularly in the right masseter and right TMJ area.  Oropharynx appears clear and uvula, soft palate and faucial pillars are all relatively normal.  I can see the scar line from the removal of the tonsils.  Maximal incisal opening is about 20 mm.  Excursive movements are essentially 0.  To palpation, she has high moderate to low severe pain over the right masseter, right TMJ and moderate pain over the temporalis muscles, left masseter, left TMJ, right styloid area.  

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears to have a little cratering in the anterosuperior head.  Seems to be a slight increased joint space vertically perhaps.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  Occlusion is apart.  

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle may have a little bit better shape and some anterior beaking.  Increased superior and posterior joint space.  Teeth are apart.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  On opening, there is virtually no translation and very minimal capability to open.  

Panorex shows the presence of four impacted third molars.  She has a root canal in mandibular left first molar.  Condyles appear a little bit arthritic but difficult to tell on these views.  The sinuses appear relatively clear.  

Waters view shows that the sinuses appear clear.  The septum is reasonably straight.  Turbinate on the right side may be enlarged.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she is basically a skeletal Class I and very close to an occlusal Class I relationship although she is unable to bite together all the way and has an open bite extending the entire occlusion.  She has a decreased oropharyngeal airway of only about 3 mm or so but part of this may be due to tongue placement as the tongue appears postured significantly posteriorly.  There is increased vertical space and also anteroposterior space between the tip of the tongue and the teeth and palate.  

Erin had two parakeets for about a year.  Both birds died.  She has a cat that catches and kills bird and lays them on the doorstep and she has five dogs that try to eat the birds.  She has a sister who has irritable bowel syndrome and asthma and gastritis.  Erin has an ovarian cyst.  There is no specific time during the day that her pain is worse.
ERIN WILSON				08/09/2006				Page 3


I gave Erin the following prescriptions:
Levaquin 500 mg dispensing 20 tablets, 1 tablet per day, refill times 3.
Relafen 750 mg dispensing 30 tablets, 1 tablet two times per day, refill times 5.
Ultram 50 mg tablets dispensing 50 tablets, 1 tablet q.4.-6h. p.r.n. pain, refill times 4.

I recommend the following laboratory tests:
CBC.
Sedimentation rate.
C-reactive protein.
ANA.
Rheumatoid factor.
Chlamydia serology.
Mycoplasma serology.
IgA, IgE, IgG and IgM.

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
Bilateral TMJ reactive arthritis and articular disc dislocations.
Bilateral TMJ closed lock.
Open bite of 2 mm (patient unable to close jaws to get teeth together.)
Severe TMJ pain.
Severe headaches.
Myofascial pain. 
Severe masticatory dysfunction. 

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Medications.
Levaquin 500 mg dispensing 20 tablets, 1 tablet per day.
Relafen 750 mg dispensing 30 tablets, 1 tablet two times per day. 
Ultram 50 mg tablets dispensing 50 tablets, 1 tablet q.4-6h. p.r.n. pain.
Consider repeating MRI if surgical treatment is delayed for more than four to six months. 
Treatment option 1.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors. 
Bilateral mandibular ramus sagittal split osteotomies to correct bite and allow space in the TMJ for the articular disc.
Treatment option 2.
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses.
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen.)  
Postsurgical physical therapy only if necessary. 





ERIN WILSON 				08/09/2006				Page 4


It appears that Erin has advanced reactive arthritis affecting both TMJs with the right side worse than the left side.  MRI shows that she has what appears to be displaced discs being worse on the right side than the left side with degenerative changes of those discs as well as arthritic changes on the condyles.  In addition, she has a history of MRSA during the past two years with recurrent infections in her legs.  She also has a history of recurrent strep infections in the oropharynx and tonsillar area that necessitated removal of her tonsils in March of 2006.  Since her tonsils were removed she has had no recurrences of sore throats.  I am concerned that she may have reactive arthritis created by either the recurrent sore throats versus the MRSA condition.

original_note:
B346 
B346 is 57 years old. Referred by Dr. __.

B346 has had clicking and popping in her right TMJ since childhood. However, it was only earlier this year that she had developed excruciating pain in the right TMJ the day after eating a steak. She did have a splint that she wore around 30 years ago, but since developing the severe pain earlier this year she has been on day and nighttime splints with some relief, but without elimination of her problem. She also has had acupuncture and she also has had cranio-sacral treatment. She also did have a splint for 2 years prior to this episode.

Primary concerns that B346 has includes the following:
Pain in the right jaw and jaw joint area. Cannot open the mouth very wide. Earaches on the right side. Airway decreased with difficulties in breathing.

She currently takes the following medications:
Lexapro 10 mg b.i.d. Wellbutrin 100 mg q.d.
Tylenol PM p.r.n. Tylenol p.r.n.
B346 gets headaches on the right side at about a level of 6 that affect the frontal and temporal area. She gets a headache about once a week at about a level of 6 in the right frontal and temporal area. She gets TMJ pain at a level of 5, average daily pain at 1, jaw function at 8, diet at 6, disability at 4. She gets earaches on the right side and has severe tinnitus that appears bilaterally. She has no problems with vertigo. No other joints bother her. She is aware of clenching and bruxing her teeth at nighttime and also during the day. She does get pain down through the right shoulder into the right arm area. She does get nausea when flying and also on boats. B346 does have a history of psoriasis that affects her elbows, her scalp, and around her ears and she also has a small area on her back. The psoriasis started 27 years ago. She has a path around her hairline almost all the way around where the psoriasis is involved. She also has it inside her ears.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
In frontal view, B346 has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 22 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is zero. The maxillary midline, mandibular dental midline, and chin are all fairly well aligned with each other. In profile view, she has some slight AP maxillary deficiency probably about 3-4 mm in the lip area, and the chin appears to be retruded by probably 10-12 mm. Chin neckline is short and chin neck angle is not defined. Intraorally, B346 has all of her teeth back through the second molars. She has a crown on the mandibular left first and second molar as well as a second bicuspid. On the right side, she has a crown on the first bicuspid. She has some mild crowding in the lower anterior arch. She has a good attached gingiva. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars present. She has a slight Class III cuspid relationship and an end-on incisor relationship in part due to the splint therapy that she has had. She has a tendency towards a crossbite on the left side.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars and root canals on the mandibular right first bicuspid, left second bicuspid, and maxillary left second bicuspid. She has crowns or large restorations on most of the posterior teeth. Right condyle appears a little erosive. Left TMJ sagittal tomogram shows a condyle with fairly good morphology and good joint space. Right joint shows erosions and arthritic changes in this joint. Articular eminences are fairly steep in both joints. Left TMJ coronal tomogram shows fairly good condylar morphology. There appears to be decreased lateral joint space. Right TMJ coronal view shows erosions in the condylar head. Lateral rim of the fossa in both joints curves inferiorly about 3-4 mm. Coronal facial x-ray shows that there is a little bit of fluid in the left sinus, but the right sinus is clear. Turbinates are fairly large in size. The bony septum looks relatively straight. Axial view shows that the turbinates are moderate in size. Left sinus has fluid and the right one appears clear. Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has basically a Class I skeletal occlusal relationship. Oropharyngeal airway measures about less than 6 mm at the soft palate level and about 8 mm at the base of the tongue.

Basic diagnoses for B346 would be as follows:
Right TMJ psoriatic arthritis. Mandibular AP hypoplasia.
Maxillary AP and posterior vertical hypoplasia.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway. Right TMJ pain. Right earache. Right-sided myofascial pain. End-on incisor relationship.
Significant crowding in the lower anterior arch. Mild crowing upper anterior arch.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
Presurgical orthodontics. Align and level maxillary arch. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. Align and level mandibular arch. Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing at least 2 mm of tooth structure to keep from flaring them too far forward. Try to expand the lower arch some if possible to better accommodate the anterior teeth. CT scan of jaws and jaw joints. Surgery. Right TMJ reconstruction with mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. Left mandibular sagittal split osteotomy to advance the mandible.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla and down graft it posteriorly. Right TMJ fat graft (harvest from the abdomen).
Right coronoidotomy. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B346  MRI 
AGE: 57/F
DOB: 
Date of Acquisition:
Date of Interpretation: 
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears somewhat arthritic but has fairly good mediolateral width. There appears to be some fluid in the superior joint space on the medial side. It is difficult to tell where the discs are in this imaging, but they may be anteriorly displaced. Joint space may be slightly narrowed, but not too significantly.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width. It appears there may be some loss of vertical volume. Joint space looks slightly increased. It is difficult to tell where the articular disc is.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology. The disc appears to be in reasonable position. It could be just slightly anteriorly displaced at the very lateral aspect of the joint. Articular eminence is fairly steep. There is some fluid in the superior joint space and to a lesser extent in the inferior joint space. On opening, there is good translation of the condyle and the disc reduces and translates well along with the condyle.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that appears quite arthritic and it appears there are erosions on the condylar head. The articular disc is anteriorly displaced and sitting beneath the articular eminence and is severely degenerated. There are osteophytic formations on the condylar head likely related to the erosions that are present. At the lateral aspect of the joint, the condyle shows a significant erosion in the mid part and also on the anterior aspect of the condyle. There appears to be decreased joint space vertically. There appears to be some inflammation in the bilateral tissues. On opening, there is moderately good translation, but the articular disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction.

IMPRESSION:
Relatively normal left TMJ. Right TMJ reactive arthritis with articular discs dislocated without reduction with significant deformation and significant erosions in the condylar head.

original_note:
HEATH HOPSON									04-26-04

Heath is 13 years old. He will be 14 in a couple of weeks. He was referred by Dr. Al Bishop, 3001 Brown Trail, #101, Bedford, TX  76021.

Patient History

Heath is in good health with no major medical problems. Heath’s problems began about three years ago as far as having noises in his jaw joints. It is of unknown etiology. When he was quite a bit younger, about 8 years old, he still had his primary incisors in and got accidentally hit by his brother, and his two front teeth got knocked out, but these were his primary teeth. Heath has had orthodontic appliances on for about two years now. He takes Advil or Tylenol for his headaches. He gets headaches about three or four times a week at an average level of about 4, although they an vary from that in either direction. Headaches are predominantly in the temporal and top of the head area. His headaches began just in the very beginning of this year. He rates his TMJ pain at 0, average daily pain at 3, jaw function at 1, diet at 2, and disability at 0. He has no earaches. Gets tinnitus sometimes but no vertigo and no hearing loss. Other joints that bother him include his back, knees, and ankles. He is aware of nighttime clenching but does no feel that he does that during the day.

Patient Concerns

Difficult to get his lips together
Shows a lot of teeth and gums
Difficulties breathing through the nose creating primarily predominant mouth breathing

Clinical Examination

Upper lip length is about 18 mm, but if the upper lip is filled out a little bit, it lengthens to about 21 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 7 to 8 mm. Maxillary, mandibular, and chin midline are all fairly well aligned with the facial midline. 

In profile view, Heath has a head postured forward neck position. Upper lip supralabrale is posterior to subnasale by probably 6 to 8 mm. Chin is retruded by probably 15 to 20 mm. Chin-neckline has moderately good length, and the angle is reasonably well-formed. Heath has lip incompetence of about 11 mm. 

Intraorally, Heath has all of his lower teeth back to the second molars. Teeth appear to be nicely aligned and leveled. In the upper arch, Heath has all of his upper teeth back through the second molars. Upper right lateral incisor looks small in size. He has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship by probably about 3 mm bilaterally. There is only about a millimeter of overjet probably a result of the tooth-size discrepancy created by the small upper lateral incisors. He has a 1 to 1.5-mm vertical overbite relationship.

To palpation, Heath has some minimal tenderness over the styloid areas but no other significant pain through the head and neck area. Joints seem to function relatively smoothly. Incisal opening is 53 mm. Excursive movements are about 11 mm in each direction. Oropharynx appears clear. Uvula is a little bit elongated but not too significantly. Appears to be fairly good dimension anteroposteriorly from the soft palate to the posterior pharyngeal wall. Tonsils are present but look normal.

Intranasally, it appears that the septum is deviated a little bit off toward the right side. Turbinates are moderately large, and the entire transverse width of the nose may be a little bit narrow. Appears that the turbinates are offering some airway compromise intranasally. Does have a wide columella base. He has been wearing elastics between the upper and lower arch. He has worn Class II elastics for about the last six to nine months.

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows that the sinuses appear relatively clear. Turbinates appear a little bit large particularly on the right side. Septum may be deviated a little bit off toward the right side. There are four impacted wisdom teeth. He has a root canal in the maxillary left first molar and has adequate space between the upper laterals and cuspids. The cuspids are tipped distally significantly. Condyles appear to have relatively normal shape. 

Waters view shows that the turbinates appear quite large intranasally. Not much of an airway space. Bony septum looks reasonably straight but cannot determine the position of the cartilaginous septum. 

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears a little bit flattened on top with some anterior projection of the condylar head. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Appears to be reasonable joint space around the condyle. A little alteration of trabeculae inside the condyle perhaps. 

Left TMJ tomograms shows a condyle with fairly good morphology with a little bit of anterior beaking and a little slight sclerosis on the anterior leading edge. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Joint space seems relatively balanced. 

On opening, there appears to be good translation of both condyles. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows the presence of a high occlusal plane angle and Class II skeletal relationship although fairly close to a Class I occlusal relationship. Large adenoid mass in the nasopharynx. Oropharyngeal and soft palate pharyngeal airways appear fairly good, but there is some constricture between the large adenoid mass and the nasopharynx and the posterior aspect of the turbinate. There is significant lip incompetence and a high occlusal plane angle. Heath has a tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of about 4 mm.

Basic Diagnoses

Anterior vertical maxillary excess
AP maxillary deficiency
AP mandibular deficiency
Class II end-on occlusion
High occlusal plane angulation of 17 degrees (normal 8 degrees)
Tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of about 4 mm
Impacted maxillary and mandibular third molars x 4
Hypertrophied turbinates
Deviated nasal septum off to the right side
Nasal airway obstruction
Rule out TMJ problems
Significant lip incompetence of approximately 10 to 12 mm
Overangulation of lower incisors
Recommended Treatment

Presurgical orthodontics 
Consider removing appliances and placing in retainers for a year-and-a-half to two years

MRI of left and right TMJ

Continue to monitor facial growth and development

Presurgical orthodontics 
Replace appliances on maxillary arch
Replace appliances on mandibular arch. Strip lower anterior teeth removing at least 2 mm of tooth structure and retract closing the spaces to help correct the tooth-size problem

Surgery
Possible bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors if discs are identified as being displaced by clinical and MRI evaluation
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance, move the maxilla superiorly approximately 7 mm, and correct any transverse problems that may be present
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction
Partial inferior turbinectomies
Nasal septoplasty
Surgical removal of impacted third molars x 4

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion
