original_note:
STEPHANIE SMITH									07/11/2007

Stephanie is 30 years old, referred off the Internet.  Previous surgeries include a C-section on November 7, 2006, and wisdom teeth removed in December of 2002.  Stephanie is otherwise in good health.  She takes birth control pills and some vitamins.  She had an adverse reaction to penicillin and vicodin gives her headaches.   She had some orthodontic treatment from about 1985 to 1989 by Dr. Reece in Ann Arbor, MI, to correct a crossbite.  Her mother first noticed that Stephanie's bite was changing when she was in college in about 1995.  Etiology is unknown.  For the past 10 years her facial asymmetry and occlusion and gotten worse.  In 1997, her upper front teeth were forward over her lower front teeth and now the lower front teeth are anterior to the upper teeth.  She was about 21 in 1998.  

In frontal view, Stephanie has fairly good facial symmetry in the middle and upper third face relative to the orbits and forehead.  The nasal tip is deviated off to the right side probably 4 mm.  Maxillary midline is off to the right about 1 mm.  Mandibular dental midline is off to the right about 5 mm.  Chin is off to the right about 8 mm.  Upper lip length is about 20 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 1.5 mm in the midline.  In profile view, she has some infraorbital deficiency probably about 4 mm.  Chin is just slightly AP deficient by probably 4 to 5 mm.  Upper lip is in fairly good balance with the cheeks and the nose.  Chin-neckline is slightly short although the chin-neck angle is reasonably well defined.  The left infraorbital area is a little bit deficient by probably 3 to 4 mm as well.  Intraorally, Stephanie has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars.  Teeth are fairly well aligned and leveled.  Some asymmetry in the lower arch with the right cuspid being 1 to 2 mm posterior to the left cuspid.  In the upper arch, Stephanie has all of her teeth back through the second molars present.  She has basically a Class I cuspid relationship on the right side but almost a full Class III cuspid relationship on the left side.  She has an anterior crossbite and a right posterior crossbite.  Transverse cant in the occlusion with the left cuspid being approximately 3 mm lower than the right cuspid.  As Stephanie shifts her mandible about 6 mm to the left at the midlines, it significantly improves her facial symmetry.  However, she is still slightly more full on the left side in the mandibular angle area as compared to the right side.  When she shifts her mandible in that direction the crossbite is corrected on the right side but develops on the left side and the anterior bite of course gets worse.  She has about a 0 vertical overbite relationship.  She has a slight yaw in the occlusion with there being just a light rotation off towards the left side in the molar area by 1 to 2 mm.  The oropharyngeal areas look healthy and structures appear normal.  Anteriorly in the nose, the left turbinate looks relatively normal.  The right one looks maybe just slightly enlarged.  The nasal septum appears reasonably straight.  Clinically looking at the anterior aspect of the nose there does not appear to be any significant airway obstruction but cannot evaluate the posterior aspect.  To palpation, she has some mild tenderness over her shoulders but no other tenderness around the head and neck area.  Joints seem to function relatively smoothly.  Maximal incisal opening is about 45 mm.  Excursive movement to the right 9 mm, left 10 mm.  





STEPHANIE SMITH				07/11/2007				Page 2

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION:
Panorex shows the absence of all 4 third molars.  Sinuses look relatively clear.  Left condyle might be just slightly larger than the right but not too significant of a difference.  The left condylar head and neck may be just slightly longer than the right side but again not real significant. 

Waters view shows that there is a little curvature in the bony septum.  Turbinates appear moderate in size and the nasal airway actually looks reasonably good.  Sinuses appear clear.  She has no frontal sinuses.  

Left TMJ tomogram shows that there appears to be a decreased joint space vertically.  Condyle has a reasonably good shape.  Condylar head and neck length may be increased over the opposite side.  

Right TMJ shows somewhat of a decreased vertical joint space although it may be slightly better than the opposite side.  A little bit of a hockey stick shape to this condyle.  On opening, there is restricted translation but clinically she has excellent translation of the joints.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has a skeletal Class III occlusal relationship.  There is a vertical discrepancy at the inferior border of the mandible.  Oropharyngeal airway looks very good.  She has a low occlusal plane angle brachiocephalic facial morphology.  There is a Bolton's discrepancy of –1 mm. 

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
Left TMJ osteoma versus osteochondroma.
Mandibular deviated prognathism and asymmetry.
Maxillary asymmetry.
Right-sided crossbite and anterior crossbite.
Transverse cant in the occlusion with the left side being 3 mm lower than the right side.
Class III occlusion on the left side, Class I occlusion on the right side.
Posterior yaw in the occlusion with the left side being 1 to 2 mm more lateral compared to the right side.
Nasal asymmetry.
Headaches.
 TMJ pain.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Consider bone scan of left and right TMJs.
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch. 
Align and level mandibular arch. 




STEPHANIE SMITH				07/11/2007				Page 3

Surgery.
Left TMJ condylectomy and articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to shift the mandible back onto the facial midline. 
Maxillary osteotomies to transversely level and set back on the facial midline.
External rhinoplasty and align the nose with the rest of the facial structures. 
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

In evaluating photographs from two years ago, her occlusion appears to have worsened particularly on the left side compatible with a slow but progressive growth of the left condylar element.  She has seen Dr. James more for consultation in 2005.  She also has seen Dr. Odell Marshall for orthognathic surgical consultation.  Dr. Marshall suggested doing double jaw surgery to correct the bite problem.  He did not address any TMJ issues.

original_note:
B188
B188 is 50 years old. She is referred by Dr. XXXX. B188 has had previous surgery for a hysterectomy, tonsillectomy, wisdom teeth, eye tumor, broken nose surgery, and melanoma removal. She takes the following medications:

Aspirin. Levothroid. Estrogen.
Progesterone. Vitamin and mineral supplements.
Occasional Ambien. Occasional Skelaxin. Occasional Fioricet. Occasional migraine medication.
Occasional Feldene.
Zomig nasal spray.
Maxalt.

She is allergic to IV iodine (anaphylaxis reaction), Demerol and codeine (vomiting), and sulfa (rash). Primary concerns that B188 has includes the following:

Clicking and popping in the jaws. Left-sided joint pain. Left-sided headaches.
Left-sided neck and shoulder pain. Asymmetry of the jaws.
Jaw joints are asymmetric.

B188 has had several traumatic injuries. When she was teenager, she fell off a roof and struck her face on a trampoline fracturing her nose and bruising her face. Again, when she was in high school, she got hit in the face with a softball, although she did not seek medical help. Fourteen years ago, she was hit as a pedestrian by a car that fractured her pelvis as well as caused some facial injury and no apparent facial fractures, although she did have a concussion and a whiplash injury.

She has had previous orthodontics at the age of 13. She wore splints in her 20s and then again in her early 30s, but neither treatments were of any help. She had physical therapy also in her late 20s. She has no problems with earaches and no tinnitus, but gets occasional vertigo. Other joints that bother her include her left hip and occasionally left shoulder and wrists. She is not aware of clenching and bruxism.

She rates her headaches at a level of 9 in the frontal, temporal, and posterior head area on the left side predominantly. Her headaches may be fairly constant, and she frequently wakes up when the headaches in the morning, but they can progress during the day. She rates her jaw function at 8, diet at 2, disability at 7. When her pain gets real severe, she will go and get injections done predominantly in the muscles of the left neck and shoulder area. She has difficulties breathing through her nose that is moderate and she tends to breathe through her mouth when she sleeps and then sometimes during the day as well. She is aware of some snoring factors, moderate daytime tiredness, but no overt sleep apnea. She does have a mild sleep disorder.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
: In frontal view, B188 has fairly reasonable symmetry in the middle and upper third face. There is some mild asymmetry in the dorsum of the nose and also in the alar domes where the left dome is higher than the right side and perhaps a little more prominent. The left dome is more narrow in the mediolateral width as compared to the right dome. There is a curvature of the nasal dorsum off towards the right side and then back towards the left side. Upper lip length is about 23 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is about zero. The maxillary dental midline is pretty much on the facial midline. The mandibular dental midline also fairly close to being on the facial midline about 6 to 7 mm. In profile view, the nasal tip has greater projection than the dorsum area. The domes are quite prominent and the skin is very thin over the alar domes. She is slightly retruded in the subnasale about 2 to 3 mm. The upper lip itself at the vermilion area is slightly prominent. Chin neck distance is quite short, although the chin neck angle is reasonably well defined. The mandible and chin appear to be significantly retruded. The chin may be retruded by approximately 12 mm. Intraorally, B188 has all of her lower teeth back through the third molars. She has some significant crowding of the lower anterior arch of probably 6 mm. The posterior teeth on the left side are at significant lingual inclination. She has a lingual tori of moderate size on the left side. In the upper arch, B188 has all of her teeth back through the second molars present. She has a Class I cuspid molar relationship on the right side as well as on the left side. She has an anterior deep bite of about 4 mm. B188 has a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the left side being 3 mm higher than the right side. Oropharyngeal tissues look normal and healthy. Intranasally, at least anteriorly, the turbinates appear to be only mildly enlarged. I cannot see posteriorly very well. The septum appears reasonably straight at least anteriorly. Maximal opening is 49 mm but only about 23 mm without pain. Excursion movement to the left 9 mm and to the right 7 mm, but painful. As she opens, she deviates to the left side approximately 3 mm. To palpation, she has high moderate tenderness over the masseters, retromandibular areas, and left TMJ and moderate tenderness over the SCMs and medial pterygoids. On opening, she has a click in the right side at about 20 mm and a closing click at 10 mm. The left side functioned reasonably smoothly, although she says at times she does hear noises in that joint as well.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. Sinuses appear relatively clear. Right condyle looks reasonably normal whereas the left one appears quite arthritic with significant loss of volume and shortening of the condylar head and neck area. Waters view shows that the nasal septum is reasonably straight. The right turbinate appears quite large and may be blocking the right nasal airway. The left side appears more open. Sinuses appear relatively clear. Asymmetry in the mandible can be seen with the left angle of the mandible being almost 1 cm higher than on the right side. Right TMJ tomogram shows that the morphology of the condyle appears fairly normal. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Joint space appears fairly well balanced.
Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is severely atrophic with loss of vertical and anteroposterior volume. There is a decreased posterior and to a lesser extent superior joint space. Articular eminence is moderately steep. On opening, there is excellent translation of the right condyle and some restricted translation of the left condyle. The joint space significantly decreases in translation on the left side. Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that the maxilla and mandible are moderately retruded. Vertical height of the mandible on the left side is about almost 9 mm short of vertically than the right side. The oropharyngeal airway is significantly decreased with an airway dimension of only about 5 mm at the soft palate level and at about 5 mm at the base of the tongue as well. This may account for some of the airway factors that B188 is concerned with. At the occlusal plane level, there appears to be about a 5 to 6 mm vertical difference at the molar area with the left side vertically shorter than the right side. B188 has significant difficulties swallowing and this has been going on since her TMJ problems developed. She cannot swallow tablets and has to chew them or crush them in order to get them down. B188 has had Restylane injections to the upper lip and has made it more full. This was 2 years ago and has not resolved away as much as expected.

Basic diagnoses would be as follows:
Left TMJ severe arthritis and articular disc dislocation without reduction and severe deformation. Right TMJ articular disc dislocation with early reduction. Slight AP maxillary hypoplasia.
Posterior vertical maxillary hypoplasia and asymmetry.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia and asymmetry.
Class I malocclusion. Transverse cant in the arches with the left side being 3 mm vertically short compared to the right side. Crowding in the lower anterior arch. Lingual inclination of the mandibular left posterior teeth and buccal inclination (increased curve of Wilson) in the left maxilla. Severe left TMJ pain. Severe headaches. Myofascial pain. Significantly decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Difficulties swallowing. Hypertrophied right turbinate. Partial nasal airway obstruction. External nasal deformities secondary to previous nasal trauma (x2).
Anterior deep bite of 4 mm.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
Presurgical orthodontics. Align and level maxillary arch. Align and level mandibular arch. Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing about 1.5 mm of tooth structure to correct for the tooth size discrepancy. Try to upright the posterior teeth on the left side decreasing the accentuated curve of Wilson.
CT scan of TMs and jaws (TMJ Concepts procotol).
Surgery.
Left TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. Left TMJ fat graft (harvest from the abdomen).
Left coronoidotomy.
Right TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor.
Right mandibular sagittal split osteotomy.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to transversely level the arch and advance both the maxilla and mandibular in a counterclockwise direction. Right inferior turbinectomy.
4. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

I did give her a prescription for Klonopin 1-mg tablets, dispensing 30 tablets, 1 tablet q.h.s., refill x5.
B188 MRI AGE: 50/F
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is quite small in mediolateral width. It appears to have undergone significant degenerative changes with loss of volume. Joint space is significantly decreased vertically. There are degenerative changes in the fossa as well. Lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly probably about 3 to 4 mm. Lateral rim is somewhat abnormal in shape.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width. There appears to be decreased joint space vertically particularly at the lateral aspect of the joint. Lateral rim of fossa curves inferiorly probably about 2 to 3 mm.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is quite small in size with evidence of both significant condylar resorption vertically as well as anteroposteriorly. The condyle is quite small compared to the size of the fossa. Articular eminence is of moderate inclination. Articular disc is anteriorly displaced and sitting for the most part at the posterior aspect of the inferior portion of the articular eminence. The disc is quite thickened particularly towards the lateral aspect of the joint. More medially, the disc has a thinner confirmation of probable questionable salvageability. The disc is short anteroposteriorly. There is a little bit of anterior beaking present. There may be some slight fluid in the superior joint space. On opening, there is fairly good translation of the condyle, but the articular disc remains anteriorly displaced and severely degenerated and deformed.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology. A little bit of flattening on the anterior head, but not too significant. Articular eminence has moderate inclination. The articular disc appears to be in relatively normal position except at the lateral aspect of the joint the disc is not seen. More medially, the disc is in good position. It is quite possible this disc could be medially displaced and perhaps anteriorly displaced at the most lateral aspect of the joint. On opening, there is good translation of the condyle. Towards the most lateral aspect of the joint, the disc may be slightly anteriorly displaced. The disc has good morphology.

IMPRESSION:
Left TMJ severe arthritis and articular disc anteriorly dislocated without reduction and severe deformation. Right TMJ very mild arthritis and articular disc may be slightly anteriorly displaced at the most lateral aspect of the joint and also may be slightly medially displaced.

original_note:
B080
B080 is 16 years old and was referred by Dr. XXXX. He was accompanied by his father. He believes his jaw problems began when he was 6 to 7 years old.

B080's primary concerns are as follows:
Facial asymmetry that is getting progressively worse.
Underdevelopment of lower face and chin.
Teeth do not fit together.
Jaw joints pop.
Daytime tiredness.
Predominantly a mouth breather because of difficulties breathing through the nose.

B080 has no problems with headaches, TMJ pain or myofascial pain. He rates his jaw function at 3, diet at 0, disability at 1. He has no problems with earaches, gets occasional tinnitus, but no vertigo or hearing loss. His knees bother him occasionally. He may clench or brux some at night time. He did have previous orthodontics from the age of 12 to 14-1/2 by Dr. XXXX. There were 4 permanent teeth extracted.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
In frontal view, B080 has fairly good facial balance in the middle and upper third face. He has asymmetry in the lower third face. Upper lip length is about 22 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is about 5 mm on the left side, 4 mm on the right side. The nasal tip is deviated a little bit towards the right side about 3 mm. The maxillary midline is off to the right side probably 2 mm. The mandibular dental midline is probably about 3 to 4 mm off to the right side. The chin is about 6 mm off to the right side. In profile, he has some prominence of the nasal dorsum. Upper lip supranasale is posterior to subnasale by probably 2 to 3 mm. The maxilla is probably AP deficient by at least 6-8 mm, and the mandible is retruded by about 18-20 mm. The chin neckline is extremely short and chin neckline is moderately well defined. He has eversion of the lower lip. He has a lip incompetence of 7 mm. Intraorally, B080 is missing all four second bicuspids. He has the rest of his teeth. He has some mild crowding of the lower anterior arch. He has a Class II cuspid relationship on the right side by probably 4 to 5 mm and on the left side he is a little bit closer to Class I, although probably still about 2 mm Class II. He has about a 3 mm overjet. He has about a 2 mm vertical overbite relationship. Oropharyngeal tissues look normal and healthy. B080 has a transverse cant to the occlusal plane being up about 3 mm on the right side. To palpation, he has some very mild tenderness over the retromandibular areas. On opening, he has a soft click at about 10 mm on the left side and a closing click at 5 mm. On the right side, he has got a much more obvious click at about 20 mm of opening and reciprocal click at 15 mm of opening. The maximal incisal opening is 47 mm, excursion movement to the right 8 mm, to the left 11 mm.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the presence of all 4 third molars that are impacted and unerupted. Turbinates appear moderately enlarged. Sinuses appear relatively clear. It is difficult to evaluate the joints.

Coronal view shows that turbinates are fairly large and probably cause some airway obstruction. The sinuses appear relatively clear. The septum appears reasonably straight.

Axial view shows that the sinuses are clear. There is a septal extension into the right maxillary sinus. The septum appears reasonably straight. The turbinates are moderately enlarged and may interfere with the functional nasal airway. Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condylar head and neck that appear elongated and somewhat longer than the opposite side. The condyle has some mild anterior beaking present. The condyle appears to be posteriorly positioned in the fossa with an increased anterior joint space and decreased vertical joint space. The articular eminence has a moderate inclination. The condyles appears somewhat narrow anteroposteriorly.

Right TMJ has some anterior beaking present. The condyle also appears to be somewhat posteriorly positioned and there may be some decreased vertical joint dimension. The articular eminence is moderately shallow. The condyles appears somewhat narrow anteroposteriorly.

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears larger in vertical dimension compared to the opposite side. The condyle appears to have moderate width. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 to 4 mm. There appears to be decreased vertical and lateral joint space.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears a little bit wider in width than the opposite side, but the condyle is shorter vertically. There is decreased vertical joint space. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 mm. The joint space appears decreased.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that B080 has a retruded maxilla and mandible and high occlusal plane angle with a Class II skeletal and occlusal relationship. The oropharyngeal airway measures about 8 mm at the soft palate level and 7 mm at the base of the tongue. Increased fullness on the left side of his face in the mandibular angle ramus area by about 4 to 5 mm. He has a yaw in the posterior maxilla and mandible being shifted towards the left side 2 to 3 mm.

Basic diagnoses for B080 would be as follows:
Left TMJ condylar hyperplasia.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc dislocation. Mandibular AP hypoplasia and asymmetry.
Maxillary AP and posterior vertical hypoplasia but anterior vertical excess as well as asymmetry. Class II end-on occlusion.
Right posterior cross bite. Missing all 4 second bicuspids extracted previously for orthodontic reasons.
Impacted third molars x4. Hypertrophied turbinates with nasal airway obstruction. Decreased oropharyngeal airway.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1. MRI of left and right TMJ.
2. Presurgical orthodontics. a.	Align and level maxillary arch. Tip lateral incisor roots slightly mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. b.	Align and level mandibular arch. Consider slenderizing the lower teeth removing 1 and no more than 2 mm of tooth structure to help correct for the slight tooth size discrepancy.
3. Surgery. a.	Left mandibular high condylectomy. b.	Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors. c.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance it in a counterclockwise direction and level it transversely. d.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction and correct the asymmetry. e.	Surgical removal of impacted third molars x4. f.	Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies.
4. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B080 MRI
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is quite large in size. The disc appears to be anteriorly displaced. The condyle extends almost beyond the lateral rim of the fossa. The lateral rim is fairly flattened. There is decreased superior joint space.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is a little bit smaller than the opposite side. There is decreased vertical joint space. The disc appears to be anteriorly and perhaps laterally displaced.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condylar head and neck that is excessively elongated. The articular disc is anteriorly displaced and sitting down along the posterior aspect of the articular eminence. There is some fluid in the superior joint space. There is good translation of the condyle and early reduction of the condyle onto the disc.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that it is a little bit arthritic on this side probably from joint overloading. There is a little bit of anterior beaking and a little bit of flattening on top of the condyle. The articular disc is anteriorly displaced and sitting along the posterior aspect of the articular eminence. There is a little bit of fluid at the lateral aspect in the superior joint space beneath the articular eminence. Open view shows that there is good translation of the condyle and the articular disc with the disc reducing early in opening.

IMPRESSION: Left TMJ enlarged condylar head and neck with anteriorly displacement of the articular disc with early reduction.
Left TMJ mild arthritis with articular disc anteriorly displaced with early reduction.

original_note:
STACY LEWIS									02/14/2008

Stacy is 53 years old, referred by Dr. George Adams, 2845 Parkwood Blvd., Suite 100, Plano, Texas 75093, and Dr. Samuel Marynick, 3600 Gaston Avenue, Suite 506, Dallas, Texas 75246.  Primary concerns that Stacy has includes the following:

1.  	The quality of her life has been reduced.
2.	Moderate to severe snoring.
3. 	Severe daytime tiredness.
4.	Sleep apnea.
5.	Popping in the left joint
6.	Pain in the left TMJ area.
7.	Headaches.

Stacy’s problem with significant daytime somnolence began in about 2005.  She had Invisalign orthodontic treatment beginning in 2005.  She finally had to have some lower orthodontic appliances placed and she finished her ortho treatment in June 2007.  She gets B12 shots bimonthly.  She gets headaches at about a level of 4 in the temporal areas and left frontal areas.  She gets headaches about two to three times per week.  She gets migraines once in a while as well.  She rates her TMJ pain at 3, average daily pain at 2, jaw function at 1, diet at 0, disability at 4.  Her disability is predominantly related to her extreme daytime somnolence.  She has no problems with earaches, gets some mild tinnitus, but no vertigo.  Other joints that bother her include her shoulders and she gets an occasional costochondritis.  She has had a weight gain of 35 pounds in the past two years and she attributes this in part to going through menopause.  She is aware of bruxing her teeth at night.  She has been diagnosed as having pernicious anemia.  

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Stacy has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is 24 mm.  Lip length is 0 to 1.  Lower dental midline and chin midline are a little bit off to the right side about 1 mm.  In profile view, she has fairly good balance between the upper lips and cheeks and nose.  Chin may be a little bit retruded and chin neckline is somewhat short.  Chin may be about 8 mm AP deficient.  Chin neck angle is not well-defined.  As Stacy postures her mandible forward, it improves her profile and her chin neckline.  She may be just slightly AP deficient in the maxilla by about 2-3 mm.  Intraorally, she has all of her teeth back to the second molars.  She has some mild crenations in her tongue.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back to the second molars.  She has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship on the right side, but Class I on the left side.  She has an anterior deep bite of about 4 mm.  Intraorally, the uvula looks relatively normal as does the soft palate, although they are both slightly elongated.  Faucial pillars are mild to moderately constricted.  Septum is deviated a little bit off to the right side.  Turbinates are moderate in size.  There may be some mild airway obstruction, but not too significant.  TMJs seem to function relatively smoothly.  To palpation, she has some mild tenderness over the styloid areas and minimal tenderness over the SCMs.  Maximum size opening is 44 mm.  Excursion movements are about 10 mm in each direction.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars.  She has root canals on the maxilla right second bicuspid and first molar and left second bicuspid.  She appears to have crowns on almost all of her posterior teeth in the maxilla.  Sinuses are difficult to evaluate.  Condyles do not look too abnormal.  Waters view shows deviation of the bony nasal septum with it going off to the right side slightly.  Turbinates appear moderate in size.  Sinuses look relatively clear.  Right TMJ tomogram shows some sclerosis on top of the condyle and anterior beaking present.  Decreased posterior joint space.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  The articular disc is somewhat increased.  Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is a little bit arthritic and appears to have a little bit of anterior beaking present.  Decreased superior and perhaps posterior joint space.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  On opening, there is good translation of both joints.  

The basic diagnoses for Stacy would be as follows:

1.	Left TMJ arthritis and medial displacement of the articular disc. 
2.	Right TMJ arthritis and lateral displacement of the articular disc.
3.	AP maxillary deficiency.  
4.	Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
5.	AP mandibular deficiency.
6.	Decreased oropharyngeal airway.
7.	TMJ pain.
8.	Headaches.
9.	Sleep apnea syndrome.
10.	Severe daytime somnolence.
11.	Class I occlusion on the left, Class II nonocclusion on the left.  
12.	Mandible deviated off towards the right side about 2 mm.
13. 	Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of probably 2-3 mm.
14.	High occlusal pain angle. 
	15.	Hyperplastic soft palate. 

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

	1.  	Presurgical orthodontics.
a.  Align and level maxillary arch.  
b.  Align and level mandibular arch.  Slenderize lower anterior teeth removing 2 mm of tooth structure and retract.
	2.  	Surgery.
a.  Multiple maxillary osteotomies to down graft it posteriorly and advance the incisal edges about 3-4 mm.  
b.  Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction approximately 9 mm.
c.  Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
d.  UPPP procedure with lateral inversion flaps.
3.  	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B128
B128 is 22 years old, referred by XXXX.
PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Difficulties eating and chewing.
Bites the inside of the cheeks.
Facial imbalance. Predominately mouth breather particularly when sleeping.

In frontal view, B128 has fairly good symmetry in the middle and upper third face. Upper lip length is about 22 mm. Upper tooth to lip relationship is about 0. Maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline or maybe just about 1 mm to the left. Maxillary mandibular dental midline is pretty much on the facial midline. The maxillary midline is about 1 mm to the left. Chin is fairly well aligned on the facial midline. The right side of the mandible appears to be elongated by probably about 3 or 4 mm. In profile view he has some sallowness in the infraorbital area. Perhaps some slight AP maxillary deficiency but not too significant. Chin actually appears a little bit retruded but lower lip is prominent and everted. Chin is retruded about 4 or 5 mm. Chin-neckline may be a little bit short and chin-neck angle has a soft curvature to it. Intraorally, B128 has all of his lower teeth back through the second molars. The teeth appear to be fairly well aligned and leveled. In the upper arch he has all of his teeth back through the second molars. He has an accentuated curve of Wilson in the upper jaw. He has a full Class III cuspid molar relationship bilaterally. In fact, just a little bit of a super Class III on the left side. He has an open bite of about 2 mm. Anterior 8 to 9 teeth in the mandible are completely out of occlusion. Transversely, the occlusal plane appears relatively level in the maxilla but the mandible may be down about 1 mm on the right side. Nasal tip appears to be deviated off to the right side about 2 mm. On evaluation it appears that the maxillary midline may be on the facial midline and it may be the mandible that is a little bit off to the right side in both the dental midline and chin. He does have an anterior crossbite with reverse overjet of about 8 or 9 mm. Maxilla has been expanded posteriorly so that the molar area there is not a crossbite. Intranasally, the septum appears deviated a little bit off towards the right side. The right turbinate appears moderately enlarged and may be encroaching on the airway a little bit. The left turbinate appears more normal in size and it appears that there may be an adequate airway on that side. B128 has quite large tonsils but states he does not have problems with sore throats or tonsillitis. The nasoseptum is deviated a little bit off to the right side and the right turbinate is moderately enlarged, left one is relatively normal. Columella is a little bit wide. No history of snoring, daytime somnolence or sleep apnea. To palpation, B128 has no tenderness around the head and neck area. He definitely has audible clicking and popping in the left TMJ at about 20 mm of opening and 15 mm of closing. The right side seems relatively smooth except B128 states that it does click and pop at times. B128 rates his TMJ pain at 0, headaches at 0, pain around the head and neck area at 0, jaw function at 1, diet at 0, disability at 4. B128 has had orthodontic appliances on for about a year and 9 months and he has had some maxillary expansion. When B128 was about 13 or 14 years of age, his bite was much better aligned than it currently is. When he went through his pubertal growth spurt, the lower jaw did grow excessively then he felt it sort of stabilized before he began the orthodontic treatment. The orthodontics has made the bite worse. B128 had his wisdom teeth removed over the Christmas holiday.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
:
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars but all the rest of the teeth are present. The left sinus appears a little bit more cloudy than the right sinus. Condylar heads and necks appear elongated.

Waters view shows that he has a significant deviation of the nasal septum with a large spur back in the bony part of the septum extending into the right side of the nose. On the left side he has a large bony spur also extending out in the left side of the nose blocking off the majority of the airway there. Right turbinate is extremely large as are the middle turbinates particularly on the right side. There is a little bit of cloudiness in the left maxillary sinus but the other sinuses all appear clear.

Tomographic evaluation of the right TMJ shows a condyle head and neck that is quite elongated. Articular eminence is moderate inclination. There appears there may be adequate joint space although the joint space is narrow indicative of either disc displacement or a thin disc.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears to have some mild changes on the top. Decreased superior and posterior joint space. Articular eminence appears a little bit more shallow on this side than the opposite side. On opening, there is good translation of both condyles.

Lateral cephalogram shows that B128 has a high occlusal plane angle with an anterior open bite. There is a skeletal Class III relationship but a greater Class III occlusal relationship. Chin appears weak. Oropharyngeal airway is about 6 mm at the soft palate level and about 13 mm at the base of the tongue. Dental model analysis shows that B128 has small upper lateral incisors creating about a 3 mm or more tooth size discrepancy. In the Class I position, the maxilla is excessively wide compared to the width of the mandible and he actually goes into a total buccal crossbite with the first and second molars. The teeth are distorted on the model so tooth size analysis cannot be performed. Transverse width of the lower arch appears somewhat narrow.

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
Bilateral condylar hyperplasia (probably arrested but further evaluation is required.)
Left TMJ articular disc dislocation with reduction. AP maxillary deficiency.
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
Transverse maxillary excess. Mandibular prognathism.
Anterior open bite.
Class III occlusion. Deviated nasal septum with septal spurring to both the left and right sides.
Hypertrophied turbinate on the right side. Nasal airway obstruction.
Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of about 2 to 3 mm.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
MRI of left and right TMJ. Presurgical orthodontics.
Continue to align and level maxillary arch. It would actually be helpful to let the upper jaw shift back into a posterior crossbite relation because in the Class I position the maxilla is excessively wide (however, this can be addressed surgically as well.)
Continue to align and level mandibular arch. Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing at least 2 mm or perhaps 3 mm of tooth structure to help correct for the tooth size discrepancy.
Surgery.
Left TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla, decrease the occlusal plane angle and transversely narrow the maxilla.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to counterclockwise rotate the mandible into proper occlusal position. Nasoseptoplasty.
Partial right turbinectomy. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B056
B056 is referred by XXXX. B056 is 60 years old. Her problems began about nine years ago at the age of 53. Her dentist told her that she needed some spacing closed or she would have difficulties in the future. This started a major downhill slide leaving B056 severely compromised. She went from having a relatively normal occlusion to where she has just some retained roots of the maxillary first and second molars bilaterally with two remaining implants in the upper jaw and missing mandibular left central incisor and left first molar. All four third molars are absent. At one point, there were ten implants placed in the upper jaw and eight of them have now been removed with only two remaining. B056 has very methodically outlined her treatment to date.

B056 rates her headaches at about a level of 8 in the frontal and temporal area bilaterally. She rates her TMJ pain at 8, average daily pain at 8, jaw function at 8, diet at 5, disability at 9. She gets earaches on the left side occasionally, but has continuous tinnitus. She gets occasional vertigo and has hearing loss in the left ear. She has left leg stress fractures and also this involves the left side of her foot. She has right shoulder problems as well as hip problems. She has chronic left side fatigue. She is aware of clenching her teeth during the day, but not aware as to whether she does it at night.

To palpation, she has high moderate pain over the TMJs and moderate pain over the masseters, posterior neck, and mild discomfort over the shoulders. On opening she has a click on both sides with the right side being somewhat softer than the left side. On the left side, the opening click is at about 20 mm and a closing click at 15 mm. On the right side, the opening click is probably a little bit earlier at 15 with a closing click at about 10. She takes the following medications:

ASA 81 mg q.d.
Multiple vitamin supplements.
He diagnoses list would include the following:
Bilateral TMJ mild arthritis and articular disc dislocations.
Absence of all maxillary teeth except for the first and second molars bilaterally.
Missing mandibular left central incisor and left first molar.
Lingual inclination of lower incisors.
Maxillary alveolar ridge atrophy.
Two osteointegrated implants in the maxilla with questionable stability.
TMJ pain.
Headaches.
Myofascial pain.
Clenching.

I had Dr. XXXX come and evaluate B056 with me, and her treatment plan at this point in time would be as follows:
Treatment of clenching with Klonopin 1-mg tablets 1 tablet q.h.s. Consultation with Dr. XXXX.
Orthodontics on the lower arch to align and level the lower arch, opening up space for the missing left central incisor as well as space around the deformed left cuspid. This will tip the lower incisors forward into a better position over basal bone. Surgery Stage 1: Bone grafting to maxilla including sinus lift procedures as well as augmenting anteriorly and vertically on the alveolar ridge. Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Osteointegrated dental implant placement to include four or more implants. The current maxillary implants will likely require removal.
Surgery Stage 2: Placement of interosseus implants. Restorative dentistry to reconstruct the arches.

B056 MRI AGE: 60/F
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears a little arthritic. Disc appears to be medially displaced. The condyle appears to have good mediolateral width. Joint space looks fairly good throughout however. Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with fairly good mediolateral width. The disc appears to be anteriorly and laterally displaced.
Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has flattening on the posterior head. The disc appears to be in reasonably good position. At the most lateral aspect of the joint, the disc may be anteriorly displaced. On opening, there appears to be good translation of the condyle and the disc bilaterally.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has some flattening on the posterior head. It appears that the disc is in position, but it does appear a little bit degenerated. There appears to be a little bit of grayish kind tissue surrounding the disc.

IMPRESSION: Bilateral TMJ mild arthritis and articular discs in reasonably good position, although the left one may be anteriorly displaced at the lateral aspect of the joint and the right one may be laterally displaced.

original_note:
B216
B216 is 25 years old.
Patient Concerns
1. Neck and shoulder pain
2. Headaches
Patient History
B216's problems began about 1-1/2 years ago related to an MVA. Someone ran into the side of her car. She was a passenger, and the other automobile hit on the passenger side and rolled the car over 3/4 of the way. The day after the accident, she had rather severe head and neck pain. She has had CAT scans and MRIs of the neck and head area. She was told that in her neck she had some discs that were swollen some, but that the bone structures were in good condition. They did a brain CAT scan, and it was negative. She has had some extensive chiropractic and physical therapy treatment for the past 1-1/2 years. For the first year, she had treatments three days per week. Now she gets it when the pain gets bad, and that is about once a month. She has been under orthodontic treatment for the past two months with Dr. XXXX. B216 has had previous orthodontics when she was about 10 years old for about 9 months.

She has no earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, or hearing loss. Other joints that bother her include her left shoulder and her hips occasionally. Has seasonal allergies. There are SOME ANTI-INFLAMMATORY MEDICATIONS that she can have a reaction to. She is not aware of having any problems wearing jewelry. She gets headaches at about a level of 4 daily in the frontal and temporal areas bilaterally. However, these headaches can get up to a 6 or a 7. She rates her TMJ pain at 0, average daily pain at 4, jaw function and diet at 3, and disability at 5.

Clinical Examination
In frontal view, B216 has good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 19 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 6.5 mm. She has lip incompetence of almost 9 mm.

In profile view, B216 has some slight AP maxillary deficiency of probably about 2 to 3 mm. She is probably about 10 mm AP deficient. Chin-neckline is a little bit short, although the chin-neck angle is reasonably well-defined.

Intraorally, B216 has all her lower teeth back through the second molars. Teeth are fairly well aligned . Adequate attached gingiva lower anterior arch although a little bit thin in the cuspid area. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. She is a little bit Class II end-on cuspid relationship bilaterally. She has small upper lateral incisors and probably has a tooth-size discrepancy that accounts for the drift in the occlusion. She is about 2 mm Class II. She has probably about 2 to 3 mm of vertical overlap. Transversely, she has a slight cant in the occlusal plane with about 0.5 mm vertical difference with the right side being lower than the left side. Oropharynx appears clear. Uvula, soft palate, and faucial pillars appear relatively normal.

Intranasally, the septum is deviated off toward the right side. Turbinates are moderately enlarged with fairly good obstruction in the right side. The left side, although the turbinate is enlarged, does not appear to be as obstructive.

To palpation, she has moderate pain over the styloid area and mild pain over the TMJS and masseter muscles. Maximal incisal opening is 48 mm, but she could open to 38 without discomfort. Excursive movements are about 9 mm in each direction. Joints actually function reasonably smoothly.

Radiographic Evaluation
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars with all the other teeth present. Right turbinate appears enlarged. Sinuses appear relatively clear. Condyles appears a little bit arthritic with some flattening on the anterior head of the condyles.

Waters view shows that the sinuses are clear. She has huge frontal sinuses. Nasal septum appears relatively straight, but this is the bony septum and not the cartilaginous which clinically is deviated. Right turbinate is enlarged whereas the left appears more normal in size.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has a little bit of flattening on the superior aspect; otherwise it looks reasonably good. Articular eminence is relatively shallow. Superior and posterior joint space may be slightly decreased but not a lot.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has a little bit of flattening on the superior head. Could have decreased superior and posterior joint space. Articular eminence is moderately shallow.

On opening, there is fairly good translation of both condyles with the left one being a little bit greater than the right one.
Left cephalometric radiograph shows there is good oropharyngeal airway. She has an anterior open bite. Appears to have vertical maxillary excess. Mandible also appears long vertically.

Basic Diagnoses
1. Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with early reduction (pre-click reduction)
2. Vertical maxillary excess
3. AP mandibular deficiency
4. AP microgenia
5. Anterior mandibular vertical excess
6. Class II end-on occlusion
7. Tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2 to 3 mm secondary to small upper lateral incisors
8. High occlusal plane angle
9. Overangulation of lower incisors
10. Hypertrophied turbinates
11. Nasal septal deviation
12. Nasal airway obstruction
13. Headaches
14. TMJ pain
15. Myofascial pain

Recommended Treatment
1. Presurgical orthodontics A. Continue to align and level maxillary arch B. Continue to align and level mandibular arch. Strip lower anterior teeth removing about 2 mm of tooth structure and close spaces
2. Surgery A. Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors B. Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the maxilla superiorly C. Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction D. Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies E. Nasal septoplasty F. Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy to advance and vertically shorten the chin
3. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

B216
Meeting Notes
:
Date of Acquisition: Date of Reading:
MEETING WITH DR. XXXX:
Left TMJ, coronal view, shows a fairly wide condyle. Could be a little bit of decreased superior joint space. Lateral rim of the fossa is fairly flat.

Right TMJ, coronal view, shows a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width. Decreased joint space vertically at the lateral aspect of the joint. The disc bilaterally may be anteriorly and medially displaced.

Left TMJ, sagittal view, shows the disc is not readily seen at the lateral aspect of the joint. Appears to be a little bit anteriorly displaced. There is a little flattening on the anterior head of the condyle and some anterior beaking present. A little osteophyte extending superiorly off the head of the condyle. The disc is a little bit better positioned toward the medial aspect of the joint. No significant fluid seen in the joint space.

On opening, there is good translation of the condyle and the disc with early reduction of the disc.
Right TMJ, sagittal view, shows some anterior beaking and a little osteophyte off the superior head of the condyle. Disc is anteriorly displaced and not seen very well at the lateral aspect of the joint. Some flattening on the anterior head of the condyle. Articular eminence is moderately steep. No particular fluid seen in the joint space.

On opening, there is early reduction and good translation of the condyle and the articular disc.
Impression:
1. Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with early reduction
2. Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with early reduction

B216
MRI Scan Evaluation
Date of Acquisition:
Date of Reading:
Left TMJ, coronal view, shows a condyle with fairly good mediolateral width. There appear to be some degenerative changes on the superior surface of the condyle. There may have been some previous resorption. Lateral rim of the fossa is quite flat with very minimal downward curvature. Lateral pole of the condyle actually extends out to maybe just slightly beyond the lateral rim of the fossa. Appears to be discal tissue anterior to the head of the condyle. Superior joint space appears decreased.

Right TMJ, coronal view, shows a condyle with good mediolateral width. Appear to be less arthritic changes on the superior surface as compared to the opposite side. Fossa is fairly flat with very minimal downward curvature of the lateral rim of the fossa. Decreased superior joint space. Appears to be discal tissue anterior to the condylar head.

Left TMJ, sagittal view, shows a condyle that appears to be a little bit postured forward. Some flattening on the anterior head. A little bit of anterior beaking present. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Articular disc appears to be anteriorly displaced. There may be some compressed tissue up in the depth of the fossa or perhaps some discal tissue there. The condyle appears to be functioning on the bilaminar tissues posterior to the disc. Some arthritic changes on the superior surface of the condyle. A little bit of fluid in the superior and inferior joint space but not too significantly. Disc appears to have fairly good morphology.

On opening, there is good translation of the condyle, and the articular disc does reduce and appears to translate fairly well with the condyle.
Right TMJ, sagittal view, shows a condyle that has flattening on the superior head. A little bit of anterior beaking is present, but it doesn't appear to be as significant as the opposite side. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Articular disc appears to be anteriorly displaced. Condylar head appears to be functioning on the bilaminar tissues. Disc appears to have fairly good morphology. Could be a little bit of fluid in the inferior joint space but not much.

On opening, there is good translation of the condyle, and the disc is reduced and appears to translate well with the condyle.
Impression:
1. Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with early reduction
2. Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with probable early reduction
3. Both discs are salvageable
4. Patient would probably benefit from bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors and any orthognathic procedures indicated from clinical, cephalometric, and dental model analysis

original_note:
JAMES WADE									05/20/2008

James is 7 years old.  Birth date is 10/06/2000.  Referred by Dr. Thomas Czarnecki, 754 N. Carroll Avenue, Southlake, TX 76092.  Her dentist is Dr. Sandra Armstrong, 2915 E. Southlake Blvd., #200, Southlake, TX 76092.  James with born with unilateral cleft lip and dental alveolus.  Palate is intact.  He has a fairly nice loop repair performed by Dr. Ken Salyer.  James has had just one surgery to date and this was for the lip repair.  He may have had a little gum surgery done at the same time.  
CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, James has a little bit of deficiency in the mid part of the upper lip.  The scar line actually looks very nice.  Alar base on the left side is slightly wide compared to the right side and the alar cartilage is at a lower level in the tip of nose on the left side as compared to the right side.  Left nostril is more flared laterally, although the luminal valve appears constricted more so on the left side of the nose as compared to the right side.  In the midline, he is absent a tubercle and there is less vertical height of the vermilion in the midline as compared to laterally.  The vertical length of the upper lip is fairly level on both sides.  There is a slight mid-alignment at the mucocutaneous junction.  Upper lip length is about 17 mm.  Upper lip tooth relationship is about 5 mm.  In profile, he may have just some slight increased fullness in the maxilla.  The chin may be just slightly AP deficient.  Intraorally, his dental midlines are pretty much on the facial midline, but the maxillary left central incisor is rotated distally and at about 90 degrees to its proper orientation.  He has primary B’s, C’s, D’s, and E’s still remaining.  In the lower arch, he has C’s, D’s, and E’s still in position.  The lower lateral incisor on the right side is not completely erupted in yet.  He has all of his permanent teeth developing back through the second molars.  The third molars are not real evident at this time.  He has an anterior deep bite of about 4-5 mm.  He has a Class I cuspid relationship on the right side and a slight Class II on the left side by about 2-3 mm.  It is difficult to tell if he has a fistula through the alveolar cleft area, but radiographically, he has a fairly large bony defect in alveolar cleft area.  High beneath the upper lip it appears that there is an oronasal communication.  Intranasally, the very anterior aspect of the septum is displaced off to the right side, but then there is a significant deviation of the septum off to the left side that seems to block off a good part of that airway.  It towards the anterior aspect of the nose.  Intraorally, the uvula, soft palate, and fascial pillars are all look relatively normal.  Tonsils are moderate in size.  I cannot see the adenoid tissue from an intraoral view.  He likely has moderate nasal airway obstruction.  
His relatives state that he breathes through his mouth when he sleeps.  He is predominantly a mouth breather at nighttime and also during the day.  This could account for some of his anterior vertical maxillary excess.  He has no TMJ issues and no pain or headaches around the head and neck area.  He is taking an antibiotic, Cefdinir, for strep and began that last Friday night.  He currently weighs 68 pounds.  Katy Wade, James’ mother, apparently was developing lupus when she was pregnant with James.  She had blood clots in her placenta and had unexplained fevers.  She subsequently had developed some cardiac problems and has had heart surgery.  She had pericardial effusion and they put in a window apparently to allow the fluid to drain out.  On the questionnaire, they do have marked down that he has some mild snoring.  He does have significant difficulties breathing through his nose.  
RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the presence of numerous primary teeth including the maxillary B’s, C’s, D’s, and E’s and mandibular C’s, D’s, and E’s.  The first molars are in.  The second molars are unerupted yet.  He has a primary lateral incisor that is tipped over in toward the cleft area.  He has a 90 degree rotated maxillary left central incisor.  He has a fairly good-sized alveolar cleft measuring about 10 mm across.  He has a diastema between his upper central incisors.  In the lower arch, the mandibular right lateral incisor is not erupted in yet.  The condyles look relatively normal.  
Waters view shows that he has mild deviation of the bony nasal septum, but the cartilage septum is much worse.  Turbinates are moderate in size.  Sinuses look relatively clear.  Right frontal sinus is not developed.  The bony septum is deviated a little bit off towards the left side.  
Tomograms look relatively normal.  
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that James may have some slight maxillary protrusion, but vertical maxillary excess.  The mandible may be slightly AP deficient.  Oropharyngeal airway looks reasonably good except he has got a large adenoid mass in the nasopharynx with significant constricture between the nasal and oropharyngeal cavities.  He has a relatively low occlusal plane and mandibular plane angle facial morphology.  
Dental model analyses shows that he has got a Class I cuspid relationship on the right side and Class II end-on on the left side by about 3 mm. The primary left lateral incisor is still in position, but drifted over into the cleft area.  The maxillary incisors are both rotated, but the left one is significantly greater than the right side, being at almost 90 degrees to the frontal plane.  He has some space between the three lower incisors that are either erupted or partially erupted, but the right lateral incisor is not evident clinically.  The midlines are coincidental.  The right maxillary cuspid may be about 2 mm anterior to the left cuspid in the lower arch and that could be contributory to the asymmetry.  This may explain the Class I cuspid relationship on the right side and Class II end-on cuspid relationship on the left side.  Upper incisors do appear to be oriented somewhat vertical.  The chin appears just slightly AP deficient.
Basic diagnoses for James would be as follows:
Repaired maxillary left cleft lip.
Left maxillary alveolar cleft.
Missing maxillary left permanent lateral incisor.
Class I occlusion on the right side, slight Class II end-on occlusion (2-3 mm) on the left side.
Slight anterior vertical maxillary excess of 2-3 mm.
Malrotation of the maxillary incisors, particularly the left one. 
Anterior deep bite of about 4 mm.
Residual cleft lip deformity with decreased vermilion and tubercle in the midline.
External nasal deformity secondary to the cleft lip.
Nasoseptal deviation with significant blockage of the left side of the nose.
Significant hypertrophy of the nasopharyngeal adenoid tissues.
Nasal airway obstruction. 
Retained primary teeth including bilateral maxillary B’s, C’s, D’s and E’s and mandibular C’s, D’s, and E’s. 
Unerupted mandibular lateral incisor.




Recommended treatment would be as follows:	
 
Continue to monitor growth and development.  (He has excellent growth of the maxilla considering having an unilateral cleft lip and maxillary alveolus.) 
Consider some aesthetic orthodontics to correct malrotations of the maxillary central incisors.
Consider bonding on the maxillary left primary lateral incisor to make the crown larger and help make it look like a normal lateral incisor.
Monitor the development of the permanent cuspid on the left side and monitor its eruption pattern.
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch.
Align and level mandibular arch.
Surgery.  
When cuspid is approaching or threatening to erupt into the alveolar cleft area, place a bone graft in the left maxillary alveolar cleft area. 
b.  Closure of oronasal fistula (if present).
c.  Consider nasal septoplasty.
Consider cleft lip revision.
Consider nasal reconstruction. 
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
Restorative dentistry.
Placement of implant to maxillary left lateral incisor alveolus.
Placement of crown on the dental implant.

original_note:
DIANE SALARANO									06-28-04

Diane is 48 years old. 

Patient History

Diane’s problems began in 02/1988 when she had a sudden onset of TMJ problems. She was involved in an MVA in 1986, but this primarily created problems in the neck and shoulder area, and she wasn’t aware of any particular problems in the joint. She went to see Dr. John Delfino. Dr. Delfino did bilateral open joint arthroplasties in 1988. In 1989, she saw Dr. Ken Rotskoff, who did a left TMJ discectomy and placed a temporal fascial flap. In 1989, she also had a left coronoidectomy and also later she had the temple fascial graft clipped. In 02/1991, she had a left TMJ Christensen total joint prosthesis with chromium cobalt on methyl methacrylate condylar head. In 1991, she had decompression through a craniotomy of the fifth cranial nerve. 

She has had an adverse reaction to CECLOR and CEPHALOSPORINS. She also had a reaction this past week to GALLIUM she believes.

Diane has no problems with earaches but does have tinnitus but no vertigo except occasionally when she gets up. She has some hearing loss. She has difficulties wearing jewelry, particularly cheap jewelry that may contain nickel. She is going through menopause. She uses a CPAP machine although she has been told she does not have sleep apnea, but she does have a significant sleep disorder problem of unknown etiology. No one has been able to really help her with this situation to date. She gets headaches about once a month that she rates at a level of 8 that hurt predominantly in the left frontal and temporal area. She rates her TMJ pain with medications at a level of 7, overall facial pain at 4, jaw function at 3, diet at 6, and disability at 7. She is on disability and unable to work at this time.

Patient Concerns

TMJ pain
Headaches 
Pain to the left side of head and neck area particularly when sleeping
Sleep disorder with prolonged arousals at night even with medications
Ringing in the areas
Chills
Difficulties eating and chewing

Current Medications

Zoloft 50 mg q.d. 
Wellbutrin ER 100 mg
Methadone 10 mg t.i.d. and 5 mg tablet usually in the afternoon
Vioxx 25 mg at bedtime
Seroquel 25 mg at bedtime
Zyrtec 10 mg at bedtime
Zonegran 100 mg q.h.s. 
Serevent inhaler 2 inhalations b.i.d. 
Roxicet 1 tablet q.6h. p.r.n. breakthrough pain
Allergies

CECLOR, CEPHALOSPORINS, and GALLIUM. 

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Diane has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Some decreased vertical height on the left side with the left side being probably 3 mm or so higher than the right side. Upper lip length is about 21 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is 4 mm. Maxillary dental midline is a little bit off to the left. Mandibular dental midline is close to being on the facial midline, but chin is deviated a couple of millimeters off to the left. 

In profile view, she has some AP maxillary deficiency of probably 4 to 6 mm. Mandible is AP deficient by probably about 10 mm. Chin-neckline is not well-defined.

Intraorally, she is missing lower first bicuspids. Lower arch is constricted. The rest of the teeth are fairly well aligned and leveled. In the upper arch, she is also missing the first bicuspids and has some transverse deficiency in the upper arch as well. She has basically a Class I cuspid-molar relationship with an anterior deep-bite of about 4 mm. Oropharynx appears clear. Soft palate might be a little bit posteriorly positioned. Uvula is relatively normal in size and shape. Faucial pillars look relatively normal in transverse width. She has some snoring problems, but her husband is not sure how severe it is. She has severe problems with daytime somnolence. She takes Provigil 1 tablet a day. She had to take 1-½ tablets just to get to my office today. She does have problems with chills. She is on a CPAP machine. She has increased fullness of her right temporalis muscle compared to the left temporalis muscle. Even when biting together, there is more activity on the right side than the left side. 

On opening, she has significant audible crepitation in the right TMJ. On the left side, it seems fairly smooth in function. As she opens, Diane shifts significantly off toward the left side probably 6 to 8 mm. Maximal incisal opening is about 40 mm. Excursive movements to the right are 3 mm and to the left 7.5 mm.

Intranasally, turbinates appear to be maybe slightly enlarged but not too significantly. Appears to have a good nasal airway inside. Luminal valves appear somewhat constricted. When I told those valves open, she could breathe better through her nose. She does use the Breathe Right nasal strips to help open up her airway. 

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows that the sinuses are clear. Nasal airway looks reasonably clear. All four third molars and all four first bicuspids are missing. Right TMJ condyle appears flattened on the anterior aspect with a little bit of peaking on the condylar head. Left TMJ demonstrates the presence of a Christensen total joint prosthesis with an acrylic head. Seven screws support the mandibular component to the ramus. Coronoid process has been cut off, but it is still sitting there and probably joined back to the mandibular ramus area through a fibrous attachment. Difficult to tell if there is any fracture of the fossa component, but it appears from what I can see that it is probably intact.

Left TMJ tomogram shows the presence of the Christensen prosthesis. It appears that there may be some acrylic still remaining between the pin and condylar component and the fossa. Screws appear to be in position. Difficult to rule out the possibility of any fracture of the fossa component.

Right TMJ tomogram shows the condyle posteriorly positioned in the fossa with decreased posterior and superior joint space. The superior head of the condyle appears sclerotic. A little bit of anterior beaking present. Sort of a shallow prominence of the articular eminence. 

Open view shows good translation of the right condyle but minimal translation of the left condyle. 

Waters view shows that the sinuses all appear to be clear. There is a significant difference in vertical position of the angle of the mandible with the left mandibular angle being at least 10 to 12 mm elevated compared to the right side. Nasal airway looks good, although she does have a nasal septal spur back in the bony part of the nose extending out toward the right side. 

Cephalometrically, Diane has a Class I skeletal relationship. The head is tipped up somewhat. May want to retake this x-ray with the head at a more normal angulation. Occlusal plane may be somewhat elevated. Upper incisors in a vertical inclination. Oropharyngeal airway, however, looks to be fairly adequate with an airway of about 8 mm at the base of the soft palate level and at the base of the tongue about 13 mm. 

Other Systems

She has problems with her little fingers where they catch, some problems with her left hip, and left knee. In the past few years, she has had problems with urinary tract infections. She has polyuria and nocturia two to four times per night. She has had one miscarriage and has no children. She does have fibrocystic breast disease. She has had a cervical problems and has had a conization. For the past two years, she has been getting some oral yeast infections and has been attributed to an oral steroid that she was taking. She has discontinued that, and this has helped her. She has some itching associated with allergies around her eyes but nothing really significant. She gets some seasonal sinus problems. She used to smoke up to a pack a day but stopped in 1988. She has tinnitus and some hearing loss. She has had bronchitis probably about four times. She has adult-onset asthma that began at about the age of 35. It not sure if she has irritable bowel syndrome. She does have problems with constipation that she relates to the medications. She has severe fatigue. She tends to run a low-grade fever. No thyroid problems she is aware of. No really significant exposure to birds. She has had some cats and dogs most of her life. No hoofed animals.

Basic Diagnoses

Left TMJ Christensen prosthesis with a possible reactive heterotopic bone
Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation without reduction and probable perforations
AP maxillary deficiency
AP mandibular deficiency
Missing all four first bicuspids
Anterior deep bite
Transverse cant in the occlusion with the maxillary left side being 1 mm vertically short compared to the right side but the mandible being 3 mm vertically short compared to the right side
Decreased oropharyngeal airway at the soft palate level
Severe sleep disorder
Severe daytime somnolence
Decreased oropharyngeal airway at the soft palate level with a dimension of 5 mm
Frequent sleep disruption at night
Left-sided headaches
Left-sided TMJ pain
Left-sided myofascial pain
Difficulties eating and chewing

Recommended Treatment

Trial of Klonopin 1 mg tablet, disp. 10, 1 tablet q.h.s., refill x 4

CT scan of TMJs to include axial, coronal, and sagittal views

Evaluation at Environment Center to rule out hypersensitivities (i.e. materials in the prosthesis, environmental factors, molds, etc.)

Presurgical orthodontics 
Align and level maxillary arch 
Align and level mandibular arch 

Surgery
Remove left TMJ Christensen prosthesis
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest graft from abdomen)
Bilateral coronoidectomies
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance it forward and level transversely

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion 

They live in an old house. They have been there for about 15 years. Quite possibly she should have mold or yeast problems or other factors around the house that could be contributing to hypersensitivities and subsequently her pain. She says, however, she feels that when she wakes up at night it is primarily due to the fact that she can’t catch her breath more than she feels that it is related directly to pain. It is possible that she could be having some muscle spasms that may be causing some hyperfunction of the soft palate, and, thus, decreasing her functional airway. Therefore, the pain might indirectly be contributing to her sleep problems and her impression that she cannot get her breath at night when she is sleeping.

original_note:
AREN NEAL										12/01/2006

Aren takes the following medications:
Allegra.
Nuvarin (BCP.)
Tizamine.
Maxalt.

In the past she has had significant TMJ pain but currently she does not have as much pain in the joints.  She rates her headaches at a level of 8 that occur predominately in the left side in the frontal, temple and behind the eyes.  She rates her average daily pain at 8, jaw function at 4, diet at 0, disability at 2.  Aren's problems began in about 2000 when she was involved in an MVA.  She cannot remember having any appreciable problems before that with the joints.  In college she had pain in her jaw, neck and shoulders that was quite serious.  Previously she has had orthodontics beginning in the 4th grade through about the 7th or 8th. 

In frontal view Aren does demonstrate some asymmetry.  Upper lip length is about 21 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 4 mm on the left and 3 mm on the right.  The nasal tip is probably about 4 to 5 mm off towards the right side.  The left eye may be about 2 to 3 mm higher than the right eye.  Maxillary dental midline is off to the right side probably at 2 mm.  Mandibular dental midline is off to the right side probably 3 mm.  Chin is off to the right side probably about 2 mm.  At the angle of the mandible, the right side is about 8 to 10 mm lower than the left side near the chin area.  The chin on the right side is 1 to 2 mm lower than the left side.  In profile view, she has some problems with the nasal dorsum.  The upper lip may be retruded by about 3 mm and the chin may be retruded about 4 mm.  Chin-neckline actually looks good and the chin-neck angle is fairly well defined.  The lower third face could be just slightly elongated.  Intraorally, Aren has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  The teeth on the right side have all been built up fairly significantly probably to close the open bite that is established on that side.  There is some mild crowding in the lower anterior arch.  She appears to have adequate attached gingiva.  The lower arch is fairly symmetric left to right side as far as AP position of the cuspids.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  She also had the right maxillary teeth all built up vertically again to likely try to close the open bite on that side.  She is basically a Class I cuspid bilaterally.  She has an anterior open bite of about 1 mm on the left side and 2 mm on the right side.  She has transverse constricture of both the maxillary and mandibular arches.  Without the buildups in the teeth she would have probably about a 2 mm open bite in the cuspid area and probably about a 4 to 5 mm open bite in the molar area.  Intranasally, the septum may be deviated just a little off towards the right side.  The turbinates appears relatively normal in size but the transverse width of the nose is a little bit constricted and luminal valves are slightly constricted.  When I hold her nostrils open she can breathe a little bit better but did not note any significant improvement.  She is predominately a mouth breather particularly when she sleeps.  There is a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right cuspid being probably about 1 mm to 1.5 mm lower than the left side.  In the lower arch, the cuspid on the right side is 2 mm lower than the left side.  Without the buildup on the tooth it could be even slightly greater.  There is a yaw in the maxilla and mandible with the posterior aspect being about 2 mm to the left and the mandible being probably 3 mm to the left.  

AREN NEAL					12/01/2006				Page 2


The left side of the mandible at the angle area is probably 5 to 6 mm more prominent than the right side.  To palpation, she has some moderate discomfort over the styloid areas, left SCM and left posterior neck, left TMJ, bilateral shoulders and more minor discomfort over the temporalis and left maxillary.  There is a little crepitation in the right joint with the left side functioning reasonably smoothly.  Maximal incisal opening is about 60 mm but about 32 with reasonable comfort.  Excursive movement to the right 8 mm, left 7 mm.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION:
Panorex shows the absence of all 4 third molars.  There may be a little root tip or a piece of enamel left in the right side.  The sinuses look relatively clear.  The left one is a little bit more cloudy than the right one.  

Right condyle and neck appear larger and thicker than the left side.  The mandible is longer on the right side as compared to the left side.  The buildups in the teeth are evident in both the maxillary and mandibular right posterior teeth.  

Waters view shows that the bony nasal septum deviates significantly off towards the right side with a bony spur that is probably creating some significant airway obstruction on that side.  Turbinates appear quite large particularly towards the posterior aspect of the nose.  Right side is constricted but the left side is significantly constricted with probably an enlarged turbinate.  Sinuses, however, all appear clear.  

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology.  Decreased superior joint space.  Articular eminence is fairly steep.  

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is enlarged and much larger than the opposite side.  Joint space, however, seems to be fairly well balanced.  There appears to be some anterior beaking that may be present.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  On opening, there is good translation of both joints.  However, the right condyle is much lager than the left condyle.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that there are significant vertical differences from the right side compared to the left side.  The angle of the mandible at the inferior border of the right angle is 15 mm lower than the left side.  In the chin area the difference is more around 2 mm.  At the occlusal area vertical difference of the second molars is about 5 to 6 mm on the right side compared to the left side.  Cuspids are about 2 mm vertical difference.   The left angle of the mandible is anteriorly positioned compared to the right side.  

BASIC DIAGNOSES: 
Right mandibular condylar osteochondroma.
Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation.
Right vertical mandibular hyperplasia.
Right maxillary hyperplasia and asymmetry.
Significant mandibular asymmetry.
AREN NEAL					12/01/2006				Page 3


TMJ pain.
Headaches.
Myofascial pain.
Septal deviation with a septal spur.
 Bilateral inferior turbinate hyperplasia. 
 Nasal airway obstruction.
 Right mandibular open bite.  
 Transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right side being lower than the left side.
 Accentuated curve of occlusion of upper arch.
 Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2 to 3 mm.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies.   
Align and level mandibular arch.  Slenderize the lower anterior removing at least 2 mm of tooth structure to correct for significant tooth size discrepancy and to keep from flaring the lower incisors any further forward.
Remove bonding on the maxillary and mandibular teeth on the right that was placed to build the teeth up to achieve some semblance of occlusion.  
Consider slowly expanding the arches, perhaps with the Damon to try and improve arch width in both the maxillary and mandibular arches which will also aide in decreasing in the over angulation of the incisors. 
Surgery.
Right TMJ condylectomy and articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor.
Left TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to reposition the mandible.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to level the maxilla and correct the asymmetry. 
Right mandibular inferior border ostectomy.
Nasal septoplasty. 
Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies.
Rhinoplasty.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
