original_note:
SHERRY STARRICK								11/17/2008

Sherry is 58 years old.  She is referred by Dr. Duane Keller.  Her prosthodontist who has been managing her for the past 20+ years is Dr. James K. Richey, 115 W. Union Street, Marion, IL 62959, phone number (618) 997-4514.

Sherry has been treated for myofascial pain problems since 1986.  She has been treated with splints since that time.  She had splints constructed in 1986, 1987, 1993, 1998, 2002, 2005, and 2008.  She began dental reconstruction in about 2002 and has had crowns placed on most of the teeth.  She has had no other specific treatment for the TMJ issue.  In July 2008, her situation got significantly worse when she developed significant difficulties eating, chewing, and even with relatively minimal jaw function.  

Sherry currently takes the following medications:

Relafen 500 mg 2 caps b.i.d. 
Neurontin 300 mg q.i.d.
Caltrate 600 mg q.d.
Extra Strength Tylenol 500 mg 2 tablets p.r.n. pain.

She gets occasional earaches, has tinnitus, but no vertigo or hearing loss.  Other joints that bother her include her elbows, knees, feet, ankles, toes, shoulders, and her neck at C5-C6.  In 2005, she had a right toe joint replacement and in 2007 she had an implant in the left great toe.  Her shoulders bother her and she has had manipulations in her shoulders.  She is aware of clenching and bruxism at night time.  She has depression likely related to the pain and dysfunctional factors.  She rates her TMJ pain at 8, average daily pain at 7, jaw function at 10, diet at 10, disability at 7.  Headaches are a level of 9 and occur primarily in the back of the head and behind the left eye.  These usually occur early mornings from about 3:00 to 9:00 a.m.  

Primary concerns that Sherry has include the following:

Severe TMJ pain.
Headaches. 
Pain through the jaw muscles. 
Significantly decreased jaw function.
Sherry also notes some changes in her bite relationship.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Sherry has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 24 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is probably -1 to 2 mm.  The maxillary midline is fairly close to being on the facial midline.  The mandibular dental midline is shifted about 2 mm to the left.  Chin, however, is fairly close to being on the facial midline.  In profile view, Sherry has fairly good facial symptom.  Intraorally, Sherry has crowns on all of the teeth back through the second bicuspids.  The first and second molar on the right side and the second molar on the left side are uncapped.  In upper arch, she appears to have caps on all the teeth except for the second molar on the right side.  She has an end-on Class III cuspid relationship on the right side and a full Class III cuspid relationship on the left side.  She has an anterior deep bite of probably 4 to 5 mm.  She is missing either a bicuspid or a molar on the mandibular left side.  She appears to have adequate attached gingiva.  Uvula is moderately elongated.  The soft palate may be mildly elongated.  The fascial pillars are asymmetric with the right side showing a little bit more constricture than the left side.  Tonsils appear probably relatively okay except maybe the right one is a little bit enlarged.  Intranasally, the septum is deviated off to the left side toward the medial and anterior aspect.  The turbinates appear moderate in size on the right side, but smaller on the left side.  Sherry complains of no nasal airway obstructive problems.  To palpation, she has moderate pain over the temporalis, masseters, retromandibular area, and significantly greater pain in the shoulders and the TMJs.  She has some mild tenderness in the posterior neck area.  Maximal incisal opening is 29 mm with about 1 mm of excursion movements.  Jaw joints seem to function fairly smoothly, but she does have a glitch in the right joint.
RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of the four third molars and the mandibular left first molar.  She has crowns on practically all the teeth except for the mandibular right first and second molars and the left second molar.  She has root canals on the maxillary first and second molars bilaterally and the mandibular left first bicuspid.  The sinuses appear clear.  It is difficult to evaluate the joints.  A little bit of vertical difference in the height of the mandible with the bony height of the body on the right side being slightly greater than the left side.  
Waters view shows that the sinuses appear clear.  She has a spur off the septum towards the left side in the bony area.  Right nasal airway appears wide open.  The left side appears to be a little more constricted.  
Left TMJ tomogram shows that she has arthritic changes there and sclerosis on the head of the condyle.  The joint space is vertically and posteriorly significantly reduced.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.    
Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has some anterior beaking and some arthritic changes.  In one view, it appears that the joint space is decreased.  Articular eminence is moderately shallow.  On opening, there is severely reduced translatory movements.  
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she does have a skeletal and occlusal Class II relationship.  However, the oropharyngeal airway appears relatively normal. 

Basic diagnoses for Sherry would be as follows:    

Atypical connective tissue autoimmune disease with poly-arthritic involvement.
Bilateral temporomandibular joint severe arthritis secondary to atypical connective tissue autoimmune disease. 
Severe TMJ pain. 
Severe headaches. 
Myofascial pain. 
Significantly limited jaw function. 
Significant masticatory dysfunction. 
Class II end-on occlusion on the right side with Class II occlusion on the left side. 
Nocturnal and daytime clenching and bruxism. 

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Evaluation by rheumatologist.
Medications:  Klonopin 1 mg q.h.s. to control nocturnal clenching and bruxism.
CT scan of jaws and joint joints for construction of 3-D model.
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction with TMJ total joint prostheses.
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen). 
Postsurgical management as necessary to control her pain, dysfunction, and rehabilitate her jaw function.

original_note:
CAROL JOHNSON									10/20/2006

Carol is 55 years old, referred by Dr. Petre Schubert.  She has had problems with hypertension.  She had an angioplasty in January of 1990.  She is in good health with no major medical problems.  She has a list of medications that she takes that is in her chart.  Carol really does not really have any concerns problems.  She is here at Dr. Schubert's request.  Dr. Schubert is aware that Carol has an anterior open bite and is concerned as to whether it would be stable to close that orthodontically or whether a surgical approach would provide the best result for Carol.  Carol has also had an open bite.  She had orthodontics at the age of 12 to try and close it and it was unsuccessful.  She believes her bite is closed more now than it was prior to orthodontic treatment.  She had gum surgery about four years ago.  She has no trouble with earaches or tinnitus but gets occasional vertigo.  She does have some problems with her right hip joint but no other joints cause any problems.  She is aware of clenching and bruxism at nighttime.  She has no history of headaches or TMJ pain or pain around the head and neck area.  While Carol was having orthodontics done the first time as a teenager, she would occasionally get a closed lock situation and would have to go to the orthodontist and get massaged in order to get her jaw open.  Therefore, she has had displaced disc in her joints at least since the age of 12 to 14.  She has no clicking or popping in her joints currently.  

In frontal view, Carol has fairy good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 24 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about –1.  Maxillary, mandibular and chin midline are all pretty much on the facial midline.  In profile view, Carol has good facial balance.  She has some slight AP maxillary deficiency of maybe 3 mm in AP mandibular deficiency of maybe 5 to 6 mm but not real significant.  Intraorally, she is missing lower first bicuspids but has the rest of her teeth back through the second molars.  A little bit of spacing between the cuspids and the second bicuspids.  In the upper arch she is also missing first bicuspids.  She has small upper lateral incisors.  It appears that she occludes only on the first and second molars on the left side and perhaps the same on the right side.  The rest of the teeth appear to be out of occlusion.  She has about 1 mm of vertical open bite.  She has about 1 to 2 mm overjet.  She has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship by about 2 to 3 mm.  Carol rates her jaw function, diet and disability at 0, TMJ pain and headaches at 0.  To palpation she has no pain around the head and neck area.  Joints seem to function relatively smoothly.  Transverse occlusal plane is level.  Oropharyngeal areas look normal.  Soft palate, uvula and faucial pillars all appear within normal limits.  She has no problems with snoring or sleep apnea symptoms.  Intranasally, the right turbinate is moderately enlarged.  The left turbinate is relatively normal.  There is a little septal spurring off to the left side at the base.  There does not appear to be any significant nasal airway obstruction.  The columella is wide and nostrils are somewhat narrow.  Luminal valves look relatively normal.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION:
Panorex shows that she has some arthritic changes in the condyles.  Sinuses look relatively clear.  Turbinates appear relatively normal.  She is missing all four third molars and all four first bicuspids.  Roots may be a little bit short in the bicuspid area, cuspid and lower incisor areas. 

Waters view shows the sinuses appear clear.  The bony septum appears reasonably straight.  Turbinates appear moderate in size.  There could be a septal spur off to the left side but difficult to tell.
CAROL JOHNSON				10/20/2006				Page 2


Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has arthritic changes with anterior beaking and some loss of vertical height.  Decreased posterior and posterosuperior joint space.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  

Left TMJ tomogram likewise shows decreased superior and posterior joint space and there are some arthritic changes on the condyle.  There is anterior beaking and there appears to be loss of height.  On opening, there is some restricted translation but the condyles do come beneath the articular eminences.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has some AP maxillary deficiency and AP mandibular deficiency.  She has a Class II skeletal and occlusal relationship.  Occlusal plane angle is moderately steep.  Oropharyngeal airway is difficult to evaluate because the soft palate was functioning when the x-ray was taken.  At the base of the tongue there appears to be adequate airway of probably about 7 mm.  On the Panorex she actually has good space between the lateral incisors and cuspids but diminished space between the roots of the cuspid and second bicuspids.  Dental model analysis shows that she does have a fair amount of wear on her posterior teeth.  She has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship.  She has increased curve of Wilson and a significantly accentuated curve of Spee with the incisors being probably 2 mm above the occlusal plane level.  Cuspids are also elevated at least 2 mm above the occlusal plane area.  Occlusion is all in the lingual cusps on a flat plane including the second bicuspids, first and second molars.  Buccal cusps are up and out of occlusion by probably almost 2 mm.  Clinical incisal opening is about 40 mm.  Excursive movements are 11 mm in each direction.

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
AP maxillary deficiency.
Vertical maxillary deficiency.
AP mandibular deficiency.
Class II end-on occlusion.
Anterior open bite.
Occluding only on first and second molars.
Increased curve of Wilson in the upper arch.
Increased curve of Spee in the upper arch.
Missing all four first bicuspids extracted for previous orthodontic reasons.
 Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation without reduction.
 Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 3 mm. 

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Presurgical orthodontics.  
Align and level maxillary arch.  Place step in the maxillary archwire to align the four incisors and elevate plane level and extrude each of the cuspids 1 to 2 mm to get them in better vertical alignment with the posterior teeth.  
Continue to align and level the lower arch.

CAROL JOHNSON 				10/20/2006				Page 3


Surgery.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the posterior aspect of the maxilla superiorly, expand the palatal level to decrease the curve of Wilson and segments to decrease the curve of Spee.
Post-surgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B259 MRI evaluation
Age: 57 y/o female
Date of Birth: 
Date of Acquisition: 
Date of Interpretation: 

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is quite large and actually extends beyond the lateral rim of the fossa. There is osteophytic formation on the lateral pole that extends beyond the lateral rim. Vertical and medial joint space looks fairly. The lateral joint space is significantly decreased and possibly could have bone-on-bone contact there. The articular disc is medially displaced and covers only the medial half of the condylar head. Some sclerotic areas on top of the condyle. Moderate downward curvature at the lateral rim of the fossa probably 3 to 4 mm.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is a little bit smaller in mediolateral width though the lateral aspect does come out almost adjacent to the lateral rim of the fossa. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly probably about 3 to 4 mm. There is a significantly decreased lateral joint space. The disc appears to be medially displaced covering only the medial half of the condyle. Some areas of sclerosis on the head of the condyle.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a decreased joint space particularly at the lateral aspect of the joint. No evidence of discal tissue at the lateral aspect. Some mild anterior beaking present at the lateral aspect of the joint. Articular eminence is moderately steep. There is more normal architecture towards the medial side of the joint. Articular disc is anteriorly displaced and medially displaced towards the lateral aspect of the joint but more medially the disc is in a more normal anteroposterior position relative to the condyle. Out laterally there is significant flattening on the superior head of the condyle. The lateral aspect of the condyle extends beyond the articular eminence. No significant inflammatory response seen in the joint. On opening, the disc does reduce and there is good translation of both the condyle and the disc.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has some flattening and some osteoarthritic changes particularly at the lateral aspect of the joint. More medially the condyle has a better architecture. Out laterally the disc is not readily identifiable and the joint space is significantly decreased indicative of medial displacement of the disc. More towards the medial aspect of the joint the disc is in a little bit anteroposterior direction although just slightly forward to the most normal position. There is a little bit of fluid in the superior joint space and also the inferior joint space. Articular eminence is moderately steep. On opening, there is good translation of the condyle and articular disc with the disc reducing.

IMPRESSION: Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc medially displaced as well as anteriorly displaced towards the lateral aspect of the joint. There is a lateral osteophyte extending beyond the lateral rim of the fossa.
Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc medially displaced as well as slight anterior displacement. B259 

B259 is 57 years old and is referred by Dr. __. B259 has a previous history of allergies and bronchitis. She had her nose injured in a car accident at one time. She had a partial hysterectomy 25 years ago, breast enhancement in 23 years ago and 3 years ago, she had a brow, face, and neck lift and lip enhancement. 11 years ago, B259 was diagnosed with hypothyroidism and currently takes Levoxyl 88 mcg per day. She has had an adverse reaction to Keflex and cephalexin. MEDICATIONS: Klonopin 0.5 mg q.h.s.
Effexor XR 35 mg per day.
Niacin 1000 mg per day. Estradiol 0.53 mg per week.
Levoxyl 88 mcg per day.
Lipitor 20 mg daily.
Aspirin 0.81 mg daily.

B259's problems began in her 20s when she started clenching her teeth. Her jaw joints did not begin clicking until her 40s. All these factors are of unknown etiology.

PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Jaw and muscle pain.
Clicking particularly in the left jaw joint.
Clenching and grinding of the teeth is uncontrollable.
Occasional difficulties chewing.
Problems with rather severe snoring. However, she has no problems breathing through her nose and has no history of mouth breathing.

B259 does have genetically predisposed depression. She did have a difficult childhood. Her mother was born with a cleft lip and palate that was not repaired until the mother was about 20 years of age. She wakes up at night clenching her teeth and sometimes will wake up at night with her hands clenched making them a little bit sore and achy as well. She is very much aware of the clenching and grinding of her teeth at night as well as during the day.

B259's father was abusive and actually put B259's sister in the hospital. B259 and her sister were taken away from the custody of her parents and institutionalized for at least 3 years. The only communication from her mother during that time was through letters. Then her dad passed away and B259 went back with her mother.

In frontal view, B259 has fairly good left to right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 20 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip length is 0. Maxillary dental midline is pretty much on the facial midline as is the mandibular dental midline and chin. Nose is tipped a little bit off to the right side about 2 mm. In profile view, B259 has fairly good balance between the upper lip, cheeks and nose. Chin is a little bit retruded by probably 5 to 6 mm. Chin-neckline is somewhat short and chin-neck angle is not real well defined. B259  Page 2

Intraorally, B259 has all of her teeth back through the second molars. The lower molars particularly on the right side and the bicuspids are in significant lingual inclination. She has some mild crowding in the lower anterior arch. She has fairly good attached gingiva though is quite weak around the right lateral incisor and may need to have that evaluated for possible gingival grafting. In the upper arch she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. Does have some palatal tori and she has some huge labial tori that extends well out beyond her braces. They extend from the second molars around to the cuspid bilaterally but they are much larger on the right side as compared to the left side. Anterior deep bite is about 5 mm. She has a Class II end-on occlusion being about 5 mm Class II. She has some constriction in the upper arch in the bicuspid molar area. The oropharyngeal tissues appear reasonably healthy. The uvula is a little bit enlarged. The soft palate may be a little bit elongated. Faucial pillars are moderately constricted. Tonsils are absent as they had previously been removed. B259 had multiple bouts of bronchitis and pneumonia in her early childhood. Her mother worked at ___. It was suggested that they move to a warmer climate and thus brought B259 to ___. Intranasally, the turbinates appear normal in size and the septum has a little waviness in it. It is relatively in the midline. There does not appear to be any significant nasal airway obstruction. The right TMJ seems to function relatively smoothly. However, she does give a history of having clicking and popping at times in the joints moreso on the left side as compared to the right side.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
: Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. She is also missing, maxillary right lateral incisor and has a 3-unit bridge replacing that tooth. She also has crowns on the left central lateral incisor. All other teeth are present. The sinuses appear relatively clear. The condyles look fairly large anteroposteriorly but have reasonable contour to them.

Water's view shows that the nasal septum in the bony aspect is deviated off to the left side at the base and off to the right side at the superior aspect. This may be due to the previous nasal trauma that she had resulting in nasal fracture. The sinuses appear clear.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that may be just slightly posteriorly positioned in the fossa. There is some flattening in the anterior aspect as well as anterior beaking present but not too significant. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Joint space appears fairly well balanced although slightly decreased posteriorly.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a joint that is more difficult to evaluate. Difficult to determine joint space as well as condyle morphology. The styloid process appears fairly prominent. On opening, there is good translation of both condyles. The styloid process on the left side can be seen to extend forward quite significantly.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that B259 has basically a Class II skeletal relationship and occlusal relationship. Oropharyngeal airway at the soft palate level measures about 7 to 8 mm, base of the tongue measures about 7 mm. B259 

The turbinate may extend beyond the hard palate. Lower incisors are slightly over-angulated. He does have a Bolton's tooth size discrepancy of 1 mm, however, I believe it is greater than that. The AP thickness of the incisor crowns contribute to the tooth size discrepancy that is noted on the models. Repositioning her models trying to achieve a Class I position results in a Class II end-on position by probably about 2 to 3 mm on each side. There is transverse excess width of the upper arch in the cuspid area that probably contributes some to this. There is probably at least 3 mm of excessive width in the upper arch in the cuspid area.

Lateral cephalometric analysis shows that the lower incisors are over-angulated and there is a Class II skeletal and occlusal relationship. The occlusal plane is at 16 degrees and there is decreased oropharyngeal airway posteriorly. There is a 9-degree ANB difference.

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation in a medial direction.
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
AP mandibular deficiency.
Class II end-on occlusion.
Transverse maxillary excess in the cuspid bicuspid area.
High occlusal plane angel.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Snoring.
Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of probably 2 to 3 mm. Severe clenching and bruxism.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Medications to try and control severe clenching and bruxism. a. Klonopin 1 mg tablet q.h.s.
Presurgical orthodontics.
Continue to align and level maxillary arch. Try to narrow transverse width between the cuspids and tip incisors forward to create more of "U" shaped upper arch.
Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing at least 2 to 3 mm of tooth structure and retract. Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to down graft the maxilla and advance slightly at the incisors as well as correct any transverse problems that may remain.
Bilaminar mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction.

B259 
The patient presents for follow-up evaluation. The patient is doing fairly well. Incisal opening is about 44 mm. Excursive movement to the right 2 mm, left 3.5 mm. No pain around the head and neck area. No other joint problems except for in her lower back. The Klonopin 4 mg p.o. q.h.s. controls her clenching and bruxism. She would like to cut back. She is to cut back ½ mg every 3 weeks and see how she does. If she starts to clench and brux again then we will need to increase slightly to keep that under control. She is very happy with her results. No noises in her joints. Occlusion is stable. No tenderness to palpation around the head and neck area. She is progressing very well. Occlusion is fine and I have given the approval that she can have her braces removed. She is doing well and is to return to see me in a few months after she gets her retainers and allows her bite to settle in a little bit.

B259 
She presents for followup evaluation. She is now 1 year 7 months post surgery. Having no pain. Rates her jaw function at 2, diet and disability at 0. She has no headaches. Incisal opening is 45 mm. Excursion movements are 2 mm in each direction. She is on a number of medications as listed on her evaluation form. She currently is taking Klonopin 4 mg at bedtime and she would like to cut back. I told her to cut back a half tablet and stay at that level for at least 2 weeks and then cut back to another half tablet for at least 2 weeks. If she starts to clench again or wakes up hurting, then she needs to go back up to the next higher dose. She is to keep informed of her progress, and she is to return to see me in August.

original_note:
LALITA TAWJAREON								02/01/2010

Lalita is 36 years old.  She has had TMJ problems since the age of 19 in 1993 related to wisdom teeth removal.  Prior to that, she had no TMJ problems.  Following that incident, she was unable to play her clarinet after that.  She did have orthodontics from grade 4 through 9.  Since 1993, she has been under the care of multiple TMJ specialists and has had multiple splints with an estimated 10 previous splints.  Her current splint has been made by Dr. Keller and is helping her some.  In the past, she has had physical therapy, chiropractic treatment (which worsened her problems) and acupuncture.  During the past year, her jaw has shifted and she has had increased pain, headaches, and jaw dysfunction.  

Medications she currently takes include:

Diclofenac, an anti-inflammatory.
Vicodin p.r.n. 
Robaxin p.r.n. 

She gets headaches at about a level of 5 in the temporal areas.  She rates her TMJ pain at 10, average daily pain at 10, jaw function and diet at 8, disability at 7.  She gets continuous earaches and has tinnitus and vertigo.  Other joints that bother her include her SCMs, hips, fingers, feet, and back.  She has problems with her sternoclavicular joints that developed as a result of an injury sustained at work in April 2009.  She has continuous problems with those joints.  She is also aware of clenching and bruxing at nighttime and also during the day.  

CLINICAL EVALUATION.  In frontal view, Lalita has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 20 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 1-2 mm.  The maxillary, mandibular, and chin midlines are all pretty much aligned with one another.  In profile view, she has fairly facial balance.  The mandible appears to be retruded by about 5-8 mm.  The maxilla may be retruded by 2-3 mm.  Intraorally, Lalita has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  She is fairly close to being a Class I cuspid-molar relationship.  She has about a 1 mm overbite relationship.  The transverse occlusal plane is fairly level.  Oropharyngeal tissues look fairly healthy.  Fascial pillars, however, are moderately constricted.  Nasal airway is difficult to visualize, but seems reasonably good.  Luminal valves are somewhat constricted.  To palpation, she has some mild tenderness over the right TMJ and the other areas are fairly normal.  On opening, she has some noise in the left joint at about 35 mm of opening and she may have closing noises at about 30 mm.  On the right side, she has some opening noises at about 20 mm and closing at probably around 5 mm.  She does have noises in her joints on opening.  The incisal opening is 46 mm, opening without significant discomfort about 20 mm.  The excursion movements are about 11 mm in each direction.  She has muscle spasms on the left side of her face that affect the left buccinator and perioral muscles as well as perhaps her eyelids to a lesser extent.  This is only on the left side.  
RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of the four third molars, but the rest of the teeth are in place and look reasonably healthy.  The turbinates appear moderate in size.  There may be a septal spur present.  Sinuses look reasonably clear.  Joints look a little bit arthritic.  

Left TMJ sagittal tomogram shows a condyle that has flattening on the anterior head.  The articular eminence is fairly steep.    

Right TMJ sagittal tomogram shows a condyle that has some flattening on the anterior head and a little bit on the posterior head as well.  There are a little bit more degenerative changes on the anterior aspect of the condylar head as compared to the opposite side.  The joint space looks better posteriorly on the right side as compared to the left side. 

Left TMJ coronal view shows a joint that has fairly good mediolateral width.  The joint space looks fairly good, although there appears to be a small osteophyte in the mid portion of the condyle.  The lateral rim curves inferiorly about 2 mm. 

Right TMJ coronal view shows that the lateral rim is fairly flat.  The joint space looks fairly good.  

Coronal view shows that she has large turbinates that appear to be blocking the airway.  The sinuses appear clear.  The septum appears reasonably straight.  

Axial view shows that the turbinates are quite large.  The nasal septum is fairly straight and sinuses are clear.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has a little bit of a retruded maxilla and mandible with a high occlusal plane angle.  Oropharyngeal airway just measures about 4 mm at the base of the tongue area and soft palate area.  

Basic diagnoses would be as follows:

Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with reduction but with probable systemic disease process present.
Maxillary AP and posterior vertical hypoplasia.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Severe TMJ pain.
Severe headaches. 
Myofascial pain. 
Decreased oropharyngeal airway.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Presurgical orthodontics.
	a.	Align and level maxillary arch.  
	b.	Align and level mandibular arch.  
2.	CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol).
3.	Surgery.
	a.	Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts 			total joint prostheses.
	b.	Maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla in a counterclockwise 				direction. 
	c.	Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen).
	d.	Bilateral coronoidotomies.
4.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B107
B107 is 62 years old. She is referred by Dr. XXXX. B107's TMJ problems began at age 33 when she had a bicycle accident where she sustained facial fractures. She was treated. She subsequently developed some TMJ problems. At age 35, she had a left arthroplasty and disc plication; at 36 left disentomb with temporal fascial muscle flap; at 42 left TMJ reconstruction with a rib graft; at 43 right TMJ diskectomy and temporal fascial flap; at 44 right rib graft. The surgeries were performed by Dr. XXXX and Dr. XXXX. She has had no further TMJ surgery. She does have severe sleep apnea that was diagnosed 6 years ago. She has tried a CPAP and a BiPAP, but either the seals do not work because of her facial structure or she cannot tolerate them.

B107 gets daily headaches at a level of 6 that occur predominantly in the right frontal area and in the back of the head on the right side. She rates her TMJ pain at 6, average daily pain at 6, jaw function and diet at 7, disability at 10. She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, nor vertigo. Other joints that bother her include her wrists, hips, and knees. She was diagnosed with lupus and Sjogren's syndrome. She has a chronic bowel inflammatory process present as well. B107 has some significant facial nerve damage from the operations that she has had. Both frontal branches are out and the right zygomatic branch is weak. In fact, she has to wear eye patches at night to keep her eyes from drying out. She has some dysfunction of the marginal mandibular nerve as well. She is numb on the right side in the lower lip, chin, and cheek area. She has prepared a list of medications that is attached. She does take Lortab for pain.

CLINICAL EVALUATION. In frontal view, B107 has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 20 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is 0. The maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline.. The mandibular dental midline is shifted about 3 mm to the left. The chin midline is about 2-3 mm to the left. The nasal tip is about 3 mm to the right. In profile, upper lip is about 6 mm AP deficient and the chin is probably 10-12 mm AP deficient. Chin neckline reasonably is well defined. Intraorally, she is missing the mandibular left molars and right first and second molars with a bridge there in the third molar place. In the upper arch, she is missing her molars on the left side and right side. She is also missing 4 incisors and left cuspid. B107 has a Class I cuspid relationship on the right side and Class II on the left side. She has a 2 mm overbite relationship. The mandibular left cuspid is about 2 mm posterior to the right cuspid. Oropharyngeal airway looks healthy. Uvula and soft palate may be a little bit elongated. The fascial pillars are mild to moderately constricted. Intranasally, the turbinates look relatively normal in size. The columella is wide and the nostrils are relatively narrow. To palpation, she has high moderate to low severe pain through most of the head and neck area and just moderate pain in the temporalis area. Maximal incisal opening is 22 mm and opening without severe pain 15 mm. Excursion movement to the right is about 1 mm and to the left less than 1 mm.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the presence of costochondral grafts in both joints. There are extremely large screws x2 in the left jaw area securing the grafts in place. On the right side, there are 3 smaller screws. It appears she has had previous hyaloid suspension performed in the chin area, and there is a large bony defect there currently. She is missing the mandibular left molars, mandibular right first and second molars which have been replaced with a 5-unit bridge that extends back to the third molar. In the upper arch, she is missing all 6 molars as well as the 4 incisors and left cuspid. She has a 10-unit bridge in the upper arch. Her bridge is apparently loose.

Coronal view shows that she has a deviated nasal septum, but turbinates appear moderate in size and the sinuses appear clear.
The axial view shows that the sinuses are clear. Turbinates are moderate in size and the septum has some slight deviation towards the left side, but there appears to be a good airway through the nose bilaterally.

Left TMJ sagittal view just shows that there must be some cartilage on the rib graft that extends upward. There are 2 large screws stabilizing the graft in place. The articular eminence is fairly flat.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows the presence of a rib graft that has some cartilage on top yet. The articular eminence is fairly steep on this size. There are 3 screws holding the graft in place.

Left TMJ coronal view shows that the lateral rim is quite flat. There is some bony structure that is actually almost out lateral to the lateral rim of the fossa for the majority of the graft. Part of the graft is contained underneath the lateral rim of the fossa. The 2 screws are seen in the mandible.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a lateral rim of the fossa that curves inferiorly about 2-3 mm. The rib graft is seen with osteophyte kind of extension upwards at the lateral aspect of the condyle. Three screws are seen in the mandible and they extend quite a ways through the mandible.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has retruded maxilla and mandible and the posterior maxilla is quite vertically deficient. The vertical height of the mandibular ramus is also quite deficient. Oropharyngeal airway actually measures about 5 mm at the tip of the soft palate and about 5-6 mm at the base of the tongue. B107 has a transverse cant in the maxillary occlusal plane with the left side being 3 mm higher than the right side.

Basic diagnoses would be as follows:
Bilateral failed TMJ rib grafts with fibrous ankylosis.
Maxillary AP and posterior vertical hypoplasia.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia.
Missing all the maxillary molars, mandibular left molars, and mandibular right first and second molars replaced a 5-unit bridge. Also missing maxillary 4 incisors and left cuspid replaced with a 10-unit bridge.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of 5 mm (normal 11 mm).
TMJ pain.
Myofascial pain. Headaches.
Sleep apnea.
Lupus and Sjogren's syndrome.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1. CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol).
2. Surgery. a.	Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. b.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla in a counterclockwise direction. c.	Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen). d.	Bilateral coronoidotomies.
3. Restorative dentistry that may include dental implants and dental reconstruction.

B107 had previous breast implants with silicon gel in it. This initiated her lupus and Sjogren's syndrome problems. She had the silicone implants removed and had saline implants placed. However, the right saline implant is ruptured and she has not been able to get it taken out because of her significant airway problems and the fact that the surgeons and anesthesiologists are afraid to put her to sleep.

MRI
B107 AGE: 62/F
Left TMJ coronal view shows that she has had previous surgery and by history she had a rib graft placed in the left TMJ at age 42. The left tomogram shows the presence of a graft and it appears that there is almost a separate body of tissue that is somewhat calcified within the joint space. The anatomy is quite deformed.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is narrow in width. The disc may be in position, but difficult to tell on these views. The lateral rim of the fossa appears fairly flat in some areas, but in other areas there is a part that curves down about 2 mm.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows that there is some kind of foreign and calcified material or artifact within the ramus area that is actually somewhat anterior to the fossa. Somewhat lateral, there appears to be some material in the fossa area. On opening, there is virtually no translation present.

The right TMJ shows significant degenerative changes in this joint. There is some fluid in the joint space. It appears she has had some previous surgery in this side as there is some metal artifact meaning she could possibly have a rib graft done on the right side as well. There is no translation present.

IMPRESSION: Bilateral TMJ severe destruction of the joints with probable bilateral rib grafts placed.

original_note:
CHERYL LAXTON									02-07-03

Cheryl is 27 years old and referred by Dr. George Adams, 3115 W. Parker Road, #538, Plano, TX  75023.

Patient History

Cheryl is in good health with no major medical problems. She takes no medications. She has had an adverse reaction to BACTRIM in the past. 

Cheryl had orthodontics from the ages of 12 to 15. That first round of orthodontics she had her upper first bicuspids extracted and had all four third molars removed. She then again had orthodontics done from the ages of 25 to 27. About midway through the orthodontic treatment, she began developing clicking and popping in her jaw joints. She was on no interarch mechanics that she is aware of when the condition developed. 

She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, or hearing loss. She has some problems with her neck but is not sure if it is muscular or bony, but has no other joint problems. She can not wear earrings of any type as any of them make her ears itchy. She may have nocturnal clenching, but that is not definite. She rates her headaches at a level of 7 in the temporal area, and these occur on a daily basis. She rates her TMJ pain at 7, overall pain at 2, jaw function and diet at 7, and disability at 2. 

Patient Concerns

Feeling of fluid running through her ear
Does not know where her bite should be
No comfortable place to put her bite
Headaches 
TMJ pain
Chewing difficulties

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Cheryl has fairly good facial symmetry and balance. Right side of the face may be just a little bit vertically longer than the left side by maybe 2 to 3 mm. Upper lip length is 21 mm. Upper tooth to lip relationship is 5 to 6 mm. Maxillary dental midline may be just a little bit off to the left side by about a millimeter as is the mandibular dental midline. Chin is probably about 3 mm off toward the left side. Right eye is about 2 or 3 mm higher than the left eye.

In profile view, she may have about 2 to 3 mm of AP maxillary deficiency. Chin may be retruded by probably approximately 8 mm. Chin-neckline is a little bit short, although chin-neck angle is reasonably well defined. 

Intraorally, Cheryl has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. Teeth appear to be nicely aligned and leveled. She has a 3 x 3 retainer. Has good attached gingiva around the lower anterior arch. Has good attached gingiva around the lower anterior arch. In the upper arch, she is missing the first bicuspids and has some residual space between the cuspids and second bicuspids. In centric relation, mandibular dental midline is shifted about 2 mm off to the right of the maxillary midline. May be Class II end-on cuspid relationship being about 3 to 4 mm Class II. She has an overjet of about 2 to 3 mm. Right side is probably about 2 mm lower than the left side in the cuspid area. She has a vertical overbite of about 2 mm.

To palpation, Cheryl has no tenderness around the head and neck area. On opening, she has a definite click in the left side at about 20 mm and on closing at about 10 to 5 mm. Maximum incisal opening is about 35 mm. Excursive movements are about 9 mm in each direction. In excursive movements, she has significant popping particularly on the left side. 

Cheryl has a tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of almost 2.5 mm. This is probably due to small upper lateral incisors. However, hand articulating the models does not seem to demonstrate quite as significant of a tooth size difference. 

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars and the maxillary first bicuspids. Roots of the maxillary molars appear a little bit short. Sinuses are clear. Nasal airway looks reasonably good. Condyles appear to have a little bit of chance in their morphology.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle posteriorly positioned in the fossa. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Increased anterior joint space. Slightly decreased vertical joint space as well as the posterior joint space.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is significantly posteriorly positioned in the fossa. Increased anterior joint space. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Appears to be a little bit more degenerative changes in the bone on this side as compared to the opposite side.

Waters view shows that the sinuses are all clear. Turbinates are moderately large, but it appears that there is adequate airway. Septum appears reasonably straight in the bony area. Vertical asymmetry is seen. The vertical difference in the orbits is also noted. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows a skeletal Class II relationship and significant vertical inclination of the maxillary incisors. Oropharyngeal airway looks very good.

Basic Diagnoses

Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with reduction
AP maxillary deficiency
Anterior vertical maxillary excess
AP mandibular deficiency
Transverse occlusal asymmetry with the right side being 2 mm lower than the left side
Class II end-on cuspid relationship
Severe vertical inclination of the maxillary incisors
Missing maxillary right first bicuspids with approximately 2 mm of residual spacing bilaterally 
Headaches 
TMJ pain
Orbital dystopia with the right orbit being 2 to 3 mm higher than the left orbit

Recommended Treatment

Presurgical orthodontics
Align and level maxillary arch. Open up first bicuspid space to equal approximately 6 or 7 mm by tipping the upper anterior teeth forward and slightly distalizing the posterior teeth
Align and level mandibular arch

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors 
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the anterior aspect superiorly, advance it slightly, and level it transversely

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

Restorative dentistry for placement of osseointegrated implants in the maxillary first bicuspid area and placement of crowns

original_note:
BEATRICE MOLLER							02/11/2011

Beatrice is 20 years old, birth date 11/16/1990.  She is referred by Dr. Marc Lemchen, 553 Park Avenue, New York, NY 10021.  

Beatrice is in good health.  To the best of her knowledge, her TMJ problems began when she was about 18 years of age of unknown etiology.  She did have orthodontics in the seventh and eighth grade from approximately the ages of 12 to 15 by Dr. Patrick Grossman in London, England.  She did have splint therapy and an NTI device on August 2009.  In December 2010, she began orthodontic treatment with Dr. Lemchen.  She has headaches that occur predominantly in the frontal and temporal areas at a level of 4.  She rates her TMJ pain at 6, average daily pain at 5, jaw function at 6, diet at 5, and disability at 4.  She takes birth control pills, Aleve, and Advil.  She has no problems with earaches, gets occasional tinnitus and vertigo.  The only other joint that bothers her is her left knee.  She is not aware of clenching or bruxing.

In frontal view, Beatrice has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 19 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 6 mm.  The maxillary midline is pretty close to being on the facial midline.  The mandibular dental midline is a little bit to the right about 1 mm, but the chin is fairly close to being on the midline.  In profile, the maxilla is retruded about only 4 to 5 mm.  The chin is retruded about 20 mm.  The chin neckline and chin neck angle are not well defined.  Beatrice has a lip incompetence of close to 12 mm.  Intraorally, Beatrice has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars present.  She has braces on all of her teeth except for the lower first molars.  The lower teeth appear over angulated.  There is some mild crowding in the lower anterior arch.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars present.  All of the teeth are bracketed except for the first bicuspids.  As Beatrice postures her mandible, her lower midline is about 2 mm off to the right side.  She has an end-on Class II occlusion being probably about 3 or 4 mm Class II and in that position which is very painful for her, she has a zero overbite relationship.  She probably has about a 4 mm over jet in her centric relation.  Oropharyngeal tissues look normal and healthy.  Intranasally, the right turbinate appeared moderately enlarged.  The anterior aspect of the septum appears to be tipped a little bit off towards the left side.  To palpation, Beatrice has severe pain over the TMJs, high moderate pain over the masseters and retromandibular area, and moderate pain over the temporalis muscles, and mild tenderness over the posterior neck muscles.  On opening, she has a click at about 5 mm in the right side and 10 mm in the left side with it being more obvious on the left side.  The closing click is about 5 mm on each side.  The maximum incisal opening is about 52 mm.  Excursion movements are about 8 mm in each direction. 

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the presence of four impacted third molars.  The turbinates appear moderately enlarged with the right one larger than the left one.  The sinuses appear reasonably clear.  The condyles appear somewhat arthritic.  
Coronal view shows that the bony septum is deviated significantly off to the right side with a bony spur and appears to obliterate most of the functional airway.  On the left side, the turbinates are moderately enlarged, but the airway appears adequate.  The sinuses appear clear.  

Axial view shows that the turbinates are of moderate size with the right one being more obstructed than left one.  Sinuses appear clear.  

Left TMJ sagittal tomograms show that the condyle is posteriorly positioned in the fossa.  There is flattening on the anterior head and just slight tendency towards anterior beaking.  There may be a small osteophyte off the head of the condyle.  The posterior joint space is decreased, but the anterior space appears increased.  The articular eminence has a relatively normal inclination and is relatively short in length.  

Right TMJ sagittal tomogram shows some anterior beaking present and flattening of the anterior head.  The condyle appears a little bit larger than the opposite side.  The articular eminence is a little bit longer in length and slightly steeper.  There appears to be just some slight cupping out of the anterior head of the condyle.  

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has moderate mediolateral width.  There appears to be good vertical joint space.  The lateral rim curves inferiorly only about 2 mm.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with moderate mediolateral width.  There is reasonable joint space.  The lateral rim curves inferiorly only about 2 mm. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Beatrice has over-angulated maxillary incisors with vertical maxillary excess.  In the mandible, the lower incisors are over-angulated fairly significantly.  Oropharyngeal airway is constricted with an AP airway dimension of about 3 mm at the soft palate and 4 mm at the base of the tongue.  

Basic diagnoses for Beatrice would be as follows:

Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly dislocated with reduction.
Maxillary AP and vertical hyperplasia.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia.
AP microgenia.
Class II end-on occlusion. 
Maxillary and mandibular dental alveolar protrusion.
Significantly decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of 3 mm (normal dimension 11 mm).
Deviated nasal septum off to the right side. 
Hypertrophied turbinates.
Impacted third molars x4.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Extract maxillary and mandibular first bicuspids.
2.	Presurgical orthodontics.
	a.	Align and level maxillary arch closing the extraction spaces.  Keep lateral 			incisor roots tipped mesially and cuspid roots tipped distally to make room 		for interdental osteotomies.  
	b.	Align and level mandibular arch and close the extraction spaces.  				Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing 1 mm of tooth structure.  
3.	Surgery.
	a.	Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek 			anchors.
	b.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a 			counterclockwise direction.
	c.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move it superiorly and advance it in a 			counterclockwise direction. 
	d.	Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomies to augment the chin.
	e.	Nasal septoplasty.
	f.	Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies. 
	g.	Surgical removal of impacted third molars x4.  
4.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
SAM MOSES										12-14-04

Sam is 15 years old and referred by Dr. Rick Roblee, Fayetteville, AR.

Patient History

Sam has had previous ear tubes when he was an infant and has had his wisdom teeth removed. Sam has allergies, and he has reactive to Bermuda grass, molds, and dust mites. Sam is not aware of any food allergies. 

Sam has had previous orthodontics and had appliances on for approximately two years. He got them off about six months ago, and there is already some shifting occurring in the teeth and in Sam’s bite. Sam is aware that his teeth are shifting, and they don’t fit together very well. They actually fit together apparently fairly well after braces but in the last six months there have been some significant changes that have been occurring. Sam doesn’t have any problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, or hearing loss. No other joint problems. Not aware of clenching or bruxism at all, in fact, is a mouth breather and sleeps with his mouth open. No headache problems except occasional headache. No TMJ pain or dysfunction and no myofascial pain around the head and neck area. Rates his jaw function at 2, diet at 0, and disability at 0. He has no tenderness to palpation around the head and neck area.

Medications

Clarinex
Flonase
Accutane
Singulair

Clinical Examination

On opening, he has an opening click at about 10 mm in the left TMJ and closing click at about 5 mm. Right joint seems to function relatively smoothly. Incisal opening is 48 mm. Excursive movements are about 7 mm in each direction. 

In frontal view, Sam has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 22 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 6 mm. Maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline and chin midline are aligned with each other but about a millimeter off to the left side. Has lip incompetence of about 11 mm. 

In profile view, Sam has some AP maxillary deficiency of probably about 3 to 4 mm. Chin-neckline is short, and chin-neck angle is moderately well-defined. Chin is probably AP deficiency by about 18 to 20 mm. 

Intraorally, Sam has all of his lower teeth back through the second molars. Lower arch is fairly well aligned and leveled. A little inflammation around the lower anterior teeth and could improve his tooth brushing a little bit. Appears to be adequate attached gingiva around the lower anterior arch. In the upper arch, Sam has all of his teeth back through the second molars. Upper lateral incisors appear small in size and could present a tooth-size discrepancy. A little bit of spacing between the upper incisors. Upper incisors have a tapered crown tip and are wider up in the midpart of the tooth as compared to the incisal edge. Sam has just a slight Class III cuspid tendency on the right side by about a millimeter and on the left side by approximately 2 mm. Has an end-on incisor relationship and has crossbite tendencies on the lateral incisor areas. Uvula appears a little bit long. Soft palate may be slightly elongated. Faucial pillars may be slightly constricted but not too significant. Tonsils are present and are relatively normal in size with no evidence of inflammation. 

Intranasally, the turbinates are hyperplastic. Appears to be a boggy, purplish kind of tissue associated with the turbinates. They are quite large and appear inflamed. Sam says that when he lies down he essentially cannot breathe through his nose at all. Septum appears within normal relative to deviation. 

Tooth-size analysis shows a Bolton’s difference of 5 mm. 

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. Sinuses appear relatively clear, although the left one is a little bit more cloudy than the right one. Difficult to see the condyles on this particular radiograph.

Waters view shows a little bit of cloudiness in the left sinus. Turbinates appear enlarged vertically, and the right one is larger than the left one. Ethmoid and frontal sinuses appear clear. Bony septum appears relatively straight.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has fairly good shape to it. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Joint space could be a little bit compromised vertically and posteriorly but difficult to tell on these views. 

Left TMJ shows a condyle that appears to have some anterior beaking and a little bit of flattening on the anterior head. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Appears to be decreased posterior joint space and increased anterior joint space. Articular disc may be sitting in a more vertical position within the fossa with just slight anterior displacement based on radiographic and clinical examination. 

On opening, there is moderately good translation of the left condyle and moderate translation of the right condyle. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that he does have vertical maxillary excess. It appears that he may have a moderately high occlusal plane angle. Oropharyngeal airway looks good, but the nasopharyngeal airway appears to be fairly well blocked off with a fairly good size adenoid mass present, and the turbinates are quite large and almost reach all the way back to the adenoid tissues. AP dimension from soft palate to posterior pharyngeal wall and from the base of the tongue to the posterior pharyngeal wall is just about within normal range. 

Basic Diagnoses

Bilateral mandibular condylar hyperplasia
Bilateral TMJ articular disc dislocations
Mandibular hyperplasia
Maxillary vertical hyperplasia
Hypertrophied turbinates
Hypertrophied nasopharyngeal adenoid tissue
Nasal airway obstruction
Significant lip incompetence of 11 mm
Class III end-on occlusion 
Tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 5 mm
Overangulation of lower incisors
Decreased oropharyngeal airway
Chronic mouth breathing

Recommended Treatment

MRI of left and right TMJ

Presurgical orthodontics 
Align and level maxillary arch. Open up some space around the upper lateral incisors of about a millimeter around each tooth
Align and level mandibular arch. Slenderize lower anterior teeth removing at least 2 mm of tooth structure and retract

Reassess growth to determine if condylar hyperplasia is present

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ high condylectomies and articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors 
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the maxilla superiorly
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to shift the mandible posteriorly and counterclockwise rotate it
Bilateral partial turbinectomies
Nasopharyngeal adenoidectomy
Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy with bone screw stabilization

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

original_note:
LISA WILLIAMS									03/07/2008

Lisa is 40 years old, referred by Dr. James Baker, 8301 S. Walker, Suite 101, Oklahoma City, Oklahoma 73139.  Lisa’s jaw problems began in 2000 when she was 33 years old.  She was on vacation in San Antonio and the pain just began of unknown etiology.  She had extensive root canal therapy done beginning in 2000.  This did not seem to help her significantly.  She eventually went to the pain center at Texas Tech and saw Dr. Razc.  She had some nerve injections and some nerve blocks performed that were ineffective.  She had low voltage electroshock treatment in 2002 without success.  She had a trigeminal nerve block performed in 2003 without success.  She had a spinal cord stimulator implanted in 2004.  This also was ineffective.  The doctor finally refused to see her and she had to see someone else to have the device removed from her left pectoral area.  She then went to a pain clinic in Tulsa where she was initially treated with fentanyl patches and oral medications, but was essentially nonfunctional.  She then went to another pain specialist who put her on morphine up to 1000 mg per day.  Finally, in 2006, the root canals were redone and this did for a few weeks at least get rid of her pain, but then it started to return again.  She had kelation treatments performed IV and, with this and the root canal, she was able to get off her narcotic medications, but then the pain started to return again.  She has been on numerous medications including most all migraine medications that have been ineffective for her.  

She currently is on the following medications:

Estratest.
OxyContin 60 mg b.i.d. 
Nexium 40 mg q.d.
Elavil 40 mg q.h.s.
Allegra.
Reglan 5 mg per day.
Amantadine.
Ergomar 2 mg for headaches.

Lisa gets headaches at least five days per week at a level of 10.  They occur predominantly in the frontal and temporal areas.  She rates her TMJ pain at 10, average daily pain at 7, jaw function at 0, diet at 3, disability at 8.  She has no problems with earaches.  Gets occasional tinnitus and has moderate occurrence of vertigo.  She has no other joints problems.  She gets tingling and numbness in her fingers and hands and this occurs bilaterally.  She went to see a DO who said that one leg was shorter than the other and she now wears shoe lifts, but this has not affected her pain significantly.  She has problems with constipation because of the narcotic medication she is taking.  She had a hysterectomy in September 2002.  She isolates the pain predominantly in the left masseter muscle area.  She is not sure if she has pain directly in the TMJ joints themselves.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Lisa has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 24 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 1 mm.  Maxillary midline is just off to the right side maybe about 1 mm as is the mandibular dental midline, but chin is fairly close to being on the facial midline.  In profile view, Lisa has an obtuse nasolabial angle.  Upper lip supra labrale is posterior just subnasale by probably about 3 mm.  The maxilla is retruded by probably at least 4 mm.  Chin is retruded about 12 to 15 mm.  Lisa has extensive restorative dentistry done with all of the teeth essentially replaced with crowns and bridges.  All the dental units are present or have been replaced back to the second molar on the right side and third molar on the left side.  In the upper arch, she has all the dental units present or replaced from the left second molar to the right third molar.  She has a full Class II cuspid relationship with an overjet of about 4 mm.  She has about 1 mm of vertical overbite.  Lisa has had MRIs and CAT scans of the brain looking for tumors and nothing has been found that has been abnormal or to account for her significant pain problems.  Oropharyngeal tissues all look healthy and normal. Intranasally, turbinates and septum look relatively normal.  She was a wide columella.  She has narrow nostrils.  Holding the nostrils open allow her to breathe through her nose easier.  She is not aware of snoring.  She has moderate daytime somnolence that could be medication related perhaps.  She is not aware of sleep apnea issues.  Maximum incisal opening is 48 mm.  Excursion movements are 10 mm in each direction.  To palpation, she has moderate pain over the left masseter muscle and left TMJ area.  Lisa did have some cockatiel birds back in the 1990s that died.  She has a significant tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of 4 mm.  In addition, the maxillary incisor crowns are quite thick linguolabially which would further increase the tooth size discrepancy.  In a Class I cuspid position, she has a transverse maxillary deficiency. In addition, she has an almost anterior crossbite relationship. When Lisa was 17, she fell and knocked her front teeth out in the upper arch.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the left central incisor, first bicuspid, and first molar on the right side missing, the central incisor, lateral incisor, and cuspid, then there are crowns on the bicuspids, then missing the first molar replaced by a pontic.  In the lower arch, she has all of her natural teeth on the left side and on the right side is missing the first and second molars and maybe the third molars in the second molar position.  She has root canals on every single tooth remaining.  Sinuses look relatively clear.  Condyles look relatively normal.  Styloid processes are elongated. 

Waters view shows that sinuses look fairly equally opacified, though a little bit cloudy in the maxillary sinuses.  Nasal septum appears a little bit wide.  Turbinates are moderate in size.    

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has very good morphology.  Articular eminence is moderate in angulation. There appears to be good joint space vertically posteriorly and anteriorly.  

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is postured forward in the fossa, but it may be just that she was not biting in a centric relation at the time the x-ray was taken.  There appears to be increased vertical and posterior joint space.  Articular eminence is moderate in steepness.  The condyle is a little flattened on the anterior surface, but otherwise relatively unremarkable.  On opening, there is good translation of both condyles. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows she has some slight AP maxillary retrusion of a perimandibular retrusion. Occlusal plane looks relatively normal.  Oropharyngeal airway is good at the soft palate level, but decreased at the base of the tongue measuring only about 5 to 6 mm.  

New tomogram on the left side with the condyle seated shows that there is more normal joint space.  In fact, the posterior joint space may be just slightly decreased.  It appears there may be a small osteophyte off the top of the condyle.  There could possibly be a slight anteriorly displaced disc based on this radiograph.

Basic diagnoses for Lisa would be as follows:

AP mandibular hypoplasia. 
AP maxillary hypoplasia. 
Posterior vertical maxillary hypoplasia.
Transverse maxillary hypoplasia.
Class II occlusion.
Left TMJ mild arthritis and articular disc dislocation at the lateral aspect of the joint.
Severe myofascial pain in the left masseter muscle.
Bilateral headaches.
Significant tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of at least 4 mm.
Missing multiple teeth including maxillary left central incisor, first bicuspid, first molar, right central incisor, lateral incisor, cuspid, first molar, and mandibular left first and second molar. 
Root canals on all remaining teeth.
Crowns and/or pontics for all maxillary and mandibular teeth.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Injection of left TMJ with Marcaine (this was performed on 3/7/08).
New medications.
a.   Klonopin 1 mg tablet dispensing 30 tablets 1 tablet q.h.s., refill x5.
Removal of anterior pontics extending from the maxillary left central incisor through right cuspid and replace with removable prosthesis.
Surgery.
Left TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible approximately 12 mm of pogonion with a slight counterclockwise rotation.  
Maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla, down graft it, and expand it.
Application of arch bars to maxillary and mandibular teeth.
      5.   Restorative dentistry to reconstruct the dental prostheses.

original_note:
ELIZABETH MIRACLE								05-21-04

Elizabeth is 15 years old. She is in the 9th grade. She was referred by Tom Czarnecki, DMD, MS, 754 Carrol Rd., Southlake, TX  76092.

Patient History

Elizabeth has some allergy problems but otherwise is in good health. Elizabeth’s problems with her jaw joints may have begun in the past two years of unknown etiology. Elizabeth had her first stage of orthodontics at the age of 6 with a palatal expander. Dr. Czarnecki started treating Elizabeth in 04/2003, and he did a palatal expander and then initiated fixed appliances in 09/2003. She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, or hearing loss. The only joints that really bother her are her fingers in which she has lots of popping and crackling noises. She is allergic to pollen, trees, dogs, cats, etc., and allergic to nickel. She is not aware of clenching or bruxism. She gets headaches two times a week at about a level of 3 in the temporal areas bilaterally. They are worse during allergy season. She rates her TMJ pain at 2, average daily pain at 2, jaw function and diet at 4, and disability at 0. 

Patient Concerns

Lower jaw is out in front of the upper jaw
She has an underbite with the upper jaw

Current Medications

Allergy shots
Aleve for headaches at times

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Elizabeth has good facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 18 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 3 mm. Maxillary, mandibular, and chin midline are all pretty much on the facial midline.

In profile view, she has some maxillary AP deficiency. Upper lip supralabrale is posterior to subnasale by probably 3 mm. Filling out the upper lip does improve her facial balance. Lower lip is protrusive. Chin is actually in fairly good AP balance, although it may be just slightly deficient. Has a high mandibular plane angle. Chin-neckline looks relatively normal in length, and chin-neck angle is reasonably well-defined. 

Intraorally, Elizabeth has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. Second molars are slightly hypererupted particularly on the left side. Teeth otherwise are fairly well-aligned. Small diasthema between the lower central incisors. Power chain is in place to help close it up. Appears to have adequate attached gingiva around the lower anterior arch. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. Increased curve of Wilson is present in the upper arch. She has a Class III cuspid relationship that may be just slightly worse on the left side than the right side. On the left side she is probably 3 mm Class III and on the right side probably 5 mm Class III. She has an anterior open bite of 1 to 2 mm. Has a slight accentuated curve of occlusion in the upper arch. Lower midline is shifted about a millimeter off to the left. Transversely, she has fairly good symmetry. Left cuspid may be just about a millimeter posterior to the right cuspid in the lower arch. Upper arch is fairly symmetric. Oropharynx is clear, and tissues all look healthy. Intranasally, turbinates are of moderate size as best I can see in the front but cannot see in the back. She has a very wide columella width, and the nostrils are quite narrow because of the wide columella. Holding open the nostrils, however, could not significantly increase her ability to breathe through her nose. Elizabeth believes that she probably breathes through her mouth during allergy seasons, but otherwise she thinks she breathes through her nose when she sleeps. Dad has not observed her snoring at all. 

To palpation, she has some mild tenderness over the TMJs and styloid areas. On opening, the left joint is relatively smooth; however, on the right side she has a click at about 30 mm of opening and closing click at probably 20. The closing click is a little bit more obvious than the opening click. Maximal incisal opening is 63 mm. Excursive movements are about 12 mm in each direction. 

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows she probably has a mucous retention cyst perhaps in the left sinus that appears more crowded than the right side. Lower arch shows all of her teeth are erupted through the second molars, and she has impacted third molars. In the upper arch, she has unerupted second and third molars. Difficult to evaluate the condyles, but the right one looks a little bit different than the left one but hard to tell on these radiographs.

Waters view shows the mandibular dental midline is a little bit to the left of the maxillary midline. Sinuses actually appear fairly clear on this view. Right turbinate appears larger than the left turbinate. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows significantly decreased joint space and some mild arthritic changes on the head of the condyle. Articular eminence is relatively shallow. Decreased posterior and superior joint spaces. 

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle with decreased superior and posterior joint space. No pseudocyst present in the condylar head. Articular eminence is relatively shallow. 

On opening, there is good translation of both condyles with them coming completely out of the fossa. 

Lateral cephalometrically, she has a high occlusal plane angle and high mandibular plane angle. Maxilla appears AP deficient and mandible may be also AP deficient particularly in the chin area. She has vertical asymmetry on the radiograph, but I do not see that clinically. 

Dr. Czarnecki did take a hand-wrist film on 05/11/04. It shows that the sesamoid bone is calcified but may not be completely calcified just yet. 

Basic Diagnoses

Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocations
AP maxillary deficiency
Transverse maxillary excess
Mandibular prognathism
Class III end-on occlusion being slightly worse on the right side as compared to the left side
Mandibular dental midline shifted a millimeter to the left
Anterior open bite
Impacted maxillary and mandibular third molars
Unerupted maxillary second molars with possible ankylosis
TMJ pain
Headaches
Myofascial pain
High occlusal plane angle of 15 degrees (normal 8 degrees)
Slight overangulation of maxillary incisors
Tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 1.5 mm
Slight increased curve of Wilson in the upper arch
Hypertrophied turbinates
Extremely wide columella creating narrow nostrils
Partial nasal airway obstruction

Recommended Treatment

Presurgical orthodontics 
Continue to align and level maxillary arch. Try to decrease transverse width in the bicuspid area. Molars appear to have fairly good transverse width to match up with the lowers
Continue to align and level mandibular arch. Strip lower anterior teeth removing about a millimeter of tooth structure to help compensate for the tooth-size discrepancy. Include the second molars in the arch wire. Try to tip the first molars lingually slightly as they have just a slight reverse curve of Wilson associated with them

Preliminary surgery
Consider uncovering maxillary second molars and see if they can be erupted into occlusion (be careful as they may be ankylosed, in which case it may be of advantage to have the maxillary second molars removed but let the third molars alone for the time being and see if they would eventually erupt into the second molar position)

Continue to monitor growth and development to be sure that mandibular growth is complete

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors 
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla forward, upright the incisors, and correct any residual transverse discrepancy as well as decrease the occlusal plane angle
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to rotate the mandible back into good occlusion with the maxilla
Removal of four impacted third molars
Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies
Reconstruct columella and enlarge nostrils
