original_note:
CARMEN WATERS									01/22/2010

Carmen is 58 years old.  She is referred by Dr. David Curtis, 5866 S. Staples #201, Corpus Christi, Texas 78413.  Carmen is not sure when her TMJ problems began, but thinks it was back around her 20s of unknown etiology.  She was not really treated until 2006, but she since then has been wearing splints.  She has a daytime and a nighttime splint.  She does have improvement with the splint therapy.  Carmen gets headaches at about a level of 4 in the frontal, temporal and posterior head area.  She rates her TMJ pain at 5, average daily pain at 5, jaw function at 4, diet at 8, and disability at 6.  She wakes with headaches in the morning which would go along with the clenching that she does at nighttime, but she also clenches during the day.  She has constant earaches but no tinnitus, occasional vertigo but no hearing loss.  Other joints that bother her include her knees, ankles, neck and back.  She has moderate snoring, moderate daytime, somnolence, but is unaware of sleep apnea issues.  She has a moderate problem sleeping and is a nighttime and daytime mouth breather.

CLINICAL EVALUATION.  In frontal view, Carmen has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 23 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 1.5 mm.  The maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline.  The mandibular dental midline is about 1 mm off to the right side, and the chin is about 2 mm off to the right side.  She has a 5 mm anterior open bite.  In profile view, Carmen has some AP maxillary deficiency of probably about 3-4 mm.  The chin is deficient by about 15 mm.  The chin neckline is not defined.  Intraorally, Carmen has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  She has some huge lateral exostoses on the mandibular left side.  She also has a little bit of that around the cuspids.  She has some mild crowding of the lower anterior arch, but has adequate attached gingiva.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  She has some buccal exostosis in the maxillary right posterior quadrant.  She has a slight Class II cuspid relationship on the right side of 1-2 mm, but on the left side she is basically Class I.  She hits primarily on her second molars and I believe all the other teeth are out of occlusion.  Intraorally, Carmen has a large lingual tori as well.  Intranasally, Carmen has a pretty open airway and turbinate area on the right side and in the left side it is a little bit more narrow.  She does have some webbing in the luminal valve area.  The nostrils appear a little bit narrow. 

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars.  She has the rest of her teeth.  She has a root canal on the mandibular right second bicuspid and a crown on the first molar.  The left sinus appears a little bit cloudy.  The bony septum appears reasonably straight.  The condyles appear arthritic.

Coronal view shows that the sinuses actually appear pretty clear.  The turbinates are significantly enlarged.  She has a significantly large septal spur that deviates off towards the left side and seems to impinge on the lateral nasal wall.  

Axial view shows that the sinuses are clear.  The septum has some curvature to it, and the turbinates appear moderately enlarged and appear to fill up a good part of the nasal airway.
Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is quite arthritic and appears to have lost volume.  There is anterior beaking and sclerosis on the head of the condyle.  There is actually decreased anterior joint space and the superior and posterior joint space is increased.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is quite arthritic with anterior beaking and sclerosis.  There is decreased anterior joint space, but superior and posterior joint space may be slightly increased. 

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is arthritic and appears to have lost volume and perhaps a little bit of transverse width.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2-3 mm.  There appears to be almost bone-on-bone contact at the lateral rim of the fossa.  The medial side appears to have pretty good joint space.  

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is severely arthritic and there appears to be bone-on-bone contact at the lateral rim of the fossa.  The lateral rim curves inferiorly about 3-4 mm.  The medial vertical joint space looks relatively normal. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Carmen has a retruded mandible.  The maxilla may be slightly AP deficient as well.  She has a significantly reduced oropharyngeal airway of only about 2 mm at the soft palate level and 4 mm at the base of the tongue.  She has a high occlusal plane angle in the mandible.  

Basic diagnoses would be as follows:

Bilateral TMJ severe arthritis.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia.
Maxillary AP hypoplasia.
Anterior open bite (5 mm).
Occluding only on the second molars bilaterally.
TMJ pain.
Headaches.
Myofascial pain. 
Significantly decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Sleep apnea. 
11. Hypertrophied nasal turbinates	
12. Decreased oropharyngeal airway		
13. Nasal septal deviation and spurring	

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol).
2.	Surgery.
	a.	Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts 			total joint prostheses. 
	b.	Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen).
	c.	Maxillary osteotomies to advance it forward about 4 mm.
            d.	Application of maxillary and mandibular arch bars	
            e.	Nasoseptoplasty
            f.	Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomy

I gave Carmen a prescription for Klonopin 1-mg tablets, dispensing 30 tablets, 1 tablet q.h.s., refill x5.

original_note:
B504
B504 is 49 years old and referred by Dr. XXXX.

B504 was not aware of having facial asymmetry when she was 15 years of age. At the age of 19, she developed Bell's palsy on the right side. That has never fully recovered. She said there may have been a little bit of asymmetry noticeable at that time. She feels as though the facial asymmetry has gotten progressively worse over the years. Although she feels the asymmetry has been getting progressively worse, she has not had any jaw problems until 11 months ago when she began noticing difficulties with her jaw including pain as well as limited function. She also began developing muscle spasms. Her problems began 11 months ago of unknown etiology. Since then she has had a number of treatments which include the following:

Novocain injections to the right TMJ x3 with only temporary relief.
Acupuncture. Chiropractic treatment. Splint therapy, but the splint broke secondary to clenching. Eight months ago, she was put in the hospital and under general anesthesia, her jaws were manipulated and then she had an arthrocentesis of the right TMJ, but this has not relieved her symptoms or improved her jaw function.

B504 gets headaches predominantly in the back of the head at about a level of 7, and she gets those headaches about 3 times per week. She rates her TMJ pain at 7, average daily pain at 5, jaw function at 9, diet at 7, disability at 9. She gets occasional right-sided earaches and occasional vertigo. She does get tinnitus and the tinnitus is on the right side. She sometimes gets a squishy in her joints, particularly in the right side. She is aware of clenching and bruxing her teeth and jaws at nighttime and also during the day. However, she does have some moderate snoring, mild daytime somnolence, but she is not aware of any sleep apnea issues. She is predominantly a mouth breather at nighttime.

Primary concerns that B504 has at this time includes the following:
Limited mouth opening. Unable to get food in her mouth properly with utensils. Jaw aches when she chews.
Jaw is locked and out alignment. Teeth and jaw out of alignment. Difficulty speaking properly.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
In frontal view, B504 has some facial asymmetry present. Upper lip length is about 17 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is 6 mm. The maxillary midline seem to be pretty much on the facial midline. Nasal tip is almost 6 mm off towards the right side. The center of the upper lip is off to the right side about 7 mm. The mandibular dental midline and chin midline are probably about 8 mm off towards the right side. There is a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the left side being 3 mm lower than the right side. In profile view, she has prominence of the nasal dorsum. The maxilla appears to be AP deficient by probably a good 6 mm. Chin appears to be retruded by probably 15 mm. Chin neckline is short, although chin neck angle is reasonably well defined. Vertical height of the mandible on the left side may be just slightly longer than the right side by maybe 2-3 mm, but not too significant. The left angle of the mandible is more prominent than the right side by probably 5 mm. Intraorally, B504 has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars and all of her upper teeth back through the second molars. She is missing an upper bicuspid on the left side. She has an anterior deep bite of about 6-7 mm. She is Class I cuspid on the left side and Class II cuspid on the right side. The upper arch is quite asymmetric with the left cuspid being probably 5-6 mm posterior to the right cuspid. The lower arch is more difficult to evaluate relative to asymmetry. She has a hyper-erupted mandibular left lateral incisor. Transversely, the lower arch is a little bit more symmetric, being only about 1 mm lower on the left side than the right side, whereas the upper cuspid is 3 mm lower on the left side compared to the right side. As best as can be evaluated with the extreme limited opening, the oropharyngeal tissues look relatively normal. Incisal opening is about 12 mm counting her overbite. Excursion movement to the right 9 mm, to the left less than 2 mm. Curve of Wilson on the maxillary right side is somewhat increased and on the left side is somewhat decreased. She feels as though she gets significant muscle strain down through her neck and into her shoulder area. This may involve the platysmal muscle as well. To palpation, she mild to moderate pain through most of the head and neck area. She has some decreased sensation in the right cheek. She has limited function of the buccal and mandibular branches of the facial nerve on the right side.

Nineteen years ago, she had a horse accident and it may have broken her nose at that time. She apparently had a fracture along the base of the nose, at least on the right side. This may account for some of the nasal asymmetry. About 11 or 12 years ago, she was moving a TV and it fell and struck her on the nose and in the right infraorbital area and perhaps she had a fracture in that area as well. Joints seem to function relatively smoothly from a clinical impression, but she hears a clicking noise in her ear on the right side. When she swallows, she gets a clicking noise that is more obvious on her right side than her left side. Intranasally, she has a deviated septum off towards the left side, and the turbinates appear fairly significant in size.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars and the maxillary left first bicuspid. Left condyle appears enlarged compared to the right side, and the condylar neck appears elongated. Vertical height of the mandible may be just slightly increased on the left side compared to the right side. The turbinates appear enlarged. Axial view shows that sinuses are clear. Turbinates are quite large. The septum deviates off to the left side anteriorly, but seems more straight back in the bony septal area. Coronal view shows that the sinuses are clear, but the turbinates are quite large and probable obstruct the nose significant. Left TMJ coronal view tomograms shows that the condyle is significantly larger than the opposite side in mediolateral dimension as well as vertical. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly probably about 2-3 mm. There is decreased lateral joint space.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is much small in size. There is decreased superior joint space. The condyle is well confined within the fossa. Lateral rim curves inferiorly probably about 3 mm. Width of the right condyle is about 13 mm at its greatest dimension and on the side is 16 mm in transverse dimension. Left TMJ sagittal tomograms shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology. There is decreased posterior joint space and vertical joint space, particularly towards the lateral aspect of the joint. The condylar head and neck are quite long. Left articular eminence is moderately shallow.
Right TMJ sagittal views shows decreased superior and posterior joint spaces. The condyle appears a little bit more arthritic on this side with some flattening on the anterior head at the lateral aspect of the joint. The articular eminence is relatively normal. Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that maxilla and mandible are retruded anteroposteriorly. There is a short upper lip. There is an anterior maxillary excess. Oropharyngeal airway at AP dimension is only about 4 mm at the soft palate level and at the base of the tongue about 5 mm. She has an anterior deep bite and a skeletal Class II jaw relationship.

In reevaluation in the frontal view, I believe the upper incisors are probably 2 mm to the left of the facial midline and the mandibular dental midline is 6 mm to the right and chin probably 7-8 mm to the right.

Basic diagnoses for B504 would be as follows:
Left mandibular condylar osteoma with questionable active growth. Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly dislocated with a closed lock situation. Maxillary AP hypoplasia and transverse asymmetry with the left side being 3 mm lower than the right side.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia and asymmetry with the lower mandibular dental midline being shifted 6 mm off to the right and chin shifted 8 mm off to the right. There is a transverse cant where the left side is 1-2 mm lower than the right side. Class I cuspid relationship on the left side, Class II cuspid relatively on the right side. Hypertrophied turbinates with nasal airway obstruction. Decreased oropharyngeal airway of 5 mm (normal dimension 11 mm).
Maxillary arch asymmetry with the left cuspid being 5-6 mm posterior to the right cuspid.
Nasal asymmetry with the tip being shifted approximately 6 mm to the right.
TMJ pain. Myofascial pain. Headaches.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
Surgery Stage 1: Right TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor. Presurgical orthodontics.
Extract maxillary right second bicuspid. Align and level maxillary arch closing the extraction site. Burn posterior anchorage and retract the first bicuspid-cuspid back against the molar. Shift maxillary midline to the right 2 mm. Open spaces between the laterals and cuspids if necessary to keep from over-retracting the upper incisors. Align and level mandibular arch. Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing about 2 mm of tooth structure. Surgery Stage 2. Left TMJ high condylectomy if active growth is still occurring with TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to shift the mandible back on the facial midline. Maxillary osteotomies to shift the maxilla towards the left and back onto the facial midline. Bilateral partial turbinectomies. Nasal osteotomies to shift the nose towards the left side about 6 mm.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B013 B013 is 41 years old and referred by XXXX. The primary concerns that B013 has include the following: Uncomfortable around the jaws and jaw joint area.
Headache.
Eye pain. TMJ pain. Ear pain. Difficult to eat.
Changes in her facial appearance since undergoing previous orthodontic treatment.

HISTORY. B013's TMJ problems began about one year ago when she developed some pain and noises in the TMJ joints of unknown etiology. She previously had orthodontic treatment beginning three years ago and had her braces removed one year and 10 months ago. She is unhappy with the result.

B013 gets headaches at a level of 5 in the frontal and temporal area and behind her eyes. She rates her TMJ pain at 5, average daily pain at 6, jaw function at 5, diet at 1, disability at 1. She gets earaches, occasional tinnitus, and she has significant problems with vertigo. She feels that she may have some hearing problems, but her tests come out relatively normal. Other joints that bother her include her shoulders, wrists, hands, fingers, spine, knees, and hips. She has been previously diagnosed with the following conditions:

Ankylosing spondylitis.
Fibromyalgia.
Hashimoto thyroiditis.
Inflammatory arthritis.
She gets significant pain behind her eyes and, even though her vision is good, she gets this significant pain. She is aware of clenching at night time and also during the day time. She takes the following medications:

Topamax 50 mg q.h.s.
Relpax.
Oracea.
Fish oil.
Vitamin D with calcium.
B013 has had both of her children via C section. She had a hysterectomy on one year and eight months ago, and tonsillectomy four years ago. She did have breast implants nine years ago.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
In frontal view, she has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 20 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is about 2 mm. The maxillary midline is just slightly to the left at about 1 mm. Mandibular dental midline and chin are actually pretty much on the facial midline. In profile view, there is about a 2 to 3 mm AP maxillary deficiency. The mandible appears to be AP retruded. She has a prominent labial mental fold. The chin appears to be retruded by probably about 4 to 6 mm. Chin neckline is somewhat shortened and chin neck angle is not well defined. Intraorally, B013 is missing the crown on her mandibular right second bicuspid and may be missing the first molar and the second and third molar have perhaps drifted forward. On the left side, she is missing the lower first molar and the second and third molars have drifted somewhat forward in medial inclination. In the upper arch, B013 has all of her teeth back through the second molars. She has a Division 2 occlusal relationship. She has a full Class II cuspid relationship on the right side and Class II end-on on the left side by about 5 mm. She has an anterior deep bite of probably a good 8 to 9 mm. The entire mandibular crown is covered by the maxillary incisors when she bites together. She has a significant reverse curve of occlusion in the upper arch and an accentuated curve of occlusion in the lower arch. She has a buccal crossbite on the right side affecting the maxillary molars and bicuspids as well as the cuspid. Oropharyngeal tissues all look normal. Intranasal structures all appears quite healthy. In the frontal view, the left side of the jaw appears slightly longer vertically than the right side, so the left side is longer than the right side. There is some transverse asymmetry in the upper arch with the right cuspid being about 1 mm lower than the left cuspid, but in the lower arch the lower right cuspids are about 1 to 2 mm higher than the left cuspids. Nasal airway looks normal. She does have a history of some snoring, and she has moderate to severe daytime somnolence. She may have some mild sleep apnea symptoms. She is uncomfortable in the neck and chest area when walking and she is not sure why that is. To palpation, she has moderate to high moderate pain through the temporalis, masseters, TMJs, styloid areas, shoulders, and left SCM. In joint function, the joints function reasonably smoothly, although she could hearing a rubbing like bone-on-bone kind of noise in the right joint and she said she has had this in the left side as well. Maximal incisal opening is 50 mm. Excursion movements in each direction were 8 mm, but this was painful for her. In excursion movements, she could feel a painful grinding nose in the left TMJ. She could open about 30 mm without significant discomfort.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of the mandibular left first molar, mandibular right first molar, and on both sides the second and third molars have drifted forward. She has a retained mandibular right second bicuspid, but without a crown. Sinuses look relatively clear. Condyles appear to have reasonably good shape. She has a crown on the maxillary left first molar and mandibular left second bicuspid root. Waters view shows that the bony septum is reasonably straight. Turbinates are moderate in size, and they may be larger posteriorly than they are anteriorly. Sinuses appear relatively clear. Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle with fairly good morphology. There may be a slight decreased joint space superiorly and posteriorly. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology. Joint space may be a little bit narrow superiorly. There is a little bit of flattening on the anterior head of the condyle. On opening, there is good translation of both condyles. Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has a skeletal Class II jaw position with a probably relatively low occlusal plane angle. She has a Class II Division 2 malocclusion. Oropharyngeal airway actually looks fairly good with an AP dimension of about 10 to 11 mm at the base of the tongue and also at the soft palate level. She has a brachiocephalic facial morphology. Pogonion is strong compared to the AP position of the lower incisors. Tooth size analysis shows she has a Bolton's difference of 1.5 mm, but it is probably greater than that because of the AP dimension of the maxillary incisors which are thick, probably increasing it to about 2 to 2.5 mm. Basic diagnoses for B013 include the following:
Bilateral TMJ ankylosing spondylitis.
Mandibular hypoplasia.
Transverse maxillary hyperplasia.
TMJ pain.
Headaches.
Myofascial pain.
Class II Division 2 malocclusion.
Anterior deep bite of 9 mm. Left posterior buccal crossbite.
Missing mandibular left and right first molars and a crown of the mandibular right secondary bicuspid.
Reverse curve of occlusion upper arch.
Accentuated curve of occlusion lower arch.
Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of about 2 to 2.5 mm. Ankylosing spondylitis.
Fibromyalgia.
Hashimoto's thyroiditis.
Inflammatory arthritis.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch. Open about 1 mm of space around each maxillary lateral incisors and cuspid. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally in case interdental osteotomies are necessary.
Align and level mandibular arch. Maintain lower spacing in the left first molar area to equal the size of a bicuspid and maintain spacing on the right side to equal the size of a bicuspid in the area of the retained second bicuspid root.
Consider turbos on the maxillary incisors and even possibly on the second molars to open the bite and aid in leveling of the maxillary and mandibular arches. CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol).
Surgery. Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses.
Bilateral coronoidectomies.
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen).
Possible maxillary osteotomies if necessary to achieve a good functional occlusion.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
Dental reconstruction to replace the mandibular left first molar and right second bicuspid.

B013 MRI EVALUATION
Left TMJ coronal views shows a condyle that is fairly enlarged for the size of the fossa. It extends slightly beyond the lateral rim of the fossa. Decreased lateral joint space is present. Discs may be medially displaced.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is quite large for the size of the fossa. It may just extend slightly beyond the lateral rim, but not too much and not quite as much as the opposite side. There is decreased lateral joint space. The disc appears to be medially displaced.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has some flattening of the head of the condyle particularly at the lateral aspect of the joint. I do not see any discal tissue at the most lateral aspect, but there is discal tissue medially and appears to be in reasonably good position. On opening, there is good translation of the condyle and disc. The condyle has a slight hockey stick shape to it.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that likely has a little bit of flattening at the lateral aspect of the joint. Decreased joint space laterally. Disc tissue not really seen. More medially, the disc appears to be good position. Translations of the condyle and disc are very good.

IMPRESSION:
Bilateral TMJ arthritis, particularly at the lateral aspect of the joint and medial displacement of the articular disc.

JUSTIN OLSEN									03/10/2010
DATE OF BIRTH:	06/17/1982
AGE:	27 

The patient previously was diagnosed with unilateral right condylar hyperplasia and on April 15, 1994, had a condylectomy performed on the right TMJ.  He apparently has had no other surgical procedures to the jaws.  The primary concerns that he has at this time are as follows:

Jaw pain right side. 
Headaches right side and top of head as well as in the forehead area.
Facial pain and pressure in both cheeks.
Eyes burning and pain behind right eye.

He had a pituitary tumor removed in August 2007 and he was on prednisone until November 7.  Then his jaw started to hurt and he got daily headaches on the right side and top of the head and forehead area since that time.  The headaches occur daily and in addition he has moderate neck pain.  He is not aware of clenching or bruxing at night.  He has no trouble with earaches, ringing in the ears, or lightheadedness or dizziness.  No other joints bother him.  He rates his TMJ pain at 4, headaches at 7, and average daily pain around the head and neck area at 3.  He rates his jaw function at 1, diet at 1, and did not rate his disability.

He takes the following medications:

Diazepam 5 mg tablets.
Magnesium 250 mg tablets.
Prilosec 25 mg.
Unisom sleeping tablet 1 tablet each night.

He is currently suffering from an upset stomach with the use of Tylenol with Codeine No. 3.  He does not appear to have any significant airway issues, although he does have occasional loud snoring and moderate daytime tiredness.  He does have some mild difficulties sleeping at night. 

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars, but the rest of the teeth are present.  The right condyle is quite flattened and very short in vertical height secondary to previous surgery.  There is cortical bone across the top of the condyle.  The condyle has a mushroom shape to it.  The articular eminence looks quite flat.  The sinuses look relatively clear.  The nasal septum looks good and turbinates appear relatively normal in size.


Left TMJ appears to have a relatively normal architecture.  There is a little bit of anterior beaking on the condylar head.  The joint space may be slightly decreased posteriorly.  The articular eminence is moderately steep.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is postured somewhat forward in the fossa beneath the flattened articular eminence.  The head of the condyle is quite flat.  There is decreased vertical joint space at the anterior aspect of the condyle.  The articular eminence is fairly flat.

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology and width, but there is probably decreased joint space on the anterior left side.  

Right coronal view shows a condyle that is quite flattened with significant loss of vertical volume.  It is fairly close between the fossa and the head of the condyle.  There is good airway.  He has a skeletal and occlusal Class I relationship.  There is a slight vertical discrepancy at the occlusal plane level and inferior border of the mandible with the right side being shorter by about 3 mm.  

Dental model analyses shows that he has a Class I cuspid-molar on the left side and a Class II end-on cuspid-molar relationship on the right side.  The mandibular dental midline is shifted 2 mm off to the right side.  

I suspect he had a low condylectomy performed and the articular disc is probably repositioned over the condyle.  

Diagnoses:

Previously treated condylar hyperplasia of right mandibular condyle with low condylectomy.
Right TMJ pain. 
Right-sided headaches.
Class II end-on occlusion on the right side and Class I occlusion on the left side.
Probable mild facial asymmetry with the chin shifted to the right (no photographs were available for evaluation).

Recommended surgery would be as follows:

Right TMJ reconstruction with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses.
Fat graft to right TMJ.

original_note:
TRAVIS LANTZ								04/04/2011

Travis is 18 years old, birth date 12/17/1992.  Referred by Dr. Farhad Moshiri, 1320 E. Kingsley, Suite B, Springfield, MO 65804.

Primary concerns that Travis has are as follows:

Difficulties eating and chewing.  
Headaches. 
TMJ pain. 
Teeth and jaws are not lined up properly.

Travis’ TMJ problems began at about the age of 10 years old in 2001 when he began having clicking in his jaw joints of unknown etiology.  When Travis was 9 years old, he got shot in the mouth with a BB gun and it hit the maxillary left central incisor shattering it.  The tooth was extracted.  Also, about that same time, he had some orthodontic treatment consistently predominantly a maxillary expander.  He has had some other basic treatments done.  In December 2009, he got full orthodontic appliances on for the first time and continues to have them on.  On November 13, 2008, TADS were placed in the anterior maxilla so that a face mask could be used which he wore for 2 to 3 years, but this was ineffective as far as correcting his jaw malalignment problems.  

Travis gets headaches about 3 times per week in the temporal and posterior head area bilaterally at a level of 7.  He rates his TMJ pain at 3, average daily pain at 7, jaw function at 6, diet at 3, disability at 3.  He has no problems with earaches, but gets occasional tinnitus and vertigo but has no hearing loss.  There are no other joints that bother him.  He is aware of clenching at nighttime and also during the daytime.  Travis does have problems with car sickness and it is thought it may be related to his jaw joint issues.  Travis is not aware of snoring, daytime somnolence, or sleep apnea.  He does have a moderate sleep disorder.  At nighttime and also during the daytime, he breathes a lot through his mouth.  

In frontal view, Travis has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 17 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is 2.5 mm.  The mandibular dental midline and chin are pretty much on the facial midline.  The maxillary dental midline is shifted about 2 mm to the left.  In profile view, Travis is about 6 mm AP deficient.  The chin may be retruded about 8 mm.  The chin neckline is a little bit short, although the chin neck angle is reasonably well defined with some increased fullness in the submental area.  He has a basically a Class III cuspid relationship by about 3 mm.  There is an anterior cross bite of probably 5 mm.  He has crenations on the borders of his tongue all the way around.  He has about a 0.5 mm anterior open bite.  He has an anterior cross bite, but the maxilla may be slightly transversely hyperplastic.  Oropharyngeal airway looks like the tissues are all healthy.  He has a moderately enlarged uvula.  To palpation, he has some mild tenderness over the left styloid area, but other no tenderness to palpation.  Right joint function smoothly.  The left joint has a click at about 35 mm of opening and 10 mm of closing.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows absence of the mandibular left central incisors.  He has TADS in bilaterally that extend down just mesial to the lateral incisors.  The third molars are missing, but all the rest of the teeth are present.  

Coronal view shows that the septum is reasonably straight.  The inferior turbinates are quite enlarged blocking most of the functional airway.   There is some significant increased thickness of the sinus membrane in the left maxillary sinus compatible with sinusitis.  

Axial view shows that the turbinates are fairly significant in size and could be blocking a significant part of the airway.  The septum is fairly straight.  The inferior turbinates are hyperplastic and appear to block some of the functional airway.  The left sinus has some significant thickening of the mucosal lining indicative of likely sinusitis.  The right sinus looks fairly clear with just some very minimal elevation and thickening of the mucous membrane.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology.  The condyle is larger than the opposite side.  The joint space is fairly well balanced.  The articular eminence has a moderate inclination, but is fairly short in length.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has some flattening on the anterior head.  The joint space, however, looks reasonably good.  The articular eminence is relatively short in length.  Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with fairly good mediolateral width.  The lateral pole is significantly lower than the medial pole.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 4 mm.  There is decreased joint space at the lateral aspect of the joint between the condyle and the lateral rim of the fossa.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has very good width, although not quite as large as the opposite side.  The lateral pole is lower in vertical position compared to the medial condylar pole.  There is decreased lateral joint space.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves fairly significantly probably 4 or 5 mm and there is decreased joint space there. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Travis has a very low occlusal plane angle.  He has a Class III skeletal and occlusal relationship.  Oropharyngeal airway looks very good.  


Basic diagnoses would be as follows:

Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation.
Maxillary AP hypoplasia but transverse hyperplasia.
Mandibular AP hyperplasia (prognathism).
Mandibular microgenia.
Anterior cross bite extending from cuspid through cuspid. 
Class III end-on occlusion being approximately 3 mm Class III.
Missing maxillary left central incisor.  
Over-angulation of maxillary incisors and mandibular incisors.
Excessively expanded maxillary arch.  
Increased curve of Wilson in the bicuspid molar area.
Mild form of hypernasal speech quality.  
Hypertrophied turbinates with nasal airway obstruction.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Extract maxillary right first or second bicuspid (the second bicuspid may be a 	better selection for extraction).
2	Presurgical orthodontics.
	a.	Align and level maxillary arch.  Loose some posterior anchorage, but 			retract the bicuspid and incisors approximately 4 or 5 mm.  Open up 1 mm 		of space between the upper cuspid and lateral incisors.  Center the 				maxillary left lateral incisor between the right central and left cuspid so 
                        that there is a span of 8 mm from the mesial of the right central to the 
                        mesial of the left cuspid.  Tip the left cuspid root mesially and first 
                        bicuspid root distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. Narrow 			the transverse width of the upper arch some if possible particularly in the 			bicuspid and molar areas.
	b.	Continue to align and level lower arch.  Slenderizing the lower anterior 			teeth removing about 2 mm of tooth structure and retract.  
3.	Surgery.
	a.	Left TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek 			anchors.
	b.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to correct the transverse width.
	c.	Bilateral mandibular rams osteotomies to set the mandible posteriorly. 
	d.	Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy to augment the chin.
	e.	Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies. 
4.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion. 
5.	Restorative dentistry to build up the maxillary left lower incisor that will now be 	functioning as a central incisor.  Lateralize the left maxillary cuspid to make it 	appear more like a lateral incisor.

AMANDA RACZ									02/15/2008

Amanda is 13 years old and a self-referral.  She has a history of asthma and uses a ProAir HFA inhaler and albuterol.  She has had an adverse reaction to amoxicillin.  According to Amanda, her jaw problems began in about June 2007 when she developed some clicking noises in her jaw joints of unknown etiology.  She began orthodontic treatment in August 2004 and the braces were removed in September 2007.  Earlier in the orthodontic treatment, she  had expanders in both the upper and lower jaw, but did not receive her full braces until she was 11 ½ years old.  She does have retainers. She has had no other treatment for her TMJ issues.  

Amanda gets daily headaches about a level of a 6 on the right side and perhaps a level of 4 on the left side in the frontal, temporal, and top of the head area.  She rates her TMJ pain at 7, average daily pain at 6, jaw function at 4, diet at 5, disability at 2.  She gets earaches, right side worse than the left side, occasional tinnitus, occasional vertigo.  No other joints bother her.  She is not aware of clenching or bruxism.  Primary concerns that Amanda has would include the following:

	1.	Jaw joint pain.
	2.	Headaches.
	3.	Difficulties eating and chewing.
	4.	Difficulties breathing through the nose.
	
CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  Amanda does have some dark circles beneath her eyes.  Upper lip length is 20 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is 3 mm.  Maxillary, mandibular, and chin midline are all pretty much on the facial midline.  In profile view, Amanda has a head postured forward neck position.  Maxillas are retruded by probably 5-6 mm and chin is retruded by at least 12 mm.  Chin neckline is short and chin neck angle is not well defined.  Intraorally, Amanda has all of her teeth back to the second molars.  She has a 3 x 3 retainer in the lower arch.  She appears to have adequate attached gingiva.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back to the second molars.  She has a slight Class II occlusal tendency and about a 2-mm overjet.  She has about 0.5 mm vertical overbite relationship.  Oropharyngeal tissues all look relatively normal.  Tonsils are moderately enlarged.  In the past, she has had trouble with strep throat, getting it at least twice a year, but she has not had it for about 2 years.  Amanda may have some mild snoring problems, but nothing real significant.  She apparently has some moderate daytime tiredness that she feels has been getting worse more recently.  She is not aware of sleep apnea problems.  She has some mild tenderness of the temporalis muscles, masseters, medial pterygoids, styloid areas, and slightly higher with TMJs.  Joints seem to function reasonably smooth on opening, but on excursion she had a glitch in the right joint.  On opening, the mandible does deviate significantly probably 3-4 mm to the left side.  Maximal opening is 45 mm, opening without significant discomfort 35 mm.  Excursion movements to the left 8 mm, to the right 9 mm.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the presence of four developing third molars.  She has a 3 x 3 retainer in the lower anterior arch.  Sinuses look relatively clear.  Turbinates appear moderately enlarged.  Difficult to evaluate the joints.  Waters view shows that the sinuses are clear.  Nasal septum is reasonably straight.  Turbinates are moderately enlarged.  Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has undergone some resorption.  There is flattening on the anterior head and a little bit of peaking on the top of condyle.  Articular eminence is moderately steep and somewhat elongated.  The joint space looks fairly well balanced.  Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears to have a little bit of flattening on the anterior and it appears it has lost some volume.  There may be some decreased superior and posterior joint space.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  On opening, there is moderate translation of both joints.  Lateral cephalometric radiographic shows that she has a rotated maxilla and mandible.  She has a high occlusal plane angle.  There is some adenoid tissue still in the nasopharynx.  Tonsils are moderately enlarged.  However, the oropharyngeal airway looks reasonably good.  She has a skeletal Class II and slight occlusal Class II occlusal relationship with about a zero overbite and about 2 mm overjet.  

The basic diagnoses would be as follows:

1.	Bilateral TMJ adolescent internal condylar resorption.
2.	AP maxillary deficiency.  
3.	Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
4.  	AP mandibular deficiency.
5.	Slight Class II malocclusion. 
6.	TMJ pain.
7.	Headaches.
8.  	Hypertrophied turbinates.
9.	Nasal airway obstruction.
10.	Impacted third molars x4.
11.	Nasopharyngeal adenoid tissue, but probably not causing a significant airway problem.  

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

	1.  	Presurgical orthodontics.
a.  Align and level maxillary arch.  Tip lateral incisor roots medially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. 
b.  Align and level mandibular arch.  
	2.  	Surgery.
a.  Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.  
b.  Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance it forward and down graft it posteriorly.  
b.  Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction.
d.  Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies. 
e.  Surgical removal of impacted third molars x4. 
f.  Consider possible reduction of the nasopharyngeal adenoid tissue.
3.  	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B418 
B418 is 19 years old . Referred by Dr.XX

B418's TMJ problems began when she was about 15 years old of unknown etiology. For the past 2 years, she has noted that her chin is becoming more retruded and, particularly in the last 2 months, it seems to be a little bit more progressive. She had what sounds like a night guard 2 years ago that was not particularly effective. She received a TMJ splint this year. This splint helps decrease some of her pain issues. Drs. XX made the splint. She has had chiropractic treatment including adjustments and ultrasound treatment. She gets headaches at about a level of 5 in the temporal and posterior head area. She rates her TMJ pain at 6, average daily pain at 5, jaw function at 6, diet at 7, disability at 6. She takes Advil as her only medication. She has no earaches, tinnitus, gets occasional vertigo but no hearing loss. The only other joints that bother her are her lower back. She is allergic to AMOXICILLIN and SULFA. She is aware of clenching at nighttime, but does not believe she clenches during the day. She does not snore. She has some mild daytime somnolence but no sleep apnea. She is predominantly a mouth breather at night.

In frontal view, Chrsitine has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 20 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is about 4 mm. The maxillary, mandibular, and chin midline are all pretty much on the facial midline. In profile view, B418 has a retruded maxilla with upper lip supralabrale being probably at least 5 mm posterior to subnasale. She is probably 6 to 8 mm AP deficient at the incisor tips and probably 15 to 18 mm AP deficiency in the chin area. Intraorally, B418 is missing the lower second bicuspids and some mild crowding in the lower anterior arch. She has lack of attached gingiva around the right central incisor. In the upper arch, she is probably missing the first bicuspids. As she closes together, it appears that she may hit around the first molar area on the right side and then shifts into a better occlusion. She is Class II by about 3 mm on the left and 2 mm on the right. She has about a 1 mm vertical overbite. The maxillary arch appears transversely narrow particularly in the cuspid-bicuspid area. Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively normal and healthy. Oropharyngeal airway looks normal and healthy. Intranasally, turbinates are enlarged but septum is relatively straight. She probably has a moderate amount of nasal airway obstruction. Columella is slightly widened and nostrils are somewhat narrow. To palpation, she has high moderate tenderness over the left TMJ and moderate tenderness over the left masseter muscle. In jaw function, she experiences crepitus in the left joint on opening, but in both joints on excursion movements. The maximal incisal opening is 35 mm, opening without significant pain 27 mm. The excursion movement to the right is 4 mm and to the left 2 mm.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molar, maxillary first bicuspids, mandibular second bicuspids. Sinuses look relatively clear. Turbinates appears enlarged. Both condyles appear arthritic.

Coronal view shows enlarged turbinates, but the septum appears straight and the sinuses appear clear.
Axial views show the sinuses to be clear and turbinates to be moderately enlarged.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a destructive lesion on the anterior head of the condyle. In other areas, there is beaking of the condyle and some peaking of the condyle as well. The articular eminence appears to have some erosions on the anterior aspect of it. There is decreased joint space in one of the views with the anterior aspect of the condyle and posterior aspect of the eminence. The articular eminence is relatively short in length particularly towards the medial side. There appears to be increased posterior joint space. The condyle appears small anteroposteriorly and appears to have lost vertical volume.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has undergone significant degenerative changes. The condyle appears small in size anteroposteriorly and appears to have lost vertical volume. The articular eminence is slightly longer on this side compared to the opposite side. There is decreased anterior joint space. There appears to be an erosion but to a lesser extent compared to the opposite side on the anterior aspect of the condylar head.

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has undergone significant arthritic changes. There are erosions and significant degeneration of the cortical and subcondylar bone on the head of the condyle. There is a lateral extension however at the lateral pole that appears to be somewhat sclerotic. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly probably about 5 mm and there appears to be bone-on-bone contact with the lateral aspect of the condyle and the lateral rim of the fossa.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears to have lost vertical volume and has some erosive areas in it. The lateral rim curves inferiorly about 4 to 5 mm. There is a lateral extension of the condyle that appears somewhat sclerotic extending out almost beyond the lateral rim of the fossa.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has a retruded maxilla and mandible and appears to occlude predominantly on the second molars. She has an anterior over jet of about 4 mm and a tendency towards an open bite. Oropharyngeal airway measures about almost 8 mm at the soft palate level and 6 to 7 mm at the base of the tongue. She has significant difficulty eating and chewing and has to eat extremely soft foods.

Basic diagnoses for B418 would be as follows:
Bilateral TMJ resorptive arthritis. Severe mandibular AP hypoplasia.
Maxillary AP and transverse hypoplasia. Class II end-on occlusion.
Anterior open bite tendency. Moderate crowding in lower anterior arch. High occlusal plane angle of 16 degrees (normal 8 degrees).
Missing maxillary first bicuspids and mandibular second bicuspids.
Hypertrophied turbinates causing nasal airway obstruction. TMJ pain.
Headaches. Gingival recession on mandibular right incisor.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1.	Periodontal evaluation and gingival grafting lower anterior arch.
2.	Presurgical orthodontics. a.	Align and level maxillary arch. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. b.	Align and level mandibular arch. Consider slenderizing the lower anterior teeth removing about 1 mm of tooth structure to keep from flaring them too far forward.
3.	CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol).
4.	Surgery. a.	Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement of about 16 mm at pogonion using TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. b.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance it in a counterclockwise direction. c.	Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen). d.	Bilateral coronoidotomies. e.	Bilateral partial turbinectomies.
5.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B418  MRI  
DOB:	 
DATE OF ACQUISITION: 
DATE OF INTERPRETATION:  
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that seems to have lost some volume. There appears to be reasonably good vertical joint space, but the disc appears to be more anteriorly and perhaps laterally displaced. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 to 3 mm.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that may have lost some vertical volume. Difficult to see any discal present, although it appears it may be anteriorly displaced. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 mm.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has undergone severe arthritic changes. There appears to be some bony erosion occurring particularly on the anterior head of the condyle. The disc is hard to identify and appears degenerated. There is increased posterior joint space. The articular eminence is moderately steep. There appear to be some areas on the articular eminence that are eroded. The disc appears significantly degenerated and posterior band is thickened but hot areas within the posterior band. On opening, there is significantly limited translation.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle with some anterior beaking. The articular eminence is moderately steep. The disc is anteriorly displaced and appears significantly degenerated. There is a hot spot in the head of the condyle on the superior surface. On opening, there is limited translation of the condyle. There is a hot spot in the soft tissues superior and posterior to the condyle on opening.

IMPRESSION: Bilateral TMJ destructive arthritis with degeneration of the articular discs.

original_note:
JULIE SASSENRATH									01-14-04

Julie is 31 years old and referred by Dr. Moody Alexander.

Patient History

Julie has had some previous surgeries. They include the following:

At the age of 2, a tumor was removed blocking the right eardrum
Tonsils and adenoids removed
Septum and sinus surgery
Hole in left eardrum repaired with a graft

Julie is in good health. 

Medication wise, she takes birth control pills, and she takes Imitrex when she gets migraine headaches.

Julie is not aware of when her jaw problems began. It has only been about a month ago that her dentist identified that she had a click in her left jaw joint. The onset, therefore, is unknown, and the etiology is also unknown.

She has had no specific treatment for her jaws. 

She has no known allergies. She can’t wear cheap earrings. She is not aware of any problems with clenching or bruxing in the day rule out night. She gets headaches two to three times a week that she rates about a level of 4 that occur predominantly in the frontal and temporal areas. Her migraines she has one or two times per month, and they usually register about a level of 8. She rates TMJ pain at 0, overall pain at 4, jaw function and diet at 0, and disability at 2. The disability is associated predominantly with the migraine headaches that she periodically gets.

Patient Concerns

Teeth and jaws are not properly aligned
Pop in the jaw joints
Headaches which she has had all of her life
Discomfort in the cheeks
Discomfort in the right temple area

Medications

Birth control pills
Imitrex
Excedrin, Aleve, etc., for headache control

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Julie has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. She has prominent masseter muscles and has a low mandibular plane angle facial morphology. Nasal dorsum is a little bit broad, and she has a hump in the mid dorsum part. The lower alar cartilages are somewhat wide. Upper lip length is about 20 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is 5 mm. Maxillary, mandibular, and chin midlines are all pretty much on the facial midline. 
In profile view, she has a little bit of fullness in the upper lip area down by the incisor aspect. Seems to have fairly good projection of the subnasale. The nasal dorsal prominence is more obvious. Chin appears AP deficient by perhaps 6 to 8 mm. Chin-neckline is short, and chin-neckline is moderately well-defined. If she Julie postures her mandible forward, it does improve the facial balance some. Chin still looks a little bit AP deficient with her mandible postured forward into an end-on incisor relationship. 

Intraorally, Julie has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. She has some large lingual tori bilaterally. She has some mild crowding, lower anterior arch. There is a slightly accentuated curve of occlusion in the lower arch but not too bad. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. She has a reverse curve of Spee in the upper arch. She is a little bit Class II end-on occlusion being a little bit greater on the left side than the right side with the right side being probably 3 mm Class II and the left side being probably 2 mm Class II. She has an anterior deep bite of probably about 6 mm. Transversely, the occlusal plane appears fairly level. Transversely, the arches look fairly symmetrical. 

Intranasally, she has had previous complete turbinectomies performed with a little bit of turbinate remaining on the left side way posteriorly. She has had previous septal surgery with evidence of that surgery. Airway looks good and wide open. She has been skiing and has noticed no problems with the cold air and breathing since her turbinectomies and nasal septoplasty. 

Julie has had some temporary buildups of her crowns on the maxillary left central and lateral incisors. 

TMJs seem to function relatively smoothly, except on the left side, if she opens really widely, there is a definite pop on opening and closing that appears to be at about the same level at about 40 mm. The closing click is at about the same level. It may be just the condyle popping out over the articular eminence at that point. To palpation, she has about a level of 1 tenderness over the right temporalis muscle but all other areas are apparently free of discomfort and pain with palpation. Maximum incisal opening is about 51 mm and about 41 mm with reasonable comfort. Excursive movement to the right is about 7 mm and to the left about 8 mm. 

She had her turbinectomies and nasal septoplasty done in St. Louis by an otolaryngologist. 

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows that both maxillary sinuses appear fairly clear except there is a little bit more cloudiness on the left one. She is missing all four third molars. Maxillary left central incisor has a root canal, and the crowns are built up on that tooth as well as the adjacent left lateral incisor. Condyles look a little flattened anteriorly and appear to have some anterior beaking present. 

Waters view shows that the nasal airway is wide open. Septum is a little bit deviated at the very top part of it off toward the left side. Left sinus appears a little bit more opacified than the right side, but there is not too much difference.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has some superior peaking of the condyle and flattening of the anterior head of the condyle. Condyle, however, appears to be fairly well balanced and positioned in the fossa. Articular disc could possibly be a little bit vertically oriented in the right joint and could possibly be acting as a wedge pushing the condyle posteriorly. 
Left TMJ has significant flattening on the anterior head of the condyle, and a little bit of anterior beaking may be present. Vertical joint space appears may be decreased in dimension. Articular eminence is moderately steep as it is on the opposite side. Disc may be vertically oriented. 

On opening, there is good translation of both condyles, but the left one has a little bit more forward translation as compared to the left one and may be part of the reason for the click in that joint. 

Cephalometric radiograph shows that there is a slight Class II skeletal relationship. Occlusal plane may be just a little bit high but difficult to tell. Oropharyngeal airway looks very good. Angles of the mandible appear quite prominent. Anterior deep bite is seen, and the Class II end-on occlusion is appreciated. Oropharyngeal airway appears very good except down by the hyoid bone there is a slightly dimension of probably about 6 mm. At the soft palate level, the AP dimension is about 18 mm and at the base of the tongue is about 10 mm. Appears to be a little bit of maxillary prominence and a little bit of AP chin deficiency.

Basic Diagnoses

Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation
Maxillary AP hyperplasia
Slight vertical maxillary hyperplasia
AP mandibular hypoplasia
AP microgenia
Class II end-on occlusion with division II relationship in the incisors
Reverse curve of occlusion, upper arch
Mild crowding, lower anterior teeth
Tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of 1 to 2 mm
Slight overangulation of the lower incisors
Anterior deep bite
Headaches 
Myofascial pain
External nasal deformity

Recommended Treatment

Presurgical orthodontics
Align and level maxillary arch. Try to intrude the maxillary central incisors 1 to 2 mm if possible. Be careful of the left maxillary central incisor as it possibly could be ankylosed. If ankylosis is realized on that tooth, then align and level the maxillary arch in three segments:
Segment 1:  Left cuspid through second molar
Segment 2:  Left lateral incisor through right lateral incisor
Segment 3:  Right cuspid through right second molar
Place a downward step in the arch wire to align the four incisors at the lower occlusal plane level. This will involve extruding the maxillary lateral incisors slightly. Keep the lateral incisor roots tipped mesially and cuspid roots tipped distally
Align and level mandibular arch. Strip lower anterior teeth removing 1 to 2 mm of tooth structure to help compensate for the tooth-size discrepancy 
Surgery
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors 
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the maxilla superiorly and posteriorly
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to rotate the mandible into occlusion
Augmentation genioplasty of approximately 5 mm
External rhinoplasty

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

original_note:
MI-KYONG (MEGAN) KANG							07/24/2008

Megan is 14 years old.  Referred by Dr. Michael Steffen, 1601 South Boulevard, Edmond, OK 73013.  Megan is in good health. Primary concerns that she has are as follows:

Pain in the jaw joints. 
Headaches.
Difficulties eating and chewing. 
Mouth breathing at night with drooling.

Megan believes that her joints began popping as young as the age of 6 to 7 of unknown etiology.  Megan was in splint therapy from 2006 to 2007 with Dr. Robert Talley.  In 2008, Dr. John Polkinghorne made her a different splint.  She began orthodontic treatment in June 2008 with Dr. Mike Steffen.  She takes no medications.  She gets headaches predominantly on the left side in the frontal and perhaps the anterior part of the temporal area at about a level of 7.  She rates her average TMJ pain at 8, average daily pain at 4, jaw function at 4, diet at 4, disability at 6.  She gets occasional earaches, tinnitus, and vertigo.  No other joints bother her.  She is aware of clenching and grinding her teeth at night, and she also clenches during the day.  

CLINICAL EXAMINATION:  In frontal view, she has fairly good symmetry in the middle and upper third face.  Upper lip length is about 22 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 3 mm.  The maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline as is the mandibular dental midline.  The chin may be shifted just slightly to the right about 2 mm.  In profile view, she has some prominence to the nasal dorsum.  Upper lip subnasale is posterior to supralabrale.  There may be some slight deficiency in the subnasale area.  The chin appears retruded about 10 mm.  Chin neckline is a somewhat shorter and chin neck angle is fairly well defined.  She has some lip incompetence of probably about 6 to 7 mm.  Intraorally, Megan has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars.  Teeth are actually fairly well aligned and leveled with just some mild crowding in the lower arch that needs to be corrected yet.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth also back through the second molars present.  She has a slight Class II cuspid relationship by maybe 1 mm or so and has maybe a 1 mm overjet.  She has about a 1 mm anterior overbite.  Oropharyngeal tissues look reasonably healthy.  Facial pillars may be moderately constricted particularly the posterior.  Tonsils may be mildly enlarged.  The turbinates are moderately enlarged.  The nasal septum at the base is deviated a little bit off to the left side anteriorly and then off to the right side a little bit posteriorly.  There may be some moderate nasal airway obstruction.  Columella is moderately wide, although the nostrils are fairly normal in size.  To palpation, she has high moderate to low severe pain over the TMJs, retromandibular area, left medial pterygoid, and left masseter area.  She had moderate pain over the temporalis muscles, right masseter, and the retromandibular areas.  On opening, she has clicking of both joints at about 10 mm of opening and 5 mm of closing.  Maximal opening is 41 mm with opening without significant discomfort 21 mm.  Excursion movements are about 9 mm in each direction.   
RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the presence of four developing and unerupted third molars.  The rest of the teeth of all present.  Sinuses look reasonably clear.  It is difficult to evaluate the TMJs.  
Waters view shows that the sinuses appear clear.  Nasal septum appears reasonably straight in the bony area.  Turbinates are fairly large, particularly on the right side with not much airway remaining.  
Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle with fairly good morphology.  Joint space  is significantly reduced vertically and posteriorly.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.    
Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle with good morphology.  The superior and posterior joint space appears to be decreased.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  On opening, there is good translation of both condyles.  
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has a high occlusal plane angle skeletal Class II jaw relationship.  The oropharyngeal airway actually looks reasonably good with a distance from the soft palate to the posterior pharyngeal wall of about 8 mm and the base of the tongue to the posterior pharyngeal wall of 10 to 11 mm.  She has a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right side being 1 mm higher than the left side. 

Basic diagnoses for Megan would be as follows:    

Bilateral TMJ adolescent internal condylar resorption (AICR).
Posterior vertical maxillary hypoplasia. 
AP mandibular hypoplasia. 
Class II end-on cuspid relationship on the right side and slight Class II on the left side. 
High occlusal plane angle.  
Transverse asymmetry with the right side being higher than the left side.
Hypertrophied turbinates.
Nasal airway obstruction. 
Impacted third molars x4.  
Snoring. 
TMJ pain. 
Headaches.
Myofascial pain. 
Clenching and bruxism.
Impacted third molars x4.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Medications:  Klonopin 0.5 mg 1 tablet q.h.s.
Presurgical orthodontics.  
Align and level maxillary arch.  Tip lateral roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies.  
Align and level mandibular arch.  Consider slenderizing the lower anterior arch removing 1 to 2 mm of tooth structure to decrease the slightly over angulated lower anterior teeth.  
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors. 
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the posterior aspect inferiorly, level it transversely, and upright the incisors.  
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction.  
Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy to augment the chin. 
Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies. 
Removal of impacted third molars x4.
4.   Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
MARY BETH MOORE								10/15/2008

Mary Beth is 66 years old, referred by Dr. Jeffrey Rocakacy, 591 Horsebarn Road, Suite 100, Rogers, Arkansas 72758.  Mary Beth does smoke one pack of cigarettes per day.  She has smoked for 51 years.  Primary concerns that Mary Beth has includes the following:

Constant severe pain in the right side of the face that is there constantly.
Clicking, popping, and grinding in the jaw joints.
Difficulties eating. 
Difficulties talking. 
Difficulties sleeping, particularly on the right side. 

She takes the following medications;

Cardizem 240 mg q.d.
Lipitor 40 mg q.d.
Boniva 1 time per month.
Mobic 7.5 mg b.i.d. 

She sometimes has tachycardia and she has mild myocardial bridging.  She had a mild heart attack in 1998.  She does have some difficulties breathing through her nose and breathes predominantly through her mouth.  She has moderate snoring, but is not aware of sleep apnea problems.  

Mary Beth’s problems began in the end of November 2007 with the gradual onset of unknown etiology of increased pain in the right side of the face and jaw area.  It continued to get worse until in January 2008 she began seeking help to fix the problem.  To date, she has had no treatment.  One doctor thought it might be a salivary gland stone and another one thought that it was an impacted third molar.  Dr. Rocakacy was the first to identify that it was a joint problem and referred Mary Beth here.  

She gets no headaches, but rates her TMJ pain at 8, average daily pain at 6 and this is predominantly on the right side.  She rates her jaw function at 8, diet at 8, disability at 5.  She has constant tenderness, but no earaches or vertigo.  Other joints that bother her include her left knee, right elbow, upper spine, and fingers.  The fingers, however, only swell and they are not painful.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Mary Beth has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 30 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about -4 mm.  The maxillary and mandibular dental midlines are fairly well aligned as in the chin.  The nasal tip is just slightly off to the right side about 2 mm.  In profile view, Mary Beth appears to have some AP maxillary deficiency and some AP mandibular deficiency probably 10 to 15 mm.  There is probably a B deficient maxilla probably by 6 mm.  Intraorally, she has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars present.  She has significant crowding of the lower anterior arch.  She has adequate attached gingiva.  In upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  She has a Class I cuspid relationship.  She has about a 3-mm deep bite.   She has some moderate crowding in the upper arch and the upper incisors at the mesial aspect are rotated posteriorly.  Oropharyngeal tissues look reasonably healthy.  She has some constricture of the fascial pillars, but otherwise everything looks fairly normal.  She has hypertrophied inferior turbinates and partial nasal airway obstruction.  She has some mild constricture of the luminal valves.  She does have moderate snoring, but no daytime somnolence.  She is not aware of sleep apnea and has no sleep disorder.  She does breathe through her mouth at nighttime.  To palpation, Mary Beth has no tenderness around the head and neck area.  Left joint functions smoothly, however, the right side has audible severe crepitus present.  Maximal incisal opening about 33 mm.  She can get up to about 30 mm without significant pain.  Excursion movement to the right 8 mm and to the left 7 mm.  
RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the presence of impacted third molars in the upper arch and the mandibular left lower arch.  She has some increased calcification around the apex area of the mandibular left first molar.  Sinuses look relatively clear.  The right condyle appears quite flattened with some heterotopic bone extensions anteriorly and posteriorly. 
Waters view shows that the turbinates appear enlarged.  Bony septum appears reasonably straight.  Sinuses look clear.  
Right TMJ tomogram was a little difficult, but it appears that there are some significant degenerative changes within the joint and probably bone-on-bone contact.  
Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle with some anterior beaking.  There is a decreased vertical joint space and the articular eminence is relatively shallow.  This joint likewise may have a displaced articular disc.  On opening, there is fairly good translation of both joints. 
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has a skeletal Class I jaw relationship.  However, she has a severely decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension at the soft palate level of only 3 mm and at the base of the tongue at no more than 4 mm.  

Basic diagnoses for Mary Beth would be as follows:    

Right TMJ severe arthritis with articular disc dislocation and severe degeneration and perforation.  
Right TMJ severe pain. 
Possible left TMJ pathology, but cannot confirm without MRI, but left joint is asymptomatic. 
AP maxillary hypoplasia.  
Posterior vertical maxillary hypoplasia.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia.
High occlusal plane angle. 
Significantly decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of 3 to 4 mm (normal dimension is 11 mm).
Sleep apnea symptoms. 

I discussed with Mary Beth and her husband Jimmy in reference to the TMJ and airway issues as well as the jaw malalignment factor.  However, at this point in time, they are only interested in 
the right TMJ issue as it is creating severe pain and significantly interfering with their abilities to carry on normal life activities.  Therefore, the recommended treatment would be as follows:

CT scan of jaws and jaw joints 
Surgery.
Right TMJ reconstruction with total joint prosthesis.
Right TMJ fat graft (harvest from the abdomen).
Application and removal of arch bars.

original_note:
KATHLEEN “KATE” PLAXCO						10/10/2011

Kate is 16 years old, birth date is 10/26/1994.  Kate had approximately 5 years of orthodontics and the orthodontist was unable to get the bite together.  In December 2010, she had maxillary and mandibular osteotomies and a genioplasty likely using Medpor.  She got out of her braces on July 30, 2011.  Her bite apparently is good and she is pleased with most of the outcome.  The primary concerns she has are as follows:

Chin is too strong.
There is a protrusion of the implant on the left side that is palpable.
There are scar bands inside the lower lip vestibule.
There is no space on the right side between the mandibular ramus and maxillary tuberosity.

Kate had braces from 2006 until 2011.  

Kate has no problems with headaches or TMJ pain or myofascial pain.  She rates her jaw function at 1, diet at 2, disability at 1.  She has some decreased sensation to the lower lip and chin area.  She takes no medications.  She has no earaches, tinnitus, vertigo or hearing loss.  No other joints bother her.  She is not aware of clenching or bruxing at nighttime or during the day.  She has no problems with snoring, daytime somnolence, nor sleep apnea.  She is predominantly a mouth breather at night.  

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Kate has good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 21 mm.  Upper tooth lip  relationship is about 2.5 to 3 mm.  The maxillary, mandibular, and chin midline are all pretty much on the facial midline.  In profile view, she has fairly good facial symmetry.  Chin appears to be in reasonably good balance.  Chin neckline is relatively normal in length and chin neck angle is fairly well defined.  She does have a slight prominence in the labial mental fold.  Oropharyngeal airway looks relatively normal and healthy.  To palpation, she has no tenderness around the head and neck area.  The maximal incisal opening is 46 mm, excursion movement to the right 8 mm and to the left 7.5 mm.  Joints seem to function reasonably smooth, although there is an occasional very slight glitch in the left joint on wide opening.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars, but the rest of the teeth are present. She has a 3 x 3 retainer in the lower arch.  There is evidence of four bone plates in the maxilla and a total of 16 screws.  In the mandible, she has 5 screws in the left ramus and 4 screws in the right ramus.  She has 2 screws in the chin where the chin implant has been placed.  Her condyles show significant problems with condylar resorption, and the condyles appear small in a vertical and anteroposterior dimension.  

Coronal view shows the cut was done way posteriorly in the molar area so most of the sinuses are missed, but it does appear that there is some thickened lining in the wall of the sinuses.  The left turbinate has been completely excised, but the right middle turbinate is still present and fairly large.  The middle turbinate on the right side appears to obstruct against the septum.  

Axial view shows that in the left sinus that the mucosal lining is extremely thickened.  In the right side, likewise it appears that there is increased thickness of the sinus membrane.  The septum has some slight curvature in it.  The right inferior turbinate appears to block a good portion of the nasal airway.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows that there is evidence of significant loss of vertical condylar volume.  There is decreased vertical joint space.  There is flattening on the head of the condyle and some anterior beaking present.  The articular eminence is fairly shallow.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is quite small in size with loss of vertical and anteroposterior volume.  There is decreased vertical joint space in some areas.  The articular eminence is moderately shallow.  

Left TMJ coronal view shows that the condyle is narrow mediolaterally and has lost significant vertical volume.  There is decreased vertical joint space.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 to 4 mm. 

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has lost volume mediolaterally as well as superiorly.  There is some medial beaking of the condyle.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 to 4 mm.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has some adenoid tissue still present in the nasopharynx.  She has a moderate increased occlusal plane angle.  The chin appears to have reasonably good AP dimension.  There is some lower lip ptosis with the lower lip being about 3 mm lower than ideal relative to the position of the lower incisors.  She has a relatively prominent labial mental fold. 

Basic diagnoses for Kate would be as follows:

Bilateral TMJ adolescent internal condylar resorption (AICR) versus connective tissue autoimmune disease.
Maxillary and mandibular yaw shifted towards the right side about 3 or 4 mm.
Anterior vertical overbite of 1 mm.
Presence of Medpor chin implant slightly large for the patient’s facial morphology.
Slight Class II occlusion by 1 to 2 mm in the cuspid area.  
Wide chin and prominent chin in AP dimension (parents’ concern).

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1. MRI of TMJs.
2.	Placement of maxillary and mandibular orthodontic appliances.
3.	Surgery (this will be dependent on MRI findings).
	a.	Consider bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair 			with Mitek anchors (if AICR and discs are salvageable) as well as bilateral 		sagittal split osteotomies to create space in the joint area to accommodate 			the discs.
	b.	Consider bilateral TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses if TMJ pathology 			related to connective tissue autoimmune disease.
