original_note:
B032
B032 is 49 years old. B032 recalls that she had problems with her jaw joints in her teenage years. She did have orthodontics from the age of 12 to 16 which included head gear which she wore 24 hours a day plus she had extraction of her maxillary lateral incisors and her mandibular first bicuspids. She also had her third molars removed. The etiology of her joint problems is unknown. Her bite was off so in 3 years and 5 months ago she began orthodontic treatment in preparation for mandibular advancement. The orthodontics was done by Dr. XXXX. Ten months ago, B032 underwent bilateral sagittal split osteotomies to advance the mandible. One week later, her dog hit her in the jaw and apparently fractured her rami bilaterally. Initially, it was not diagnosed. Finally, three months after the initial surgery, bone plates were placed and one week later they became displaced again. She was taken back to surgery the next week, and longer larger bone plates were placed and her jaws were wired together for six weeks. Since then, she has been maintained on Class II elastics to try to control her occlusion. B032 rates her headaches at a level of 7 in the frontal, temporal, and posterior head area. She rates her TMJ pain at 5, average daily pain at 10, jaw function at 8, diet at 9, and disability at 7. She gets severe earaches, but no tinnitus, vertigo, but she has developed increased hearing sensitivity. Her joints are hyperflexible and she has no other joint involvement. She has been diagnosed with having atypical scleroderma, optic nerve neuritis, and Raynaud's syndrome. She was put on a mixture of medications that caused her kidneys to shut down. She was on dialysis for about 3-1/4 years. She then took what is call Puva treatments that seemed to help her significantly. B032 apparently has recently developed some iritis and dryness of her eyes similar to Sjogren's syndrome. She is aware of clenching and bruxing at nighttime and she also is aware of clenching during the day. She has total anesthesia to the lower lip and chin area. She has some frontal nerve weakness.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
In frontal view, B032 has some slight facial asymmetry. Upper lip length is about 25 mm. She has a fullness in the upper lip and she has had a previous fat graft done there. The nasal tip is off to the right side about 3 mm. The maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline. The mandibular dental midline also is fairly much on the facial midline. The chin may be just slightly off to the left side a couple of millimeters. Upper tooth lip relationship is 1 mm on the right and 0 on the left. Right mandibular ramus may be about 3 mm transversely more prominent than the left side. In profile view, she has some slight AP maxillary deficiency of probably about 3 mm. The chin is retruded about 20 mm. She has an extremely short chin neckline, although the chin neck angle is reasonably well defined. She has eversion of both the upper and the lower lip. She has a lip incompetence of almost 10 mm. With the lower lip relaxed, she has about 7 to 8 mm lower incisor tip to lower lip relationship. The height of her lower lip is almost at the gingival margin. Intraorally, B032 is missing probably the lower first bicuspids. She does have her lower four incisors and cuspid. Occlusion is very flat indicative of either equilibrations or ___________. She has adequate attached gingiva along the lower anterior arch. In the upper arch, she is missing upper lateral incisors and has the cuspids apparently pulled into the lateral incisor position and they have been reshaped. She has a slight anterior open bite of about 1 mm. She has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship by about 2 mm. There is a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the left side being 1 to 1.5 mm higher than the right side. B032 chews predominantly on her left side and not on the right side. Her open bite in centric relation may actually be closer to 2 mm. Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively normal and healthy. To palpation, she has moderate to high moderate pain through most of the head and neck area and actually relatively severe pain through her posterior neck and shoulder areas to palpation. Joints function reasonably smoothly, but as she opens she deviates about 3 to 4 mm to the left. The maximal incisal opening is about 29 mm, opening without significant pain 14 mm. The excursion movements surprisingly were about 7 mm in each direction.

Basic diagnoses would be as follows:
Scleroderma. Left TMJ severe arthritis with articular disc degeneration related to scleroderma.
Right TMJ arthritis, condylar displacement, and medial displacement of the articular discs.
Previous orthognathic surgery to include bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies for mandibular advancement.
Bilateral mandibular ramus fractures from trauma that occurred a few days post surgery. Two subsequent surgeries to try to fix the fractured rami.
Severe malposition of the proximal segment of the left mandibular ramus and condyle.
Significant malposition of proximal segment due to previous fractures.
Severe mandibular hypoplasia.
Maxillary AP and posterior vertical hypoplasia. High occlusal plane angle.
Severely reduced oropharyngeal airway of 3 mm (normal dimension is 11 mm).
Missing maxillary lateral incisors and mandibular first bicuspids. Anterior open bite of 2 mm.
Class II end-on occlusion.
TMJ pain.
Headaches. Myofascial pain.
Sleep apnea symptoms. Deviated nasal septum blocking off the left nasal airway.
Bone plates and screws in the left and right mandibular rami.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1. CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol).
2. Presurgical orthodontics. a.	Continue to align and level maxillary arch. b.	Continue to align and level mandibular arch.
3. Surgery. a.	Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses with repositioning of the severely displaced mandibular left proximal ramus section. b.	Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen). c.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla in a counterclockwise direction. d.	Bilateral coronoidotomies. e.	Nasal septoplasty. f.	Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy. g.	Removal of previously placed hardware including bone plates x2 and bone screws x10.
4. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B032 MRI
Left TMJ coronal view shows a joint that has a very arthritic condyle. There is increased superior joint space. There is a divot in the center of the condyle. There appears to be loss of vertical volume of the condyle. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 to 3 mm.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has better morphology than the opposite side, although still may have lost a little bit of vertical volume but not nearly as much. There may be some discal tissue medial and anterior to the condyle. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 mm.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is arthritic with significant anterior beaking and loss of the volume. The condyle is positioned downwards and forwards in the fossa with a inflammatory response in the joint area. The disc is anteriorly positioned and sitting beneath the articular eminence, but appears degenerated and extremely thin. The articular eminence is moderate in angulation. The patient has some fluid in the joint and almost looks somewhat cystic. The condyle is positioned quite a ways down and forward in the fossa. On opening, there is slight translation of the condyle but not much. The articular disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction.

Right TMJ shows a condyle that a better morphology, but may have an osteophyte sticking up posterior behind the articular disc. The disc appears to be in reasonable position except the whole condyle and discal component are positioned forward in the fossa. The posterior band of the disc appears somewhat degenerated. There is some translation and is slightly better than the opposite side. On opening, the disc does move with the condyle in a reduced position.

IMPRESSION:
Left TMJ advanced arthritis and articular disc anteriorly displaced without reduction and with significant deformation. Condyle significantly anteriorly positioned in the fossa.
Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc medially displaced but with a condyle positioned significantly forward in the fossa. B032 I-CAT

Left TMJ shows a condyle that is quite arthritic. There seems to be some increased joint space. However, there is one area where there appears to be almost bone-on-bone contact in the medial side which is probably forward on the articular eminence. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 mm.

Right TMJ tomogram shows that the condyle has better morphology, although a little bit sclerotic on the top of the condyle. Over medially, there appears to be some decreased joint space in the area of the articular eminence. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 mm.

Sinuses appear relatively healthy and clear. She has a deviated septum off towards the left side with a bone spur extending further towards the lateral nasal wall. Left turbinate appears quite large and it appears it may be obstructing the airway significantly. The right turbinate appears to have better morphology. There are some bone plates in the ramus of the mandible.

Axial view shows that the sinuses are clear. The nasal septum is deviated significantly towards the left side with a large spur and appears to be some major obstruction to the left side of the nose. The turbinates are moderate in size with the left one being the largest. The left condyle is smaller and more arthritic appearing than the right side.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is postured somewhat forward in the fossa, but not quite as far as in the MRI. There is increased vertical joint space and posterior joint space, although the anterior joint space is fairly narrow particularly towards the medial side of the joint. The articular eminence is moderately steep. She has a bone plate on the mandible that extends from up in the coronoid process down along the ramus of the mandible. Oropharyngeal airway appears quite narrow at the soft palate level where there may be only 2 or 3 mm of airway space. At the base of the tongue, she probably has about 4 to 5 mm of airway space. She has a high occlusal plane angle facial morphology.

Left TMJ coronal view shows that she has advanced arthritis in the condyle with significant anterior beaking and decreased joint space between the beak and the articular eminence where there could be almost bone-on-bone contact. The condyle is postured significantly forward and downward relative to its normal position in the fossa. The articular eminence is fairly elongated and comes down almost to the sigmoid notch area. She has a bone plate that extends through the entire ramus down into the posterior body area of the mandible and some additional screws in the ramus as well. She demonstrates significant lip incompetence. The lower lip is extremely everted and well below the lower incisor tips.

PANOREX: The patient is missing all four third molars and there is one bicuspid missing in each quadrant. Bone plates extend down on the left side from the ramus down onto the posterior body with four screws. There are some wires and an additional screw in the ramus. It appears that there was a fracture through the mid portion of the ramus with the superior aspect of the ramus and condyle rotated significantly forward of the rest of the mandible. The styloid process extends down on that side and is visible posterior to the forward rotated proximal segment. On the right side, there is a bone plate that extends from the base of the coronoid process downward with six screws in that plate and two additional screws in the ramus area.

3-D rendering shows that the right condyle is postured forward in the fossa. There is a large plate on the ramus area where it appears there was a fracture through the superior aspect of the ramus and maybe also at a lower level as well. There are two screws in addition to the bone plate in the anterior aspect of the ramus. On the left side, there is a significant upward and forward rotation of the proximal segment where it appears there was a fracture through the ramus. The coronoid process is actually rotated through and above the zygomatic arch. The condyle appears severely arthritic and postured way forward in the fossa. There is a bone plate in this ramus, but there may be a nonunion through the fracture site with a wire and a bone screw positioned anteriorly as well.

IMPRESSION:
Bilateral TMJ arthritis being significantly worse on the left side as compared to the right side. Bone plates bilaterally in the mandibular ramus areas extending into the posterior body. High occlusal plane angle.
Significantly decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension estimated at approximately 3 mm. Fracture through left ramus with displacement upwards and forwards of the proximal segment. Possible nonunion in the left ramus area.

original_note:
JENNIFER ROVEZZI								07/14/2008 

Jennifer is 19 years and referred by Dr. Hilton Goldreich.  Primary concerns that Jennifer has are:

Her teeth are not aligned properly.
Her jaws are not aligned properly. 

She has a bipolar disorder and she takes Lamictal 200 mg q.d. and Seroquel 400 mg q.h.s.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION:  In frontal view, Jennifer has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  She has some slight increased fullness in the masseter area on the left side compared to the right side.  Upper lip length is 20 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is -3 mm.  The maxillary dental midline is pretty much on the facial midline.  The mandibular dental midline may be just slightly off to the right side.  Chin appears to be off to the right side probably about 2 to 3 mm.  The left side of the face appears to be 2 to 3 mm longer than the right side.  In profile view, she has just some slight AP deficiency in the maxilla maybe 2 to 3 mm.  Chin appears in pretty good AP projection.  The lower lip appears a little bit retruded relative to the projection of the chin.  Intraorally, Jennifer has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars present.  Teeth are fairly well aligned with no particular crowding present.  She has good attached gingiva.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  Upper lateral incisors are tipped in lingually as are the left bicuspids.  She has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship in the right side by probably 4 mm and the same on the left side.  She has about a zero vertical overbite.  A slight accentuated curve of Spee in the upper arch is present.  Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively normal except the tonsils are moderately enlarged and she has some tonsillar/adenoid kind of tissue on the left side of the posterior aspect of the soft palate.  Fascial pillars appear to be somewhat posteriorly positioned.  Intranasally, the right turbinate is enlarged.  The left turbinate I can only see the very anterior aspect of it and it appears moderate in size at that level, but the septum is deviated significantly off towards the left side decreasing the functional airway.  Jennifer has bilateral masseteric hyperplasia.  There is a cant in the occlusal plane with the right side being about 1 to 1.5 mm higher than the left side.  To palpation, she has no tenderness around the head and neck area.  The right joint seems to function reasonably smoothly.  She has a little glitch occasionally on opening in the right joint at probably about 40 to 45 mm.  Maximal incisal opening is 62 mm.  Excursion movements are about 6 mm in each direction.  
RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all third molars, but the rest of the teeth are all present.  Sinuses look relatively clear.  The condyles look somewhat enlarged.  
Waters view shows that there is deviation of the septum off towards the left side.  The right turbinate is somewhat enlarged.  The left turbinate does not appear as large, but there appears to be significant decreased oral airway bilaterally.  Sinuses look relatively clear.  The left sinus looks slightly larger than the right sinus.  There is slight vertical asymmetry with the left side being a little bit lower than right side.  
Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears to have reasonably good morphology.  Articular eminence is moderate in steepness.  Joint space looks relatively balanced.  

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is a little bit more flattened on the posterior aspect of the head.  Articular eminence has moderate inclination.  There appears to be good joint space.  There is a little bit more of a hockey stick shape to this condyle.  On opening, there is good translation of both joints. 
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Jennifer has a skeletal Class II relationship with an extremely low occlusal plane angle.  Oropharyngeal airway at the soft palate level measures about 7 mm and at the base of the tongue about 10 mm.  There is a skeletal and occlusal Class II relationship.  Tooth size analysis reveals a Bolton's difference of 1 mm.  
Basic diagnoses for Jennifer would be as follows:
Anterior vertical maxillary hypoplasia.
Posterior vertical maxillary hyperplasia. 
Transverse maxillary hypoplasia. 
AP mandibular hypoplasia. 
Class II end-on occlusion.
Low occlusal plane angle and mandibular plane angle. 
Hypertrophied turbinates.
Deviated nasal septum off to the left side. 
Nasal airway obstruction.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Presurgical orthodontics.  
Align and level maxillary arch.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. 
Align and level mandibular arch.  
Surgery.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the anterior maxilla inferiorly and the posterior maxilla superiorly to increase the occlusal plane angle and expand. 
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a clockwise direction.
Nasal septoplasty. 
Bilateral partial turbinectomies. 
  3.  Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B103
B103 is 34 years old and is a self-referral. B103 had an onset of her TMJ problems at the age of 14 probably related to AICR. She did have an accident of age of 7 on a diving board where she hit the underneath of her chin and damaged some of her primary teeth. She had orthodontics from the age of 11 to 12 and then again from the age of 14 to 15. She had her third molars removed at the age of 15. At the age of 16, she had double jaw surgery by Dr. XXXX. That same year she also had a nasal septoplasty and an external rhinoplasty. She has been in splint therapy for 14 years and estimates she has had 20 to 30 splints. Following completion of her orthodontics, her bite did open again. After the surgery, the bite opened once again. B103 rates her headaches at a level of 7 that occur in the temporal areas bilaterally. They occur at a rate of two times per day. She wakes up with the headaches and then in the afternoon around 5 o'clock the headaches begin again. She rates her TMJ pain at 8, average daily pain at 8, jaw function at 2, diet at 3, disability at 7. She gets occasional earaches, gets tinnitus about 2 times per week, but has no vertigo or known hearing loss. Other joints that bother her include her wrists and her neck. She is aware of clenching and bruxing at nighttime. She takes the following medications:

Lexapro 10 mg q.d.
Advil 1200 mg p.r.n.
In frontal view, B103 has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip is 20 mm in length and upper tooth lip relationship is about 2 mm on the left and 2.5 on the right. The maxillary midline is about 1 mm off to the right side. The nasal tip is about 1 mm off to the left side. The chin is about 2 mm off to the left side. In profile, the maxilla is retruded about 4 to 5 mm and the chin is retruded about 10 to 12 mm. The chin neckline is short and chin neck angle is not defined. Intraorally, B103 has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. She has adequate attached gingiva around the lower anterior teeth. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. She has got crowns in the maxillary left first molar and right first and second molars. She is fairly close to a Class I cuspid-molar relationship. There is some mild crowding in the lower arch. She has a 2 mm anterior deep bite. Oropharyngeal tissues look healthy and normal with the soft palate appearing maybe a little bit elongated with a deep crease dimension perhaps from the soft palate to the posterior pharyngeal wall. Intranasally, the turbinates appear relatively normal in size and the septum appears reasonably straight. There appears to be good nasal airway. To palpation, she has some moderate discomfort over the TMJs and milder tenderness over the temporalis, masseters, medial pterygoids, and SCMs. The maximal incisal opening is 47 mm but 25 mm without discomfort. Excursion movements to the right is 9 mm, to the left 8 mm.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. There are four bone plates in each side, but each bone plate only has two screws with one screw above and one screw below the osteotomy site. There are two screws in the left ramus, but none in the right ramus but apparently these were removed. She has two screws in the chin area. She had root canals on the maxillary left second bicuspid, right cuspid, first bicuspid, and mandibular right cuspid and left first molar. She has crowns on the maxillary right first and second molars, mandibular left first and second molars, and maxillary left first molar. The condyles appear arthritic.

Coronal view shows that the septum is reasonably straight and turbinates are relatively normal in size. The sinuses appear clear.
Axial view shows that the septum has a little deviation off towards the right side. The turbinates are relatively normal in size and the sinuses appear clear.

Left TMJ sagittal tomograms show condyles that have undergone significant resorption with loss of vertical and anteroposterior volume. The articular eminence is moderately shallow. There is decreased joint space more towards the lateral aspect of the joint. There are some pseudocysts in the head of the condyle.

Right TMJ sagittal tomograms show a condyle that has undergone significant condylar resorption with loss of vertical and anteroposterior volume. There are some pseudocysts in the head of the condyle. The articular eminences are just slightly steeper on this side but are still moderately shallow. The condyle is positioned somewhat posterior in the fossa.

Left TMJ coronal view shows that there is significant loss of vertical volume and mediolateral width of the condyle. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly probably about 5 mm. There is decreased vertical joint space particularly towards the medial side of the joint.

Right coronal view shows a condyle that has lost significant vertical volume and mediolateral volume and may be a little bit more narrow than the opposite side. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly probably 5 to 6 mm extending down beyond the head of the condyle. There is somewhat decreased vertical joint space.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has a Class I occlusal relationship, but probably a slight Class II skeletal relationship. She has a high occlusal plane angle. Oropharyngeal airway measures 4 mm at the soft palate level and about 6 mm at the base of the tongue.

Basic diagnoses for B103 would be as follows:
Bilateral TMJ severe arthritis (AICR).
Maxillary AP and posterior vertical hypoplasia.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia. Class I occlusion.
Moderate crowding of the lower anterior arch.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Sleep apnea. Bilateral TMJ pain. Headaches.
Myofascial pain.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1. Presurgical orthodontics. a.	Align and level the maxillary arch. b.	Align and level mandibular arch.
2. CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol).
3. Surgery. a.	Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement in a counterclockwise direction with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. b.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to down graft the posterior aspect and advance in a counterclockwise direction. c.	Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen). d.	Bilateral coronoidotomies.
4. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B103 MRI
Left TMJ coronal view show a condyle that is very narrow in mediolateral width and has lost significant vertical volume. There is decreased vertical joint space. The lateral rim appears to curve inferiorly 5 mm.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle is very narrow mediolaterally and has lost vertical volume. The lateral rim of the fossa appears to curve inferiorly about 4 mm. There is decreased superior joint space.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is somewhat narrow anteroposteriorly. The articular eminence is moderately steep. The articular disc is anteriorly displaced and sitting beneath the articular eminence. The disc is significantly deformed and degenerated. The condyle is sitting posterior and superiorly in the fossa with decreased joint spaces. The anterior joint space is increased. On opening, there is fairly good translation of the condyle, but the disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction with significant deformation.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is arthritic with some sclerosis in the ________ portion of the head of the condyle. There is some anterior beaking present. There is decreased superior joint space. The articular disc is anteriorly displaced and sitting beneath the articular eminence and appears significantly deformed and degenerated. On opening, there is fairly good translation of the condyle, but the disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction with significant deformation.

IMPRESSION:
Bilateral TMJ resorptive arthritis and articular disc anteriorly displaced without reduction with severe deformation and degeneration. Discs appear non-salvageable. Patient probably a candidate for total joint prostheses.

original_note:
B318 
B318 is 14 years old, referred by __.
CURRENT MEDICATIONS: Advair 250/50 one puff daily.
Nasonex 50 mcg one spray to each nostril daily.
Doxycycl HYC 100 mg.
Loratadine (Claritin) 10 mg daily.
Phenylephrine HCL 10 mg daily.
Ibuprofen and Tylenol for headaches.

B318's problems began about 4-1/2 years ago when she became aware of clicking and popping in her joints. There is no known etiology for the situation. She had stage I orthodontics for about 18 months from about 3 to 4 years ago. This included a Herbst appliance and palatal expansion. She began stage II which she currently is in, earlier this year.

She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo or hearing loss. The other joints that bother her include her right wrist but is actually a tendonitis problem. She also has problems with her right ankle where she has had previous surgery for a congenital deformity. She has no known allergies. No particular problems wearing jewelry. She had a tonsillectomy and adenoidectomy at age 6-1/2 years. This was done because she had some sleep apnea problems at that time. B318 and her family do not believe she clenches and grinds her teeth at night. She thinks that she breathes predominately through her nose when sleeping. She gets headaches about 2 to 3 times per week generally in the frontal and temple area and they get to about a level of 5. Sometimes they do not last too long but other times they last for awhile and she requires some medication for it. B318 rates her TMJ pain at about a level of 5, average daily pain at 3, jaw function at 3, diet at 4, disability at 1.

In frontal view, B318 has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about almost 24 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 3.5 mm to 4 mm. Maxillary midline is shifted to the right about 1 mm. Mandibular dental midline and chin are actually a little bit better on the facial midline. There is some slight orbital dystopia of about 1 to 2 mm with the left side being slightly higher than the right side. In profile view, B318 has some AP maxillary deficiency probably about 3 to 4 mm. The chin appears to be AP deficient by about 18 to 20 mm. Chin-neckline is short and chin-neck angle is actually fairly well defined. Intraorally, B318 has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. Teeth are fairly well aligned and leveled. She has good attached gingiva around the lower anterior teeth. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. Teeth likewise are well aligned and leveled. She has a Class II cuspid molar relationship and almost a full Class II on the left side and less Class II on the right side. She is probably 5 to 6 mm Class II on the left and probably 4 to 5 mm on the right. She has an overjet of probably about 4 to 5 mm. She has about a 0 overbite relationship. As B318 postures forward, it appears that she has a fairly good alignment of the jaw structures. B318 

She has a slight tendency of a crossbite in the second molar areas. She has some small upper lateral incisors and probably has a mild tooth size discrepancy of probably about 2 to 3 mm. Intraorally, the uvula looks normal. Soft palate is mildly elongated but not too significant. Faucial pillars may be slightly constricted but not too significant. Scarring from previous tonsillectomy can be seen in the faucial pillars particularly on the right side. Intranasally, the septum appears relatively straight. The turbinates appear moderately enlarged. She does not feel she has any nasal airway obstruction. The columella is fairly wide at the base. The nostrils may be slightly constricted but not too significant. Luminal valves might be just slightly constricted but not enough to cause any problems for her. To palpation, she has some mild tenderness over the TMJs and styloid areas. No other tenderness to palpation around the head and neck area. Joints seem to function relatively smoothly. Maximal incisal opening is about 45 mm. Excursive movement is about 8 mm in each direction.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
:
Panorex shows the presence of all the teeth. She has impacted third molars and she has some developing fourth molars that appear as well in the maxilla. Sinuses look relatively clear but difficult to tell on this particular x-ray. The condyles appear somewhat arthritic and so they have lost some volume. The turbinates appear moderately enlarged. The septum appears relatively straight. X-ray is a little bit dark making it difficult to interpret some of the bony structures.

Waters view shows that the sinuses appear relatively clear. A little bit of cloudiness just on the right side of the x-ray though not particularly involving the sinus. The septum looks relatively straight. The turbinates look moderately enlarged. Asymmetry between the maxilla and mandible can be appreciated.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is posteriorly and superiorly positioned in the fossa with significantly decreased posterior and superior joint spaces. Articular eminence is fairly long and moderately steep. The condyle appears quite narrow anteroposteriorly and appears to have lost some significant volume.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that likewise has lost volume and appears shorter in vertical dimension. Significantly decreased superior and posterior joint space. Articular eminence is moderately steep and somewhat elongated. The condyle appears very narrow anteroposteriorly. On opening, there is some restricted translation in both joints where the condyles come down toward but not completely beneath the articular eminences.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that B318 has slight anterior vertical maxillary excess and posterior vertical maxillary deficiency. The mandible is AP deficient. She has a Class II skeletal and occlusal relationship. It appears to occlude only on her second molars, maybe the first but all other teeth are out of occlusion. Correlating this to clinical examination it shows that she occludes only on the right first and second molars and the left second molars. All other teeth are out of occlusion.

B318 
She has a decreased oropharyngeal airway with the airway dimension at the base of the soft palate being about 4 mm to 4.5 mm but less than 5. At the base of the tongue the airway dimension measures about 6 mm.

BASIC DIAGNOSES: Bilateral TMJ idiopathic condylar resorption and disc dislocations without reductions.
Anterior vertical maxillary excess and slight posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
AP mandibular deficiency.
Apertognathia. Class II occlusion.
Occluding only on the left second molars and right first and second molars.
Impacted maxillary and mandibular third molars. Impacted bilateral maxillary fourth molars.
Headaches.
TMJ pain.
Tooth size discrepancy with a 's difference of approximately 2 mm.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Presurgical orthodontics.
Continue to align and level maxillary arch.
Continue to align and level mandibular arch.
Slenderize lower anterior teeth removing about 2 mm of tooth structure and close the spacing.
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the anterior maxilla superiorly, advance it and down graft the posterior aspect.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction.
Surgical removal of impacted teeth x6.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B318  MRI Evaluation
Date of Birth:
Age: 14/F
Date of Acquisition:
Date of Interpretation: 

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has lost considerable volume. Decreased superior joint space. There is discal tissue seen medial and anterior to the condyle. Minimal downward curvature at the lateral rim of the fossa of less than 2 mm. The condyle is narrow mediolaterally. The joint space is actually not too diminished.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has lost consider volume and perhaps even a little bit more so than the left side. She condyle is very narrow mediolaterally. There is discal tissue medial and anterior to the condyle but predominately medially displaced. Decreased joint space particularly at the lateral aspect of the fossa. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly relatively a minimal amount of probably just a couple of mm.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has reasonable anteroposterior dimension towards the lateral aspect of the joint but medial it is extremely narrow and tiny. There is a significant loss of condylar volume. The articular eminence is actually quite long and steep. The articular disc is anteriorly displaced and sitting down almost beneath the articular eminence. The disc is short anteroposteriorly. A little bit of thickening in the posterior band. The condyle has decreased joint space superior and posterior to it. The cortical bone and the fossa appear to be intact. The condylar head itself towards the medial aspect where it is quite tiny lights up significantly. There could be a little bit of fluid in the superior joint space but not much. On opening, there is moderately good translation of the condyle coming down most directly beneath the articular eminence but the articular disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction. The changes in this condyle and age are compatible with idiopathic condylar resorption.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that appears smaller than the opposite side. Medially the condyle the condyle is very narrow anteroposteriorly and has a significant hockey stick shape associated with it. Out laterally, the condyle is a little bit wider but there appears to be more loss of bone structure as compared to opposite joint. The condylar head internally lights up significantly particularly at the lateral aspect of the joint. There is decreased superior and posterior joint space. The articular eminence is moderately steep and long in length. The articular disc is sitting almost beneath the articular eminence. The disc is foreshortened anteroposteriorly. There could be a small osteophyte sticking off the top of the condyle towards the lateral aspect of the joint. There does not appear to be any significant inflammatory process in the adjacent tissues. On opening, there is moderate translation of the condyle but the articular disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction.

IMPRESSION: Left TMJ idiopathic condylar resorption with articular disc dislocation nonreducing and with moderate degenerative changes. Right TMJ idiopathic condylar resorption with articular disc dislocation nonreducing and with moderate degenerative changes.
Discs are probably still salvageable.

original_note:
B369 
B369 is 26 years old and is referred by Dr. _. She was born with choanal atresia that was repaired 23 years ago. She has suffered depression since last year in relation to her constant pain problems. She had myringotomy tubes placed and a tonsillectomy. She had her wisdom teeth removed 7 years ago. B369 is in very good health except for the jaw problem.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS: Lexapro 2.5 mg every other day.
Percocet 10 mg p.r.n. for severe pain.
Diazepam, used when necessary to help her sleep.
Naproxen b.i.d.
Ibuprofen 1 to 2 tablets p.r.n. Prednisone 10 mg daily, a 10-tablet protocol which she starts at a 100 dose and then tapers downward.

B369's problems relative to her TMJs began when she was about 15 or 16 years old of unknown etiology. She noticed clicking and popping in her joints at that time. She had orthodontics from the age of 11 to 13. She has had multiple splints made over the years but many of these tended to make her clench more increasing her problems. She has had chiropractic treatment and this has helped her at least temporarily. She again had a splint made , by Dr. _ and this has provided some relief for her. That splint now is being managed and adjusted by Dr. _. In addition, she has had physical therapy which among other things included ultrasound and moist heat. She is getting some physical therapy or chiropractic treatment on her vertebrae particularly in her neck and shoulder areas. She has had counseling for anxiety and depression.

B369 gets headaches at about a level of 7 throughout most of the head area. These occur several times per week. Her pain involving her ears is worse than her headaches. She rates her TMJ pain at 10, average daily pain at 7, jaw function, diet and disability at 9. She gets frequent earaches, tinnitus and occasional vertigo. She thinks she may have a little bit of hearing loss. Other joints that bother her include her left knee which developed about 2 years ago and also she feels she has some neck problems also. She has had an adverse reaction to Wellbutrin. She also has trouble wearing jewelry particularly cheap earrings. She is aware of clenching at night when she sleeps.

The patient states that her pain is worse in the morning when she wakes up. This is likely due to clenching and bruxism.
B369 had clicking and popping continuously in her joints up until . That is when she developed the closed lock situation. She has not had clicking or popping in her joints since that time and has not been able to open her mouth since that time. Dr. _ apparently attempted to open her jaw and some very loud pops were heard. Dr. _ saw B369  and had her open her jaws as much as she possibly could after he did some novocaine injections into the joint. She was able to open wider after that but in the process she heard a very loud pop occur.

B369 
In frontal view, B369 has fairly good left to right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 17 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is 3 mm. Maxillary, mandibular and chin midline are all pretty much on the facial midline. Nasal tip may be just slightly off to the left side 1 to 2 mm. In profile, she has fairly good balance vertically and AP-wise. Intraorally, she has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. In the upper arch, B369 has all of her teeth back through the second molars. She has basically a Class I cuspid molar relationship. On the right side, her teeth appear to be open from back through the second molar. Oropharynx looks good and uvula, soft palate and faucial pillars all look relatively normal. Intranasally, the septum is deviated off to the right side. Left turbinate is quite large and right turbinate is more moderate but there appears to be a decreased nasal airway on the right side. The left side is somewhat decreased because of the large turbinate on that side but there does appear to be a passable airway more so on the left. To palpation she has moderate pain over the left TMJ and milder pain over the temporalis, masseters and shoulder areas. On function, she has limited opening and the joints seem reasonably smooth though she could hear a little crepitation in the left joint. She says that when she wears her bite splint at night she wakes up and her lower is postured forward and she actually feels better for awhile until the jaw settles back into position. Maximal incisal opening is 30 mm but she can only open about 10 mm without significant discomfort. Excursive movements are about 6 mm in each direction.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
:
Panorex shows the absence of all 4 third molars but the rest of the teeth are all present. Sinuses appear relatively clear as best as can be seen here. The septum is deviated significantly off to the right side. Difficult to evaluate the joints.

Waters view shows that the sinuses appear relatively clear. Septum appears a little bit straighter in these views although there is some shifting a little bit in the bony area but the cartilaginous area is not seen.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that seems to have relatively good size with a little hockey stick shape to it. There appears to be decreased superior joint space. Articular eminence appears moderately steep.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears to have maybe a little flattening on the superior aspect. There appears to be significantly decreased joint space vertically. Articular eminence is moderately steep. There may be slight decreased posterior joint space as well. On opening, there is some translation but restricted opening. Condyles fall short of the articular eminence.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has basically a skeletal Class I jaw and occlusal relationship. Maxilla and mandible is slightly rotated backwards. Oropharyngeal airway is probably pretty much normal with the tongue in a normal vertical position. Occlusal plane angle is moderately high.

B369 
BASIC DIAGNOSES: Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocations that are nonreducing and with significant degenerative changes.
Severe TMJ pain.
Severe headaches.
Myofascial pain.
Bilateral TMJ closed lock situation.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Medications.
Klonopin 1 mg tablets dispensing 30 tablets, 1 tablet q.h.s.
Bactrim 800/160 mg dispensing 30 tablets, 1 tablet b.i.d., refill x1.
Rifampin 300 mg tablets dispensing 30 tablets, 1 tablet b.i.d., refill x1.
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch.
Align and level mandibular arch. Surgery option 1.
Bilateral disc repositioning with Mitek anchors.
Bilateral mandibular ramus sagittal split osteotomies.
Surgery option 2.
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction with total joint prostheses. Bilateral TMJ fat grafts.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B369 is to call me in 2 weeks to let me know how she is doing with medications. She is to begin the Klonopin for 1 week prior to using the antibiotics to monitor the improvement she gains just from that. These results of the medications will have a bearing on which treatment option will work best for her.

B369  Evaluation Age: 25/F
Date of Acquisition: Date of Interpretation:
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width. There appears to be decreased joint space vertically. It appears that there could be discal tissue displaced laterally. However, it appears there may be some discal tissue anterior as well. Cortical bone across the top seems to be intact although there could be a little bit of unevenness across the top. Lateral rim of the fossa is relatively flat. The lateral pole of the condyle extends well beyond the lateral rim of the fossa in some areas by probably 2 to 3 mm.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears quite large. In fact, it appears a bit larger than the opposite side. There is a decreased superior joint space. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly to a greater extent on this side as compared to the opposite side. It appears that the discal tissue is probably anteriorly displaced. Cortical bone appears to be intact. The lateral pole of the condyle extends beyond the lateral rim of the fossa.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that may be a little bit narrow anteroposteriorly compared to the size of the fossa. Articular eminence is quite long and fairly steep. Articular disc is anteriorly displaced and maybe somewhat deformed. There is some significant fluid in joint space. Some parts are sitting beneath the articular eminence and some in the superior aspect of the joint. The condyle has a little bit of a hockey stick shape to it. A little bit of sclerosis around the head of the condyle. On opening, there is limited translation of the condyle with it just basically approaching the articular eminence. Disc is anteriorly displaced and nonreducing. This appears somewhat deformed.

Right TMJ sagittal shows a condyle that appears a little bit larger than the opposite side. It appears to have a hockey stick shape to it. Articular eminence is steep in some areas as compared to the opposite side. It appears there may be a little bit of a decreased joint space vertically. The disc is anteriorly positioned and actually is in sort of a vertical inclination along the posterior slope of the articular eminence. It could be that the disc is in a position where it is displaced but upon opening it may not evidence clicking or popping. No significant fluid in the superior joint space. On opening, there is restriction of translation and the disc may reduce. The disc itself does not appear to translate.

IMPRESSION: Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation without reduction. Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with it opening with some reduction of the disc.

original_note:
MICHAEL SWINNEY								01/21/2010

Michael is 16 years 11 months and 3 weeks old.  Referred by Dr. Kevin Porter, 2453 E. 11th Street, Odessa, TX 79762.  Michael is in good health with no major medical problems.  Michael’s primary concerns are as follows:

Jaws lock.
Jaws click and pop.
Pain when eating.

Michael has had his wisdom teeth removed in June 2009.  Michael’s joint problems began when he was about 12 years old in 2007 of unknown etiology.  He has had no treatment to date.  Michael rates his headaches at a level of 5 and they occur in the temporal area and are worse when he eats.  He rates his TMJ pain at 5, average daily pain at 4, jaw function at 7, diet at 7, disability at 3.  He gets earaches and tinnitus occasionally as well as vertigo.  Other joints that bother him include his left wrist, knees, and ankles.  Being a hockey player though may contribute to some of his joint problems.  He has been diagnosed with ADHD.  He is not aware of clenching or  bruxism.  

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Michael does have good facial symmetry and balance.  He has got problems with his masseter muscles.  In profile, he has good facial balance.  Intraorally, Michael has all of his lower teeth back through the second molars.  There is some mild crowding in the lower anterior arch.  In the upper arch, he has all of his teeth back through the second molars.  He has a good Class I cuspid-molar relationship bilaterally, and he has about a 2 mm vertical overbite.  Intranasally, the septum is deviated a little bit off towards the left side and turbinates are moderate in size.  To palpation, he has no tenderness around the head and neck area.  On opening, he has clicking in both joints and actually multiple clicks on the right side and does not click quite as much as usual on his left side.  The incisal opening is 55.  The excursion movements are 11 mm in each direction.  The opening clicks are somewhere around 15 mm and there are closing clicks around 10 mm.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars.  Sinuses look clear.  Turbinates appears moderate in size.  Bony septum at least appears straight.  Difficult to evaluate the joints. 

Coronal view shows that he has hypertrophied turbinates particularly on the right side.  Sinuses look reasonably clear with just a little bit fluid with a little retention cyst in the right sinus along the medial wall.  The septum is deviated with a spur off towards the left side and then deviates off a little bit towards the right side near the superior aspect of the septum. 

Axial view shows that the right turbinate is moderately enlarged, and the left one appears a little bit normal.  Sinuses appear relatively clear except for a little mucous retention area in the posterior medial recess of the maxillary sinus. 

Left TMJ sagittal tomogram shows a condyle that is narrow anteroposteriorly.  There are some degenerative changes present.  There is a little bit of anterior beaking present.  The articular eminence has a moderate inclination.  The anterior joint space is increased.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is narrow anteroposteriorly, but not quite as much as the opposite side.  The articular eminence is moderately steep.  The joint space appears adequate with some slight increase anteriorly. 

Left TMJ coronal view shows flattening on the condylar head.  The joint space appears reasonably good, although slightly decreased at the lateral aspect of the joint. 

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has a little bit better shape than the opposite side and there appears to be an adequate joint space.  There is some slight decrease at the lateral aspect of the joint. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Michael has fairly good AP and vertical facial balance and appears to have a good Class I and skeletal relationship.  Oropharyngeal airway measures about 8 mm at the base of the tongue and probably about 6 mm at the soft palate level. 

Basic diagnoses would be as follows:

Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with reduction. 
Mandibular hyperplasia.  
TMJ pain.
Headaches.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Presurgical orthodontics. 
	a.	Align and level maxillary arch.
	b.	Align and level mandibular arch.
2.	Surgery.
	a.	Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor. 		b.	Bilateral ramus osteotomies to maintain the occlusion and allow the condyle to 			move down and forward to create space for the articular disc.  
3.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
CAROL JOHNSON									10/20/2006

Carol is 55 years old, referred by Dr. Petre Schubert.  She has had problems with hypertension.  She had an angioplasty in January of 1990.  She is in good health with no major medical problems.  She has a list of medications that she takes that is in her chart.  Carol really does not really have any concerns problems.  She is here at Dr. Schubert's request.  Dr. Schubert is aware that Carol has an anterior open bite and is concerned as to whether it would be stable to close that orthodontically or whether a surgical approach would provide the best result for Carol.  Carol has also had an open bite.  She had orthodontics at the age of 12 to try and close it and it was unsuccessful.  She believes her bite is closed more now than it was prior to orthodontic treatment.  She had gum surgery about four years ago.  She has no trouble with earaches or tinnitus but gets occasional vertigo.  She does have some problems with her right hip joint but no other joints cause any problems.  She is aware of clenching and bruxism at nighttime.  She has no history of headaches or TMJ pain or pain around the head and neck area.  While Carol was having orthodontics done the first time as a teenager, she would occasionally get a closed lock situation and would have to go to the orthodontist and get massaged in order to get her jaw open.  Therefore, she has had displaced disc in her joints at least since the age of 12 to 14.  She has no clicking or popping in her joints currently.  

In frontal view, Carol has fairy good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 24 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about –1.  Maxillary, mandibular and chin midline are all pretty much on the facial midline.  In profile view, Carol has good facial balance.  She has some slight AP maxillary deficiency of maybe 3 mm in AP mandibular deficiency of maybe 5 to 6 mm but not real significant.  Intraorally, she is missing lower first bicuspids but has the rest of her teeth back through the second molars.  A little bit of spacing between the cuspids and the second bicuspids.  In the upper arch she is also missing first bicuspids.  She has small upper lateral incisors.  It appears that she occludes only on the first and second molars on the left side and perhaps the same on the right side.  The rest of the teeth appear to be out of occlusion.  She has about 1 mm of vertical open bite.  She has about 1 to 2 mm overjet.  She has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship by about 2 to 3 mm.  Carol rates her jaw function, diet and disability at 0, TMJ pain and headaches at 0.  To palpation she has no pain around the head and neck area.  Joints seem to function relatively smoothly.  Transverse occlusal plane is level.  Oropharyngeal areas look normal.  Soft palate, uvula and faucial pillars all appear within normal limits.  She has no problems with snoring or sleep apnea symptoms.  Intranasally, the right turbinate is moderately enlarged.  The left turbinate is relatively normal.  There is a little septal spurring off to the left side at the base.  There does not appear to be any significant nasal airway obstruction.  The columella is wide and nostrils are somewhat narrow.  Luminal valves look relatively normal.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION:
Panorex shows that she has some arthritic changes in the condyles.  Sinuses look relatively clear.  Turbinates appear relatively normal.  She is missing all four third molars and all four first bicuspids.  Roots may be a little bit short in the bicuspid area, cuspid and lower incisor areas. 

Waters view shows the sinuses appear clear.  The bony septum appears reasonably straight.  Turbinates appear moderate in size.  There could be a septal spur off to the left side but difficult to tell.
CAROL JOHNSON				10/20/2006				Page 2


Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has arthritic changes with anterior beaking and some loss of vertical height.  Decreased posterior and posterosuperior joint space.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  

Left TMJ tomogram likewise shows decreased superior and posterior joint space and there are some arthritic changes on the condyle.  There is anterior beaking and there appears to be loss of height.  On opening, there is some restricted translation but the condyles do come beneath the articular eminences.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has some AP maxillary deficiency and AP mandibular deficiency.  She has a Class II skeletal and occlusal relationship.  Occlusal plane angle is moderately steep.  Oropharyngeal airway is difficult to evaluate because the soft palate was functioning when the x-ray was taken.  At the base of the tongue there appears to be adequate airway of probably about 7 mm.  On the Panorex she actually has good space between the lateral incisors and cuspids but diminished space between the roots of the cuspid and second bicuspids.  Dental model analysis shows that she does have a fair amount of wear on her posterior teeth.  She has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship.  She has increased curve of Wilson and a significantly accentuated curve of Spee with the incisors being probably 2 mm above the occlusal plane level.  Cuspids are also elevated at least 2 mm above the occlusal plane area.  Occlusion is all in the lingual cusps on a flat plane including the second bicuspids, first and second molars.  Buccal cusps are up and out of occlusion by probably almost 2 mm.  Clinical incisal opening is about 40 mm.  Excursive movements are 11 mm in each direction.

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
AP maxillary deficiency.
Vertical maxillary deficiency.
AP mandibular deficiency.
Class II end-on occlusion.
Anterior open bite.
Occluding only on first and second molars.
Increased curve of Wilson in the upper arch.
Increased curve of Spee in the upper arch.
Missing all four first bicuspids extracted for previous orthodontic reasons.
 Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation without reduction.
 Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 3 mm. 

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Presurgical orthodontics.  
Align and level maxillary arch.  Place step in the maxillary archwire to align the four incisors and elevate plane level and extrude each of the cuspids 1 to 2 mm to get them in better vertical alignment with the posterior teeth.  
Continue to align and level the lower arch.

CAROL JOHNSON 				10/20/2006				Page 3


Surgery.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the posterior aspect of the maxilla superiorly, expand the palatal level to decrease the curve of Wilson and segments to decrease the curve of Spee.
Post-surgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

LIA OBERSTAR									09-17-03

Lia was referred by Dr. Moody Alexander, 5500 Preston Road, Ste. 360, Dallas, TX  75205.

Patient History

She had an episode of pneumonia when she was much younger but then had a bout of bronchitis about five years ago and then another bout of pneumonia about three years ago. She has osteochondritis dissecans in her right knee and has had two surgeries on that. 

Lia is currently takes no medications and has no allergies.

Lia gets headaches about one time a week in the frontal area and might drift a little bit over into the temporal area. No TMJ pain. She gets earaches but relates that to swimmer’s ear and usually gets that when she switches swimming pools. She gets occasional tinnitus but no problems with vertigo and no hearing loss. No other joints bother her except she does have significant problems with tendinitis and other tendon problems affecting some of her joints due to her swimming. She is not aware of clenching and bruxing at night and, in fact, tends to sleep with her mouth open. Her mother and her grandmother have some significant headache problems.

Patient Concerns

Chin is retruded and less visual
When she smiles, she shows a lot of her gums
Her teeth don’t fit together
The upper teeth appear too far forward
Difficulties getting her lips together

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Lia has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 24 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 7 on the right side and 6 on the left side. Left commissure is down probably 2 to 3 mm compared to the right side. Maxillary dental midline is fairly close to being on the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline is about 1 mm to the right. Mandibular dental midline and chin are pretty much aligned to each other. 

In profile view, the lip is a little bit AP deficient right at the point B level by maybe 2 to 3 mm. Upper lip supralabrale might be forward just a millimeter or 2. In profile view, chin appears retruded about 15 mm. With the lips relaxed, lip incompetence is about 13 mm. Lower third face appears somewhat elongated compared to the middle third. Chin-neckline is not well-defined.

Intraorally, she has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. Some mild crowding, lower anterior arch. Has good attached gingiva around the lower anterior teeth. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars present. Some mild crowding in the upper anterior arch. She has a Class II end-on occlusion bilaterally being at least 5 to 6 mm Class II. She has an overjet relationship of about 8 mm. She has about a 0 to 1 vertical overbite relationship.

Intranasally, she has an enlarged turbinate on the left side and a bone spur on that side. On the right side, the turbinate appears relatively normal in size. Appears that she may have some obstruction on the left side. She can breathe easier through the right side than the left side. Oropharynx appears clear and healthy. 

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. The rest of the teeth are all present, and there appears to be good bone support to them. Sinuses appear clear. Condyles appear relatively normal. Difficult to tell the morphology of the turbinates on this x-ray.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle with some anterior beaking on it. It appears that there is a pseudocyst on the condylar head. May be a decreased joint space in some area. Articular eminence is moderately steep. 

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that also has some anterior beaking. Appears there may be a pseudocyst on the condyle. Decreased superior and posterior joint space. It looks like there almost might be a small calcification in the posterior aspect of the joint space. Articular eminence is moderately steep. 

On opening, there is fairly good translation of both condyles. Anterior beaking is much more obvious, particularly on the right side. On the open views, the joint space seems a little bit decreased on the left side.

Waters view shows that the left turbinate is larger than the right turbinate. Sinuses appear reasonably clear, although the x-ray may be just slightly overrotated and difficult to determine. Frontal sinuses are quite large, but frontal and ethmoid sinuses appear clear.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph demonstrates vertical maxillary excess and significant AP mandibular deficiency with perhaps some slight asymmetry. Oropharyngeal airway appears quite constricted at the base of the soft palate where it measures about 4.5 mm, and at the base of the tongue in some areas it may be only a couple of millimeters wide. Turbinates appear to extend just slightly beyond the hard palate.

Tooth-size analysis reveals no Bolton’s discrepancy. Hand articulating the models shows that Lia has good transverse width of the occlusions that she can be put into fairly close Class I cuspid-molar relationship with no transverse discrepancies. Still has about a 2-mm anterior overjet, but this may be due to the mild crowding in the lower anterior arch and some malrotations in the maxillary cuspid area. Lia does have about a 0.5-mm tooth-size discrepancy in the bicuspid areas involving all of the teeth with the lowers being larger than the uppers. 

Basic Diagnoses

Vertical maxillary hyperplasia
AP maxillary hyperplasia
AP mandibular hypoplasia
AP microgenia
Class II occlusion
Anterior open bite tendency
Bilateral TMJ arthritis and possible articular disc dislocations
Severely decreased oropharyngeal airway with an airway measurement of 4 mm or less
Overangulation of the maxillary incisors
Overangulation of the mandibular incisors
Anterior mandibular vertical hyperplasia
Hypertrophied turbinates
Partial nasal airway obstruction
Headaches 

Recommended Treatment

MRI of left and right TMJ

Extractions
Extract maxillary and mandibular first bicuspids

Presurgical orthodontics 
Align and level maxillary arch closing the extraction space
Align and level mandibular arch closing the extraction space

Surgery
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the maxilla superiorly
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors if discs are displaced and still salvageable (will depend on MRI evaluation)
Bilateral partial turbinectomies
Bony augmentation genioplasty

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

B378 
B378 is 16 years old and is referred by Dr. _. B378's jaw joint problems began about 2 years ago of unknown etiology. He had orthodontics at the age of 10 and had a palatal expander at the age of 4. He has had myofunctional therapy and some tongue thrust therapy as well. B378 gets headaches at about a level of 5 in the frontal, temporal and then on top of the head areas. He rates his TMJ pain at 3, average daily pain at 1, jaw function and diet at 5, disability at 3. B378 was in orthodontic appliances from about the age of 10 through the age of 14.

When B378 lies on his back he sometimes has difficulty breathing and also some difficulty swallowing. Sometimes during the day also when he is up particularly like if he is driving and he is turning his head to look behind him, he can develop difficulties breathing.

In frontal view, B378 has some asymmetry in the nasal structures. Upper lip length is about 21 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 1 mm. The right eye may be just slightly elevated 1 to 2 mm compared to the left one. Maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline. The nasal tip is probably about 5 mm off towards the right side. Mandibular dental midline is about 2 mm to the right side. Chin is fairly close to being on the facial midline. In profile view, B378 has prominence of the nasal dorsum. He has some slight AP deficiency of maybe 2 to 3 mm. Chin appears to be retruded by probably about 15 to 18 mm. Chin-neckline is short although the chin-neck angle is reasonably well defined. He does have some definite prominence of the nasal dorsum. Intraorally, B378 appears to have all of his lower teeth back to the second molars and also the upper teeth back through the second molars. I do not see any maxillary third molars but he does have a developing mandibular left third molar but no developing right third molar. In the lower jaw, the arch is quite asymmetric with the right cuspid being about 3 mm posterior to the left cuspid. Midline of the mandibular right central incisor appears to be in line with the chin. He appears to have a full Class II cuspid relationship on the right side. He is fairly close to being Class I on the right side. He has about a 2 mm vertical overbite relationship. Oropharyngeal area looks relatively normal as does the uvula, soft palate, faucial pillars and tonsils. Intranasally, B378 has a significantly deviated septum with it being tipped off to the right side at the superior aspect, but at the base the septum it is tipped off to the left side. He has a fairly large spur from the septum on the left side that is actually touching against the turbinate on the left side. The right turbinate is quite hyperplastic whereas the left one is moderate hyperplastic but he probably has more nasal airway obstruction on the left because of the septal spurring. To palpation, he has some mild to moderate tenderness over the TMJs, left medial pterygoid, left masseter and bilateral styloid areas. Joints seem to function relatively smoothly. Maximal incisal opening is 44 mm. Excursive movements are about 6 mm in each direction. On B378's right side he has an open bite posteriorly and he may have only an occlusion on the maxillary right second bicuspid, lower first bicuspid. He also has crossbites in the molar areas bilaterally.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
:
Panorex shows the presences of the mandibular third molars and perhaps some beginning development of the maxillary third molars. Sinuses look relatively clear.

 
Waters view shows that there is a deviation in the bony nasal septum and turbinates appear moderately enlarged. The sinuses look relatively clear.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has sort of a hockey stick shape to it. It appears to be a little bit decreased in vertical volume. Articular eminence is fairly steep.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that also appears to be a little bit flat on top. There appears to be almost increased vertical joint space. Articular eminence is fairly steep. On opening, there is moderately good translation of both condyles.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that B378 has a skeletal Class II jaw relationship. Oropharyngeal airway particularly at the base of the tongue measures about 8 mm but at the soft palate level it measures about 12 mm. Prominence of the nasal dorsum is noted.

B378's diagnoses is as follows:
Rule out TMJ pathology ( shows articular discs in place, but possible reactive tissue present).
AP maxillary deficiency.
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
AP mandibular deficiency. AP microgenia. Class II occlusion on the right side and Class I occlusion on the left side.
Arch asymmetry with mandibular dental midline being shifted off towards the right side. Mandibular right cuspid may be 1 to 2 mm posterior to the left cuspid. Probable tooth size discrepancy with small upper lateral incisors.
Open bite on the right side. Impacted wisdom tooth or teeth in the mandible. Nasal septal deviation. Hypertrophied turbinates. Difficulties breathing through the nose. Nasal asymmetry with it being shifted significantly towards the right side. Transverse cant in the occlusion with the left side being lower than the right side. Over-angulation of lower incisors.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT: Presurgical orthodontics. Align and level maxillary arch. Expand the arch transversely some and open up 1 mm of space around each upper lateral incisor. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies.
Align and level mandibular arch. Consider slenderizing the lower anterior teeth and removing 1 to 2 mm of tooth structure and retract to help decrease the significant over-angulation of the lower incisors.

 
Surgery.
Perform any indicated TMJ surgery.
Maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla forward, downgraft it posteriorly and level transversely.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible forward in a counterclockwise direction.
Augmentation genioplasty.
Removal of impacted third molar(s)
Bilateral inferior nasal turbinectomies. Nasal septoplasty if indicated.
External rhinoplasty.
Post surgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B378  Evaluation Age: 16
DOB:
Date of Acquisition: Date of Interpretation:
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears abnormal. It is difficult to tell if the cortical plate is intact. It appears there may be some discal tissue medially displaced. There appears to be some decreased vertical joint space. The lateral rim of the fossa is fairly flat with a downward curvature of perhaps a couple of mm. The lateral pole of the condyle appears to expand lateral to the lateral rim of the fossa.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears similar in size to the opposite side. There appears to be decreased vertical joint space. The disc appears to be medial and perhaps anteriorly displaced. The lateral rim of the fossa is fairly flat similar to the opposite side. It curves inferiorly 1 to 2 mm.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that appears a little bit pointed and blunted at the anterosuperior aspect of the condyle particularly towards the medial side. There appears to be some significant flattening on the posterior head of the condyle. Articular disc appears to be in position. The joint space almost appears to be excessive. It appears there may be some fluid or tissue in the superior joint space and there appears to be increased soft tissue between the disc and condyle in the lower joint space. Articular eminence is fairly steep. On opening, there is moderate translation of the condyle and the articular disc appears to translate with the condyle in a reduced position. There appears to be some additional tissue between the disc and the condyle.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that appears flattened on top. There appears to be an increased vertical joint space. Articular disc appears to be in position. There may be some tissue superior and inferior to the disc. Articular eminence is fairly steep. There appears be fluid in the superior and inferior joint spaces. On opening there appears to be reasonable translation of the condyle and the articular disc appears to move with the condyle in a reduced position.

IMPRESSION: Mild TMJ arthritis with both articular discs in position but with possible reactive tissue surrounding the discs.
Possible reactive arthritis.
