original_note:
REBECCA WILSON									04/20/2009

Becky is 24 years old and referred by Dr. Duane Keller, 3955 Bayless Avenue, Suite 100, St. Louis, MO 63125.  Becky’s TMJ problems began she was about 14 years in 1999.  Prior to that, she had orthodontics from the third grade and had treatment for about 2 years.  She had splint therapy from about 2002 to 2004, but not continuously.  Orthodontics was repeated in 2004 through 2006 without relief.  She has recently gotten a new splint in March 2009 to help keep her from clenching at night.  Medications she currently takes are birth control pill, Advil, Tylenol, and ibuprofen, etc. 

Becky rates her TMJ pain at a level of 10, average daily pain around the head and neck area at 10 predominantly in the neck area.  She rates her jaw function at 5, diet at 6, disability at 4.  She gets occasional earaches, occasional tinnitus, and she does get vertigo.  She is very much aware of clenching and bruxing at nighttime and this causes her to wake up with significant pain in the mornings.  She also clenches and bruxes during the day.  Joints that bother her include her shoulders, right worse than left, elbows, right ankle, and neck.  She has no known allergies to jewelry.  She had an adverse reaction to Keflex when she was younger. 

Becky feels that her occlusion is slowly but continuously changing.  

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Becky has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 24 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 3 mm.  The maxillary, mandibular, and chin midline are all pretty on the facial midline.  The nasal tip is just slightly to the left side about 2 mm.  In profile, the lower third face appears somewhat elongated.  The chin neckline is short, although the chin neck angle is reasonably well defined.  She has a head postured neck position.  The maxilla is retruded about 3 mm.  The mandible is retruded about 8 mm.  She has a lip incompetence of about 6 mm.  Intraorally, Becky has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars.  She has a 3 x 3 retainer on the lingual side.  She has adequate attached gingiva in the lower anterior arch.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  She has an inferior open bite of about 2 mm.  She is probably 5 mm Class III bilaterally.  She occludes on only her first and second molars.  In centric relation, she occludes only on the left second molars and all other teeth are out of occlusion.  With a little bit more force, then the right side will touch in the second molar area.  Actually, in centric relation, she occludes only on the left first molars and all other teeth are out of occlusion including at least 1-2 mm open bite posteriorly on the right side.  Actually, in her centric relation, the mandible is deviated off towards to the left side at the dental midline about 1 mm and in the chin area bout 2 mm.  Transversely, the maxilla appears level.  Oropharyngeal tissues all look normal and healthy.  Intranasally, the turbinates appear maybe mildly to moderately enlarged, but not too significant.  There does not appear to be any significant nasal airway obstruction.  She has no problems with snoring, but has probably mild daytime somnolence, but no sleep apnea.  She does not sleep well at night mainly because she wakes up with grinding of her teeth and with pain and discomfort.  She believes that she is predominantly a nasal breather.  To palpation, she has no real significant pain around the head and neck area.  Maximum opening is about 47 mm, opening without discomfort 32 mm.  Excursion movement to the right about 12 mm and the to the left 9 mm.  On opening, she does deviate towards the left side bout 3-4 mm.  In the right joint on opening, she would initially have a pop in that joint and then it would be smooth after that.  On excursion movements, she would have some glitches in the right joint.  The left side functions smoothly.  Sometimes in the morning, she will wake up with a closed lock situation that is predominantly on the right side and she will have to manipulate her jaws and she will get a pop and then the jaw opens adequately.  At one point, Becky was placed on tetracycline to try to identify if there was a reactive arthritis present.  Apparently, one of the tablets got caught in her esophagus and created an ulcer which required hospitalization for treatment 

I-CAT EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the presence of a horizontally impacted mandibular right third molar, but all other third molars are absent.  Sinuses look clear.  Right turbinate appears enlarged.  Left joint appears some arthritic as compared to the right side. 
Coronal view shows that the right turbinate does appear quite large.  Sinuses appear clear.    
Axial view shows that the right turbinate appears enlarged.    
Left TMJ tomogram sagittal view shows condyle that appears to have some erosive changes and loss of vertical volume.  Articular eminence looks relatively flat.  It is extremely flat compared to the opposite side.  
Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is flattened anteriorly and may actually have an erosive lesion in the anterior head as well.  The condylar head has more of a hockey stick shape as compared to the opposite side.  Articular eminence is significantly steeper in this side than the opposite side.  
Left TMJ coronal view shows that there is significant loss of vertical volume.  In some areas, there appears to be almost bone-on-bone contact with significantly decreased vertical joint space.  There is some lateral beaking of the lateral pole of the condyle.  The lateral rim curves inferiorly about 2 to 3 mm.   
Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is relatively flat across the top.  Joint space looks a little bit better balanced.  The lateral rim of the fossa is fairly flat.  
Lateral cephalometric radiograph has her postured forward with the mandible not back in centric relation.  Oropharyngeal airway at the base of the tongue measures about 5 mm and at the soft palate level measures about 6.5 mm.  She has an anterior open bite, but in this x-ray she is postured forward.  

Basic diagnoses for Becky would be as follows:    

Left TMJ resorptive arthritis with articular disc dislocation without reduction and significant deformation.
Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly displaced with occasional reduction. 
Mandibular AP hypoplasia. 
Maxillary AP, posterior vertical, and transverse hypoplasia.
Occluding only on the left first molars with all other teeth out of occlusion. 
Anterior and right posterior open bite. 
Class II cuspid relationship.  
Decreased oropharyngeal airway. 
Severe TMJ pain. 
Severe myofascial pain affecting predominantly the neck, shoulders, and back. 
Difficulties eating and chewing. 
Nocturnal and daytime clenching and bruxism.  

Recommended treatment option 1 would be as follows:

Medications:  Klonopin 1 tablet q.h.s.
Removal of mandibular right impacted third molar.
Presurgical orthodontics.  
Align and level maxillary arch.  
Align and level mandibular arch.  Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing about 1-2 mm of tooth structure to correct for slight tooth size discrepancy.  
CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol).
Surgery. 
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction with mandibular advancement and TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. 
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance it forward, down graft it slightly posteriorly, and expand.  
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen).
Bilateral coronoidotomies. 
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

Recommended treatment option 2 would be as follows:

Medications:  Klonopin 1 tablet q.h.s.
Surgery Stage 1.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and supposition with Mitek anchors.  
Surgical removal of impacted mandibular right third molar.
 Presurgical orthodontics.  
Align and level maxillary arch.  
Align and level mandibular arch.  Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing about 1-2 mm of tooth structure to correct for slight tooth size discrepancy.  
Surgery Stage 2. 
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance it forward, down graft it slightly posteriorly, and expand.  
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
JAKE VINES									05/17/2011

Jake is 20 years old, birth date 07/30/1990.  Referred by some former patients.  Jake is in good health with no major medical problems.  He takes no medications.  Jake’s primary concerns are as follows:

Clicking in the jaws.
Sore muscles.
Occasional locking of the jaw.
Sore mouth.
Occasional headaches. 
Tiredness of the jaws.

Jake’s jaw problems began when he was about 19.  At the age of 19, he did get hit in the jaw on the right side and since then has had some clicking in his jaw joints.  To date, Jake has had no treatment for his jaw situation.  Jake gets headaches once or twice a week at about a level of 4 in the temple and back of the head.  Jake has had 3 major headaches in the past month where he wakes up with the headaches in the morning.  Jake is aware of clenching his teeth at nighttime.  He rates his TMJ pain at 4, average daily pain is at about level of 7 and predominantly in the masseter muscle areas.  He rates his jaw function at 4, diet at 0, disability at 2.  He has no trouble with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo nor hearing loss.  Other joints that bother him include his knees.  He takes no medications.

In frontal view, Jake has fairly good facial balance in the middle and upper third face.  There is some slight asymmetry in the mandible.  Upper lip length is about 20 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 3 mm.  The mandibular dental midline is about 1 mm off to the left side.  The chin is probably about 3 mm off to the left.  In profile view, Jake has some slight AP maxillary deficiency of probably 3 to 4 mm.  He may be just slightly AP prominent by a couple of millimeters.  Jake does have about 5 mm of lip incompetence.  The chin neckline and chin neck angle are well defined.  He has a high mandibular occlusal plane angle facial morphology.  Intraorally, Jake has all of his lower teeth back through the second molars erupted in.  He has some mild crowding of the lower anterior arch and some lingual inclination of the lower incisors.  He has good attached gingiva.  In the upper arch, he has got all of his teeth back through the second molars.  Jake has a full Class III occlusal relationship bilaterally, being just slightly greater on the right side than the left side.  He has got an anterior cross bite a full posterior cross bite on the left side and an end-on relationship on the right side.  The maxilla has a little bit of a transverse collapse on the left side with it being a little bit more narrow on that side compared to the right side.  The mandibular posterior teeth may be rotated just a little bit to the left side a couple of millimeters.  He has a zero vertical over bite relationship.  Oropharyngeal tissues look healthy, but he does have moderately enlarged tonsils and a mildly enlarge uvula.  Intranasally, turbinates appear mild to moderate in size.  He has had some sinusitis problems, and, in fact, his x-rays which were taken on May 10 show that the left sinus was totally obliterated with fluid.  To palpation, Jake has no significant tenderness around the head and neck area.  On opening, he has a very soft pop in the right joint at probably about 20 mm and a closing pop at about 10.  The left side seems to function relatively smoothly.  The maximal incisal opening is 61 mm and excursion movements are 7 mm in each direction.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars.  Turbinates appear moderate in size, but the bony septum appears reasonably straight.  He has a Class III cuspid-molar relationship.  He has a root canal in the maxillary right first molar.  The condylar head and necks appear somewhat elongated.  

Coronal radiograph shows that he has complete obliteration of his left maxillary sinus and it is completely full of fluids and/or mucous.  The right turbinate is quite large, although the left one is relatively normal in size.  The right turbinate appears to  be causing some obstruction in that side of the nasal airway.  The septum is reasonably straight.  The maxillary right sinus looks normal and healthy.  

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has reasonably morphology.  There is decreased joint space superior and posteriorly.  The articular eminence is relatively shallow.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle with fairly good morphology and actually healthier looking than the opposite side.  The joint space is decreased superior and posterior.  The articular eminence is relatively shallow.  

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width.  The lateral pole is lower than the medial pole.  There is decreased vertical joint space.  The condyle appears a little bit long vertically.  The lateral rim curves inferiorly about 2 to 3 mm.  

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle where the lateral pole is lower than the medial pole.  Vertical joint space is less than normal.  The condyle appears to have good mediolateral width.  There is significant decreased joint space vertically.  The lateral rim curves inferiorly about 3 to 4 mm. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows a skeletal Class III and occlusal Class III relationship.  Cephalometric analysis shows that cephalometrically he has a Class III skeletal occlusal relationship.  Oropharyngeal airway looks good.   

Basic diagnoses for Jake would be as follows:

Bilateral mandibular condylar hyperplasia with displaced articular discs.
Mandibular deviated prognathism.
Maxillary AP and transverse hypoplasia.
Class III occlusion.
Anterior and bilateral posterior cross bite with the cross bite being worse on the left side.
Hypertrophied turbinates creating nasal airway obstruction.
Headaches.
Myofascial pain. 
TMJ pain.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Presurgical orthodontics. 
	a.	Align and level maxillary arch.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and 			cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. 
	b.	Align and level mandibular arch.
2.	Surgery.
	a.	Bilateral TMJ high condylectomies and articular disc repositioning and 			ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
	b.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to rotate the mandible back on the 		facial midline and reposition it posteriorly.
	c.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance and expand.
	d.	Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies. 
3.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
ANA LOZANO										10-14-03

Patient History

Ana was last seen in 08/1997. It is now about six years since her last visit here, and her situation has gotten worse. She had had previous orthognathic surgery before she came to see me. Currently she has earaches, tinnitus, sometimes vertigo, some hearing loss, and sometimes hypersensitive hearing. She clenches and bruxes her teeth at night. She does have a night guard, though, for this. She rates her headaches at a level of 6 that occur in the frontal and temporal area. She rates her TMJ pain at 5, overall pain on an average day at 5, jaw function at 7, diet at 6, and disability at 5. She has a little bit of decreased sensation in her chin area from her previous surgery. She takes Advil, Motrin, and Tylenol for pain medication. Ana is very uncomfortable at night when she tries to sleep if she doesn’t have her mouth guard in. She doesn’t know where to place her lower jaw. Other joints that trouble her include her hips and her wrists. She has a bite plate that she has to wear at night in order to sleep, or she has severe pain problems. 

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Ana has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 21 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is 4 mm. Maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline is a little bit off to the left as is the chin, probably 1 mm at the incisor and 2 mm at the chin. 

In profile, upper lip supralabrale is posterior to subnasale by probably a couple millimeters; therefore, the maxilla is probably about 4 mm AP deficient. The mandible at the chin area is retruded about 25 mm. Chin-neckline is extremely short, although the chin-neck angle is reasonably well-defined. She has some lip incompetence of probably about 7 mm.

Intraorally, she is missing the lower first bicuspids and has a 3x3 retainer in place. Teeth are fairly well-aligned. She has good attached gingiva around the lower anterior teeth. Second molars are showing some evidence of wear. In the upper arch, she is missing the first bicuspids and has a 2x2 retainer in place there. In centric relation, she has about a 5 to 6-mm Class II occlusion, being a little bit worse on the left side than the right side. She has an anterior open bite of probably a couple of millimeters. Occludes on basically the first and second molars on the right side and maybe only the second molar on the left side. Ana states that when she is in her most centric relation position that only her second molars on each side touch, and all other teeth are out of occlusion. Ana’s open bite is a little bit worse on the left side as compared to the right side. Mandibular occlusal plane is fairly level. Maxillary left cuspid is about 2 mm higher than the right cuspid. Mandibular occlusal plane is fairly level. Ana is aware of snoring when she sleeps. There are times when she is awake she feels as though she doesn’t get enough air when she is breathing. Oropharynx appears clear. 

Nasal airway looks good. Turbinates are of moderate size, but there appears to be adequate airway around them.

To palpation, she has moderate to severe pain throughout the head and neck area. She has crepitation in the joints bilaterally indicative of probably perforations through the bilaminar tissues. Maximal incisal opening is about 38 mm. Excursive movement to the right is 7 and to the left about 6 mm. She can open about 30 mm without too much discomfort. However, it is painful for her to try to chew anything of any substance.
Basic Diagnoses

Bilateral TMJ severe resorptive arthritis (probable ICR) and articular disc dislocation without reduction and nonsalvageable
AP maxillary deficiency
Anterior vertical maxillary excess
Transverse maxillary deficiency
Posterior vertical deficiency
AP mandibular deficiency
Apertognathia
Class II occlusion
Missing all four first bicuspids and all four third molars
High occlusal plane angulation
Occluding only on the second molars bilaterally
Headaches 
TMJ pain
Myofascial pain
Previously placed bone plates in the maxilla x 4 with 15 bone screws
Decreased oropharyngeal airway
Severe snoring
Daytime somnolence
Sleep apnea

Recommended Treatment

Presurgical orthodontics 
Align and level maxillary arch. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies
Align and level mandibular arch

CT scan of TMJs and jaws (TMJ Concepts protocol)

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance it and downgraft it posteriorly with bone plate stabilization and hydroxyapatite grafting
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvested from abdomen)
Bilateral coronoidectomies
Removal of bone plates (x 4) and bone screws (x 15) from maxilla

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

original_note:
MATTHEW JENKINS								11-11-02

Matt is 23 years old and referred by Dr. Don Lowrance.

Patient History

Matt is in good health with no major medical problems. He has some allergy problems and some sinus problems. 

Matt became aware of his problems when he was about 17 years old. That’s when he and his family and others noticed he was developing facial asymmetry of an unknown etiology. He had previous orthodontics from the ages of 14 to 16. He has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, or hearing loss. No other joints bother him. He has a dust allergy but NO OTHER KNOWN ALLERGIES. He did have migraines growing up but not so much now. He does get nocturnal clenching of which he is aware. He doesn’t observe much of a pattern, but he does state that at times he does wake up with significant headaches and at other times they develop more toward the evening. He does get significant headaches about two times per week he rates between a level of 4 to 7 in the frontal and temporal areas bilaterally. He rates TMJ pain at 5, overall average daily pain levels at 1, jaw function and diet at 4, and disability at 3. 

Patient Concerns

Primary concerns that Matt has include the following:

The lower jaw is going off toward the left side
Teeth don’t touch. Particularly on the right side, there is a big gap between the upper and lower teeth
Headaches
Soreness in the jaw area
Pain in the back of the neck and base of the skull, more so on the right side than the left side
Difficulties eating and chewing
Speech affected by the jaw problem

Current Medications

He uses Rhinocort for his allergies and does about one spray per day.

Clinical Evaluation

In frontal view, Matt has fairly good facial symmetry in the middle and upper third face. He has some significant asymmetry in the lower third face. Upper lip length is 20 mm. Upper tooth to lip relationship is 0. Maxillary dental midline is shifted about 2 mm to the left, and mandibular dental midline is shifted 3 mm to the left. Chin is shifted about 8 mm to the left.

In profile view, Matt has probably 4 to 6 mm of AP maxillary deficiency. Chin appears to be about 8 to 10 mm AP deficient. Chin-neckline is short, although the chin-neck angle is reasonably well defined.

Intraorally, Matt has all of his lower teeth back through the second molars. He has good attached gingiva in the lower anterior arch. Fairly good symmetry anteroposteriorly from cuspid to cuspid. In the upper arch, Matt has all of his teeth back through the second molars. Matt has a slight Class III cuspid relationship on the left side by about 3 to 4 mm, and on the right side it is about 4 to 5 mm Class III. He has anterior and bilateral posterior crossbites. He has about a 
0.5-mm open-bite anteriorly on the right side and has an open-bite involving the central incisor back through the first molar. As Matt shifts his mandible to the dental midline to the right about 4 or 5 mm, it improves his facial symmetry fairly significantly. There is a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the left side being about 2 mm higher than the right side.

Intraorally, oropharynx appears clear. Uvula is a little bit hyperplastic but seems to sit forward. When he lies on his back to sleep, he opens his mouth and breathes mainly through his mouth.

Intranasally, he has significant deviation of the nasal septum off towards the left side, and it seems to block most of the airway. Turbinates are not particularly large at all. If the septum were straightened up, the left airway would probably be pretty much normal. Right nasal airway is excessive right now because of the severe deviation of the septum. Straightening it up should balance the airways out equally on both sides.

To palpation, he only has some moderate tenderness over the TMJs, being a little bit greater on the right side than the left side. Joints seem to function relatively smoothly. He is not aware of any clicking or popping of his jaw joints. Maximal incisal opening is about 48 mm, excursive movement to the right 5 mm and to the left 4 mm.

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. Sinuses look clear. Turbinates look relatively small in size, and septum appears reasonably straightened in this view. Right condyle looks larger vertically and anteroposteriorly than the left condyle. Left condyle appears as though it is flattened on the head somewhat. Sinuses look clear. 

Waters view shows that the sinuses are all clear. Has huge frontal sinuses. Nasal septum is deviated off towards the left side. Significant asymmetry in the mandible is noted with the left inferior border being elevated probably at least 10 mm higher than the right mandible angle.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle with fairly good morphology. Articular eminence is moderately steep and moderate in length. Joint space looks quite good. A little bit of flattening on the head of the condyle perhaps but not too significant.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condylar head and neck that appear elongated. Condyle appears to fill a good part of the fossa. Decreased superoposterior joint space. There is a recess at the base of the articular eminence where the posterior band of the disc is probably sitting. 

On opening, there is good translation of both condyles with the right one actually translating a greater amount than the left side.

Cephalometrically, there is vertical asymmetry noted with the inferior border on the right side of the mandible being 11 mm lower than the left side. Differences in occlusal plane angulation with the occlusal plane being about 5 mm lower on the right side as compared to the left side. Class III occlusal relationship is noted and probably slight Class III skeletal relationship.

Tooth-size analysis revealed no significant difference between the upper and lower anterior teeth. Measuring the vertical height of the mandibular body in the first molar area on the tomograms, on the left side it measures 33 mm and on the right side 37 mm. This indicates a 
4-mm vertical height discrepancy.

Basic Diagnoses

Right TMJ osteochondroma versus condylar hyperplasia
AP mandibular deficiency and severe asymmetry
Maxillary AP deficiency and transverse asymmetry
Class III occlusion being worse on the right side as compared to the left side
Anterior bilateral posterior crossbite tendencies
Transverse cant in the occlusion with the left side being 2 mm higher than the right side 
Open-bite extending from right central incisor to right first molar and on the left side in the lateral incisor area
Severely deviated nasal septum
Nasal airway obstruction
Headaches 
TMJ pain

Recommended Treatment

Presurgical orthodontics
Align and level maxillary arch. Consider including the second molars in the treatment 
Align and level mandibular arch. Consider stripping the lower anterior teeth, removing about 1 mm of tooth structure to help compensate for a tooth-size discrepancy created by the slight overangulation of the lower incisors. Consider stripping lower bicuspids removing no more than 0.5 mm off of each tooth, maximizing posterior anchorage and retracting

Surgery
Right TMJ condylectomy and articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance and level the transverse occlusal plane
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible and to correct the asymmetry
Nasal septoplasty
Genioplasty
Right mandibular inferior border ostectomy

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

original_note:
ADAM RANKIN									10/04/2010

Adam is 24 years old, birth date 11/26/1985.  Referred by Dr. Tara Hardin who gave them my name.  The correspondence we will do will be to Dr. Ben Kaseff, 6417 Branch Hill Guinea Pike, Suite 101, Loveland, OH 45140.

By history, Adam’s joint problems began when somewhere between the ages of 11 to 13.  He did have orthodontics beginning around 10 or 11 and continued through the age of 17.  Relative to his joint issues, he was some aware of some popping in his joints back then of unknown etiology.  Adam has no problems with headaches, but rates his average daily TMJ pain at about a level of 5 with average daily overall pain at 5, jaw function at 4, diet at 4, and disability at 5.  He has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo nor hearing loss.  The only other joints that really bother him are his knees.  He is aware of clenching and bruxing at nighttime and also during the day.  He does not take any medications.  

In frontal view, Adam has fairly good facial symmetry.  The left side may be just slightly longer than the right side by 2 to 3 mm.  Upper lip length is about 25 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is 4 mm.  The maxillary dental midline is fairly close to being on the facial midline.  The mandibular dental midline is fairly close to being on the maxillary midline, although the chin is about 3 mm to the right.  Adam is missing the lower central incisor.  He appears to be missing the left central.  He has a significant transverse cant in the occlusion with the right side being at least 3 mm higher than the left side in both arches.  In profile view, upper lip supralabrale is posterior to subnasale by probably 2 to 3 mm.  The maxilla is probably 5 to 6 mm AP deficient.  The chin is retruded about 15 mm.  The chin neckline is short, although the chin neck angle is fairly well defined.  He does have some prominence of the nasal dorsum.  Intraorally, he is missing the mandibular left central incisor.  He has some slight asymmetry in the lower arch with the left cuspid being probably about 1 mm posterior to the right cuspid.  The upper arch likewise may be 1 mm posterior with the cuspid on the left side compared to the right side.  Intraorally, he is missing the rest of his teeth back through the second molars, but he has a retained primary E on the mandibular left side that may be ankylosed and is submerged compared to the other teeth.  In the upper arch, he has all of his teeth back through the second molars.  He has a Class I cuspid molar relationship on the right side and slight Class II end-on cuspid relationship on the left side and a Class II molar relationship.  He has a 5 to 6 mm deep bite anteriorly.  Oropharyngeal tissues look reasonably healthy.  Tonsils have previously been removed.  Intranasally, the turbinates appear moderately enlarged.  The septum appears to be deviated a little bit off towards the right side.  The columella is wide at its base.  The nostrils may be somewhat constricted.  To palpation, Adam has no tenderness around the head and neck area.  On opening, the right joint seems fairly smooth.  On the left side, he has an open click of 5 to 10 mm and closing click of probably 5 mm.  The maximal incisal opening is 47 mm, opening without significant discomfort 40 mm.  The excursion movements are 10 mm in each direction.  With excursion movements, he had audible popping in both the left and right TMJs.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars and a retained E in the mandibular left quadrant.  The permanent second bicuspid is not present.  The turbinates look enlarged within the nasal cavity.  The sinuses look relatively clear.  It is difficult to evaluate the joints in this view.  He does have a more prominent gonial notch on the right side compared to the left side. 

Coronal view shows he has enlarged turbinates that appear to block most of the nasal airway.  The sinus look relatively clear and healthy.

Axial view shows that the sinuses appear clear.  He has a little deviation of the nasal septum anteriorly with a bulge out towards the right side.  The turbinates appear enlarged and appear to block most of the airway.  

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has a little bit of sclerosis on the top and a little bit of anterior beaking present.  The articular eminence is moderately shallow.  There is decreased superior and posterior joint space and increased anterior joint space.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has a little sclerosis on top.  The condyle is significantly positioned posterior in the fossa with decreased posterior and superior joint space and significantly increased anterior joint space.  The articular eminence is relatively shallow.  

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width.  There is definite decreased joint space.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 to 4 mm.  

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with fairly good mediolateral width.  The condyle is actually postured out laterally in the fossa with an increased medial joint space.  There is decreased vertical joint space, particularly at the lateral rim of the fossa.  There is almost like a small osteophytic development extending out almost beyond the lateral rim of the fossa.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly probably 3 mm or so.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows there is a definite vertical discrepancy at the occlusal plane level with the left side being almost 5 mm lower than the right side in the bicuspid and first molar area.  The inferior border of the mandible appears to only have about a 2 mm vertical difference.  The maxilla and mandible appear to be retruded.  The oropharyngeal airway space may only be about 3.5 mm at the soft palate level and 5 mm at the base of the tongue.  It appears that there was some movement during the x-ray relative to the soft tissues.  

The orthodontist we are going to use for Adam is Dr. Shelley Tretter, 11831 Mason Montgomery Road, Suite A, Cincinnati, OH 45249, phone #513-489-3736.  

Basic diagnoses for Adam would be as follows:

Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation being worse on the right side as compared to the left side with early reduction of the disc on the left side and nonreducing on the right side.
Maxillary AP and posterior vertical hypoplasia as well as transverse asymmetry with the left side 3 mm lower than the right side. 
Mandibular AP hypoplasia with vertical asymmetry in the dental alveolus with the left side being 3 mm lower than the right side.  
Class I occlusion on the right side, Class II end-on occlusion on the left side.
Anterior deep bite.
High occlusal plane angle.
Ankylosed mandibular left primary E without a successor.  
Hypertrophied turbinates creating nasal airway obstruction.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of 5 mm (normal dimension 11 mm).
TMJ pain.
Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of 5 mm (secondary to missing mandibular left central incisor).

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Presurgical orthodontics.
	a.	Extract mandibular left primary E.
	b.	Place turbos on maxillary central incisors, mandibular right first and second 			molars, and at least one of the bicuspids to open the bite.  Consider using TADs 			on the left side of the maxilla to vertically stabilize those teeth so that they are not 		extruded and to consider possible slight intrusion of those teeth 1 to 2 mm.  Tip 			lateral incisors roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for 				interdental osteotomies.
	c.	Vertically extrude and align the left mandibular cuspid, bicuspid, and first molar.  			Close the extraction space about 2 mm by losing posterior anchorage and having 			the molars move forward.  Make the space equal to the size of the second bicuspid 		on the opposite side of the arch.  Consider placing a turbo on the mandibular left 			second molar to provide some vertical stability on that side.   Tip lower incisors 			forward opening up 1 mm of space around each of the anterior 5 teeth to 				compensate for the tooth size discrepancy.  These teeth can later be bonded or 			veneers or crowns placed. 
2.	Surgery. 
	a.	Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
	b.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible forward 				approximately 14 mm and level it transversely.  
	c.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance it forward approximately 6 mm at the 			incisal edge as well as level it transversely and correct any transverse 				discrepancies that may be present.  
	d.	Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies.
	e.	Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy to advance the chin forward about 4 			mm.
3.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion. 
4.	Restorative dentistry to place a dental implant in the left mandibular second bicuspid area 	with placement of bonding, veneers, or crowns on the lower incisors to correct for the 	tooth size discrepancy. 

The vertical asymmetry affecting the left maxillary and mandibular dental alveolus is the result of the ankylosed mandibular left primary second molar.  Apparently, it was attempted to extrude that tooth out vertically into the arch, but instead because it was ankylosed, it intruded the adjacent teeth and allowed the maxillary teeth to be pulled downwards as well creating the vertical asymmetry on the left side.

original_note:
B459 
B459 is 17 years old. B459 was referred by Dr. XX.

This year, B459 was involved in a motor vehicle accident where the car she was a passenger in was T-boned by an 18-wheeler. B459 sustained a pelvic fracture, right clavicle fracture, a coccyx fracture, and multiple fractures to the mandible including a left subcondylar fracture with significant displacement, left parasymphysis non-displaced fracture, and a right mandibular ramus non-displaced fracture. She was treated with the placement of arch bars and intermaxillary fixation. She was in intermaxillary fixation for 2 months. B459 is now 3 months post injury.

B459 has no problems with headaches, TMJ pain, nor myofascial pain, but she rates her jaw function at 7, diet at 7, disability at 6. She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, but does get occasional vertigo if she gets up too fast, but this was present prior to her injury. No other joints bother her. She is not aware of clenching or bruxing. The only medications she takes are BCP's. B459 has never had orthodontic treatment performed. Surgery was this year and consisted of closed reduction of the left subcondylar fracture, left parasymphysis and right ramus fractures. She has had the intermaxillary fixation off for 2 months.

In frontal view, B459 has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 17 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is about 2.5 mm. The maxillary, mandibular, and chin midline are all pretty much on the facial midline. In profile, she has good facial balance. Intraorally, B459  has all of her teeth back through the second molars present. She has thin attached gingiva around the lower anterior arch. In the upper arch, she also has all of her teeth back through the second molars. She has basically a Class I cuspid-molar relationship with about a 2.5 mm overbite. On opening, B459 deviates about 3 mm to the left. The incisal opening is about 38 mm. The maximal incisal opening is 38 mm and excursion movement to the right 3 mm and to the left 13 mm. To palpation, she has no tenderness around the head and neck area except for the masseter muscles on the left side at a level of 4 and the left TMJ at 3. Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively healthy. She has very large tonsils, but she does not have much problems with tonsillitis apparently.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. There is evidence of a previous fracture on the right ramus area and also evidence of a left subcondylar fracture. The left parasymphysis fracture is not readily identified.

Coronal view shows that the septum is reasonably straight. Turbinates are of moderate size, but sinuses are relatively clear.
Axial view shows that the sinuses are clear. The septum is reasonably straight and the turbinates are of moderate size, but clinically there is no airway obstruction.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows the presence of a subcondylar fracture with the condyle rotated medially. The condylar stump/neck of condyle function against the articular eminence. It is completely out of the fossa.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is postured just slightly forward in the fossa. The condyle otherwise looks relatively normal.
Left TMJ coronal view shows not much present in the fossa. The head of the condyle is completely missing in the x-ray.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with good shape to it as well as a good fossa but some decreased lateral joint space.
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows good skeletal Class I occlusal relationship. She appears to have good oropharyngeal airway.

Diagnoses for B459 would be as follows:
Involvement in MVA this year sustaining left mandibular subcondylar fracture with severe displacement, left parasymphysis fracture (non-displaced) and right mandibular ramus fracture (non-displaced) Closed reduction of mandibular right ramus fracture, left parasymphysis, but open exploration of left subcondylar fracture. Malunion of left subcondylar fracture of the condyle at a 90-degree angle to the vertical ramus.
Articular disc positioned between the stump of the mandibular condylar neck and articular eminence.
Class I cuspid-molar relationship.
Maxillary incisors slightly retropositioned since the accident likely due to the arch bars.

The treatment options were discussed with the patient, her boyfriend and her parents and included the following:
Do no surgery and function with the current anatomical relationship. Consider left mandibular TMJ surgery with removal of the condylar stump, placement of the articular disc over the remaining condylar stump, and sagittal split osteotomy to reposition the condyle stump posteriorly and superiorly into the fossa. Left TMJ reconstruction with total joint prostheses.

Since the patient is functioning well with her current normal but adequately functioning left TMJ joint relationship and the fact that she is pain free with reasonably good jaw function, I would suggest option 1 at this time. If she develops symptoms or problems, then option 2 or 3 can be performed at any time in the future. However, her good occlusion, lack of pain, and reasonably good function suggest that option treatment 1 benefits supersede the risks of doing the other two treatment options. However, if the patient develops symptoms such as TMJ pain, headaches, myofascial pain, malocclusion, significant difficulties eating and chewing, etc., then either option 2 or 3 could have benefits that supersede the risks.

B459 and her family understand the risks and I have currently recommended that she follow option 1 unless symptoms develop.
B459 MRI  
DOB:	 

DATE OF ACQUISITION: 
Right TMJ shows a condyle with fairly good morphology and articular eminence with a moderate inclination. The disc appears to be in position in this joint. There is moderate translation. There is some flattening on the anterior head of the condyle, however, the disc appears to be in normal position on this side. There is a large area of fluid filling most of the fossa in the left joint space.

Left mandibular sagittal view shows the condyle positioned beneath and almost anterior to the articular eminence. It appears to the disc is in position. There is some translation of the condyle. The articular disc is sitting beneath the articular eminence and difficult to tell the alignment to the actual head of the condyle, but the head may be tipped over medially.

Left TMJ coronal view shows that the condyle is fractured at the subcondylar neck and the condyle is sitting at a 90-degree angulation to the vertical aspect of the ramus. The condylar head is pointing to the center of the skull. The articular disc is sitting over the condylar neck and not on top of the condyle.

Right TMJ condyle appears relatively normal.
IMPRESSION: Left TMJ subcondylar fracture with the condyle displaced medially at 90 degree angle to the vertical ramus and with the articular disc sitting over top of the neck of the condyle.
Normal right TMJ.

original_note:
BETTY WRIGHT									10-07-O4

Betty is 70 years old and referred by Dr. Duane Keller, 3929 Bayless Avenue, St. Louis, MO  63125-1437.

Patient History

Previous surgeries Betty has had include tonsillectomy, gallbladder, kidney, hysterectomy, motor cortex stimulator implant, and cataract surgery. She has high blood pressure and also she has had surgery to remove the motor cortex stimulator implant. Previous history of stomach ulcers but not now. She has had an adverse reaction to CODEINE and to DEMEROL.

She gets occasional earaches on the left side but no tinnitus, vertigo, or hearing loss. The only other joints  that bother her are her knees. She is aware of clenching and bruxism at night. She tends to do that during the day but has been taught some methods to try to minimize that. 

Her problems began relative to clicking and popping in her joint probably at least 10 years ago that she can recall. It has only been in the past five years that her problems got significantly worse. The etiology is unknown. Betty developed rather severe pain. She has had the following treatments since then:
Numerous medications
Stellate ganglion block
Radiofrequency ablation of the left trigeminal nerve
Motor cortex stimulator
Extraction of teeth
Nicos therapy
Splints
Partial dentures

None of these has been effective in managing her severe pain problems. In the last three months, Betty has begun to have headaches. They occur now about three to four times per week in the left temporal area at about a level of 4. She frequently wakes up with these headaches, and they tend to get worse during the day. She rates her TMJ pain at 10, overall pain at 10, jaw function at 7, diet at 7, and disability at 7. She has numbness on the left side of her face involving her left lower lip, chin, and cheek area.

Current Medications

Hydrocodone 7.5/500 mg, 1 tablet 3 times a day
Effexor 75 mg 1 per day
Zantac 150 mg 1 tablet 2 times a day
Topamax 25 mg 1 per day
Amitriptyline 10 mg tablets, 1 per day
Tiazac 300 mg 1 per day
Methadose 5 mg 4 times a day
Oxazepam 15 mg 1 twice a day
Avalide 300/12.5, 1 per day
Centrum Silver 1 per day

Patient Concerns

Severe pain, left side of face, that has been present for four or five years
Difficulties eating
Difficulties talking
A pulling sensation recently developed in the left eye

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Betty has fairly good facial symmetry. Chin may be deviated a little bit off toward the left side probably 2 to 3 mm. Maxillary and mandibular dental midlines are pretty much on the facial midline. Left side of the mandible is shorter vertically by probably 3 to 4 mm. 

In profile, she has good facial balance. 

Intraorally, she is missing a third molar on the mandibular right side, and on the left side is missing a second bicuspid and all three molars. She has a crown on the first bicuspid. In the upper arch, she has a partial denture in. She is missing the right first, second, and third molars. She is also missing the second bicuspid and has a cantilever crown replacing that tooth. On the left side, she is missing the second and third molars. She has basically a Class I cuspid relationship. She has just a very slight cant in the occlusal plane with the left side being about 1 to 2 mm higher than the right side. The oropharynx appears clear. Uvula and soft palate look pretty much normal as does the posterior pharyngeal wall area and the faucial pillars. She reports no difficulties breathing through her nose. She is not aware of any problems with snoring. Maximum incisal opening is 21 mm, and she can open about 15 without developing severe pain. Excursive movement to the right surprisingly is about 10 mm and to the left 9 mm. However, there is severe crepitus on that left joint if she does that, and it is very painful for her to do those movements. 

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex evaluation shows the sinuses appear relatively clear. Left side appears a little bit more fuzzy than the right side. Missing mandibular left second bicuspid and all three molars and missing the right third molar. In the maxillary arch, missing the left second and third molar and the right second bicuspid and all three molars. Appears that she may have a fairly good size lesion beneath the crown on the first bicuspid on that side. Right condyle appears somewhat arthritic. Left condyle appears severely arthritic with significant loss of anteroposterior dimension of the condyle. There is a significant amount of alveolar ridge atrophy in the mandibular arch on the left side. Appears to have loss of vertical height of the condyle on the left side as well.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is quite small in anteroposterior dimension. Condyle is posteriorly positioned in the fossa. Moderate decreased slope to the articular eminence. Condyle is postured posterior in the fossa. Could be a small osteophyte coming off the head of the condyle perhaps. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears to be fairly well-centered in the fossa. A little bit of a hockey stick shape to the condyle. A little bit of anterior beaking present. Joint space looks reasonable. Moderate slope to the articular eminence. 

On opening, there is moderately good translation. The condyle is coming down toward the inferior aspect of the eminence. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows a skeletal Class I occlusal relationship. Inferior border of the mandible is at different levels with probably the left side being shorter than the right side. Appears to be missing some of the molars in the upper arch. 
Waters view shows that the nasal airway appears good, and the sinuses appear clear. She has a plate in the right cranium. Betty did have some significant problems with vertigo when she first developed the more severe aspect of her current situation. That has subsided. On two different occasions, Betty has had local anesthetic injected into her left TMJ area. In both instances, the pain went away. One time it was injected by Dr. Ken Rotskoff and the other time by Dr. Duane Keller. 

Basic Diagnoses

Left TMJ severe arthritis and articular disc dislocation with severe deformation of the disc and perforations
Left TMJ severe pain
Left side myofascial pain
Left side headaches
Masticatory dysfunction
Missing mandibular left second bicuspid and all three molars and missing the right third molar. In the maxillary arch, missing the left second and third molar and the right second bicuspid and all three molars
Partial dentures have been constructed, but she is unable to wear the mandibular partial

Recommended Treatment

Dental treatment as necessary to assure preservation of the remaining teeth

CT scan (TMJ Concepts protocol)

Surgery
Left TMJ reconstruction with TMJ Concepts total joint prosthesis
Left TMJ fat graft (harvested from abdomen)
Application of arch bars

Any dental restorations and restorative work that may be indicated that could include the placement of osseointegrated implants to replace missing teeth

original_note:
JILL ROUSH										03-17-04

Jill is 47 years old and referred by Dr. Bill Wyatt, Hurst, TX. Jill has also seen Dr. Charles Holt, Bedford, TX, and also Dr. Monte Collins, Southlake, TX.

Patient History

She has had problems with severe head pain. Her previous surgeries include tonsillectomy, appendectomy, C-section, and hysterectomy. She has some allergy problems and has had problems with hay fever. When she gets her severe headaches, she can have fainting spells. Currently she takes Fiorinal with codeine, but only when the pain is really bad, and she takes Phenergan at the same time.

Jill is aware of developing jaw joint problems back at about the age of 12. Her dad told her that all of a sudden her lower jaw began to disappear. I suspect this is probably related to idiopathic condylar resorption. She proceeded on with life. Sometime around 1992 or 1995, she started with splint therapy in Columbus, Ohio. She worked with Dr. Shankland there. For the past six to nine months, she has been working with Dr. Charles Holt in reference to splint therapy. She has had at least five different splints. The ones by Dr. Holt are the first that actually have fit her fairly well. To complicate matters, in 1997 she had a fall where she fracture-dislocation her sacrum. The displaced disc was between C6 and C7, but she has had some kind of shift between the vertebrae at C1 and C2. 

Jill has no problems with earaches but does get occasional tinnitus and vertigo but has no hearing loss. Other joints that bother her include her neck, back, shoulders, hips, and left knee. She has a problem with her back and has to carry a special pillow with her to sit on to help decrease the discomfort. 

She has multiple drug allergies and will send us a list of those as she is not aware of all of the ones that she is allergic to. She also has allergies to wheat, dairy, beef, corn, and some other food allergies. She has major difficulties wearing jewelry. Since her fall, she says she feels better without wearing jewelry. She says she has had all of her silver fillings removed from her teeth and replaced with nonmetallic materials. She is aware of clenching her teeth at night, and she says she thinks that this may help her breathe better.

Jill has fairly constant headaches that are usually around a level of 4, and they start in the back of the head and go into the temple area and then around the front and even up on top. These are fairly consistent. About every two weeks, her headaches will escalate to a 10, and she can have some significant problems with nausea and vomiting at that level. She rates her TMJ pain at 2, on an average day her overall pain levels are at 5, jaw function at 6, diet at 5, and disability at 8. She is having such significant airway problems that her physician has recommended she have a tracheostomy done to take care of her airway until resolution of her jaw problems can be achieved. Jill has attempted to use a CPAP machine, but she was unable to tolerate it. 

Heather has observed her mother sleeping, and she has severe apneic episodes and significant snoring problems. She wakes up very weak in the mornings and is usually drenched in perspiration as well. She has pain in her chest almost as though her lungs hurt. She has daytime somnolence as well.

Patient Concerns

Severe airway problems
Difficulties breathing particularly at night when sleeping
Low oxygen saturation
Severe sleep apnea problems 
Headaches and head pain
Nausea and vomiting with severe head pain
Difficulties eating and chewing
Pain in the right jaw joint

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Jill has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is probably about 23 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 2 mm. Maxillary dental midline is pretty much on the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline is shifted about 2 to 3 mm off to the left, though the chin midline is pretty much on the facial midline.

In profile view, Jill has a very prominent head postured forward neck position. Upper lip is probably a good 6 mm AP deficient. Chin is at least 25 mm AP deficient. Chin-neckline is extremely short, and chin-neck angle is actually fairly well-defined but probably has only about 22 mm of AP chin-neck length. Where Jill postures her head to breathe the best, she holds her head quite forward on her neck and shoulder complex and tends to tip her head back.

Intraorally, Jill is missing the mandibular left and right first molars. Has some moderate crowding, lower anterior arch. In the upper arch, she is missing the bicuspid on both sides. She is missing a cuspid on the left side and first molar with the left bicuspid drifted way forward from its original position. This probably accounts for the reason that the lower dental midline is shifted 3 mm off toward the left side. She is essentially a Class II cuspid occlusion, and Jill has an overjet of about 4 mm. In the upper arch, she is missing first bicuspids, left second molar, and she is missing all four third molars. Intraorally, the oropharynx appears clear, although the soft palate is positioned almost directly back onto the posterior pharyngeal wall area. At first I thought she may have had a pharyngeal flap procedure but she denies this, and radiographically she does not have this either. The soft palate is so posteriorly positioned that it just appears to touch against the posterior pharyngeal wall on oral examination.

Intranasally, turbinates are relatively small in size. Septum is deviated a little bit off toward the right side up at the mid portion, but it is shifted significantly off to the left side at the base of the nose, although there is still an adequate airway. Luminal valves are a little bit constricted but don’t seem to cause any problem for her.

To palpation, she has moderate pain over the right shoulder and milder pain over the TMJs, masseters, styloid areas, and minimal tenderness over the temporalis and medial pterygoid areas. On opening, maximal incisal opening is 33 mm but only about 23 mm without significant pain or discomfort. Excursive movement to the right is 5 mm and to the left 3 mm. Joints seem to function relatively smoothly. She does say that she does sometimes get some popping in her joints. 

Radiographic Evaluation

Old Radiographs
We have no new radiographs on Jill, and the latest ones are from 06/30/03, approaching a year old. However, Panorex evaluation on these radiographs show that she is missing the mandibular left cuspid, first molar, third molar, right first molar and third molar, and in the maxillary arch missing maxillary first bicuspid, left second molar, and bilateral third molars. Turbinates appear moderate in size. Sinuses appear reasonably clear although a little bit cloudy on the left side compared to the right side toward the anterior part of the sinus. Condyles have undergone significant condylar resorption and are quite small. 

Transorbital views of the left condyle show extremely small condyles bilaterally. 

Left closed tomograms show significant evidence of previous condylar resorption, and condyles are quite small vertically as well as anteroposteriorly. Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is even smaller on this side as compared to the left side in anteroposterior dimension as well as vertical dimension. There is increased joint space in both joints, but this is probably secondary to the chronic wearing of splints. Condyles are both postured forward in the fossa. 

We don’t have an adequate lateral cephalogram as soft tissues are not visible. It does, however, show that she has a severely decreased oropharyngeal airway of probably only about 2 mm at the base of the tongue. Extremely high occlusal plane angle and high mandibular plane angle orientation. Severe Class II skeletal and moderate Class II occlusal relationship.

Tooth-size discrepancy demonstrates a Bolton’s difference of 3.4 mm.

New Radiographs
The Panorex shows essentially the same findings as before. There is severe resorption of the condyles. The sinuses looks clear, and turbinates appear about moderate in size.

Right TMJ tomograms show that the condyles are severely resorbed and quite small anteroposteriorly as well as vertically. Sigmoid notch is fairly close to the articular eminence. Articular eminence is moderately steep on these views with a little bit of tendency toward sallowness. Significantly decreased vertical and posterior joint space is present. Could be some anterior beaking present on the condylar head.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that likewise is very small anteroposteriorly and with significant evidence of loss of vertical component. Articular eminence tends a little bit toward a shallow side except is fairly long in length. There is a close proximity between the sigmoid notch and the articular eminence. Condyle appears to have a pseudocyst within the head of the condyle. Joint space is extremely narrow vertically and posteriorly.

On opening, there is some restriction of translation of both joints with the condyles coming down toward but not beneath the articular eminence. Severe degenerative changes are seen in the condyles on this view. 

Waters view shows the sinuses are fairly clear. She has huge frontal sinuses. The turbinates are moderate in size, but there does appear to be adequate airway. There is some deviation of the nasal septum present. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows severe retrusion of the mandible and moderate retrusion of the maxilla. She has a full Class II malocclusion and has a high occlusal plane angle. Oropharyngeal airway is decreased but not as severe as on the previous x-rays, and this could be due just to tongue posture and Jill trying to keep her mandible in a retruded position. Jill has a fairly prominent gonial notch bilaterally. 

Basic Diagnoses

Bilateral TMJ severe condylar resorption and articular disc dislocations with nonsalvageable discs. Condylar resorption probably due to idiopathic condylar resorption with an onset at about the age of 12
Severe AP mandibular deficiency
Anterior AP maxillary deficiency
Severe posterior vertical maxillary deficiency
Class II occlusion
Extremely high occlusal plane angle of 34 degrees (normal 8 degrees)
Missing mandibular left cuspid, first molar, mandibular right first molar, maxillary left second molar, bilateral first bicuspids, and all four third molars
Mandibular asymmetry with the mandibular dental midline being shifted 3 mm to the left of the midline of the chin and facial midline
Significant crowding, lower anterior arch
Residual spacing in the lower arch between the second bicuspids and second molars
AP microgenia
Tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 3.4 mm
TMJ pain
Headaches 
Myofascial pain
Severe decrease of oropharyngeal airway of approximately 2 mm (normal 11 mm)
Severe snoring
Observed severe apneic episodes 
Daytime somnolence
Chest pains
Significant diaphoresis at night
Rather severe head postured forward neck position most likely to aid in breathing
Masticatory dysfunction
Multiple allergies to medications, food allergies, and difficulties wearing jewelry
Occasional tinnitus and vertigo

Recommended Treatment

Presurgical orthodontics 
Align and level maxillary arch. Open up 1 mm of space around each maxillary lateral incisor. Open up 1 mm of space around each maxillary lateral incisor. Be sure to keep maxillary lateral incisor roots tipped mesially and cuspid roots tipped distally to provide space for interdental osteotomies if they become necessary at the time of surgery
Align and level mandibular arch nonextraction. Shift mandibular dental midline to the right 3 mm to get it on line with the maxillary dental midline and chin. This can be accomplished by retracting the bicuspids and cuspids on the right side. Anchorage will be lost in the second molar mostly likely, and the majority of the space on that side may end up being closed. Try to intrude the incisors or extrude the cuspids and bicuspids slightly to level the lower arch. Upright the roots on the left first and second bicuspid with the first bicuspid functioning as the missing cuspid. Strip lower anterior teeth removing about 1.5 mm of tooth structure to help compensate for the tooth-size discrepancy. Be careful not to retract lower incisors as they are currently in a good angulation to meet the presurgical orthodontic goals and will be in a more ideal angulation once the occlusal plane angle is changed

Testing at the Environmental Center at Dallas for hypersensitivities to the materials that would be used in the total joint prostheses

Place samples of chromium cobalt alloy and titanium allow subcutaneously to see if there is a reaction to the alloys as will be used in the prostheses

Sleep study

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ condylectomies, reconstruction, and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla and downgraft it posteriorly
Bilateral coronoidectomies
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvested from abdomen) 
HTR implant to anterior mandible

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion 

Dental reconstruction which may include two implants on the mandibular left side and perhaps one in the maxillary left side

Other Systems

Jill has problems with the bones in her neck, back, shoulders, hips, and left knee. As a teenager, she had a lot of problems with urinary tract infections and occasionally as an adult. She does have some polyuria. She was born with a double uterus, double vagina, and double cervix. Probably had fertility problems but had no miscarriages. She has one child, Heather, who is 21 years old. She had a total hysterectomy in 1984 and this is because of endometriosis. She also has had ovarian cysts. She was diagnosed with pelvic inflammatory disease in the 20’s. She has had no abnormal Pap smears. She had some problems with vaginal yeast infections particularly in her 20’s. She has some seasonal allergy problems. She gets occasional tinnitus and vertigo. She has been told she has asthma but she feels that maybe it is more of a problem with her airway than a true asthma problem. She gets hoarseness frequently particularly if she talks long. She gets burning in her lungs and sometimes a squeezing kind of pain in her chest that she relates to her breathing problems. She has had bronchitis at least 15 times, and she has had pneumonia at least 12 times or more. She has problems with nausea and vomiting particularly when she develops her severe pain problems. She does get abdominal pain and cramping and states that she has a partial bowel obstruction with adhesions. Not aware of any STDs. She gets tired a lot. She feels like she is running a fever, but she says when she takes her temperature it is usually okay. She has some though process issues and perhaps some memory factors. She apparently has been diagnosed through blood tests with having Lyme’s disease. Jill apparently has been diagnosed with having Chlamydia through electrodermal screening. Through the electrodermal screening they also diagnosed Jill as having Mycoplasma bacteria present.

From ages 6 to 9, she had some parakeets, at least three to four of them. Her grandfather had a dairy farm with chickens and probably had about 30 chickens, and she and her family would visit there once a week. She has had lots of cats all of her life, and some of those ate birds. She has had dogs all of her life, and some of those ate birds as well. She has been around daily cattle all of her life and horses x 2 for the past 15 years. No implants.

original_note:
B312 
B312 is 19 years old.
Patient History
She has had previous surgeries around the head area including an osseous genioplasty and some work on her turbinates to try to improve her nasal airway, which has not been very successful. She also has a lot of stomach pains and has seen Dr. __ earlier today in reference to those problems. Dr. __ has planned to do some additional testing while B312 is here in Dallas to try to figure out the stomach problems for her. She had a previous problem with a seizure and took medications for about four years but is now not taking any medicines and is having no problems. She is taking two medications, Clariflu and Zinnat, which is an antibiotic.

B312 has no problems with headaches, TMJ pain, earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, nor is she aware of any noises in her jaw joints.
Patient Concerns
Difficulties breathing through her nose
Esophageal gurgling (she is seeing Dr. __ in reference to this)
Teeth do not fit together in the front

Clinical Examination
In frontal view, B312 has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 20 mm. Upper tooth to lip relationship is 5 mm. Maxillary dental midline is pretty much on the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline is just about a millimeter to the left. Nasal tip is about a millimeter or two to the right. Chin is pretty much on the facial midline.

In profile, B312 may have just some slight AP maxillary deficiency but not too significant. Lower third face appears a little bit long. Chin is just slightly retruded. Chin-neckline may be a little bit short, but chin-neck angle is well defined.

Intraorally, she has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars present. Still has the mamelons on her lower anterior teeth. She has a 3 x 3 retainer in the lower anterior arch. Has adequate attached gingiva in the lower anterior arch. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars present. She has a good Class I cuspid-molar relationship on the right side and on the left side just a slight tendency toward Class II but not too significant. Has almost an end-on incisor relationship and 0 overbite relationship. In the oropharynx, everything looks pretty clear except she has a small soft tissue lesion at the posterior aspect of the soft palate adjacent to the uvula that measures probably about 4 mm x 4 mm. It is nonirritated and nonfirm.

Intranasally, the turbinates are moderately enlarged and seem to constrict the nasal airway. The base of the columella appears somewhat wide. When using the nasal scope inside the nose and opening her nares on each side, it made her breathing much easier.

To palpation, she had no really significant myofascial pain although a little bit of tenderness in the styloid area at about a level of 3. TMJs were nontender to palpation and seemed to function reasonably smoothly.

Radiographic Evaluation
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars but all of the rest of the teeth are present. She has three screws in the chin area from the previous chin surgery. A 3 x 3 retainer is seen on the lower anterior teeth. Sinuses appear relatively clear, although she may have a mucocele in the left maxillary sinus. Turbinates appear enlarged. Septum looks fairly straight. Condyles as best as can be seen look relatively normal. Seems to have an excessive distance from the apices of the teeth to the base of the nose. Sinuses come down and extend down below the levels of the root apices.

Waters view shows the left sinus appears to be a little bit more cloudy than the right sinus. Other sinuses appear relatively clear. Septum may be a little bit deviated. Turbinates appear enlarged particularly on the right side. Jaws look relatively symmetric.

Left TMJ tomogram shows condyles that appear fairly normal although there appears to be a little bit of a pseudocyst on the condylar head. Appears to be decreased joint space vertically. Posterior joint space looks pretty good.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that also appears to have sort of a pseudocyst on the condylar head. Decreased joint space vertically and posteriorly with appearance of some calcification behind the condylar head. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Joint space vertically looks a little bit better on one view but in another view it looks quite narrow.

Translation is very good in both joints. In the open view, there appears to be perhaps some flattening on the head of the condyle on the right side.
Cephalometrically, she has a high occlusal plane angle and evidence of a previous chin procedure. She has a huge adenoidal mass in her posterior nasopharynx and only has about a 2-mm airway space between the adenoid tissue and the posterior aspect of the turbinates. Oropharyngeal airway at the base of the soft palate is about 4 mm and at the base of the tongue is about 4.5 mm. There is about a 3-mm tooth-size discrepancy.

Basic Diagnoses
Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocations (to be verified by MRI)
Vertical maxillary hyperplasia
AP mandibular hypoplasia
Slight II occlusion on the left side, Class I on the right side
End-on incisor relationship
Hypertrophied turbinates
Hypertrophied nasopharyngeal adenoid tissue
Wide columella base
Nasal airway obstruction
Decreased oropharyngeal airway
Soft tissue lesion in the right soft palate/uvula area (5 mm x 5 mm)
High occlusal plane angulation
Significant tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 3 mm
Overangulation of the lower incisors

Recommended Treatment
MRI of left and right TMJs
Presurgical orthodonics
Align and level maxillary arch but place a step in the upper arch wire to align the four incisors at an elevated plane level compared to the cuspids and the posterior teeth. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies
Align and level mandibular arch. Strip lower anterior teeth removing at least 2 mm or possibly 3 mm of tooth structure and close the spacing

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors (only if articular discs are shown to be displaced on the MRI)
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the maxilla superiorly, level the occlusal plane, and re-establish a normal anterior overbite relationship
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction to aid in opening the oropharyngeal airway
Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies
Nasopharyngeal adenoidectomy
Remove lesion from soft palate/uvula area

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion
Conversation with Dr. __: He feels that she has reflux, abdominal pain, and perhaps even gallstones. He plans to do a cine esophagogram, upper GI series, and a sonogram to look for gallstones. He wanted to alert me of the lesion above the vocal cords which is actually the lesion on her soft palate/uvula area. He said that when we do her surgery, if she goes ahead with it, he may want to do a scope on her at the same time when she is asleep. We could certainly arrange this.

B312 MRI Evaluation 
Date of Acquisition:	
Date of Reading:	
Left TMJ, coronal view, shows a condyle with fairly good mediolateral width. Lateral rim of the fossa appears to curve inferiorly probably about 3 to 4 mm. Joint space appears significantly decreased particularly out laterally. May be some evidence of discal tissue medially positioned. May be some slight rough areas on the coronal head but difficult to tell.

Right TMJ, coronal view, shows a condyle with fairly good mediolateral width. Lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 to 4 mm. Decreased joint space superiorly and laterally. Difficult to determine where the discal tissue is but may be medial.

Left TMJ, sagittal view, shows a condyle with some flattening on the anterior head. Some superior peaking present with a little bit of sclerosis. Posterior joint space is decreased. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Articular disc is anteriorly displaced, but it appears to have reasonably good architecture yet. No significant inflammation seen around the joint.

On opening, there is good translation of the disc, and the disc does appear to reduce.
Right TMJ, sagittal view, shows a condyle that demonstrates a little bit of flattening on the anterior head and some significant thinness of the cortical bone in a couple of views. Slightly decreased posterior joint space. Superior joint space appears somewhat decreased. Out laterally, the disc appears to be in a more vertical orientation. I believe the disc is anteriorly displaced particularly at the lateral aspect.

On opening, there is fairly good translation. The disc appears to be in position.
Impression:
Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with reduction
Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with reduction
Both discs appear to be salvageable
