B315  MRI 
AGE:	33/F
DATE OF MRI ACQUISITION:	
DATE OF INTERPRETATION:	
The quality of the MRIs is not very good.

Left TMJ MRI shows a condyle that appears to be a little short in vertical height perhaps. The joint space looks reasonably good, but difficult to identify discal tissue. The lateral rim of the fossa is quite flat with virtually no downward curvature.

Right TMJ condyle also that it may be a little bit short in vertical height. There is a little bit less joint space on this side. It is difficult to identify where the discs are. There is a very flat lateral rim of the fossa.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has a little bit of sclerosis and a little bit of anterior beaking present. The articular eminence is moderately steep. The articular disc appears to be anteriorly displaced and in a vertical orientation in the joint space. There is fluid in the superior and inferior joint space. The left condyle has limited translation. The disc does not appear to move significantly. It may be adhesed to the fossa as well. The condyle has limited translation and it stops along the posterior aspect of the articular eminence.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has a little sclerosis on the top and a little bit of anterior beaking. The articular eminence is moderately steep. The articular disc is anteriorly displaced and in a vertical orientation within the joint space. There is decreased posterior joint space. There is a little bit of fluid in the joint space, but not quite as much as the opposite side. On opening, there is restricted translation in the joint. The disc does not appear to move and movement appears to be between the condyle and the disc. The disc may be adhesed to the fossa. The condyle has restricted translation.

IMPRESSION:
Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly displaced with early reduction but with adhesions of the discs to the fossa.
B315  I 

B315 is 33 years old. She is in good health. B315's TMJ problems began 2 months ago, when she was involved in a motorbike accident where she flipped over the handlebars and struck her chin on the cement. She did go to the emergency room and there were no fractures identified. She was referred to Dr. __ and he likewise took additional x-rays and no fractures were identified. She continued to have significant pain problems and limited jaw opening. A month ago, she was put under anesthesia and her jaws were manipulated with apparently good mobility with Dr. __ manipulating the mandible while she was asleep. She was then sent to physical therapy with __ who is a TMJ physical therapy specialist. The physical therapy made her pain worse with headaches, TMJ pain and severe neck and shoulder pain. He referred her to Dr. __, who is an orthopedic spine specialist. An angiogram was done and that was negative. B315 has significant problems with vertigo and nausea. Although she has bilateral pain, it is worse on the right side. Few weeks ago, she underwent right arthroscopy and manipulation of the jaws as well as steroid injections into the right joint. She has had no improvement and, in fact, she feels worse now with increased pain and limited jaw function, than before the procedure. The severe pain and jaw dysfunction is severely affecting her abilities function at work and in all other aspects of her life.

B315 currently takes the following medications:
Celebrex 200 mg tablets one per day. Hydrocodone 5/325 which she takes about one tablet at night currently. She previously was on a liquid form where she was taking 2 to 4 tsp. every 4 hours and this did not control her pain. Tramadol 50 mg six tablets per day. Cyclobenzaprine 5 mg one to two times per day. Klonopin 0.5 mg one tablet b.i.d. Zolpidem 5 mg at bedtime. B315 rates her headaches that are continuous and are in the frontal, temporal, and posterior head area at a level of 6 on the right side and at a level of 4 on the left side. She rates her TMJ pain at 5, average daily pain at 6, jaw function at 6, diet at 8, disability at 5. She gets earaches, tinnitus, and vertigo. She also experiences significant problems with nausea. The only other joint that bothers her is her neck vertebrae. She is not aware of clenching or bruxism.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
In frontal view, B315 has good facial balance. She also has good balance in profile. B315 has all of her teeth except for her wisdom teeth which were removed previously. She has basically just a slight Class II occlusal relationship of about 1 to 2 mm and has about a 3 mm anterior overbite relationship. B315 has high moderate to severe pain around the head and neck area with it being a little bit worse on the right side. Maximum incisal opening is 22 mm and opening without pain 5 mm. Excursion movement to the right is 0 and to the left about 1.5 mm.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. She has a root canal on the maxillary right first molar. It is difficult to evaluate the joints. Sinuses appear reasonably clear.

Coronal view shows that she has moderately enlarged turbinates, but sinuses appear clear.
Axial view shows that the turbinates are large particularly on the right side, but the sinuses are clear.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is posteriorly positioned in the fossa with some anterior beaking present and an increased anterior joint space. The articular eminence is fairly steep.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is a little bit more centered in the joint. The articular eminence is fairly steep. There is a little bit of anterior beaking on the condyle in one of the views.

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has good mediolateral width. The lateral rim of the fossa is fairly flat. The joint space may be slightly decreased particularly at the lateral aspect of the joint.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width. There is fairly reasonable superior joint space.
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that B315 basically has a Class I cuspid-molar relationship, and she has a good oropharyngeal airway.

Because of her bike injury and unsuccessful previous treatments that included physical therapy (which made her condition worse) and arthroscopic surgery (which also has made her situation worse), she is now suffering from severe pain around the head and neck area all related to the traumatic injury. She is becoming progressively more debilitated. She is an event organizer and planner. She is having significant difficulties carrying out her responsibilities. She is becoming ineffective at her workplace because of her severe pain problems. She cannot even open her mouth wide enough to even think about getting impressions for splint therapy. Her incisal opening is only 22 mm maximum. She likely has close locks in both TMJs.

Based on clinical, radiographic, and MRI analyses, B315's diagnoses are as follows:
Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation without reduction. Relative mandibular hyperplasia.
Severe myofascial pain.
TMJ pain. Continuous headaches. Earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, and nausea.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1.	Surgery. a.	Bilateral TMJ articular disc respositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors. b.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to maintain the occlusion. c.	Application of maxillary and mandibular arch bars.
2.	Postsurgical physical therapy.

I gave her a prescription for Lortab liquid 2.5 mg/5 mL, dispensing 500 mL, 4 to 6 tsp. q.4 hours p.r.n. pain, refill x2.
B315 

The patient presents for final presurgical workup. Final records were taken. History and physical performed and consent form reviewed and signed. I answered all the questions that she and her boyfriend Jim Trungale had. She is ready for surgery tomorrow.

original_note:
TYLER REYNOLDS									03/14/2009

Tyler is 18 years old, birth date 08/30/1990.  Primary concerns Tyler has include the following:

Jaw joint pain.
Frequent headaches. 
Teeth only hit in 3 places.
Jaw opening is limited.
On opening, the jaw shifts towards the right side.

Tyler had orthodontics from the age of 9 to 12.  He began orthodontics again in August 2008.  His TMJ problems began at about the age of 16.  It is unknown exactly what caused the joint situation.  Tyler had his wisdom teeth removed in 2006 and has also had two root canals that may be related to clenching of his teeth.  In 2008, he had significant problems with laryngeospasms due to acid reflux.  He currently takes Prevacid 60 mg b.i.d. to control that and the only other medication he takes is Allegra.  Tyler did have splint therapy from October 2007 to August 2008.  

Tyler rates his headaches that are quite frequent at about a level of 6, and they occur in the frontal, temporal, and posterior head area.  They occasionally elevate to 9.  He rates his TMJ pain at 8, with the right side worse than the left side.  His average daily facial pain is at 3.  He rates his jaw function at 8, diet at 6, disability at 2.  He gets right-sided earaches occasionally and gets occasional tinnitus and occasional vertigo.  Other joints that bother him include his fingers and occasionally his toes.  He is aware of clenching at nighttime.  He has had a previous ENT evaluation by Dr. Troost who suggested nasal septoplasty and bilateral turbinectomies.  Tyler is aware of having some moderate snoring at times.  He did have a sleep study done in April 2008 and nothing significant was found.  He also has been diagnosed as having asthma.  Tyler was born 5 weeks premature. 

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Tyler has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is 25 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about -1.  The maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline.  The nasal tip is just slightly to the left side by 1 or 2 mm.  The mandibular dental midline is also to the left about 1 mm and the mandibular dental midline and chin are fairly well aligned with each other.  In profile, upper lip supralabrale is just slightly posterior to subnasale.  In profile, upper lip is retruded about 3 to 4 mm and chin is retruded about 10 mm.  Chin neck is short and chin neck angle is moderately well defined.  Intraorally, Tyler has all of his teeth back through the second molars.  Teeth are reasonably well aligned and level.  There is a little bit of spacing between the lower anterior teeth.  There is a little gingival inflammation around the maxillary lower anterior teeth.  In the upper arch, he has all of his teeth back through the second molars.  He has a slight Class III cuspid relationship on the right side by about 2 mm and about 1 or 2 mm also on the left side.  He has an anterior cross bite and a slight anterior open bite of about 2 mm.  Uvula is moderately hyperplastic.  Otherwise, the rest of the tissues in the oropharyngeal airway look reasonably good.  Posterior fascial pillars are a little bit posteriorly positioned.  Intranasally, the septum is deviated a little bit off towards the left side.  Turbinates appear moderately enlarged, particularly in the right side.  Tyler has moderate nasal airway obstruction.  Columella is wide and nostrils are moderately narrowed.  To palpation, he has moderate pain  over the right TMJ, left masseter area, and milder tenderness over the right masseter, medial pterygoids, left TMJ, and retromandibular area.  On opening, he could feel an occasional glitch in the right TMJ.  Otherwise, the joints are reasonably smooth.  On excursion movements, there was a click in the left joint on excursion towards the right side.  Maximal incisal opening is 36 mm, opening without discomfort 19 mm.  Excursion movement to the right was 7 mm and to the left 5 mm.  On opening, the mandible does deviate towards the right side probably about 3 to 4 mm.  Tyler has a tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of 2 mm.  There is a reverse curve of occlusion in the lower arch and a slightly accentuated curve of occlusion in the upper arch.  

Basic diagnoses for Tyler would be as follows:    

Bilateral TMJ reactive arthritis versus connective tissue autoimmune disease. 
AP mandibular hypoplasia. 
AP maxillary hypoplasia and posterior vertical hypoplasia.
Slight Class III end-on occlusion. 
Anterior cross bite.
Moderately high occlusal plane angle. 
Hypertrophied turbinates. 
Deviated nasal septum off to the right side. 
Nasal airway obstruction. 
TMJ pain. 
Headaches. 
Myofascial pain. 
Reversed curve of Spee in the lower arch. 
Accentuated curve of Spee in the upper arch. 

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Evaluation by rheumatologist to rule out connective tissue autoimmune disease. 
Presurgical orthodontics.  
Continue to align and level the maxillary arch.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. 
Continue to align and level mandibular arch.  Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing about 2 mm of tooth structure and retract.  Try to elevate the lower incisors slightly to decrease the slight reverse curve of Spee.   
CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol).
Surgery. 
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla in a counterclockwise direction. 
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen or buttock).
Bilateral coronoidotomies. 
Bilateral inferior partial turbinectomies. 
Nasal septoplasty.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B520
B520 is 17 years old. B520 was referred by Dr. XXXX. B520's TMJ issues began when she was about 13 years old. That is when she first started to notice that her chin was becoming somewhat retruded and she developed pain. Etiology is unknown. B520 had orthodontic treatment the first time at age 8 and then from about the age of 12 to 13. She wore elastics and apparently her bite was reasonably well corrected. She had splint therapy for the past 2 years to present time. Splints have been made by Dr. XXXX. B520 has had lots of adjustments on the splints, and she can tell that it is ineffective at the present time. She also has had physical therapy last year that included dry needling and exercises. She has also been treated with medications and is currently on Daypro 600 mg two times per day and birth control pills. She has been prescribed Enbrel but has not started taking that medication yet.

B520 rates her TMJ pain at a level of 8 and her daily pain levels reach a level of 8 to 9. She rates her jaw function at 4, diet at 5, disability at 6. She has no problems with headaches. She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus nor vertigo. She has no hearing loss. She has problems with her right hip. She has stiff hands and fingers in the mornings. B520 has had reactions to penicillin, cephalosporins, naproxen, and more recently she has had a reaction to adhesives and tape. She is not aware of any kind of reaction to wearing jewelry. She is aware of clenching her teeth at nighttime. B520 currently wears her splint only at night. She did wear it during the day, but it did not seem to help her situation so she discontinued daytime wear. She does not snore when she sleeps. She has some mild daytime somnolence, but no sleep apnea. She has a moderate sleep disorder where she tosses and turns a lot at night. She does breathe through her mouth at night, but not so much during the day. She sleeps on her sides and stomach.

In frontal view, B520 has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is 20 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is 3.5 mm. The maxillary midline looks as though it is on the facial midline. The mandibular dental midline is 1 mm to the right and chin is about 3 mm to the right. In profile, B520 has a head postured forward neck position. In profile view, upper lip supralabrale is about 3 mm posterior to subnasale. The maxilla is retruded about 6 to 7 mm. The chin is retruded about 22 mm. The chin neckline is short, although the chin neckline is reasonably well defined. She has a lip incompetence of about 5 to 6 mm. Intraorally, B520 has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. She has some mild crowding of the lower anterior arch. She has some lack of attached gingiva around the lower incisors. She already has a little bit of gingival stripping present. In the upper arch, she is missing first bicuspids that were extracted for orthodontic reasons. She has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship being a little bit greater on the right side than the left side. She has a zero over bite relationship. She has an over jet of about 2 mm. Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively normal and healthy. There is a significant cant in the transverse occlusal plane with the right maxillary cuspid being at least 3 mm higher than the left side. The mandible is elevated on the right side about 1.5 to 2 mm at the most. The vertical discrepancy is greater in the maxilla with the right cuspid being at least 3 mm higher than left side. She does have some constricture of the luminal valves. The maximal incisal opening is 44 mm, opening without pain 18 mm. The excursion movements are 9 mm in each direction. To palpation, she has moderate pain over the masseters, retromandibular area, and TMJ area with the right TMJ being worse than the left.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. It appears as though she may be missing her maxillary second bicuspids or could be the first bicuspids. The sinuses appear relatively clear. The septum appears reasonably straight and turbinates appear moderately enlarged. She has severely resorbed condyles.

Coronal view shows that she has enlarged turbinates. The septum appears reasonably straight. Sinuses appear clear.
Axial view shows that the septum is reasonably straight. Turbinates are moderately enlarged and appear to block the airway. Sinuses appear relatively clear.

Left TMJ sagittal tomograms shows a severely resorbed condyle with loss of vertical as well as anteroposterior bone structure. The articular eminence appears to have some uneven areas, although it has a moderate inclination. The joint space actually appears somewhat increased vertically.

Right TMJ sagittal tomograms shows a severely resorbed condyle with loss of vertical as well as anteroposterior bone structure. The articular eminence appears to have some uneven areas, although it has a moderate inclination. The joint space actually appears somewhat increased vertically.

Left TMJ coronal tomograms show a condyle that shows evidence of major condylar resorption. There is some medial beaking of the condyle. The lateral rim of the fossa curves downward about 2 mm.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has undergone greater resorption than the left side with extremely narrow mediolateral dimension of the condyle. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 mm. Both condyles are severely resorbed and deformed.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has a retruded maxilla but anterior vertical maxillary excess and posterior vertical maxillary hypoplasia. The mandible is severely retruded. She has a high occlusal plane angle. Oropharyngeal airway at the soft palate level measures 4 mm and at the base of tongue measures 4 mm. She has adenoid tissue in the nasopharyngeal area and she has an airway dimension of about 4 mm between the adenoid tissue and the turbinates and between the adenoid tissue and the soft palate. She was not in centric relation and she has almost an end-on incisor relationship.

Basic diagnoses for B520 would be as follows:
Bilateral TMJ juvenile rheumatoid arthritis.
Maxillary AP and posterior vertical hypoplasia.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of 4 mm (normal AP dimension 11 mm).
Hypertrophied turbinates.
Nasopharyngeal hyperplastic adenoid tissue.
AP microgenia.
End-on Class II occlusion.
Anterior open bite.
High occlusal plane angle of 25 degrees (normal angle is 8 degrees).

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1.	Presurgical orthodontics. a.	Align and level maxillary arch. Keep lateral incisor roots tipped mesially and cuspid roots tipped distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. b.	Align and level mandibular arch. Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing approximately 2 mm of tooth structure and retract to decrease the over angulation.
2.	CT scan of jaws and jaw joints.
3.	Surgery. a.	Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement in a counterclockwise direction with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. b.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance in a counterclockwise direction. c.	Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen). d.	Bilateral coronoidotomies. e.	Nasopharyngeal adenoidectomy. f.	Bilateral partial nasal turbinectomies. g.	Medpor implant to mandible.
4.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B520 MRI
BIRTH DATE:
DATE OF ACQUISITION:
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has lost significant volume. There may be some discal tissue present, but there appears to be a reactive pannus surrounding the disc. The disc could be a little bit medially displaced. The condyle is severely arthritic and narrow mediolaterally as well as a loss of vertical volume.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has undergone significant condylar resorption. There may be some discal tissue present within the joint, although it appears to be a reactive pannus surrounding the articular disc. There is significant destruction of the condyle with loss of mediolateral and vertical height.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has lost significant vertical height and some anteroposterior dimension as well. There is some anterior beaking of the condyle. The articular disc actually appears to be anteriorly positioned underneath the articular eminence and there is some sort of dense tissue between the condyle and the fossa. There is some inflammation around the articular disc and in the joint space. There is increased vertical joint space between the condyle and fossa. The articular eminence had a moderate inclination. In the T2 images, it shows significant inflammation within the joint surrounding the anteriorly displaced disc as well within the joint space between the condyle and fossa. On opening, there is restricted translation of the condyle and the deformed degenerated disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is extremely small in size with loss of vertical and AP volume. The disc is hard to identify but may be anteriorly displaced. There is dense tissue within the fossa again. There is increased vertical joint space between the condyle and fossa. The articular eminence had a moderate inclination. On the T2 imaging, there is significant inflammation in the joint space. The disc is difficult to identify. On opening, there is significant restricted translation and difficult to identify any remnant of the disc.

IMPRESSION: Bilateral TMJ severe arthritis with significant condylar resorption being worse on the right side than the left side with articular disc anteriorly displaced, degenerated and with reactive pannus filling a majority of the superior joint space area. Findings are compatible with connective tissue autoimmune type of process.

original_note:
MELODY HUTCHINS								02-19-03

Melody is 30 years old and referred by Dr. Paul April and Dr. Pat Shannon. 

Patient History

Melody was recently diagnosed with rheumatoid arthritis in January 2003. Previous surgeries involved removal of her wisdom teeth in 1993 and a gum graft in November of 2002. Melody is aware of having TMJ problems beginning as a child of unknown etiology. However, she only became aware of shifting changes in her bite and jaw alignment in the past eight years. She was involved in an MVA where she was hit from behind by a drunk that hit her quite hard. She developed some joint problems at that time and in 1993 was receiving splint therapy. She also did have some physical therapy performed as well. She has had really no other significant treatment for her current condition.

She has no earache problems, gets occasional tinnitus, but no vertigo, and she is not aware of having any significant hearing loss. Other joints that bother her include her elbows, right thumb, knees, right hip, and right ankle. She is not aware of clenching and bruxism. She says that virtually none of her teeth touch anymore. She cannot wear cheap jewelry as this tends to make her break out and itch, etc.

Current Medications

Vioxx
Liquid vitamins

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Melody has fairly good facial symmetry in the middle and upper third face. Upper lip length is about 19 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 3 m. Maxillary dental midline is pretty much on the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline is shifted about a millimeter to the left, and lower dental midline and chin are fairly well aligned with each other.

In profile view, she may have just some slight AP maxillary deficiency maybe a couple of millimeters. Chin is deficient by probably 15 to 18 mm. Chin-neckline is quite short. Chin-neck angle is not well defined at all. She has some fullness in the submental area. Very short distance between the trachea and the genial tubercles. 

Intraorally, Melody has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. Lower cuspids are almost totally blocked out of the arch. She probably has at least 12 mm of crowding in the lower anterior arch. Fairly good attached gingiva in most areas except is quite thin around the cuspids. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars present. She has some mild malrotations particularly around her incisors and maybe just some mild crowding in that area. In centric relation, she appears to occlude only on her second molars bilaterally. She has an anterior open bite of about 3 mm. She had an AP overjet of 10 mm. She is fairly close to being a full Class II malocclusion. Melody does tend to posture her mandible forward where it will fairly closely approximate a Class I position, although she still maintains a 2 to 3 mm anterior open bite.

Melody rates her headaches at a level of 6 in the temporal and posterior head area. She rates her TMJ pain she gets about twice a week at about a level of 4 on the average but it can escalate up to 9. Average daily pain is at 4. Jaw function is a 8, diet at 5, and disability at 5. Maximal incisal opening is 37 mm. Excursive movements in each direction are approximately 7 mm. To palpation, she has mild to moderate tenderness over the temporalis, masseters, TMJs, styloid areas, and SCMs. On opening, she had a palpable click on the right side, and she feels as though she has some crepitation on the right side as well. On the left side, she had some noises in the joints on excursive movements as she also did on the right side. Melody is aware when she wakes up in the morning that she hasn’t been breathing really well at night.

Intraorally, the oropharynx looks pretty much normal. 

Intranasally, the turbinates appear moderately enlarged. She has a deviated nasal septum, and anteriorly it goes significantly off toward the left side constricting the nostril on the left side. I can hold her nostrils open, and she can breathe easier.

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. Appears to be significant crowding in the lower arch. Turbinates appear moderately enlarged. Septum appears deviated and appears to be a septal spur off on the right side. Sinuses on the right side appear a little bit cloudy. Condyles appear severely deformed and resorptive and are postured out on the articular eminences. She has relatively prominent gonial notches.

Waters view shows that there is some deviation of the nasal septum. Turbinates are moderately enlarged. Airway doesn’t appear to be really good intranasally. Sinuses, however, do appear relatively clear. Anterior open bite is quite evident. 

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has undergone severe resorptive changes with an osteophyte extending posterior and a small one anterior. Articular eminence is somewhat shallow although quite long in length. Bone thickness in the eminence area is thinner than usual. Condyle is postured forward in the fossa. May be some decreased anterior joint space between the anterior osteophyte on the remaining condyle and the articular eminence. Increased vertical and posterior joint space. Probably at least half to two thirds of the condyle and neck have been lost relative to original volume. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has undergone severe condylar resorption. Appears to be an osteophyte sticking off the top of it. Joint space is decreased superiorly but increased posteriorly. Articular eminence is moderately shallow and somewhat elongated. 

Opening shows that there is fairly good translation with it being greater on the right side as compared to the left side. The smallness of the condyles and the deformity of the condyles is well appreciated in the open views.

Cephalometrically, Melody has a severely retruded mandible. She has a high occlusal plane and probably some posterior vertical maxillary deficiency. Oropharyngeal airway is surprisingly good. From the soft palate to the posterior pharyngeal wall measures about 8 mm and from the base of the tongue to the posterior pharyngeal wall also about 8 mm. Down around the hyoid bone measures about 6 mm. 

Basic Diagnoses

Bilateral TMJ rheumatoid arthritis with severe condylar resorption
Severe AP mandibular deficiency
Slight AP maxillary hypoplasia
Class II occlusion 
Anterior open bite of approximately 3 mm
Overjet of 10 to 11 mm
TMJ pain
Headaches 
Myofascial pain
Severe crowding, lower anterior arch
Tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference approximately 4 mm
Hypertrophied turbinates
Deviated nasal septum
Nasal airway obstruction

Recommended Treatment

Evaluation at Environmental Center at Dallas to evaluate for hypersensitivities to materials to be used in the prostheses

Surgery, Stage 1
Placement of distraction osteogenesis device
Vertical midline interdental osteotomy

3.	Distraction
       Wait 7 to 10 days, then activate distraction osteogenesis device at the rate of mm per day for approximately 10 to 14 days
      Retain device for at least three months

Presurgical orthodontics
Align and level maxillary arch
Align and level mandibular arch. Strip lower anterior teeth removing at least 2 to 3 mm of tooth structure to help adjust for the significant tooth-size discrepancy 

CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol) to aid in manufacturing of a 3-D model of Melody’s jaws

Surgery, Stage 2
Bilateral TMJ condylectomies, debridement, reconstruction, and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to expand the maxilla, advance it, and level the occlusal plane
Bilateral coronoidectomies
Fat graft to bilateral TMJs (harvested from abdomen)
Bilateral partial turbinectomies
Nasal septoplasty

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

The consultation lasted for approximately 3 hours and 45 minutes.

original_note:
CELIA MCCADDEN-MARTINEZ						08/11/2011

Celia is 55 years old, birth date 07/12/1956.  She did have hepatitis when she was 18 years old, but otherwise she is healthy.  She takes a number of medications:

Covaryx-HS.
Aspirin 81 mg.
Zeid 10 mg.
Paxil 1/8 of a 20 mg tablet.
Vitamin D. 
Melatonin.
Acido folico.
Placimine 300 mg.
Surfak.
Vitamin C.
Echinacea.

The onset of Celia’s jaw and joint problems is unknown, but she thinks they began at least 2 years ago.  The etiology is unknown.  She went to see an ear doctor and he treated her with some medications and some exercises.  

Celia gets headaches about once a week at about a level of 4 predominantly in the temple areas.  These are fairly well controlled with Tylenol.  She rates her TMJ pain that occurs only on the left side at 4, average daily pain at 4, jaw function and diet at 3, disability at 0.  She has no earaches, but get right-sided tinnitus, but no vertigo.  She may have some hearing loss.  The only other joint that really bothers her is her left shoulder and her left knee does make some noises from kneeling a lot.  Celia does states that if she drives in a car a long way or is in a boat she does develop vertigo.  She is aware of clenching her teeth at nighttime.  She sometimes will wake up in the morning with her right side of her jaw and neck hurting.  

Celia was a thumb sucker when she was younger.  She had orthodontics from about the age of 9 to 10 and it appears as though she had head gear to reposition the upper teeth.  

In frontal view, Celia has good facial symmetry and balance. Upper lip length is about 22 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 2 mm. The maxillary dental midline, mandibular dental midline and chin are all pretty much on the facial midline.  In profile view, she has some fairly good facial balance.  She is just slightly retruded maybe about 6 mm in the mandible and maybe 2 or 3 mm in the maxilla.  Chin neckline is short and chin neck angle is not as well defined.  Intraorally, Celia has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars.  She has some very mild crowding in the lower anterior arch.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  She has a Class I cuspid molar relationship and about a 2 mm anterior overbite.  To palpation, she has moderate pain over the right SCM and milder pain over the left TMJ and left shoulder.  On opening, she has a loud audible click at about 40 mm of opening in the left joint and a closing click at probably about 30 to 35 mm.  The maximal opening is 50 when she can pop her joint and reduce her disc on the left side.  The excursion movement to the right is 9 mm and to the left 11 mm.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars.  The roots are fairly short on the mandibular second molars.  She has a root canal on the maxillary second bicuspids and left first molar.  The condyles may appear a little bit arthritic.  The sinuses appear relatively clear.  The turbinates appear moderate in size.  

Coronal view shows that the septum appears reasonably straight.  Turbinates appear enlarged.  Sinuses appear clear. 

Axial view shows that the septum appears fairly straight.  Turbinates are moderate in size with a somewhat constricted airway.  Sinuses appear relatively clear.  

Left TMJ sagittal view shows that the condyle is somewhat arthritic.  It appears somewhat small in size.  It is posteriorly positioned in the fossa and there is decreased posterior and superior joint space but increased anterior joint space.  The articular eminence is moderately steep.  There is some sclerosis on the head of the condyle.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is larger than the left side.  There is a little bit of anterior beaking present.  The condyle is slightly posteriorly positioned in the fossa.  The articular eminence is much more shallow on this side as compared to the opposite.  There appears to be decreased posterior joint space.  

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves probably about 3 mm.  There is a decreased lateral joint space with what appears to be almost bone-on-bone contact.  

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 mm or so.  There is a significantly decreased lateral joint space, but towards the center of the condyle and medially the joint space appears more normal.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that there is a Class I skeletal and occlusal relationship.  Oropharyngeal airway at the soft palate level measures about 9 mm and at the base of the tongue about 6.5 mm, although the tongue is postured low in the mouth with a distance from the tongue to the palate of about 9 mm.  

Relative to the ringing in her ears, she has been to see ENT doctors in Mexico.  They have done extensive testing on her and have not been able to identify any etiology from their standpoint to cause the tinnitus.  

Basic diagnoses for Celia would be as follows:

Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteromedially displaced with late reduction on opening and with significant deformation of the articular disc.
Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc medially displaced and slightly anterior.  
Relative mandibular hyperplasia.
TMJ pain.
Headaches.
Myofascial pain.
History of clenching.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Presurgical orthodontics:  Place maxillary and mandibular orthodontic appliances.
2.	Surgery.
	a.	Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek 			anchors.
	b.	Bilateral ramus sagittal split osteotomies to maintain the occlusion and 			create space in the joints to accommodate the articular discs.
3.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.  

I believe that the displaced discs particularly in the left TMJ have been present for many years and is not a recent development.  She did report getting hit by a car and striking the right side of her jaw which could have been the etiology for the displacement of the left disc.  The length of time that the disc has been out of place and the significant deformation of the disc will affect the prognosis.  However, I still feel that the disc would be the best option for Celia.  She understands that the prognosis is somewhat guarded because of the length of time and deformation of that left disc.  However, I still feel the disc would be the best approach to try to remedy her problems at this time.  I did inform her that if it did not hold up that a total joint prosthesis would be indicated.  She will think about the treatment options as I also told her she could have nonsurgical treatment such as splint therapy and also could do arthroscopy and/or medications, but that these modalities of treatment will not fix the joint situation.

original_note:
JAMES KASTEN									03/03/2008

James is 19 years old, referred by Dr. Gary Wolf, 137 Benedict Avenue, Norwalk, Ohio 44857, and indirectly referred by Dr. Peter Sinclair from USC in Los Angeles.  James had previous surgery for a right leg osteotomy in 1990.  James is in good health with no major medical problems.  James had orthodontics in about the second grade through the ninth grade with all different kinds of devices and expanders.  He finished his orthodontics when he was a freshman in high school and had a good bite at that time.  He has been a faithful wearer of his retainers since then, but in spite of this his bite has shifted and changed.  He has no problems with headaches, no TMJ pain or pain around the head and neck area.  He rates his jaw function at 7, diet at 5, disability at 4.  He has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, or hearing loss and no other joints bother him.  He is not aware of any significant problems with clenching or bruxism.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, James has fairly good symmetry in the middle and upper third part of  the face.  Upper lip length is about 22 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 5.5  mm to 6 mm.  Maxillary midline is fairly close to being on the facial midline.  The nasal tip is just a little bit off towards the right side.  Mandibular dental midline is shifted about 3 mm off to the left.  Chin is shifted about 6 mm to the left.  Mandibular dental midline and chin midline are fairly well aligned with each other.  In the profile view, the maxilla and upper lip are retruded about 3 to 4 mm.  Chin is retruded about 12 mm.  Chin neckline is short, although the chin neck angle is fairly well defined.  James has a lip incompetence of approximately 15 mm.  Back in the frontal view, with the lips relaxed, James shows probably about 5 to 6 mm of his lower teeth as well.  James has about a 4 mm anterior open bite.  He has a Class III end-on occlusion on the right side and slightly Class II on the left side, each about 2 to 3 mm in each direction.  James has had first bicuspids extracted in all four quadrants, but does have his third molars in position.  He has a crossbite tendency on the right side.  As James bites, he occludes only on the left second and third molars and all other teeth are out of occlusion.  With a little bit more force, he can get the right posterior teeth to touch.  On the right side, it is predominantly the third molars that touch.  Oropharyngeal tissues look reasonably healthy.  Uvula is probably mildly elongated.  Soft palate looks relatively normal.  Faucial pillars are constricted; particularly the posterior pillars show some significant constricture.  Tonsils are mildly to moderately enlarged.  Intranasally, James has moderately enlarged turbinates.  Columella is moderately wide causing the nostrils to be a little bit narrow.  He has a significant constricture, however, of the luminal valves bilaterally.  Holding those nasal valves open enabled him to breathe easier through his nose.  Transverse occlusal plane appears relatively level.  AP position of the teeth in the arches appears fairly symmetric.  To palpation, James had some mild tenderness over the styloid areas, but no other tenderness around the head and neck area.  Joints seem to function reasonably smoothly.  Incisal opening is 51 mm.  Excursion movements are about 7 mm in each direction.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the presence of all four third molars, but the absence of probably all four first bicuspids.  Left sinus appears a little bit cloudy and it appears there could be a mucous retention cyst there.  No other significant findings noted.  Waters view shows that the sinuses appear clear.  I do not see evidence of mucoceles in the left sinus in this view.  Bony septum is reasonably straight.  Turbinates are moderately large.  Particularly on the left side, it appears he that has some significant obstruction. Asymmetry of the mandible is noted with the lower dental midline and chin being shifted off towards the left side.  Frontal sinus is quite large.  Left TMJ shows a condyle that looks reasonably good.  Joint space is thin, but may be due to thin articular discs.  Left TMJ tomogram is a little bit more difficult to read.  The condyle appears to have relatively normal configuration.  On opening, there is moderate  translation of both condyles.  Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that James has a vertical maxillary excess and an accentuated curve of Spee in the upper arch.  The mandible appears to be in grossly a Class I position.  There is slightly reversed curve of Spee in the lower arch.  Oropharyngeal airway is very good.  He appears to have relatively short roots on the maxillary incisors.  Tooth size analysis shows a Bolton’s difference of 1 mm.  

Basic diagnoses for James would be as follows:

1.	Bilateral TMJ articular disc dislocations with early reduction. 
2.	Right mandibular condylar hyperplasia.
3.	Anterior vertical maxillary hyperplasia.
4.	AP maxillary hypoplasia.
5.	Transverse maxillary hypoplasia. 
6.  	AP mandibular hypoplasia.
7.	Class III end-on occlusion on the right side, Class II end-on occlusion on the left side.
8.	Anterior overbite of 4 mm.
9.  	Left posterior crossbite.  
10.	Accentuated curve of Spee in the upper arch. 
11.	Slightly reversed curve of Spee in the lower arch.
12.	Hypertrophied nasal turbinates.
13.	Constricture of bilateral nasal luminal valves.
14.  	Lip incompetence of 15 mm.
15.	Maligned third molars.
16.  	Short roots on maxillary incisors.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

	1.  	Presurgical orthodontics.
a.  Align and level the maxillary arch at two different occlusal plane levels. Align the cuspids, second bicuspids, first molars, and second molars.  Place a step in the arch wires so that the four incisors are aligned at a higher occlusal plane level than the cuspids and posterior teeth. Keep lateral incisor roots tipped mesially and cuspid roots tipped distally.  
b.  Align and level mandibular arch.  Try to extrude the lower four incisors about 1 mm to decrease the reverse curve of Spee.
2.  	Surgery.  
a.  Right TMJ high condylectomy to arrest the condylar hyperplasia.
b.  Left TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor. 
c.  Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the maxilla superiorly, expand, and   level the occlusal plane. 
d.  Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance and superiorly reposition the mandible as well as align it to the facial midline.  
e.  Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies. 
f.  Reconstruct nasal luminal valves.
g.  Extract maxillary and mandibular third molars if the orthodontist agrees.  
3.  	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion and retain.

original_note:
B166
B166 was referred by Dr. XXXX. B166 is 40 years old. Primary concerns she has are as follows:
She has a cant in the occlusion. The teeth do not fit together. Left-sided TMJ pain. Left-sided headaches. Difficulties eating and chewing.

B166 had orthodontics as a teenager. She began orthodontic treatment again at age 34 and continues to be under orthodontic treatment since that time. Five years ago, she had lower jaw surgery to advance the mandible and also a blepharoplasty. She has had significant complications as a result of that surgery. She has total anesthesia to the lower lip and chin area. She rates her TMJ pain on the left side at a level of 5, average daily pain at 4, jaw function at 5, diet at 6, disability at 4. She gets headaches on the left temporal and top of the head area at a level of 6. She gets earaches on the left side, but no tinnitus and gets occasional vertigo. She feels she is developing decreased hearing. Other joints that bother her include her left elbow and wrist. She states that her speech is affected since the surgery and talking increases the pain. Talking is an important part of her living. She is not aware of clenching or bruxism.

She takes the following medications:
Celebrex. Tramadol.
Aleve.
Fish oil.
Flax seed oil.
The TMJ problems did really become significantly apparent until after the surgery was done. Her speech problems developed after she began having the TMJ problems. B166 became aware of having TMJ problems and headaches when she was about 18 or 19 years of age. She remembers two traumas, one when she was in the seventh grade and she got hit in the jaw with a door, and then when she was 15 she got hit in the jaw with a boom on a tiny sailboat that she remembers being a little bit sore afterwards. Otherwise, the etiology is unknown. B166 does have some problems with her left arm in her elbow joint, but also if she types too long she develops numbness down through her left arm.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
B166 has chronic tearing due to the fact that her lacrimal ducts apparently were injured with her blepharoplasty surgery. Upper lip length is about 23 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is about 2 mm. The maxillary dental midline and mandibular dental and chin are all fairly well aligned to the facial midline. In profile view, B166 has fairly good facial asymmetry. Chin neckline may be just slightly shorter and for the most part has fairly good facial balance. Intraorally, B166 is missing the lower first bicuspids extracted for orthodontic reasons probably. She has a space between the left second bicuspid and cuspid that is about 2 mm wide. In the upper arch on the left side, she is fairly close to being Class I cuspid and maybe just a slight Class II tendency by 1 or 2 mm. On the left side, she is closer to Class I and has a space between the cuspid and the second bicuspid. She has a slight open bite tendency in that side in the bicuspid area and cuspid area. Transversely, the maxillary left cuspid is about 1.5 to 2 mm higher than the right cuspid. In the mandible, the left cuspid is up about 3 mm compared to the right cuspid. She has an anterior deep bite of about 2 mm. The oropharyngeal tissues all look relatively normal. As B166 bites her teeth together, she feels that she occludes only on the left cuspids and the rest of her teeth are apart. To palpation, B166 has some mild tenderness on the left side over the masseter, retromandibular area, SCMs, posterior neck and shoulder area. Maximum incisal opening is 45 mm. Excursion movement to the right 7 mm, to the left 6 mm. On opening, she has a pop in the left joint about 35 mm and a closing click at about 25. The right side functions relatively smoothly. Intranasally, the turbinates are mild to moderate in size. The septum seems reasonably straight. It is difficult to tell if there is any significant nasal airway obstruction. She is not aware of whether she snores. She states she has rather severe daytime somnolence, but is not aware of having sleep apnea. She has probably a moderate sleep disorder and wakes up every 2 hours or so. She does not know if she is a mouth breather or not. She does wake up dry in the morning however. She does have some lower lip ptosis of about 4 to 5 mm.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars and the mandibular first bicuspids. There are a few screws in the ramus on the right side with at least one appearing to go through the mandibular second molar. There are four screws on the left mandibular ramus. There is a bone plate in the left lateral incisor area in the chin position. She appears to have significant arthritis in the left jaw joint. The right side is a little bit more difficult to evaluate. The sinuses look reasonably clear. Waters view shows that the sinuses are clear. Septum in the bony area is fairly straight. Turbinates are moderately enlarged. The screws are extremely long that were used in the mandible. Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears a little bit arthritic with some flattening on the anterior head. Superior joint space appears narrow. Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears arthritic with some flattening and broadening of the head. Joint space posterior actually appears a little bit increased, although superiorly it appears decreased. On opening, there is slightly better translation of the right condyle as compared to the left condyle. The right condyle actually appears a little bit smaller in anteroposterior width as compared to the left side, but the condylar head and neck appears longer on the right side as compared to the left side. Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that B166 has a high occlusal plane angle facial morphology. She is somewhat retruded in the chin area. Oropharyngeal airway is about 6 to 7 mm at the soft palate and about 6 to 7 mm at the base of the tongue.

NEUROLOGICAL ASSESSMENT. With 2-point discrimination, B166 could distinguish 2 points at 20 mm on the left side, but could not detect that on the right side. With von Frey hairs, she was 1.65 in the upper lip, 3.84 in the left lower lip, and 4.08 in the lower right lip. With brushstroke, she could tell direction fairly reasonably on the left side. She also got some correct on the right side, but not quite as accurate as on the left side. With sharp, she could distinguish between sharp and dull sensation bilaterally. With cold, she could sense cold in both sides of the lower lip. Therefore, her loss of sensibility in the lower lip is probably between 1 and 2.

Basic diagnoses for B166 would be as follows:
Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with possible reduction on wide opening. AP mandibular hypoplasia.
AP maxillary hypoplasia.
Transverse maxillary asymmetry with the right side being 2 mm lower than the left side and the mandible being 3 mm lower than the left side. Spacing in the mandible lower arch between the cuspid and first bicuspid. Ankylosed mandibular right second molar.
Vertical discrepancy in the marginal regions between the mandibular right first and second molars.
Bone screws through the roots of the mandibular right second molar.
Bilateral deformity at the inferior border of the mandible secondary to previous mandibular ramus osteotomies.
Left TMJ pain. Left-sided headaches. Left-sided myofascial pain. Bilateral infraorbital hypoplasia.
Permanent nerve and muscle injuries to the eyelids secondary to previous blepharoplasty surgery with the left side being worse than the right side. Anterior mandibular vertical hypoplasia.
Lower lip ptosis.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Hypertrophied turbinates.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
Presurgical orthodontics. Continue to align and level maxillary arch. Keep lateral incisor roots tipped mesially and cuspid roots tipped distally to make room for interdental osteotomies.
Align and level mandibular arch. Close all the spacing in the lower arch. Adjust the bracket heights on the mandibular right molars and bicuspid so that the teeth are aligned to each other. Surgery Stage 1.
Left TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor. Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible forward about 5 to 6 mm at the Pogonion, level it transversely, as well as slightly decrease the occlusal plane angle. Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla approximately 3 to 4 mm, down graft it slightly posteriorly, level it transversely, and correct any transverse discrepancy that may be present. Partial bilateral inferior turbinectomies. Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy to down graft the chin about 3 to 4 mm.
Resuspension of the mentalis muscle to eliminate lower lip ptosis. Augmentation of inferior border of the mandible bilaterally with HTR.
3. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
a. Finalize the alignment in occlusal relationship.
b. Stabilize using permanent retention in the lower arch if necessary to keep the spaces closed. Use Hawley type retainers for post orthodontic retention..
4. Surgery Stage 2. a. Bilateral infraorbital osteotomies to advance and superiorly reposition the infraorbital rims.

B166 MRI AGE: 40/F
Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that appears somewhat arthritic. There appears to be some loss of condylar volume. There is some anterior beaking present. The articular disc is anteriorly displaced and sitting anterior to the condyle along the posterior surface of the articular eminence. The articular eminence appears moderate in size. The condyle appears a little bit wider at the lateral aspect of the joint. The disc appears extremely short and degenerated. Over medially, the condyle is extremely narrow. Articular eminence is moderate in steepness. The disc may be medially displaced as well as anteriorly displaced. The disc is thickened in the posterior band area and is quite short anteroposteriorly.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that appears arthritic and a little sclerotic on top. There is flattening on the anterior head and a little bit of anterior beaking present. The condyle has more of a hockey stick shape to it. The articular disc is anteriorly displaced and significantly degenerated. There is a decreased posterior joint space. There does not appear to be any significant inflammatory process present. The disc appears to a little bit vertically oriented and the disc appears short anteroposteriorly.

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears quite arthritic and it appears that there is loss of volume vertically. The disc appears to be anteriorly displaced, but appears degenerated. Lateral rim of the fossa is quite flat.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has better morphology, although there is sclerosis on the top of it. It is degenerated. There is decreased vertical joint space. Articular disc is anteriorly and medially displaced. Lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 to 3 mm.

On opening, it appears at the left joint that the disc is nonreducing, however, on the right side, the disc appears to be in normal position throughout the opening sequence.

IMPRESSION:
Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation without reduction and with significant deformation of the articular disc. Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc anterior and medially displaced. Prognosis for the left joint is not nearly as good because the disc is so degenerated, but still may be salvageable.

B166 I-CAT AGE: 40/F
DOB:
Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has undergone arthritic changes. There is significant anterior beaking present. The condyle appears to be postured slightly forward in the fossa. There may be decreased superior joint space. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Maxillary sinuses appear clear.

Right TMJ joint sagittal view appears to have some decreased posterior joint space. The condyle has a little bit of anterior beaking. Joint space appears a little bit better balanced. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Turbinates appears moderately enlarged in the coronal view. Sinuses appear clear. The inferior turbinates are moderately enlarged. Bony septum appears fairly straight. In the sagittal view from the right side, there are 2 bone screws that are in the proximity of the mandibular right second molar. One screw appears to go right through the mesial root, and the distal screw appears to go adjacent and against the distal root.

In coronal view, the left TMJ shows arthritic changes and the condyle appears to have lost some volume. There appears to be decreased vertical joint space. The condyle appears to be postured out towards the lateral aspect of the fossa. Very minimal downward curvature of the lateral rim of the fossa.

Axial views show that the inferior turbinates are quite large and appear to fill up most of the nasal airway. Sinuses look clear and healthy.
IMPRESSION:

Bilateral TMJ arthritis being worse on the left side. Hypertrophied inferior turbinates. Bone screws through the mandibular right second molar.

original_note:
WENDY STEWART									07/27/2009

Wendy is 35 years old and referred by Dr. Rob Fuentes, oral maxillofacial resident at Baylor University Medical Center and at Baylor College of Dentistry.  Wendy is in fairly good health.  She weighs 180 pounds and is 5 feet 7 inches tall.  Her primary concerns are as follows:

Increasing jaw pain, worse on the right side. 
Headaches with increased frequency. 
Ear pain, worse on the right side. 
Increasing difficulty eating and chewing.
Increasing noises with chewing. 
Increased flare-ups in the jaw area relative to pain and dysfunction. 
Molars do not touch. 

Wendy takes the following medications:

Zoloft 75 mg per day. 
Aleve 1 to 2 tablets 1 to 2 times a day. 
Motrin 4 tablets per day. 

She does have a history of anxiety and depression and is currently under treatment for this.  Wendy’s problems began when she was about 24 years old in 1977 that she feels likely were related to stress.  She has really had no treatment, but has had consultations for treatment in 1997, 2004, and 2008.  She rates her headaches at a level of 4 in the temporal and posterior head area.  She rates her TMJ pain at 7, average daily pain at 1, jaw function at 4, diet at 3, disability at 2.  She gets occasional earaches, no tinnitus and no vertigo.  Other joints that bother her include her right knee and her SI joint.  She is aware of clenching and bruxing at night time, but not during the day.  She has severe snoring and occasionally is tired during the day, but does not claim any sleep apnea.  She has no difficulties sleeping at night. 

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Wendy has fairly good facial symmetry with just some slight increased fullness in the right side.  Upper lip length is about 20 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 2.5 mm.  In profile view, Wendy has fairly good facial balance.  Intraorally, Wendy has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars present.  Teeth are fairly well aligned and level, and she appears to have adequate attached gingiva of the lower anterior arch, although thin in some areas.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  Wendy is basically a Class I cuspid-molar relationship.  She has an anterior deep bite of about 2.5 mm.  Wendy has really good bite relationship.  She did have orthodontics when she was 15 for about 1 year.  To palpation, Wendy has no pain around the head and neck area today.  However, she states that she frequently gets pain on the right side in the posterior body of the masseter muscle in about the vertical mid portion area.  She does not have pain to palpation directly over the joint itself.  On opening, Wendy has bilateral popping in her joints that is almost audible at about 5-10 mm of opening and pops at about 5 mm of closing.  Maximal incisal opening is 47 mm, opening without significant discomfort 35 mm.  Excursion movements are about 10 mm in each direction.  Going into excursion movement, she does have significant clicking and popping in both joints.  Oropharyngeal tissues looks relatively normal and healthy.  Intranasally, the turbinates appear moderately enlarged.  The septum deviates a little bit off towards the left side anteriorly, and it is difficult to see how it goes posteriorly.  Wendy previously has had her wisdom teeth removed. 

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars, but the rest of the teeth are all present.  Turbinates appear moderately enlarged.  The condyles appear flattened on the front with a little bit of peaking in the condyles.  
Coronal view shows that the sinuses are actually clear.  Septum appears reasonably straight.  Turbinates appear extremely large and appear to obliterate most of the nasal airway.  The lateral turbinates are moderately enlarged as well. 
Axial view shows that the sinuses are clear.  The septum appears reasonably straight.  Turbinates appear quite large and appear to obliterate most of the airway.  
Left TMJ sagittal tomograms shows that there is some flattening on the anterior head of the condyle and some very slight anterior beaking.  There appears to be a decreased posterior joint space with slightly increased vertical joint space at the most anterior part of the condyle.    
Right TMJ likewise shows flattening on the anterior head of the condyle and some posterior position of the condyle.  Articular eminence in both joints are moderately steep.  Joint space appears a little bit better balanced on the right side as compared to the left side.  
Left TMJ coronal tomograms shows a condyle that has some flattening at the lateral pole of the condyle.  There appears to be decreased vertical joint space and lateral rim space.     
Right TMJ coronal tomogram shows a condyle that has a little flattening on the lateral pole of the condyle and a little bit of peaking in the condyle towards the medial aspect of the joint.  Joint space superiorly and laterally may be somewhat decreased.  
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has an oropharyngeal airway space of about 6 mm and at the base of tongue perhaps 5 mm of airway space.  It is difficult to tell where the superior surface of the tongue is positioned relative to the hard palate.  She does appear as though she has a Class I cuspid-molar relationship.  The tongue itself does not clinically look excessively large.  In the lateral ceph, the soft palate may be somewhat elongated.  That coupled with the hypertrophied turbinates may account for the snoring problems that she has. 

Basic diagnoses for Wendy would be as follows:    

Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with early reduction. 
Mandibular hyperplasia. 
TMJ pain. 
Headaches. 
Bilateral hypertrophied nasal turbinates causing nasal airway obstruction. 

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Presurgical orthodontics.  
Align and level maxillary arch.  
Align and level mandibular arch.  
Surgery. 
Bilateral TMJ articular discs repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to maintain the Class I occlusion and create space in the joints for the discs. 
Bilateral partial nasal inferior turbinectomies. 
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
JANIS WESTLAKE									11-14-03

Janis is 55 years old and referred by Dr. Danny Dugan, 613 Harwood Road West, Hurst, TX.

Patient History

Janis has had previous surgery including gallbladder surgery and some spinal cord surgery x 2. She does have a history of supraventricular tachycardia. Her last spinal cord surgery to untether the spinal cord was in December of 2002. 

Janis’ problem began probably at least 30 years ago. She was aware of having clicking and popping in her jaw joints. However, it has just been in the past three years that her problems have gotten severe. The etiology is unknown. She was over in Romania when the problems really flared up for her. She is back obviously in this country now. Dr. Charles Holt has made some splints for her and has done some iontophoresis which has helped her. She also has had steroid injections to treat her occipital neuralgia. This has been helpful for her although not completely removing the pain problems, but she has definitely experienced improvement. She has had physical therapy for her back but not for her jaws. In February of 2002, she initiated orthodontic treatment with Dr. Al Bishop, and she has been in treatment now for nine months.

Janis also had orthodontics done when she was a teenager at the age of 17 and had four bicuspids extracted at that time. She is aware of clenching her teeth at night. 

She gets daily headaches, but she gets headaches throughout the head area two or three times a week at about a level of 8. She has TMJ pain fairly constantly at a level of 4, but it can easily escalate to an 8. Overall pain is at 6, jaw function and diet at 6, and disability at 7. 

Current Medications

Lexapro 20 mg
Toprol XL 100 mg
Vioxx 25 mg
Fioricet p.r.n. 
Cyclobenzaprine 10 mg
Hydrocodone 5 mg/500 mg as needed for the back

Patient Concerns

TMJ pain
Headaches 
Difficulties to eat and chew
Jaws and teeth do not align properly
Significant difficulty eating and chewing
Pain down through the jaws and pain down into the neck and back area

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Janis has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 21 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 4 mm. Maxillary, mandibular, and chin midlines are all pretty much aligned with the facial midline. 

In profile view, Janis has some slight AP maxillary deficiency of maybe about 3 or 4 mm. Chin is AP deficient by probably close to 15 mm. Chin-neckline is extremely short, and chin-neck angle is not well-defined. Lower third face appears a little bit elongated. She does have some lip incompetence of probably about 7 to 8 mm. 

Intraorally, she has all of the teeth back through the second molars except she is missing the first bicuspids. She has an accentuated curve of occlusion in the lower arch. Has a fairly heavy arch wire on with some mild spacing around the lower incisors. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars except she is missing the first bicuspids. She has residual space of probably about 2 mm on the left side and probably 3 to 4 mm on the right side where the first bicuspid was. Has an accentuated curve of occlusion in the upper arch with the left first molar being lower than the second molar, which appears to be elevated higher than the rest of the teeth. She has a Class II occlusion bilaterally. She has an overjet of probably about 4 mm. Has just a slight cant in the occlusal plane with the left side being about 1 mm higher than the right side. Oropharynx appears clear, and tissues appear healthy. She does have a torus on the palate of relatively small size. Actually, intraorally she has all of her wisdom teeth present except for the maxillary right third molar. She has about a 2 to 3-mm anterior overbite relationship.

Intranasally, nasal septum is deviated a little bit off toward the right side. Turbinates are only moderate size, and nasal airway appears adequate on both sides.

Today is actually a pretty good day for Janis, and she has less pain than usual. To palpation, she has high moderate pain over the left TMJ and mild pain over the right TMJ. She has mild pain to palpation around most of the rest of the head and neck area. 

Maxillary incisal opening is 48 mm, but she can only hold that momentarily. She can open about 30 mm without severe pain. Excursive movements are about 4 mm in each direction. On jaw function, she has significant crepitation in both joints, and it was actually audible.

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows the absence of all four first bicuspids and maxillary right third molar with the rest of the teeth and third molars present. Sinuses look clear. Turbinates are moderate in size. TMJs appear arthritic. There is some calcification of the styloid processes bilaterally with them extending beyond the posterior border of the mandible. Possibly could be a retained root in the right maxillary area. Appears as though there may be a fourth molar on the left side behind the erupted third molar. 

Waters view shows that the sinuses appear relatively clear and of equal radiolucency comparing right to left side. Left turbinate appears a little bit enlarged on that view. 

Left TMJ tomogram shows some significant arthritis changes present. There appears to be some loss of height in the condyle and some significant anterior beaking. Toward the anterior beaking area, it appears that there is bone-on-bone. Posterior joint space is significantly decreased as is the superior joint space, although there is a little bit of space in that area. 

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that also appears to have lost vertical dimension and appears to have bone-on-bone contact with even perhaps less space between the superior and posterior joint spaces. Condyle appears smaller in size than normal. 

On opening, there is moderately good translation of both joints with decreased vertical joint space particularly on the right side. 

Cephalometrically, Janis has a skeletal Class II relationship. She has a decreased oropharyngeal airway with the distance at the soft palate to posterior pharyngeal wall being about 4 mm to 4.5 mm and at the base of the tongue measures about 8 mm. She has Class II skeletal and Class II occlusal relationships. 

Basic Diagnoses

Bilateral TMJ severe arthritis with articular disc dislocations with severe deformation and deterioration and perforations
Mandibular hypoplasia
Transverse maxillary hypoplasia
Anterior vertical maxillary excess
Class II occlusion
Accentuated curve of occlusion, lower arch
Missing all four first bicuspids and maxillary right third molar
Probable tooth-size discrepancy of 2 to 3 mm
Severe TMJ pain
Severe headaches 
Myofascial pain

Recommended Treatment

Evaluation at the Environmental Center to test for and rule out hypersensitivities to materials in the prostheses

CT scan (TMJ Concepts protocol)

Presurgical orthodontics 
Continue to align and level maxillary arch. Retract upper cuspids back against the second bicuspids, but leave some spacing between the upper lateral incisor and cuspids to accommodate for a tooth-size discrepancy 
Continue to align and level lower arch, but place a step in the arch wire on both sides to align the anterior six teeth in an elevated plane level as compared to the second bicuspids and molars. Close all the spacing from cuspid to cuspid

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ condylectomies, debridement, reconstruction, and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses
Bilateral coronoidectomies
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvested from abdomen)
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance, expand, and downgraft the posterior aspect
Anterior mandibular subapical ostectomy

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

original_note:
TROY SWARTS									01/22/2008

Troy is 39 years old.  He got my name off the Internet.  He has had the previous surgical procedures:
Rhinoplasty.
Sliding genioplasty in approximately 1994.
On September 5, 2007, double jaw surgery to advance the maxilla and mandible.

PRIMARY CONCERNS:  Sleep apnea.

Troy had orthodontics done at the age of 18 with extraction of upper and lower first bicuspids and teeth retracted.  He had repeat orthodontics at about the age of 30 to close up some residual space but this moves his teeth back even further.  The double jaw surgery in September of 2007 was to correct his sleep apnea problems but Troy does not notice much difference or improvement.  He still needs about 10 to 12 hours of sleep at night to even be able to function the next day and sometimes still his mind is foggy.  He is getting very poor quality REM sleep.  

The only medication that Troy takes is Clonazepam 0.5 mg tablets 1 to 2 tablets at bedtime.

Troy has no problems with headaches, TMJ pain or myofacial pain.  No trouble with earaches or vertigo but does have bilateral tinnitus.  He has significant hearing loss and wears bilateral hearing aids.  He is not aware of clenching or bruxing at night or during the day.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION:
In frontal view, Troy has fairly good left to right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 22 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 2 mm.  Maxillary midline is fairly close to being on the facial midline.  In profile view, Troy has some slight AP maxillary deficiency maybe about 3 to 4 mm.  Infraorbital hypoplasia with the infraorbital rims being probably about 6 mm deficient.  The chin is probably 12 to 15 mm AP deficient.  Maxillary teeth actually are more retruded than the maxilla is because the teeth are retruded posteriorly fairly significantly.  Intraorally, Troy is missing lower first bicuspids but has the rest of his teeth back through the second molars.  The lower teeth are in a little bit in lingual inclination.  It shows some of the root.  In the upper arch, he is missing first bicuspids.  He has the rest of the teeth back through the second molars.  He has basically a Class II end-on cuspid relationship on the right side by probably 3 mm but on the left side closer to Class I relationship.  He has probably about almost 5 mm vertical overbite.  Oropharyngeal areas look reasonably healthy.  There is evidence of a previous UPPP procedure and Troy remembered that he had that done in approximately 1990, but experienced no improvement in his airway following that procedure.  He also has tried the Thornton appliance but this did not improve the situation and actually only made his teeth loose.  Intranasally, the septum appears fairly straight.  He has probably had previous turbinectomies. Perhaps on the left side, however, there are some of the turbinates still remaining posteriorly but there does appear to be adequate air space inferior and there appears to be adequate airway on both sides.  Even with the UPPP procedure, Troy is aware of having some mild snoring.  He has some moderate to severe daytime somnolence.  He has moderate to severe sleep apnea issues.  TMJ joints seem to function smoothly.  He has no apparent tenderness around the head and neck area.  

TROY SWARTS				01/22/2008				Page 2

Incisal opening is about 42 mm counting the overbite.  Excursive movements are about 9 mm in each direction and opening is 42 mm.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION:
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars and all four first bicuspids.  He has root canals on the maxillary left second molar and left lateral incisor.  He has a crown on the maxillary left first molar and central and lateral incisors.  There are bone plates present in the maxilla.  Bone screws are present in the mandible.  He has bone plates in the anterior mandible from his previous chin procedure.  There are some bony defects present in that area.  The condyles look reasonably good.  The styloid processes are hypercalcified and elongated.  

Waters view shows that the right nasal airway appears wide open and the left one appears that there is some turbinate and that may be the area seen posteriorly and may obstruct that side some.  The bony septum at least is straight. The sinuses look relatively clear.  

Right TMJ tomogram shows that the condyle appears narrow anteroposteriorly.  There appears to be adequate joint space.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.

Left TMJ tomogram shows that there is probably adequate joint space.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  Condyle is hard to identify.  On opening, there is moderately good translation of the condyles.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that there is AP retrusion of maxilla and mandible.  There is slight increase in the occlusal plane angle but too much.  The chin is strong relative to AP position of the mandible.  Upper incisors are actually angled posteriorly.  Oropharyngeal airway measures less than 3 mm (normal AP dimension would be 11 mm.)  At the soft palate level, the airway measures about 6 mm.

BASIC DIAGNOSES: 
AP maxillary deficiency.
AP mandibular deficiency.
AP macrogenia.
Significantly decreased inclination of maxillary incisors. 
Missing all four first bicuspids and all four third molars.
Severely reduced oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of 2.5 mm (normal dimension of 11 mm.)
Severe sleep apnea.
Moderately increased occlusal plane angle.
Class II end-on occlusion on the right side but fairly close to being a Class I on the left side.
 Anterior deep bite of 5 mm. 
 Previous double jaw surgery performed in September of 2007.


TROY SWARTS				01/22/2008				Page 3

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch.  Open up first bicuspid spacing by pushing the anterior teeth forward to increase angulation of the upper incisors as well as to distalize the posterior teeth at least a couple of mm.  Open up the space to equal the size of a first bicuspid tooth approximately 7 mm.  This should tip the upper incisors forward at least 5 mm from their current position.
Align and level mandibular arch. 
Surgery.
Maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla about 2 to 3 mm and downgraft it posteriorly approximately 4 mm. 
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible forward approximately 14 to 15 mm. 
Consider possible extraction of second molars if impingement on the discal tissues of the mandible.
Consider possible anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy to set the chin backwards about 3 to 4 mm. 
Removal of previously placed maxillary bone plates x4 and screws x22 as well as removal of mandibular bone plates x2 and screws x13.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
Bilateral maxillary osteointegrated implants to the first bicuspid area.
Restorative dentistry to place crowns on the maxillary first bicuspid implants.
