original_note:
ELIZABETH URIBE									7/29//05

Libby is 20 years old and goes to TCU.  She is referred by Dr. Stan Ganderson from Houston.  She takes Zyrtec and Singulair.  She has some allergy problems and a few sinus problems, but nothing real significant.  

PRIMARY CONCERNS
She feels her bite is off even though she has gone through extensive orthodontics.  She does not feel comfortable with her bite.  
She feels that her jaws are retruded.  
She has difficulty breathing through her nose.
Predominately a mouth breather.  

She has no earaches.  She gets tinnitus and vertigo.  She has heart palpitations at times and has been told it may be related to the vertigo.  She has seasonal allergies, but no known problems with jewelry.  She is aware of clenching and grinding her teeth particularly at night.  She does get some pains in her neck and her back that she relates to perhaps playing volleyball.  She has to crack her back a lot to make it feel better.  She has problems with her left shoulder although she has had previous trauma to that area.  Her knees bother her some and her ankles bother her when she exercises.  She gets headaches 1 to 2 times per week primarily in the temple area at about a level of 4.  She rates TMJ pain at about a level of 5, overall average daily pain at about a level of 4.  She rates her jaw function at 6, diet at 3, disability at 2.  The onset of Libby's TMJ problems is unknown and the etiology is unknown.  She was in orthodontics for 8 years from the age of 9 through 17.

In frontal view, Libby has good left to right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 21 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is 4 mm.  Maxillary midline is fairly close to being on the facial midline.  Mandibular dental midline may be about a mm off to the left.  Mandibular dental midline and chin midline and fairly well aligned with each other.  In profile view, Libby has some AP maxillary deficiency probably about 3 to 4 mm.  She is retruded about 15 to 20 mm.  Chin-neckline is full and the chin-neck angle is not well defined.  Intraorally, Libby has all of her lower teeth through the second molars.  She has a 3 x 3 retainer on.  She has fairly good attached gingiva and teeth appear fairly well aligned.  In the upper arch she has all of her teeth back through the second molars present.  She has a Class II end-on occlusion being a little bit greater on the left side probably by 4 mm on the left side and probably 3 mm on the right side.  She has an anterior open bite of about 1 mm.  Actually her open bite is about a 0 as far as vertical overlap.  In centric relation she occludes only on the very back teeth on the right side and all other teeth are essentially out of occlusion.  In centric relation she does hit on the second molars on the right side and also the first bicuspids on that side also touch.  The rest of the teeth are out of occlusion.  Oropharynx appears clear.  Uvula appears relatively normal length as does the soft palate and faucial pillars look relatively normal.  Apparently, sometime in the past Libby had one of her fainting spells and fell and hit the right side of her mouth knocking some of her teeth on the right side loose including the cuspid and maybe bicuspid.  She states she also lacerated her lower lip.  Intranasally, she has large septal spur coming off the right side of the septum.  Turbinates are moderately enlarged and appear a little bit inflamed.  Airway seems somewhat constricted.  She has moderate to severe nasal airway obstruction.  
ELIZABETH URIBE				7/29/05				Page 2


She is predominately a mouth breather.  She is not aware of any snoring problems.  She says she can sleep a lot if she had the time to do it.  She has moderate daytime somnolence.  She is not aware of any sleep apnea problems.  To palpation, she has moderate tenderness over the temporalis muscles and high moderate tenderness over the styloid areas, but no other tenderness around the head and neck area.  On opening, in the jaws she has lots of noises in her joints and popping, but there is no consistency as to the level of jaw opening that the noises occur.  Popping is actual audible at times when she opens.  Sometimes it occurs early on in opening and sometimes a very late stage of opening.  Maximal opening is about 50 mg, but she only opened about 20 mm where she was reasonably comfortable.  Excursive movement to the right about 8 mm and to the left 6 mm.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION 
Panorex shows presence of all the teeth.  The third molars on the left side are bony impacted and on the right side probably soft tissue impacted and perhaps partial bony impaction on the mandibular right side.  She has a 3 x 3 retainer.  It is difficult to evaluate the sinuses and the nasal airway.  No other is significant pathology noted on this film.

Waters view shows that the sinuses are clear.  The left turbinate appears quite large and obliterating.  The septum has some deviation in it.  The spur seen anteriorly clinically is in the cartilage and therefore it does not show up as well.  The offset does not show up as the bony deformity.  

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that seems to have a reasonable morphology, although there are some bony changes definitely present.  The condyle is posteriorly positioned in the fossa with decreased posterior and also superior joint space.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  There is a horizontally impacted mandibular left third molar. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle with fairly good morphology, but the condyle is posteriorly and superiorly positioned in the fossa with significantly decreased joint spaces in those areas.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  The third molar on the right side appears as though it has a partial bony and soft tissue impaction.  However, its orientation is vertical.  On opening, there is good translation of the condyles.  

Cephalometric analysis shows that she has a Class II skeletal relationship, but the maxilla also appears to be AP deficient.  She has a high occlusal plane angle and an open bite tendency anteriorly.  She has an occlusal Class II relationship.  There may be a slight transverse vertical discrepancy.

BASIS DIAGNOSES
Bilateral TMJ articular disc dislocation with reduction.
AP maxillary deficiency.
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
AP mandibular deficiency.
ELIZABETH URIBE				7/29/05				Page 3


High occlusal plane angulation.
Class II end-on occlusion being slightly worse on the side as compared to the right side.
Transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right side being 1 to 2 mm lower than the left.
Occluding only on the right first and second molar area.
Mandibular dental midline and chin shifted to the left side about 1 to 2 mm.
 Headaches.
 TMJ pain.
 Myofascial pain.
 Tinnitus.
 Vertigo.
 Nocturnal clenching and bruxism.
 Hypertrophied turbinates. 
 Nasal septal deviation with a large septal spur off towards the right side.
 Nasal airway obstruction. 
 Impacted third molars x4. 
 Tooth size discrepancy of approximately 2 mm. 

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT
MRI of left and right TMJ.

Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies in case they are needed.
Align and level mandibular arch.  Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing approximately 1 to 2 mm of tooth structure and close the spacing. 

Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla forward and down graft the posterior aspect and level transversely.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction.
Removal of 4 bony impacted third molars. 
Nasoseptoplasty. 
Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies.

4.  Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
CINDY LOU KIBBE									04/06/2009

Cindy Lou is 48 years old, referred by Dr. Edward Gray, 1516 Virginia Ranch Road, #1A, Gardnerville, NV 89410.  Primary concerns that Cindy Lou has includes the following:

Inability to open the mouth to eat. 
Nutritional complications secondary to liquid diet.
Pain.
Getting worn down with pain and difficulty eating.
Effects on her ear, eye, and now possibly related Bell’s palsy.

Previous history relative to her TMJs include braces at age 9 to 16 from 1969 to 1976.  She had a retainer at age 19.  

Her accident was in October 1989 when she was 29 years old.  An 18-wheeler ran over her car ay apparently crushed it.  The driver of the truck kept going and it was a hit and run accident.  They never identified the driver, but the truck ran over the back of the car and pushed her face forward into the steering wheel.  Her jaws were wired together after the accident as she had some fractures around her orbits and her mandible apparently.  It was not until February 1990 that they took her to surgery and reconstructed the orbits and the lower jaw.  She apparently had some subcondylar fractures that caused dislocation of the condyles.  She apparently during that operation also had the bilateral temporalis fascial flaps to reconstruct the joints.  They apparently advanced the mandible forward at that same time.  She subsequently had arthroscopies in March 1990 and June 1990.  With the big surgery on February 14, 2009, she also had a chin implant and four plates with 27 screws placed apparently up around the orbital area.  From 1991 to 1997, she had removal of the some her plates and 20 screws and in addition arthroplasties on the joints bilaterally to try to release some of the scar tissue.  She did fairly well until March 9 when she developed severe pain and significant dysfunction of her jaws.  In the chart is a list of all Cindy Lou’s other surgeries that she has had. 

Cindy Lou rates her TMJ pain at a level of 9 and average daily pain is about 7.  She gets headaches at about a level of 8 predominantly on the right side in the frontal and temporal area and behind her eye.  She also gets pain down through her right neck and shoulder area.  She rates her jaw function at about 9 to 10.  She is basically on a liquid diet so her diet is at 10, and her disability is on average about a level of 9.  It is getting more difficult for Cindy Lou to even swallow.  She is losing weight at about an average of 5 to 6 pounds per week.  She is getting some significant dizziness.  She gets earaches, tinnitus, and vertigo, more prominent on the right side as compared to the left side.  She, however, is now beginning to develop some left-sided symptoms as well.  She is not aware of clenching or bruxing of her teeth.  She has some numbness that she is now developing in her right cheek area.  She seems to have a fairly reasonable facial nerve function at this point in time, although there could be just very slight decreased function on the right side.  It is difficult to tell if that is related to Bell’s palsy or just to the significant pain on that side.

Cindy Lou currently takes the following medications:

Synthroid 300 mg per day. 
Premarin 0.9 mg per day.
Vitamin B12, vitamin D and a multivitamin. 
Ambien 10 mg p.r.n. sleep.
Soma 350 mg 4 times per day p.r.n. muscle pain.
Lortab elixir 2 to 4 tsp. q. 4 to 6 hours p.r.n. pain.
Promethazine 25 mg q.4 to 6 hours p.r.n. nausea.
Acyclovir 400 mg q.i.d.
Clindamycin 450 mg q.i.d.

The acyclovir and Clindamycin were given to her in case there was a possible Bell’s palsy developing.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Cindy Lou has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 24 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 2.5 mm.  The maxillary, mandibular, and chin midlines are fairly well aligned with each other.  In profile, she may have just some slight AP maxillary deficiency, and the chin may be retruded about 8 to 10 mm.  Chin neckline is not defined.  Cindy Lou is missing the mandibular left first molar and probably the third molar with the second molar drifted forward into almost in the first molar position.  On the left side, she is missing the mandibular second and third molar.  In the upper arch, she is missing the second bicuspid and second and third molar, and on the right side she is missing the bicuspids.  To palpation, Cindy Lou has severe pain over the right temporalis, masseter, TMJ, and styloid area, moderate to high moderate pain over the shoulders, right posterior neck, right SCM.  She does have a slight Class II end-on occlusion on the right side and close to a Class I on the left side.  Maximum incisal opening is 15 mm, but only about 5 mm without pain.  Excursion movement to the right was 5 mm, to the left 2 mm.  She does have a history of snoring, but does not claim any problems with daytime somnolence or sleep apnea issues.  Intranasally, turbinates are relatively normal in size and, in fact, look small.  She does have a huge septal perforation that is probably at least 15 x 20 mm. 

I-CAT EVALUATION.  Panorex view shows the absence of the maxillary left second bicuspid, second and third molar, maxillary right bicuspid and third molar, mandibular right first molar and third molar as well as the two central incisors and the left second and third molar.  There is some periapical pathology around the mandibular right second molar and mandibular left first molar.  She has got four bone screws in the ramus of the left mandible and three bone screws in the ramus of the right mandible.  Both joints look severely arthritic.  Sinuses look relatively clear.   
Coronal view shows her turbinates are actually moderate in size more posteriorly and the sinuses are clear.  There is a large perforation present in the septum.  
Axial view shows that the turbinates are moderately enlarged.  
Right TMJ sagittal view shows severe arthritic changes with anterior beaking and decreased joint space and bone-on-bone contact.  
Left joint shows even a more severely condyle with anterior beaking and areas of erosion and decreased joint space and should be pretty close to having bone-on-bone contact in some areas.  
Coronal view of the right joint shows the lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly significantly almost 8 to 10 mm.  There is bone-on-bone contact through a good portion of the joint.  The condyle is very small mediolaterally.  There are degenerative changes in the fossa as well as in the condyle.  
In the left TMJ, there are severe degenerative changes as well.  The condylar head is a little bit broader mediolaterally.  Lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly significantly.  There is decreased vertical joint space, and it appears that it could be bone-on-bone contact.  Both joints exhibit advanced osteoarthritic changes.  
Lateral cephalometric radiographs show that Cindy Lou has a significantly decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension at the soft palate level of about 5 mm and at the base of the tongue about 5 mm as well.  

Basic diagnoses for Cindy Lou would be as follows:    

Bilateral TMJ severe arthritis secondary to previous trauma.
AP and posterior vertical maxillary hypoplasia.
AP mandibular hypoplasia. 
Decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Sleep apnea.
Severe pain. 
Severe masticatory dysfunction.
Probable periapical abscess around the mandibular right molar and possibly the mandibular left molar.  

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Extracting mandibular right first molar if periapical abscess can be confirmed.
CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol).
Surgery. 
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. 
Maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla in a counterclockwise direction. 
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen).
Bilateral coronoidotomies. 
Application of maxillary and mandibular arch bars.
Extensive dental reconstruction to include implants and crowns.  

Cindy Lou states the following additional problems:

Currently taking in liquids and cannot eat or chew at all.
She is losing approximately 5 pounds per week due to malnutrition. 
She is extremely tired during the day. 
Difficulties swallowing likely due to the significantly decreased oropharyngeal airway. 
Severe TMJ and jaw pain, being worse on the right side than the left side.
Right-sided headaches.
Severe myofascial pain, particularly on the right side.
Approaching total disability.

original_note:
JENNA NORMAN								01/19/2012

Jenna is 27 years old, birth date 03/20/1984.  Jenna believes that her joint problems may have possibly started in May 2011 when she was riding in a boat and the water was extremely choppy and it was quite traumatic for her.  When she got off the boat, she was lightheaded and dizzy.  Her symptoms did not get significantly worse until about September 10, 2011, when she woke up with fullness in her ears.  She then saw some ENT specialists because of the ear symptoms.  She was placed on steroids and even some antibiotics without relief.  On October 10, 2011, after seeing Dr. Polson, he made her a splint.  This made the pain worse.  She got injections into her tendons intraorally.  Jenna is under quite a bit of stress as she is a 7th grade schoolteacher and also coaches three sports.  She took a month off of school at one point because she was getting significant pain and migraine type headaches.  Initially, she did have chiropractic treatment around September 3, but this did not seem to help very much.  She got an NTI splint in November 2011.  She then was referred to Dr. Shaw, a pain specialist.  He placed her on medications and also gave her Botox injections which were not beneficial.  She did have orthodontics for about 7 months when she was in the 3rd grade.  

Jenna currently takes the following medications:

Cymbalta 60 mg q.d.
Hydrocodone 7.5 mg 3 tablets per day.
Baclofen 10 mg tablets 3 tablets at bedtime.

Primary concerns that Jenna has at this time include the following:

Severe TMJ pain.
Ear pain.
Face pain.
Significant headaches.  

Jenna apparently is very emotional and prior to the Cymbalta was significantly emotionally stressed.  
   
Jenna rates her TMJ pain at 8, average daily pain at 7, jaw function 5, diet at 7, and disability at 7.  Her headaches she rates at a level of 7 and they occur all over her head including the frontal, temporal, and posterior head area.  She has no earaches, occasional tinnitus, no vertigo, and no hearing loss.  No other joints bother her.  She has no known allergies and is not aware of any allergies to jewelry.  She may have some nighttime clenching.  

In frontal view, Jenna has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 20 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 4 mm.  In profile, Jenna has good facial balance.  Intraorally, Jenna has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars.  She has some spacing between the lower anterior teeth.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  She has a Class II cuspid relationship on the left side and to a slightly lesser degree on the right side.  The midlines are fairly well aligned.  She is Class II by probably 5 mm on the right side and probably 6 on the left side.  She has an anterior deep bite of 4 to 5 mm.  She has a slight reverse curve of Spee in the upper arch.  She has an overjet of 1 to 2 mm.  Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively normal and tonsils are just mildly enlarged.  She gives no history of snoring, has mild daytime somnolence, but no sleep apnea issues.  She is predominantly a nasal breather.  To palpation, she has some mild tenderness over the temporalis muscles and over the TMJs.  The maximal incisal opening is 29 mm, opening without discomfort 23 mm.  The excursion movements are 8 mm in each direction.  On opening, she has a click in the right TMJ at about 20 mm and a closing click at 10 mm.  The left side functions reasonably smoothly, although on MRI it shows that the disc is displaced on that side.  Jenna does report that she has also lost about 15 to 20 pounds through all this.  She used to weigh about 128 and was very active and now she weighs about 112.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows that she has impacted mandibular third molars and maxillary third molars.  She has a root canal in the maxillary left first molar.  The sinuses look relatively clear.  The turbinates appear relatively in normal size.  The septum appears a little bit deviated off to the left side.  Difficult to evaluate the joints.  

Coronal view shows at least posteriorly the bony septum is reasonably straight.  Sinuses appear clear.  Left turbinate is moderately enlarged.  

Axial view shows that sinuses are clear.  The septum is deviated just slightly to the left.  Turbinates are moderate in size.  

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has a little bit of flattening and sclerosis towards the lateral aspect of the joint.  The articular eminence has a moderate inclination.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle with a little flattening on top of the condyle.  The articular eminence has a moderate inclination.

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width.  There appears to be a little extravasation of the lateral pole of the condyle out laterally creating a decreased joint space at the lateral aspect of the joint.  The lateral rim curves inferiorly about 3 to 4 mm.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has good mediolateral width.  There is decreased vertical joint space at the lateral aspect of the joint.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Jenna has a skeletal Class II relationship and a Class II occlusal relationship.  Oropharyngeal airway is decreased with an AP dimension of about 5 mm at the soft palate level and about 7 mm at the base of the tongue.  

Dental model analysis shows a tooth size discrepancy of 2 mm.

Basic diagnoses for Jenna would be as follows:

Bilateral TMJ mild arthritis and articular disc displaced with early reduction.
Mandibular hypoplasia.
TMJ pain.
Myofascial pain.
Headaches. 
Class II end-on occlusion.
Multiple diastemas lower anterior arch.
Anterior deep bite of 4 to 5 mm.
Tooth size discrepancy of 2 mm.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Presurgical orthodontics.
	a.	Align and level maxillary arch. Open 1 mm of space bilaterally between 
                         the cuspids and lateral incisors to correct for the tooth size discrepancy.
	b.	Align and level mandibular arch closing the mandibular anterior spacing.  
2.	Surgery.	
	a.	Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek 			anchors.
	b.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible.
3.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion. 
4.	Restorative Dentistry.
            Place bonding or veneers on maxillary lateral incisors to eliminate spacing created 
            to correct the tooth size discrepancy.

original_note:
LAUREN KLEIMAN									12/19/2005 

Lauren is 16 years old referred by Dr. David Goldberg, Avenue Palmas 745-PB, Mexico City DF, Mexico, 11000.  Lauren is in good health with no major medical problems.  When Lauren was at least 12 years of age she had a good bite relationship.  Now the lower jaw has grown out to be on the upper jaw.  She has had orthodontic appliances on her teeth for about four years.  Lauren has no problems with headaches, TMJ pain earaches, tinnitus, vertigo or hearing loss.  No other joints bother her.  She said she is not aware of clenching or bruxism.  

In frontal view, Lauren has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 20 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is probably 4 mm.  The nasal tip is off the right side probably a couple of mm.  Maxillary midline is off to the right about 1 mm.  Mandibular dental midline is off to the right about 3 mm.  Chin is off to the right side about 5 to 6 mm.  As she shifts her chin towards the left side, it does improve her overall facial symmetry.  Maxillary midline may actually be a couple of mm off to the right side.  In profile, Lauren has some lip incompetence of probably about 6 mm.  The maxilla appears to be AP retruded by probably 4 to 5 mm.  Chin-neckline is relatively normal and chin-neck angle is very well defined.  She has a high mandibular plane angle facial morphology.  Intraorally, she is missing lower first bicuspids.  The transverse width of the lower arches appears constricted.  In the upper arch, she has had first bicuspids also extracted.  She is end-on Class III occlusion on the right side by probably 4 to 5 mm and on the left side, she is probably a good 5 mm Class III.  The upper and lower arches are fairly symmetric in AP position of the cuspids.  The upper arch is fairly level with maybe just a 1/2 mm difference with the right side being slightly higher than the left side.  In the lower arch, the right side appears to be about 1 mm higher than the left side.  She does have an anterior open bite of about 2 mm.  No cross bite is present.  There is an anterior cross bite difference of probably about 6 mm.  Intraorally, the tonsils are present and are moderately enlarged.  The uvula and soft palate are relatively normal.  Probably some mild to moderate constricture of the faucial pillars that may be related to the tonsils being present.  She does have some mild deviation of the nasal septum off to the right side and the turbinates are moderately enlarged.  She is not aware of any problems of snoring, sleep apnea etc.  Lauren has had orthodontics for approximately 4 years.  She has had all 4 first bicuspids extracted.  At one point, the lower teeth were being tipped back inwards trying to control the occlusion.  Lauren actually has been in treatment in various treatment and various stages since about the age of 8 years old.  Her current appliances have been on about 4 years.  She at one time had a high-poll headgear apparently to try to retract the upper teeth or to keep them from erupting vertically.  She apparently was told 4 years ago that she did have a vertical growth pattern and there would be some problems keeping the teeth and jaws together.  The maximal incisal opening is about 53 mm.  Excursive movement to the right is 4 mm, to the left 5 mm.  To palpation, she has no tenderness around the head or neck area including no tenderness over the joints.  She rates her headaches at 0, TMJ pain at 0 and no pain at all or discomfort around the head and neck area.  Interestingly, she can generate clicking and popping in her joints particularly if she goes to excursive movements to where it actually can be audible.  My initial evaluation did not reflect that but she then demonstrated that she could do that.  Dental model analysis shows that there is a discrepancy in width to the upper and lower arch. 


LAUREN KLEIMAN				12/19/2005				Page 2


Lower posterior teeth are tipped lingually with an increase curve of Wilson or as in the upper arch there is an excessive curve of Wilson and manipulating the models will get the patient into a good Class I cuspid molar relationship, however, there is a transverse width discrepancy with the mandible being too narrow compared to the maxilla.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the presence of all 4 third molars except the left maxillary third molar is overlying the first and second molars.  The 4 first bicuspids are absent, but on the other hand it is possible that it could be that all 4 second bicuspids are extracted and not the first bicuspids.  There is adequate room between the lateral incisors and cuspids.  It may actually be more on the palatal side.  The sinuses look clear.  It is difficult to evaluate the TMJs.  

Waters view shows that a little deviation in the nasal septum is present.  The right turbinate appears enlarged particularly posteriorly.  The right airway seems to be occluded while the left one seems fairly open.  The sinuses appear clear.  

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears fairly normal in morphology but maybe elongated head and neck length.   Articular eminence is relatively shallow.  There may be a little bit of decreased posterior joint space and superior joint space but difficult to determine.  

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears more narrow anteroposteriorly than the opposite side.  Articular eminence is moderately shallow.  There appears to be decreased vertical joint space.  On opening, there is fairly good translation of both TMJs.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows a skeletal and occlusal Class III relationship.  High occlusal plane angle is present.  Oropharyngeal airway appears good except there is some adenoid tissue at the posterior nasopharyngeal area.  

BASIC DIAGNOSES 
Bilateral mandibular condylar hyperplasia.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc dislocations. 
Mandibular deviated prognathism.
Transverse constriction of the lower arch.
AP maxillary deficiency and asymmetry.
High occlusal plane angle. 
Slight vertical maxillary excess. 
Transverse maxillary arch excess.
Increased curve of Wilson in maxillary and mandibular arches.
Class III occlusion.
Anterior open bite.
Impacted third molars x4.
LAUREN KLEIMAN				12/19/2005				Page 3


RECOMMENDED TREATMENT
Presurgical orthodontics.
Continue to align and level maxillary arch. Try to decrease the transverse width of the upper arch to decrease the accentuated curve of Wilson.
Continue to align and level the lower arch.  Expand the lower posterior teeth from the bicuspid to the molars to decrease the accentuated curve of Wilson.
Surgery.
Bilateral mandibular high condylectomies and articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors to stop the excessive mandibular growth.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to set the mandible backwards.
Maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla.
Surgical removal of impacted third molars x4.
Post Surgical Orthodontics
                 Finish and retain

original_note:
ELIZABETH JUNG									08/12/2010

She is referred her by Mark Strickland, a physical therapist in Austin.  Elizabeth’s problems began when she was about 16 when she had received a whiplash injury in a motor vehicle accident.  Her bite may have changed some and she had orthodontics performed in the early 20s.  She also had her wisdom teeth removed in her 20s.  In her middle 30s, she had developed increased TMJ problems.  On the left side, the joint was reconstructed with a dermal graft and on the right side the disc was repositioned.  A year later, she had bilateral mandibular ramus sagittal split osteotomies with rigid fixation.  Within a couple of weeks after surgery, Mr. Strickland identified that Elizabeth had a displaced disc on the right side.  The bone screws in the right side were removed and the mandible apparently manipulated and the disc “was put back in place”.  She was wired together for 2 or 3 weeks.  She did have physical therapy after she had her joint surgeries and then after the orthognathic surgery for probably 3 months after each.  Her tinnitus started 4 years post surgery on the left side.  She has a pulsatile tinnitus in the left joint since that time.  In March 2010, the tinnitus also went over towards the right side.  She has been to see a  number of doctors in reference to this problem.  She has had various diagnoses but nothing absolute.  She takes the following medications:

Atenolol.
Omnaris nasal spray.
Astepro nasal spray p.r.n. 
Zyrtec.

She does not have any significant problems at all with headaches, but rates her TMJ pain at 8 and daily pain therefore would be about an 8.  She rates her jaw function at 6, diet at 5, disability at 8.  After eating, her ears feel very stuffy and sometimes she has to stop eating because of the feeling and discomfort that she has.  She gets earaches occasionally, has severe tinnitus and it is pulsatile on the left.  She gets occasional vertigo.  She has a hearing loss in both ears with the left side being worse than the right side.  No other joints bother her.  She has allergies to weeds, trees and grasses.  She has been diagnosed with Eustachian tube blockage.  She is aware of bruxing perhaps at nighttime.  When Elizabeth chews on her left side, she gets a throbbing burning kind of feeling.  

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Elizabeth has fairly good facial symmetry in the middle and upper third face.  Upper lip length is about 20 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 1.  The maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline as is the mandible dental midline.  The chin is just off to the left side about 2 mm.  In profile, she has good facial balance.  Intraorally, Elizabeth has all of her lower teeth back to the second molars.  She has some mild crowding in the lower anterior arch but has adequate attached gingiva.  In the upper arch, she is missing the right first and second molars and she is missing all four third molars.  She is basically a Class I cuspid relationship.  She has a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the left side being about 2 mm higher than the right side.  Elizabeth has an anterior vertical overbite of about 2 mm.  Oropharyngeal tissues all look normal and healthy.  Intranasally, the turbinates appear relatively normal.  Septum may have just a slight deviation to the right side, but there does not appear to be any significant nasal airway obstruction.  To palpation, Elizabeth has no pain around the head and neck area.  Maximum opening is 36 mm.  Excursion movement to the right is 3 mm and to the left 4 mm.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars and the maxillary left first and second molars.  Sinuses look relatively clear.  Right turbinate appears moderately enlarged, but the septum appears reasonably straight.  She appears to have some sclerosis in the right condyle and severe condylar resorption in the left condyle.  She has 3 bone screws in the left mandibular ramus area.  

Left TMJ coronal view shows severe loss of condylar volume with almost complete loss of the condylar head.  There is an osteophytic projection medially.  Joint space actually looks fairly reasonable.  There appears to be an ossified body between the condyle and lateral rim of the fossa.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 mm.  There is sort of an exophytic in-growth from the lateral rim of the fossa.  

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has good height and width.  There is decreased vertical joint space, particularly at the lateral aspect of the joint where there could be bone-on-bone contact.  There is some sclerosis on the head of the condyle.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 mm.  

Left TMJ sagittal view shows almost complete absence of the condylar head.  Even the articular eminence is quite flattened, however, there is joint space present.  The condyle is positioned forward in the fossa by probably 3 to 4 mm.  

Right TMJ sagittal tomograms show a condyle that has significant arthritis.  There is anterior beaking present and there appears to be decreased vertical joint space, more so at the lateral aspect of the joint.  The articular eminence is relatively shallow.  

Coronal view shows that the turbinates appear moderately enlarged and the sinuses appear clear. 

Axial view shows that the turbinates are moderately enlarged, but the sinuses are clear.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she is basically a Class I skeletal and occlusal relationship with some decreased angulation of the incisors.  Oropharyngeal airway looks reasonably good with an AP dimension of about 8 mm at the soft palate level and at the base of the tongue.  

Basic diagnoses for Elizabeth would be as follows:

Bilateral TMJ arthritis, being much worse on the left side. 
TMJ pain.
Severe tinnitus.
Eustachian tube dysfunction. 

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	CT scan of jaws and jaw joints. 
2.	Surgery.
	a.	Bilateral TMJ reconstruction with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses.  
	b.	Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen).
	c.	Application and removal of arch bars.

original_note:
SHEILA STANLEY									10/26/2005

Sheila is 43 years old, referred by Ronald Phillips, DDS, Lubbock, Texas.  Sheila states she has chronic sinus problems.  She had a total hysterectomy in 1991 and had her tonsils removed as a child.  Sheila is in good health except she does have some thyroid problems.  

CURRENT MEDICATIONS
Synthroid 112 mcg q.d.
Singular 10 mg q.d.
Premarin 1.25 mg q.d.
Zoloft 100 mg q.d.

She has had adverse reactions to sulfa, codeine and Mepergan Fortis.  She is unaware of any food allergies and she has no jewelry allergies. 

PRIMARY CONCERNS
Popping of TMJs.
Neck and back pain.
Headaches. 
TMJ pain.
Difficulty eating and chewing.
Teeth and jaws do not fit together.

Even as a child Sheila had problems with pain in her neck and back that her mother would take her to the doctor for.  However, it was not until about 4 years ago that she began having significant popping in her jaw joints with the right side being worse than the left side.  The etiology is unknown.  During that time she developed significant headache problems that were about a level of 8 and these headaches would occur about 3 to 4 times per week and involve the frontal, temple and posterior head area.  She would have significant TMJ pain at about the level of 7 and average daily pain at about a level of 7.  She received a splint about a year ago and this has helped significantly reduce her pain around the head and neck area.  Her headaches now when they do occur is still about an 8 but do not occur as frequently as they did.  The TMJ pain is now about a level of 3 and average daily pain at about a level of 2.  She still rates her jaw function at 7, diet at 5 and disability at 5.  Sheila's mother also has TMJ problems so it is possible there could be some genetic factors.  She has no problems with earaches, vertigo or hearing loss but does get some tinnitus.  The only other joints that bother her are her knees and they are not real significant.  She is aware of clenching and bruxism at night and feels that she does this during the day as well.

In frontal view, Sheila has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 20 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 3 mm.  In profile view, she may have just some slight AP maxillary deficiency.  She has some macrogenia.  Chin-neckline has good and perhaps some slight increased length.  Chin-neck angle is moderately well defined.  Intraorally, she has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars.  She has generalized fluorodosis.  In the upper arch she has all of her teeth through the second molars.  She is slight Class II cuspid relationship on the right side, close to a Class I on the left side.  




SHEILA STANLEY 				10/26/2005				Page 2


Lower dental midline shifts off to the left about 1 mm to 1.5 mm.  They are fairly aligned with each other.  The chin is also just slightly off to the left side 1.5 mm.  She does have some wearing and chipping off the lower anterior teeth.  There is probably about a 2 to 3 mm Class II on that right side.  She has a little bit crowding in the lower anterior arch.  A little bit of posterior open bite tendency right now probably due to the splint therapy that she has been on.  The splint she has does open her bite up and positions her mandible forward and is somewhat hidden because of the open bite that has been created with the splint.  It is a lower appliance that she has.  She had only about 1 mm overbite anteriorly.  Prior to the splint she has a lot of myofascial pain problems affecting the temporalis muscles and masseter muscles.  Currently she has high moderate pain to palpation over the shoulders, left TMJ, moderate discomfort over the left styloid area and then milder tenderness over the right TMJ and posterior neck area.  On excursive movement, she occasionally has a pop in one of the jaw joints but it is difficult to figure out which one it was.  Since wearing the splint, the popping has significantly decreased from what it was.  Maximal incisal opening is 56 mm.  She could open to about 40 without too much discomfort.  Excursive movement to the right was 8 mm to the left 8 mm.  She did have an occasional popping noise in the right joint as she would go into excursive movements.  She has a little pop in both sides but inconsistent.  Again, prior to having the splint she had much more significant clicking and popping.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION 
Panorex shows the presence of all the teeth except for the 4 third molars.  The sinuses may be a little bit cloudy perhaps moreso on the right side than the left side.  The condyles are off the x-rays so I cannot evaluate those.  I cannot evaluate the nasal airway.

Waters view shows that the septum is deviated off to the left side but there could be some spurring off to the right side.  The turbinates appear moderate in size.  The right side appears a little bit cloudy compared to the left sinus.  The rest of the sinuses appear clear.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has some flattening on the anterior aspect and a little bit of peaking on the top of the condyle.  The condyle is displaced posteriorly.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  Anterior joint space is increased.  The disc is likely anteriorly displaced in the vertical orientation.  

Left TMJ shows a condyle that has some arthritic changes with flattening on the anterior head and some anterior beaking present.  Decreased joint space superiorly.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  Anterior joint space appears somewhat decreased.  On opening, there is good translation of both condyles.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that he has skeletal Class III relationship but closer to a Class I cuspid and molar relationship.  She appears to have bilateral posterior open bites present.  Airway space appears good.  Transversely, the occlusal plane is fairly level.  
SHEILA STANLEY				10/26/2005				Page 3

BASIS DIAGNOSES
Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocations with reductions.
AP maxillary deficiency.
Mandibular prognathism.
AP macrogenia.
Low occlusal plane angle.
Class III nonocclusion on the right side.
TMJ pain.
Headaches.
Myofascial pain.
Masticatory dysfunction. 
Mild crowding of the lower anterior arch.
Chronic sinus problems. 
Hypertrophied turbinates.
Probable blockage of the maxillary sinus ostiums. 
Partial nasal airway obstruction.
Nasal septal spurring.
Probable tooth size discrepancy. (Models were not available at the time of evaluation.)

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies.  Open up 1 mm space between each of the laterals and cuspids to provide room for interdental osteotomies.  
Align and level mandibular arch. 
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla forward and move the posterior aspect superiorly.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to rotate the mandible in a clockwise direction.
Partial inferior turbinectomies. 
Nasoseptoplasty. 
Consider possible nasal antrostomies.
Debridement of maxillary sinuses particularly on the right side.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion. 

I also discussed with her the possibility of doing the joints and just the lower jaw osteotomies.  She, however, is concerned about her airway problems and chronic sinus problems which can best be addressed in conjunction with performing maxillary surgery as well.  I did discuss the potential risks and complications associated with the surgery. 

cc:  	Joseph Forsman, DDS
6400 S. Quaker
Lubbock, TX 79413
406-795-6415

original_note:
MARGARET OWENS								05/04/2009

Margaret is 57 years old.  She has had TMJ problems at least from the age of 37 or possibly earlier.  At 37 is when she first began developing pain in the right ear and TMJ area.  She had orthodontics for 18 months from the age of 37 to 39.  She has had numerous splints over the past 13 years.  She has had extensive dental work, a lot of it performed by Dr. Galaros in Houston, Texas.  She unfortunately has had many of her posterior teeth removed in the maxilla and also in the right mandible.  These were taken out because of pain factors.  She gets earaches, right side much greater than the left side, no tinnitus, but does get occasional vertigo.  Almost all of her other joints are problematic for her.  She has been diagnoses with Epstein-Barr infection and still has the virus present.  She is very much aware of clenching and bruxism at night time and also during the day time.  She rates her headaches at a level of 4 on the right side and 2 on the left side and the headaches daily at a level of 4 on the right side and 2 on the left side predominantly in the frontal and temporal areas.  She rates her TMJ pain in the right joint at a level of 7 and on the left side at 5.  Average daily pain is at 8.  Jaw function is at 4, diet at 3, disability at 7.  She was diagnosed with fibromyalgia approximately 12 years ago. 

She takes the following medications:

Atenolol 25 mg q.d.
Klonopin 0.5 to 1 mg q.h.s. 
Armour thyroid 30 mg 2 times per day. 
Natural progesterone 1 tablet at night. 
Testosterone shot 1 time per month.
Estrogen patch (Vivelle) 0.025 two times per week.
MSM 1000 mg q.d.
Super Green Food q.d.
Krill oil 500 mg 2 times per day.
Digestive enzymes 2 at each meal.
Zinc 50 mg 1 per day. 
Vitamin D 5000 mg q.d.
Cordyceps CS-400 mg 8 per day.
B12 shots.
Folamin tablets 1 per day. 
 Vitamin C.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Margaret has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Maxillary and mandibular midline are all pretty much on the facial midline.  Chin may be just slightly to the left side a couple of millimeters.  In profile, Margaret has fairly good facial balance.  In the mandibular right quadrant and maxillary left and right quadrant, she is missing all bicuspids and molar teeth.  On the mandibular left side, she is missing only the third molar, but has crowns on the first and second bicuspid and first and second molars.  She is basically a Class I cuspid molar relationship.  She has relatively an anterior deep bite. Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively normal.  Intranasally, the turbinates are moderate in size, but she states she has no significant problems breathing through her nose.  She does have some mild snoring and moderate daytime somnolence, but is not aware of any sleep apnea issues.  Since taking the Klonopin, she sleeps fairly well at night.  She is not aware of mouth breathing.  To palpation, she has very high moderate pain over the right TMJ, right SCM, right posterior neck, and bilateral shoulders.  She has moderate pain over the masseter, right medial pterygoid, left SCM, left posterior neck, and moderate tenderness over the left TMJ, left masseter, left medial pterygoid and the retromandibular areas.  Maximal incisal opening is about 45 mm, opening without significant pain to 35 mm.  Excursion movements are about 10 mm in each direction, but were painful, particularly on excursion towards the right side. 

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence in bilateral maxilla and right mandibular of all bicuspids and molars.  On the left side, just the third molar is missing.  There are crowns on the second bicuspid and first and second molar.  Turbinates appear fairly large.  Sinuses appear clear.  Both joints appear arthritic with sclerosis on the head.  Both have a hockey stick shape to them.  
Coronal view shows the sinuses are clear and turbinates are fairly large.  
Axial view shows that turbinates are moderately large, but sinuses are clear. 
Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that appears arthritic.  There is some sclerosis on the head of the condyle.  The condyle is posteriorly positioned in the fossa with decreased posterior and superior joint space, but with an increased anterior joint space where the articular eminence is moderately steep.  There appears to be a little osteophyte sticking out from the articular eminence.  The head of the condyle is somewhat sclerotic.  There appears to be some peaking of the condyles in a couple of the views, particularly towards the lateral aspect of the joint.
Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that appears a little more arthritic than the opposite side.  There is peaking of the condyle and anterior beaking of the condyle.  Articular eminence is fairly steep.  There is decreased posterior joint space and an increased anterior joint space through most of the joint. 
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with fairly good mediolateral width.  There is decreased vertical joint space.  There is an extension of the lateral pole out laterally.  Lateral rim of the fossa is fairly flat.   
Right condyle appears to have fairly good mediolateral width.  There is decreased superior joint space.  There is a little osteophytic kind of extrusion off the lateral pole of the condyle.  Lateral rim curves inferiorly about 2-3 mm.  
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows an AP dimension between the soft palate and posterior pharyngeal wall of about 7 mm and in the base of the tongue posterior pharyngeal wall of 8-9 mm.  There is a slight increased occlusal plane level in the remaining lower arch.  Margaret has basically a Class I skeletal and occlusal relationship. 

There is some question as the possibility of having an allergic reaction to certain metals.  She has had previous titanium implants to the maxilla and mandible.  In the mandible, she tolerated it for several years before the implant was removed from the right side.  She apparently had 1 or 2 implants in the maxillary left side that caused problems and had to be removed.  She has had numerous teeth extracted because of pain, but there was no definitive pathology noted with the teeth or with the bone.  It is possible that the pain could have been referred pain from the TMJ area.  It may be helpful in preparation for any potential surgery for Margaret to go through hypersensitivity testing to determine if she is allergic to the metals in the TMJ joint devices.  

Basic diagnoses for Margaret would be as follows:    

Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocations. 
Missing mandibular right first and second bicuspids, first, second, and third molars, and missing maxillary bilateral first and second bicuspids and first, second, and third molars.  
Class II cuspid molar relationship.
TMJ pain. 
Headaches. 
Myofascial pain. 
Pain in the teeth and jaws. 
History of Epstein-Barr infection.  
Fibromyalgia.
Rule out hypersensitivity to implant and prosthetic materials. 
Polyarthritis with most all other joints affected.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Hypersensitivity testing at Environmental Center in Dallas. 
Hypersensitivity testing through Rush Memorial Hospital Laboratory in Chicago. 
Consider titanium alloy and chromium cobalt allow implants to forearm.  
CT scan of jaws and jaw joints. 
Surgery. 
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. 
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts. 
Prosthetic reconstruction of the dental arches with consideration for placement of implants and restorative prosthetics versus partial dentures.

Margaret reports that about 30 years ago she had a parrot for about 18 months.  Some time after that, she had problems with getting lumps on her head.  She has also had frequent urinary tract infections.  She also gets pain in her lower right side.  It is possible that a Chlamydia bacteria (psittaci and trachomatis) could also be a causative factor in her situation.

original_note:
ABIMAIL PRATES									05-17-02

Abimail is 45 years old. He is referred by Dr. Al Bishop to us.

Patient Concerns

Primary concerns that Abimail has are as follows:
His teeth don’t fit together.
He has an open bite.
Can wake up with pain in the jaw area.
Headache problem and he sometimes wakes up with headaches.

He doesn’t have any problems he is aware of in reference to clicking and popping in his jaw joints. There are no earaches, ringing in his ears, except occasionally, and he has no vertigo.

Current Medications

Currently he takes no medications.

Patient History

He has no other joint problems. He recently had an injury to his foot, and that still bothers him some. 

Clinical Evaluation

In frontal view, Abimail has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry in the middle and upper third face. Upper lip length is about 20 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 2 mm. Maxillary dental midline is shifted to the right probably 2 mm. Nasal tip is also off to the right probably 1 mm or 2. Mandibular dental midline is shifted off to the right about 2 to 3 mm. Chin is off to the right approximately 4 to 5 mm.

In profile view, Abimail has good facial balance between the upper lip, cheeks, and nose. There could be just slight AP deficiency of possibly 2 to 3 mm. Mandible is retruded probably 10 to 15 mm in the chin area. Chin-neckline is not well-defined, and submental area is quite full.

Intraorally, Abimail has all of lower teeth back through the third molar bilaterally. He has crowns on the mandibular left first and second molars and right first molar. He has some minor spacing between some of his lower teeth. He appears to have adequately attached gingiva. He has a slightly accentuated curve of occlusion in the lower arch.

In the upper arch, he has all of his teeth back through the third molars. There is basically a Class I cuspid relationship on the right side and just slight Class II cuspid on the left side. He has an open bite that extends from the left first bicuspid through the right first bicuspid. The open bite is worse on the left side as compared to the right side.

Maxillary arch actually appears relatively level or the left cuspid may be up about a millimeter. Mandibular arch is asymmetric with the left cuspid/bicuspid being about 2 mm lower than the right side. Lower arch is asymmetric with the right cuspid being almost 2 mm posterior to the left cuspid. Likewise, in the upper arch, the right cuspid is 2 mm posterior to the left cuspid.

TMJs function smoothly.

Oropharynx appears clear. Uvula may be slightly enlarged. However, the posterior faucial pillars appear to be attached more anteriorly and the lateral pharyngeal wall there appears to be good oropharyngeal airway space at least on clinical exam.

Intranasally, the turbinates are moderate-sized, but there appears to be adequate airway.


Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows the presence of all the teeth including the third molars. He has root canals on the mandibular left second molar and mandibular right first molar. Sinuses appear as though there is a mucous retention cyst in the right sinus, but otherwise the sinuses look fairly clear. Left turbinate appears to block the airway. Anterior open bite appears to be present. The condyles look fairly normal. 

Waters view shows that the sinuses appear relatively clear, although there is some increased cloudiness in the right maxillary sinus. Nasal airway looks reasonably clear. Frontal sinuses are huge.

Left TMJ tomogram shows the condyles appear to have fairly good morphology. Joint space looks fairly good though maybe a decreased anterior joint space.

Right TMJ tomogram shows good joint morphology. Articular eminences are fairly steep in both joints. Joint space is good in the right joint around the condyle. 

On opening, there is good translation of both condyles.

Lateral cephalometric evaluation shows evidence of the mucous retention cyst in the maxillary right sinus. Lips are forced together with hyperfunction of the mentalis muscle. Oropharyngeal airway is relatively normal with about 11 mm of airway space. There appears to be a skeletal Class II jaw relationship with overangulation of the lower incisors.

Basic Diagnoses:

Maxillary asymmetry and posterior vertical deficiency.
Mandibular hypoplasia and asymmetry.
AP microgenia.
Anterior open bite of about 5 mm on the left side, about 2 mm anteriorly, and about a millimeter on the right side.
Slight Class II occlusion on the left side.
Arch asymmetry in the maxilla and mandible with the right cuspids being approximately 2 mm posterior to the left cuspids.
Accentuated curve of occlusion, upper arch.
Slight accentuated curved of occlusion, lower arch.
Mandibular left cuspid and bicuspids are vertically lower in the occlusal plane as compared to the opposite side.
Rule out ankylosis of left cuspid/bicuspids.
Over-contour of mandibular left first molar crown with that crown being built up into a previously existing open bite.
Retained third molars x 4.
High occlusal plane angulation.
Probable nocturnal clenching and bruxism.
Bolton’s tooth-size discrepancy with almost a 2-mm difference with the upper teeth being larger compared to the lower teeth.
Headaches.
Myofascial pain.

Recommended Treatment

Consider using Klonopin to control nocturnal clenching

Redo crown on mandibular left first molar to build it slightly out of occlusion, creating an open bite in that area with a temporary crown (the current crown was built to put the tooth into occlusion when it probably originally was in an open bite position)

Presurgical orthodontics
Align and level maxillary arch. Place compensating step in the maxillary arch wire between the cuspids and the laterals to align the anterior four teeth at an elevated level compared to the cuspids and the posterior teeth. Try to extrude the cuspids inferiorly some, particularly on the left side.
Align and level mandibular arch. Be careful because of possible ankylosis of the left cuspid/first bicuspids (PDL is not as clear on the left side as the right side, and, thus, the teeth are slightly submerged compared to the opposite arch).

Surgery
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to level the occlusal plane, correct asymmetries, downgraft the posterior aspect, and move the cuspid area upwards.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to shift the mandible back on the facial midline and close the open bite.
Osseous augmentation genioplasty.
Consider removal of third molars if desired from an orthodontic standpoint.

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

Restorative dentistry to place permanent crown on the mandibular left first molar

GLORIA REED									06-07-04

Gloria is 64 years old and referred by Dr. Mike Steffen, 1601 S. Blvd., Edmond, OK  73013.

Patient History

She had previous colon cancer and had a sigmoid resection in 09/1999. She takes Miacalcin 1 spray per day. She is allergic to “SOLAMEDIAL.”

Gloria’s TMJ problems have an unknown onset and unknown etiology. She has seen Dr. Metcalf and Dr. Hough in reference to her ear problems, and they cannot find anything specific from their standpoint as to the cause of the hearing loss except perhaps association with the TMJ. She has no problems with earaches but has significant problems with tinnitus with the right side being significantly greater than the left side. No problems with vertigo, but she has significant hearing loss on the right side. Other joints that bother her include her right shoulder, right elbow, and right wrist. She is unaware of whether she has any problems with clenching and bruxism at nighttime, but she is sure she doesn’t have any daytime factors. She gets headaches about once every two to three weeks, and they occur in the frontal and temporal area bilaterally at about a level of 4. She recently has developed pain in the right TMJ at about a level of 5. On the average day, she really doesn’t have much pain around the jaws and claims no pain on an average day around the head and neck area. She rates her jaw function at 2, diet at 2, and disability at 0. The major concern she has is that when she sleeps she has to sleep on her back now because of pain on her right side and she used to sleep on the right side all the time. She does notice that before she goes to sleep she feels as though she has difficulties breathing and has to take a deep breath. Once she is asleep, her symptoms are not quite as significant. She does have snoring problems. 

She has significant hearing loss, particularly in the right ear. This is interfering with her abilities to do her job as a transcriptionist. It has become increasingly difficult for her to transcribe recorded work. 

Patient Concerns

TMJ problems
Tinnitus
Headaches 
Hearing loss
Sleep apnea symptoms 
Difficulties eating and chewing some foods

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Gloria has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 21 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is -2 mm. Maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline is shifted off to the left a couple of millimeters. Chin is pretty much on the facial midline. 

In profile view, Gloria has probably about 3 or 4 mm of maxillary AP deficiency. Chin is retruded about 10 to 12 mm. Chin-neckline is extremely short, and chin-neck angle is not well-defined. As she postures her mandible forward as far as she can, it does improve her overall facial balance although the chin-neckline is still not really well-defined.

Intraorally, she is missing the mandibular right first and second bicuspids but has all the rest of her teeth back through the third molars. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the third molars. Has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship on the right side and a full Class II cuspid relationship on the left side. She has an anterior deep bite of probably 7 mm. She has an accentuated curve of occlusion in the lower arch and in the upper arch may have just a slight reverse curve but not too significant. 

Intranasally, the airway looks fairly good. The septum may be deviated a little bit off toward the right side. She had a skin cancer removed from the right side of her nose, and there is a little bit of a cave in on the right side making her right luminal valve somewhat constricted, although the left side looks pretty much normal. Turbinates are relatively moderate in size, and there appears to be a good airway back inside the nose. 

Maximum opening is 52 mm. Excursive movements are about 12 mm in each direction. On excursive movements to the right, she has pain in the right TMJ area. On opening, her mandible does deviate to the right a couple of millimeters. On opening, she has an opening click at about 15 mm and a closing clicking at about 10 mm. She has crepitation in both TMJs that is actually audible. To palpation, she has some mild tenderness over the right temporalis muscle but no other pain to palpation. 

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows that the mandibular right first and second bicuspids are missing. Crowns appear a little bit smaller than normal size. Two pontics are part of a five-unit bridge. She has a root canal on the mandibular right second molar and mandibular left third molar. She has crowns on all three posterior teeth in the left mandible and also has a crown on the maxillary left second molar. Sinuses look clear. Nasal airway looks reasonably good. She has a significant amount of arthritis in the right TMJ area and to a lesser extent in the left TMJ area. Styloid process looks quite long and calcified on the left side and somewhat shorter on the right side. No significant pathology is seen. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows a lot of sclerosis within the joint. Not a very good image of the joint itself. Some anterior beaking is seen. Condyle appears somewhat sclerotic. 

Left TMJ condyle shows up on one view. Condyle appears narrow anteroposteriorly. Decreased posterior joint space and increased anterior joint space. No flattening on the anterior head. Styloid process appears fairly long and overlaps the posterior border of the mandible.

On opening, there appears to be good translation of both condyles, and the condyles are not in really good focus. Translation on the left side is better than the right side. 

Waters view shows that the sinuses appear clear, and the nasal airway appears clear with just moderate size of the turbinates.

Cephalometrically, her head is tipped up some. Oropharyngeal airway appears to be moderate in size. At the level of the soft palate, the airway dimension is about 9 mm and at the tongue base is about 9 mm. Appears to have a Class II skeletal and occlusal relationship. Has an anterior deep bite. She has a tooth-size discrepancy, and if we include the increased widths of the upper incisors, there is probably about 2 mm. Left mandibular bicuspids are larger compared to the upper left bicuspids. Right mandibular bicuspids pontics are smaller than the upper cuspids. 
Basic Diagnoses

Right TMJ severe arthritis and articular disc dislocation without reduction and significant inflammatory tissue surrounding the condyle
Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with reduction
AP mandibular deficiency
AP maxillary deficiency
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency
Slight vertical maxillary deficiency		SAME AS #5?
Class II end-on occlusion 
Anterior deep bite of 7 mm
Mandibular dental midline shifted 2 mm to the left of the chin midline and facial midline
Missing right mandibular first and second bicuspids replaced by pontics that are smaller than the actual original tooth size
Grossly enlarged mandibular right cuspid crown
Accentuated curve of occlusion, lower arch
Mild crowding, lower anterior arch
Left first and second bicuspids slightly enlarged compared to the left maxillary bicuspids
High occlusal plane angle
Retained third molars
Difficulties eating and chewing
Decreased oropharyngeal airway of approximately 6 mm (normal dimension 11 mm)
Right TMJ pain
Headaches 
Severe tinnitus 
Severe hearing loss, right side
Sleep apnea symptoms 
Snoring

Recommended Treatment

Decrease size of crown on the mandibular right cuspid to equal the size of the left cuspid. Keep cuspid aligned with the bicuspids and the space created leave it anteriorly between the cuspid and right lateral incisor

Presurgical orthodontics 
Align and level maxillary arch 
Align and level mandibular arch. Close up space on the right side and try to shift the mandibular dental midline 1 to 2 mm to the right. Consider stripping lower left first and second bicuspids to reduce the mesial distal width to make it more compatible with the maxillary bicuspids. Strip lower anterior teeth removing about a millimeter of tooth structure to help compensate for the tooth-size discrepancy of 1 to 2 mm

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ condylectomies, debridement, meniscectomies, reconstruction, and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla and downgraft it posteriorly
Bilateral coronoidectomies
Bilateral fat grafts to the TMJs (harvest from the abdomen)

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine and retain occlusion 

Restorative dentistry as necessary to achieve the best interdigitation which could include remaining of the bridge on the right side
