original_note:
B255 
Met with him and his mother at my home for about 2-1/2 hours discussing his conditions and recommended treatment. Reviewed the MRIs showing the arthritis and also the indicators of a displaced articular disc that does reduce bilaterally. Doesn't have many TMJ symptoms but does at times get some pain in his jaw joints. Has no problems with headaches, earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, or any of those factors as previously recorded. He and his mother definitely agree that the mandible has grown off to the left side in the last couple of years, and it has been a couple of years since his completed his previous orthodontics. Discussed the pros and cons of doing the joint procedure. If the high condylectomies are not done, he has a good probability of continuing to grow on the right side. If the discs are not repositioned, then there is a risk of worsening arthritis particularly since he already has arthritic changes present in both joints. Discussed activities postsurgery as well as dietary restrictions. Discussed the necessity for doing both jaws in order to get the best functional and aesthetic result as compared to doing just the lower jaw or just the upper jaw. Discussed the locations of incisions. The biggest problem affecting them in the financial aspect and the fact that I am not on their plan. They have already met with Dr. . Dr. has told them that he doesn't do joint work. Suggested that possibly Pedro Franco is probably on their plan and would be a possibility for them if the financial factors will control whom they will see. I answered all the questions that B255 and his mother had at this time. Discussed at length sinuses problems, turbinates, etc. The mother, who is an allergist, is more concerned about the middle turbinates. A CAT scan apparently was done that I have not seen, but she says that there are some abnormalities in the middle turbinates. I told her that those could be reduced at the time of surgery. Apparently Dr. told them that the middle turbinates can't be approached during the procedure. I also strongly suggested that the inferior turbinates be reduced in size, and the mother is concerned as to how much is removed as she says she has experienced people who have developed atrophic rhinitis as a result of that treatment. I think likely those are patients that may have been on long-term steroids or other nasal conditions prior to the reduction turbinectomies. They are to contact me if they have any other questions or concerns.

B255  CT Scan Evaluation
Date of Acquisition:	
Date of Reading: 
On CT of the sinuses, he has extremely large frontal sinuses particularly on the right side where the sinus extends the entire way out to the lateral orbital rim. On the right side, the sinuses are quite a bit smaller but still extend probably almost half way across the orbit. Appears to have a sinusitis in the left frontal sinus where there is some definite thickening, perhaps a mucous retention cyst in the sinus toward the lateral aspect. Maxillary sinuses are quite large. There is no evidence of any sinusitis there. The ethmoid sinuses are also quite large with more septae on the left side than the right side. The inferior turbinates are hyperplastic particularly on the right side. The right turbinate almost totally obliterates the right nasal airway. Left turbinate is quite large, but there is better airway passage on that side. The middle turbinates have a very abnormal shape to them where they actually curve in toward the lateral nasal bones and have essentially a reverse curvature associated with them. The ostium is located quite high beneath the superior aspect of the middle turbinates. The nasal septum is relatively straight. Only the right TMJ is seen in the coronal view, and it shows a significantly decreased joint space vertically. Lateral pole of the condyle is located quite inferior to the medial pole. Impression:
Normal-appearing bilateral maxillary sinuses and bilateral ethmoid sinuses
Extremely large maxillary right frontal sinus but clear of pathology
Left frontal sinus mucous retention cyst versus thickening of the lining at the lateral aspect of the sinus
Hypertrophied inferior turbinates with the right side being extremely large and almost obliterative of the nasal airway
Bilateral middle turbinates with significantly abnormal pathology with a reverse curvature and hyperplasia

Recommendations:
Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies
Bilateral partial middle turbinectomies
B255  MRI Evaluation 
Date of Acquisition:	
Date of Reading:

Left TMJ, coronal view, shows a condyle that is a little bit hard to identify. Head may be overrotated. Condyle appears a little bit narrow. Joint space is difficult to determine. Disc position is difficult to determine. Does appear that there is a decreased joint space vertically.

Right TMJ, coronal view, shows a condyle with good mediolateral width, and the condyle appears larger than the opposite side. Appears to be significantly decreased joint space. Articular disc may be medially displaced. Cortical bone appears to be intact on the condyle and in the fossa.

Axial views show that both condyles are fairly broad mediolaterally. Cannot identify the disc on these views.
Left TMJ, sagittal view, shows a condyle that out laterally appears to have a fairly good shape but toward the middle and medial aspect there is anterior beaking present. Decreased vertical joint space is present. Difficult to tell where the articular disc is but appears that it may be anteriorly and medially displaced. Appears to be a little bit of fluid in the superior joint space.

On opening, there is restricted translation. Appears that the disc may be in position. Condyle looks narrow anteroposteriorly compared to the opposite side.

Right TMJ, sagittal view, shows a condyle that appears a little bit broader anteroposteriorly compared to the opposite side. Out laterally there appears to be a little bit of flattening on the anterior head of the condyle. Appears to be decreased joint space superiorly. Articular disc appears to be anteriorly displaced sitting beneath the articular eminence. Articular eminence is relatively shallow in its slope. On opening, there is a question as to whether the articular disc reduces or not.
Impression:
Left TMJ arthritis and possibly anteromedial displacement of the articular disc Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation and possibly nonreducing

B255 
B255 is 17 years old.
Patient History
As far as noting any problems, this has only been within the last one to two years. B255 has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, or headaches. He gets periodic TMJ discomfort, more so on the left side. B255 has undergone significant previous orthodontics with Dr. __. He had ortho for about five or six years and had palatal expanders and a number of different appliances. B255 originally did have a lot of difficulties eating and chewing because of a crossbite problem. Dr. ____ retired, and they transferred to Dr. ____. At the completion of orthodontic treatment approximately two years ago, the bite was satisfactory. Since then, there has been a significant shift of the lower jaw off toward the left side and development of an anterior crossbite. He has no headache problems. He gets periodic TMJ discomfort, the last time being a couple of weeks ago that registered at about a level of 4 predominantly on the left side. On an average day, he really doesn't have any significant pain or headache problem. He rates his jaw function at 2 and diet and disability at 0. He does have a significant history of sinus problems and difficulties breathing through his nose. He does experience significant problems with his ears when he flies probably due to change in air pressure. He has difficulties clearing his ears after plane flights. Recent tympanogram revealed that there was fluid in just the left ear.

Clinical Examination
In frontal view, B255 has fairly good facial symmetry in the middle and upper third face. Upper lip length is about 20 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is 2 mm. Maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline is shifted off to the left side approximately 3 mm. Mandibular dental midline and chin are fairly well aligned with each other. Therefore, chin is also shifted off to the left side about 3 mm.

In profile view, B255 has probably about 4 or 5 mm of AP deficiency in the maxilla. In the upper orbital area, may be just slightly retruded by about 2 to 3 mm. Chin also may be just slightly AP deficient by about 3 to 4 mm. Chin-neckline appears fairly normal in length and chin-neck angle is fairly well-defined.

Intraorally, B255 has all of his lower teeth back through the second molars. He has a 3 x 3 retainer in the lower anterior arch. Has fairly good attached gingiva around the lower anterior teeth. A little bit of inflammation about his lower anteriors. In the upper arch, B255 has all of his teeth back through the second molars present. B255 has a Class III cuspid relationship on the right side by about 4 mm and on the left side by probably about 2 mm. Maxilla appears fairly symmetric in anteroposterior position. Slight cant in the transverse plane with the left side being about a millimeter lower than the right side. Oropharynx appears clear. May have a bifid uvula present, but otherwise everything looks relatively normal. Appears to have good oropharyngeal airway space. B255 has a 0 over bite relationship. Is tending toward a crossbite on the left side, and he has an anterior crossbite tendency particularly on the left side. Right side central and lateral incisor are end-on with the lower lateral incisor and cuspid.

Intranasally, the nasal septum is deviated a little bit of toward the right side but not really significantly. Right turbinate is quite enlarged creating a decreased airway on that side. Left inferior turbinate is moderate in size, but there appears to be a little bit greater airway on the left side as compared to the right side. Both turbinates could be hyperplastic and causing some airway problems. Cannot see posteriorly to evaluate the airway dimension at that level.

Radiographic Evaluation
Panorex shows that all four third molars are absent. Sinuses appear reasonably clear. Appears there could be some obstruction in the left nasal airway more toward the posterior aspect. Right side shows a turbinate that is enlarged, but it appears there is a little bit of airway at the base of the nose. Has a 3 x 3 retainer in the lower arch. Right condyle appears larger than the left one both in anteroposterior width and in vertical height.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that actually appears narrow anteroposteriorly compared to the size of the fossa. Articular eminence is moderately shallow. Decreased superior joint space indicative of possible anterior and/or medial displacement of the articular disc. Occlusion is Class III.

Left TMJ tomogram shows that there is a decreased superior and posterior joint space. Condyle appears somewhat narrow anteroposteriorly compared to the size of the fossa. Articular eminence is relatively shallow. Less of a Class III occlusal tendency on this side.

On opening, there appears to be a greater restriction of translation on the left side as compared to the right side, although both of they are a little bit shy of translating onto the apex of the articular eminence.

Waters view demonstrates that the septum may be a little bit of toward the right side. Right turbinate is extremely large and essentially obliterates the right nasal airway. Left side does demonstrate some passable airway. There is either enlargement of the middle turbinate or a polyp present in the right side. It most likely may be the middle turbinate. Sinuses look relatively clear and equally lucent on either side.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that he has a skeletal Class III relationship. Has and end-on incisor relationship. Vertical height of the mandible is fairly symmetric with just about a 2-mm difference at the inferior borders. Oropharyngeal airway looks relatively normal. Soft tissues thickness at pogonion is decreased. Tongue looks relatively normal size. Maximal incisal opening for B255 is 41 mm and about 36 mm without discomfort. Excursive movement to the right is 6 mm and to the left 7 mm.

Basic Diagnoses
Right TMJ condylar hyperplasia and medial displacement of the articular disc Left TMJ arthritis and medial displacement of the articular disc AP maxillary hypoplasia
Mandibular deviate prognathism
Class III occlusion on the right side and moderate Class III occlusion on the left side
Anterior crossbite and developing left posterior crossbite
TMJ pain
Hypertrophied nasal turbinates
Nasal airway obstruction
Chronic sinus problems secondary to nasal airway obstruction

Recommended Treatment
Presurgical orthodontics Align and level maxillary arch. Keep the lateral incisor roots tipped mesially and cuspid roots tipped distally. May need to tip the right lateral incisor root more mesial than it currently is to provide adequate room for interdental osteotomies
Align and level mandibular arch

Surgery
Right TMJ high condylectomy and articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor
Left TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance, expand, and level the occlusal plane
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to align the mandible back on the facial midline
Bilateral partial turbinectomies

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion
B255 
Telephone conversation. Last week he had a couple of days when he had increased clicking in his jaw joints where it would click about every hour. This was slightly uncomfortable, but not too bad. This week he is feeling better. He is going to get a night guard made. He has an Essex retainer which is an equal thickness over the occlusal surfaces and it is not good for his occlusion. I told him that could intrude the posterior teeth creating a posterior open bite for him. He understands. He does have an appointment to met the splint man and I told him to be sure that all of the teeth touch in the splint. He has not been taking Klonopin. I explained to him what the problems are that are going on. I explained to him that since his mother did not allow us to use a Mitek anchor that the system we had to use is not strong and supportive of the disc. With his clenching and bruxism that could tear any system apart. Hopefully, he will get better with the splint. Otherwise, he is doing well and enjoying dental school. He is to keep me informed of his progress

MARY LEAHY ENBOM								11/14/2007

Mary is 41 years old and is referred by Dr. William Schmohl, San Rafael, CA.  Mary has been diagnosed with fibromyalgia, irritable bowel syndrome and allergies.  She had gum surgery in March of 2007.  

CURRENT MEDICATIONS:
Flexeril 10 mg t.i.d. 
Doxepin 20 mg daily.
Ambien 2.5 mg q.h.s.
BCP.

She has had an adverse reaction to penicillin. 

PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Severe jaw pain.
Headaches. 
Bite does not fit together very well.

Mary gets headaches about 2 to 4 times per week at about a level of 5 and about once a month she gets severe headaches that range about 9 that occur predominately in the frontal and temporal areas.  She rates her TMJ pain at 9, average daily pain at 7, jaw function at 5, diet at 3 and disability at 7.  She gets occasional earache and tinnitus.  She has moderate problems with vertigo.  No hearing loss and no other joints bother her.  She has been diagnosed with fibromyalgia about 15 years ago.  She has had irritable bowel syndrome for around 7 years. 

In frontal view, Mary has fairly good facial symmetry in the middle and upper third face.  Upper lip length is about 20 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 4 mm.  Maxillary midline is pretty close to being on the facial midline just about 1 mm to the left.  The nasal tip is a little bit off towards the right side about 2 mm.  Mandibular dental midline and chin are fairly well aligned with each other and with the facial midline.  In centric relation, however, the lower jaw is off to the left side about 1.5 mm at the incisor edge and also the chin.  Intraorally, Mary has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars.  Teeth are fairly nicely aligned and leveled.  She has good attached gingiva.  She has some gingival clefting around the centrals and lateral incisors particularly the right lateral and also the cuspids.  In the upper arch she is missing first bicuspids.  In centric relation she has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship being a little bit worse on the left side than the right side.  She has a tendency towards buckle crossbites.  There is about a 3 mm overjet and 0 vertical overlap.  In centric relation she occludes only on the first and second molars on the right side and all other teeth are out of occlusion.  Mandibular first molars are poorly developed relative to the enamel on them and will require crowning at a later time.  Oropharyngeal tissues look healthy although the uvula is moderately elongated, soft palate mildly elongated and faucial pillars are moderately constricted.  Tonsils appear normal.  Intranasally, the septum is deviated a little bit off to the left side.  Turbinates are moderate in size with the left one being a little bit larger.  A little bit more constricture of the airway on the left side as compared to the right side.  She does have some moderate daytime somnolence that she relates to fibromyalgia as well as moderate sleep disorder.  
MARY ENBOM				11/14/2007				Page 2

Joints seem to function reasonably smoothly.  Maximal incisal opening is 45 mm.  She can open to 18 mm without much discomfort.  Excursive movements are about 12 mm in each direction.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION:
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars and the maxillary first bicuspids.  Sinuses appear relatively clear.  Mary had hair extensions done for her wedding a year ago and some of them are still in place and show up on the radiographs.  

In the waters view, sinuses are clear and the bony septum is fairly straight.  The left turbinate appears a little bit enlarged. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows what appears to be reasonably good space around the joint.  Anterior head is a little bit flat and there is a little bit of anterior beaking and a little superior peaking.  

Left TMJ tomogram is a little bit harder to read.  There appears to be some abnormal shape to the condyle.  The joint space does not look too bad although the posterior joint space appears decreased.  On opening, there is good translation of both condyles.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that the maxillary and mandible are both moderately retruded.  Oropharyngeal airway, however, is relatively normal with about 10 mm dimension.  

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation particularly at the lateral aspect of the joints.
Transverse maxillary excess.
Slight AP maxillary deficiency. 
AP mandibular deficiency. 
Class II end-on occlusion.
Occluding only on the right first and second molars.
Missing maxillary first bicuspids extracted for previous orthodontic work.
TMJ pain.
Headaches.
 Myofacial pain. 
 Fibromyalgia. 
 Nocturnal and daytime clenching and bruxism.
 Periodontal problems associated with lower first molars and anterior teeth. 

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT: 
Periodontal treatment is required in the lower arch.




MARY ENBOM				11/14/2007				Page 3

Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies.  Include the second molars in the archwire.
Align and level mandibular arch.  Include the second molars in the archwire.
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move it superiorly slightly and narrow it transversely.
Mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction. 
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

CRAIG OGLESBY									02/27/2009

Craig is 23 years old, referred by Dr. Moody Alexander in Arlington.  Primary concerns Craig has are as follows: 

Difficulties breathing through the nose. 
Teeth and jaws are malaligned. 
Some difficulties chewing foods. 
Some digestion problems related to inability to chew foods properly.
Snoring. 
Moderate to severe daytime tiredness. 

Craig has no problems with TMJ pain, headaches, or myofascial pain.  He has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, nor vertigo.  He is not aware of clenching or bruxing.  No other joints bother him.  Craig is aware of having some snoring problems, moderate to severe daytime tiredness, but no sleep apnea and no sleep disorder.  He is apparently a mouth breather at nighttime and also during the day.  He takes no medications.  

Craig had orthodontics when he was 7 to 8 years of age and then from the age of 10 to 12.   According to his mother, Craig has always had jaw malalignment issues similar to what it is now, although it has gotten somewhat worse.  

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Craig has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is 24 mm, and tooth lip relationship is zero.  The maxillary and mandibular dental midlines are all pretty much aligned with each other as is the chin.  In profile view, Craig has fairly good facial balance.  Lower jaw is slightly strong and upper jaw is just slightly weak by perhaps 3 to 4 mm.  Lower jaw, chin neckline, and chin neck angle are in fairly good balance.  Intraorally, Craig has all his lower teeth back through the second molars.  He has got crowns on the left second bicuspid and first second molars.  He has root canals I believe in all four posterior teeth on that side.  The cuspids are slightly elevated compared to the rest of the teeth and the four incisors are somewhat depressed compared to the cuspids.  He has good attached gingiva.  The lower incisors appear to be in slightly more inclination.  In the upper arch, he already has his appliances on up there.  He has all of his teeth back through the second molars and has appliances on all of the teeth back through the second molars.  The upper arch is fairly well aligned.  Craig has a Class III end-on cuspid relationship bilaterally, being probably about 4 mm Class III.  He has anterior and bilateral posterior cross bites.  He has a reverse overbite of about 0.5 mm.  He has probably got about a 5 mm reverse overjet.  Uvula is extremely long and may be partly responsible for the snoring.  The soft palate may be mild to moderately elongated, but not too significant.  Fascial pillars and tonsils appear relatively normal.  Intranasally, turbinates appear enlarged.  The septum appears reasonably straight.  Joints seem to function reasonably smoothly.  No myofascial pain problems around the head and neck area. 

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  I-CAT coronal view shows that there are hypertrophied turbinates.  Septum appears reasonably straight.  He is missing all four third molars.  There is some movement in the imaging.  Condyles seem in reasonably good shape.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that he has got a Class III skeletal relationship.  Oropharyngeal airway looks good.  

Basic diagnoses for Craig would be as follows:    

Mandibular prognathism. 
Maxillary AP and transverse hypoplasia.
Class III end-on occlusion. 
Anterior and bilateral posterior crossbite.
Significant tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of 3 mm.
Slight reverse curve of Spee in the lower arch.
Excessively wide maxillary incisors labial lingually.
Hypertrophied turbinates. 
Nasal airway obstruction. 
Hypertrophied uvula. 
Low occlusal plane angle of 4 degrees (normal 8 degrees).

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Presurgical orthodontics.  
Align and level maxillary arch.  Open up at least 1 mm of space between the upper laterals and cuspids to help correct for the tooth size problem.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. 
Align and level mandibular arch.  Try to either depress the cuspids slightly and/or elevate the incisors slightly to help level the lower arch.  
Surgery. 
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the posterior aspect superiorly to increase occlusal plane angle, expand, and advance it about 4 to 5 mm.    
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to rotate the mandible in a clockwise direction.
Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies. 
Uvulectomy.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
PETER MYHRE									10/28/2009

Peter is 62 years old and referred by Dr. Perry Goldberg.   Peter’s primary concerns at this time include the following:

Daily headaches that occur usually in the afternoon between 2:00 and 5:00 p.m. 
Facial numbness after 3:00 p.m. 
Ear pain, being worse on the left side.
Ringing in the ears.
Difficulty swallowing. 
Cervical issues particularly involving the disks at C4 and C5.

Peter’s problems began in 1996.  Peter’s current problems began in December 2008 when he developed headaches.  He has been treated with medications and has had bite splints previously, but has a new one that he got just about 3 weeks ago.  Peter feels best in the morning, but moving and activity, particularly talking or other functions, will induce the headaches, although they usually begin between 2:00 and 5:00 in the afternoon.  Talking certainly worsens the headache situation.  

Peter began having joint problems, particularly degenerative lumbar disks, in 1996.  He had to quit a lot of his activities because of the pain.  He was treated with medications, particularly opiates, but by September 2008 he pretty much had weaned himself off of the opiates.  He had a previous bite plate from about 1999 to 2004 that he wore basically at night.  He usually feels best in the morning.  He has had MRIs of the brain and body and everything is okay except for the cervical and lumbar problems.  He gets pain in the left ear area particularly in the mornings.  He feels he has to move and manipulate his jaws to release the ear canals.  Peter gets headaches daily in the afternoon at about a level of 7 that affect the frontal and temporal areas.  He does not have any specific TMJ pain, but his average daily pain reaches 7.  He rates his jaw function at 3, diet at 1, disability at 7.  

He takes the following medications:

Tenormin 50 mg 2 times per day. 
Aspirin 81 mg 3 times a week.
Verapamil 40 mg 2 times per day.
Temazepam 30 mg q.d.
Amitriptyline 10 mg t.i.d.

Peter used to chew a lot of gum and would do that for a few hours a day.  Now he has discontinued that.  Other joints that bother him include his back, neck, right shoulder, and his left toes particularly when he swims. 

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Peter has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 26 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about -2.  The maxillary, mandibular, and chin midline are all fairly well aligned with each other.  In profile view, Peter has some AP maxillary deficiency of probably about 8 mm at the incisal edge and a good 10-12 mm in the chin.  Chin neckline is short.  Intraorally, Peter has all of his lower teeth back through the second molars present.  The lower incisors are in lingual inclination.  He has heterotopic bone around the outside of his teeth, particularly around the anterior teeth from the cuspid through the incisors.  In the upper arch, he has all of his teeth back through the second molars.  Peter has a Class I cuspid-molar relationship, however, the maxillary and mandibular incisors are tipped lingually.  He does have an anterior deep bite of about 3-4 mm.  Peter has had 2 previous surgeries, one to repair a septal perforation with a temporal muscle flap and the second procedure occurred when he was in college and sustained a left zygomatic orbital fracture.  To palpation, Peter has got moderate pain in the temporalis and TMJs and milder pain to the masseters, medial pterygoids, and retromandibular area.  On opening, he had a definite click in the left joint at about 10 mm of opening and about 5 mm of closing.  In the right joint, it functioned a little bit more smoothly, but occasionally he would have a loud audible pop.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows that Peter is missing the maxillary third molars and the mandibular left third molar, but he does have a mandibular right third molar in place.  Sinuses look relatively clear.  Turbinates appear moderate in size.  It is difficult to evaluate the joints.    
Coronal views show the sinuses are clear.  Inferior turbinates look actually quite large in the posterior aspect of the nasal airway.    
Axial view shows that the sinuses are clear and the turbinates appear large in the posterior two-thirds of the nasal area.  
Left TMJ sagittal tomograms show a condyle that seems to have fairly good morphology with a little bit of anterior beaking in one area.  Articular eminence is moderate in inclination and relatively normal in length.  Joint space appears fairly well balanced. 
Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology.  Joint space appears fairly balanced.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  
Left TMJ coronal tomograms show that there is a little extrusion of the lateral pole of the condyle in a lateral direction.  There is decreased joint space laterally which could be indicative of a medial displacement of the disc.  The mid part of the joint appears to have fairly good joint space.     
Right TMJ coronal tomogram shows a condyle that has a mild extension of the lateral pole of the condyle.  There is decreased lateral joint space indicative of medial displacement of the disc.  
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Peter has AP maxillary and mandibular deficiency, however, the oropharyngeal airway is reasonably good with an AP dimension of about 9 mm at the soft palate level and about 7 to 8 mm at the base of the tongue.  He has a skeletal occlusal Class I jaw relationship, although the incisors are in significant vertical orientation. 

Basic diagnoses for Peter would be as follows:    

Bilateral TMJ articular disc dislocation with reduction. 
Maxillary AP and vertical hypoplasia.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia.
Class I occlusion but with an anterior deep bite of 3-4 mm. 
Attrition of the anterior teeth to the bicuspids.  
TMJ pain. 
Headaches. 
Myofascial pain. 
Maxillary and mandibular incisors in lingual inclination.

Recommended treatment would be as follows with three treatment options.

Option 1. 
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors. 
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to maintain the current mandibular position and occlusion.  
Option 2. 
Presurgical orthodontics. 
Align and level maxillary arch.  Increase inclination of the maxillary incisors.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. 
Align and level mandibular arch.  Increase inclination of the lower incisors.  
Surgery. 
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible forward approximately 12 mm.
Maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla forward approximately 8 mm with a counterclockwise rotation. 
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion. 
Option 3. 
Surgery Stage 1.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors. 
Construction of bite splint to protect the occlusion in the joints.
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch.  Increase the inclination of the maxillary incisors.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. 
Align and level mandibular arch.  Increase the inclination of the lower incisors.  
Surgery Stage 2.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible forward 12 mm in a counterclockwise direction. 
Maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla approximately 8 mm at the incisal edge with a counterclockwise rotation. 
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B098
B098 is 44 years old and referred by Dr. XXXX. For the most part, she is in good health. About 2 years ago she began developing some problems in her jaw joints of unknown etiology. She is not aware of any trauma or other insightful factors that may have started the problem. She has been in orthodontic treatment for the last 18 months. Once Dr. XXXX got her started in treatment, the headache and joint pains that she was having prior to then went away. She did pretty well for quite some time, but has had 2 episodes since then of developing pain and headache problems associated with her jaw joints and back of her head. She has had her previous sleep study that was normal.

B098 rates her headaches at 0, TMJ pain at 3, average daily pain at 1, jaw function 1, diet at 4, disability at 2. She has no earaches, tinnitus, vertigo nor hearing loss. Other joints that bother her include her shoulders and hips sometimes. She has had previous surgery on both knees, but they are doing very well. She is aware of clenching and bruxism at nighttime and she is aware of clenching during the day, too.

In frontal view, she has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 18 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is 0. The maxillary midline may be about 1 mm off to the left. The mandibular dental midline is fairly close to being on the facial midline. The chin is shifted about 3 mm to the left. She does have almost a full Class II occlusion and she has an anterior open bite in centric relation. In centric relation, she occludes only on the first bicuspids on the right side and all the rest of her teeth are out of occlusion. She has about a 2 mm open bite anteriorly and probably 2 mm posterior on the left side, 3 mm on the right side. In profile view, she has a little fullness in the upper lip by about 2 to 3 mm. The chin is retruded about 10 to 12 mm. The chin neckline is short, although the chin neck angle is reasonably well defined. She has hyperfunctioning mentalis muscle. She has a head postured forward neck position. In centric relation, she actually has a fairly good Class I cuspid-molar relationship, although buccally she is a little bit wide on the left side where her maxillary lingual cuspids touch almost on top of the lower buccal cuspid. She has good attached gingiva around the teeth. Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively normal and healthy. The uvula is slightly elongated. B098 does not report any difficulties breathing through her nose. B098 has a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the left side being 2 to 3 mm higher than the right side. There is some upper arch asymmetry with the maxillary left cuspid being at least 2 mm posterior to the right cuspid and the mandibular left cuspid is about 1 mm posterior to the right cuspid. The left inferior border is about 2 to 3 mm elevated compared to the right side. There is tenderness over the masseter muscles. The incisal opening is 56 mm but only 26 without pain and discomfort. The excursion movements are 10 mm to the right and 11 mm to the left. It does appear on clinical photos that B098 does have a slight yaw towards the right side of probably a couple of millimeters.

Basic diagnoses for B098 would be as follows:
Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly dislocated without reduction. Right TMJ medial displacement of the articular disc. AP mandibular hypoplasia. Slight AP maxillary hyperplasia and posterior vertical hypoplasia. AP microgenia. Class II end-on occlusion, in centric relation occluding only on the right first bicuspids. Anterior and bilateral posterior open bites with occlusion only on the right first bicuspids. Missing mandibular right first molar, maxillary 1st molars and left 2nd biculpid. Decreased oropharyngeal airway of 5 mm (normal 11 mm). Moderate increased occlusal plane angle. TMJ pain. Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1. Presurgical orthodontics. a.	Continue to align and level maxillary arch. b.	Continue to align and level mandibular arch.
2. Surgery. a.	Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning with ligament repair with Mitek anchors. b.	Bilateral mandibular ramus sagittal split osteotomies to advance the mandible into a centric occlusion position.
3. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
4. Restorative dentistry to reconstruct the occlusion.

B098 MRI AGE: 44/F
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears arthritic with some dips in the coronal surface and elevation in the mid part of the condyle and decreased vertical joint space. The condyle extends out even with the lateral rim of the fossa. There appears to be discal tissue probably anteriorly and medially displaced. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly only about 1 mm or so.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has better morphology than the opposite side. The lateral rim of the fossa curves probably about 2 mm. The articular disc is medially displaced.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that appears arthritic, particularly at the lateral aspect of the joint. There is a little bit of anterior beaking present towards the lateral aspect. The articular eminence has a moderate inclination. There is increased superior and posterior joint spaces. The articular disc is anteriorly displaced and sitting in front of the condyle. The disc degenerated and foreshortened anteroposteriorly. There is a little bit of fluid in the superior joint space. There appears to be some edema in the retro discal area. On opening, there is good translation of the condyle, but the disc appears to remain anteriorly displaced without reduction.

Right TMJ sagittal view show a condyle that has better morphology. The articular eminence is relatively shallow. The articular disc appears to be in position. The condyle appears postured forward in the fossa. On opening, there is good translation of the condyle and the disc. \

IMPRESSION:
Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly displaced without reduction.
Right TMJ medial displacement of the articular disc.

original_note:
MARK KRAMERPUGH							10/10/2011

Mark is 54 years old, birth date is 04/09/1957.  Referred by Max Ebrahimian, DDS. 

Mark’s primary concerns are as follows:

Severe sleep apnea.
Clicking in the left jaw area.

Mark was diagnosed with sleep apnea about 4 or 5 years ago.  He is aware of having clicking in his jaw joints up to10 years ago of unknown etiology.  He is on 3 medications that include:

Ambien.
Toprol (BP) 50 mg q.d.
Celexa 20 mg q.d.
Flonase nasal spray.

Mark does not really have any significant problems with headaches.  He rates his TMJ pain at about a level of 3, average daily pain at 3, jaw function at 2, diet at 1, disability at 5.  He does have some joints that bother him including his knees, ankles, and feet, but he relates this to his physical athletic commitments.  He is aware of clenching at nighttime.  

In frontal view, Mark has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 27 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is probably about -2 to 3.  The maxillary, mandibular, and chin midline are all pretty much on the facial midline.  

In profile view, Mark has some AP maxillary deficiency of probably about 4 to 5 mm.  The chin is retruded about 15 mm.  Chin neckline is short.  He has a chin postured forward neck position.  Mark tends to tilt his head upwards likely to help open up the airway.  Mark does have some crenations on his tongue.  In the lower arch, he is missing the mandible right second and third molar and on the left side probably the third molar.  He has all of his bicuspids present in the lower arch.  He has some mild crowding in the lower anterior arch, but has adequate attached gingiva.  Slight accentuated curve of Spee in the lower arch.  In the upper arch, he is missing probably first bicuspids, but has the rest of his teeth back through the second molars.  He probably is missing his third molars.  He has got a Class I cuspid relationship and Class II molar relationship.  He has about a 3 mm anterior deep bite.  He is missing maxillary first bicuspids and all four third molars are absent.  Oropharyngeal tissues shows that the uvula is excessively enlarged probably to a moderate to severe category.  The soft palate is probably mild to moderately elongated.  The fascial pillars are mild to moderately constricted.  The tonsils are present and may be just mildly enlarged.  Nasal examination shows that the septum is deviated off towards the left side.  The turbinates are moderately enlarged anteriorly and may be more enlarged posteriorly creating some nasal airway obstruction.  To palpation, he has some mild tenderness over the retromandibular area, but nowhere else in the head and neck area.  On opening, he has an audible pop in the left joint at about 5 to 10 mm of opening and a closing click at 5 mm of closing.  The right joint seems to function relatively smoothly.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars and mandibular right second molar as well as the maxillary first bicuspids.  He has a root canal on the maxillary left central incisor and a dowel and post on the left lateral incisor.  Sinuses appear relatively clear.  He has some bulbous extensions of his septum.  The condyles appear to have reasonably good morphology.  

Coronal view shows that the sinuses are clear.  The turbinates are enlarged on the right side and to a lesser degree on the left side.  The septum appears reasonably straight at the area of the current cut which appears to be posterior to the molar teeth.

Axial view shows that the sinuses are relatively clear with just one little area that there may be a slight mucocele on the lateral sinus wall.  The septum deviates off towards the left side, but the posterior aspect appears fairly straight.  The turbinates are enlarged, more so on the right side than the left side and could be contributory to the airway deficiencies.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows that there is some flattening on the anterior head of the condyle.  The condyle is posteriorly positioned in the fossa with a slight increased anterior joint space.  The articular eminence is moderately steep.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has a little bit of sclerosis on the top and some slight anterior beaking towards the medial aspect of the joint.  The articular eminence is moderately steep.

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with good mediolateral width.  The lateral pole is lower than the medial pole.  There is decreased lateral joint space.  

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width with a more even vertical position of the condylar poles.  There is decreased lateral joint space indicative of perhaps medial displaced articular discs.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that the maxilla and mandible are somewhat retruded.  The head is tipped up somewhat.  The oropharyngeal airway at the soft palate level measures 5 mm, although at the base of the tongue the airway dimension is more normal.  The soft palate appears elongated.  He has a Class I skeletal and occlusal relationship.  

Basic diagnoses for Mark would be as follows:

Left TMJ mild arthritis and articular disc dislocation with early reduction.
Maxillary AP and posterior vertical hypoplasia.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia.
Hypertrophied turbinates.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of 5 mm (normal AP dimension 11 mm).
Hyperplastic soft palate and uvula.
Severe sleep apnea. 

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Presurgical orthodontics. 
	a.	Align and level maxillary arch.
	b.	Align and level mandibular arch.
2.	Surgery.
	a.	Left TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek 			anchor.
	b.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a 			counterclockwise direction with pogonion moving forward 15 mm.
	c.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla in a 					counterclockwise direction about 6 to 7 mm at the incisal tips.
	d.	Uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (UPPP procedure).
	e.	Bilateral reduction turbinectomies.
3.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
PAM SMITH 										08/23/2006

Pam is 38 years old, referred by Dr. Larry Byrd, 7660 Woodway, Ste. 325, Houston, TX 77063.  Pam's problems in her joints probably began back when she was a teenager as her mother recalls her complaining of popping at that time.  Things may have become much worse in about 1993.  TMJ problems are of unknown etiology.  She does get earaches, tinnitus and vertigo but no hearing loss.  Other bones that bother her include her neck and right hand.  She is aware of clenching and bruxism at nighttime.

Previous treatment includes orthodontics as a teenager and then she has had splint treatment beginning about 10 years ago that has been on and off.  

MEDICATIONS:
Trazodone 50 mg at bedtime.
Valium 2.5 mg at bedtime.
Flonase nasal spray.
Birth control pills.

She gets headaches about every 10 days that escalates to about a level of 10 that are predominately in the right temple and posterior head area.  She gets severe pain at a level of 10 in the TMJs but not everyday.  Some days her pain level is only about a level of 1.  Average daily pain and headaches is 2, diet is 2 and disability is rated at 3.  She says her headaches are a level of 10 in the temporal and posterior head and neck area.  She used to get headaches two to three per week prior to her current splint therapy.

PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Difficulty swallowing with a feeling of choking.
TMJ pain. 
Headaches.
Bite is off.
Earaches and ringing in the ear as well as lightheadedness and dizziness.

In frontal view, pam has fairly good symmetry in the middle and upper third face with significant asymmetry in the lower third face.  Upper lip length is about 23 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is probably 2 mm.  Maxillary midline is probably a good 2 mm off to the right side.  Mandibular dental midline is about 3 mm off to the right side.  Chin is probably about 6 to 7 mm off to the right side.  Mandibular dental midline and chin are fairly close to being on the facial midline.  In profile view, Pam has fairly good balance between the upper lips, cheeks and nose.  Chin appears somewhat retruded although the chin neckline actually looks reasonably good in length.  The chin may be about 10 mm AP deficient.  Pam has a head postured forward neck position.  Intraorally, Pam is missing lower first bicuspids but has the rest of her teeth.  Some moderate crowding in the lower anterior arch.  Appears to have good attached gingiva.  In the upper arch she is missing first bicuspids as well.  She has an end-on Class II occlusion being about 2 mm Class II on the left side and probably 4 to 5 mm Class II on the right side.  She has an anterior deep bite of 5 to 6 mm.  Pam has a significant shift in transverse occlusal plane with the right side being about 4 mm shorter than the left side.  
PAM SMITH					08/23/2006				Page 2


Uvula, soft palate and faucial pillars are all within normal limits.  Intranasally, the septum is deviated off to the right side with some spurring.  Right turbinate is a little bit large and significantly decreased oropharyngeal airway is present.  The left turbinate appears relatively normal in size and there appears to be good airway on the left side.  To palpation, she has no tenderness around the head and neck area.  Joints seem to function smoothly.  Excursive movement to the right about 9 mm, left 10 mm.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION:
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars and all first bicuspids.  The sinuses appear relatively clear.  The left condyle appears large with a little notch in the posterior head of the condyle.  The left side appears to have undergone significant resorptive and degenerative changes.

Waters view shows that the bony nasal septum does have some deviation.  The sinuses appear relatively clear.  Turbinates appear moderately enlarged with the right side appearing larger than the left side.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is broad anteroposteriorly.  Joint space, however, seems to be fairly well balanced.  Articular eminence is moderately steep. 

Right TMJ shows severe osteoarthritic changes in the condyle and joint.  The condyle is posteriorly positioned in the fossa.  On opening, there is good translation of both joints but significant difference in the size of the condyles can be appreciated. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has a skeletal Class II relationship with some vertical asymmetry.  Oropharyngeal airway that the tip of the soft palate measures about 6 mm and the base of the tongue about 7 mm.  

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
Right TMJ severe arthritis and articular disc dislocation being nonreducing and with severe deformation.
Left TMJ mild arthritis and articular disc dislocation at the lateral aspect of the joint.  
AP maxillary deficiency. 
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
AP mandibular deficiency. 
TMJ pain.
Headaches. 
Myofascial pain. 
Decreased oropharyngeal airway.
 Class II occlusion.
 Anterior deep bite. 
 Missing all four first bicuspids from previous orthodontic treatment.  
 Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2 mm. 
PAM SMITH					08/23/2006				Page 3


RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch.  Open up spacing around the upper lateral incisors to equal 1 mm on each side to correct for the tooth size discrepancy.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to facilitate interdental osteotomies. 
Align and level mandibular arch.
CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concept protocol.)
Surgery.
Right TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses.
Left TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor.
Left mandibular sagittal split osteotomy to advance the mandible on that side.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla and down graft it posterior.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion. 
Restorative dentistry to place bonding or Veneers on the maxillary lateral incisors to eliminate the spacing created to correct the tooth size discrepancy.

original_note:
PAMELIA LEE									05/13/2009

Pam is 43 years old.  Referred by Dr. Jay Lustig, 2550 Hospital Drive, Bossier City, LA 71111.  She was also referred by Dr. Lisa Matthews, 4440 Viking Drive, Bossier City, LA 71111.  

Pam had no TMJ problems or issues until she was injured on the job as a police office on May 30, 2008, at the age of 42. She received trauma to the jaw and it is believed that her jaw became displaced on the left side.  She was able to at least partially reduce it.  She continued to have problems, and she did see an oral surgeon, Dr. David Kim.  The following surgeries have been done so far on Pam by Dr. Kim:  

7/29/2008, left arthrocentesis.
9/15/2008, left intraoral vertical oblique osteotomy. 
11/14/2008, left intraoral vertical oblique osteotomy.

These procedures have failed to correct Pam’s problems.  The main concerns that Pam has at this time include the following:

Left jaw dislocation. 
Unable to open and use the jaw properly. 
Jaws and bite are shifting.
Pain is increasing.
Scar tissue problems in the joint.

Pam currently takes the following medications:

Lisinopril 5 mg q.d.
Zyrtec 10 mg q.d.
Tylenol p.r.n. 

Pam in addition has environmental asthma.  She has high blood pressure that just was initiated from her injury; she had no problems with that before that time.  She also has arthritis in some other joints.  Other joints that bother her include her left knee, left ankle, and wrists.  She gets occasional earaches.  She has left-sided tinnitus, but no vertigo and no hearing loss.  She gets headaches in the left temporal area at about a level of 8.  She gets her headaches about 3 times a week at a level of 8.  She rates her TMJ pain at 8 in the left joint which is worse on the right side.  She rates her average daily pain at 3, jaw function at 8, diet at 7, disability at 9.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Pam has reasonably good facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 20 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 1 mm.  The maxillary, mandibular, and chin midlines are all fairly well aligned with each other.  In profile, the maxilla may be just slightly AP deficient by 2-3 mm and chin by 5-6 mm, but nothing real significant.   Intraorally, Pam has all her lower teeth beck through the second molars.  There is some mild crowding of the lower anterior arch.  She is basically a Class I cuspid-molar relationship.  She has an anterior deep bite of about 3 mm.  Sometimes Pam feels that her right teeth do not touch as well as the left side does.  As Pam opens her jaws, she opens fairly symmetrically.  Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively healthy.  Intranasally, turbinates appear relatively minimal to mildly large with the left side being slightly larger than the right side.  It appears that there is adequate airway.  To palpation, she has high moderate to low severe pain in the left masseter area and left retromandibular area.  She has moderate pain over the left temporalis, left medial pterygoid, and high moderate pain over the left TMJ with relatively mild pain over the right masseter muscle.  Maximal incisal opening is 28 mm.  Opening without significant discomfort 20 mm.  Excursion movement to the right 3 mm, to the left 5 mm.  Other joints seem to function relatively smoothly, although she states she does hear sort of a squishy noise in the joints on the left side when she opens. 

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the presence of impacted maxillary left third molar, but all other teeth are erupted and she is missing the left mandibular third molar and the right maxillary and mandibular third molars.  She appears to have probably a mucocele in the maxillary right sinus with a bulbular mass there.  Turbinates are moderate in size.  Septum appears relatively straight.  Left mandibular ramus shows a wire at the inferior border with evidence of probably previous vertical mandibular osteotomies, but it appears that the condyle may have rotated significantly including likely the whole section.  On the right side, it does appear as though the condyle is posteriorly positioned in the fossa. 
Coronal view shows that the turbinates are moderate in size.  There does appear to be some fluid versus some sinusitis of the tissues on the floor of the sinus.  The turbinates appear moderate in size.  
Axial view shows that the turbinates are moderate in size.  Sinuses in this view look relatively clear.  
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is postured forward beneath the articular eminence.  The condyle appears to have moderately good mediolateral width.  There is a definitely decreased vertical joint space.  The condyle appears to be postured on the articular eminence, but towards the lateral aspect of the joint.   
Right TMJ coronal view shows a decreased vertical joint space.  Lateral rim barely clears the condyle.  
Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is postured way anterior functioning on the articular eminence.  It probably has a strange rotation to it as it is perhaps anteriorly displaced at the medial aspect of the joint as compared to the lateral.  The more lateral we get the more the condyle appears to rotate back in towards the fossa area.  
Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is posteriorly positioned in the fossa.    
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has a slightly retruded maxilla and mandible.  Oropharyngeal airway appears constricted with an AP dimension at the base of the tongue of only about 5 mm and about 4 mm at the soft palate level. 

Basic diagnoses for Pam would be as follows:    

Post 2 previous surgeries that included vertical oblique osteotomies of the left mandibular ramus area.
Left mandibular condylar dislocation with adhesions. 
Left TMJ pain. 
Left-sided headaches.
Left-sided myofascial pain. 
Intraoral scar band residual from 2 previous surgeries limiting jaw opening.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Presurgical orthodontics.  
Align and level maxillary arch.  
Align and level mandibular arch.    
CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol). 
Surgery. 
Left TMJ reconstruction with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. 
Left TMJ fat graft (harvest from the abdomen).
Possible placement of arch bars if orthodontic treatment is not initiated by the time of surgery. 
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

The MRI on the right TMJ shows that the disc is anteriorly and laterally displaced, although Pam is asymptomatic in that joint at this time.  Therefore, my recommendation is to continue to observe and monitor the right joint. It is possible that she could develop symptoms in the future which may require surgery later on in that joint.  The injury to the right joint is a result of the trauma she received on May 30, 2008.

original_note:
B337 
B337 is 25 years old, referred by Dr. __. has had no previous surgeries. She is in good health.
MEDICATIONS:
Yasmin.
Doxycycline.

She gets nauseated when taking minocycline but has no other known allergies.
PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Left TMJ locking.
Difficulties eating. Pain worse on the left side.
Wakes up with pain in the mornings. Decreased mouth opening.
Jaw and bite may be changing. Speech changes.

B337 's problems began about 4 to 5 years ago when she first noted clicking. Etiology is unknown. However, she was eating a bagel about 5 years ago and that is when the pain problems first began. She had Invisalign orthodontic treatment 5 years ago that took about 9 months. She has had equilibration and splints x2. She had an arthrogram of the left TMJ last year. The arthrogram was just of the left joint and showed that the disc was displaced.

B337 has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo or hearing loss. No other joints bother her. She has no known allergies and no problems wearing jewelry. She is however, very much aware of clenching and bruxing her teeth at night but does not believe she does it during the day.

B337 gets headaches once or twice a week predominately in the left frontal area and the temple area behind her eye at about a level of 6. She used to get these more often but they have decreased some maybe due to the splint wear. She rates her TMJ pain on an average day at about 8 and particularly when she wakes up in the morning when it is the worse. With function, it can go up to a 9. The pain is much worse on the left side as compared to right side. She rates her average daily pain around the head and neck a level of 5. She rates her jaw function at 6, diet at 5, disability at 5.

In frontal view, has good left to right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 19 mm. Upper tooth to lip relationship is about 5 mm. She has a little bit more fullness in the right mandibular angle area as compared to the left side by maybe a couple of millimeters. Maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline is off to the left about 1 mm and chin is off the left maybe 2 mm. In profile view, she has a C-shaped curve to the upper lip. Upper lip supralabrale is posterior to the subnasale by a couple of mm. The maxilla may be AP deficient by probably about 3 to 4 mm. Chin is retruded by probably about 15 mm. B337 

Chin neckline is somewhat shorter though the chin-neck angle is well defined. has lip incontinence of probably about 8 mm or so. Intraorally, has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars present. A little amount of crowding in the lower anterior arch but not too bad. She has good attached gingiva around the lower anterior teeth. In the upper arch, has all of her teeth back through the second molars. In centric occlusion she has slightly reverse curve of occlusion in the lower arch with the incisors being 1 to 2 mm lower than they should be. Upper arch looks relatively flat. She has a slight Class II end-on cuspid relationship being about 3 mm on the left side and 2 mm on the right side. Anterior open bite is about 2 mm. In centric relation, tends to occlude on the left side and in and around the second bicuspid, first and second molar area. As opens, she does deviate towards the left side probably about 3 to 4 mm. Intraorally, the uvula may be just mildly elongated but not too significant. Soft palate and faucial pillars look fairly normal. The tonsils are present and appear normal. Intranasally, the turbinates appears maybe mildly enlarged but not too significant. The septum is reasonably straight. Nasal airway appears good. Columella is wide and the nostrils are somewhat narrow. Luminal valves appear relatively normal. To palpation, she has high moderate pain over both TMJs, moderate pain over the masseters, shoulders and milder tenderness over the temporalis muscles and styloid area. Joints seem to function reasonably smoothly. Maximal opening is 28 mm but only 20 mm without pain and discomfort. Excursive movement to the right 4 mm, to the left 3 mm. did have her wisdom teeth taken out when she was about 16 years old. Transverse occlusal plane appears fairly level. It is possible that the right side could be down just slightly compared to the left side. There is some vertical asymmetry in the incisors but the cuspids appear fairly level. Left central and lateral is slightly elevated compared to the right side.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
: Panorex shows presence of all the teeth except for the four third molars. Teeth appear to be relatively nicely aligned and with reasonably good root formation. The sinuses appear clear. Nasal airway appears clear. Articular eminences are moderately steep. The left condyle appears a little bit more arthritic than the right condyle. The right condyle appears a little bit larger than the left condyle.

Waters view shows that the sinuses are clear. Turbinates are moderate size. The septum is fairly straight in the bony area. There appears to be passable airway bilaterally.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has some flattening on the anterosuperior aspect of the head. There is a little bit of anterior beaking present but not too much. Could feel a little bit of an osteophyte on the superior aspect of the condyle. Decreased superior and posterior joint space. Articular eminence is moderately steep. On one view it appears there could a little bit of erosion in the fossa area.

B337 
Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is superiorly and posteriorly positioned in the fossa. There could be a little bit of an osteophyte sticking up off the top of it. Articular eminence is moderately steep. There could be a little bit of sclerosis around the head of the condyle. On opening, there is restricted movement in both joints with perhaps a slight greater decreased translation on the left side compared to the right side.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that has some AP mandibular deficiency with an anterior open bite. It appears there may be a cant in the occlusion as there is a difference in vertical position of the occlusal surfaces and inferior border of the mandible. Oropharyngeal airway is somewhat decreased with the distance in the base of the tongue to posteropharyngeal wall of about 3 to 4 mm and from the soft palate to posteropharyngeal wall about 5 mm. She shows some vertical maxillary excess. She demonstrates lip incompetence of about 10 mm.

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation without reduction and significant degenerative changes.
Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with early reduction.
Anterior vertical maxillary excess.
AP maxillary deficiency, AP mandibular deficiency.
Anterior open bite.
Class II end-on occlusion.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway. TMJ pain.
Myofascial pain. Headaches.
Nocturnal clenching and bruxism.
Reverse curve of occlusion in the lower arch. Slight accentuated curve of occlusion in the upper arch. Anterior open bite of 2 mm. Masticatory dysfunction.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Medications:
Klonopin 1 mg tablet q.h.s. Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally. Try to extrude the upper cuspids at least 1 mm. Align the four incisors at an elevated occlusal plane by placing a step in the archwire.
Align and level mandibular arch. Try to extrude lower incisors at least 1 mm to help correct the reverse curve of occlusion in the lower arch.

B337 
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors. Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the maxilla superior, level the curve of Spee and correct any transverse discrepancies that may be present.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B337 MRI Evaluation 
Date of Birth: 
Age: 25
Date of Acquisition:
Date of Interpretation: 

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that appears somewhat arthritic. There is an osteophyte sticking up off the top of the condyle and some anterior beaking present. There are some areas of sclerosis on the condyle a little bit towards the front side. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Anterior disc is anteriorly positioned and sitting beneath the articular eminence. Towards the lateral aspect of the joint the disc is quite narrow anteroposteriorly. More medial it does have a little bit better forward-backward dimension but it is still foreshortened over the normal dimension. There is decreased superior and posterior joint space between the condyle and fossa. On opening, there appears to be restricted translation with possible closed lock on this side. There appears to be some fluid in superior and inferior joint spaces surrounding the disc. Arthritic changes in the left joint look worse than the right side.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that appears a little bit larger than the opposite side. There is some flattening on the anterior head. Decreased superior and posterior joint spaces. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Articular disc is anteriorly positioned and sitting beneath the articular eminence. A little bit of fluid in the superior joint space. On opening, there is better translation on this side and the articular disc appears to reduce into position.

IMPRESSION: Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocated with deformation and probable closed lock situation. Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with reduction.

original_note:
BLAIR SYNDER								08/29/2011

Blair is 26 years old, birth date 07/14/1985.  Blair is referred by Dr. Laura Philipps, 2300 West Michigan, Suite 2, Midland, TX 79704.   She has had a consultation with Dr. Jim Compton, 4610 N. Garfield, Suite A-9, Midland, TX 79705.  

Blair’s joint problems began she believes when she was about 24 years old of unknown etiology.  She was a cheerleader in high school and knows that she has struck her jaw several times while cheerleading.  She did have orthodontics from the age of 13 to 14.  Relative to her TMJ problems, the only treatment she has had currently is splint therapy which was initiated in April 2011.  However, this made her pain significantly worse so she discontinued using it.  She has had her third molars previously removed, but has retained maxillary third molars.  Blair rates her headaches at a level of 6 in the frontal, temporal and posterior head area.  She rates her TMJ pain at 9, average daily pain at 7, jaw function at 9, diet at 7, and disability at 9.  She gets frequent earaches, daily tinnitus, and occasional vertigo.  She has no hearing loss and no other joints bother her.  She does have allergies to outside stuff.  Earrings and rings cause discoloration of her skin, but she has no pain or discomfort that she has associated with those.  She is aware of clenching and bruxing at nighttime and she catches herself clenching during the day as well.

She takes the following medications:

Adderall XR 30 mg q.d.
Lexapro 10 mg q.d.
Birth control pill.
Cyclobenzaprine 10 mg q.d.
Valacyclovir 1000 mg when needed for cold sores.  

In frontal view, Blair has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 19 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 3 mm.  The maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline and lower dental midline is about 1 mm to the right and chin is about 2 to 3 mm to the right.  In profile, she has fairly good facial balance.  Intraorally, she has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars.  She has some mild crowding in the lower anterior arch particularly on the right side.  She has adequate attached gingiva.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  The upper arch appears fairly well aligned.  She has a Class I cuspid relationship bilaterally.  She has about a 3 mm vertical overbite relationship.  Oropharyngeal tissues look normal and healthy.  Turbinates are relatively normal, although the right side is slightly larger than the left.  Nasal anatomy looks relatively normal.  To palpation, she has moderate pain over the TMJs, milder pain over the right temporalis and high moderate pain over the right styloid area.  On opening, she has some crepitation in the right joint.  The left side is not particularly noisy today, although she says that side does click and pop at times.  On her right side, she does get closed lock episodes periodically.  The maximal incisal opening is 57 mm, opening without pain 31 mm.  The excursion movement to the right is 9 mm and to the left 10 mm.  There is a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right side being 2 mm higher than the left side.  Blair has just slight increased fullness on the left side of the mandible being about 2 to 3 mm more prominent with the left angle as compared to the right side.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of mandibular third molars that were previously extracted.  Maxillary third molars are present and appear erupted.  The septum appears relatively straight and the turbinates appear relatively normal in size.  The sinuses appear clear.  Difficult to evaluate the joints on this radiograph.

Coronal view shows that the nasal septum is deviated off a little bit towards the left side.  The turbinates are relatively normal in size with the right one being slightly larger than the left one.  Airway looks good however.  Sinuses appear clear.

Axial view shows the base of the septum fairly straight and the turbinates are relatively normal in size with the right one being larger than the left one.  The sinuses appear clear.  

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology.  There may be a little bit of an osteophytic kind of development on the top of the condyle towards the medial aspect of the joint.  The articular eminence has a moderate inclination.  The condyle appears almost too large for the fossa.  There is a little bit of anterior beaking at the lateral aspect of the joint.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology laterally, but medially there is some flattening on the anterior head and a little bit of anterior beaking present.  The posterior joint space is decreased.  The articular eminence is fairly steep.  There is an increased anterior joint space.  

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 to 4 mm.  The joint space appears fairly well balanced in the joint.  

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with fairly good mediolateral width, although just slightly more narrow than the opposite side.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly probably 4 to 5 mm.  There appears to be some decreased vertical and lateral joint space.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has basically a Class I skeletal relationship.  Oropharyngeal airway may be slightly narrow, but it may be partially due to head posture and position.  

Basic diagnoses for Blair would be as follows:

Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation being worse on the right side than the left side. 
Mandibular relative hyperplasia.
Transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right side being 2 mm higher than the left side.
Mandibular dental midline 1 mm to the right and chin 2 to 3 mm to the right.
TMJ pain.
Headaches.
Myofascial pain.
Malaligned maxillary third molars.
Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of 2 mm. 

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Presurgical orthodontics. 
	a.	Align and level maxillary arch.  Consider opening 1 mm of space between 			the upper laterals and cuspids to accommodate for the tooth size 				discrepancy.
	b.	Align and level mandibular arch.  Consider slenderizing the lower anterior 		teeth removing 2 mm of tooth structure to correct for the tooth size 			discrepancy.  
2.	Surgery.
	a.	Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek 			anchors.
	b.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to maintain the Class I occlusal 			relationship.
3.	Postsurgical orthodontics.
	a.	Refine occlusion. 
	b.	Retain occlusion with a Hawley retainer in the maxillary arch and a fixed 			retainer in the lower anterior arch.  Do not use Essix type of retainers.  
4.	If space is opened in the upper arch, then consider bonding to fill those spaces.

original_note:
KELSEY VINCENT									03/17/2008

Kelsey is 13 years 6 months of age, referred by Dr. Peter Crump, 24 S. Gore Avenue, Webster Groves, MO 63119.  Kelsey’s problems began perhaps when she was 11 years old or could have even developed earlier than that.  The primary concerns that she has are:  
She has pain on opening her mouth. 
Bottom jaw is too small.
Headaches.
Fitful sleeping.

The etiology of Kelsey’s joint issues are unknown. She has some moderate earaches, occasional tinnitus, and occasional vertigo.  The knees and hips bother her some particularly with dance.  She is aware of clenching and bruxing her teeth at night time.  She does predominantly breathe through her nose when she is sleeping and sometimes during the day.  She snores mildly and she has some mild daytime tiredness.  She is not aware of any sleep apnea problems.  She does toss and turn a lot.   
CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Kelsey has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 19 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 2 mm.  Maxillary midline, mandibular dental midline, and chin are pretty much on the facial midline.  In profile view, Kelsey has fairly good balance between the upper lip, cheeks, and nose.  Chin is retruded and chin neckline is short, although the chin neck angle is fairly well-defined. The upper lip may be retruded by 2 mm, but her chin is retruded by probably 10 to 15 mm.  Intraorally, she has got all of her lower teeth back to the first molars.  The second molars have not erupted in yet.  She has good attached gingiva around the anterior arch and some very mild crowding.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back to the first molars.  The second molars are not erupted in.  She has a slight Class II cuspid relationship bilaterally and probably about 2 mm Class II.  She has an overjet of about 1 to 2 mm.  She has about a 2 mm anterior vertical deep bite.  Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively healthy.  Uvula is normal.  Soft palate may be mildly elongated and fascial pillars are moderately constricted.  Tonsils are moderately enlarged.  Turbinates appear moderate in size.  Septum appears fairly straight.  To palpation, she has some moderate tenderness over her shoulders, mild tenderness over the styloid areas and posterior neck area.  Joints seem to function reasonably smoothly, although she felt she could hear a little pop.  Maximum incisal opening is 44 mm.  Excursion movements are 7 mm in each direction.   
RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the presence of impacted second molars and third molars.  Condyles appear a little bit resorbed particularly on the left side.  Sinuses appear relatively clear.  Left turbinate appears larger than the right one.  The tips of the unerupted mandibular second molars are at the inferior border of the mandible.    
Waters view shows that the bony septum is fairly straight and turbinates appear moderate in size.  Sinuses appear relatively clear.  
Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is a little bit flattened on top, and there may be a little bit of anterior beaking.  Decreased superior and posterior joint spaces.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.    
Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears a little bit more resorbed than the opposite side. Articular eminence is moderately steep.  On opening, there is restricted translation of both condyles, but clinically she seems to have fairly good translation. 
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Kelsey has a skeletal Class II jaw relationship.  Oropharyngeal airway actually looks fairly good.  Occlusal plan is moderately steep.  She does have some AP microgenia.  Kelsey gets frequent strep throat probably related to the hyperplastic tonsils.  I plan on getting the tonsils removed during the first stage of surgery.  
Basic diagnoses would be as follows:
Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation without reduction and severe degeneration.
AP mandibular deficiency. 
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
Unerupted and impacted second molars x4 and third molars x4.
Hypertrophied tonsils with recurrent tonsillitis.
Slight Class II occlusion.
High occlusal plane angle. 
TMJ pain.
Headaches.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
Surgery Stage 1.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Uncover maxillary and mandibular second molars x4.
Bilateral tonsillectomy (by Dr. Larry Weprin).
Presurgical orthodontics. 
Align and level maxillary arch.
Align and level mandibular arch.
Surgery Stage 2.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to down graft it posteriorly and advance it slightly.
Bilateral ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction.
Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy to augment the chin.
Removal of impacted third molars x4. 
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion and retain.
