JOYCE STEMPAK									02/22/2010

Joyce is 44 years old, referred by Dr. Petra Schubert, 6128 Sherry Lane, Dallas, TX 75225-6301.  Her dentist is Joanne Hoppe.  The only previous surgery she has had is bladder suspension.  

Joyce’s problems with her jaws began probably in her teenage years.  She says her dad, brother, and three sisters have a similar condition with the jaw shifted off towards one side with probably a genetic factor playing a role.  She has had no treatment to date.  She takes no medications.  She rates her headaches at a level of 4 that occur in the temporal area about one time per week.  She rates her TMJ pain at 2, average daily pain at 1, jaw function at 2, diet and disability at 0.  She has no problems with earaches, gets occasional tinnitus but no vertigo.  No other joints bother her.  She is aware of clenching and bruxing at nighttime and also some during the day, but this has gotten worse since she had her upper arch braces placed about a month ago.  She is getting the lower braces on tomorrow. 

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Joyce has fairly good symmetry in the middle and upper third face.  Upper lip length is about 21 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is 2 mm.  The maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline.  The mandibular dental midline is about 2 mm to the left and chin is about 6 mm to the left.  In profile view, she may have must some light AP maxillary deficiency at subnasale by maybe 2-3 mm.  Chin actually appears to have fairly good projection.  Chin neckline looks relatively normal in length.  Right ramus is about 2-3 mm towards to the right compared to the left side.  Intraorally, Joyce has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars present.  She has some spacers in.  She has very minimal crowding in the lower anterior arch.  However, the lower teeth incisor tips are leaning towards the right side and the roots are angled towards the left.  The left posterior teeth are tipped in lingually, and the right teeth are tipped relatively more buccally.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars present.  She has a slight Class III cuspid relationship on the left side by probably 2 mm and on the right side by probably 4 mm.  She has an end-on incisor relationship.  Posterior maxillary arch appears to be shifted about 2 mm to the right and the lower arch is rotated that way as well.  Joyce has a tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of 1+ mm.  The upper left lateral incisor is smaller in size than the right side.  Oropharyngeal tissues look reasonably healthy.  Tonsils are moderately enlarged.  She has a very strong gag reflex.  Intranasally, the right turbinate appears very slightly enlarged, but the left one looks relatively normal.  The septum is reasonably straight.  There does not appear to be any significant airway obstruction.  She has no history of snoring, has mild daytime somnolence, but no sleep apnea and no difficulties breathing through her nose.  She has 4 children, ages 17, 15, 12, and 7.  Joyce has no tenderness to palpation around the head and neck area.  On opening, the right joint functions smoothly, but with the left side particularly on excursion movements, she has sort of a crunchy noisy joint.  The incisal opening is about 40 mm.  The excursion movements are about 8 mm in each direction.  

RADIOGRAPH EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars.  Sinuses appear relatively clear.  The right condyle appears large compared to the left one.  The third molars are absent, but all the rest of the teeth are present.

Coronal view shows that the turbinates are normal in size and the sinuses are clear.  

Axial view shows that turbinates are normal in size with a good airway and the sinuses are clear. 

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle may have just some very slight anterior beaking in a couple of views, but appears to have reasonable joint space.  The articular eminence is moderately steep.  Out laterally, the joint space appears to be less than medially.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle with a little bit of anterior beaking.  The condylar head appears slightly larger than the opposite side.  The condyle is posteriorly positioned in the fossa.  The articular eminence is shallow compared to the opposite side.  

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly probably about 3 mm, but there is decreased lateral joint space.  

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears wider in width than the opposite side.  The vertical height is also greater.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 mm.  There appears to be decreased lateral joint space.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Joyce has good oropharyngeal airway.  She has a skeletal Class III jaw relationship.  However, the inclination of the incisors appears reasonably normal.  

Basic diagnoses for Joyce would be as follows:

Bilateral condylar hyperplasia being worse on the right side than the left side but in arrested state.
Mandibular deviated prognathism off to the left side. 
Maxillary hypoplasia and asymmetry.
Class III end-on occlusion being worse on the right side than the left side. 
End-on incisor relationship with significant tipping of the maxillary incisors with the coronal portions shifted towards the right side and root apices shifted towards the left side.  
Mandibular left posterior teeth tipped lingually. 
Headaches. 
TMJ pain. 
Transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the left side being 2 mm higher than the right side. 

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	MRI of bilateral TMJs.
2.	Presurgical orthodontics. 
	a.	Align and level maxillary arch.  Open about spacing between the 
                        upper laterals and cuspids, particularly on the left side where the lateral incisor is 
                        smaller. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room 
                        for interdental osteotomies, if they are necessary.
	b.	Align and level mandibular arch.  Try to tip the mandibular left posterior teeth 			buccally to get them set properly over the basal bone.  This may shift her into a 			cross bite on the left side. 
3.	Surgery.
	a.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to shift the mandible towards the right 
                        side and level it transversely.
	b.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla slightly, leveling it 				transversely and correcting for any transverse discrepancies. 
	c.	TMJ surgery if indicated from MRI evaluation.
4.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
JOANNA HOUSTON								01/31/2007

Joanna is 25 years old and is referred by Dr. Brian Stone.  She has had some previous surgery which included some eye surgery (1990), birthmark treated with 2 laser treatments and then 1 surgery (1993, 1994 and 2003) on her forehead by Dr. Jay Burns, chin implant in 2002 done by Dr. Jay Burns.

Joanna has had previous orthodontics by Dr. Frank Moranda.  He followed her from the age of 8 through the age of 25 and just recently retired.  He treated with orthodontics from 1997 to 1998 and used headgears, retainers and splints.  

CURRENT MEDICATIONS:
Enbrel. 
Ortho-Cyclen.
Prednisone 5 mg daily. 

Joanna has no problems with earaches, tinnitus or vertigo.  Other joints that bother her include her neck, fingers and toes.  The neck does significantly affect her range of motion.  At one time she was told that she was allergic to sulfa but she has had some more recent treatment with medications that did include sulfa so it is questionable whether that in fact is true or not.  

Joanna was diagnosed with having juvenile rheumatoid arthritis at the age of 4.  She was treated at Scottish-Rite Hospital from the age of 4 to 18. 

She is not aware of clenching or bruxism at night.  She has no problems with headaches, no TMJ pain and in fact has no pain in any of her other involved joints either.  She has no pain on an average day.  She rates her jaw function at 7, diet at 0, disability at 3.  In frontal view, Joanna has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 17 to 18 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 4 mm.  She has some slight increased fullness on the left side of her face as compared to the right side by maybe about 2 mm.  Maxillary and mandibular dental midlines are pretty much on the facial midline.  Nose is deviated a little bit off to the right side about 2 mm.  Chin is deviated off to the right side about 3 mm.  Transversely, there is a cant in the occlusal plane with the left side being about 1.5 mm lower than the right side.  

In profile view, she is probably at least 6 mm AP deficient in the maxilla.  The chin is probably somewhere around 20 to 22 mm AP deficient.  Chin-neckline is short and chin-neck angle is not well defined.  Joanna has some prominence to the nasal dorsum.  She has good projection of the cheeks and good balance around the eyes.  She has a slight end-on Class II occlusion bilaterally.  She has about a 2 mm vertical overbite relationship.  Very difficult to evaluate the oropharynx, but as best I can see the tissues look healthy and the uvula and soft palate looked relatively normal in length.  Cannot really evaluate the faucial pillars.  I could not see the tonsils.  Transverse width of the nasal cavity appears somewhat narrow.  The nasal septum has a little bit of a spur at the base on the left side.  There could be some slight deviation.  The turbinates appear somewhat moderate in size.  It appears that there could possibly be some decreased airway in the nasal area though she does not really complaint about it.  The columella appears a little bit wide and the nostrils appear a little bit constricted.  
JOANNA HOUSTON			01/31/2007				Page 2

Joanna has had all of 4 first bicuspids extracted.  To palpation, Joanna has no tenderness around the head and neck area.  Joints seem to function smoothly and she is not aware of any noises in the joints.  Incisal opening is 27 mm and surprisingly the excursive movements are 6 mm in each direction.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION:
Panorex shows that all 4 first bicuspids had been extracted.  Mandibular third molars are absent but apparently never developed.  She does have impacted maxillary third molars.  Sinuses appear clear.  She appears to have significant deviation of the nasal septum that may be a septal spur.  Turbinates appear moderately enlarged.  Condyles are functioning completely out of the sockets and anterior to the articular eminences. 

Waters view is more of a PA ceph because of the lack of ability to flex or extend the neck.  The septum appears deviated significantly off towards the right side.  Turbinates appear significantly large and the passable airway appears somewhat constricted.  Difficult to evaluate the maxillary sinuses but the frontal sinuses and ethmoids appear clear.

Left TMJ tomogram shows that the condyles are functioning 12 mm or so anterior to the posterior wall of the fossa.  It may be part of the orthodontics and perhaps some long-term Class II elastics.  

Right TMJ tomogram likewise shows that the condyle is functioning anterior to the articular eminence about 12 to 14 mm anterior to its normal position.  On opening, there may be some translation of the condyles.  In the open view, the severe resorption of the condyle is noted.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that in more of a centric relation she does have a significant Class II occlusion with about a 5 mm overjet.  She has a high occlusal plane angle and vertical orientation of the maxillary incisors.  Mandibular incisors have been over retracted.  There is evidence of a chin implant of silastic that has previously been placed.  Her oropharyngeal airway is significantly decreased with an airway dimension of only about 4 mm between the soft palate and posterior pharyngeal wall and base of tongue and the posterior pharyngeal wall with perhaps a maximum of about 5 mm.  

BASIC DIAGNOSES: 
Bilateral TMJ juvenile rheumatoid arthritis with severe condylar resorption. 
Severe AP mandibular deficiency.
AP maxillary deficiency.
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
Class II occlusion.
High occlusal plane angle.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway. 
Previously placed silastic implant to chin.
Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2.5 mm. 
 Impacted maxillary third molars x2.
JOANNA HOUSTON			01/31/2007				Page 3

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Presurgical orthodontics. 
Align and level maxillary arch.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies.  Open up 1 mm of space between the crowns of each maxillary cuspid and lateral incisor to help correct the tooth size discrepancy. 
Align and level mandibular arch.  Consider slenderizing lower anterior teeth removing only about 1 mm of tooth structure.
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. 
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla and down graft it posteriorly.
Bilateral coronoidectomies. 
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen.)
Bilateral coronoidectomies. 
Removal of impacted maxillary third molars x2.
Remove previously placed silastic chin implant.
HTR implant to anterior mandible.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.


cc:  Dr. Brian Stone

original_note:
MADONNA WEAN								07/15/2011

Madonna is 55 years old, birth date 12/04/1955.  Referred by Dr. Tom Walsh, 99696 Overseas Highway, Key Largo, FL 33037.  

Madonna is not sure when her TMJ problems began, but at least 3 years ago she began developing problems predominantly in the right TMJ area of unknown etiology.  To date, she has had no treatment, but she gets headaches about 2 times per week at about a level of 4 in the right frontal and temporal area and behind the eye.  She rates her TMJ pain at 6, average daily pain at 6, jaw function, diet, and disability at 0.  She gets frequent earaches in the right side.  She has no tinnitus or vertigo, but does have some hearing loss.  No other joints bother her.  She is aware of clenching sometimes at night.  She has glaucoma, high cholesterol, and sleep apnea.  She takes the following medications:

Lumigan eye drops for her glaucoma.  
Welchol 625 mg 3 tablets 2 times a day for her cholesterol.
Prilosec 1 tablet q.d.
Lots of additional supplements.

In frontal view, Madonna has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 21 mm.  Tooth lip relationship is 0.  The maxillary, mandibular and chin midline are pretty much on the facial midline.  In profile view, Madonna has probably about a 3 to 4 mm deficiency in upper lip projection.  The chin appears to be about 12 mm AP deficient.  The chin neckline and angles are not defined.  As Madonna postures her lower jaw forward, it improves her facial profile.  Intraorally, Madonna has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars present.  Teeth are fairly well aligned and leveled.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  She has a Class II end-on cuspid molar relationship being probably 4 to 5 mm Class II.  She has an anterior deep bite of about 3.5 mm.  Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively normal.  To palpation, Madonna has high moderate pain over the right temporalis, right shoulder, and mild tenderness over the right medial pterygoid, right TMJ.  Joints function relatively smoothly.  The incisal opening is 52 mm and excursion movements in each direction about 10 mm.  Intranasally, the turbinates appear moderate in size.  There has been some previous trauma to the nose and so the septum is deviated off to the left side about 1 cm back in, but then off to the right side anterior to that.  Madonna does report moderate snoring at night, moderate daytime somnolence, moderate sleep apnea, and a moderate sleep disorder  at night.  She tosses and turns a lot.  She sweats significantly and wakes up numerous times at night.  She breathes through her mouth and nose at nighttime, but makes a conscious effort to breathe through her nose during the day.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars and also missing the maxillary left first molar replaced by an implant and crown.  Bony septum posteriorly looks fairly straight.  The right turbinate appears enlarged.  Difficult to evaluate the joints.  

Coronal view shows that the nasal septum is deviated off towards the right side close to the junction of the cartilage and bony areas.  Turbinates are mild to moderate in size and there does appear to be an airway through the nose.  There is a little bit of obstruction on the right side because of the deviation of the septum towards that area.  The sinuses look clear.  

Axial view shows at this particular cut the septum is straight indicating that the deviation off to the right side is limited to a higher area than the level of the inferior turbinates.  Airway does look good at this level.  Sinuses appear clear. 

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology.  There is decreased superior and posterior joint space.  The articular disc is moderately shallow.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has reasonably good morphology with some slight anterior beaking present.  There is decreased vertical and posterior joint space.  The articular eminence is moderately shallow.  

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has adequate mediolateral width, but there is decreased vertical joint space.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 to 4 mm.  

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has adequate mediolateral width.  There is decreased vertical and lateral joint space.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 to 4 mm. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has a relatively low occlusal plane angle.  She has a slight skeletal Class II jaw relationship.  AP dimension from soft palate to posterior pharyngeal wall is about 5.5 mm and from the base of the tongue to posterior pharyngeal wall likewise about 5.5 mm.  

A Bolton’s analysis shows that she has a 4.4 mm tooth size discrepancy with the lower teeth being smaller compared to the upper teeth.

The basic diagnoses for Madonna would be as follows:

Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular discs laterally displaced.
Maxillary AP and posterior vertical hypoplasia.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia.
Class II end-on occlusion.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway.
TMJ pain.
Sleep apnea.
Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of about 3 to 4 mm.
Nasal septal deviation and spurring off to the right side. 
Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Presurgical orthodontics.
	a.	Align and level the maxillary arch.  Open up 1 mm of space between the 			upper laterals and cuspids.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid 			roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies.
	b.	Align and level mandibular arch.  Slenderize the lower anterior teeth 			removing 1.5 to 2 mm of tooth structure and close the spacing.
2.	Surgery.
	a.	Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek 			anchors.
	b.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible forward 			approximately 11 mm.
	c.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance it forward 5 mm.
	d.	Nasal septoplasty.
3.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.  
4.	Restorative dentistry to build up the maxillary lateral incisors either with bonding, 	veneers, or crowns to correct for the tooth size discrepancy.

original_note:
GUILHERME RODRIQUES								09/15/2006

Guilherme is 17 years old.  The primary concerns that he has are that he has only 2 permanent teeth that have erupted in and that his jaws do not align properly.  Guilherme has no problems with headaches, TMJ pain or symptoms.  

In frontal view, he has fairly good facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 21 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 3 to 4 mm.  Maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline and may be just slightly to the right 1 mm.  Chin is pretty much on the facial midline.  In profile view, he has good facial symmetry and balance in vertical and AP dimension.  He has an overlay denture in the upper arch that extends back to the first molars.  In the lower arch he has permanent first molars.  He has 2 primary central incisors.  There appears to be 2 lateral incisors with large diastemas between the anterior 4 teeth, primary first molar on the left side and on the right side primary second molar and probably first molar.  With his overlay denture in place his occlusion is reasonable with the remaining lower teeth that he has.  With the overlay denture out, he had an end-on incisor relationship and actually almost an anterior crossbite.  Also, he has transverse maxillary deficiency bilaterally.  He appears to occlude perhaps on the right primary second molars and on the right maxillary central and lower lateral incisors.  All other teeth appear to be out of occlusion.  Intranasally, he has little bit of septal spurring off to both sides of the nose with the right side having a little bit larger deflection and spurring.  Turbinates appear moderate in size.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION:
Panorex shows the absence in the lower arch of second and third molars bilaterally.  The permanent first molars and permanent right first bicuspid are present.  He has retained primary teeth including the right A, B and E and left A, B and D.  In the upper arch he has retained permanent central incisors and first molars with his primary teeth on the right side to include, C, D and E and on the left side B, C, D and E.  Sinuses look relatively clear.  Condyles look symmetric.  Left turbinate appears somewhat enlarged.  

Waters view shows that there is some septal spurring off to both sides with actually the left side looking like it has much large spurring particularly in the bony septal area.  Turbinates are a little hard to identify.  It appears that the left side may be a little bit enlarged.  The sinuses appear relatively clear.  

The left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has fairly good shape with what appears to be reasonable joint space and a moderately steep articular eminence.  

Right TMJ appears relatively similar.  On opening, there is good translation of both joints.  

Cephalometric evaluation shows the maxilla and mandible may be reasonably aligned to each other.  There may be a slight Class III skeletal relationship.  Tonsils appear to be fairly large.  They seem to fill up a good part of the oropharynx.  I may suggest that they be removed.  I believe they are posteriorly displacing the soft palate. 


GUILHERME RODRIQUES			09/15/2006				Page 2


BASIC DIAGNOSES:  
AP maxillary deficiency.
Transverse maxillary deficiency.
Slight vertical maxillary deficiency.
AP macrogenia.
Class III end-on occlusion.
Anterior and bilateral posterior crossbites.
Partial anadontia with maxillary permanent teeth to include central incisors and first molars and mandibular permanent teeth to include mandibular first molars and right first bicuspid.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Surgery.
Maxillary advancement and expansion as well as slight down graft.
Consider extracting all primary teeth.
Consider dental implants to the maxilla and mandible.  
AP reduction genioplasty. 
     2.   Restorative dentistry to reconstruct dental arches.

Treatment option 2.
1.   Stage 1 Surgery
a.	Maxillary advancement and expansion as well as slight down graft.
AP reduction genioplasty. 
Stage 2 Surgery   
Reconstruct the arches with dental implants and grafting if necessary. 
Restorative dentistry.

original_note:
BARRY WARD									01-28-05


Patient Concerns

Severe sleep apnea
Severe daytime somnolence
Severe snoring
Apneic episodes that occur 39 times an hour
Teeth don’t fit
Difficulties eating and chewing
TMJs click and pop
Difficulty breathing through the nose
If he lies on the left side, the airway becomes totally blocked
Predominantly a mouth breather
Restless leg and arm problems
Severe headaches
Severe TMJ pain
Vertigo

Patient History

Barry has been aware of having clicking and popping in his jaw joints since high school of unknown etiology. More currently he started his workup for his sleep apnea and treatment on 09/08/02. He has had orthodontic appliances on for about two years and had lower first bicuspids extracted and the lower anterior teeth retracted. Approximately four years ago he had his four wisdom teeth removed. He does get chiropractic treatment about once a month for the past five years mainly for his neck problems. He takes Zoloft 100 mg at bedtime. Other joints that bother him include his neck and knees. He is not aware of clenching or bruxism. He rates his headaches that he gets about three times a week at a level of 8 in the temporal area, TMJ pain at 8, average daily pain level of 4, jaw function at 2, diet at 4, and disability at 5. 

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Barry has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 25 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about -3 to -4 mm. Maxillary dental midline is shifted about a millimeter off to the left. Mandibular dental midline is shifted probably about 2 mm off to the right. Mandibular dental midline is about a millimeter off to the right of the chin midline.

In profile view, Barry has probably a good about 4 to 5 mm AP deficiency in the maxilla. Upper lip supralabrale is probably about 3 to 4 mm posterior to subnasale. Chin is probably at least 25 mm AP deficient. Chin-neckline is extremely short, and chin-neck angle is not well-defined. 

Intraorally, he is missing the lower first bicuspids. Has a severe accentuated curve of occlusion. Anterior teeth have been dumped severely lingually. Spacing in the first bicuspid area is fairly well closed except about a millimeter open on the left side. Cuspids are fairly symmetric anteroposteriorly. There is a cant in the cuspids with the right cuspid being probably a millimeter or two lower than the left cuspid. In the upper arch, Barry has all of his teeth back through the second molars present. He has a super Class II cuspid relationship. Has a buccal crossbite from the first bicuspid forward bilaterally. As he postures his mandible forward as far as he can, he hits on his posterior teeth first and has a little bit of an open bite. A little tendency toward crossbite yet in the molar area. Uvula is somewhat hyperplastic but not too bad. Soft palate may be a little bit elongated. Faucial pillars are a little bit constricted. 

Turbinates are moderately enlarged. He has a severely deviated nasal septum off toward the right side. He has a large spur, however, going off to the left side. The nasal septum is visible anteriorly being off to the right side. Has relatively severe nasal airway obstruction. Columella is increased in width, but this is secondary to the deviated septum off to the right side. He has some fairly severe constricture of the luminal valve on the right side. I could hold the nose open and hold that valve open, and he could breathe significantly better on that side. He does have a severe snoring problem, severe daytime somnolence, and severe sleep apnea syndrome.

Maximal incisal opening is 53 mm. Excursive movements to the right are 12 mm and to the left 12 mm. To palpation, he has some mild tenderness over the temporalis, medial pterygoids, and mild to moderate over the TMJs, styloid areas, SCMs, and mild tenderness again over the posterior neck and shoulder areas. 

Barry from a centric relation function has audible pops in both TMJs about somewhere around 25 mm of opening and probably about 15 mm of closing. Has some crepitus as well. Some of the noises were quite audible. 

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows the absence of the lower first bicuspids. All four third molars are absent. Upper second molars have no brackets on them. He has rather severe arthritis of both TMJs with significant anterior beaking on the right condyle and loss and significant sloping on the anterior aspect of the left condyle. Septum deviates significantly off toward the right side. Turbinates appear significantly enlarged. Sinuses appear relatively clear with the left one being just a little bit more cloudy than the right side. Styloid processes are quite prominent and calcified down toward and overlap the posterior aspect of the mandible. 

Left TMJ tomogram shows that there is rather advanced arthritis in the left TMJ. There is virtually no posterior or superior joint space remaining. Articular eminence has sort of a soft slope to it, although it is quite long. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that also has some arthritic changes and some anterior beaking present. Significantly decreased vertical joint space and sort of a moderate-sized posterior joint space. Articular eminence also has sort of a soft gentle slope to it.

On opening, there is good translation of both TMJs. 

Waters view shows that the sinuses are clear. The turbinates are moderately enlarged. Bony septum has some deviation to it but not nearly as severe as the cartilaginous portion which cannot be seen on the radiographs. Shift of the mandibular dental midlines is noted and fairly significant.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Barry has a high occlusal plane angle facial morphology. Upper arch looks good and good angulation of the upper incisors, although the maxilla is anteroposteriorly deficient, and the posterior aspect of the maxilla is vertically deficient. The mandible is severely AP deficient. Lower incisors have been pulled back in significantly and are rather severely decreased in angulation. Roots seem to be coming out through the buccal cortex. Oropharyngeal airway is totally blocked off, but part of this is due to tongue posture and Barry trying to get his lower jaw back in centric relation. He does have a large chin implant probably Silastic measuring 12 mm in AP dimension. Barry has a tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of about 2 mm. Hand articulating Barry’s models into a Class I cuspid position shows that there is transverse deficiency of the maxilla yet remaining. Significant accentuated curve of occlusion with the lower anterior teeth being probably 3 mm higher on the left side and probably 4 mm higher than the posterior teeth on the right side. The lower anterior six teeth are severely  tipped posteriorly. In a Class I molar position, the transverse width of the maxilla is relatively normal.

Basic Diagnoses

Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocations with probable significant deformation and perforations 
Severe AP mandibular deficiency
AP maxillary deficiency
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency
Slight transverse maxillary deficiency
Class II molar super Class II cuspid relationship
Severely decreased inclination of the mandibular anterior teeth
Severely accentuated curve of Spee in the lower arch
Missing mandibular first bicuspid extracted for orthodontic reasons
Missing all four third molars
Tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2 mm
Severely deviated nasal septum off toward the right side and a large bone spur off toward the left side
Hypertrophied turbinates 
Nasal airway obstruction 
Decreased oropharyngeal airway
Severe headaches
Severe TMJ pain
Myofascial pain
Severe sleep apnea
Severe snoring
Daytime somnolence
Vertigo

Recommended Treatment

MRI of left and right TMJs

Presurgical orthodontics 
Continue to align and level maxillary arch 
Align and level mandibular arch and re-open up first bicuspid extraction spaces to equal 6 to 7 mm. This movement also should level the curve of Spee. Strip lower anterior teeth removing 2 mm of tooth structure to correct for the tooth-size discrepancy

CT scan of TMJs and jaws (TMJ Concepts protocol)

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla and downgraft it posteriorly
Bilateral coronoidectomies
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvested from the abdomen)
Bilateral partial turbinectomies
Nasal septoplasty

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion 

Dental implants to bilateral mandibular first bicuspid area

Restorative dentistry to place crowns on dental implants

original_note:
ALBERT SEYMOUR								03/14/2006

Albert is 79 years old and is referred by Dr. Alfred DiStefano, 906 W. Randol Mill Road, Arlington, TX 76012.  The primary reason Albert has been referred to me is for evaluation of his dysfunctional speech problem.  He has to speak in a whisper and has lost his voice quality he had prior to the onset of his current situation.  

Albert was diagnosed with leukemia approximately 13 years ago.  He has had ongoing treatment for that since that time.  On November 13, 2004, Albert was hospitalized and was in the hospital for 51 days for treatment of his leukemia.  Apparently it got pretty bad.  In fact, they had a no stat program for him.  They apparently game him quite a few drugs which I do not have a record of what they were.  Prior to this hospital admission, his voice quality was okay and normal.  For about 20 days he was on nonlucent.  He could not talk or hear.  He is hearing impaired anyway and wears hearing aids.  This is a genetic problem as both his parents were hard of hearing as well.  However, he did regain his hearing of over what it was during this initial time period.  However, he started to have speech difficulties during the end of November over approximately 2 weeks into whatever medicine treatment he was receiving at the time.  His speech has not improved since then.  He was finally discharged from the hospital in January of 2005.  

The only other significance in reference to speech problems was that he had a stroke about 4 years ago and had a very transient loss of his speech that he was able to regain almost by the time that the paramedics came to take him to the hospital.  But since the time of the stroke, he has had difficulties with comprehension.  This also is a problem genetically in his family.  Both of his parents, his older brother and some uncles I believe suffered from dementia as they went into their later 70s.  In August of 2005 Albert was readmitted to the hospital because of bleeding problems and anemia.  He had almost 2 full blood transfusions.  He was having bleeding from his bladder and they finally identified a cancer tumor and removed his right kidney and part of his bladder.  During his admission of November 2004, he was not intubated.  He was on extensive oxygen treatment, however.  Albert did have a tracheostomy performed in 1973 following an auto accident where he almost died.  He had to have part of his lung removed at that time.  They had to do a tracheostomy in the elevator on the way to the operating room in order to save his life.  He had rheumatic fever as a child but this does not seem to have created any problem for him.  Albert states that when he gets out in the wind that this makes his speech worse.  He says his friends could understand him and tell him not to worry about it but other people he does not know asks him what is wrong with his voice.  

He has basically a Class I occlusal relationship.  Teeth are in reasonably good shape.  His soft palate moves reasonably well.  He has minimal velopharyngeal incompetency.  Nasopharyngoscopy was performed.  It shows that the soft palate does elevate and move well.  There could be some slight incompetence but nothing real significant and not enough to create the changes in the voice that he is experiencing.  The vocal cords could be visualized well and appear to function reasonably well and appear to close during speech.  However, he does have significant speech dysfunction problem.
ALBERT SEYMOUR 			03/14/2006				Page 2


In the past he has had an adverse reaction to Benadryl that apparently made him extremely hyper and uncontrollable in the hospital.  They were giving it to try and quiet him down but he had just the opposite effect.  Morphine helped him settle down more during his long hospitalization stay.  During that stay, he was diagnosed as having aviary TB and was placed on some TB medication in the hospital that his wife states "almost killed him."  They finally found out it was not a contagious form of TB and discontinued the treatment.  

I believe that one of the medications given to him for his leukemia or for his TB treatment has created an irreversible toxic neurological injury.  

BASIC DIAGNOSES: 
Dysphonia.
Possible toxic injury to the laryngeal nerves.  This pathology may be irreversible. 

RECOMMENDATIONS:   Referral to Dr. Larry Weprin (otolaryngologist) for diagnostic and treatment considerations.

KATHLEEN MUNGER-FISK							08/23/2006

Kathleen is 43 years old and is referred by Dr. Odell Marshall.  She has two boys, ages 3-1/2 and 1-1/2 boys.  She has had one miscarriage.  She has had previous TMJ surgery.  Kathleen has endometriosis and had surgery three times by Dr. Godat to remove tumors.  She has had one D&C and she has had two C-sections.  She does have some diarrhea problems that she feels are related to stress.  She is going through some difficult personal times right now.  She currently takes vitamins and occasionally Advil.  

PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Severe TMJ pain.
Severe headaches.
Muscle pain. 
Difficulties eating and chewing.
Difficulties opening the mouth. 
Bite has shifted some.
Earaches. 
Severe tinnitus.
Vertigo.

Kathleen does experience problems with her equilibrium.  Kathleen's TMJ problems began back in her teenage years.  Her problems got fairly severe and she then underwent surgery in about 1989 with Dr. R.V. Walker for bilateral TMJ reconstruction.  She has done fairly well for a number of years.  The reoccurrence of her pain and dysfunction problems began about four years ago.  Apparently, some family factors have caused significant increased stress for her. 

In frontal view, Kathleen has fairly good symmetry in the middle and upper third face.  Upper lip length is about 20 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 2.5 mm.  Maxillary midline is fairly close to being on the facial midline.  Mandibular dental midline may be just about 1 mm off to the right.  Chin is fairly close to being on the facial midline.  In profile view, Kathleen has fairly good balance between the upper lip, cheeks and nose.  Chin appears a little bit retruded.  Chin-neckline is not well defined.  Chin may be retruded perhaps 6 to 8 mm.  Intraorally, Kathleen has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars.  It appears to have adequate attached gingiva.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  She has division II alignment of her incisors.  She actually has a Class II division II malocclusion.  There is an anterior deep bite of probably close to 7 mm.  She has a significant reverse curve of Spee in the upper arch.  Intranasally, the septum is deviated a little bit off to the left side.  Left turbinate appears enlarged and a little bit boggy in nature.  Right turbinate appears relatively normal and the right airway looks fairly good but there seems to be some decreased airway in the left side.  To palpation, she has high moderate to severe pain in the temporalis, masseters, medial pterygoids, TMJs, styloid areas, SCMs, posterior neck and shoulders.  Kathleen has severe loud audible crepitus in the left TMJ.  On the right side she likely has some crepitus but it is so overwhelming the left side is difficult to detect on the right side.  However, her pain is much more severe on the right side than the left side.  Maximal opening is 39 mm.  She can open to about 29 mm without severe pain.  Excursive movements are about 7 mm in each direction.  

KATHLEEN MUNGER-FISK 		08/23/2006 				Page 2


However, going into excursive movements created muscle cramping for her around the right anterior aspect of the masseter muscle up into the cheek area. Transverse occlusal plane appears relatively level. 

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION: 
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars.  A little calcification around the bicuspids on the right mandible.  Sinuses look relatively clear.  Left turbinate appears enlarged.  Joints appear arthritic with the right side appearing worse than the left side.

Waters view shows that there is a nasal spur coming off the right side of the septum and left side of the nose appears fairly well blocked with the turbinate as well as with septal deviation.  The sinuses, however, appear relatively clear.  She has quite a large frontal sinus but it is clear as well. 

Left TMJ sagittal view is hard to read but it appears that there are some severe degenerative changes within the joint.

Right TMJ tomogram likewise shows severe arthritic changes within the joint.  Articular eminences on both sides are moderate.  There appears to be decreased superior and anterior joint space.  On opening, there is restricted translation in both joints.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has a slight skeletal Class II relationship with a division II occlusion.  Oropharyngeal airway at the soft palate level appears to be about 8 mm and at the base of the tongue is closer to about 6 mm down to 4 mm at the lower aspect of the tongue base. 

BASIC DIAGNOSES: 
Bilateral TMJ severe arthritis.
AP mandibular deficiency. 
AP maxillary deficiency. 
Posterior maxillary deficiency. 
Class II division II malocclusion. 
Anterior deep bite of 7 mm. 
Severe TMJ pain. 
Severe headaches.
Myofascial pain. 
 Severe earaches, tinnitus, and vertigo.
 Nocturnal clenching and bruxism.
 Left nasal turbinate hyperplasia. 
 Left nasal airway obstruction.  



KATHLEEN MUNGER-FISK		08/23/2006 				Page 3


RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Medications.
a.  Klonopin to control nocturnal clenching and bruxism. 
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch. 
Align and level mandibular arch.
CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol.)
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. 
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla and down graft it posteriorly. 
Bilateral coronoidectomies. 
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen.)
Left partial turbinectomy. 
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B302 
B302 is 46 years old, referred by Dr. __. B302 is in good health with no major medical problems except he does have some allergies. He does not take any medications. Apparently no known allergies to medications. B302 has never had orthodontics. Apparently congenitally missing maxillary lateral incisors in the upper arch but appears to have the rest of his teeth. B302 is not sure when his jaw joint problems began and there is no known etiology. B302 has had not treatment for his jaw problems to date. He has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo or hearing loss. Other joints that bother him include his back where he has a herniated disc and he has been diagnosed as having scoliosis. He is not aware of clenching or bruxing. Headaches that he gets occur around two times per month at about a level of 3 but can reach a level of 7 to 8. TMJ pain is about a level of 2 with the right side worse than the left side. Average daily pain is about a 1, jaw function at 5, diet at 1, disability at 2.

PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Jaws popping and aching.
Difficulty breathing through the nose.
Difficulty swallowing.
Headaches.
TMJ pain.
Daytime tiredness that requires sometimes taking naps.
Snoring.
Sleep apnea concerns.

In frontal view, B302 has fairly good left to right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 26 mm. Upper tooth to lip relationship is 0. Mandibular dental midline is 2 to 3 mm off to the left side. Maxilla is retruded probably about 6 mm or so. Chin is retruded probably 20 mm. Chin-neckline is short and chin-neck angle is absent. Intraorally, B302 does have all of his lower teeth back through the second molars. Some mild crowding in the lower anterior arch. Transverse narrowness in the lower arch is present. In the upper arch, he is missing lateral incisors. Transverse width of the arch is narrow. He has a curve of Wilson. Lower arch has a slight increased curve of Wilson. He has basically a Class I cuspid relationship. He has a 2 mm vertical overbite relationship. Slight transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right side being maybe 1 mm higher than the left side. He has oral dystopia with the right eye being 1 to 2 mm higher than the left eye. As B302 bites his teeth together, his mandible tends to shift an extra mm towards the right side. In centric relation, however, the mandibular midline and chin midline appear to be fairly close to the facial midline. Oropharyngeal airway demonstrates what appear to be healthy tissues. Uvula may be slightly elongated. Soft palate may be mildly elongated. Faucial pillars are relatively normal or perhaps slightly constricted. Intranasally, the anterior part of the septum is tipped off towards the left side but the mid part of the septum is off to the right side. Turbinates are moderate in size. The right side seems to have more airway obstruction compared to the left side and today he can breathe better through the left side although he says it does switch from side-to-side at times. Holding the left nostril open, he can breathe better. On the right side he cannot determine too much difference. Snoring is moderate. Daytime somnolence is moderate and sleep apnea syndrome is mild.

B302 

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
:
B302 is missing maxillary lateral incisors and all four third molars. He has a little calcification around the root apex of the mandibular left cuspid. Both condyles appear arthritic. Sinuses appear reasonably clear. Turbinates appear moderate in size.

Waters view shows that the bony nasal septum is deviated and there may be a spur at the superior right side. The right turbinate appears moderately enlarged while the left one looks relatively normal in size. Sinuses look relatively clear with essentially no development of the frontal sinuses.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears a little bit arthritic with a pseudocyst in the top of it. Decreased superior and posterior joint spaces. Articular eminence is moderately steep.

Left TMJ tomogram likewise shows a condyle that appears to have a pseudocyst in it. Decreased superior joint space. Articular eminence is moderately steep. On opening, there is moderately good translation of the left joint and not quite as good of translation on the right side although on the the translation was quite good in both joints.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that B302 has AP retrusion of the maxilla and mandible. Oropharyngeal airway measures about 3 mm at the soft palate level and about 5 to 6 mm at the base of the tongue. The tongue seems to fill a good portion of the oral cavity except for the very anterior aspect. He has a skeletal Class II jaw relationship. He has a moderately steep occlusal plane angle. To palpation he has some mild tenderness over the right masseter but no other pain around the head and neck area. On jaw function he does have a definite pop in the right TMJ probably 30 mm of opening and 20 mm of closing. The left joint seems to function relatively smoothly. The right joint also has some crepitus types of noises. Maximal incisal opening is 63 mm. Excursive movement is about 12 mm in each direction.

B302's wife states that she feels that B302's profile has become more retruded in the chin area than when they first met when B302 was 20. Tooth size analysis evaluating the maxillary cuspids and centrals with the lower anterior six teeth shows that there is a difference of about 15 mm of spacing that would need to be created to accommodate the maxillary incisors and coordinate that with the lower anterior six teeth. However, both the maxillary and mandibular cuspids and incisors are excessively wide anteroposteriorly which will create a little bit more discrepancy in tooth size analysis. In fact, using the bicuspid as the cuspid and the cuspids and lateral incisors in the upper arch would actually work out as a pretty good tooth size balance. Space would be required to almost equal the size of the maxillary cuspids. An option might be to open up spacing back in the bicuspid area instead of in the lateral incisor area. Using the first bicuspids as cuspids reveals a good match tooth-size-wise between the upper and lower anterior six teeth.

B302 
BASIC DIAGNOSES:
Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with significant degenerative changes.
AP maxillary deficiency.
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
AP mandibular deficiency.
AP microgenia.
High occlusal plane angle.
Missing maxillary lateral incisors. Class II cuspid molar relationship.
Significantly decreased oropharyngeal airway of about 3 mm (normal 11 mm.) Deviated nasal septum. Hypertrophied turbinates. Nasal airway obstruction. TMJ pain. Headaches. Sleep apnea symptoms. Snoring. Daytime somnolence.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch. Open up bicuspid space between the bicuspids bilaterally. Align and level mandibular arch. Consider slenderizing the lower anterior teeth removing about 2 mm of tooth structure to keep from flaring them any further forward.
CT scan (TMJ Concepts protocol.)
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance in a counterclockwise direction. Bilateral coronoidectomies.
Bilateral fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen or buttocks.)
Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies. Nasal septoplasty.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
Osseointegrated implants and crowns to place teeth in the bicuspid area.

B302 Evaluation Age: 46/M
Date of Birth: 
Date of : Date of Interpretation:
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width. A little bit of deformity in the superior aspect of the condyle right in the center. There appears to be decreased vertical joint space. There appears to be discal tissue medial and anterior to the condyle. It is quite flat and I see no curvature.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears as though it has undergone some resorption and it appears smaller vertical in dimension. Decreased vertical joint space. There appears to be discal tissue medial and anterior to the condyle.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is arthritic. There is anterior beaking and sclerosis on the anterosuperior head. Articular eminence is moderately steep. The disc is anteriorly displaced and significantly degenerated. It is actually sitting beneath the articular eminence. No particular inflammatory process seen in the joint. On opening, there is good translation of the condyle and disc but the disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that appears to have undergone condylar resorption. There is anterior beaking and sclerosis on the anterior aspect of the condyle. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Articular disc is anteriorly displaced and sitting beneath the articular eminence. The disc is quite deformed. There is a little bit of fluid in the superior joint space at the articular eminence area by the disc. On opening, there is good translation of the condyle but the articular disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction.

IMPRESSION: Bilateral TMJ advanced arthritis and articular disc dislocated without reduction with significant degenerative changes.
B302

original_note:
DARCY KINDSCHY								04-02-03

Darcy is 33 years old and referred by Dr. David Ten Hulzen.

Current Medications

Darcy takes the following medications:

Nortriptyline 125 mg q.h.s. 
Neurontin 400 mg, 400 mg--600 mg per day
Relafen 750 mg b.i.d. 
Vicodin approximately 2 tablets in the evening

Darcy has to limit her pain medication because she cannot be on narcotics and work, although she feels she could certainly benefit by better pain control during the day.

Patient History

Darcy’s problems began in about 1986 in a roll over school bus accident, and a student fell on her right jaw and head. Two months later, she had her wisdom teeth removed and since that date has had jaw problems. Darcy feels that the onset of her TMJ problems was more related to the removal of her wisdom teeth than to the bus accident. She has had fairly extensive treatment since about 1986. She had her first surgery in 1994 where she had bilateral arthroscopies of the TMJs performed. In 1994, she had bilateral TMJ arthroplasties with temporalis fascia flaps that worked relatively well. In 1999, she had bilateral Christensen TMJ total joint prostheses placed. She had difficulties recovering from that particular surgery. By mid 2001, she developed severe disabling headaches again, vertigo, tinnitus, jaw, neck, and shoulder pain. She is on her current medications but in the past has had prednisone, Botox, acupuncture, physical therapy, and muscle relaxants. She has been to see several surgeons in reference to her current situation. She gets earaches, tinnitus, and vertigo, although the vertigo is somewhat controlled with anti-inflammatory medications. If she doesn’t take her anti-inflammatory medications, her vertigo is so severe that she cannot drive. She has no other joints that bother her at this time. She has an allergy to SULFA. She is aware of having any problems wearing jewelry. 

Her pain in the jaw areas does increase throughout the day. It is usually worse in the evenings. She does have continuous headaches even when medicated that range at about a level of 6 in the high temporal area and in the posterior head. Without medications, the headaches would be around a 9 or a 10. With medications, her TMJ pain is at a level of 6 and without medications it would likely be at 8. Overall pain with medication on an average day is about 6. Darcy rates her jaw function at 2 and diet and disability at 6.

Allergies

SULFA.

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Darcy has good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 20 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 1 to 2 mm. Maxillary, mandibular, chin, and nasal midlines are all pretty much aligned with each other. 

In profile view, Darcy demonstrates some AP maxillary deficiency of probably about 3 to 4 mm. Mandible is probably 15 to 18 mm AP deficient at the chin area. Chin-neckline is extremely short, and chin-neck angle is not well-defined. 

Intraorally, Darcy is missing the lower first bicuspids. She has severe crowding, lower anterior arch with probably 6 mm of crowding present. Right cuspid is somewhat blocked out of the arch. In the upper arch, she is also missing the first bicuspids. She has an anterior deep-bite of about 3 mm and has basically a Class I cuspid-molar relationship. There is a slight cant in the occlusion with the left side being about 2 mm higher than the right side. Oropharynx is clear. To palpation, she has a moderate to high moderate pain to palpation throughout most of the head and neck area with some mild to moderate tenderness over the SCMS, posterior neck, and shoulder area. Maximal incisal opening is about 40 mm but without significant discomfort about 30 mm. Excursive movements are 3 mm in each direction. On opening and closing, she feels that she hears some sandy kind of sounds in the right joint, and in the left side, she just hears some popping noises. 

Intranasally, the septum may be deviated a little bit off toward the right side. Right turbinate is fairly large and seems to block a good portion of the airway. Left turbinate is relatively normal in size with what appears to be a good airway.

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows the sinuses appear relatively clear. Turbinate on the left side actually looks large compared to the right side. The four first bicuspids and all four third molars are missing. She has bilateral L-shaped Christensen total joint prostheses with metal condylar heads. She has L-shaped mandibular components, and she has 11 screws in each side. She has metal on metal function. There are some vascular clips present as well in the area with probably three of them on the right side and one on the left side that can be seen. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows the mandibular prosthesis and the fossa prosthesis, and they appear as though they are in good functional position. I cannot see any significant evidence of heterotopic bone around the area but cannot rule that out either.

Left TMJ tomogram shows the metal fossa and metal condyle functioning together. Appears to be some reactive bone that is overgrown in the posterior aspect of the fossa component. There is a little vertical gap between the posterior aspect of the fossa and the fossa area itself. 

Opening shows that there is fairly good translation of the right condylar mandibular component, whereas the left side does not appear to move very much.

Waters view shows that the condylar components are pretty well centered in the fossa components. Appears that the screws are in place, and there are at least four to five on the right side and probably four to five on the left side. 

Cephalometric evaluation shows that the maxilla and mandible are both retruded. She has a high occlusal plane angle. Oropharyngeal airway may be somewhat compromised but difficult to tell because I can’t see the posterior aspect of the soft palate. At the base of the tongue, the oropharyngeal airway dimension measures only about 3 mm. 

Darcy does have a sleep disorder that she feels is probably related to pain factors. She does snore some. 

Other Systems

Darcy has no other joint problems except for the TMJ. She has had probably two urinary tract infections during her life. She gets occasional nocturia. She does on a more frequent time frame have difficulties urinating and difficulties emptying her bladder. She has two children, ages 2 years and 1 year. She had ovarian cysts x 2, and this was just in the past year. She had an eye infection about two years ago. She has dryness of her eyes. She does get occasional burning and itching. She has earaches, tinnitus, and vertigo. She has had bronchitis x 2 in the past. She gets occasional nausea with her severe pain. No known STDs. She has some significant fatigue problems and relates this to pain and her medications. Although she has no thyroid problems, thyroid disease does run in her family. She does have some memory loss but attributes that to perhaps the medications. She has had quite a bit of exposure to birds and raised chickens, usually 50 at a time, from about the age of 1 up to 15 years. She has had occasional contact with cats growing up, and these were mainly barn cats, but she didn’t like cats and didn’t have too much contact with them. She has had dogs essentially all of her life, and occasionally they may have eaten birds. Growing up on her farm, she had cows and horses. Her husband now raises cows and has over 450 cows and some horses as well. When she was growing up, her brother raised pigs and usually had 20 to 50 at a time. The only implants in her body are the bilateral TMJ Christensen prostheses.

Basic Diagnoses

Bilateral TMJ Christensen total joint prostheses
Bilateral TMJ metallosis and possible foreign body reaction
Rule out hypersensitivity to chromium cobalt alloy
Severe AP mandibular deficiency
AP maxillary deficiency
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency
Class I cuspid-molar relationship but only occluding on two teeth, which are the right cuspids. All other teeth as far as she can tell are out of occlusion
Missing all four first bicuspids and all four third molars secondary to previous extractions
Moderate crowding, lower anterior arch
Headaches 
Severe TMJ pain
Myofascial pain
Earaches 
Tinnitus
Vertigo
Severely decreased oropharyngeal airway space of 3 to 4 mm (normal 11 mm)
High occlusal plane angle

Recommended Treatment

Testing at the Environmental Center at Dallas to evaluate for hypersensitivities to metals in the prostheses

Presurgical orthodontics 
Apply brackets and stabilizing arch wire to the maxillary teeth
Place appliances and stabilizing arch wire to the mandibular teeth
 
Surgery
Removal of bilateral Christensen total joint prostheses
Bilateral TMJ debridement
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses
Bilateral coronoidectomies
Fat grafts to bilateral TMJs (harvested from abdomen)
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla and downgraft it posteriorly

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

I spent about 2-1/2 hours with her today in the consultation. We discussed all the potential risks and complications involving nerves, bleeding problems, infection, malocclusion, etc. She seemed to have a good concept. Having been through three surgeries before, and she has had I believe three to four consultations in the past few months, she has a fairly good grip on the situation. She is to contact us if she has any other questions or concerns. I answered all the questions that she had at this time in reference to her condition and the recommended surgery.

original_note:
LAUREN SCORZAFAVE								02/02/2007

Lauren is 17 years old and is referred by David J. Dattilo, DDS, 320 E. North Ave., Pittsburgh, PA 15212.  Lauren became aware of having some difficulties with her jaws at about the age of 15.  She, however, had been in orthodontic treatment since about the age of 8 years old.  In June of 2005, she underwent surgery for a mandibular advancement.  At 6 months a relapse was already noted.  She had her orthodontic appliances removed in about October of 2005 and then shortly after that the relapse was noted and it has gotten progressively worse for her.  Lauren takes no medications.  She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus or vertigo.  No other joints bother her except she does have some bunions on her toes but they do not bother her.  No known allergies and no problems wearing jewelry.  She does not clench or brux to her knowledge.  She gets headaches maybe two times a week at about a level of 2 that occur predominately in the frontal and near the anterior temple area.  She gets just an occasional pain in her TMJs at about a level of 2 predominately on the right side.  Otherwise, on a daily basis she has no facial pain or headache problems.  She rates her jaw function, diet and disability at 0.  

In frontal view, Lauren has some asymmetry in the nasal structures.  Upper lip length is about 18 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 3 mm on the left and 4 mm on the right.  Maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline.  Nasal tip is off to the left side probably about 3 mm.  Chin is deviated off to the left side probably about 2 to 3 mm but lower midline is pretty much on the facial midline.  Actually in her centric relation the lower dental midline is about 1 mm off to the left and the chin is probably about 4 to 5 mm off to the left.  In profile view, she has some AP maxillary deficiency of about 3 mm.  Chin appears to be retruded by approximately 15 mm.  Chin-neckline is short and chin-neck angle is not well defined.  As Lauren postures her mandible forward as far as she can it significantly improves her facial profile.  Intraorally, Lauren has all of her teeth from the second molars.  The transverse width of the arch is somewhat narrow.  She has a 3 x 3 retainer.  She has adequate attached gingiva around the lower anterior arch.  There is some minor crowding in the lower anterior arch.  In the upper arch, Lauren has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  She has some accentuated curve of Spee in the upper arch.  Perhaps a slight accentuated curve of Wilson in the posterior arch.  In centric relation she hits a little bit prematurely on the right side in the second molar area but then as she bites down harder the left side also comes in contact.  It appears that the molars touch together in her centric relation.  There is some transverse deficiency in the maxillary arch as well.  She has a Class II end-on occlusion being worse on the left side as compared to the right side.  She is probably a 5 to 6 mm Class II on the left and probably 3 to 4 mm Class II on the right.  She has about a 2 mm anterior open bite.  As Lauren postures her mandible forward in a Class I position, she has adequate maxillary width.  Intraorally, the oropharyngeal tissues look healthy.  The uvula is mildly enlarged.  Soft palate and faucial pillars look normal.  Intranasally, turbinates are moderately enlarged particularly on the left side.  Septum is deviated off to the right side.  It appears that she probably has mild to moderate nasal airway obstruction.  The condyle is a little bit wide and nostrils are a little bit narrow particularly on the left side.  She does relate that she does snore.  However, she is not aware of any daytime somnolence except for when she does not go to bed early.  She is not aware of any sleep apnea problems.  To palpation, Lauren has some very minimal tenderness over the masseter muscles in the styloid areas.  She actually has no tenderness over the TMJs.  Joints function smoothly.  Maximal incisal opening is 49 mm.  

Excursive movements are about 7 to 8 mm in each direction.  She has some decreased sensation to the right lower lip and chin area.  This occurred following the previous surgery.


LAUREN SCORZAFAVE			02/02/2007				Page 2

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION:
Panorex shows the presence of bone plates in the mandible bilaterally from the previous sagittal split osteotomies.  Sinuses look relatively clear.  Difficult to evaluate the nasal airway.  Both TMJs show severe condylar resorption vertically and anteroposteriorly.  Condyles appear to be postured on the articular eminences.  Maxillary incisors have significantly foreshortened the roots.  In fact, the crown length is actually longer than the root system.  This also could be true in the lower anterior arch although the angulation of the lower teeth obscures some of the ability to tell the root length. 

Waters view shows that the sinuses appear clear.  Frontal sinuses are large.  Nasal septum is a little bit off to the right side in bony area.  Turbinates appears moderately enlarged.  

Left TMJ tomogram shows that the condylar heads are essentially gone from resorption.  Condyles are postured forward in the fossa probably about 4 mm and are sitting on the inferior posterior aspect of the articular eminence.  There is probably only about 5 mm of vertical height left above the sigmoid notch.  Bone plate is seen in the left sagittal split area.  

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that also is pretty much resorbed away.  Condyle is sitting a little bit forward in the fossa probably about 3 mm.  Articular eminence is a little bit flattened.  Condyle is functioning on the posterior slope of the articular eminence.  The bone plate is seen in the left mandibular sagittal split area.  On opening, there is actually fairly good translation of both condyles.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Lauren has some anterior vertical maxillary excess.  Mandible is AP retruded.  She has a Class II skeletal and occlusal relationship.  She has anterior open bite.  She has a significantly decreased oropharyngeal airway of only about maybe 3.5 mm at the base of the soft palate (normal 11 mm.)  Airway at the base of the tongue, however, is closer to about 9 mm.  She has a high occlusal plane angle facial morphology.  Turbinates extend posterior to the hard palate probably about 4 to 5 mm.  She has a little bit of adenoid tissue still remaining in the nasopharynx but not too much.   

Dental model analysis shows that Lauren has some mild crowding in the lower anterior arch.  Transverse width of both maxillary and mandibular is quite narrow.  She does have a 3 x 3 retainer in the lower anterior arch.  She has a significantly accentuated curve of occlusion in the upper arch.  The incisor and cuspids are elevated relative to the bicuspids and first molar.  Second molars are also elevated due to the abnormal occlusion which she currently has.  She does have a tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 1.5 to 2 mm.

BASIC DIAGNOSES: 
Bilateral TMJ juvenile rheumatoid arthritis. 
Anterior vertical maxillary excess.
AP maxillary deficiency.
AP mandibular deficiency.
AP microgenia.
Class II occlusion.
Anterior open bite.
Occluding only on the molar teeth.
Accentuated curve of Spee in the upper arch.
LAUREN SCORZAFAVE			02/02/2007				Page 3

 Decreased oropharyngeal airway.
 Hypertrophied turbinates.
 Deviated nasal septum.
 Nasal airway obstruction.
 Snoring.
 High occlusal plane angle.
 Severe resorption on maxillary and mandibular incisors.
 Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2 mm.
 Mild crowding of lower anterior arch.
 External nasal deformity. 

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Blood tests.
ANA.
Rheumatoid factor.
Coralline.
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch in two occlusal plane levels.  Align the 4 incisors at a higher level than the posterior teeth and extrude the cuspids 1 to 2 mm to level them with the posterior teeth.  Place brackets on all the teeth but do not incorporate the second molars in the archwire.
Align and level mandibular arch.  Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing 2 mm of tooth structure to correct for the tooth size discrepancy.  Place brackets on all the teeth but do not incorporate the second molars in the archwire. 
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla, move the anterior aspect superiorly, correct the transverse cant in the occlusion and correct the curve of Spee.
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen.)
Bilateral coronoidectomies. 
Augmentation genioplasty.
Bilateral partial nasal turbinectomies.
Nasal septoplasty.
External rhinoplasty. 
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B515 
B515 is 19 years old. Referred by Dr. XXXX. B515 is not real sure when his TMJ problems began, but thinks it may be around the age of 14 of unknown etiology. He did have orthodontics from the age of 7 to 14. Currently, he gets headaches about five times per week in the frontal, temporal, and top of the head bilaterally. He gets TMJ pain at about a level of 8, average daily pain at 8, jaw function at 8, average daily pain at 8, jaw function at 8, diet at 4, and disability at 6. He does get earaches, the left side greater than right side, but no tinnitus, frequent vertigo, but no hearing loss. Other joints that bother him include his shoulders, hips, knees, spine, wrists, and ankle. He has been diagnosed as having kyphosis, scoliosis, Sheuermann's disease that involves degenerative disks in the back, and it has even been suggested that her has Ehlers-Danlos syndrome. That diagnosis has not been confirmed, but apparently the family is going to look into going to the University of Washington for that testing. In addition, he was born with pectum excavatum and had surgery to correct that at the age of 14, though had complications including pneumonia 2 weeks afterwards and a malrotation of the bar. B515 takes the following medications:

Norco 10/325 two tablets per day.
Diazepam 10 mg b.i.d. but he does not take it all the time.
Adderrall 20 mg time release q.d.
Ibuprofen four times per day.
Soma 350 mg two to three times per day.

His primary concerns are as follows:
Dislocation of his jaws and displaced discs in his joint.
Right side of lower jaw growing faster than the left side. Bite continues to shift and change.
TMJ pain, headaches, and myofascial pain.
Facial asymmetry.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
In frontal view, B515 has fairly good symmetry in the middle and upper third face, but some asymmetry in the lower third. Upper lip length is about 21 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is about 2 mm. The maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline. The mandibular dental midline is 6 mm off to the left and chin is about 9 to 10 mm off to the left. There is some increased fullness in his right mandibular angle area extending about 2 to 3 mm wider than the left side. In profile view, B515 has got some AP maxillary deficiency of probably about 4 mm and some sallowness in the cheek area. Chin is retruded about 15 mm. Chin neckline is short, but the chin neckline has reasonable contour. Intraorally, B515 has all of his lower teeth back through the second molars. He has got a lot of pitting and enamel decalcification in his teeth, particularly on the cervical margins. In the upper arch, he has all of his teeth back through the second molars. He has an end-on incisor relatively but a zero over bite. He has a full Class III cuspid relationship on the right side and basically Class I on the left side, but has a cross bite extending from the left central incisor through the second molar. There is a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the left side being 2 mm higher than the right side. Oropharyngeal tissues look normal and healthy. B515 has got high moderate pain over the masseters and mild to moderate pain over most of the rest of the head and neck area except the temporalis muscles are at zero. The incisal opening was 63 mm and where he was comfortable 39 mm. The excursion movement to the right was 7 mm, to the left 4 mm. Joints seem to function relatively smoothly, although he did feel a slight glitch in excursion movements in the left joint.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. He has got a full Class III occlusion on the right side and Class I on the left side. He has got a 3 x 3 retainer in the mandible. Cannot really evaluate the TMJs.

Coronal view shows that the turbinates are relatively moderate in size and sinuses are clear and septum is reasonably straight.
Axial view shows that the sinuses are clear and the turbinates are of relatively normal size and the septum is reasonably straight.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that he has an adenoid mass in the nasopharyngeal area. He has got a high occlusal plane angle and probably a Class III skeletal relationship or close to it. He has an end-on incisor relationship.

Basic diagnoses for B515 includes the following:
Probable Ehlers-Danlos syndrome. Right mandibular condylar hyperplasia and possible disc dislocation.
Left TMJ condylar resorption with articular disc anteriorly displaced and with multiple cystic synovial hyperplasia. Deviated mandibular prognathism off to the left side. Maxillary AP and posterior vertical hypoplasia. Mandibular dental midline shifted 6 mm to the left and chin shifted about 10 mm to the left of the facial midline. Full Class III cuspid relationship on the right side and Class I cuspid on the left side.
Anterior and left posterior cross bites.
Transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the left side being 2 mm short vertically compared to the right side.
Generalized decalcifications and some caries.
TMJ pain.
Headaches.
Myofascial pain. Earaches.
Vertigo.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1.	Restorative dentistry to repair areas of decalcification and caries.
2.	Presurgical orthodontics. a.	Align and level maxillary arch. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. b.	Align and level mandibular arch.
3.	Surgery. a.	Right TMJ high condylectomy and articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor. b.	Left TMJ removal of pathological tissue in the joint with articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor. c.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to shift the mandible back on the facial midline and level it transversely as well as rotate it in a counterclockwise direction. d.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance it forward, down graft it posteriorly and upright the incisors.
4.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
5.	Restorative dentistry if necessary.

B515  MRI
DOB:
DATE OF ACQUISITION:
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is smaller than the opposite side. There appears to be disc tissue in place, but there appears to be multiple small cystic cavities within the disc tissue. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 to 3 mm. There is increased vertical joint space.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is fairly large in size and fills most all of the fossa. There is decreased vertical joint space. The disc is hard to identify.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is small in size with what appears to be loss of vertical and anteroposterior dimension. There is a mass inside the joint cavity that has displaced the condyle inferiorly. There appears to be small cyst lesions within the tissue between the condyle and the fossa. The articular eminence is moderately steep. The condyle is posteriorly positioned in the fossa. There may be some involved discal tissue anterior sitting almost beneath the articular eminence. The condyle is significantly displaced inferiorly with the mass inside the fossa area. The disc is anteriorly displaced, but there is this large cystic lesion posterior to the disc. On opening, there is severe limitation of motion with the condyle coming forward about a third of the normal amount. The articular disc remains anteriorly displaced. The cystic type of lesion overlying the condyle appears to thin some as the condyle loads onto this tissue.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has some flattening on the superior head. The disc may be posteriorly displaced. There appears to be decreased superior joint space. The articular eminence has a moderate inclination and is fairly long in length. There is virtually no translation and only a moderate amount of rotation. There appears to be fluid in the superior joint space. I still believe the disc could be posteriorly displaced. In some views, it appears that the disc could be posteriorly displaced or anteriorly displaced. Vertical joint space is narrow.

IMPRESSION: Left TMJ arthritis and condylar resorption, anteriorly displaced disc and with a cystic formation within the joint space. Right TMJ enlarged condyle with an articular disc may be anteriorly or posteriorly displaced.

B515 MRI
DATE OF ACQUISITION:
Only the dynamic views are shown here. The left TMJ opening sequence shows that there is some arthritis in the joint and the articular disc is anteriorly displaced and nonreducing on opening, although there is restricted translation. The disc is deformed as it is short in anteroposterior length and thickened.

Right TMJ shows a disc that has a little bit better morphology, but it is anteriorly displaced. On opening, it does not reduce. In fact, there is virtually no translation of the right condyle.

IMPRESSION: Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly displaced without reduction with moderate deformation but still may be salvageable. Right TMJ mild arthritis and articular disc anteriorly displaced with reduction.
