original_note:
B140
B140 is 28 years old. B140 is basically a self-referral. B140's TMJ problems began to the best of her knowledge about 5 years ago shortly after she had a tonsillectomy performed. She had a lot of pain in the joints at that time and clicking in the joints. It was not until 3 years ago that she began noticing a change in her occlusion and jaw alignment. She feels she is still in a progressive state of her lower jaw and occlusion continue to change. Most of the pain has subsided that she did have initially. She has no problems with headaches, rates her TMJ pain at 2, but on the average day has no significant pain around the head and neck area. She rates her jaw function at 4, diet at 3, disability at 4. She has no earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, nor hearing loss. The only other joint that bothers her is her right knee where she collects fluid and she has had it drained seven times. The only treatment she has had to date is Invisaline which ended 3 months ago. She has been to see seven doctors in reference to her current situation. She has had a number of different opinions and recommendations.

CLINICAL EVALUATION. In frontal view, B140 has fairly good symmetry in the middle and upper third face, but has some asymmetry in the lower third face. Upper lip length is 25 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is 5 mm. The maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline and so is the mandibular dental midline and chin. She appears to have a little bit more fullness on the right side of her face than the left by probably about 3 or 4 mm. In profile, the maxilla appears to be retruded about 4 to 5 mm. The chin appears to be retruded about 20 mm. The chin neckline is short and chin neck angle is not defined. Intraorally, B140 has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars in place. She has good attached gingiva. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. She has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship being about 4 to 5 mm Class II and she has an overjet of about 4 mm. She has an anterior open bite of about 2 mm. In centric relation, she occludes only her first and second molars bilaterally. B140 has a posterior yaw with the maxilla and mandible being rotated off towards the right side about 2 mm. Transversely, the occlusal plane is level. Oropharyngeal tissues look reasonably healthy except the soft palate is a little bit shorter on the left side than the right side and it appears to be a little scar there probably related to the tonsillectomy. B140 has a wide columella and narrow nostrils. Holding the nostrils open enabled her to breathe much easier through her nose. In profile, B140 tends to tip her head up significantly and posture her chin forward and has a slight head postured forward neck position. To palpation, she has some very mild tenderness over the masseters and TMJ area. Maximum opening is 43 mm, excursion movement to the right 7 mm, to the left 8 mm.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all third molars. She has severe condylar resorption present bilaterally. Turbinates appear moderate in size. The sinuses appear reasonably clear. Coronal radiograph shows that the sinuses are relatively clear with just some slight increased thickness of the mucosal on the floor of the left sinus. Inferior turbinates are quite large.

Axial view shows that the sinuses are clear in this perspective, but the turbinates are quite large and appear to block off a good portion of the airway.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows severe destruction of the condyles with sclerosis on the head of the condyle. There is increased joint space. Articular eminences are fairly shallow.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a severe resorption of the condyle and condylar neck. The articular eminence is relatively flat. There is increased joint space.

Left TMJ coronal view shows severe destruction of the condyles. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 mm. There is increased vertical joint space present.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has undergone more loss of bone than it appears than the left side. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 to 4 mm. There is increased joint space vertically.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that her mandible is postured forward and she was not in centric relation when the x-ray was taken. She has a Class II skeletal and occlusal relationship. Oropharyngeal airway is significantly constricted with an AP airway of only about 3 mm at the base of the tongue. At the soft palate level, she has about 4 mm of airway space. The lower incisors appear over angulated. The upper incisors appear somewhat vertical in orientation. She has a moderately high occlusal plane angle.

Dental model analysis shows there is not any significant tooth size discrepancy, but there is a transverse deficiency in the maxilla. The transverse deficiency is approximately 5 mm.

B140 has had a rheumatology workup. She has HLA-B27 antibodies that have been detected.
Basic diagnoses for B140 would be as follows:
Bilateral TMJ idiopathic condylar resorption compatible with connective tissue autoimmune process.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia. Maxillary AP posterior vertical hypoplasia, but anterior maxillary vertical hyperplasia and transverse hypoplasia. Hypertrophied turbinates. Severely decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of 3 mm (normal AP dimension 11 mm).
Wide columella and narrow nostrils.
Class II occlusion.
Anterior open bite.
Occluding only on the first and second molars bilaterally with all other teeth out of occlusion.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1. Presurgical orthodontics. a.	Align and level maxillary arch. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. b.	Align and level mandibular arch.
2. CT scan of jaws and jaw joints.
3. Surgery. a.	Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. b.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla in a counterclockwise direction and expand. c.	Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen). d.	Bilateral mandibular coronoidotomies. e.	Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies. f.	Columella reconstruction.
4. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B140 MRI
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that lost significant volume vertically and mediolaterally. The head of the condyle is very sclerotic and this extends down into the neck. There is decreased vertical joint space towards the medial aspect of the joint. The lateral rim of the fossa is fairly flat. It is difficult to identify discal tissue.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has lost significant vertical and mediolateral volume. The head of the condyle is quite sclerotic and this continues down into the neck of the condyle. The joint space appears somewhat decreased. Difficult to identify discal tissue.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has lost significant volume vertically and anteroposteriorly. The head of the condyle and remaining neck are quite sclerotic. The articular eminence has a relatively shallow inclination. There appears to be some remnants of the disc anterior and sitting beneath the articular eminence. Otherwise, there is no discal tissue present. At the most lateral aspect of the joint, there appears to be some remnant of disc sitting beneath the articular eminence. On opening, there is restricted translation.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has lost significant vertical volume and some anteroposterior dimension. The articular eminence is moderately shallow. The condylar head is sclerotic. There may be some discal tissue anterior beneath the articular eminence, but severely degenerated. At the most lateral aspect of the joint, there is some remnant of discal tissue, but severely deformed and degenerated. On opening, there is restricted translation.

The imaging in both joints is suggestive of rheumatoid arthritis.
IMPRESSION: Bilateral TMJ severe condylar resorption and degeneration of the displaced articular discs. MRI changes are suggestive of rheumatoid arthritis or a similar connective tissue autoimmune disease.

B140 MRI
Left TMJ coronal view is very sclerotic with no evidence of medullary bone. The lateral rim of the fossa is very flat. There is an amorphous type of tissue within the joint space and no significant evidence of distal tissue present. The mediolateral width of the condyle is very narrow. There is evidence of vertical as well as mediolateral condylar resorption.

Right TMJ coronal view is very sclerotic with no evidence of medullary bone. The lateral rim of the fossa is very flat. There is an amorphous type of tissue within the joint space and no significant evidence of distal tissue present. The mediolateral width of the condyle is very narrow. There is evidence of vertical as well as mediolateral condylar resorption.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is very sclerotic with virtually no evidence of medullary bone. There is significant loss vertically and anteroposteriorly of condylar volume. The articular eminence is moderately flat. There is no evidence of discal tissue.

Right TMJ sagittal view likewise shows a condyle that appears to have lost the vertical aspect of the condyle and some narrowness anteroposteriorly. The articular eminence is moderately flat. There is amorphous tissue within the joint space with virtually no evidence of discal tissue present. There does not appear to be any significant inflammatory process present. On opening, there is restricted translation of the condyle in both joints.

IMPRESSION: Bilateral TMJ severe condylar resorption with sclerosis, absence of the articular disc, and amorphous tissue surrounding the condyles compatible with connective tissue autoimmune disease.

original_note:
B141
B141 is 33 years old and referred by Dr. XXXX. B141 had orthodontics when she was younger at about the age of 12 to 13, and she wore head gear to pull the upper teeth back to meet the lower teeth. She then had a lower retainer that was fixed in place for about 3 years. Her jaw joint problems began at about the age of 29. Her problems began initially in the masseter muscle area particularly on the left side. She was placed in a splint and has been in splint treatment from ages 30 to 33; She has had 3 splints so far. She had her wisdom teeth removed when she was about 21 years old. Her bite has been getting progressively worse with it opening anteriorly.

She rates her headaches at about level 2 in the frontal and temporal areas that occur about once per week. She rates her TMJ pain at 2, average daily pain at 3, jaw function, diet, and disability at 0. She has no earaches, gets occasional tinnitus, but no vertigo or hearing loss Other joints that affect her include her shoulders and right ankle. She does have anxiety and some depression. She is very much aware of clenching and bruxing at nighttime and also during the day. She takes Wellbutrin 300 mg q.d. and has been off this for about 3 months. Primary concerns that B141 have include the following:

Anterior open bite. Lower jaw moving posteriorly. Changes in facial asymmetry. Pain and discomfort. In frontal view, she has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 16 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is about 5 mm. The maxillary midline may be just slightly to the right side and the mandibular dental midline is just slightly to the right side about 1 mm. The chin is probably about 3 to 4 mm toward the right side. In profile view, his upper lip is retruded about 3 to 4 mm. She has a C-shaped curve to the upper lip. The chin is retruded about 10 to 12 mm. The chin neckline is short, although the chin neck angle is fairly well defined. She has a high mandibular occlusal plane angle facial morphology. Intraorally, B141 has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. Transversely, the lower arch is somewhat narrow. She has some lingual tori. She has adequate attached gingiva around the lower anterior teeth and some mild crowding. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. In centric relation, she has a Class II occlusion being probably 5 mm Class II on the right side and maybe 4 on the left side. She has an anterior open bite of about 2 mm. She has an over jet of probably 4 mm. She has a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right maxillary cuspid being about 2 mm higher than the left one. The oropharyngeal tissues look normal and healthy. Intranasally, the turbinates relatively normal. The septum I believe is reasonably straight. Columella is wide and nostrils are narrow. Holding the nostrils open, she could breathe a little bit easier. To palpation, B141 has no tenderness around the head and neck area. On opening and closing, she occasionally will have a click in her joint at little bit quieter than on the right side. She says that when she chews that she is more aware of the clicking and this is something that has just occurred in the last few weeks. The incisal opening is 41 mm and excursion movements are about 5 mm in each direction. Most of her complaints are with pain and discomfort in the left side of her face over the masseter muscle, however, she has a little nodule there that sometimes gets more swollen. Today it is not swollen too much, but it is mainly the left side that keeps her uncomfortable. Recently, Dr. XXXX injected that area with Lidocaine and it took the pain away for a day and B141 felt pretty good.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars, but the rest of the teeth are all present. She appears to be occluding predominantly on the second molars with all of the other teeth out of occlusion. She has arthritis in both TMJ areas with evidence of condylar resorption. The sinuses look relatively clear.

Coronal radiograph shows that she has a significantly deviated nasal septum off towards the right side. The turbinates are relatively normal in size, but practically block the nasal airway, especially blocked on the right side because of the deviation of the septum that it is almost touching against the inferior turbinate. The sinuses look clear.

Axial view shows that the septum is deviated significantly off to the right side. The sinus appear clear. Turbinates appear relatively normal in size but the right side appears blocked because of the deviated septum.

Left TMJ sagittal tomograms show a condyle that has undergone some definite condylar resorption and flattening of the condylar head. There is some anterior beaking present. The articular eminence has a very diminished slope. The joint space may be slightly decreased.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has undergone significant resorption. There is a little bit of anterior beaking. The articular eminence is flattened. There may be some decreased vertical space. In a couple of views, the condyle appear to be mushrooming slightly.

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has undergone significant arthritic changes and there are also arthritic changes in the fossa. There is some osteophytic kind of growths off the condylar head extending off the lateral poles on both sides and also on the midline. There is decreased vertical joint space particularly at the lateral aspect of the joint.

Right TMJ coronal view shows that there has been significant loss of vertical condylar height. There is decreased joint space. Vertically, there is an osteophyte coming off the lateral aspect of the condyle. Lateral cephalometric radiograph appears no to be all the way back in centric relation, but at least partially in the most retruded position. She appears to occlude only on the second molars. Oropharyngeal airway at the soft palate level measures about 5 to 6 mm and at the base of the tongue measures about 6 mm. The maxilla and mandible appear somewhat retruded.

Basic diagnoses would be as follows:
Bilateral TMJ rheumatoid arthritis or other connective tissue autoimmune disease.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia.
Maxillary AP hypoplasia, but vertical hyperplasia.
Class II occlusal and skeletal relationship.
Occluding only on the second molars with all other teeth out of occlusion. Anterior open bite of 2 mm.
Wide columella.
Deviated nasal septum.
Nasal airway obstruction. TMJ pain.
Headaches.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1. Evaluation by rheumatologist.
2. Presurgical orthodontics. a.	Align and level maxillary arch. Open up 1 mm of space between the upper laterals and cuspids. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally. b.	Align and level mandibular arch. Consider some expansion in the first molar and bicuspid areas.
3. CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol).
4. Surgery. a.	Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement in a counterclockwise direction with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. b.	Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen). c.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move it superiorly, expand it, and advance. d.	Nasal septoplasty.
5. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
6. Restorative dentistry to build up maxillary lateral incisors.

B141 MRI
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears a little bit narrow mediolaterally. There could be some discal tissue displaced medially and anteriorly. The joint space appears reasonably good. The lateral rim of the fossa curves about 3 to 4 mm.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is a little bit narrow mediolaterally. It appears that there is some loss of vertical condylar volume. It appears that there may be some discal tissue in place, but there could be a pannus surrounding it. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 mm.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is significantly degenerated particularly at the lateral aspect of the joint and at the medial aspect of the joint. In the middle, it seems to have a little bit better morphology, although there is some anterior beaking present. On the left side, there may be some discal tissue in place, but it is degenerated. The articular eminence is moderately shallow, but there is a little divot in the articular eminence in about the mid part of the vertical length. There is a little bit of fluid in the posterior superior joint space. On opening, there is moderate translation of the condyle and the disc translates with the condyle, but the disc is significantly degenerated.

Right TMJ shows a condyle that appears flattened on top with some anterior beaking. The disc appears to be in position, but there appears to be a reactive pannus surrounding the disc. The articular eminence appears flatter on this side, as though it has undergone some type of resorptive process. There is fluid in the superior joint space and there appears to be a pannus surrounding the articular disc. Right TMJ opening shows that there is fairly good translation of the condyle and the disc, but the disc is severely degenerated with a reactive pannus surrounding it.

MRI findings are compatible with a connective tissue autoimmune type of disease process.
IMPRESSION: Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular discs degenerated with connective tissue autoimmune disease.

original_note:
B479 
B479 is 15 years old, referred by Dr. XX. B479 is in good health with no major medical problems. She does have problems with asthma. She takes Advair and albuterol as needed.

PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Jaws do not function.
Cannot open correctly.
Pain.
B479 has been aware of having clicking and popping in her jaw joints beginning about three years ago of unknown etiology. Last year, her jaw locked closed and has been that way since then. She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo or hearing loss. Other joints that bother her include her ankles, knees, wrists, hips and shoulders. She has some hyperflexibility in a lot of her joints apparently. I need to rule out Ehlers Danlos syndrome. She rates her headaches that she gets about three times a week at about a level of 8 predominately in the temporal and posterior head area. She rates her daily TMJ pain at 6, average daily pain at 4, jaw function at 7, diet at 5, disability at 2.

In frontal view, B479 has good facial symmetry and balance. Upper lip length is about 20 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 1 mm. Maxillary, mandibular and chin midline are all pretty much on the facial midline. In profile view, B479 has some fairly good facial balance vertically and anteroposteriorly. Slight obtuse nasal labial angle. Intraorally, B479 has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. Accentuated curve of Spee in the lower arch is present. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. A little bit of lingual inclination in the upper incisors. She is very close to being Class I cuspid although slight Class II end-on by may be 1 to 2 mm. She has bilateral posterior open bites as a result of the splint therapy. There is an anterior deep bite of about 3 mm. Oropharyngeal tissues appear healthy. The uvula, soft palate and faucial pillars appear relatively normal. Tonsils are present and may be mildly enlarged. She does not report any difficulties breathing through her nose. To palpation, she has moderate pain over her shoulders and mild to moderate pain over her posterior neck and styloid areas. She does not have pain directly over the TMJs. Incisal opening is 43 mm. As she opens she deviates off to the left side about 4 mm. Excursive movement to the left is about 13 mm, right is 6 mm.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
:
Panorex shows the presence of impacted third molars in all four quadrants. The left sinus appears a little bit cloudy. Difficult to evaluate the joints. There is no other significant pathology noted.

Waters view shows that the sinuses appear clear. The bony nasal septum appears relatively straight. The turbinates appear moderate in size.
 

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears to have fairly good morphology. There appears to be decreased superior and posterior joint space. Articular eminence is moderately steep.

Left TMJ tomogram likewise shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology but is posteriorly and superiorly positioned within the fossa. Articular eminence is moderately steep. On opening there is some restricted translation in both joints.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that B479 has a fairly good Class I skeletal and occlusal relationship. Oropharyngeal airway appears relatively normal. She has some retained adenoid tissue in the nasal pharynx.

BASIC DIAGNOSES: Left TMJ mild arthritis and articular disc dislocation without reduction.
Right TMJ mild arthritis and articular disc dislocation with early reduction. Relative mandibular hyperplasia.
Bilateral posterior apertognathia. TMJ pain.
Myofascial pain.
Headaches.
Impacted third molars x4.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Presurgical orthodontics. Align and level maxillary arch. Align and level mandibular arch. Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to maximize occlusion.
Surgical removal of impacted third molars x4.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
Physical therapy is necessary to maximize jaw function.

B479  MRI  
DOB: 
DATE OF ACQUISITION: 
DATE OF INTERPRETATION:  
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has arthritic changes associated with it. There is decreased vertical joint space. There is a little bit of fluid in the superior joint space. It appears that there is discal tissue lateral to the condyle and there is discal tissue anterior to the condyle. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 to 4 mm.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is fairly flat across the top with decreased superior joint space. There appears to be a little hot area around the top of the condyle and medial side. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 to 3 mm.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows some mild arthritis with some flattening on the anterior head and some anterior beaking present. The articular disc is anteriorly displaced and sitting beneath the articular eminence.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle with some arthritis and flattening on the anterior head and some superior peaking. The disc is anteriorly displaced and appears to be sitting beneath the articular eminence. The disc appears as though it may be salvageable yet. On the left side, there appears to be some fluid in the T2 imaging.

IMPRESSION: Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocations without reduction, although the discs still may be salvageable.

original_note:
JULIA JAMES										06-23-03

Julia is 26 years old and referred by Dr. Ken Rotskoff (Missouri Baptist Medical Center, Bldg. C, Ste. 360, 3009 N. Ballas Road, St. Louis, MO  63131, 314-567-5522) and Dr. Peter Crump (24 S. Gore Ave., St. Louis, MO  63119).

Patient History

Her TMJ problems began about 10 years ago, and it was the first time that she developed a closed lock situation. She was able to tolerate things pretty well, but in the last three years, her problems have gotten a lot worse. She had previous orthodontics at the age of 10 to 12 years and had maxillary first bicuspids extracted. She has a low-grade headache most all the time at about a level of 4 on the average day in the frontal and temporal area, but about every week or two it gets as bad as a level of 8, and she becomes pretty much dysfunctional at that pain level. She rates her TMJ pain at 7, overall pain at 8, jaw function at 7, and diet and disability at 6. Maximal incisal opening is 50 mm. She can open reasonably comfortably after her pop at about 36 mm. Excursive movements are about 10 mm in each direction. 

Julie began her current round of orthodontics on 04/30/03. Prior to that, she had had three different splints. She had two soft splints by two previous dentists that didn’t seem to help very much. Dr. Rotskoff made her a splint that postured her mandible forward at night when she slept, and this did help with her headaches and with her jaw pain. Obviously it did not fix the problem, however.

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Julia has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is fairly close to 20 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 1.5 mm. Mandibular dental midline and chin are on the facial midline.

In profile view, upper lip supralabrale is posterior to the subnasale by probably about 3 mm. Maxilla appears AP deficient by probably at least 6 mm. Chin is probably 12 to 15 mm AP-deficient. Chin-neckline is short, although the chin-neck angle is well-defined.

Intraorally, Julia has adequate attached gingiva around the lower anterior teeth. In the upper arch, she is missing the upper first bicuspids. She has basically a Class I cuspid, Class II molar relationship. She has a little bit of spacing between the left maxillary central and lateral incisors.  She has an anterior deep bite of about 2.5 mm. Oropharynx appears clear. Turbinates are moderate in size with the left one being a little bit larger than the right one. Right airway looks really good. Left side is a little bit compromised but not too significantly. Septum may be deviated just a little bit back and forth from side-to-side but not too significant. Luminal valves are just slightly constricted, but in opening the nose, it enabled her to breathe just a little bit better but not really significant differences. 

She has no pain to palpation around the head and neck area. She has a definite loud pop in the left TMJ at 35 mm and a closing click at 15. On the right side, she has an opening pop at less than 10 mm of opening and a closing pop at about 5 mm. She has no earaches, no tinnitus, no vertigo, and no hearing loss. She is aware of clenching at nighttime. 

The only medication she takes is Aleve. 

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows she has a little cloudiness in her left sinus as compared to the right one. Maxillary first bicuspids are absent as well as all four third molars. Condyles really don’t look in too bad of shape. Articular eminence looks much steeper on the left side as compared to the right side. Nasal airway looks reasonably good.

Waters view shows that the sinuses appear clear in all areas. I do not see anything in the left sinus. Has a huge nasal spur on the septum extending into the right side that extends almost to the lateral wall of the nose. This is probably located posteriorly in the nasal septum. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears a little bit narrow anteroposteriorly. Slightly decreased posterior joint space. Articular eminence is fairly steep although not very long in length. Increased anterior joint space. This could be compatible with an anteriorly displaced disc. No particular degenerative changes noted on the condylar head itself. 

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is somewhat posteriorly positioned in the fossa. Articular eminence is quite steep and essentially perpendicular to the horizontal plane. Articular disc could be anteriorly positioned between the condyle and the articular eminence. Could be a little bit of a pseudocyst in the condylar head. 

On opening, there is good translation of both condyles. Significant steepness in the articular eminence can be observed in both joints.

Cephalometrically, Julia has a skeletal Class II jaw relationship but has both a retruded maxilla and mandible. Some vertical discrepancy at the inferior border of the mandible with one side being significantly lower than the other side. Occlusal planes also look as though there is canting on one side compared to the opposite side. However, clinically the transverse occlusal plane appears fairly level.

Basic Diagnoses

Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocations with reductions
AP maxillary deficiency
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency
AP mandibular deficiency
Mild crowding, lower anterior arch
Class I cuspid, Class II molar relationship
Headaches 
Myofascial pain
TMJ pain
Large nasal septal spur blocking a considerable portion of the right nasal airway

Recommended Treatment

Presurgical orthodontics 
Continue to align and level maxillary arch 
Align and level mandibular arch. Consider stripping lower anterior teeth to keep from flaring them any further forward, but be careful not to remove more than 1 to 2 mm of tooth structure maximum so as not to create a reverse tooth-size discrepancy 


Surgery
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors 
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible forward approximately 10 mm
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla forward and downgraft it posteriorly
Removal of extremely large nasal septal spur from the right nasal airway

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

original_note:
RANDELL CRAIG THOMAS							03/08/2006

Craig is 25 years old.  His history began with his current problems 09/29/2005.  He was getting some dental work done and his mouth was open for awhile.  They put a prop in his mouth to try and finish some things up and heard a pop in the jaw joint.  Since that time he has had significant difficulty particularly with opening his jaws and with residual pain factors ongoing.  The only treatment he has had for his joint problems to date includes medications.  He had electrolysis procedure performed.  Electrolysis did not really help at all.  Medications have not really helped either.  When he does take the anti-inflammatory medication this does seem to help him and his pain levels are lowered.  

CURRENT MEDICATIONS: 
Amitriptyline 25 mg 4 tablets at bedtime.
Oroproxin 600 mg 1 to 2 tablets per day. 

He has no trouble with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo or hearing loss.  No other joints bother him.  He does have a significant allergy to penicillin and penicillin-related products.  No one else in the family has TMJ problems that they are aware of.  The problem began around 09/29/2005 involving dental work.  The mouth was propped open somewhat and that is when they heard a click or pop in the left jaw and has had significant pain ever since.  

He has no trouble with headaches.  He rates his TMJ pain of 4, average daily pain at 4.  Jaw function is at 6, diet at 6, disability at 6.  

In frontal view, Craig has good left to right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 25 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is probably about 2 to 3 mm.  Maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline or maybe just a mm to the left.  Mandibular dental midline is pretty much on the facial midline but he only has 3 lower incisors.  The chin is pretty much aligned on the facial midline as well.  It appears that the right mandibular lateral incisor was previously extracted but the rest of the incisors are there.  In profile view, Craig has fairly good facial balance.  The lower jaw may be retruded maybe 6 to 8 mm.  Intraorally, Craig has all of his lower and upper teeth back through the second molars.  It is pretty close to Class I cuspid molar relationship bilaterally.  He has a deep bite of about 5 mm.  Transversely, the occlusal plane is fairly level.  Maximal incisal opening is 25 mm.  Excursive movements are about 1.5 mm in each direction.  Craig has no problems with headaches.  He rates his TMJ discomfort at 4, facial pain and headaches 1, jaw function at 6, diet at 6, disability at 6.  To palpation, he has mild tenderness over the masseters, TMJs and a little bit around the right styloid area.  The joint seems to function fairly smoothly except he can hear a little bit of a glitch in the left joint at times.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION: 
Panorex shows the presence of all the teeth except for the right mandibular central incisor.  He has horizontal impacted third molars in the mandible and vertically partially erupted third molars in the maxilla.  Sinuses look relatively clear.  Condyle is hard to visualize.

Waters view shows that the sinuses appear clear.  The bony septum appears straight.  Turbinates appear moderate in size.  
THOMAS CRAIG RANDELL		03/08/2006				Page 2


Left TMJ tomogram shows what appears to be some arthritic changes in the condyle in this joint.  Decreased superior and posterior joint space.  It appears that there may be some erosion on the anterior aspect of the condyle.  Articular eminence is moderately steep. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears a little flattened anteriorly but hard to evaluate on this x-ray.  There appears to be decreased superior joint space and posterior joint space.  Articular eminence is moderately long.  On opening, there is severe restriction of translation in both joints.

Lateral cephalometric radiographic shows that there is maybe a slight I skeletal relationship.  Oropharyngeal airway measures about 7 mm.  The base of the tongue is closer to 10 mm.

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
Left TMJ arthritis with condylar erosion and articular disc dislocation with severe deformation and adhesions. 
Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with deformation and adhesions. 
Bilateral closed lock (maximum 25 mm of opening.)
TMJ pain.
Masticatory dysfunction. 

TREATMENT OPTIONS: 
Option #1: 
Bilateral TMJ arthroscopy to try and release adhesions and improve jaw opening and function.
Option #2:
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to maintain occlusion.
Option #3:
Bilateral TMJ total joint prosthesis.
Bilateral TMJ fat graft (harvest from the abdomen.)

original_note:
B374
B374 is 38 years old, referred by Dr.  . B374's problems began 16 years ago at the age of 23 of unknown etiology. He believes it may have been related to bruxism. He began to have some symptoms that then got worse. Prior to that time, he had had orthodontics at the age of 15. He went back into orthodontic appliances from 14 to 16 years ago by Dr.  . Following his accident, he had seen several ENT specialists and neurologists relative to his pain and headaches. Finally, he was referred to Dr.  . 15 years ago, he underwent double jaw orthognathic surgery by Dr.   in . Since that time, he has continued to have significant pain problems. He has had approximately 4 splints including one through neuromuscular programming. He has had physical therapy and chiropractic treatment well. 2 years ago, he was involved in a motor vehicle accident that significantly exacerbated his symptoms. B374 rates his headaches at a level of 7, occurring in the temporal and posterior head area. He rates his TMJ pain at 9, average daily pain at 10, jaw function and diet at 3, and disability at 9. The only medication he currently takes is Amrix (cyclobenzaprine) 15 mg q.h.s. He gets daily earaches, occasional tinnitus, and significant vertigo problems. No other joints bother him. Interestingly, when he eats or chews, he will get muscle spasms and pain through his shoulders, particularly on the shoulder blades, as well as in the abdomen area and in his legs. He believes he does perform some clenching at night time.

Primary concerns that B374 has at this time includes the following:
Increased crepitus in the joints.
Muscle imbalance in the TMJ area and neck after the car accident 2 years ago.
Vertigo.
TMJ pain. Headaches.
Teeth do not fit together.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
In frontal view, B374 has fairly good facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 25 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is 3 mm. The maxillary midline is probably 1 mm off to the right side. The mandibular dental midline is a little bit more centered. The chin may be just slightly off to the right side a couple of millimeters. In profile view, B374 has some AP maxillary deficiency of probably a good 4 mm. The chin is retruded probably 10 mm. Intraorally, B374 has all of his lower teeth back through the second molars. The teeth appear to be reasonably aligned. He has good attached gingiva. In the upper arch, he seems to have all of his teeth back through the second molars. He has a Class III end-on occlusal relationship on the right side by probably 3 to 4 mm as well as on the left side. He appears to occlude only on the maxillary first molar, mandibular second molar and hits about the same on the right side. He has an anterior open bite of about 2 to 3 mm anteriorly, but it is greater on the left side in the cuspid area and less on the right side in the cuspid area. Transversely, the maxillary arch appears fairly level, and it appears there is a little bit of asymmetry in the lower arch with it being down about 2 mm in the cuspid area on the left side. He has some transverse maxillary deficiency with a tendency towards crossbite. He has a tendency towards a crossbite anteriorly and posteriorly. Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively normal. Intranasally, the septum anteriorly is deviated off towards the left side. The left turbinate at least anteriorly appears relatively moderate in size and the right side appears somewhat enlarged. Maximal incisal opening is about 43 mm and he can get to about 40 mm without significant discomfort. Excursion movements are about 11 mm in each direction. In excursion, he is able to generate significant crepitation, actually being greater on the left side than the right side. Crepitation on the left side is worse if he protrudes the mandible some and then does the excursion movements.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. All the rest of the teeth are all present. The sinuses look relatively clear. He has bone plates in the anterior maxilla and 2 bone screws on each side of the ramus. Right condyle quite small compared to the left side and appears more arthritic. The bony septum looks reasonably straight. Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that the oropharyngeal airway looks reasonably good. He appears to occlude only on the maxillary first molars and the mandibular second molars. He has an open bite involving the rest of the teeth. He appears to have some retrusion of the maxilla and mandible. Axial views shows that the sinuses are clear. Turbinates appear moderately enlarged. The septum seems to shift off towards the left side anteriorly. Otherwise, it seems fairly straight. Turbinates are moderately enlarged. TMJ sagittal views shows a left condyle that has fairly reasonable morphology. There is some flattening on the head of the condyle. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Joint space looks reasonably good. Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that appears arthritic with perhaps some slight osteophytic development on the top of the condyle. The condyle is narrow anteroposteriorly. There appears to be reasonable joint space. Joint spaces may be affected by the use of the splint that he wears at night. Articular eminence is moderately steep. There appears to be some anterior beaking present. Left TMJ coronal view shows he has a little extension on the lateral pole of the condyle out laterally, showing that he may likely have bone-on-bone contact at that area, particularly if he protrudes the mandible slightly. Joint space is quite narrow towards the lateral aspect of the joint. Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is more arthritic. There appears to be an extension off the lateral pole of the condyle. Joint space otherwise looks reasonably good, except that it is somewhat decreased at the lateral aspect of the joint. The condyle appears more arthritic on the right side as compared to the left side. Dental model analysis is based on the models taken on . On these models, he has an anterior open bite of about 4 mm. He has bilateral posterior cross bites and a tendency towards an anterior cross bite. He has a Class III end-on cuspid relationship. His occlusion actually fits together a little better now than it did in February, at least relative to the transverse dimension of the maxilla and mandible. There does not appear to be any significant tooth size discrepancy. Basic diagnoses for B374 would be as follows:

Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc medially displaced. Right TMJ advanced arthritis and articular disc dislocated without reduction with severe deformation and degeneration. AP maxillary hypoplasia.
Transverse maxillary hypoplasia.
AP mandibular hypoplasia. Class III end-on occlusion. Anterior open bite of 3 mm.
Transverse cant of the mandibular arch with the left side being 2 mm lower than the right side. Severe TMJ pain.
Headaches. Myofascial pain. Vertigo.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1. Presurgical orthodontics. a. Align and level maxillary arch. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies.
b. Align and level mandibular arch.
2. CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol).
3. Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla and expand it transversely. Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen).
Bilateral coronoidotomies. Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies.
Nasal septoplasty.
4. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B374 MRI AGE: 38/M
DOB:
Date of Acquisition: Date of Interpretation:
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with good mediolateral width. The condyle appears to have good morphology. The disc appears to be medially displaced. There appears to be decreased joint space at the lateral aspect of the joint. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 mm.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears much smaller in size and more narrow mediolaterally. The condyle appears arthritic with some sclerosis on the head of the condyle. There seems to be reasonable joint space, but the disc appears to be a little anteriorly and medially displaced. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 mm.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology. A little bit of flattening on the posterior head. Articular eminence is moderate in inclination. The articular disc for the most part appears to be in reasonably good position. A little bit of fluid in the superior and inferior joint space around the posterior aspect of the joint. On opening, there is good translation of the condyle and articular disc. At the lateral aspect of the joint in the closed views, the disc is not very evident, indicative of a medial displacement.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that appears more arthritic. The condyle is smaller in size than the opposite side. There is actually fairly good joint space present, but the articular disc is anteriorly displaced and sitting beneath the articular eminence. The disc is significantly deformed and short anteroposteriorly with significant loss of the intermediate zone. There appears to be an inflammatory process perhaps in the bilaminar tissue. On opening, there is moderately good translation of the condyle, but the articular disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction.

IMPRESSION:
Left TMJ partial medial displacement of the articular disc. Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocated anteriorly with significant deformation and non-reducing.

original_note:
ERIN ROBBINS									10/04/2007

Erin is 42 years old.  Erin is essentially a self-referral through her husband.  She has had previous jaw and orthognathic surgery as well as shoulder.  She had a septoplasty.  She also has had her tonsils out.  She also has had knee surgery on the right knee.

PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Pain around the jaws particularly on the left side.
Difficulties eating and chewing. 
Sleep apnea.
Chronic fatigue.

Erin's history includes that at the age of 4 she fell 14 feet and struck her face and had multiple facial fractures requiring her jaws to be wired together.  She had orthodontics from about the age of 12 to 14 then she had it again at the age of 19.  In 1983, Dr. Pete Walters performed surgery including TMJ surgery as well as maxillary and mandibular surgery.  The surgery lasted 14 hours.  She had orthodontics again at the age of 32 to improve her bite.

She currently gets earaches, left side worse than the right side and she has tinnitus on the left side worse than right side.  She experiences vertigo particularly when walking down stairs although she has been tested and the doctors tell her that she does not have vertigo.  She may have some hearing loss but again she has been told that the testing shows that her hearing is fairly normal.  The only other joints to cause her any problems include her shoulders.  

She has been diagnosed with having obstructive sleep apnea.  She is aware of clenching her teeth at night and during the day.  She rates her average TMJ pain at about 3, average daily pain at 7, jaw function at 7, diet at 7 and disability at 6.  Oropharyngeal tissues as best as I can see look fairly healthy.  The uvula is relatively mildly elongated and the soft palate maybe moderately elongated.  The faucial pillars are moderately constricted.  Turbinates are normal to moderate enlargement with the left one being a little bit larger than the right one.  The septum is reasonably straight or at least susceptible.  She appears to have adequate nasal airway bilaterally.  An interesting phenomenon that Erin has is when she gets pain her ear turns red on the lower two-thirds of the ear.  She went to see an ENT doctor and they were unable to come up with any specific cause of that. 

In frontal view, Erin has fairly good left to right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 22 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is probably about –2 mm.  Maxillary, mandibular and chin midline are all pretty much along the facial midline.  In profile view, Erin has some significant AP maxillary deficiency probably about a good 6 mm probably at the incisor level.  Mandible is retruded probably about 15 mm.  Chin-neckline is short and chin-neck angle is moderately well defined.  Upper lip supralabrale is probably about 3 to 4 mm posterior to subnasale.  She has high occlusal plane angle facial morphology.  Intraorally, Erin is missing lower first bicuspids but has the rest of her teeth back through the second molars present.  Teeth are fairly well aligned and leveled.  Transverse width of the arch may be slightly narrow.  She has good attached gingiva.  In the upper arch, likewise she is missing the upper first bicuspids.  She has a slightly reverse curve of Spee in the upper arch.  
ERIN ROBBINS				10/04/2007				Page 2

It is basically a Class I cuspid molar relationship.  She has a deep bite of about 3 to 4 mm.  To palpation, Erin has some moderate discomfort over the left masseter and mild to low moderate over the temporalis, masseters, TMJs, left styloid area and left SCM.  Maximal incisal opening is 37 mm but that creates significant pain on the left side.  She can open about 28 mm without significant pain.  Excursive movement to the right was less than 1 mm, left 7 mm.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION:
Panorex shows the absence of all 4 third molars and all 4 first bicuspids.  The sinuses appear relatively clear.  There are wires in the maxilla in the 4 areas from the previous surgery.  There are some pins in the symphysis area from previous genioplasty. There are also some pins in the ramus area, one on each side down towards the angle area.  

Left TMJ shows that there is some heterotopic bone enlargement of the condyle.  The condyle is severely deformed.  The right side of the condyle appears smaller in size as though it has undergone some osseous changes and degenerative change.

Waters view shows that she has wires in the maxilla.  Sinus appears relatively clear.  There is a little bit of cloudiness in the left side and there appears to be an image that appears to be some fatty tissue or a little mucocele on that side.  Ethmoid and frontal sinuses appears relatively clear.  The nasal airway appears good.  

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is severely deformed.  It is larger than the size of the fossa.  Articular eminence is short in vertical length.  Joint space is essentially compromised.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is significantly malformed but appears to have a superior extension off it with some anterior beaking present.  Difficult to identify any joint space.  There is radiopaque aspect of the condyle although down below the top of the condyle.  The condyle is narrow anteroposteriorly.  On opening, there is no translation of the left TMJ and there is some mild to moderate translation on the right side with it coming down along the posterior slope of the articular eminence. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Erin has an AP deficiency of both the maxilla and mandible.  She has a moderately high occlusal plane angle.  Oropharyngeal airway measures about 5 mm at the base of the tongue and at the soft palate level.

original_note:
HELEN WELLS									11/14/2005

Helen is 52 years old, referred by Dr. Wayne Ku, 6101 Chapel Hill Blvd., #203, Plano, TX 75093.  Helen has had previous surgery for gallbladder removal in October of 2002.  Helen is in good health.  Helen has had problems with headaches since childhood of unknown etiology.  Her mother also has migraines.  Helen had orthodontic treatment at the age of 22 for approximately two years in Rochester, NY.

PRIMARY CONCERNS
Teeth and jaws do not align.
Headaches.
Nasal airway obstruction.
Severe snoring.
Awakes at night, unable to catch her breath.
Predominate mouth breather.

MEDICATIONS:
Corgard 40 mg, which is a beta blocker for migraine headaches.
Cholesterol medication. 
Imitrex which controls her migraines quite well.

She has no problems with earaches, gets occasional tinnitus but no vertigo or hearing loss.  She has a reaction to aspirin.  She is not aware of clenching or bruxism.  She gets headaches when not on Imitrex that can escalate to a level of 10.  On Imitrex, she can function fairly well.  Without Imitrex she would be totally debilitated.  She rates her TMJ pain at 0, average daily pain at 3, although it can escalate to 10, jaw function at 0, diet at 2.  Other joints that bother her include her shoulder occasionally in which she would get some tendonitis and her right knee bothers her occasionally.  

In frontal view, Helen has good left to right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 20 mm.  Upper tooth to lip relationship is about 2.5 mm.  Maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline as is the chin.  Mandibular dental midline is pretty much on the facial midline but she has only 3 lower incisors as 1 tooth was extracted probably for orthodontic reasons.  She also has had her wisdom teeth removed.  In profile view, Helen has some slight AP maxillary deficiency probably about 3 to 4 mm.  The chin appears to be retruded by probably 15 to 18 mm.  Chin-neckline is very short and chin-neck angle is not well defined.  It appears that probably the right lower central incisor was extracted.  The lower incisors are in lingual inclination in relative to the cuspids.  She appears to have adequate attached gingiva around the lower anterior arch.  She has the rest of her teeth back through the second molars.  In the upper arch, she has had first bicuspids extracted bilaterally.  She has an end-on Class II cuspid relationship bilaterally by probably about 3 mm.  She is super Class II in the molar area.  She has almost a 2 mm overbite relationship anteriorly.  When Helen occludes in centric relation, she hits only on her second molar and all other teeth are out of occlusion.  Helen had a UPPP procedure and a tonsillectomy done at the age of 33.  This helped her with her sore throats but did not help her significantly with her snoring or sleep apnea problems.  

HELEN WELLS				11/14/2005				Page 3


To palpation, she has some mild tenderness of her TMJs and styloid area but otherwise no areas of pain or tenderness.  On opening, she had a click in the right TMJ about 25 mm of opening but difficult to identify the closing click.  The left joint seems to function relatively smoothly.  On excursive movements, she did have some popping noises in the right side.  Helen does sleep on her side.  When she lays on her back, she cannot breathe very well and has to elevate her head.  Intranasally, the turbinates are moderately to severely enlarged relative to the size of the nasal airway.  In fact, the left turbinate appears to have chronic inflammatory changes.  The septum kicked off at the base towards the left side and she has a spur in that area.  She has constricture of the luminal valves intranasally.  Holding those valves open did enable Helen to breathe easier through her nose.  At least anteriorly the right turbinate appeared larger than the left most likely because the septum is deviated off towards the left side.  Tooth size analysis shows that there is a Bolton's difference of a +4.5 mm with the upper arch being larger than the lower arch primarily due to the missing mandibular right central incisor.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all 4 third molars and right central incisors as well as maxillary first bicuspids.  She has root canals on the maxillary right lateral incisor cuspid, mandibular right second bicuspid first molar and mandibular left first molar.  She has a significant amount of bone loss around the mandibular right first molar and perhaps bifurcation involvement.  The sinuses look reasonably clear but difficult to assess.  She has what appears to be relatively severe arthritis at least on the right joint and to a lesser degree in the left joint.  She appears to have hypertrophied turbinates and a wide septum. 

Waters view shows that the sinuses appear relatively clear.  The bony septum appears relatively straight.  The turbinates appear enlarged particularly on the left side.  

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears to be maybe postured slightly forward in the fossa but has a decreased superior joint space.  Articular eminence is moderately shallow.  There appears to be some arthritic changes in this joint.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is significantly posteriorly positioned in the fossa with a decreased posterior and superior joint space.  Articular eminence is a little bit steeper on this side.  The condyle appears much smaller anteroposteriorly as compared to the opposite side.  On opening, the condyle seems to translate reasonably well.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that the maxilla is retruded and the mandible is really retruded.  The UPPP procedure is noted on the soft palate.  However, she has only about maybe 1 mm of airway space between the soft palate and posterior pharyngeal wall.  At the base of the tongue she has probably about 3 mm of airway space.  She has a high occlusal plane angle.  

BASIC DIAGNOSES
Significant AP mandibular deficiency.
AP maxillary deficiency.

HELEN WELLS				11/14/2005				Page 3


Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
Class II end-on occlusion. 
High occlusal plane angles. 
TMJ pain.
Severe headaches.
Severe decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Severe snoring.
Moderate to severe sleep apnea.
Missing maxillary first bicuspids, mandibular right central incisor and all 4 third molars.
Significant periodontal problems associated particularly with the mandibular right second molar.
Significant reversed tooth size discrepancy of +4.5 mm (secondary to missing mandibular right central incisor.) 
Hypertrophied turbinates.
Significantly deviated nasal septum with septal spurring. 
Significant nasal airway obstruction.
Moderate crowding of lower anterior arch.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT
MRI of left and right TMJs.
Periodontal treatment and management of the mandibular right first and second molars. 
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch.  Slenderize maxillary lateral incisors removing 1 mm of tooth structure from each tooth to help compensate for the significant tooth size discrepancy.  Close the spacing.
Align and level mandibular arch.  Open up 1/2 mm of space between each of the incisor teeth extending from cuspid to cuspid.  This will help compensate for the significant tooth size discrepancy.
CT scan of TMJs and jaws (TMJ Concepts protocol.) 
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advance with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. 
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla in a counterclockwise direction and stabilize with bone plates and hydroxyapatite grafting.
Bilateral coronoidectomies.
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest fat grafts from the abdomen.)
Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies.
Nasoseptoplasty.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion. 
Restorative dentistry to build up the lower 3 incisors to eliminate the spacing in that arch.

original_note:
LINDSEY HISLOP									07/10/2008

Lindsey is 39 years old.  Referred by Dr. Mark Piper and also Dr. Jim Tyree, who is a dentist in Austin.  To Lindsey's knowledge, her TMJ problems began about a year ago.  She noted her bite began to change and has continued to worsen.  She has developed some speech associated problems since then including a lisp.  Her bite continues to shift.  Initially, when her joint problems began, she had clicking and popping in the left TMJ, but not the right side.  This clicking and popping has since gone away.  At this time, she feels her joints function relatively smoothly.  She rates headaches that she gets about 2 times a week at about a level of 2 in the frontal area.  She rates her TMJ pain at about 1, average daily pain at 1,  jaw function and diet at 2, disability at 1.  She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, or hearing loss.  No other joints bother her.  She is aware of clenching and bruxism at nighttime.  The only treatment she has had so far was a night guard made in December 2007 that she wears at night.  

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Lindsey has fairly good facial symmetry in the middle third face.  Upper lip length is about 23 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship appears to be about 2.5 mm.  The maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline as is the mandibular dental midline.  Chin is off the right side probably about 3 mm.  In profile view, Lindsey has fairly good projection of the upper lip and maxilla.  The mandible appears retruded and chin neckline appears short, although the chin neck angle is reasonably well defined.  Chin appears to be retruded by 10 to 12 mm.  Intraorally, Lindsey has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars.  In the upper arch, she likewise has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  In centric relation, the lower dental midline is 1 mm off to the right side and chin is 3 to 4 mm off to the right side.  She hits on the right side in the second molar area perhaps distal to the first molars, but all other teeth are out of occlusion.  She has about a 2 mm anterior open bite and on the left side she has a 3 to 4 mm open bite.  She has a slight Class II cuspid relationship on the right side by probably 2 mm and on the left side by probably 3 to 4 mm.  Oropharyngeal tissues look healthy.  Fascial pillars may be somewhat constricted and they are slightly posteriorly positioned.  She has some mild snoring, but no daytime somnolence or sleep apnea problems. To palpation, she has no tenderness around the head and neck area.  Incisal opening is 40 mm.  Excursion movements are 5 mm in each direction. 
RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION (from Dr. Tyree).  I-CAT evaluation:  Right TMJ shows severe condylar resorption with almost complete loss of the condylar head.  Lateral rim of the fossa appears to be intact.  Joint space looks almost excessive.  There is a significant osteophyte sitting superiorly off the anterior aspect of the head of the condyle. 
Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is severely degenerated.  It has got anterior beaking and a posterior osteophyte present.  There are erosions in the lateral aspect of the condyle.  Nasal septum is deviated off to the left side and there is a septal spur present.  
Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has severe osteoarthritis.  There appears to be decreased joint space in some of the views and a lot erosions on the condylar head.  There are some 3-D images of the joints that show significant arthritic changes present.  The 3-D shows significant resorption and arthritic changes of both condyles in the coronal views. 
MRI of the right TMJ shows a condyle that has undergone severe resorption with almost total loss of the condyle.  The remaining condylar neck appears to have some osteophytes seen off of it.  The disc appears to be anteriorly displaced, but significantly degenerated.  She has excessive fluid in the superior joint space and also in the inferior joint space.  The coronal view shows severe condylar resorption as well.  
Left TMJ MRI sagittal view shows severe degenerative changes.  There are some dark areas within the joint due to perhaps some calcifications within the joint.  There are erosions on the head of the condyle.  There is fluid in the superior joint space.  There are osteophytes extending superiorly and anteriorly.  The disc is anteriorly displaced and severely degenerated. I do not believe there is any way to save these discs, particularly in their current situation.  The articular eminences are quite flat which may be indicative of a connective tissue autoimmune kind of disease process.  She apparently has no other symptoms in relation to this process.
Lindsey had a tomogram taken 7/10/08 in our office.  Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle postured significantly forward in the fossa almost functioning on the articular eminence. There is significantly increased posterior joint space.  Left TMJ tomogram showed the condyle postured forward in the fossa.  On opening, there is fairly good translation of both joints.  
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars.  Sinuses look relatively clear, although there could be something perhaps in the left maxillary sinus.  Both condyles look quite arthritic and have undergone resorption, particularly on the right side.  She demonstrates an anterior open bite and posterior open bites all the way through except for the right second molars that appear to occlude some.  
Waters view shows that the turbinates appear fairly large in the sinuses.  The maxillary sinuses and frontal sinuses appear fairly clear.  
Clinically, Lindsey has a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right cuspid being about 1.5 to 2 mm higher than the left cuspid.  Dental model analysis shows that she has an anterior open bite.  She has some transverse maxillary deficiency particularly in the bicuspid area and slightly in the first molar area.  Her upper left lateral incisor appears smaller than normal.  Recommended orthodontic treatment would be as follows:

Align and level maxillary arch.  Expand the bicuspids and first molars. Tip lateral roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies.  Open up 1 mm of space between the left lateral incisor and cuspid to correct for tooth size discrepancy. 
Align and level mandibular arch.  

Basic diagnoses would be as follows:
Bilateral TMJ severe arthritis being worse on the right side than the left side.
AP mandibular deficiency. 
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency. 
Anterior open bite. 
Occluding only the right second molars. 
Transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right side being 1.5 mm higher than the left side. 
Chin deviated off to the right side about 3 mm.   


Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Presurgical orthodontics.  
 Align and level maxillary arch.  Expand the bicuspids and first molars. Tip lateral roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies.  Open up 1 mm of space between the left lateral incisor and cuspid to correct for tooth size discrepancy. 
Align and level mandibular arch.  
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance it in a counterclockwise direction.  
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen or the buttock).
Bilateral partial coronoidectomies. 
3.   Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
BRENDA NELSON 									03/14/2007

Brenda is 50-year-old, referred by Drs. Arnie and Tom Pitts.  She has been diagnosed as having fibromyalgia.  Previous surgeries include a breast implant in 1994.  She has had no problems with those.  She smokes 1 pack of cigarettes per day and has done that for about 3 years.  She is in good health except for the jaw problems.  She has had an adverse reaction to sulfur. 

PRIMARY CONCERNS:
TMJ pain.
Difficulties eating and chewing.
Pain down through the neck and shoulder area.
Sleep apnea. 

CURRENT MEDICATIONS:
Effexor.
Wellbutrin. 
Amitriptyline. 
Nexium.

She gets headaches in the frontal and temporal area at about a level of 5.  These occur about 2 times per week.  She rates her headaches at 7, average daily pain at 6, jaw function at 6, diet at 5 and disability at 3.  The fibromyalgia is all through her body and not just confined to the head and neck area.  She does clench and brux at nighttime and also during the day.  

CURRENT HISTORY:  
Brenda just became aware of having significant joint problems in about 2006 that she relates to significant stress.  In the past year she has had Veneers placed on her teeth.  In the past she has had a mouthguard x2 to help keep her from clenching.  She put in a nightguard because she was bruxing significantly and her dentist identified this.  Brenda does not remember in the past when her joints would click and pop.  She has had chiropractic treatment.  She has also ART (active release therapy.)  In addition, she has had muscle massages intraorally which she found helpful to help decrease her pain and tension. 

In frontal view, Brenda has good left to right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 25 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 0.  Maxillary, mandibular and chin midline are all pretty much on the facial midline.  Nasal tip is just deviated a little bit off to the right side about 2 mm.  In profile view, Brenda has AP deficiency in the maxilla of about 6 to 8 mm.  She has retruded at least 20 mm.  Chin-neckline is extremely short and no significant definition of the chin-neck angle.  Brenda tends to posture her head in an upward and forward position.  Intraorally, Brenda has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars except she is missing mandibular left first molar and has a bridge replacing that tooth.  In the right side she may be missing the first molar and the second and third molars have drifted forward into the first and second molar position.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  She has a full Class II malocclusion.  She has a reverse curve of Spee in the upper arch.  She has an anterior deep bite of about 4 mm.  Transversely, the occlusal plane is fairly level.  Oropharyngeal tissues look healthy.  
BRENDA NELSON				03/14/2007				Page 2

Uvula looks relatively normal as does the soft palate but she has significant constricture of her faucial pillars and significant decrease of the transverse width of the oral airway.  Intranasally, the turbinates appear moderately enlarged.  It does appear she has a passable airway.  The septum looks reasonably straight.  To palpation, she has moderate pain over the left masseter, left styloid area and milder pain over the left TMJ.  On opening, she can hear some crepitus sounds in the left joint and on the right side I could palpate a glitch.  Maximal opening is about 37 mm.  Excursive movements are 8 mm in each direction.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION:
Panorex shows the absence of the maxillary third molars.  She is missing mandibular right first molar with the second and third molar drifted forward and on the left side it appears she is missing first molar and second molar with the third molar drifted forward supporting a bridge from the second bicuspid to the third molar with a molar replacement pontic.  She has a root canal in the maxillary right first molar.  Sinuses appear relatively clear.  Condyles appear arthritic.  It is actually difficult to see the right one on this radiograph.  

Waters view shows that she has a deviated septum.  The turbinate on the right side appears somewhat enlarged.  Her hair clip was left in and sits over the left side of the nose.  Sinuses appear clear. 

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears to have lost some vertical volume.  There is some anterior beaking present.  Joint space actually looks reasonably good.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  There could be slight posterior joint decrease but not too significant. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears a little bit arthritic.  It is posteriorly positioned in the fossa.  There may be some decreased joint space vertically but not too significant.  On opening, there is moderate translation of the left TMJ.  It also appears that the right joint translates fairly good.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Brenda has a retruded maxilla and a more severely retruded mandible.  There is Class II skeletal and occlusal relationship.  She has a significantly decreased oropharyngeal airway with a dimension of 3 mm at the most at the soft palate level and also at the base of the tongue.  She has a high occlusal plane angle facial morphology.  Reverse curve of Spee in the upper arch is about 2 to 3 mm.  Tooth size analysis reveals a Bolton's difference of 1.5 mm, although hand articulation shows that the discrepancy may be larger at least perhaps 3 or 4 mm.  She has Veneers on her anterior teeth and some of the teeth have built up considerably such as the left lateral incisor that probably has a labial lingual width at the incisor tip of almost 4 to 5 mm.  The right central incisor likewise has an increased thickness of the crown that measures a little bit better than 3 mm labial to lingual.

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
Bilateral TMJ severe arthritis.
Significant AP mandibular hypoplasia.
AP maxillary hypoplasia.
BRENDA NELSON				03/14/2007				Page 3

Posterior vertical maxillary hypoplasia.
Transverse maxillary hypoplasia.
Class II occlusion.
High occlusal plane angle.
Severely decreased oropharyngeal airway and AP dimension of 3 mm (normal 11 mm.)
Sleep apnea.
 TMJ pain.
 Headaches.
 Missing mandibular left first and second molars with the third molar in the second molar
       position and pontic in the first molar position. 
 Missing mandibular right first molar with the second and third molars drifted forward.
 Veneers in place that are over contoured on some of the teeth.
 Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 1.5 or mm. 
 Reverse curve of occlusion in the upper arch. 
 Slight increase curve of occlusion in the lower arch.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Remove Veneers where necessary to allow access for orthodontic treatment.
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch.  Open some spacing of at least 1 mm around each upper lateral incisor.  Tip lateral incisors roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies.
Continue to align and level mandibular arch.  Consider slenderizing the lower anterior teeth removing about 1 or 2 mm of tooth structure at the most.
CT scan (TMJ Concepts protocol.)
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses.  
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla in a counterclockwise direction and expand.
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen.)
Bilateral coronoidectomies.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
Dental treatment to provide whatever restorations are necessary.
