original_note:
B423 
B423 is 44 years old and is in good health with no major medical problems. Primary concerns involve primarily some facial aesthetics and some functional factors.

PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Facial asymmetry.
Nose shifted to the right.
Bumps on the nose.
Asymmetry around the chin area. Noises in the joints with popping in the left joint and crunching noises on the right side. Left TMJ pain and discomfort.
Right TMJ or jaw discomfort.
Less definition of the mandibular angle on the right side as compared to the left side and desires to have the right side look as prominent as the left side.
Jaws are retruded. Difficulty getting lips together and dimpling of the chin when he does put his lips together. Difficulty chewing on the left side. Nose is not prominent enough in a forward direction and width-wise towards the nasion. Left mandible is shorter vertically than the right mandible particularly in the angle area.
14. He wants a strong chin. 15. Gingival recession

PREVIOUS HISTORY: B423 had a left zygomatic orbital fracture 10 years ago. This was reduced and corrected by Dr. XX. 9 years ago, Dr. XX performed a LeFort I osteotomy as well as bilateral mandibular ramus sagittal splint osteotomies and chin advancement. After that, there was some additional work done to build up the left side of the mandible in the angle area with HA grafts. Finally, a block was placed on the left side along with some other augmentation material. The HA block was actually done before some of lateral buildup was performed. He has had orthodontics x2. The first time from 13 to 15 years ago at the age of 16. The second round of orthodontics was from 14 to 12 years ago at the age of 28. He had lingual appliances at that time and apparently it has caused some extrusion of teeth about a mm or so that perhaps created some periodontal concerns.

B423 takes no medications. He has no problems with earaches, tinnitus or vertigo. The only other joint that bother him is his right knee. Also, his neck bothers him as he does take chiropractic treatment to treat the cervical problems. His neck has improved significantly with the chiropractic treatment he is currently undergoing. He may clench his teeth sometimes at night. He does not have any trouble with headaches. When he uses his jaw he is aware of clenching noises in the right joint and popping noises in the left joint. He rates his TMJ discomfort at a level of 2 to 3 and sometimes the right side is worse than the left side. The right side is particularly a little bit more sensitive when he opens wide. Average daily pain about a level of 2, jaw function at 3, diet a 2, disability at 2.

 
In frontal view, he has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. B423  is very pleased with his orbital position. He has a little prominence of his lateral nasal bone on the right side of the nose. His nose is just a little bit off to the right side probably about 2 mm. Maxillary dental midline is also off to the right side by at least 2 mm as is the mandibular dental midline. Chin may be just slightly off to the right side as well. The inferior border of the mandible on the right side is a little bit longer vertically in the chin area about 1 to 2 mm perhaps and back at the angle probably 3 to 4 mm vertical difference with the right side being lower. As B423 shifts his mandible about 2 mm to the left, it improves his facial balance transversely. He has some lip incompetence when he relaxes his lips of about 8 mm. Upper lip length is about 21 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 1.5 to 2 mm. He has a transverse cant in the occlusal plan with the left side being about 2 mm higher than the right side. Slight crossbite tendency on the right side. Otherwise, very close to being a Class I. B423 has a yaw in the position of the maxilla and mandible with the posterior maxilla and mandible being shifted about 2 to 3 mm to the right. Intranasally, turbinates appear relatively normal in size. Nasal septum is deviated significantly off to the left side although the tip of the nose is off to the right side. To palpation, he has no tenderness around the head and neck area. Right joint seems to function relatively smoothly. In the left joint there is actually an audible click at about 20 mm of opening and probably closing click at about 10 mm. Maximal opening is 51 mm. Excursive movements are about 10 mm in each direction.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
:
Panorex shows the absence of all 4 third molars. All the rest of the teeth are present. He has a 3 x 3 retainer in the lower arch. He has a couple of bone screws in the right ramus, one in the left ramus and what appears to be interosseous wires in the left ramus area. He has a bone plate in the left zygomatic arch from his previous injury. Sinuses look reasonably clear. Left turbinate appears a little bit large compared to the right.

Waters view shows the presence of 2 bone plates. One in the zygomatic frontal area and one along the arch. Sinuses look relatively clear. Septum appears deviated. Turbinates on the left side appears enlarged and with a little airway on that side.

Left TMJ tomogram shows some arthritis present in the joint. Articular eminence is moderately steep. The wires in the ramus are seen and may be supporting an HA graft. There is a bone screw in the ramus with some lytic area of rounded.

Right TMJ tomogram is a little bit hard to interpret and there may be some arthritic changes here as well. Articular eminence is moderately steep. On opening, there is some restricted translation but better than seen on the MRI. The condyle seems to come down close to the inferior aspect of the articular eminence.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that he has a little bit more of a Class II occlusion than his centric relation. He has significant lip incompetence. He appears to have a high occlusal plane angle. Chin may appear a little bit strong compared to the AP position of the mandible.

 
He has decreased oropharyngeal airway with an airway dimension of only about 3 mm between the soft palate and posteropharyngeal wall area. The tongue area appears to have more normal functional airway. When B423 is placed in centric relation, he does have about a 2 mm overjet relationship and a little bit more of a Class II cuspid relationship bilaterally. B423 does have posterior crossbites at least in the first molar area. He says he is not really concerned about that, however.

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
AP maxillary deficiency.
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
AP mandibular deficiency.
AP macrogenia.
Slight Class II end-on occlusion.
Transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the left side being 2 mm lower than the right side.
Maxillary midline shifted 2 mm to the right, as is the mandibular midline and chin midline.
High occlusal plane angle.
Maxillary and mandibular posterior yaw with them being shifted towards the right side about 3 mm. Increased prominence of the left angle of the mandible. Patient's perception of the right angle is deficient compared to the left side. Lower lip ptosis with accompanying lip incompetence and hyperfunction of the mentalis muscle. (The patient has had 2 previous chin procedures.) Patient perception that the maxillary teeth on the right side have been extruded by 1 mm and he desires to have these repositioned by orthodontic anchor mechanics. Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with reduction. Left TMJ pain. Extensive abrasion of most of the posterior teeth. Soft tissue and muscular changes around the chin area.
Gingival recession on the right side, maxillary teeth primarily.
Premature contact of right side teeth and slight open bite on the left side.
Chronic clenching on the right side of jaws.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch. Consider osteointegrated anchors to intrude the right maxillary teeth if the patient insists on this. (I do not believe this is a very good idea).
Align and level mandibular arch.
Surgery.
Maxillary osteotomies to level the maxilla transversely as well as advance it in a counterclockwise direction with the incisal tips coming forward approximately 6 to 8 mm as well as correct the posterior yaw shifting the maxilla to the left about 3 mm.
 
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to shift the mandible off to the left side about 2 to 3 mm and advance it in a counterclockwise direction. Left TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor.
AP reduction and possible vertical reduction genioplasty.
Re-suspension of mentalis muscles. Consider augmentation of left angle and posterior body of the mandible with Medpore implant.
Consider reduction of prominence of left mandibular angle by reducing the HA material approximately 3 mm.
Consider nasal septoplasty.
Consider rhinoplasty. (It may be best to do this as a secondary procedure.)
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
Surgery stage II.
a. Esthetic refinements as requested by the patient.

I went through his diagnoses and the recommended treatment with him. He is concerned about his chin and reducing the chin. I told him without doing that his chin is going to be too strong and reducing the chin about 4 mm will fit into better balance with the rest of the facial structures following repositioning of the jaw structures. I answered all the questions I had time to answer today. We just ran out of time because of my schedule and we will try to address more of his questions through the phone or by E-mail.

B423 MRI Evaluation  
Age: 43/M
DOB: 
Date of MRI:  
Date of Acquisition: 
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears to have undergone some arthritic changes. Sclerosis on the top of the condyle. There may be some medial beaking but difficult to tell. Decreased lateral joint space. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly probably about 3 to 4 mm. There appears to be discal tissue medial and anterior to the condyle. It appears that there may be some extra bony deposition along the lateral rim of the fossa but difficult to tell.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears to have arthritic changes although the condyle itself appears a little bit wider than the opposite side. There is definitely decreased joint space vertically. The disc appears to medially displace. A little bit of sclerosis on the top of the condyle.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows as condyle that undergone some significant arthritic changes. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Articular disc is seen to be anteriorly positioned and significantly deformed. It short anteroposteriorly. Joint space actually looks fairly good. Towards the medial side of the joint the condyle appears very thin and arthritic. A little bit of fluid in the superior joint space. On opening, there appears to be restricted translation. The disc appears to remain anteriorly dislocated without reduction.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that also has arthritic changes. It is flattened and toward the medial side is very narrow anteroposteriorly but not quite as severe as the opposite side. Articular eminence is moderately steep. On this side it actually looks as though the disc is in position. There is a little bit of anterior beaking present on the condyle. The disc appears to be in reasonable condition. It is almost as there is a pannus surrounding the disc. On opening, there is restricted translation although the disc is in position. There is slightly more translation on this side than the opposite side but still significant limitation.

IMPRESSION: Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly and medially dislocated without reduction and with significant deformation. Right TMJ arthritis but articular disc in position but with limited translation.

original_note:
B304 
B304 is 48 years old and is referred by. B304 has had a previous tonsillectomy, three laparoscopies, previous hand surgery and she has had rib resections for vascular problems.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS: Lipitor 10 mg.
Nortriptyline 30 mg q.h.s.
Tylenol, Advil and occasionally Excedrin for her headaches.
B304 has had headaches all of her life as long as she can remember. She had a fall when she was seven years old and hit the back of her head. She has had headaches at least since that time. Seven years ago, a truck hit the car that she was driving and she hit the left side of her face. She did have a concussion in that accident.

She had orthodontic treatment including headgear as a teenager. Her pain had gotten more intense. She saw Dr. __, who placed her in an orthotic appliance which she continues to use. She also started physical therapy treatments initially three times a week. She now goes in one to three times a week depending on the necessity.

She gets earaches that are severe on the right side but no tinnitus. She gets vertigo but has no hearing loss. Other joints that bother her include her neck where she has a herniated disc and a bulging disc in her neck. She also has some problems of the right shoulder and does need some chiropractic treatment on that occasionally. No other joints bother her. She does clench and brux at nighttime and during the day she is aware of clenching.

B304 gets daily headaches at a level of 9 predominately in the temporal and posterior head area and occurs more frequently on the right side than the left side. Overall, her pain is worse on the right side than the left side. She rates her TMJ pain at 9, average daily pain at 9, jaw function at 9, diet at 5, disability at 5. B304 wore the orthotic for quite some time and it did help her some but then her pain problems got significantly worse again. She had not seen Dr. __ in some time and went back to see him this year and he made a new splint for her but this has not really improved her situation. B304 had an done on as ordered by Dr. __. The films are not available so I cannot interpret them myself but the impression was that there was gross anterior displacement of the articular discs with failure to recapture and moderate to large joint effusion. On the left side, there is anterior displacement of the articular disc without recapture.

In frontal view, B304 has fairly good symmetry to middle and upper third face. She has some slight orbital dystopia with the right eye being about 2 to 3 mm higher than the left eye. Maxillary midline is pretty well aligned with the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline is about 2 mm off to the left side but it is fairly well aligned with the chin. The chin is off to the left side probably about 3 or 4 mm. Upper lip length is 20 mm.

B304 
Tooth-to-lip relationship is 1 mm. In profile view, B304 has some slight AP maxillary deficiency maybe a couple of mm. Chin appears to be retruded about 10 or 12 mm. Chin-neckline and angle are not well defined. Intraorally, she is missing mandibular right first molar replaced by a bridge. She has the rest of her teeth back through the second molars. She has good attached gingiva in the lower anterior arch. In the upper arch she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. She has a Class I cuspid relationship on the right side. Class II end-on cuspid relationship being about almost 5 mm Class II on the left side. She does have about a 2 mm anterior overbite relationship. Transverse cant is present in the occlusion with the left cuspid being about at least 2 mm higher than the right side. Uvula, soft palate and faucial pillars appear to be relatively normal. Intranasally, the septum is deviated significantly off towards the right side. Turbinates are moderately enlarged. It appears there is moderate nasal airway obstruction. B304 has had a sleep study done and will send the report later. In the lower arch, the left cuspid is about 1 to 2 mm posterior to the right cuspid. The upper arch appears fairly symmetric anteroposteriorly. To palpation, B304 has some moderate discomfort over the temporalis muscles, right masseter, very high moderate pain over the right TMJ and mild tenderness over the right medial pterygoid and the styloid areas bilaterally. Joints seem to function relatively smoothly. Maximal incisal opening is 31 mm, 17 mm without discomfort. Interestingly, excursive movements are about 8 mm in each direction. Joints seem to function smoothly and B304 reports no noises with jaw function in her joints. B304 does have a CPAP machine. However, she does not tolerate it well and has significant difficulties wearing the device.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
:
Panorex shows the presence of impacted mandibular left third molar but the other third molars are absent. The mandibular right first molar is absent but replaced with a 3-unit bridge. She has a root canal on the right second molar. Pulp chambers in the canals are difficult to identify particularly in the molar teeth. The left sinus looks a little bit cloudy compared to the right side. The condyles appear arthritic with the left side actually looking worse than the right side.

Waters view shows that the sinuses appear relatively clear. Bony nasal septum is straight indicative of the deviation primarily in the cartilaginous portion of the septum. Right turbinate appears quite large. Facial asymmetry is noted. Slight difference in orbital heights can be noted as well.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears arthritic with some flattening on the anterior head. There is actually increased superior and posterior joint space and decreased anterior joint space. Articular eminence has a moderate inclination.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has some anterior beaking and some flattening on the superior head. Joint space may be better balanced. Articular eminence has some moderate inclination. On opening, there is restricted translation of both condyles with them coming down towards the articular eminence but not beyond.

B304 
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has a slight skeletal Class II occlusal relationship. Oropharyngeal airway dimension at the base of the tongue measures about 6 mm and soft palate level closer to 8 mm. B304 has a tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2 mm. Unilateral thickness of the crowns may indicate even a greater discrepancy probably estimated 4 mm. B304 does remember that her jaw joints did click and pop before she got her first appliance. It was after that when the disc stopped clicking and popping.

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation without reduction.
AP mandibular deficiency.
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency. AP mandibular deficiency.
Facial asymmetry with the left side of her face being shorter than the right side.
Transverse cant in the occlusion with the left side being 2 to 3 mm shorter vertically than the right side.
Class I occlusion on the right side.
Almost a full Class II occlusion on the left side. Severe TMJ pain. Severe headaches. Myofascial pain. Masticatory dysfunction. Deviated nasal septum. Hypertrophied turbinates. Nasal airway obstruction. Decreased oropharyngeal airway. Sleep apnea syndrome. Tooth size discrepancy (estimated 4 mm.)

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Medications.
a. Klonopin to control nocturnal clenching and bruxism. Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch. Open up 1 mm space around each upper lateral incisor. Tip the lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally and make room for interdental osteotomy. Align and level mandibular arch. Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing no more than 1 or 2 mm of tooth structure to help compensate for the significant tooth size discrepancy. Presurgical orthodontics.
CT scans of TMJs and jaws (TMJ Concepts protocol.)

B304 
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla in a counterclockwise direction and level transversely.
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts.
Bilateral mandibular coronoidectomies.
Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies.
Nasal septoplasty.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

Evaluation Age: 48/F
Date of Acquisition: Date of Interpretation:
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is quite flattened and it appears to have lost vertical dimension. Some sclerosis in the cortical bone on top of the condyle. Decreased vertical joint space. Fairly significant downward curvature at the lateral rim of the fossa probably 3 to 4 mm. Articular disc is anteriorly displaced.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has a little bit better architecture with less loss of vertical bone. There appears to be sclerosis on the head of the condyle. Lateral rim of the fossa curves significantly inferiorly. Joint space is decreased but not as much as the opposite side and more so at the lateral rim of the fossa area. The disc is anteriorly displaced.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle with a little bit of flattening on the anterior aspect with maybe just some slight anterior beaking. There may be a little superior peaking in the condyle and in some views towards the lateral side of the joint. Decreased superior and posterior joint space is present. Particularly at the medial side of the joint and then towards the lateral side of the joint there is loss of vertical space. Articular disc is sitting anterior to the articular eminence. It is quite short anteroposteriorly in some of the views and it appears quite deformed and distorted. It appears that it has some medial displacement as well. In some of the views it is rather difficult to identify the tissue. However, it is significantly degenerated. On opening, there is restricted translation of the condyle and the disc remains anteriorly dislocated. The disc appears to be adhesed to the articular eminence as there is virtually no movement forward. There is a little bit of fluid in the superior joint space but not too significant.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has some flattening. A little osteophyte is sticking off the superior aspect. A little bit of anterior beaking may be present. Articular eminence is moderately steep. The articular disc is anteriorly displaced and sitting beneath the articular eminence but actually angled quite inferiorly beneath the articular eminence. This is foreshortened and deformed, thickened but not as severely degenerated as the opposite side. On opening, there is restricted translation although slightly better than the opposite side. The disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction. In one view it does appear that there could be an osteophytic projection that could almost touch against the fossa. This is towards the lateral aspect of the joint. The disc does seem to have some ability to translate.

IMPRESSION: Left TMJ significant arthritis and articular disc dislocation with major deformation non-reducing and probably adhesions to the articular eminence. Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation without reduction and significant degeneration.
Right disc could possibly be salvaged. The left disc appears non-salvageable.

original_note:
BRAD POE										08/06/2008

Brad is 18 years old.  Referred by Dr. Maryam Mojdehi-Barnes, Allen, TX 75013.  Brad was born with a bilateral cleft lip and palate.  He had repair in September 1990 and then additional repair in December 1990.  He had a cleft palate repair in 1991.  He had a lip/nose revision in 1995 and a bone graft to the palate and probably alveolar cleft area in November 1999.  He has had seven sets of ear tubes.  He had nose and lip scar revisions in July 2003.  He had a mastoidectomy in November 2004 and he had nasal polyps removed in November 2007.  He had tympanoplasty and cholesteatoma in December 2007 and inner ear repair also in July 2008.  

Primary concerns that Brad has includes the following:

Flatness in the face particularly around the base of the nose. 
Possibility of requiring upper and lower jaw advancement.
Fistula present in the palate.

The only medication that he currently uses is Nasonex two times per day.  Brad does have some difficulties breathing through his nose which apparently had been related to nasal polyps.  Brad thinks he breathes through his nose when he sleeps, but occasionally through his mouth, but is not sure.  He does have some mild snoring.  He has no sleep apnea symptoms.  He has no TMJ related issues.  Brad has had orthodontic treatment with Dr. Ed Genecov.  He had orthodontic appliances on for approximately 8 years.  

CLINICAL EXAMINATION:  In frontal view, Brad has good left-to-right facial symmetry.  He has a beautiful repair on his lip.  Upper lip length is about 23 mm.  There is a soft tissue defect in the tubercle area of the upper lip.  If the tubercle were there, upper tooth lip relationship would probably be about zero.  The right eye may be elevated just 1 to 2 mm.   The maxillary midline is off to the left side about 3 mm.  Chin likewise is off to the left side probably about 3 mm.   In profile view, the maxilla is probably AP deficient by 6 to 8 mm and the chin AP deficient by probably about 10 mm.  Chin neckline is a little bit short and chin neck angle is a little soft in angulation.  He has good nasal projection.  Intraorally, Brad is missing lower first bicuspids and lower teeth appear to be in a little bit of lingual inclination.  He has decalcification on some of his lower teeth.  In the upper arch, Brad has a transposed cuspid and first bicuspid on the left side.  He has the rest of his teeth back through the second molars, although the second molar is tipped buccally quite a ways.  On the right side, he is missing upper right lateral incisor and has the rest of his teeth.  The upper cuspid is in the lateral incisor position.  The first central incisors are narrow at the incisal edge.  It appears particularly in the right side that the tooth was fractured or recontoured, but is very narrow at the incisal edge compared to the normal formation of that tooth.  Brad has an anterior and bilateral posterior crossbites.  He has a small upper left lateral incisor that probably creates a significant tooth size discrepancy.  He has a basic Class I cuspid relationship on the right side, but a Class III molar relationship.  On the left side, he has got a full Class III cuspid relationship if the first bicuspid is used as the cuspid.  All incisors appear to be in vertical position.  He has an end-on incisor relationship and zero overbite.  Transversely, the arches are fairly level with perhaps the right cuspid being up 1 mm on the right side compared to the left side.  TMJs seem to function relatively smoothly.  He has no tenderness to palpation around the head around the head and neck area.  Incisal opening is 43 mm.  Excursion movements are about 4 to 5 mm in each direction.  The oropharyngeal tissues look healthy.  He does appear to have an oronasal fistula up in the roof of his mouth.  He has a repaired cleft hard and soft palate.  The palate appears somewhat short in length.  Fascial pillars are relatively normal, although because of the short soft palate they actually turn a little bit anterior in the AP position of the palate.  Intranasally, the turbinates are hard to see, but appear maybe just mildly enlarged, but difficult to tell for sure.  The septum appears reasonably straight at least anteriorly.  He has significant constricture of the nostrils particularly on the right side and has severe constricture of the luminal valves particularly on the right side.  On holding his valves open, he could breathe a little bit easier through the right side, but he has quite a severe constricture there.  Brad probably has significant nasal airway obstruction where at night he probably is predominantly a mouth breather and also during the day he breathes significantly through his mouth.  
RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of the maxillary right lateral incisor, mandibular first bicuspids, and all four third molars.  The sinuses look reasonably clear.  The condyles look relatively normal.  Right condylar head and neck appear somewhat elongated compared to the left side.  Roots on the maxillary central incisors as well as on the bicuspids appear somewhat foreshortened.  
Right TMJ tomogram appears relatively normal.  
Left TMJ tomogram also appears relatively normal.  On opening, there is moderately good translation of the condyles.  
Waters view shows that there is a significant deviation of the nasal septum.  The septum appears wide or there could be a double curve in it back in the bony area.  Left turbinate appears to be quite large and appears to block most of the airway.  On the right side, the turbinate is moderate in size, but the septum is deviated off towards the right side posteriorly and this may be causing some of the airway obstruction.  
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that it appears Brad has some AP maxillary and mandibular deficiency.  Oropharyngeal airway dimension at the base of the tongue to the posterior pharyngeal wall measures about 5 mm and as best I could tell from the soft palate area also measures about 5 mm.  Maxillary and mandibular incisors appear to be slightly over-retracted.  Brad has a transverse yaw with the right side of the mandibular angle being about 5 mm more prominent in the lateral direction than the left side.  

Basic diagnoses for Brad would be as follows:    

Repaired bilateral cleft lip and palate.
AP maxillary hypoplasia.  
Transverse maxillary hypoplasia.
AP mandibular hypoplasia.
Anterior bilateral posterior crossbite and greater in the posterior right than left side.
Transverse facial asymmetry with a posterior yaw to the right of approximately 5 mm.
Missing maxillary right lateral incisor and mandibular first bicuspids.
Transposition of the maxillary left cuspid and first bicuspid. 
Class I cuspid on the left side, Class III cuspid on the right side.
Mild velopharyngeal incompetency. 
Mild hypernasal speech.
Probable hypertrophied turbinates. 
Severe constricture of right luminal valve.
Nasal airway obstruction.
Primarily a mouth breather.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Presurgical orthodontics.  
Align and level maxillary arch.  On the left side, be sure that the lateral incisor root is tipped mesially and first cuspid root tipped distally to make room for interdental osteotomies.
Align and level mandibular arch.  
Surgery.
Advance the maxilla approximately 7 to 8 mm.
Advance the mandible approximately 5 mm at the incisal tips.
Partial turbinectomy on the left side.  
Reconstruct right side cleft nose deformity (severely constricted nasal valve).

original_note:
JENNA SPEAR										05-24-04


Patient History

Jenna’s problems began for unknown reasons, and the onset is not really known either. Her husband states that he first noticed asymmetry about two years ago but wasn’t aware of it prior to that. She was involved in an MVA in ’97 where she sustained a head injury and lost consciousness and also some internal injuries. Earlier she had orthodontics at the age of 14 for about a year-and-a-half, and then she noticed her teeth were shifting and changing, so she underwent orthodontics again beginning in 06/2002 and ending in 06/2003. During that time, the orthodontist tried to use elastics to try to get the teeth together on the right side but was unable to accomplish that. She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, or hearing loss. She is aware that she does clench and night. She gets daily headaches at a level of 8 that occur in the frontal, temporal, and posterior head area. She rates her TMJ pain at 6, overall pain on an average day at 7, jaw function at 4, diet at 2, and disability at 0. She has no other joint problems. The only previous surgery was she had her gallbladder removed as a consequence of the auto accident in ’97. 

She is aware of having clicking in the right TMJ area.

Patient Concerns

Headaches 
TMJ pain
Teeth on the right side don’t touch
Difficulties eating and chewing

Current Medications

Allegra
Ortho-Tri-Cyclen
Advil for her headaches and takes between 2 and 4 per day

Allergies

She has had an adverse reaction to DEMEROL and MORPHINE, so we want to use an alternative pain medication, and we may consider Dilaudid in her situation immediately postsurgery.

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Jenna has fairly good facial symmetry in the middle and upper third face. Upper lip length is about 16 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is 2 mm. Maxillary dental midline is about 1.5 to 2 mm off to the left of the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline is about 3 mm off. Chin is about 7 to 8 mm off to the left side.

In profile view, Jenna has good facial symmetry and balance. Chin-neckline is long, and chin-neck angle is well-defined. Chin may be just slightly prominent compared to the lower lip, but overall she has fairly good balance.

Intraorally, Jenna has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. In the upper arch, she also has all of her teeth back through the second molars. Has relatively thin vascular gingiva around the lower anterior teeth. Has a Class I cuspid-molar relationship on the left side as well as on the right side. She has an open bite on the right side and tendency toward a buccal crossbite. She has an open bite of about 2 to 3 mm on that side. She has a fairly significant cant in the occlusal plane with the maxillary right cuspid being about 2 mm lower than the left and the right cuspid being about 3 mm lower than the left. 

To palpation, she has no pain around the head and neck area. Joints function relatively smoothly, although she does hear a little bit of a click on the right side. Incisal opening is 47 mm. Excursive movements to the right are 8 mm and to the left 7 mm. 

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows the sinuses are clear. The third molars are absent, and all other teeth are present with good bone and root structures. The left TMJ condyle looks relatively normal. Right TMJ condyle demonstrates the presence of a mass extending anterior to the condylar head. Comparatively, the left condyle measures a little bit less than 10 mm anteroposteriorly whereas the width of the right condyle measures 25 mm. 

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is flattened a little bit on the anterior aspect. Slightly increased anterior joint space and decreased posterior joint space. Articular eminence is moderately steep. It is possible the disc could be just slightly anteriorly displaced based on the spacing present. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows a large osteochondroma extending up from the anterior aspect of the mandible with increased width of the condylar head and neck area. The condyle is completely out of the fossa. Good joint space is present between the condyle/osteochondroma and the fossa. Posterior open bite is present of at least 2 to 3 mm in the molar areas. 

On opening, there is good translation of both condyles with the condylar portion on both sides coming beneath the articular eminence. On the right side, the mass moves forward with the condylar head.

Waters view shows that the sinuses are clear. Turbinates are of moderate size. Septum is a little bit wide. There is some significant vertical asymmetry at the maxillary occlusal plane level with the right side being probably close to 4 mm lower than the left side at the molar area. At the inferior border, there is probably a good 10 mm of vertical height difference. Vertical height of the mandible is greater on the right side as compared to the left side. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows good AP balance between the maxilla and mandible. At the occlusal plane level, however, the right side is 5 to 6 mm lower in the second molar area as compared to the left side. At the inferior border of the mandible, there is a vertical difference of about 11 mm back at the angle area, 8 or 9 mm at the mesial of the first molar, and about 4 mm at the cuspid area. Oropharyngeal airway looks adequate, although the space from the soft palate to the posterior pharyngeal wall measures about 7 mm. 

CAT scan shows the osteochondroma protruding anteriorly with no medial or lateral extensions. The growth is strictly anterior. 

Bone scan taken 04/26/04 shows that the right condyle is quite hot and active pathology ongoing there. 

Basic Diagnoses

Right TMJ large osteochondroma
Maxillary asymmetry with the right side being significantly lower than the left side
Mandibular vertical hyperplasia and asymmetry
Right posterior open bite of 2 to 3 mm
Maxillary midline shifted 2 mm to the left, mandibular dental midline shifted 3 mm to the left, and chin shifted 7 to 8 mm to the left
Headaches
TMJ pain
Difficulties eating and chewing

Recommended Treatment

Presurgical orthodontics 
Place appliances on upper arch and align and level the teeth. Do not use any interarch elastics
Place appliances on the mandibular arch and align and level the teeth (no interarch elastics)

Surgery
Right TMJ low condylectomy, recontour the condylar neck to function as a condyle, and reposition the articular disc with Mitek anchor and artificial ligaments
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to shift the mandible back on the facial midline and transversely level the occlusal plane
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to transversely level the occlusal plane and correct any transverse discrepancies that may be present

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

original_note:
KEVIN SCHOCH 									07/14/2006

Kevin is 14 years old, referred by Dr. William Hang.  The only surgery Kevin has had is a mole removed from his finger.  He takes no medications.  He has had an adverse reaction to Ceclor.  

PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Chin is dropping and receding.
Airway is narrowed.
Unable to breathe through nose and must breathe through the mouth.
Nose being pulled down by the chin.
Snoring.
Daytime tiredness. 

Kevin has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, hearing loss or other joint problems.  He has no problems with headaches, no TMJ pain or myofascial pain around the head and neck area.  Incisal opening is 58 mm.  Excursive movement to the right 8 mm, to the left 7 mm.  Joints seem to function reasonably smooth although there are some significant changes within the joints.  

In frontal view, Kevin has some asymmetry particularly in the nose and perhaps in the jaw area as well.  Upper lip length is about 21 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 5 to 5-1/2 mm.  Maxillary midline is shifted off to the right side about almost 2 mm.  There is some lip incompetence of probably about 7 to 8 mm.  Maxilla is retruded by about 6 mm.  He has a 
C-shaped curve to the upper lip.  Upper lip supralabrale is about even with the subnasale.  Chin is retruded by about 20 to 25 mm.  Chin-neckline is extremely short.  Chin-neck angle is reasonably well defined.  Kevin has a head postured forward neck position.  Intraorally, Kevin has all of his lower teeth back through the second molars.  The teeth are nicely aligned and leveled.  He has good attached gingiva around the lower anterior teeth although it is quite thin in vertical dimension.  Roots are prominent through the labial aspect.  In the upper arch he has all his teeth back through the second molars present.  Kevin has a slight Class II cuspid relationship bilaterally by probably about 2 mm.  He has about ½ mm of vertical overbite relationship.  The uvula, soft palate and faucial pillars are all relatively normal.  There may be a slight decreased AP dimension between the soft palate and the posterior pharyngeal wall area.  Intranasally, Kevin has a huge septal spur off towards the right side almost totally obliterating the nasal airway.  Cannot see posterior on that side to see if the turbinates enlarged beyond the spur.  In the left nose the turbinate is extremely large and is blocking off a good portion of the airway.  Kevin also has a wide columella and extremely narrow nostrils.  Using a nasal scope, he could breathe a little bit easier through both sides of the nose holding the nostrils open with a little bit more improvement on the right side than the left side.  Transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the left side being 2 mm lower than the right side.  The chin is rotated off towards the right side in the frontal view about 6 mm. 






KEVIN SCHOCH				07/14/2006				Page 2


RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION:
Panorex shows the presence of all four third molars impacted.  Sinuses look relatively clear.  The condyles appear a little bit arthritic being more so on the right side than the left side. 

Waters view shows severe deviation of the nasal septum off to the right side with a huge septal spur and it almost touches the lateral nasal wall.  The turbinate on the left side appears enlarged below the level of the septal spur and on the right side it almost totally obliterates the nasal airway.  Sinuses look relatively clear.  

Right TMJ tomogram shows the condyle appears somewhat arthritic and a decreased joint space.  Articular eminence is relatively shallow.  

Left TMJ tomograms shows a condyle is siting superior and posterior in the fossa.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  The condyle appears a little bit arthritic.  On opening there is reasonably good translation of both joints.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Kevin has a high occlusal plane angle.  He has anterior vertical maxillary excess and probably posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.  The mandible is AP deficient and AP microgenia.  Oropharyngeal airway shows a large adenoid mass in the nasopharynx area.  The turbinate appears to extend a little bit posterior to the hard palate and the airway space between the posterior aspect of the turbinate and the nasopharyngeal adenoid tissue is only about 3 to 4 mm.  The lateral pharyngeal airway at the base of the soft palate appears more normal, however, the distance from the base of the tongue to the posteropharyngeal wall is decreased but may be due more to tongue posture than actual posterior displacement.  

On the nose, Kevin does have a nasal dorsal hump, although the nose itself is reasonably straight.  His face looks asymmetric mainly because of the mandible and chin are shifted off towards the right side. 

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocations (possible ICR.)
Anterior vertical maxillary excess.
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
AP maxillary deficiency.
AP mandibular deficiency.
AP microgenia.
Class II end-on occlusion.
Maxilla shifted 3 mm to the right.
Mandible shifted 3 mm to the right at the dental midline and 6 mm at the chin.
  Anterior open bite tendency.
  Impacted third molars times four.
  Deviated nasal septum with a large nasal spur.
KEVIN SCHOCH				07/14/2006				Page 3


  Hypertrophied turbinates.
  Major nasal airway obstruction.
  External nasal deformity.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the anterior aspect superior, posterior aspect inferiorly and advance. 
Nasal septoplasty.
Partial inferior turbinectomies.
Nasal pharyngeal adenoidectomy.
Surgical removal of impacted third molars times four.
Augmentation genioplasty with HTR versus Medpor.
External rhinoplasty.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B280 
B280 is 55 years old and is referred by ___.
PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Constant pain in the jaws, neck and back. Jaw sometimes gets stuck.
Popping and clicking in the jaws.
Bites inner cheek.
Jaw aches on the left side.
Tongue pressure on the right side.
Her teeth presses against her cheek.
Sore throat.
Sinus problems. Eye pain and blood shot. Cranial pain. Extreme difficulty in working. Chronic fatigue. Terrible headaches.

Her objectives are to get free of pain and help decrease or eliminate other symptoms. She would like to regain her former lifestyle and be able to function normally and enjoy life.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS:
Ginseng and Ziziphus, plus several other Chinese herbs.
Green magma angelica.
Provigil 20 mg.
Valtrex p.r.n. Lyrica occasionally.
Triamcinolone cream for rash.

She has significant difficulties taking a lot of these drugs because it makes her get bloated and constipated.
Her TMJ problems may have begun in childhood where she was aware of jaw sounds and they continued to get worse over the years. During the past year, however, they have gotten significantly worse.

PREVIOUS TMJ TREATMENTS: Physical therapy since 23 years ago until last year.
Spints. She has had many splints over the years. She has had at least six splints over the years. Orthotics in her shoes.
Acupuncture continuously. The acupuncture treatments began at least 7 years ago.
Chiropractic treatment for over 20 years.
Reiki for at least five years.
Craniosacral therapy for 10 years. Anti-inflammatories were used around 20 years ago and also last year. She still continues to use those periodically but it causes her GI problems. Muscle relaxers. She was also on those around 20 years ago and also last year. They also caused GI problems. Massage for over 20 years and said it does help but only temporary.

PREVIOUS SURGERIES: Appendectomy 48 years ago.
Tonsillectomy 34 years ago.
Wisdom teeth removed 33 years ago.
Morton neuroma in 31 years ago.
She was involved in a car accident 25 years ago and she hit her head and her jaw on the steering wheel at that time. Etiology of B280's problems is unknown. She did have an MVA 25 years ago where she hit her mandible and forehead. She was in another minor accident 6 years ago where she backed into a car and may have felt a little bit of a whiplash.

Her jaws currently feel very tired and achy. She gets a burning sensation. She feels as though her tongue is pushing her lower jaw off to the right side. She does get a feeling of locking. She gets earaches, tinnitus, vertigo but no hearing loss. Other joints that bother her include her hips, knees and shoulders. She has chronic fatigue problem and it seems to be getting worse. B280 feels she is getting a head postured forward neck position that she did not have before. She has had some changes in her right foot where she gets some numbness involving primarily the side of the big toe. Her headaches without any treatment are at level of 10 predominately in the left forehead and center of the head as well as the right temple, back of the head, top of the head and behind the left eye. She rates her TMJ without treatment at a level of 10, average daily pain at 10. After acupuncture, chiropractic treatment etc., the pain may drop down to 8 but this does not last more than 48 hours. She rates her jaw function on the average at about a 9. She rates her diet at a level of 3 and her disability on an average about 9. She feels that her disability is continuing to get worse. She gets pain in her chest area from the sternum lateral up through her shoulder and down through the upper part of the left arm. Usually in the morning she is all stuffed up in her nose and sinuses. She does the pressure point treatments and this seems to improve her situation. She is not sure if she snores. She has daytime somnolence that is severe and chronic. She is not aware of sleep apnea. She does not know if she wakes up unable to catch her breath. She does wake up frequently at night. She tosses and turns a lot. Her sister has been diagnosed as having sleep apnea.

In frontal view, B280 has fairly good left to right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 23 mm. Upper tooth to lip relationship is -1. Maxillary, mandibular and chin midlines are all pretty much aligned with each other. In profile view she has fairly good facial balance. There may be just slight AP mandibular deficiency but not too significant.

Maxilla may be back just a touch but again not real significant. Chin-neckline is a little bit short and chin-neck angle is not well defined. Intraorally, B280 has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. She has some moderate crowding in the lower anterior arch. She has crowns in the mandibular right first and second molar and left first and second molars. She has good attached gingiva around the lower anterior arch. In the upper arch she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. She is very close to being Class I cuspid molar relationship. She has a crossbite on the right side in the second bicuspid area. She has about 2 mm vertical overbite. She also has a crown on the maxillary right central incisor. Oropharyngeal tissues look healthy and normal. Intranasally, the septum deviates a little bit off to the right side. Turbinates are moderately enlarged moreso on the right side than the left side. There may be some obstruction present. To palpation, she has high moderate to low severe pain over the TMJs, high moderate pain over the styloid areas and shoulders, moderate pain over the medial pterygoids and milder pain over the temporalis and posterior neck muscles. On opening, she has significant noises in her joints with opening pops probably at about 15 mm on the right side though it is a soft one and on the left at about 20. Closing clicks are fairly close to being about 10 mm. On wide opening when she moves her jaw, there is crepitus noise present. At times shen she lays down she will get jittering of her muscles and teeth where the will open and close rapidly making clacking kind of noises with the teeth. Maximal incisal opening is about 48 mm. Excursive movements about 10 mm in each direction. She can open about 15 mm and be reasonably comfortable but beyond that she develops significant pain and discomfort.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
:
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars. She has root canal on maxillary right central incisor and mandibular right first molar. She has crowns on all four molars in the mandible and maxillary left first molar. Condyles appear to have reasonable confirmation. Sinuses appear relatively clear.

Waters view shows that she has a bony spur off the septum into the right nasal airway. Turbinates appear moderate in size. Sinuses appear relatively clear.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is posteriorly positioned in the fossa. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Anterior joint space is increased. Condyle actually has fairly good confirmation.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is a little bit posteriorly positioned in the fossa. The condyle has a little bit of flattening on the posterior head. Articular eminence is moderately steep. The joint space is actually better on this side than the opposite side. The condyle has a little bit altered morphology compared to the opposite side. On opening, there is good translation of both condyles particularly on the right side.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows she has basically a skeletal and occlusal Class I relationship. Oropharyngeal airway measures about 9 mm at the soft palate level and about 8 mm at the base of the tongue. In the chin area I see a white round area but I believe that is just an artifact on the film as there appears to be nothing clinically present.

BASIC DIAGNOSES: Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocations with reduction.
Relative mandibular hyperplasia.
Severe headaches.
Myofascial pain.
Severe TMJ pain.
Earaches, tinnitus and vertigo.
Deviated nasal septum.
Hypertrophied turbinates.
Nasal airway obstruction. Sleep disorder. Chronic fatigue. Crowding lower anterior arch. Maxillary right cross bite in the second bicuspid area.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
MRI of left and right TMJs with sedation (Valium 5 mg tablets x2 prescribed.)
Medications.
Klonopin 0.5 mg tablets dispensing 30 tablets, 1 tablet q.h.s., refill x5.
Magnesium 225 mg tablets, 2 tablets daily.
Presurgical orthodontics (if patient elects orthodontic approach.)
Align and level maxillary arch. Align and level mandibular arch. Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies with rigid fixation. Application of arch bars (if patient decides against orthodontics.)
Turbinectomies (by Dr. __.)
Nasoseptoplasty (by Dr. __.)
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion (if patient elects orthodontic treatment.)

The patient could not undergo MRI today because of claustrophobia. We will try again later today to see if we can accomplish it. I gave her a prescription for Valium 5 mg tablets dispensing 2 tablets, take 1 tablet 45 minutes prior to scheduled MRI and take second tablet if necessary. She is to call me in one week to let me know if the Klonopin is helping her. She is to keep me informed of her progress.

B280  MRI EVALUATION
AGE: 55/F
DOB: 
Date of Acquisition:
Date of Interpretation:
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width. Lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 mm. There appears to be some discal tissue displaced medially and anteriorly. At the lateral aspect of the joint and anteriorly the disc appears to be laterally displaced.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears a little bit wide and almost extends beyond the lateral rim of the fossa. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 to 3 mm. There is discal tissue medially displaced and likely anteriorly displaced as well.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has fairly good confirmation. Articular disc is prominent at the lateral aspect of the joint and is anteriorly displaced. More medial the disc is in a little bit better position. Medially the top of the condyle appears uncovered by the disc. The disc is in actually good condition. Articular eminence is moderately steep. A little bit of fluid in the superior joint space but not too much. On opening, there is early reduction of the disc and there is good translation of both the condyle and the disc forward with them both coming anterior to the articular eminence. Significantly decreased posterior joint space.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has more morphological changes as compared to opposite side. There is flattening on the posterior head of the condyle and on the anterior head. A little bit of anterior beaking is present. A little sclerosis also present in the cortical bone around the top of the condyle. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Articular disc is anteriorly displaced at the lateral aspect of the joint but more medially it appears to be in better position. At the far medial side of the joint the disc is not seen. The disc appears to be in fairly good condition. There is decreased posterior joint space. There is a little bit of fluid in the superior joint space moreso than the opposite side and a little bit in the inferior joint space as well. On opening, there is early reduction of the disc and there is good translation of both the condyle and the disc.

IMPRESSION: Left TMJ articular disc dislocation with early reduction. Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly dislocated but with early reduction.

original_note:
KARA KEMP									02/07/2011

She is referred by Dr. Frank Stitch, 495 S. Denton Tap Road, Coppell, TX 75019.  Kara is 28 years old, birth date is 06/10/1982.  

HISTOR OF CURRENT CONDITION.  Kara apparently had clicking and popping in her jaw joints even from her teenage years, but relatively asymptomatic.  It was not until 2005 that she had significant onset of pain, headaches, and developed a closed lock situation.  She had orthodontics from the age of 8 to 14 including head gear, and this was performed where she grew up in Maryland.  In 2005, she received a splint and wore that through 2009.  It did help decrease the pain and the headaches, but did not fix the problem.  Her bite has shifted and changed and so the splint does not fit any more and so she has not worn it since 2009.  She does get some massage therapy that helps with the neck and shoulder area.  She gets headaches 2 to 3 times a week at about a level of 5 in the frontal and temporal area and usually wakes up with these in the morning.  Her headaches can escalate to 9.  Her TMJ pain is at 7 and is usually worse in the mornings.  Her average daily pain is at 7, jaw function at 7, diet at 5, and disability at 4.  She does not have any problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, or hearing loss.  The only joints that may bother her are possibly her hips.  She is aware of clenching and bruxing at nighttime.  

In frontal view, Kara has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  She does have some fullness in the masseter muscle areas bilaterally probably as a result of the clenching and bruxism.  Upper lip length is about 22 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 6 mm.  The maxillary, mandibular and chin midline are all pretty much on the facial midline.  The nasal tip is just slightly off to the right side 1 to 2 mm.  In profile view, she has fairly good balance with the upper lip, cheeks, and nose.  Chin may be a little bit retruded by maybe 8 mm.  She has about 7 mm of lip incompetence.  Chin neckline and angle are moderately defined.  Intraorally, Kara has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars present.  She has some moderate crowding of the lower anterior arch and has adequate attached gingiva.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  She has an end-on Class II occlusion being about 4 to 5 mm Class II.  She has an anterior deep bite of about 3 mm.  The transverse occlusal plane is fairly level, although may down slightly on the left side 1 mm.  She has had her wisdom teeth removed.  Kara is not aware of snoring at night when she sleeps, she has moderate daytime somnolence.  She is not aware of sleep apnea problems per se, but does wake up frequently at night.  She believes that she probably breathes through her mouth when she sleeps at night.  Oropharyngeal tissues looks relatively normal and healthy.  Intranasally, it is hard to see the turbinates, but the septum is deviated off towards the left side.  The columella is moderately wide.  Nostrils are narrow particularly on the left side.  Kara has moderate pain over the masseters, TMJs, posterior neck and shoulder areas and mild tenderness over the medial pterygoids.  On opening, joints function relatively smoothly, but in excursion movements she has a palpable click on the right side.  The maximal opening was 25 mm, opening without pain 18 mm.  The excursion movements were 9 mm in each direction.  It appears that if she opens vertically that she has a closed lock situation, but in excursion movements she can reduce the right disc.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars.  She has a root canal on the maxillary left central incisor.  The sinuses look reasonably clear.  She has a brachiocephalic mandibular morphology.  Bony septum is fairly straight.  Turbinates are moderately enlarged particularly on the left side where it fills a good portion of the inferior aspect of the nasal airway.  Sinuses appear clear.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is posteriorly positioned in the fossa.  The articular eminence has a moderate inclination.  There is increased anterior joint space indicative of an anterior displaced disc.  The joint space is decreased posteriorly and superiorly.  A little bit of sclerosis on top of the condyle.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle with more sclerosis on top.  The condyle is somewhat posteriorly positioned in the fossa.  There is an increased anterior joint space.  The articular eminence has slightly less inclination compared to the opposite side.  

Left TMJ coronal view shows some decreased vertical joint space.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 to 3 mm.  

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has some slight extrusion of the lateral pole of the condyle.  The joint space may be somewhat decreased, but does not appear to be as significant as the opposite side.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Kara has her head tipped up somewhat.  Oropharyngeal airway looks good.  She has a skeletal and occlusal Class II end-on occlusion. 

Basic diagnoses for Kara would be as follows:

Bilateral TMJ mild arthritis and articular disc dislocations with a closed lock on the right side.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia. 
Maxillary vertical hyperplasia.
Class II occlusion.
Crowding of the lower anterior arch.
Bilateral TMJ pain. 
Headaches.
Myofascial pain.
Hypertrophied turbinates with nasal airway obstruction. 
Clenching and bruxism.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Medications:  Klonopin to control nocturnal clenching and bruxism.  
2.	MRI of left and right TMJ.
3.	Presurgical orthodontics.
	a.	Align and level maxillary arch.  Keep lateral incisor roots tipped mesially 			and cuspid roots tipped distally in case interdental osteotomies are 				required.  
	b.	Align and level lower arch.  Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing 
                        1 to 2 mm of tooth structure to keep from flaring them too far forward.  
4.	Surgery.
	a.	Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek 			anchor (dependent on MRI evaluation).
	b.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move it superiorly and correct any 			transverse discrepancy.
	c.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible.
	d.	Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy to augment the chin. 
	e.	Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies. 
5.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion. 

I gave her a prescription for Klonopin 1 mg tablet, dispensing 30 tablets, 1 tablet q.h.s., refill x3.

original_note:
MAYOLA WILLIAMS									     01-05-05

Mayola is 71 years old and referred by Dr. Steve Parel, 3202 Gaston Ave., Baylor College of Dentistry, Dallas, TX  75246.

Patient History

Previous surgeries include a tonsillectomy and a hysterectomy. She has glaucoma, and she has a central retina vein occlusion. She has some type of autoimmune lung condition called MAC. Apparently it comes from virus in water and can also be carried by birds. Mayola was diagnosed with rheumatoid arthritis back in the early 60s. She has problems with most of her joints. The symptoms she primarily has are achiness and pain and stiffness in her joints. She has had a past history of stomach ulcers but not now.

Mayola’s problems began in reference to her jaw joints sometime around the late ‘70s of unknown etiology. She was involved in a motor vehicle accident in the 50s where they feel she probably had a spontaneous fusion of C2 and C3. She has bulging discs below that level at probably C3-C4. She has had steroid injections into her neck for her cervical-associated problems. She gets some earaches on the right side, no tinnitus, but has some significant problems with vertigo particularly when her bite shifts. Also, if she turns her head significantly, and if she tips her head downward or concentrates on something for a while, she will develop the vertigo. Probably has some hearing loss. Relative to the vertigo, she has been evaluated by ENT and also a neurologist. One diagnosis involved cervical vertigo. She has problems with most all of her other joints and was diagnosed with rheumatoid arthritis in the late ‘60s. She gets some pain and achiness as well as stiffness in her joints. She is aware is aware of clenching and bruxism at night and sometimes during the day. Approximately 10 years ago she had ridge augmentations performed. About six years ago, dental implants were started by Dr. Bullard, and then Dr. Steve Parel completed the full-mouth reconstruction. In the summer of 2004, she did receive a night guard, and this has helped her fairly significantly. Relative to her diet, Mayola cannot eat any meat or even chew any bread. Her food has to be fairly soft.

She gets headaches almost daily, and they occur  predominantly in the temporal and posterior head area at an average level of 5. She rates her TMJ pain at 8 and overall facial pain and headaches at 8 during the last couple of weeks. She rates her jaw function and diet at 4 and disability at 2.

Current Medications

Premarin 0.312 mg q.d.
Allegra 180 mg 
Betoptic eye drops
Ambien 0.5 mg q.h.s. 
Tylenol for headaches 

Patient Concerns

Jaw locking
Headaches 
Jaw pain
Popping in the jaw joints
Pain to the jaws and head and neck area
Vertigo 
Right earaches
Difficulty eating some foods
Clinical Examination

Mayola has good facial symmetry and balance. 

Intraorally, she has dentures affixed to dental implants. She is set up in a good Class I cuspid-molar relationship. She has about a 2-mm anterior overbite. Oropharynx appears clear and healthy with a normal-looking uvula, soft palate, and normal width of the faucial pillars. 

To palpation, Mayola has some moderate tenderness over the left TMJ, left styloid area, and milder tenderness over the left SCM, posterior neck, shoulder, and right styloid area. She has significant crepitation in the left TMJ. Right joint seems to function fairly smoothly. Maximal incisal opening is about 43 mm. Excursive movements to the right are 3 mm and to the left 5 mm. 

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows the absence of all the teeth and the presence of seven osseointegrated implants in the maxilla but six actively engaged in the prosthesis. In the lower arch, there are probably about five to six implants present. There is a framework over the implants. Both TMJs appear to be arthritic.

Left TMJ tomogram shows what appears to be decreased superior and posterior joint space. There are arthritic changes on the condylar head. Articular eminence is moderately steep. There could be a couple of areas of sclerosis on the head of the condyle. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is a little bit more visible than the opposite side but significantly decreased superior and posterior joint space. Articular eminence is somewhat flattened. Translation is reasonably good with it being a little bit greater on the right side than the left side. Condyles appear to come down underneath the articular eminences.

Waters view shows that the sinuses appear clear. Septum appears centered. Turbinates appear moderate in size. There are five implants present in the lower arch and seven present in the upper arch. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has basically a skeletal Class I relationship. She has a good oropharyngeal airway. Implants and framework seem appropriately aligned. 

Basic Diagnoses

Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocations with severe deformation and perforations
TMJ pain
Headaches
Myofascial pan
Earaches 
Vertigo
Five osseointegrated implants in the mandible and seven in the maxilla with six functional
Maxillary and mandibular prostheses attached to implants
History of clenching and bruxism



Recommended Treatment

Medications
Klonopin 0.5 mg, ½ to 1 tablet q.h.s. to see if this will eliminate nocturnal clenching and bruxism
Other medications such as muscle relaxers, anti-inflammatory drugs, and analgesics if necessary to try to control pain and dysfunction

Splint therapy to open the bite and take stress off the joints. The patient currently has a night guard that has been very helpful for her, and she does wear this some during the day, but the daytime wear could be extended if necessary to improve her comfort

CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol)

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ condylectomies and reconstruction with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts

Postsurgical management as necessary

original_note:
B291 
B291 is 17 years old, referred by Dr. __. B291 was diagnosed with Crouzon's syndrome and had reconstructive surgery in April of 19__. 3 and 5 years ago, there were some oral surgical procedures performed to remove some primary teeth and some permanent teeth. Other than that, B291 is in good health. He takes Excedrin for migraine headaches as needed. He does get migraine headaches but usually when under stress. There is no schedule or pattern to their occurrence. They just occur occasionally. B291 tends to breathe through his mouth when he sleeps and he does have some mild snoring.

B291 does get regular headaches as well as migraine headaches. He says he cannot figure out what triggers them. The headaches occur predominately in the forehead and temporal areas. He does get some mild back pain. He has some lower back pain that is attributed they think to his height and it is aggravated by standing in the upright position. He has some problems with lightheadedness and dizziness if the stands too quickly. No TMJ pain. Headaches he rates at a level of 6 to 7. Shoulder pain around the head and neck area about a 1 to 2. Jaw function is essentially normal.

PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Jaw malalignment.
Teeth malalignment.
Migraines.
He is unaware of clenching and grinding his teeth. No other joints bother him except for his lower back factors.

In frontal view, he appears to have fairly good facial symmetry in the middle and upper third face. Upper lip length is about 27 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 2 mm. Maxillary midline may be about a mm off the side. Mandibular dental midline is about 4 mm off to the right side. Chin is fairly close to being on the facial midline. In profile view, B291 has a little bit of an obtuse nasolabial angle. It seems there may be some AP maxillary deficiency of about 4 mm and AP mandibular deficiency probably 8 to 10 mm. He has some fullness in the submental area. Chin-neckline is well defined. He has a head postured forward neck position. Intraorally, he has all of his teeth back through the third molars except for the lower left cuspid which was previously because of ankylosis problems. No crowding in the lower arch. Attached gingiva appears adequate. In the upper arch he has all of his teeth back through the third molars. He has a reverse curve of Wilson in the upper arch. He has an anterior open bite of about 2 to 3 mm. He has as Class III cuspid relationship bilaterally. He has an anterior end-on incisor relationship. Oropharyngeal airway looks relatively normal with normal uvula, soft palate and faucial pillars. However, he has huge tonsils that approach the midline and probably block off at least half of the available oropharyngeal airway space. Intranasally, the turbinates appear relatively normal in size. The septum is deviated off towards the right side and there is fairly good size septal spur coming off it. It appears to cause some obstruction in the right side of the nose. Holding the nostrils open does not seem to improve B291 's airway significantly. Columella is somewhat wide and it may narrow the nostrils some. He has some mild snoring problems, some moderate daytime somnolence but he is not aware of any sleep apnea problems. B291 

To palpation he has no particular tenderness around the head and neck area except over the TMJs at a level of about 3. Maximal incisal opening is about 54 mm. Excursive movements are 6 mm in each direction. He rates his headaches at a level of 7 in the frontal in and temple area. He rates his TMJ pain at 0, average daily pain 2, jaw function and diet disability at 0. Joints seem to function smoothly.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
:
Panorex shows the presence of all the teeth back through the second molars except missing mandibular right cuspid. Sinuses are clear but difficult to evaluate.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is a little hard to visualize. Joint space looks reasonably good. Articular eminence is moderately steep.
Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears to have some increased vertical joint space and perhaps a little bit increased posterior joint space. Articular eminence is fairly steep. On opening, there appears to be adequate translation in both joints.

Waters view shows that the bony septum has some deviation associated with it. Nasal airway actually looks reasonably good. Sinuses appear relatively clear although the sinuses are relatively small with the left side being somewhat smaller than the right side. Frontal sinus is quite large. There is evidence of wires in the skull area from his previous surgery.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows Class III occlusal relationship and a slightly increased occlusal plane angulation. Accentuated curve of Spee and perhaps some slight over angulation of the maxillary incisors and mandibular incisors. Tonsils are quite enlarge and are set fairly low in the oropharynx. Maxilla and mandible may be slightly AP deficient.

BASIC DIAGNOSES: Crouzon's syndrome.
Rule out mandibular condylar hyperplasia and articular disc dislocations. AP maxillary deficiency.
Transverse maxillary deficiency.
Posterior vertical maxillary excess.
Mandibular prognathism.
Class III occlusion.
Bilateral posterior crossbites.
Anterior end-on incisor relationship.
Anterior open bite.
Missing mandibular right permanent cuspid. Mandibular arch asymmetry with the right bicuspid being about 3 mm anterior to the left bicuspid. Accentuated curve of occlusion in the upper arch. TMJ discomfort. Headaches.
B291 

Deviated nasal septum with spurring off to the right side. Nasal airway partial obstruction. Hypertrophied tonsils.
RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
MRI of left and right TMJs.
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch in three segments but using a continuous archwire. Extrude both cuspids 1 to 2 mm to get them level with the posterior teeth. Align the four incisors. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. Align and level mandibular arch. Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing about 2 mm of tooth structure. Tip the lateral incisor roots mesially and left cuspid and right first bicuspid distally to make room for interdental osteotomies.
Assess for active condylar hyperplasia. Surgery.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to reposition in the mandible and advance the right side.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to expand, advance and superiorly reposition. Anterior mandibular subapical osteotomy to advance the four incisors and rotate to the left.
Right mandibular ostectomy to advance the posterior mandibular segment.
Nasal septoplasty.
Tonsillectomy (by Dr. __)
Consider extracting the maxillary third molars and mandibular left third molar.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

Age: 17/M
Date of Acquisition: 
Date of Interpretation: 
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears to have reasonable mediolateral dimension. It appears to have significant rotation to it but a little bit difficult to tell. He has a medial downward curvature at the lateral rim. There does appear to be decreased joint space and there appears to be some fluid in the superior joint space.

Right TMJ coronal view has a similar appearance as the left side. Joint space seems definitely decreased on this side. It appears there is some fluid in the superior joint space. The condyle likewise has a funny shape to it.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has reasonable good morphology. The disc appears to be anteriorly displaced and sitting beneath the articular eminence. On opening, there is good translation of the condyle and the disc is in a reduced position on top of the condyle. In the opening view, there is evidence of anterior beaking on the condyle and flattening on the anterior aspect of the condyle. The condyle has somewhat of a hockey stick shape to it. There is definite fluid remaining in the superior joint space.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle with a little bit of flattening on the posterior aspect at the lateral aspect of the joint. More medially the condyle appears somewhat narrow anteroposteriorly. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Articular disc appears to be anteriorly displaced and sitting beneath the articular eminence. There is fluid in the superior joint space directly above and slightly posterior to the head of the condyle. Open view shows that the condyle and disc are reduced and translate fairly good. The disc appears to have a reasonably good shape. There is some anterior beaking on the head of the condyle. Fluid can still be seen in the superior joint space although not as prominent as the opposite side.

IMPRESSION: Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with reduction but with fluid in the superior joint space greater on the left side than right side.
