original_note:
B527 
DOB:
AGE:	15 years 6 months
Referred by Dr. XXXX. B527 was born with Pierre Robin sequence with a cleft soft palate. The soft palate was repaired at the age of 6 months by Dr. XXXX. She had a pharyngeal flap performed by Dr. XXXX at the age of 7 to 8. Since the pharyngeal flap, she has had difficulties breathing through her nose. At age of 7, she started her first stage of orthodontics. At age 12 to 15, she was placed in full braces and this included extraction of the maxillary first bicuspids and mandibular second bicuspids.

Her TMJ problems began of unknown etiology and the onset is unknown, but she became aware of having problems about 2 to 3 years ago. Her parents have noted that her lower jaw has become slowly more retruded. Basic concerns B527 has at this time are as follows:

Difficulty breathing the nose.
Jaw joints are shrinking.
Jaw popping.
Bite is off.
B527 gets headaches about twice a week at a level of 2 in her temporal area. She rates her TMJ pain at 4, average daily pain at 3, jaw function at 2, diet at 0, disability at 0. To palpation, she has some minimal tenderness over her shoulders, but nowhere else. The incisal opening is 40 mm, but only 35 mm without pain. The excursion movements are 7 mm in each direction. Joints function smoothly. She has a slight Class II occlusal relationship and a 1 mm vertical overbite relationship. She has no problems with earaches, gets occasional tinnitus, but no vertigo or hearing loss. No other joints bother her. She is not aware of clenching or bruxing. She takes the following medications:

Zyrtec.
Flonase.
Clinical examination shows she has an upper lip length of 20 mm and tooth lip relationship of 3 mm. The left side of her face is 2 to 3 mm more prominent transversely than the right side. There is posterior yaw in the maxilla and mandible of about 2 mm towards the left. She has a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right side being 3 mm shorter vertically than the left side. The dental midlines are coincident with the facial midline, but the chin is 3 mm off to the right side. In profile view, the maxilla is retruded 3 to 4 mm, and the chin is retruded 15 to 18 mm. Intraorally, she does have a narrow pharyngeal flap, but the posterior fascial pillar on the left side is posteriorly positioned and appears to act as a valve shutting off the airway for the most part in the left oronasal portal while the right side appears more open. Tonsils appear normal. Turbinates and septum all appear normal and at least clinically there does not appear to be nasal airway obstruction. She has no snoring, has mild daytime somnolence, no sleep apnea. She is predominantly a mouth breather at nighttime and daytime. She has a head postured forward neck position.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of the maxillary first bicuspids, mandibular second bicuspids, but the presence of all four third molars that are developing and unerupted. The turbinates appear moderate in size. The sinuses appear relatively clear. The condyles appear somewhat arthritic and the right side appears vertically shorter than the left side.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has undergone resorption with some loss of vertical height as well as anteroposterior dimension. There appears to be an osteophytic projection superiorly and on the condyle. The condyle is positioned posteriorly and superiorly in the fossa with an increased anterior joint space. The articular eminence has a moderate inclination.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has undergone significant condylar resorption and loss of vertical height as well as anteroposterior dimension. The articular eminence is much more flat on this side compared to the opposite side. The condyle is posteriorly and superiorly positioned.

Left TMJ coronal tomograms show a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width. There is decreased vertical joint space. There are areas of breakdown in the subcondylar bone. The lateral rim of the fossa is fairly flat with slight downward curvature of 1 to 2 mm.

Right TMJ coronal view shows that has lost significant vertical height and is a little bit more narrow compared to the opposite side. There is decreased vertical joint space. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 mm.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that has a retruded maxilla and mandible with a high occlusal plane angle. There is vertical facial asymmetry with the right side being shorter vertically than the left side. The oropharyngeal airway is significantly constricted with an AP dimension of only about 2 mm at the base of the tongue. There is a hint of a pharyngeal flap present attaching the soft palate to the posterior pharyngeal wall. There is essentially no chin neckline or chin neck angle.

Basic diagnoses for B527 would be as follows:
Pierre Robin Sequence.
Repaired cleft palate.
Pharyngeal flap.
Bilateral TMJ adolescent internal condylar resorption (AICR) and articular disc dislocation and non-reducing.
Mandible hypoplasia.
Maxillary hypoplasia.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway with a dimension of 2 mm (normal dimension is 11 mm).
Impacted third molars x4.
Class II end-on occlusion. Missing mandibular second bicuspids and maxillary first bicuspids. TMJ pain.
Headaches.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1.	Presurgical orthodontics. a.	Continue to align and level maxillary arch. On the left side, tip the lateral incisor root mesially and cuspid root distally to create a little more room for interdental osteotomies. The right side is fine. b.	Align and level mandibular arch.
2.	Surgery. a.	Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors. b.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction. c.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla in a counterclockwise direction and down graft the posterior aspect. d.	Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy. e.	Lengthen pharyngeal flap. f.	Surgical removal of impacted third molars x4.
3.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B527 MRI
DOB:
AGE:	15/F
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width. The articular disc is medially displaced. There is decreased superior joint space. The lateral rim of the fossa is fairly flat.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is small in size mediolaterally and appears to have lost vertical height. The disc appears to be laterally and anteriorly displaced. The left condyle is significantly larger than the right condyle. The lateral rim of the fossa is fairly flat with just about 1 or 2 mm of downward curvature.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that appears somewhat narrow anteroposteriorly particularly towards the medial side of the joint. Towards the lateral side, there is a little bit more normal width. There is decreased superior joint space. There is some flattening on the anterior head of the condyle. Medially, there is a little sclerosis on the condyle. The articular disc is anteriorly displaced as well as medication. Out lateral there is not much discal tissue seen. The articular eminence is moderately steep. There is a little bit of fluid in the superior joint space and also a little bit of fluid in the posterior superior joint space. On opening, there appears to be restricted translation of the condyle and the disc appears to be anteriorly displaced without reduction. On opening, it appears that the discs are non-reducing.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is small in size and appears to have lost vertical volume. There is decreased superior and posterior joint space. The articular disc is anteriorly displaced, but somewhat hidden by an artifact. The articular eminence appears a little bit more shallow on this side. The disc may be laterally displaced as well. On opening, it appears that the discs are non-reducing.

IMPRESSION:
Left TMJ enlarged condyle with anterior and medially articular discs without reduction.
Right TMJ arthritic condyle with loss of vertical volume with an anteriorly displaced disc without reduction.
Discs appear salvageable.

original_note:
PATRICIA PARK									01/07/2011
DOB:	07/08/1984
AGE:	26

Patricia is a first year orthodontic graduate student.  She has had previous orthodontics with maxillary first bicuspid extractions.  She has also had her wisdom teeth removed.  Primary concerns she has are:

Retruded maxilla and mandible.
Difficulties breathing through her nose.
Myofascial pain.

She gets headaches about once every 2 weeks at a level of 4 in the temple area.  She has no direct TMJ pain.  She rates her average daily pain at about 3.  She rates her jaw function, diet, and disability at 0.  The incisal opening is normal with good excursion movements.  Joints seem to function reasonably smoothly.  She has not trouble with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, or hearing loss.  No other joints bother her.  She is aware of clenching at nighttime and also some during the day.

CLINICAL EXAM.  Upper lip length is 20 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is 4.5 mm.  The maxillary midline is shifted about 1 mm to the right as is the mandibular dental midline.  Chin is shifted 2 to 3 mm to the right.  There is a slight cant in the occlusal plane with the right side being about 1 mm vertically shorter than the left side.  She is basically a Class I cuspid relationship and is missing maxillary first bicuspids.  In profile, upper lip is retruded about 2 to 3 mm and chin is retruded about 15 mm.  She has about 8 mm of lip incompetence.  She has a Class I occlusion and 0 overbite relationship.  She has bilateral posterior cross bites.  Oropharyngeal tissues look normal and healthy.  Turbinates are enlarged and she likely has some moderate nasal airway obstruction.  Patricia is not aware of snoring, daytime somnolence or sleep apnea.  She is a mouth breather particularly when she sleeps.

Radiographic evaluation shows the absence of all four third molars and maxillary first bicuspids.  Sinuses appear relatively clear.  

Coronal view shows that the turbinates are quite large and appear to obliterate most of the airway. 

Axial view shows that the sinuses are clear and the turbinates are quite large and appear to block most of the airway.  The septum appears reasonably straight.  

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condylar head and neck that are larger than the opposite site.  The articular eminence has a moderate inclination.  There is decreased posterior joint space.  The disc might be just slightly anteriorly displaced.  Out laterally, there is an increased anterior joint space and further decreased posterior joint space and some flattening on the head of the condyle.  

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology and is smaller than the opposite side.  The articular eminence has a moderate inclination.  There is decreased posterior joint space particularly at the lateral aspect of the joint.  

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is narrower than the opposite side.  The joint space appears fairly reasonable.  The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 mm.  

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is actually wider than the left side.  There is a little lateral extrusion of the lateral pole of the condyle.  The fossa curves inferiorly about 2 mm.  The joint space looks fairly reasonable.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has a high occlusal plane angle facial morphology.  There is anterior vertical maxillary hyperplasia.  The mandible is retruded.  There is AP microgenia.  The lower incisors are over angulated.  Oropharyngeal airway at the soft palate measures about 5 mm and at the base of the tongue measures about 7.5 to 8 mm.  She does have some lip incompetence.  

Basic diagnoses would be as follows:

Rule out bilateral TMJ articular disc dislocations.
Maxillary anterior vertical hyperplasia and AP and transverse hypoplasia.
AP mandibular hypoplasia.
High occlusal plane angle.
Bilateral posterior cross bites.
Anterior open bite tendency. 
Decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Hypertrophied turbinates.
Nasal airway obstruction. 

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	MRI of left and right TMJs.
2.	Orthodontics.
	a.	Align and level maxillary arch.  Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots 			distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. 
	b.	Align and level mandibular arch.  Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing at 			least 2 mm of tooth structure and retract.
3.	Surgery.
	a.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move it superiorly, decrease the occlusal plane 			angle, and expand.
	b.	Bilateral ramus osteotomies to rotate in a counterclockwise direction.  
	c.	Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy to augment the chin.
	d.	Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies. 
	e.	Consider TMJ surgery if MRI confirms articular disc dislocations.
4.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B364 
B364 is 30 years old. He was referred by Dr. __. B364's primary concerns are as follows:

Sleep apnea.
Popping in the right TMJ. Discomfort in the right jaw area.
B364 takes the following medications:
Propecia.
Asmanex inhaler which he began just a couple of months ago.

B364 was diagnosed with Hodgkin's lymphoma 5 years ago. He has had previous surgery for pectus excavatum 20 years ago. He had some foot surgery 13 years ago. He had a lump removed from his left neck in 5 years ago which is when the diagnosis for the Hodgkin's lymphoma was identified.

B364 has no problems with headaches, but gets TMJ pain at about a level of 2, but on the average day does not have any significant pain. He rates his jaw function, diet, and disability all at 0. He has a little bit of numbness in the left submandibular area where the neck mass was removed 5 years ago.

B364's TMJ problems began as best he can remember about age of 20 when he noted clicking and popping in his right joint. The etiology is unknown. He has had not treatment to date for it. B364 has severe snoring. He has probably some daytime somnolence. Sleep apnea is probably at a moderate level, although he does have 70 breathing interruptions per hour. He has probably a moderate sleep disorder, and he is a predominant mouth breather particularly at night. B364 does have a CPAP machine, but does not notice any significant improvement in his situation with using it or not using it.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
In frontal view, B364 has fairly good symmetry in the lower and upper third face with maybe some slight asymmetry in the lower third face. There is a little bit of increased fullness in the left side of the face by probably 3 to 4 mm. Upper lip length is 22 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is zero. The maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline. The nasal tip is just slightly to the left side about 1 or 2 mm. The mandibular dental midline is shifted about almost 2 mm to the left, but the chin is fairly well aligned with the facial midline. In profile view, B364 may have about 3 mm of AP deficiency in the maxilla. The chin may be 10 to 12 mm AP deficient. Chin neckline and angle are not well defined. Intraorally, B364 has all of his lower teeth back through the second molars present. The lower incisors are tipped towards the left side at the incisal tip. The left cuspid is 1 mm posterior to the right cuspid. In the upper arch, he has all of his teeth back through the second molars. B364 has a Class I cuspid-molar relationship on the right side and Class II end-on relationship on the left side by probably about 3 mm. B364 has an anterior deep bite of about 4 mm. B364 does have some transverse constricture of both maxillary and mandibular arches. This may contribute to displacement of his tongue posteriorly. He seems to have adequate attached gingiva, although there is a little retraction of the gingiva around the incisors, particularly the 2 centrals and the left and lateral as well as the left cuspid. Oropharyngeal tissues look healthy. Uvula is slightly elongated. Intranasally, the turbinates appear relatively normal in size. The septum however is deviated significantly off towards the left side anteriorly. Posteriorly, it may be a little bit off towards the right side, but difficult to tell. To palpation, B364 has no tenderness around the head and neck area. He appears to have clicking and popping in both joints with the right one significantly louder and more predominant compared to the left side. On the right side, the clicks occur at about 20 mm of opening and about 10 mm of closing, whereas on the left side, a softer click occurred at about 15 mm of opening and about 5 mm of closing. Maximal incisal opening is 57 mm. Excursion movements to each side is 11 mm. B364 appears to have some mild masseteric hyperplasia bilaterally, but it seems fairly even on both sides.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of the four third molars, but all the rest of the teeth are present. There is a root canal in the maxillary right second molar. B364 has relatively prominent gonial notches, being more prominent on the left side than the right side which would go along with the maybe slight increase buildup of the masseter muscle on the left side compared to the right side. Sinuses look relatively clear. Waters view shows that the right side of the nose looks fairly open, but the left side appears pretty much obliterated in part due to deviation of the septum as well as the turbinate appears quite large posteriorly. Sinuses look relatively clear.
Right TMJ tomogram shows that there appears to be decrease posterior and superior joint space, although the condyle appears to have reasonably good morphology. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that likewise appears to be a little bit superior and posterior positioned in the fossa. Articular eminence has a moderate inclination. On opening, there is good translation of both condyles. Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that B364 has a moderately high occlusal plane angle and retrusion of the maxilla and mandible. The oropharyngeal airway measures about 5 mm at the soft palate level and about 5 mm at the base of the tongue. Soft palate appears a little bit elongated.

Basic diagnoses for B364 would be as follows:
Sleep apnea syndrome. Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocations with reduction.
AP maxillary hypoplasia. Posterior vertical maxillary hypoplasia. AP mandibular hypoplasia. Class II end-on occlusion on the left side, Class I occlusion on the right side.
Transverse narrowness of both the maxillary and mandibular dental arches. Moderate crowding of lower anterior arch. Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 1.5 mm. Deviated nasal septum. Hypertrophied left turbinate.
Nasal airway obstruction. Significantly decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of 5 mm (normal AP dimension 11 mm).

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
MRI of left and right TMJ. Presurgical orthodontics. Align and level maxillary arch. Align and level mandibular arch. Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing about 1.5 mm of tooth structure.
Try to expand the mandibular arch orthodontically by up righting the teeth over distal bone. Hopefully, we can gain about 4 mm of transverse width in the arch. Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors. Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction. Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla in a counterclockwise direction by down grafting it posteriorly as well as expand the arch transversely. Nasal septoplasty.
Left turbinectomy.
4. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B364 MRI 
AGE: 30/M
DOB: 
Date of Acquisition: 
Date of Interpretation: 
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has a little bit of an odd shape to it and appears to be lacking the lateral pole of the condyle. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 mm. There is almost an exophytic bump on the lateral aspect of the condyle, but down where the lateral pole should be. The condyle is confined to the inner portion of the fossa. It appears that there is discal tissue anterior to the condyle.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle appears larger and appears to have more of a lateral pole. Lateral rim curves inferiorly about 3 mm. There appears to be discal tissue anterior the condyle.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology. A little flattening on the anterior head and some slight anterior beaking present, but not too significant. The disc is anteriorly displaced, but towards the medial side the disc is in better position. Articular eminence is moderately steep. There is fluid in the superior joint space. On opening, the disc reduces and there is good translation of the condyle and the disc.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology. Articular eminence is moderately steep. The articular disc is anteriorly displaced, particularly at the lateral aspect of the joint. A little medial, it is in a better position. Posterior joint space is somewhat decreased. There is fluid in the superior joint space anteriorly beneath the articular eminence. On opening, there reduction of the disc and good translation of the condyle and the disc.

IMPRESSION:
Left TMJ mild arthritis and articular disc anteriorly dislocated with reduction. Right TMJ anteriorly displaced articular disc with early reduction. Both joints demonstrate a rotational displacement with the discs displaced at the lateral aspect of the joint, but over medially are in better position. Indicated treatment would be bilateral TMJ disc repositioning with Mitek anchors.

GEORGIA KEITH									05/03/2006

Georgia is 13 years old.  She had previous surgery that was an adenoidectomy.  Otherwise, she is in good health.  She takes minocycline for acne.  No apparent allergies.  Georgia’s problems began to the best of the patient’s knowledge about three years ago of unknown etiology.  In 2003/2004 she had a maxillary expansion appliance placed.  About nine months ago she had a hypo headgear placed but this did not seem to do any good according to the orthodontist.  She received full upper and lower braces in December of 2005.  

She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo or hearing loss.  No other joints bother her.  She used to grind her teeth at night but now her father says that usually when she is sleeping her mouth is open.  She has no problems with headaches, TMJ pain or myofascial pain.  She rates her jaw function at 1, diet at 3, disability at 0.  

In frontal view, Georgia has fairly good left to right facial symmetry with just a slight asymmetry in the mandible.  Upper lip length is about 20 mm.  Upper tooth to lip relationship is about 
3 mm.  Maxillary midline and mandibular dental midlines are about 1 mm off to the right side.  Chin is probably about 2 mm off to the right side.  In profile view, she has some AP maxillary deficiency of probably about 2 to 3 mm.  Chin is retruded about 20 mm.  Chin-neckline is not well defined.  Georgia has a head postured forward neck position.  Intraorally she has all of her lower teeth back through the second molar present.  Teeth are actually fairly well aligned and leveled.  She has fairly good attached gingiva.  She has a high labial frenulum.  In the upper arch she has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  She has bands on the first molars that might be interfering with the MRI.  Upper teeth appear fairly well aligned and leveled.  She has an anterior open bite a little bit greater on the right side than the left side.  She hits prematurely on the posterior left side.  The first and second molars on the left side may hit first followed by the right side but from the bicuspids forward she appears to have an open bite.  Her bite seems to have closed up by history since the orthodontic appliances were placed.  She has about a 0 overbite relationship.  She has a 1 mm overjet relationship.  Intranasally, the septum is deviated just slightly off to the left side.  The left turbinate is a little bit enlarged and there is slight decreased airway space on that side compared to the opposite side which has a relatively normal airway space.  To palpation, Georgia has no pain around the head and neck area.  The left joint seems to function relatively smoothly.  She did have a little glitch in the right joint about 25 mm of opening.  Maximal incisal opening is 40 mm.  Excursive movement is about 5 mm in each direction.  There could be just a slight transverse cant in the occlusion with the right side being maybe 1 mm higher than the left side but difficult to tell.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION:
Panorex shows the presences of all four third molars.  All other teeth are present and accounted for.  Sinuses appear clear.  Nasal airway appears adequate.  She demonstrates severe condylar resorption being just slightly greater on the right side than the left side.  Condyles are almost to the level of the sigmoid notch particularly on the right side.  




GEORGIA KEITH 				05/03/2006				Page 2


Waters view shows that the sinuses are clear.  Nasal septum is deviated just slightly to the left.  Turbinates appear moderate in size with a little bit decreased airway in the left side compared to the right side.  There is some very mild asymmetry with the dental midlines and chin being shifted just slightly off to the right side. 

Left TMJ tomogram shows severe condylar resorption.  The condyle is actually functioning beneath the articular eminence.  Definition in the fossa is somewhat obscured.   The condyle is definitely functioning anterior to the fossa.  It appears there is almost some form of calcification in the fossa.  It could be that the articular eminence is severely resorbed as well and perhaps some reactive bone in the base of the fossa. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle functioning slightly forward in the fossa and the articular eminence is very shallows.  Some increased posterior joint space.  Condyle is extremely resorbed and moreso than the opposite side. 

Open view shows moderately good translation of the condyles with a little bit greater translation on the right side as compared to the left side.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has a reverse curvature in the neck.  She has a significant Class II skeletal relationship and a slight Class II occlusal relationship.  She has a high occlusal plane angle.  She has decreased oropharyngeal airway at the soft palate and also at the base of the tongue measuring less than 5 mm at the soft palate level and 5 mm at the base of the tongue.  

REVIEW OF PREVIOUS RADIOGRAPHS:
Previous Panorex radiographs begin on 06/25/2003.  In this view, there is already evidence of degenerative changes occurring in the right TMJ although the left one appears in better condition.  Acceptable length of condylar head and neck is present.  Articular eminences appear reasonable at that time.  She is in the mixed dentition.  She would have been 9 years old at the time.  

Panorex 03/23/2004, shows presence of significant osseous changes during that time period with flattening of the condylar heads and loss of condylar volume and shortening of the condylar head and neck dimension.  

Panorex 05/25/2005, shows that there is considerable loss of vertical height and volume of the condyles.  She only has one retained primary crown at this time and the permanent teeth are erupted through back to the first molars and the second molars are almost erupted through.  Age 11.




GEORGIA KEITH				05/03/2006				Page 3


Panorex 03/20/2006, shows severe condylar resorption with the condyles almost to the sigmoid notch level.  She has an anterior open bite occluding only on what appears to be the maxillary first and second molars, mandibular second molar on the left and similar occlusion on the right side with all other teeth out of occlusion. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph 04/29/2002, demonstrates that she has actually an anterior deep bite of probably about 4 mm.  Relatively normal occlusal plane angle and basically fairly close to a Class I skeletal and occlusal relationship.  She has a large adenoid mass in the nasopharynx.  Airway looks reasonably adequate.  

Comparison with lateral cephalometric tracings of 04/29/2002 with 05/24/2005 shows that she has had significant vertical growth and downward and backwards rotation of the mandible.  She went from about a 4 mm deep bite relationship anteriorly to 1 mm open bite and a 3 mm overjet relationship.  It was after the 05/24/2005 radiograph that she did have the hypo headgear placed with probable superior displacement of the posterior teeth that did allow the bite to rotate up and forward some to close the open bite.  It appears that there might be just a tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of maybe 1 to 2 mm. 

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
Bilateral TMJ severe condylar resorption and degenerative of articular disc with a reactive pannus. 
Severe AP mandibular deficiency.
AP maxillary deficiency. 
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
Slight Class II end-on occlusion.
Anterior open bite.
High occlusal plane angle.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Impacted maxillary mandibular third molars.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
MRI of left and right TMJs.
Presurgical orthodontics.
Continue to align and level maxillary arch.  Rotate mesial aspect of lateral incisor labially to improve alignment.    
Continue to align and level mandibular arch.  Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing about 2 mm of tooth structure and close the spacing to correct for the tooth size discrepancy as well as slightly decrease the over-angulated lower incisors.
Surgical removal of mandibular third molars x2. 
CT scan of jaws and jaw joints.  (TMJ Concepts protocol.)


GEORGIA KEITH 				05/03/2006				Page 4


Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. 
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to down graft the posterior aspect and slightly advance it.
Bilateral coronoidectomies.
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts. (Harvest from the abdomen.)
Removal of impacted maxillary third molars x2. 
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B007
B007is 23 years old.
When B007 was 14, he was in school and one day noticed that his jaw locked on him of unknown etiology. He kept it to himself and did not tell anybody about it. He had had previous orthodontics from about the age of 9 through 12 and had a really good bite relationship. Following this incident over time, his bite began to shift and change. He started wearing a splint two years ago but stopped last year without significant relief of his symptoms. Primary concerns that B007 has include the following:

Right condylar resorption.
Bilateral facial muscle pain.
Headaches. Neck muscle pain.
Uneven bite. Difficulties eating and chewing.
B007gets headaches probably 5 out of 7 days during the week at average level of 6 in the temporal and posterior head area bilaterally. He does not have any overt TMJ pain, but has myofascial pain through the jaws that he rates at about a level of 5. He rates his jaw function at 3, diet at 2, disability at 1. He has no problems with earaches, gets occasional tinnitus, but no vertigo and has questionable hearing loss. The only other joints that really concern him are his knees. He is not aware of clenching or bruxism, but occasionally during the daytime finds he may occasionally clench some. B007 feels that his speech is affected because of his bite relationship.

In frontal view, B007 has a good facial symmetry in the middle and upper third face. He has some slight asymmetry in the lower third face. Upper lip length is about 20 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is about 2.5 mm. The maxillary and mandibular midline are fairly well aligned with each other. The chin may be just slightly to the right about 2 to 3 mm. In profile, he has probably about 4 or 5 mm AP maxillary deficiency and probably about 12 to 15 mm AP mandibular deficiency. The chin neckline is decreased in length, although the chin neck angle is fairly well defined. Intraorally, B007 has all of his lower teeth back through the second molars. The teeth appear nicely aligned and leveled. In the upper arch, B007 has all of his teeth back through the second molars. He has just a slight Class II tendency on the right side of about 1 mm in the cuspid area and probably about the same on the left side. He has about a 1 mm vertical over bite relationship. He has a slight transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the right cuspid in the maxilla being about 1 mm higher than the left side and in the mandible, the right cuspid is 2 mm higher than left cuspid. As B007bites his teeth together, he hits prematurely on the right side compared to the left side. Oropharyngeal tissues look normal and healthy except the uvula is slightly elongated. Intranasally, the right turbinate appears moderately enlarged and the left one appears a little bit more normal at least in the anterior aspect of the nose. The septum may be just slightly off to the right side. On opening, he had an audible pop at about 45 to 50 mm of opening on the right side, but the left side was smooth. He has a closing click at about 40 mm. On excursion, he also had a click in the right joint, but not in the left side. B007 tends to feel more comfortable if he keeps his teeth apart and postures his lower jaw forward slightly.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all 4 third molars. The condyle particularly on the right side appears smaller than the left side. The sinuses look relatively clear. The left turbinate actually appears enlarged compared to the right side. He has a 3 x 3 retainer in the lower arch.

Coronal view shows that the septum is deviated a little bit off towards the right side. The left turbinate actually is quite large and appears to block most of the airway on that side. The right turbinate appears a little bit smaller in size, although the airway is a little bit constricted there as well. The sinuses look clear.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology with a little sclerosis on top. The articular eminence is moderately steep. There appears to be reasonably good joint space around the condyle.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is smaller in size anteroposteriorly and vertically, although there appears to be good cortical bone across the top of the condyle. The articular eminence is moderately steep, but not quite as steep as the opposite side. The joint space may be slightly decreased posteriorly.

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has reasonably good mediolateral width. The joint space looks reasonably good, although could be just slightly decreased at the lateral aspect of the joint. The lateral rim of the fossa is fairly flat with just a minimal downward curvature of about 1 mm or so.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that appears to have lost some vertical volume and may be just slightly narrow mediolaterally compared to the opposite side. There is decreased vertical joint space. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 mm. There appears to be greater decreased joint space at the lateral aspect of the joint as compared to the mid portion. Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that B007's maxilla and mandible both appear to be somewhat retruded. Oropharyngeal airway is about 8 mm at the base of the tongue and about 6 to 7 mm at the soft palate level.

Basic diagnoses for B007 would be as follows:
Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with very late reduction.
Rule out left TMJ pathology.
Maxillary AP and posterior vertical hypoplasia.
Mandibular AP hypoplasia.
Transverse cant in occlusion with the right maxilla being 1 mm higher than the left side with the mandible being 2 mm on the right compared to the left side. Chin is shifted to the right 2 to 3 mm.
Bilateral myofascial pain affecting predominantly the temporalis and masseter muscles.
Bilateral headaches.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1.	MRI of left and right TMJ.
2.	Presurgical orthodontics. a.	Align and level maxillary arch. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. b.	Align and level mandibular arch. Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing about 1.5 mm of tooth structure and retract.
3.	Surgery. a.	Right TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor. b.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction and level it transversely. c.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla forward in a counterclockwise direction, down grafting it posteriorly, and uprighting the incisors.
4.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
ASHLYN RAWLS									05/26/2009

Ashyln is 16 years old and is a self referral.  She has had previous orthodontics with Dr. Michael Steffen.  Dr. Steffen did suggest during the orthodontic treatment that she have an evaluation and orthognathic surgery, but she and the family declined to proceed on with that.  Ashlyn and her parents have been aware that her lower jaw appears to be getting progressively more retruded. 

Ashlyn has been aware of having TMJ problems since about the age of 10 of unknown etiology.  She had a tonsillectomy in 2003 at the age of 10.  She did have orthodontic treatment from 2006 to 2008.  She has no problems with headaches, but rates her TMJ pain at 3, average daily pain at 3, jaw function at 3, diet at 2, disability at 0.  Incisal opening is 55 mm and she could open to 43 mm without significant pain.  Excursion movements are 10 mm in each direction.  On opening, she has a definite pop in the right joint at 25-30 mm of opening and at about 20-25 mm of closing.  The left joint is relatively smooth.  To palpation, she has moderate tenderness over the retromandibular area.  

CLINICAL ASSESSMENT:  In frontal view, she has fairly good left-to-right facial asymmetry.  Upper lip length is 20 mm and upper tooth lip relationship is 3 mm.  Nasal dorsum is wide.  In profile, she has AP retrusion of the upper lip and maxilla of 3-4 mm and AP deficiency of the chin of 15-18 mm.  Chin neckline is not well defined.  She has dorsal prominence on her nose and some nasal ptosis.  Intraorally, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars present.  Ashlyn has a slight Class II occlusion of about 2 mm.  Oropharyngeal tissues look healthy.  Some mild to moderate constricture of the fascial pillars is present.  Tonsils are absent, although she has a couple of small areas on the anterior fascial pillar where there is a little what appears to be residual adenoid tissue.  Intranasally, the septum is deviated just slightly off towards the left side.  Turbinates are moderately enlarged, and, in fact, on the left side, the turbinate almost touches against the septum.  I would guess she has probably mild to moderate obstruction.  Columella is moderately wide.  Nostrils are moderately narrow.  To palpation, Ashlyn has some mild tenderness over the retromandibular area.  On opening, she has a soft click in the right joint at about 25-30 mm of opening and at about 20-25 mm of closing.  The left side seemed reasonably smooth.  Maximal incisal opening is 55 mm, opening without discomfort 43 mm.  Excursion movements are about 10 mm in each direction.  She has a vertical overbite of 1.5 mm. 

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the presence of four impacted third molars.  There is a 3 x 3 lower retainer.  It is difficult to evaluate the joints, but they do appear a little bit different.  Turbinates appear large in size.  Sinuses appear relatively clear.  
Coronal view shows that the turbinates are extremely large and seem to fill most of the nasal cavity.  Sinuses appear relatively clear.   
Axial view shows the sinuses are clear, but the turbinates are extremely large.  
Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that appears to have undergone some resorption.  There is some anterior beaking present.  Joint space looks reasonably good.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  Joint space appears relatively normal.  
Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that also appears to have undergone some resorption.  There is a little bit of anterior beaking present.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  Joint space seems reasonably well balanced, although a little bit narrow anteriorly.  There appears to be some anterior beaking present.  
Left TMJ coronal tomograms show a condyle that seems somewhat narrow mediolaterally.  Lateral rim curves inferiorly quite significantly probably 4-5 mm.  Joint space may be mildly decreased.     
Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is a little bit narrow, but not quite as narrow as the opposite side.  Lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly quite significantly probably 5-6 mm.  The condylar head appears a little bit arthritic.  There is a lateral bulge at the lateral pole of the condyle.  Joint space may be somewhat narrowed.   
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she has a slight Class II end-on occlusal relationship, but skeletal Class II relationship.  She has some lip incompetence.  Oropharyngeal airway is somewhat narrow at the AP dimension of about 6 mm at the soft palate level and at the base of the tongue 

Basic diagnoses for Ashlyn would be as follows:    

Bilateral TMJ resorptive arthritis and articular disc dislocation (compatible with AICR). 
AP mandibular hypoplasia.
AP maxillary hypoplasia.
Posterior vertical maxillary hypoplasia.
High occlusal plane angle.
Class II end-on occlusion. 
Decreased oropharyngeal airway. 
Hypertrophied turbinates creating nasal airway obstruction. 
Impacted third molars x4. 
Over-angulation of mandibular anterior teeth, but in stable position. 
External nasal deformity.
TMJ pain. 

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

MRI of left and right TMJ. 
Presurgical orthodontics.  
Align and level maxillary arch.  Keep lateral incisors roots tipped mesially and cuspid roots tipped distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. 
Align and level mandibular arch.    
Surgery. 
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise rotation.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla and down graft it posteriorly. 
Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy to augment the chin. 
Partial inferior turbinectomies. 
Surgical removal of impacted third molars x4. 
External rhinoplasty. 
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B024
B024 is 39 years old. We are going to refer B024 to Dr. XXXX.
B024 states that her TMJ problems began when she was about 12 years old on the right side. Her jaw would click at that time. Etiology is unknown. The left side began causing problems when she was about 18 years old. Etiology is also unknown. She did have splint therapy from 14 years ago, but the treatment did not help and actually made things worse. She tried again about 6 years ago with splint therapy, but this also did not help and made her situation worse. B024 rates her headaches at a level of 8 on the right side in the frontal, temporal and top of the head. She rates her TMJ pain at 9, average daily pain at 8, jaw function at 9, diet at 8, disability at 9. She takes Advil, ibuprofen, and Bayer aspirin as needed for pain. She gets frequent earaches, frequent tinnitus, and frequent vertigo, but she has no hearing loss and no other joints bother her. She is aware of clenching and bruxing at nighttime but not during the day. She is allergic to Lortab. Primary concerns that B024 has includes the following:

Pain and soreness in the jaws and jaw joints. Jaws lock and disfigures her face.
Progressive difficulty eating to where her diet is quite limited to tuna fish sandwiches.
Jaws lock whenever she yawns.

In frontal view, B024 has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 25 mm. Upper lip tooth relationship is about 4 mm. The maxillary midline is pretty close to being on the facial midline, although the roots are angled off towards the left side of both centrals. The mandibular dental midline is off to the left side, although the chin is fairly well aligned on the facial midline. In profile, B024 has fairly good balance between the upper lips, cheeks, and nose. Chin appears a little bit retruded being probably about 15 mm AP deficient. The chin neckline is somewhat short. She does have a head forward neck position. Intraorally, she has all of her teeth back to the second molars present. She has prominent incisor tips on the cuspids. In the upper arch, she has her teeth all the way back through the second molars. She has a Class II division 2 malocclusion and an anterior deep bite of probably at least 10 to 12 mm. Oropharyngeal tissues look healthy, although fairly difficult to visualize. To palpation, B024 has some mild tenderness over the right temporalis, right TMJ area. On opening, she has a soft click in the left side at about 20 mm of opening and closing at 15 mm. She hears grinding noises in the right joint. The maximal incisal opening is 38 mm, opening without significant pain 33 mm. Excursion movement to the right 11 mm and to the left 9 mm.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex evaluation shows the presence of all teeth in the upper arch and all but the lower third molars which are missing in the lower arch. Joints look a little bit arthritic with some flattening on the heads and a little bit of anterior beaking. The sinuses look relatively clear. The right turbinate appears slightly larger than the left one. The septum appears reasonably straight.

Coronal view shows the turbinates are significantly enlarged. The sinus are relatively clear. The septum deviates slightly off to the right side.
Axial view shows that the septum actually has a slight deviation anteriorly to the left side. The turbinates are moderately enlarged. The sinuses are clear.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has some sclerosis on the head and anterior beaking. There is decreased vertical and posterior joint space. The articular eminence has a moderate inclination.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has some slight anterior beaking. There is decreased vertical and posterior joint space. The articular eminence has a moderate inclination.

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is a little bit narrow mediolaterally. There is sclerosis on the top of the condyle. There is decreased vertical joint space, particularly at the medial side of the joint with increased joint space out laterally. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 to 3 mm.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is a little bit narrow mediolaterally. There is decreased vertical and lateral joint spaces. There could be bone-on-bone contact towards the lateral aspect of the joint. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 3 to 4 mm.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that B024 has a Class II skeletal and occlusal relationship. Oropharyngeal airway is quite constricted with an airway dimension of about 2 mm at the soft palate and about 4 mm at the base of the tongue.

Basic diagnosis for B024 would be as follows:
Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation without reduction and with severe deformation.
Left TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly dislocated with reduction.
AP mandibular hypoplasia.
Maxillary posterior vertical hypoplasia but anterior vertical hyperplasia.
Class II Division 2 malocclusion. Anterior deep bite of 13 mm.
Reversed curve of Spee upper arch.
Increased curve of Spee lower arch. Retained maxillary third molars.
Severely decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of 3 mm (normal 11 mm).
Sleep apnea.
TMJ pain.
Headaches.
Severe decreased angulation of the maxillary incisors. Malaligned maxillary third molars.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1. Sleep study.
2. Presurgical orthodontics. a.	Align and level maxillary arch. Keep lateral incisor roots tipped mesially and cuspid roots tipped distally for interdental osteotomies. b.	Align and level mandibular arch.
3. CT scan of jaws and jaw joints.
4. Surgery. a.	Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. b.	Bilateral TMJ fat grafts. c.	Bilateral coronoidotomies. d.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies. e.	Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy. f.	Extraction of maxillary third molars.
5. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B024 MRI AGE: 39/F
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology, a little sclerosis on the top. The vertical joint space may be slightly decreased. It appears there may be discal tissue anteriorly and laterally displaced. The lateral rim of the fossa is fairly flat and curving downward only about 2 mm.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with fairly good mediolateral width. The joint space may be somewhat decreased vertically. It appears that there could be discal tissue anteriorly and laterally displaced.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has some flattening on the anterior head. There is a little bit of anterior beaking present. There is a little bit of peaking at the top of the condyle. There is decreased superior and posterior joint space. The articular disc is anteriorly displaced and laterally displaced. The articular eminence is moderately steep. The disc has fairly good morphology. There is fluid in the superior joint space both anteriorly and posteriorly.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has some flattening on the posterior head and a little bit of anterior beaking. There is decreased vertical joint space. The articular eminence is moderately steep. The articular disc is anteriorly and laterally displaced. There is a little bit of fluid in the superior joint space.

There were no open views available.
IMPRESSION: Bilateral TMJ arthritis with anteriorly and laterally displaced discs.

original_note:
EVA MADISON										04-11-03


Patient History 
Eva is 31 years old and was referred by Dr. Rick Roblee.  Her only previous surgery was removal of her wisdom teeth.  Eva’s problems began within the past 5 years of unknown etiology.  She did have orthodontics as a teenager with extraction of upper 1st bicuspids and lower 2nd bicuspids.  She has had a splint for the past 4 months that has helped some with the pain.  Current medications include: BCP, Advil, and Claritin occasionally.  She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, or hearing loss.  She gets headaches 3 to 4 times per week and usually wakes up with them.  The headaches are in the temporal and frontal areas at a level of 5.  She rates her TMJ pain at 0, overall pain at 5, jaw function at 5, diet at 1 and disability at 3.  She has no known problems with jewelry.  She is aware of having nocturnal and daytime clenching and bruxism.  She has no other joint problems.
Patient Concerns
Patient Concerns Teeth and jaws don’t line up very well
Headaches 
Pain through the muscles of the jaws


Clinical Examination

Lower lip and dentoalveolus is retruded significantly posterior to the chin and retruded posterior to the upper lip as well. As Eva postures her mandible forward to correct the occlusion, her lips become in much better balance, but her chin is quite prominent and probably is 4 to 6 mm stronger than it should be. Chin-neckline is perhaps a little bit short in dimension, and chin-neck angle is not well-defined.

Intraorally, Eva is missing the mandibular second bicuspids but has the rest of her teeth back through the second molars. Crowing in the lower anterior arch with much better crowding on the left side as compared to the right side. Attached gingival is somewhat sparse, but there may be adequate tissue there. She has an accentuated curve of occlusion in the lower arch. There is probably 5 mm of crowding on that left side, which will be beneficial to us. In the upper arch, she is missing the first bicuspids. Has a V-shaped upper arch. She has a Class II cuspid-molar relationship, but the overjet is significantly greater than the Class II relationship. Overjet is probably about 8 mm. Anterior overbite is about 4 to 5 mm. As Eva slides her mandible forward into a Class I cuspid relationship, her incisors are fairly tight, which is indicative of probably a significant tooth-size discrepancy. 

Intranasally, the turbinates are of moderate size, and it appears they are a little bit inflamed. Airway may be somewhat decreased on both sides. Eva is not sure if she breathes through her mouth or her nose when she sleeps. 

Transversely, the lower arch appears to have fairly reasonable symmetry. Likewise, the upper arch appears to have fairly good AP symmetry. Could be just a slight transverse cant in the occlusion with the left side being just slightly vertically longer than the right side but by no more than a millimeter. 

To palpation, she has some very mild tenderness over the styloid areas and some minimal tenderness over the right masseter area. No tenderness over the TMJs. TMJs seem to function relatively smoothly. Excursive movements are about 11 mm in each direction, and incisal opening is 53 mm.

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars as well as the mandibular second bicuspids and upper first bicuspids. Sinuses appear relatively clear. Condyles appear large but relatively normal in architecture. Significantly accentuated curve of occlusion in the lower arch is present. Can’t really identify the nasal airway structures very well on this radiograph.

Waters view shows that the sinuses are relatively clear. Septum appears fairly straight. Left nasal airway appears open, but the right side appears somewhat blocked. May be a little cloudiness in the left sinus. 

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle with a little bit of flattening on the anterior head and a little bit of peaking at the top. A little hockey stick shape to the condyle. Articular eminence is fairly steep. However, articular disc appears to be in position. The joint space appears relatively well-balanced. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle with a little bit of a hockey stick shape to it and a little flattening on the posterior head and a little flattening on the anterior head. Articular eminence is fairly steep. Joint space is fairly well-balanced. Disc may be in satisfactory position although might be in a little bit of vertical orientation.

On opening, there is good translation of both condyles and adequate space between the eminences and the condyles.

Cephalometrically, she has a skeletal Class II jaw relationship. Some slight over-angulation of the upper incisors perhaps and decreased angulation of the lower incisors. Oropharyngeal airway space looks adequate. 


Dental Model Analysis

Dental model analysis shows there is some significant attrition on the upper cuspids. Significant tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of 4.6 mm. Posturing the models into a Class I position shows that there is a bilateral posterior crossbite tendency.

Basic Diagnoses

AP maxillary deficiency
Posterior vertical maxillary excess
Transverse maxillary deficiency
Severe AP mandibular deficiency
AP macrogenia
Class II occlusion 
Missing mandibular left and right second bicuspids, maxillary left and right first bicuspids
Significant crowding, lower anterior arch
Mandibular dental midline shifted 2 to 3 mm to the left of the chin midline and 1 mm to the left of the maxillary and facial midlines
Significant mesial inclination of the mandibular first and second molars
Headaches
Myofascial pain
Anterior deep-bite
Nocturnal clenching and bruxism
Significant tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of 4.5 mm
Hypertrophied turbinates
Partial nasal airway obstruction

Recommended Treatment

Medication (Klonopin 0.5 mg tablet, 1 q.h.s.) to help control nocturnal clenching and bruxism

Presurgical orthodontics
Align and level maxillary arch. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. Try to create a little bit of space around each upper lateral incisor to equal about a millimeter. If this will cause too much over-angulation of the upper incisors, then the spacing can be created at the time of surgery
Align and level mandibular arch. Try to shift the dental midline a little bit toward the right side a millimeter or so. Strip lower anterior teeth removing at least 2 mm of tooth structure. Try to upright the molar teeth. Increase angulation of lower incisors by tipping the incisal edges forward as the crowding is corrected

Surgery
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to expand, move the posterior aspect superiorly, and advance about 3 to 4 mm
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a clockwise direction
AP reduction genioplasty
Bilateral partial inferior turbinectomies

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

original_note:
ELISA SOLOMON									07/07/2008

Elisa is 22 years old.  For the most part, she is in good health.  She has had cataract surgery in her left eye and has about 85% loss of vision in that eye.  She has had her wisdom teeth removed.  Primary concerns that Lisa has includes the following. 

Pain throughout the face, jaws, neck, back, and head that is constant.  
Difficulties eating and chewing.
Headaches that are fairly constant.

Elisa believes that her jaw problems began when she was about 14 years old in 1999 of unknown etiology.  Her jaws started to click and pop at that time, but she did not have the headaches then.  Her headaches began when she was probably about 17 or 18 years of age.  More recently, in the last several months, they have gotten progressively worse to where they are constant. 

She takes the following medications:

Allegra.
Cyclessa (BCP).
Trazodone 25 mg q.h.s.
Ibuprofen, Tylenol, and Aleve for pain control. 

She has no problems with earaches or tinnitus, but gets occasional vertigo.  The only other joint areas that bother her could be her neck and her back, although she cannot differentiate whether it is in the bone structures or in the muscles only.  She is aware of clenching and bruxing at night time and also during the day.  She rates her headaches at 8 and they are constant through the entire head area.  She rates her TMJ pain at 7, average daily pain at 9, jaw function at 6, diet at 7,  and disability at 5.  

The previous treatments that Lisa has had include the following:

Retainers prior to orthodontics to align and rotate some teeth.
1996 through 1999 orthodontic treatment with retainers following.
She had splint therapy x2 from 2001 and she continues with a splint through 2008.
She had physical therapy from 2007 to 2008.
She has had medications tried.  Elavil was tried, but it gave her tachycardia.  She currently takes trazodone for sleep and to help decrease clenching. 

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Elisa has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 20 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 2.5 to 3 mm.  The maxillary, mandibular, and chin midline are pretty much aligned on the facial midline.  In profile view, Elisa has slight deficiency in the maxillary projection of probably about 3 mm or so.  Chin appears retruded by about 10-12 mm.  Chin neckline is short, although the chin neck angle is fairly well defined.  She has some fullness in the submental area.  Intraorally, Elisa has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars.  Teeth are fairly well aligned and level with just some mild crowding in the lower anterior arch.  She has good attached gingiva.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  She has small upper lateral incisors and one on the left side is pegged shaped.  She has a slight Class II end-on cuspid relationship being a little bit more Class II on the left side than right side.  She is probably 2 to 3 mm Class III.  She has about a 1-mm overbite relationship.  Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively normal.  The tonsils look normal.  The turbinates are moderate in size and create some nasal airway obstruction.  To palpation, she has some moderate pain over the TMJs, but minor pain over the masseters, SCMs, shoulders.  On opening, the left joint functions relatively smooth, but on the right side she has a pop at about 35 mm of opening and closing at about 30 mm.  Maximal opening is 50 mm, but opening without significant discomfort to only about 14 mm.  Excursion movements are 12 mm in each direction.
Elisa has an MRI report from an MRI taken July 13, 2007, that shows that she has a disc displaced in the left TMJ anteriorly that reduces with mouth opening.  On the right side, the disc is anteriorly displaced and also is reported to reduce.  Her films are not available at this time. 
RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars.  She has a pegged left lateral incisors.  Sinuses appear relatively clear.  The left condyle and right condyle may have some arthritic changes associated with them.  
Waters view shows that the sinuses are relatively clear.  Nasal septum in the bony area is relatively straight.  The turbinates are moderately enlarged with greater obstruction of the airway on the left side and just slightly better airway on the right side.  
Left TMJ tomogram had been taken in centric relation as the condyle is postured down and forward in the fossa.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  
Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that also appears to postured down and forward slightly.  The posterior teeth do not appear to be together.  Articular eminence is moderate in steepness.  The patient may not have been in centric relation when the closed tomograms were taken.  On opening, there is restricted translation in both joints.      
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that she is postured forward.  
Basic diagnoses would be as follows:
Bilateral TMJ and lessened internal condylar resorption.
AP mandibular deficiency. 
AP maxillary deficiency. 
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency. 
Class II end-on occlusion. 
High occlusal plane angle.  
Decreased oropharyngeal airway.
TMJ pain. 
Headaches.
Myofascial pain. 
Left maxillary pegged lateral incisor. 





Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Presurgical orthodontics.  
Align and level maxillary arch.  Open approximately 2 mm of space around the maxillary left lateral incisor and 1 mm of space between the maxillary right lateral incisor and cuspid.  
Align and level mandibular arch.  
Surgery.
TMJ reconstruction depending on MRI findings:  
Option 1.  Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek 
anchor.  
Option 2.  Bilateral TMJ reconstruction with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses and mandibular advancement.  
Mandibular advancement with sagittal split osteotomies for option 1 and total joint prostheses for option 2.  
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla in a counterclockwise direction.  
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts if TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses are used.  
Possible bilateral coronoplasties if total joint prostheses are used. 
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
Restorative dentistry to build up the maxillary lateral incisors with bonding, veneers, or crowns.

original_note:
B317 
B317 is 52 years old, referred by Dr. __. B317 has had problems with kidney stones in the past. Otherwise, B317 is in good health with no major medical problems. B317 's problems started she believes probably around 5 to 6 years ago of unknown etiology. She does report being in a very severe motor vehicle accident around 23 years ago or 22 years ago where she received facial trauma and her front teeth were knocked out and she was also unconscious and was almost left for dead until someone who knew her drove by, identified the car, saw her laying in a puddle of water, turned her over and found out she was still alive. She has had no treatment for her current condition except for extensive dental work. She takes no medications except vitamins and calcium.

PRIMARY CONCERNS
Difficulties eating and chewing.
Joint noises.
Pain in the jaw, face and neck area. Headaches.
Pain in the mornings when she wakes up.
Bite is off.
Mouth breather day and night. Jaws getting very tired.
Severe drooling problems.

She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus or hearing loss but does get occasional vertigo. No other joints bother her. She does have allergies to wool and cats. She is not aware of any problems wearing jewelry. She is aware that she probably does clench at night. She rates her headaches at a level of 4 in the temporal and posterior head area that occur about once a week. She gets what she classifies as mild headaches about 3 times a week that she wakes up in the morning which is indicative of nocturnal clenching. She rates her TMJ pain at 3, average daily pain at 4, jaw function at 5, diet at 4 and disability at 2. B317 does feel that her bite has gotten worse during the past 5 to 6 years.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
In frontal view, B317 has fairly good left to right symmetry in reference to upper and lower jaw. Upper lip length is about 22 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is 6 mm. Maxillary dental midline is 1 mm off to the left. Mandibular dental midline is 1 mm off to the right. The chin is off to the right probably about 2 to 3 mm. The nasal tip is off to the right side by about 3 mm. In the profile view, the maxilla is probably 6 to 8 mm AP retruded. She is about 20 to 25 mm AP deficient. Chin-neckline is almost nonexistent. The genial tubercles are probably only about 1 cm to 1.5 cm in front of the trachea. B317 does have a head postured forward neck position. The lower third face is elongated. She has lip incompetence of approximately 10 mm. Intraorally, B317 has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars present. She has a crown on the left first and second molars. She has significant crowding in the lower anterior arch. Left cuspid is about 3 mm anterior to the right cuspid. In the upper arch, she has extensive bridge work which will be identified in the Panorex representing all of the other teeth back through the second molars. B317 

She has a full Class II cuspid relationship on the right side and a Class I cuspid on the left side. She has an anterior overbite of 2 mm. She has an overjet of about 2 mm. Upper bridgework in the front shows no crowns transversely but also they are thick. Maxillary mandibular arches are narrow transversely. The uvula, soft palate and faucial pillars all look relatively healthy and the tissues look healthy. The faucial pillars are moderately constricted but not too significant. B317 has narrow nostrils. The right nostril is a little bit more narrow than the left side. Intranasally, the turbinates are moderately enlarged. The septum deviates at the superior aspect a little bit off to the right side but significantly off to the left side. There appears to be some decreased airway there. The luminal valves are quite constricted. Holding the luminal valves open with a nasal scope significantly improved her abilities to breathe through her nose. To palpation, B317 has some mild tenderness over the masseter muscles, styloid areas and mild tenderness over the TMJs. On opening, she does have fairly good mobility but does have some crepitus sounds in the joints. Maximal incisal opening is 47 mm. Excursive movements are about 10 mm in each direction. There is some significant crepitus on her jaw movements.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of all 4 third molars. She is also missing maxillary left central and lateral incisors. She has lost alveolar bone in that maxillary area. She has root canals on maxillary left central incisor, first bicuspid, first and second molars. Sinuses look relatively clear. She also appears to have a crown on the mandibular left second bicuspid. The condyles appear severely arthritic. The sinuses dip way down around the roots particularly on the left side slightly more than on the right side.

Waters view shows severe deviation and enlarged nasal spur extending off the left side of the septum. Turbinates appear moderately enlarged. The nasal airway appears to have fairly significant occlusion present. The sinuses otherwise appear fairly clear.

Left TMJ tomogram shows severe arthritis present with sclerosis on the head of the condyle. Decreased superior joint space. Posterior joint space looks relatively normal. Articular eminence is moderately steep. There appears to be increased joint space anteriorly. The condyle appears small compared to the size of the fossa.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears significantly arthritic and appears to actually be shorter in vertical height as compared to the left side. The condyle is small in size particularly compared to the size of the fossa. There could be some calcification sitting posterior to the condylar head as there appears to be some osteophyte structure there extending into the fossa area and in close approximation to the posterior head of the condyle. Articular eminence is moderately steep. The condyle appears small compared to the size of the fossa. She has a high mandibular plane angle, occlusal plane angle shape. On opening, there is restricted translation of the condyles where the condyles come down towards the articular eminence but not quite completely underneath.

B317 
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that B317 has a retruded maxilla and severely retruded mandible. Skeletal Class II relationship is present with a significantly high occlusal plane angle. Oropharyngeal airway is constricted with an airway dimension at the soft palate level about 3 mm and at the base of the tongue it is difficult to say because there may have been some movement there but may be around 5 mm. She demonstrates significant lip incompetence. She has a slightly reverse curve in the cervical vertebrae. Some of the cervical joint space in C6 appears to be decreased in vertical dimensions compared to the vertebrae further superior.

BASIC DIAGNOSES
Bilateral TMJ severe arthritis and articular dislocation with severe deformation. Severe AP mandibular deficiency.
AP maxillary deficiency. Anterior vertical maxillary excess.
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
Class II cuspid relationship on the right side but Class I on the left.
Steep occlusal plane angle. Decreased oropharyngeal airway of 3 to 4 mm (normal 11 mm.)
Small constricted nostrils. Hypertrophied turbinates.
Deviated nasal septum with a large septal spur extending into the left side.
Nasal airway obstruction.
Missing maxillary right central and lateral incisors replaced with a bridge.
Excessively long vertical crown length on the incisors.
Significant tooth size discrepancy but if occlusion remains in a Class II position on the right side, Class I on the left it would not be a significant treatment factor.
Moderate crowding of lower anterior arch.
Headaches.
TMJ pain.
Myofascial pain.
Nocturnal clenching.
Predominant mouth breather.
Masticatory dysfunction.
Nasal asymmetry with the tip deviated about 3 to 4 mm off to the right side (the patient is not interested in having this corrected at this time.)
Sleep apnea symptoms. Lower arch asymmetry with the left cuspid being approximately 4 mm anterior to the right cuspid. B317 

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT
Presurgical orthodontics. Align and level maxillary arch.
Align and level mandibular arch. Strip the lower anterior teeth removing 2 to 3 mm of tooth structure to keep from flaring them forward. Dental preparation of decreasing the vertical height of the clinical crowns on the 4 incisors to meet the post-treatment specifications for vertical crown length.
CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol.)
Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ reconstruction and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla approximately 8 to 10 mm and down graft the posterior aspect down approximately 4 mm to level the occlusal plane angle.
Bilateral TMJ coronoidectomies.
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest from the abdomen.)
Partial turbinectomies.
Nasoseptoplasty.
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
Periodontal treatment to reconstruct the alveolar ridge, graft the maxillary right cuspid, and place osseointegrated implants. Dental reconstruction to rebuild the arches and finalize the occlusion.

B317  MRI Evaluation
Age: 52
Date of Acquisition: 
Date of Interpretation: 
Left TMJ coronal view shows significant loss of condylar volume. The condyle is quite narrow mediolaterally and a tremendous vertical decrease in dimension. There is some discal tissue medially and anterior to the remaining condyle. The lateral rim of the fossa is extremely flat.

Right TMJ likewise shows a condyle that is extremely narrow mediolaterally and has lost significant vertical dimension. The lateral rim of the fossa has minimal downward curvature but just slightly more than the opposite side. There appears to be discal tissue medial and anterior to the condyle. There is decreased signal to both condyles.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has lost volume but it actually looks better in the sagittal view as compared to the coronal view. There is significantly decreased joint space superiorly although the posterior joint space is okay. Articular eminence is quite steep. The condyle appears small compared to the size of the fossa. Articular disc is anteriorly displaced sitting beneath the articular eminence and extremely short anteroposteriorly. The disc has a little bit better confirmation towards the medial side of the joint. Towards the lateral aspect of the joint it is more difficult to see the disc. This may be indicative of anteromedial displacement. A little bit of fluid in the superior joint space beneath the articular eminence. On opening, there is good translation of the condyle but the disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction.

Right TMJ sagittal view likewise shows a condyle that appears a little bit small compared to the size of the fossa. Articular eminence is fairly steep. Articular disc is anteriorly displaced and sitting beneath the articular eminence. There is decreased signal in the condylar head. Disc is significantly deformed. The condyle appears significantly degenerated. On opening, there is good translation of the condyle but the articular disc remains anteriorly displaced without reduction.

IMPRESSION: Bilateral TMJ severe arthritis and articular disc dislocations without reductions, significant deformation of the articular discs.
Questionable salvageability of the articular discs.

original_note:
TERRELL HORTON									05/16/2006

Terry is 47 years old, referred by Dr. Al Bishop.  He had back surgery about 37 years ago.  He currently weighs 265 pounds and is 6 feet tall. 

PRIMARY PROBLEMS:
Severe snoring.
Severe daytime somnolence.
Severe sleep apnea. 
Prominently mouth breather.
Difficulty breathing through the nose.

In frontal view, Terry has fairly good left to right facial asymmetry.  Upper lip length is about 28 mm.  Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about –1.  Maxillary midline is pretty much aligned with the facial midline.  Mandibular dental midline is shifted about 2 mm off to the right.  Mandibular dental midline and chin are fairly well aligned with each other.  Terry is slight Class II cuspid on the right side by probably about 2 mm and Class I on the left side.  He has about 1 to 2 mm vertical overbite.  Terry has no problems with earaches, tinnitus or hearing loss.  He gets occasional vertigo.  No tenderness around the head and neck area to palpation.  No headaches.  No TMJ pain.  He rates jaw function, diet and disability all at 0.  Incisal opening is 51 mm.  Excursive movement is about 7 mm in each direction.  On opening, he has a loud audibile pop at about 40 mm on the right side and closing click probably at 30 mm.  The left side seems to function relatively smoothly.  In profile view, Terry has some AP maxillary deficiency of probably a good 6 to 8 mm.  The mandible is deficient by about 15 to 20 mm in AP direction.  Chin neckline is non-definable.  Intraorally, the oropharynx appears to have healthy tissues.  The uvula is mildly enlarged.  The soft palate is probably moderately elongated.  Faucial pillars appear relatively normal.  Intranasally, the septum is deviated off towards the left side but there is a spur off to the right side.  Turbinates are moderately enlarged.  Terry is missing right bicuspid tooth.  He also had the left first bicuspid extracted as well for orthodontic reasons when he was much younger.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION:
Panorex shows the presence of an impacted mandibular right third molar.  The other third molars are not present.  X-rays are a little bit cloudy.  The sinuses look clear.  It is difficult to evaluate the TMJs in this view.

Waters view shows that he has incredible deviation of the bony nasal septum.  He appears to have a lot of nasal airway obstruction.  The sinuses look relatively clear.  

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is posteriorly positioned in the fossa.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  There are some mild arthritic changes at the head of the condyle.  

Left TMJ tomogram is a little difficult to evaluate but it appears there is a more balanced joint space although there is some flattening on the posterior head and anterior head of the condyle.  On opening, there is good translation of both joints.

TERRELL HORTON				05/16/2006				Page 2


Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that the maxilla and mandible are retruded.  He has a severely decreased oropharyngeal airway of probably only about 2 mm at the soft palate level and down at the base of the tongue is probably around 5 to 6 mm.  There is a high occlusal plane angle present.  

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
AP maxillary deficiency.
AP mandibular deficiency.
Right TMJ articular disc dislocation with reduction.
Severely decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Hypertrophied turbinates. 
Severe nasal septal deviation. 
Nasal airway obstruction. 
Missing maxillary first bicuspids.
Impacted mandibular right third molar.
Severe snoring.
Severe daytime somnolence. 
Severe sleep apnea. 
Hypertrophied soft palate. 
Hypertrophied uvula. 

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
MRI of left and right TMJs. 
Presurgical orthodontics.
Align and level maxillary arch.
Align and level mandibular arch.
Surgery.
Right TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor.
Maxillary advancement and down graft off the posterior aspect. 
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomy to advance the mandible approximately 
15 mm.  
Uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (UPPP.)
Bilateral partial turbinectomies. 
Nasoseptoplasty. 
Surgical removal of impacted mandibular right third molar. 
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
