original_note:
B326 
B326 is 42 years old and referred by Dr. __.
Patient History
Except for TMJ problems, B326 is in good health. She does have an allergy to mosquitos. Blood pressure runs a little bit low.

B326 had previous orthognathic surgery on which included a LeFort I maxillary osteotomy to move the maxilla superiorly and bilateral mandibular ramus sagittal split osteotomies to advance the mandible, and she also had a sliding genioplasty. This was performed by Dr. __. On , she had reconstructive surgery to include the nose, eyelids, and implants under the eyes. On , she had bilateral cheek suspensions, removal of buccal fat pads, submental plication with neck suspension, and insertion of a specialized chin implant.

B326's TMJ problems began when she was in about the third or fourth grade of unknown etiology. She noticed a significant difference in the position of her lower jaw, that it was becoming more retruded. This continued on for quite some time. When she was 17, she was an ice skater and had a bad fall, and she hit the back of her head and had a concussion. She can't remember if this aggravated her jaw joint problems. On , she had Le Fort maxillary osteotomy and mandibular sagittal split osteotomies and chin augmentation by Dr. __. Prior to that, she began orthodontics 5 years ago, and the orthodontics was completed 2 years ago. Since the orthognathic surgery, her TMJ problems have gotten significantly worse. Initially she wasn't aware of any increased TMJ problems, and over time, however, her TMJ problems have gotten significantly worse. For a while after surgery she could function much better than she currently can.

She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, nor hearing loss. She gets occasional vertigo. No other joints bother her. She does have an allergy to mosquitos, but she also has an allergy to NICKEL and cannot wear any type of cheap jewelry. She is aware of nighttime and daytime clenching and bruxism. She gets headaches about once a month that occur in the temporal area that are probably related to the TMJ, and she rates them at a level of 8. She has constant TMJ pain at about a level of 3, but it can exacerbate to a level of 10. Overall pain is about a level of 3 on an average day. Jaw pain, diet, and disability are all at a level of 7. From a disability standpoint, there are times where she cannot work at all since she can can't talk because of the pain problems. Last year, she had a severe attack where she just fell on the floor in severe pain, and she was taken to Dr. __'s office, and he injected some local anesthetic and perhaps some steroid into the left TMJ, and this gave her some temporary relief for a couple of hours.

Current Medications
Orthocept
Vioxx 25 mg q.d.
Hydrocodone 7.5/650 mg p.r.n. Glucosamine/chondroitin
Multivitamins and extra vitamin E and vitamin C
Patient Concerns

TMJ pain
Jaws feel fatigued frequently
Face feels as though it is frozen
Difficult eating and chewing
Decreased mouth opening
Periodic headaches
Clinical Examination

In frontal view, B326 has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 23 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 1 mm. Maxillary dental midline is pretty much on the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline is shifted to the right about a millimeter. Chin is shifted to the right probably 2 to 3 mm.

In profile view, she does have some AP maxillary deficiency of probably about 4 mm. Chin appears to be AP deficient by probably 12 to 15 mm. Chin-neckline and chin-neck angle are fairly well-defined. She has a high mandibular plane angle facial morphology. Has a slight obtuse nasolabial angle. Intraorally, she is missing the lower first bicuspids extracted for orthodontic reasons most likely. Third molars also appear to be absent. She appears to have adequate attached gingiva around the lower anterior teeth. May have a slight accentuated curve of occlusion in the lower arch. In the upper arch, she is also missing the first bicuspids. Appears to have a slight reverse curve of occlusion in the upper arch. Her teeth appear to be slightly vertical in orientation. Appears to occlude more on the left side than the right side with the right side being open just slightly extending from the lateral incisor back through and including the first molar. Second molar may be in occlusion. Has a slight Class II end-on occlusion on the right side but a little bit closer to Class I on the left side. Has an anterior deep-bite of about 2 mm. Right maxilla appears to be up 1 to 2 mm compared to the left side. On re-evaluation, compared to the ears and eyes, maxillary midline is a little bit off to the right side probably about a millimeter. Nasal tip is also just slightly off to the right side 1 to 2 mm. Oropharynx appears clear. Appears to be decreased distance between the soft palpate and the posterior pharyngeal wall. There is a history of snoring that has gotten more obvious in the last six months.

Intranasally, the septum may be deviated a little bit off toward the right side. There appears to be a spur off the right side. Right turbinate is in direct contact with the septum, and there could be a little synechia joining the septum to the right turbinate. Left turbinate is quite enlarged, but the airway is better on that side since the septum is deviated off toward the right side.

Maximal incisal opening is 26 mm but only about 18 mm with reasonable comfort. Excursive movements are 5 mm in each direction. To palpation, she has severe pain at a level of 10 over the temporalis area. Moderate pain at a level of 5 over the masseters and 6 over the TMJs. She has minimal discomfort to palpation over the styloid area. She does have some numbness in her chin area, but her lower lip is fine, and the other facial structures are fine.

Radiographic Evaluation
Panorex shows the presence of four bone plates in the maxilla with four screws in each of the L plates anteriorly, and it appears that there are four screws in the posterior plate on the right side but just three screws on the left side. She has interosseous wires in the mandible. Difficult to tell, but there could be a little cystic area in the left mandibular third molar area. There is a bone plate in the chin area. It appears that it might be cut. Condyles appear severely degenerated.

Waters view shows that the sinuses appear relatively clear, although the right one may be just a little bit cloudy compared to the left one. Other sinuses appear clear.

Right TMJ tomogram shows condyles that have undergone severe resorption and are severely degenerated. Does not appear to be any significant joint space vertically. Slightly increased posterior joint space. Interosseous wires are seen in the mandible. Sigmoid notch appears to be very close to the articular eminence.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that also has undergone severe condylar resorption. Severely decreased vertical joint space. Articular eminence appears to almost touch the sigmoid notch area. Condyle is very narrow anteroposteriorly.

On opening, there is restrictive translation of both condyles with neither one clearing the articular eminence. Significant condylar resorption is again appreciated in the open views bilaterally.

Cephalometrically, B326 has what appears to be AP maxillary deficiency and AP mandibular deficiency. Upper incisors are in significant vertical inclination. Significantly decreased oropharyngeal airway with the airway at the base of the tongue only being about 3 mm and at the soft palate level may be only about 2 or 3 mm. She has a very high occlusal plane angulation. Apparently there is some kind of chin implant present but it appears to extend below the chin and is some kind of alloplastic material. Cannot discern the thickness of it. B326 does have a significant sleep disorder now. She just doesn't sleep well at all, she is very restless. Whether that is due strictly to the pain problems or the fact that she has a severely decreased oropharyngeal airway is unknown at this time. She has a lot of daytime somnolence.

Basic Diagnoses
Bilateral TMJ severe arthritis and articular disc dislocations with severe deformation and nonreducing
AP mandibular deficiency
AP maxillary deficiency
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency
Class II end-on occlusion on the right, Class I on the left
Open-bite tendency on the right side extending from the right lateral incisors back through and including the first molars
Missing maxillary and mandibular first bicuspids (removed for orthodontic reasons)
Vertical inclination of maxillary incisors
High occlusal plane angle of 21 degrees (normal 8 degrees)
High mandibular plane angle
Severely decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of 3 mm (normal 11 mm)
Severe TMJ pain
Severe myofascial pain
Headaches Nasal septal spur on the right side
Hypertrophied right inferior turbinate with probable synechiae attaching it to the septum
Right nasal airway obstruction
Chin implant
Tooth-size discrepancy with a 's difference of approximately 2 mm
Recommended Treatment

Presurgical orthodontics
Align and level maxillary arch. Open up a millimeter of space around each of the upper lateral incisors to correct for the tooth-size discrepancy.
Align and level mandibular arch.

CT scan of the TMJs and jaws (TMJ Concepts protocol)
Surgery
Bilateral TMJ condylectomies, reconstruction, and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvest graft from the abdomen)
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance and downgraft the posterior aspect with bone plate stabilization and hydroxyapatite grafting
Bilateral coronoidectomies
Remove septal spur and excise synechiae
Partial right inferior turbinectomy

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion
Restorative dentistry to bond the maxillary lateral incisors to eliminate the spacing
B326  MRI Evaluation

Date of Acquisition: Date of :
Left TMJ, coronal view, shows a condyle that is extremely small and appears to have undergone significant condylar resorption. Appears that part of the articulating area is severely resorbed with a small amount of articulation. Difficult to tell if on the medial side there is discal tissue displaced or whether there is exophytic bone formation off the medial aspect of the condyle. Lateral rim of the fossa is extremely shallow. Decreased vertical joint space. Appears to be an osteophyte extending out laterally from the condylar head. Bone in the fossa appears to be intact. Appears to be discal tissue anterior to the condyle.

Right TMJ, coronal view, shows a condyle that is narrow mediolaterally but has a slightly greater width than the opposite side and appears to have a little bit better morphology, although there is significant evidence of condylar resorption. Severely decreased joint space vertically. Lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly slightly perhaps 1 to 2 mm. Appears that there is discal tissue anterior to the condyle. Cortical bone in the fossa appears to be grossly intact.

Left TMJ, sagittal view, shows a condyle that is quite small anteroposteriorly. The condylar head and neck appear to be severely sclerotic. Appears to be some decreased vertical joint space. Articular disc is anteriorly positioned sitting beneath the articular eminence. Disc appears somewhat deformed but could be enough substance there to reposition it. Articular eminence is moderately steep and fairly long in length. There is some fluid in the superior joint space at the posterior aspect of the superior joint space. Could be a little bit of inflammatory process in the bilaminar tissue. Appears to be some restriction in translation. Disc appears to be nonreducing. Appears there may be some translation of the disc but not too significant indicative of perhaps adhesions present of the disc to the articular eminence.

Right TMJ, sagittal view, shows a condyle that is small in size. Decreased vertical joint space. Condylar head and neck appear quite sclerotic although not quite to the extent of the left side. Articular disc is anteriorly displaced and sitting beneath the articular eminence. The disc is quite short anteroposteriorly. Articular eminence is moderately steep, although not quite as steep as the opposite side. A little bit of fluid in the posterior superior joint space. Fossa appears grossly intact. Out laterally, the articular disc is extremely short anteroposteriorly and thickened. This disc appears less salvageable than the opposite side.

On opening, there is restricted translation, and the articular disc appears to remain anteriorly displaced without reduction.
Impression:
Right TMJ severe condylar resorption with sclerosis and articular disc dislocation without reduction with degenerative changes present
Left TMJ severe arthritis and sclerosis with the articular disc anteriorly displaced without reduction and with significant deformation (right side greater than the left side)
Questionable salvageability of both discs, although the left one appears to have greater salvageability than the right side

original_note:
JOHN KIMBROUGH									     02-01-05

John is 23 years old and referred by James Compton, DDS, MSD, 4610 N. Garfield Street, Ste. A-9, Midland, TX  79705.

Patient History

John takes the following medications:
Adderall 20 mg x 4 daily
Xanax 1 mg 5 times per day
Valtrex 1 g once a day

John developed stomach problems when he was on Celebrex. He will sometimes have problems with diarrhea but attributes that mainly to stress factors. John has an anxiety disorder with panic attacks. John doesn’t have any problems with the panic attacks as long as he is on his medications. Stress seems to have set those problems off. He has had about three fainting spells in his life, and the last one was about a year ago. It may be related in part to the panic attacks. John has some allergy problems that may in part be due to seasonal factors. He does have difficulties at times breathing through his nose. John has been diagnosed as  having ADHD/anxiety disorder. He previously has had a frenectomy. John rates the headaches that he has gotten particularly during the last two weeks in the frontal and temporal area at about a level of 2. Doesn’t report any pain directly in the TMJ area. Rates his average daily pain at around a level of 5, jaw function at 4, diet at 0, and disability at 4. Says that his lips feel numb essentially all the time. He had his wisdom teeth removed at the age of 16 and did not have numbness to his lips after that. He had orthodontics done from the ages of 11 to 13. Just opening his jaw and manipulating it a certain way, he had a very loud, audible pop in the right TMJ. John does not know when the onset of his problems occurred particularly in reference to clenching and grinding. He has done that as long as he can remember. This etiology is currently unknown.

No other joints bother him except he does get some tendinitis in his thumbs. He is very much aware of clenching and bruxing at night and during the day and would rate this as being a severe problem. He drinks about three to four Cokes per day. 

John has been on the Adderall for approximately three years. Has been on the Xanax about the same amount of time. He has been on Valtrex for about one year. 

John said that when he was skiing this past winter that he got fairly significant pain on the right side of his jaw through the joint and down through his jaw area. He thought it might be related to the change in altitude and the coldness. John feels that he has been clenching his teeth ever since he had his braces removed. He said also he used to be able to click and pop his right TMJ at will, and now it is more difficult to do so. He says that he can feel sometimes when he wakes up in the morning that his teeth have moved which is indicative of probably significant nocturnal clenching. Looking at his teeth, it appears that it is more of a clenching problem than a bruxing problem because the anatomy of his teeth is still well-defined, and there are really no significant wear facets. 

Patient Concerns

Significant shifting and movement of his teeth with the upper anterior teeth becoming extremely flared and the lower anterior teeth becoming quite crowded
Headaches
Pain through the jaw areas
Clenching and grinding of the teeth at night and during the day

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, John has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 22 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about -4 mm. Maxillary and mandibular midlines are pretty much on the facial midline as is the chin.

In profile view, John has excessive fullness in the upper lip and has a C-shaped curve to the upper lip with protrusion of upper lip supralabrale being about 8 to 9 mm anterior to subnasale. Has a prominent labiomental fold. Has fullness in the submental area. Chin-neckline is relatively long. 

Intraorally, John has all of his lower teeth back through the second molars. He has some significant crowding, lower anterior arch. John reports he is getting a little gingival retraction from around the lower teeth, and he has had some ulcerations on his gingiva down in that area. In the upper arch, John has all of his teeth back through the second molars. He has severe flaring of the upper anterior teeth. No spaces have developed between the teeth, however. Has just a slight Class II tendency on the right side by probably a couple of millimeters. Is getting a little bit of buccal flaring of the maxillary cuspids and lingual inclination of the lower cuspids. Has an anterior deep bite of only about 3 to 4 mm. Uvula appears a little bit enlarged and elongated. Soft palate looks reasonably normal or perhaps just slightly long. Tonsils are present but appear normal in size. Faucial pillars are maybe mildly constricted but not too significantly. 

Intranasally, turbinates have mild enlargement but not significantly. Septum is deviated off to the left, but he appears to have a reasonable airway bilaterally. Do not feel that he has any significant nasal airway obstruction at this time. Columella and luminal valves all look normal. John states that he has been told he does snore moderately. He has some mild daytime somnolence but is not aware of any problems with apneic episodes when sleeping.

To palpation, he has moderate discomfort of the masseter muscles and milder tenderness over the temporalis, SCMs, and posterior neck area. Although he did have one loud audible pop in the right TMJ earlier, when I examined him now, his joints seemed to function relatively smoothly. Maximal incisal opening is about 52 mm. Excursive movements are 6 mm in each direction. 

Previous records sent by Dr. Compton include some photographs from 11/21/96 showing completion of the orthodontics. The x-rays show that he has fairly good Class I cuspid-molar relationship although a little bit Class III cuspid relationship on the left side. Midlines are well-aligned, and there appears to be normal inclination of the maxillary and mandibular incisors without any evidence of flaring of the uppers and no evidence of crowding of the lowers. There are photographs from 09/29/94 showing that there wasn’t any particular crowding but a little bit of an anterior deep bite relationship of probably 4 to 5 mm, but the teeth appear to be in good angulation of both the upper and lower arch. Lateral cephalogram from the same date shows that both the upper and lower incisors have relatively normal inclinations and that John actually has an excessive upper tooth-to-lip relationship of probably about 4 to 5 mm and Class II end-on occlusal appearing relationship for the most part and does have an increased anterior vertical height of the mandible, although there is increased soft tissue thickness in that area. 

Panorex from the same date shows all the teeth are present, although the third molars are developing the crowns and are unerupted. At the time that these records were taken, John was 13.2 years old. There is a Panorex taken on 11/21/96 showing that all four third molars are present and the crowns are developing. The rest of the teeth appear to be well-aligned. Sinuses appear clear, and turbinates appear moderate in size with a good nasal airway. 

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows the sinuses probably are reasonably clear. All four third molars are absent. Anterior mandibular dental height appears short vertically. Upper incisor roots fade indicative of overangulation. Right condyle appears a little bit wide anteroposteriorly with maybe some slight anterior beaking present. Left condyle appears a little bit more normal. 

Waters view shows that the sinuses are clear, and the nasal airway is quite patent. Midlines appear to be on, and jaws appear relatively symmetric. Decreased vertical height in the anterior maxilla. 

Right TMJ tomogram appears to be fairly well-centered in the fossa with good joint space superiorly and posteriorly. Articular eminence is fairly steep and is moderately long in length. Condyle appears to have reasonable shape.

Left TMJ is not quite as clear. Condyle appears maybe a little bit broader anteroposteriorly. However, there does appear to be good joint space. Articular eminence does not appear to be quite as steep on this side as the opposite side. I would suspect that in both joints the discs may well be in adequate position.

On opening, there is restricted translation in both joints with the condyles coming down along the posterior aspect of the articular eminence but not reaching the inferior aspect of the articular eminence. 

Lateral cephalogram shows that there is significant vertical maxillary deficiency and vertical mandibular deficiency. There is severe overangulation of the maxillary incisors. Lower incisor angulation doesn’t appear to be too far out of the normal range. Oropharyngeal airway is quite adequate with the oropharyngeal airway at the soft palate level of about 11 mm and at the base of the tongue of about 17 mm.

Basic Diagnoses

Rule out TMJ articular disc dislocations
Severe mandibular hyperdystonia
Chronic severe clenching
Vertical maxillary hypoplasia
AP mandibular hyperplasia
Severe overangulation of maxillary incisors
Crowding, lower incisors
Slight Class II occlusal relationship by 1 to 2 mm
ADHD/anxiety disorder

Recommended Treatment

MRI of left and right TMJ

Medications to try to control and eliminate the mandibular hyperdystonia and severe clenching

Presurgical orthodontics 
Align and level maxillary arch. Try to upright central incisors some. This will likely deepen the bite more than it currently is. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies
Align and level mandibular arch

Surgery
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to upright the incisors and downgraft the maxilla
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to reposition the mandible into a proper facial balance
Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy to increase vertical height of the mandible
Consider possible TMJ surgery if articular discs are dislocated (this will be determined by MRI)

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

original_note:
TRACY SEESE									04/13/2009

Tracy is 18 years old, a self referral.  The only medication he takes is sulfa methoxazole TMT 1 tablet b.i.d.  Tracy’s problems began when he was 17 years old in 2008 and when he had his third molars extracted.  Following extraction of his third molars, Tracy began developing headaches, TMJ pain, pain through his jaws, and difficulty with jaw function.  A splint was made for him in 2008, but was of no help to him.  He has had no other treatment for his TMJ problems.  Tracy has had previous orthodontics with braces roughly from the age of 12 to 15.  

Tracy rates his headaches at a level of 5 that occur in the frontal, temporal, and posterior head area predominantly.  He rates his TMJ everyday pain to about a level of 10, average daily pain at 9, jaw function at 9, diet at 6, disability at 9.  He gets severe bilateral earaches, moderate tinnitus, and moderate vertigo.  Other joints that bother him include his knees and his spine.  He does have some problems with his feet and has to wear special shoes.   

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Tracy has good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 25 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 2 mm.  In profile view, Tracy has fairly good facial balance.  Intraorally, Tracy has all of his lower teeth present back to the second molars.  He has a 3 x 3 retainer in the lower arch.  There is a little bit of spacing between the cuspids and first bicuspids.  He has adequate attached gingiva.  In the upper arch, he has all of his teeth back through the second molars.  He has veneers on the central incisors.  He is basically a Class I cuspid-molar relationship.  He has an anterior deep bite of about 1.5 mm.  Oropharyngeal tissues all look normal.  Intranasally, turbinate on the left side looks a little bit enlarged, but on the right side it looks relatively normal.  Septum looks relatively normal.  Oropharyngeal tissues looks normal.  To palpation, he has severe pain in the retromandibular area right below the ears and in the TMJ area.  He has moderate pain to palpation in the temporalis muscles and the masseter muscles.  Joints seem to function reasonably smoothly, but he could periodically elicit an audible pop that can occur on both sides.  Maximal incisal opening is 66 mm.  Excursion movement to the right about 11 mm and to the left 12 mm.  He could generate periodic audible clicks in the jaw joint, but with routine movements the joints seem to function reasonably smoothly.  His occlusion actually fits together better than in the photographs that we took today. 

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Coronal view shows that he has moderately enlarged turbinates.  Sinuses appear clear.  The septum appears a little bit wide in some areas.   
Axial view shows that the turbinates are moderately enlarged.  There is a little septal spur off to the left side.  Sinuses appear clear.  
Panorex radiograph shows the absence of all 4 third molars and the presence of a 3 x 3 retainer.  Sinuses appear clear.  No other significant pathology is seen.  Condylar heads and necks appear elongated.  
Left TMJ sagittal views show a condyle with some slight anterior beaking and perhaps some slight flattening on the anterior head of the condyle.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  Joint space appears reasonably balanced.  
Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has some slight anterior beaking and a little bit of flattening on the head of the condyle.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  Joint space looks fairly well balanced.  
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with good mediolateral width.  Lateral rim of the fossa is fairly flat.  There is decreased lateral joint space.  
Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with good mediolateral width.  There is significantly decreased lateral joint space.  Lateral rim of the fossa in both fossas curves inferiorly about 1-2 mm.  

Basic diagnoses for Tracy would be as follows:    

Bilateral TMJ medially displaced articular discs. 
Mandibular hyperplasia. 
Severe TMJ pain. 
Severe headaches. 
Myofascial pain.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
  
Surgery:  
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors and arthroplasty.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies with bone screw stabilization.  
Application of maxillary and mandibular arch bars. 

I feel this will get us the best opportunity to eliminate his severe headache and pain problems.

original_note:
BRITTANY ROHLING								6/8/05

Brittany is 14 years old, referred by Dr. Peter Crump.  Brittany had a hernia repair at 7 months of age.  Brittany does have some problems occasionally with indigestion kind of symptoms with the feeling of her heart coming out of her chest.  It has happened recently a couple of times.  She is a big soccer player and one time it happened after she had some spicy food and another time after some greasy kind of foods and went out and had a pretty rigorous round of soccer when she experienced this.  I suggested that she check with her family physician to have that aspect evaluated.  Brittany has no problems with earaches, tinnitus or hearing loss.  She did get vertigo when she had her heart palpitation episodes.  No other joints bother her.  She is not aware of clenching or bruxism.  She is not aware of mouth breathing and feels she breathes predominantly through her nose when she is sleeping.  She takes no medications.  She first became aware of clicking or popping in her jaw joints about 2 years ago of unknown etiology.  She has been in and out of ortho treatment since about the age of 7.  She had a Mahra appliance that she wore for about 18 months but was ineffective for her.  She has been in four orthodontic appliances for approximately two years now.  She has no trouble with headaches, TMJ pain or pain around the head and neck area.  She rates her jaw function at 2, diet at 1, disability at 0.  

In frontal view, Brittany has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is 21 mm.   Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 4 mm.  Maxillary, mandibular and chin midline are all pretty much on the facial midline.  In profile view, she has fairly good balance in the upper lip/cheek area.  Upper lip is C-shaped and maybe just a little bit deficient by probably a couple of mm in the mid portion.  Chin is approximately 20 mm AP deficient.  Chin-neckline is extremely short although chin-neck angle is moderately well defined considering the amount of retrusion of the chin.  Brittany has lip incompetence of about 6 to 7 mm.  She has to really strain her lips to get them together and has significant hyperfunction of the mentalis muscles.  Intraorally, she has had lower first bicuspids extracted.  Other teeth are fairly well aligned.  She has good attached gingiva in the lower anterior arch.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  She has basically a full Class II cuspid relationship.  Slight cant in the occlusal plane with the left side being about 1 mm vertically shorter than the right side.  Oropharyngeal airway is clear.  The uvula may be slightly elongated.  Soft palate looks relatively normal as do the faucial pillars.  Essentially no vertical overbite relationship.  Intranasally, turbinates are moderately enlarged with the left side being larger than the right side.  A little bit decreased airway on the left side more so than the right side.  Septum may tip just a little bit over towards the left side.  To palpation, there is no tenderness around the head and neck area or over the TMJs.  However, on opening, there are some noises in the TMJs indicative of probably displaced disc that inconsistently reduce.  Maximal incisal opening is 43 mm.  Excursion movement to the right is 4 mm, left is 7 mm.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION: 
Panorex shows the presence of all 4 third molars that are unerupted.  The sinuses appear clear.  Turbinates appear moderate in size.  The condyles appear to have significant arthritic changes.  

Right TMJ tomograms show that she is not in centric relation and actually has her mandible postured forward.  The condyle appears to have fairly good shape but there is increased superior and posterior joint space indicative of not being in centric relation.  

BRITTANY ROHLING			6/8/05					Page 2


Left TMJ tomogram has a similar appearance.  The condyle appears a little bit smaller on this side compared to the opposite side.  Articular eminences on both sides are moderately steep with the left one being a little bit steeper than the right one.  Increased superior and posterior joint space.  On opening, there is fairly good translation of the condyles forward.  

Waters view shows that the left turbinate is quite large and appears to block most of the airway.  Sinuses are clear.  

Cephalometric radiograph shows that the maxilla and mandible are retruded.  There is a little bit of vertical excess present in the maxilla.  Head is tipped up a little bit.  Oropharyngeal airway measures about 6 mm at the soft palate level and 4 to 5 mm at the base of the tongue.  Lateral incisors may be just slightly over-angulated but not too bad.

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
Bilateral TMJ idiopathic condylar resorption and articular disc dislocation without reduction. 
Severe AP mandibular hypoplasia.  
Anterior vertical maxillary hyperplasia.
Class II occlusion.
Decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Impacted third molars x4.
Masticatory dysfunction. 
High-occlusal plane angle.
Missing mandibular first bicuspids.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Presurgical orthodontics.
Continue to align and level maxillary arch.
Continue to align and level mandibular arch.  Strip lower anterior teeth removing 2 mm of tooth structure and retract.  

Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction.
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the anterior aspect superiorly and advance it forward.
Surgical removal of impacted third molars x4.
HTR implant to anterior mandible.

3.  Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
DAVID STANLEY									09/15/2009

David is 55 years old and referred by Dr. Hilton Israelson.  In December 2008, David had all 4 of his third molars extracted.  Following surgery, he had significant change in sensation to the left side of his tongue.  The sensation to the left side of the tongue has improved since the initial occurrence.  David tends to chew food on his right side because he is afraid of biting his tongue on the left side.  He said food has a totally different feeling and sensation to him compared to before surgery.  He cannot taste as well as he could before the injury.  To sharp sensation, David actually had hypersensitivity in the left side of the tongue.  To dull stimulation, he could not feel quite as well on the left side as the right side.  David states that the left side of his tongue is more sensitive to warm foods or fluids as compared to the right side.  He has good tongue mobility with relatively symmetric movements.  To palpation, along the inner surface of the mandible and adjacent to the internal oblique ridge which is where I believe his nerve is located, it does cause some pain when I palpate the area.  It does not particularly cause any electrical shocks to the tongue.  

I discussed with David the findings that he has had a nerve injury that now is causing some hypersensitivity and decreased taste sensibility.  However, I did explain to him to attempt a nerve repair could render his situation significantly worse than it currently is.  If a nerve exploration and repair would be done, he would almost be sure to lose any chance of regaining taste sensation.  He could have significantly worsened sensation and pain.  I did tell him that the lingual nerve has a poor prognosis than almost any other nerve in the body in reference to repair prognosis.  I answered all the questions he had at this time.  I strongly suggested that he wait and give a little more time to see if better improvement will occur.  I suggested he return to see me in about 6 months. 

Basic diagnosis for David would be neuropraxia of the left lingual nerve.  

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Continue to monitor progression of return of sensation. 
Consider surgery only if symptoms become significantly worse and unbearable. 
Consider medications if the injured nerve becomes significantly hypersensitive and/or painful.

LINDA WARD								07/21/2011

info@bellairefaces.comLinda is 58 years old, birth date 01/27/1953.  Referred by Phillip Iero, MD, DDS, 6800 West Loop South, Suite 350, Bellaire, TX 77401, phone #713-665-9200.  E-mail is info@bellairefaces.com.  

Linda’s TMJ problems began as a teenager when she would have problems with the jaw getting locked open of unknown etiology.  In about 1978 or 1979, she was a victim of spousal abuse and was hit on the left side of the jaw and she had developed significant problems after that episode.  She has had the following surgical treatments:

In 1986, left TMJ Proplast Teflon interpositional implant.
In 1986 and 1987, she had numerous debridements x10.  
She developed osteomyelitis and in 1988 was treated surgically for the left TMJ osteomyelitis including a condylectomy and resection of the masseter muscle. 
In 1988, a free flap was taken from the right upper arm to graft muscle into the left jaw.  
In 1989, the left TMJ was reconstructed with a Christensen total joint prosthesis with an acrylic head.  
In 1994, the right TMJ was reconstructed with a Christensen prosthesis probably metal on metal, but the screws loosened and the prosthesis failed.  

These first procedures were all performed by Dr. Dan Caspar in Galesburg, IL.  

7.   In 1996, she had the right TMJ prosthesis removed and a new Christensen prosthesis placed with a metal-on-metal articulation by Dr. Halfrick in Houston.  

Linda’s primary concerns include the following:

Pain in left TMJ increasing in severity over the past 2 years.
Severe left jaw bone pain.
Left-sided nerve pain.
Muscle tenseness and spasms.

Linda currently takes the following medications:

Norvasc 5 mg q.d.
Ziac 10/6.25 mg q.d. (blood pressure medication).
Clonidine 0.2 mg t.i.d. 
Nexium 40 mg q.d.
Celexa 20 mg q.d.
Ultracet p.r.n.
Advil 800 mg 3 times a day.


Linda has numbness in the left lower lip and chin area.  She has significant involvement of the facial nerve with significant paralysis associated with the frontal branch, milder association with the zygomatic branch, but the mandibular buccal and mandibular branches are nonfunctional.  She gets headaches 3 times a week at about a level of 9 in the frontal and temporal area.  She rates her TMJ pain at 9, average daily pain at 6, jaw function and diet at 5, disability at 4.  

In frontal view, Linda has fairly good facial symmetry except that the left mandibular ramus posterior body area protrudes laterally about 3 to 4 mm more than the right side.  Upper lip length is about 22 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is -3 mm.  In profile, she has good facial balance.  Intraorally, Linda is missing the mandibular left second and third molar and probably right third molar.  In the upper arch, she is missing the left secondary and third molar and probably right third molar.  Linda is also missing the maxillary left cuspid.  She has an anterior deep bite of about 4 mm, but pretty close to being a Class I cuspid-molar relationship.  She does have a post in the maxillary left cuspid area, but will need to have the restorative dentistry completed yet.  Oropharyngeal tissues were difficult to evaluate because of decreased jaw opening, but what I could see appeared to be healthy tissue.  Uvula may be somewhat elongated.  Linda has no difficulties breathing through her nose.  Linda reports no problems with snoring, daytime somnolence, nor sleep apnea.  She sleeps well at night.  To palpation, she has moderate to low severe pain on the left side in the left temporalis, left medial pterygoid, left TMJ, left retromandibular area and mild tenderness over the left masseter muscle.  The maximal incisal opening is 24 mm, but only 15 mm without pain.  The excursion movements are 1.5 mm in each direction.  

Six or seven years ago, Linda states that she could open her jaws to about 38 mm, but it slowly decreased to its current level of 24 mm.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows that she has had root canals on all of her maxillary teeth and her lower left bicuspids, first molar, right side bicuspids, and second molar.  She has an implant in the maxillary left cuspid area.  She has a Christensen prosthesis on the left side with an acrylic head and a metal pin in the center and it appears as though there is metal on metal contact.  There are 6 screws in the mandibular component.  On the right side, there is a Christensen with metal on metal articulation.  There are 6 screws holding the mandibular component in place.  The sinuses look relatively normal.  

Coronal radiographic shows that the sinuses appear reasonably clear.  The septum is deviated off to the right side.  The turbinates are relatively normal in size.  

Axial view shows that the turbinates are moderate in size.  There may be a spur coming off the septum towards the left side that be blocking the airway.  The sinuses I believe are fairly clear, but the left side is not as clear as the right side.  

Left TMJ coronal tomogram show that there appears to be some space between the metal and bone in the fossa on the fossa component.  There is metal on metal contact of the centric pin of the mandibular component functioning against the metal of the fossa.  There may be some heterotopic bone in the fossa area coming off the medial side. 

Right TMJ coronal view shows a little better space between the fossa and the metal of the fossa component.  The mandibular component demonstrates metal on metal articulation.  It is difficult to tell if there is heterotopic bone on the medial side or whether it is just a shadow from the metal prosthesis.  

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a Christensen prosthesis with an acrylic head with metal on metal contact on that side.  It is difficult to tell if there is a fracture through the prosthesis, but I believe it is intact, although one view is questionable.

Right TMJ sagittal tomogram shows the presence of the Christensen with metal on metal articulation.  Do not see any evidence of any fracture.  There is definitely metal on metal articulation.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows Linda has basically a slight Class II skeletal relationship, but fairly good Class I occlusion.  The oropharyngeal airway as best it can be seen appears relatively normal.  She does have some metal vascular clips in the soft tissues below the inferior border of the mandible on the left side.

Basic diagnoses for Linda would be as follows:

Failed left TMJ Christensen total joint prosthesis with an acrylic head.
Left TMJ foreign body giant cell reaction secondary to metallosis and acrylic debris.
Severe left-sided TMJ pain, myofascial pain, and left-sided headaches. 

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol).
2.	Surgery.
	a.	Removal of left TMJ failed Christensen total joint prosthesis.
	b.	Debridement of left TMJ area.
	c.	Left TMJ reconstruction with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. 
	d.	Fat graft to left TMJ (harvest from the abdomen).
	e.	Application of maxillary and mandibular arch bars.

original_note:
JAY LISENBY									01/28/2008

Jay is 23 years old, referred by Dr. Farhad Moshiri.  Only previous surgery was for wisdom teeth extraction x4 in June 2006.  Jay developed Type 1 diabetes about 5 years ago.  Currently takes the following medications:  

NovoLog insulin--breakfast 7 units, lunch 12 units, supper 12 units +/- sliding scale.  
Lantus insulin at bedtime 12 units. 
Avapro.
Baby aspirin 1 per day.

Primary concerns that Jay has include the following:

Improper jaw alignment.
Clicking sounds in the left jaw joint.

Jay’s problems began when he was about 18 years of age in about 2003 of unknown etiology.  He was aware that his left jaw joint began clicking and popping.  He began orthodontic treatment in 2006.  

Jay denies headaches, TMJ pain, pain around the head and neck area, earaches, tinnitus, vertigo, and other joint problems.  He rates his jaw function at 1, diet at 0, disability at 0.  In frontal view, Jay has fairly good left to right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 23 mm.  Upper teeth lip relationship is about 3.5 to 4 mm.  The maxillary midline is about 2 mm off towards the left side.  The mandibular midline is somewhat toward the facial midline, but the chin is off to the left side about 2 mm.  In the profile view, Jay has some infraorbital deficiency by 5 to 6 mm and probably about 4 mm in the maxilla.  His chin appears a little bit strong, but actual chin neck length and angle actually look fairly good.  Intraorally, Jay has all of his lower teeth back to the second molars present.  The teeth are nicely aligned and level.  A little thin on the attached gingiva around the mandibular left central incisor, but the other teeth appear to be adequate.  In the upper arch, he has all his teeth back to the second molars present.  Jay has a full Class III cuspid relationship with an anterior and bilateral posterior crossbites.  Jay is missing the mandibular right lateral incisor.  The right eye is about 2 mm higher than the left eye.  Some slight asymmetry in the upper arch with the left cuspid about 1 mm posterior to the right cuspid.  Oropharyngeal tissues all look healthy, but he does have extremely large tonsils.  Intranasally, septum appears relatively straight.  Turbinates appear moderately enlarged.  Jay does state he tends to breathe through his mouth at night when he sleeps.  Jay has no pain to palpation on the head and neck area.  On opening, he does have a definite click in the left joint at about 20 mm of opening and closing click at 15 mm.  He has about 0.5 mm of reverse overbite.  With the absence of mandibular right lateral incisor, there is a tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of 5 mm with the lower arch being smaller than the upper arch.

On radiographic evaluation, Panorex shows the absence of all 4th and 3rd molars and the mandibular right incisor. Difficult to evaluate condyles and sinuses in this radiograph.  Left TMJ tomogram shows the condyle appears a little bit narrow anteroposteriorly and there is a decreased posterior joint space.  Articular eminence is moderately steep and somewhat 
elongated.  Increased anterior joint space is present.  Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is narrow anteroposteriorly and there is a decreased posterior joint space.  Articular eminence is not quite as steep.  I feel there is fairly good translation of both condyles with them coming down towards the inferior aspect of the articular eminence.  Waters view shows that right sinus looks a little bit more cloudy than the left side.  Turbinates appear quite large.  There is some deviation of the nasal septum in the bony area.  A maxillary midline shift off towards the left side is noted.  Lateral cephalogram radiograph shows a skeletal and occlusal Class III skeletal relationship.  Oropharyngeal airway looks relatively normal.  Infraorbital areas appear somewhat hypoplastic.

DIAGNOSES:
Mandibular prognathism.
AP maxillary deficiency.
Transverse maxillary deficiency.
Slight vertical maxillary excess.
Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with reduction.
Hypertrophied turbinates causing partial nasal airway obstruction.
Anterior and bilateral posterior crossbites.
Class III occlusion.
Bilateral infraorbital hypoplasia (the patient is not interested in treating this aspect).
Extrusion of maxillary left second molar.
Periodontal concerns especially with the mandibular right central incisor with lack of attached gingiva. 

Treatment plan for Jay would be as follows:
Periodontal evaluation and possible gingival grafting around mandibular right central incisor.
Surgery Stage 1.
a.  	Placement of mandibular distraction device on the lower arch.
b. 	Midline vertical osteotomy of the mandible.
c.  	Initiation of distraction 7 days post surgery with expansion to open the space 
approximately 5 mm.
      3.   Presurgical Orthodontics.
a.  	Continue to align and level maxillary arch.  Tip the cuspid roots distally and 
lateral roots mesially to make room for interdental osteotomies. Left side is a little 
tight currently.  Try to intrude the maxillary left second molar to help level the 
occlusion.
b.  	Align level of the lower arch.  Place a pontic tooth in the distraction space so as to    
maintain that spacing. 
     4.   Surgery Stage 2.
	a.  	Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning using Mitek anchors.
	b. 	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to expand, advance, and correct any 
asymmetries.
	c.  	Bilateral mandibular osteotomies to set the mandible backwards.
	d.  	Partial turbinectomies.


     5.  	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion. 
	a.   	Placement of osteointegrated implant in mandibular central incisor 
space.
	b.  	Restorative dentistry to place a crown on the dental implant.

original_note:
B239
Meeting with Dr. _____. In review, the left TMJ itself looked normal, but with a thickened posterior band. In the right joint, there are arthritic changes with a depression in the mid part of the condyle with a significantly thickened disc. There is some fluid in the superior joint space and some altered signal between the head of the condyle and articular disc in the area of the depression. Dr. ____ could not differentiate between inflammatory process versus just fatty tissue or other type of tissue present. The condyle is broad anteroposteriorly. A depression in the mid part of the condyle is also evident.

IMPRESSION:
Relatively normal left TMJ, but with a thickened posterior band.
Right TMJ arthritic changes with a depression in the mid part of the condyle, significant thickening of the articular disc, and with some form of tissue in the erosive area on the condylar head and the disc.

B239
Telephone conversation with ____.  ____ had many questions that he wanted to ask about the TMJ pathology and the predictability of the Mitek anchors. I told him under normal circumstances with displaced discs the Mitek anchor works very well with a highly predictable outcome. However, if the patient has connective tissue autoimmune disease or have a reactive type of arthritis in the joints, then that could affect the treatment outcome. The MRI does not bear out the pathology that is identified clinically. Clinically, she has clicking and popping in the left joint with a fairly significant opening and closing click. On the MRI, it does not look exactly the same. Also, when I saw her in November, her pain was worse on the right side than the left side. The right side functioned reasonably smoothly, but she has a deformed disc, and, in fact, the posterior band is extremely thick, although in relatively normal position. The disc could be laterally displaced. The treatment plan that we have worked for her at this time would give us the best treatment option, although I cannot guarantee that we can eliminate the pain or that the joints will be predictably eliminated of pathology and pain. I answered all the questions that he had at this time. He is to call if they have any further questions.

B239 
The patient presents for presurgical workup. Final records were taken. I did my final examination. History and physical performed. Consent form reviewed and signed. I answered all the questions she and her mother had. She is ready for surgery tomorrow. She has definite clicking in her left TMJ indicative of a displaced articular disc. On the consent form, we only have approved for a right TMJ surgery. I discussed it with her the fact that she has displaced disc on the left side as well. She agrees to proceed on and fix the left joint as well. She is ready for surgery tomorrow. B239

The patient presents for follow-up evaluation. The patient doing very well. Occlusion looks good. I suctioned out her nose. She is in good spirits. She says she is getting her diet down well. No particular complaints or concerns at this time. She is progressing well and to return to see me in 2 days. I talked her about to blow her nose and she has already started doing it, although she was not supposed to.

B239
The patient presents for follow-up evaluation. The patient doing very well. Occlusion is fairly good. Nasal airway was suctioned. I removed sutures. No evidence of infection or wound dehiscence. She is in good spirits. Having some discomfort and pain but not too significant. She is progressing well and to return to see me in 1 day. I answered all the questions she had today. B239
The patient presents for follow-up evaluation. She is doing very well. Occlusion is good. I did add a couple of light Class II elastics. No evidence of infection or wound dehiscence. Splint is in place. She is going to be returning home. I sent her a drawing outlining the elastics as they are currently in place. I answered all the questions she and her mother had today. She is progressing well. I suctioned out her nose one last time. She knows how to blow her nose safely. She is progressing well. She does have a concern that it appears her upper front teeth are slightly off to the left side, but her nose is off to the right side and her upper lip is also off to the right side and more aligned with the nose than the teeth. I gave her some exercises to do to try to improve that situation. With the nose being out of position, it does hold the upper lip off more towards the right side and does increase the appearance of some asymmetry, although jaw structures seem fairly symmetric. She is to get in to see Dr. ____ this coming week. She is doing well and to return to see me in about 3 months.

B239
B239is 32 years old, referred by Dr. ___. She is in good health with no major medical problems except for her jaw situation. B239 has no trouble with headaches, but gets pain in her right jaw joint at about a level of 5 and average daily pain at 4. Jaw function and diet are at 0 and disability is at 7. She believes her TMJ problems began when she was about 12 years old, possibly from the orthodontic treatment that she had. She had a faulty crown in her 20s that caused significant problems for her for a few years. She had orthodontics from the age of 11 to 14 and then again from 14 to 16 on the upper arch including the use of head gear. She takes ibuprofen and vitamins, but really nothing else. She has no trouble with earaches, gets occasional tinnitus, but no vertigo. No other joints bother her. She is aware of clenching at night time.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
In frontal view, B239 has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 18 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is about 3 mm. The maxillary midline, mandibular dental midline, and chin are pretty much on the facial midline. The maxillary midline may be 1.5 mm off to the left side as is the mandibular dental midline. The chin appears to be aligned with the lower arch. In profile view, upper lip supralabrale appears to be posterior to subnasale. The maxilla appears to be about probably 3 to 4 mm AP deficient. Chin appears to be about 12 to 15 mm AP deficient. Chin neckline is fairly short, and the chin neck angle itself is fairly well defined. She has a lot of fullness in the submental area. Intraorally, B239 has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars. She has some mild crowding in the lower anterior arch. She has good attached gingiva around the lower anterior teeth. In the upper arch, B239 has all of her posterior teeth back through the second molars present. She has pretty close to a Class I cuspid relationship, and she has an anterior open bite of probably 1 to 2 mm. She appears to have reasonable occlusion up through the second bicuspid area. The bicuspids might be a little bit out of occlusion as are all the anterior teeth. Oropharyngeal tissues all look relatively normal. The nasal airway looks relatively normal with reasonably good airway passage. To palpation, B239 has some mild tenderness over the right masseter and right medial pterygoid area. On opening, she has a click in the left joint at about 35 mm and 25 mm of closing. The right side seems to function reasonably smoothly. Maximal incisal opening is about 48 mm. Excursion movements are about 11 mm in each direction.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the absence of the four third molars. She has a crown with a root canal on the mandibular right second molar. This is the tooth that gave her so much trouble through the years. The condyles appear wide anteroposteriorly. Sinuses appear clear. Roots on the incisors, both upper and lower, appear somewhat short in length. Waters view shows that she has a little cloudiness in the left sinus, but the septum appears straight and she appears to have a good nasal airway. Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has fairly good shape with a little bit of flattening on the anterior head. Articular eminence is relatively shallow. She does have what appears to be good joint space. Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears postured somewhat forward in the fossa. Articular eminence is moderate in inclination. There appears to be increased superior and posterior joint space. Her occlusion appears fairly good. On opening, there is good translation of both condyles. There is a defect seen on the head of the right condyle where there is a little void in the superior cortex. Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that B239 has a high occlusal plane angle facial morphology with a retruded maxilla and mandible. Oropharyngeal airway surprisingly is pretty reasonable with an AP dimension of about almost 10 mm at the soft palate level and about 8 mm at the base of the tongue. She appears to have a skeletal Class I jaw relationship.

B239's diagnoses are as follows:
AP maxillary hypoplasia.
Posterior vertical maxillary hypoplasia. AP mandibular hypoplasia.
Left TMJ articular disc dislocation.
Right TMJ arthritis and significantly increased thickness of the articular disc. Anterior openbite. High occlusal plane angle. TMJ pain.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
Presurgical orthodontics. Align and level maxillary arch. Align the four incisors at an elevated plane by placing a step in the arch wire. Extrude the maxillary cuspids 1 to 2 mm to get them level with the posterior teeth. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies.
Align and level mandibular arch. Consider slenderizing the lower anterior teeth. Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ surgery for arthroplasty and to reposition the articular discs.
Maxillary osteotomies to move the anterior aspect forward, posterior aspect inferior, level the curve of Spee, and correct any transverse discrepancies that may be present. Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction.
3. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B239 MRI 
AGE: 32/F
DOB:
Date of Acquisition: Date of Interpretation:
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with good mediolateral width. The condyle appears to extend slightly beyond the lateral rim of the fossa. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 to 3 mm. The disc may be anteriorly displaced.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has a little erosive type kind of lesion in the medial portion of the top of the condyle. Joint space appears a little bit narrow on the medial side of the joint. The disc may be laterally displaced and perhaps anteriorly displaced.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that actually has fairly good shape to it. The condyle appears it may be slightly large compared to the size of the fossa. The disc appears to be in position. She has a little bit of fluid in the superior joint space. On opening, there appears to be good translation of the condyle and the disc with the condyle actually coming anterior to the articular eminence. The joint space appears a little bit excessive vertically and the disc, particularly the posterior band, appears a little bit thick.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a disc that is quite thick vertically. There actually is a depression in the mid part of the condyle. Out laterally, the disc has a little bit better shape is not as evident at the lateral aspect of the joint as is more medially. The condyle appears fairly broad anteroposteriorly. There is a little bit of fluid in the superior joint space. On opening, there is moderately good translation, although not quite as good as the opposite side. The disc appears to be in a reduced position.

IMPRESSION:
Relatively normal left TMJ, but with thickened posterior band of disc.
Right TMJ arthritic changes with a depression in the mid part of the condyle with a significantly thickened disc in the mid part of the disc.

original_note:
B174
B174 is referred by Dr. XXXX.
Primary concerns that B174 has includes the following:
Long-term indications of narrow airway.
Desire to successfully straighten teeth.
Desire to breathe through nose.

B174 is not sure when his jaw joint problems began or the original etiology. He had previous orthodontics when he was about 8 or 9 with a maxillary expander. His current orthodontics began 13 months ago with Dr. BXXXX. B174 apparently had an impacted maxillary right cuspid and he had a primary tooth extracted and the impacted cuspid uncovered and brought down into position. B174 gets headaches predominantly on the right side in the temporal area and in the back of the head that occur about twice a month. He has no overt TMJ pain or myofascial pain or headaches on a usual day. He rates his jaw function, diet, and disability at zero. He takes no medications. He has no problems with earaches, tinnitus, nor vertigo or hearing loss. He has no other joint problems. He is not aware of clenching or bruxing.

In frontal view, B174 has some various areas of asymmetry. Upper lip length is about 21 mm. Upper tooth relationship is about 2.5 to 3 mm. The maxillary midline is pretty much on the facial midline. The nose is about 2 mm to the left. The mandibular dental midline is about 1 mm to the left and the chin is slightly to the right about 1 or 2 mm. In profile, the maxilla appears to be about 5 mm AP deficient and the mandible about 10 to 12 mm AP deficient. The chin neckline is a little bit short. Intraorally, B174 has all of his lower teeth back through the second molars. He has a nice wide arch. The teeth appear to be nicely aligned and leveled. He is fairly weak on the attached gingiva around the lower anterior teeth. In the upper arch, he has all of his teeth back through the second molars. He has some springs on between the upper laterals and cuspids to open some space and he has about 1 mm of space on each side. He has an anterior cross bite and a left posterior cross bite. On that side, he is fairly close to being in a cross bite. He has a Class III end-on cuspid relationship bilaterally. He has about 0.5 mm reverse deep bite relationship. Oropharyngeal tissues look reasonably healthy. Uvula may be a little bit elongated. Intranasally, there may be a little deviation with the nasal septum. The left turbinate appears quite large and filling most of the nasal cavity. On the left side, the turbinate appears normal in size. B174 does have a transverse cant to the occlusal plane with the maxillary right cuspid being about 2 mm higher than the left side and the mandibular right cuspid being 1 mm higher than the left side. On opening, joints seems to be reasonably smooth, but there is an occasional slight glitch in the left side. He has no tenderness to palpation around the head and neck area. The maximal incisal opening is about 54 mm, and excursion movements are about 10 mm in each direction.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the presence of impacted four third molars. Nothing else abnormal is seen.
Coronal view shows the left turbinate is quite enlarged. There is some deviation in the nasal septum. Right turbinate appears more normal. Sinus appears clear.

Axial view shows that there is some deviation in the nasal septum. The left turbinate appears enlarged and appears to block the airway whereas the right turbinate appears more normal in size. Sinuses are clear.

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that appears to have a little bit of sclerosis on the top. There appears to be a little bit of anterior beaking developing. The articular eminence has a relatively soft slope to it. The joint space appears reasonably well balanced. There appears to be a little flattening on the top of the condyle.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that appears to have a little bit of flattening on the top and in some areas there appears to be some sclerosis. The articular eminence has a soft inclination. The joint space appears fairly well balanced and may be slightly decreased posterior.

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has fairly good mediolateral width. There is a slight decreased joint space at the lateral aspect of the joint, but otherwise it looks pretty good. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 mm.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has good mediolateral width. There appears to be decreased lateral joint space. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly about 2 to 3 mm.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that the maxilla and mandible are AP deficient, although there is a skeletal Class III and occlusal Class III relationship. There appears to be some vertical difference between the right and left sides.

I have previously done a workup on B174 and the outlined diagnoses and recommended treatment stands as previously reported. I will wait to see the radiologist's impression of the joint situation. Otherwise, the treatment recommendations remain as previously recorded.

Bill, if you could please send me the I-CAT from when you first saw B174 that would be very helpful so I can assess to see if he has active condylar hyperplasia. That would give us a year growth assessment.

B174
I-CAT examination shows the condylar heads and necks are elongated. The joint space looks reasonably good. The left turbinate appears enlarged. The sinuses appear clear. Oropharyngeal airway is constricted and probably somewhere around 4 to 5 mm. Cranio based references, cella and nasion are not included in the radiograph. The bridge of the head and forehead are not included in the radiograph. He has impacted third molars x4. He appears to have a moderately high occlusal plane angle. He has a Class III occlusal relationship, but may have a Class II skeletal relationship. Model analysis shows that he has spacing between the upper cuspids and first bicuspids of about 2 mm on the right side, 1 mm on the left side. He has an accentuated curve of Wilson, particularly on the right side. He has an anterior cross bite and at least a right-sided cross bite. The left side may be a tendency towards an anterior cross bite and potential for bilateral posterior cross bite.

He presents with history of headaches in the right temporal and back of the head at a level of 5 and gets this one to two times per month. He has some popping in his right joint. He has no earaches, tinnitus, nor vertigo. He rates his headaches at 5, TMJ pain at 0, average daily pain at 0, jaw function, diet and disability all at 0. Primary concerns he has include the following:

Decreased airway.
Wants his teeth straightened and fitting together properly.
Desires to breathe through his nose.
Basic diagnosis for B174 would be:
Rule out bilateral mandibular condylar hyperplasia.
Right TMJ articular disc dislocation with reduction.
AP maxillary posterior vertical and transverse hypoplasia. Mandibular AP hypoplasia. Class III occlusion.
High occlusal plane angle.
Anterior cross bite.
Increased curve of Wilson, particularly in the right side. Decreased oropharyngeal airway.
Impacted third molars x4. Hypertrophied turbinate, particularly on the left side. Spacing between the upper cuspids and first bicuspids. Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2 to 3 mm.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
1. MRI of left and right TMJ.
2. Evaluate previous records to determine if active condylar hyperplasia is present.
3. Presurgical orthodontics. a.	Align and level maxillary arch. Retract cuspids back against the bicuspids and can leave residual spacing between the lateral incisors and cuspids to help compensate for the tooth size discrepancy. Try to decrease excessive curve of Wilson in the upper arch, particularly on the right side. b.	Continue to align and level mandibular arch.
4. Surgery. a.	Consider bilateral mandibular high condylectomies if condylar hyperplasia is proven to be present. b.	Right TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchor. c.	Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction. d.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance in a counterclockwise direction as well as to maximize the occlusal fit. e.	Consider left partial turbinectomy. f.	Surgical removal of impacted third molars x4.
5. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

B174 MRI
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with fairly good mediolateral width. The lateral rim of the fossa curves inferiorly only about 1 to 2 mm. Discal tissue may be anteriorly and medially displaced. The joint space actually looks reasonably good vertically.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with fairly good mediolateral width. The disc likewise appears to be medially displaced.
Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has little changes on the surface with a little bit of flattening on the anterior head and a little tendency towards beaking. Out laterally, the disc is more difficult to see and could be just slightly anteriorly displaced at most lateral aspect of the joint, but more medially the disc appears to be in better position. The articular eminence has a moderate steepness. The disc appears to have fairly good morphology. There appears to be a little bit of fluid in the superior and inferior joint space. On opening, there is some limitation in translation, although the disc appears to be in a reduced position and there appears to be reasonable translation of the condyle and the disc.

Right TMJ has some flattening on the anterior head and a little tendency towards beaking. Out laterally, the disc is difficult to see and may be indicative of a medial displacement and perhaps an anterior displacement. More medially, the disc appears to be in reasonably good position. The articular eminence has a moderate inclination. There may be a little bit of fluid in the superior and inferior joint spaces. On opening, there is some limitation in translation, although the disc appears to be in a reduced position and there appears to be reasonable translation of the condyle and the disc.

IMPRESSION: Bilateral TMJ mild arthritis and articular discs medially displaced and anteriorly displaced at the lateral aspect of the joint. However, there may be no significant clinical symptoms.

original_note:
ANNA MARIE SOMMERHAUSER							5/5/05

Referred by:  Dr. Duane Keller.
Age: 50.
Date of Birth: 7/31/54.
Home address:  5 Toussaint Dr., O'Fallon, MO, 63366.
Home phone:  636240-1002.
Work:  314-654-7478 (work time 2 a.m. to 12 noon.)

Anna was involved in a motor vehicle accident in 2001.  She apparently had a dislocated jaw at the time.  She was taken to the ER and the jaw was reduced.  She also injured her right knee in the accident and subsequently had surgery on her right knee.  Since then, she has had continued significant problems with her jaw function and with her jaw joints.  Treatment to date includes injections in the muscles in the head, neck, back and jaw area; physical therapy for 1-1/2 years and splint therapy since 2001.  

PRIMARY PROBLEMS:
Earaches.
TMJ pain.
Headaches.
Myofascial pain around the head, neck, jaws and shoulder areas.
Difficulty with eating and chewing.

She gets earaches, tinnitus, and occasional vertigo.  The other joints that bother her include her neck, right knee predominately when there are weather changes and her left foot where she has previously fractured her 5th toe and it is malaligned.  She states that she wakes up stiff everyday but then as the day goes on at work, her pain problems become worse.  She starts work at 2 in the morning and works normally until noon or later.  She apparently does a lot of computer work and this seems to aggravate her situation.  She says that when she wears her bite plate that she does not clench her teeth but without she does clench and also does this during the day.  She said she had a bone scan done and she has decreased bone density in her left hip but she has no problems with it.  Medications she takes include ibuprofen 800 mg tablets as needed and usually takes it daily for her headaches.  She also takes Celebrex daily.  She gets daily headaches predominately in the temple and back of her head area at a level of 5.   She rates her TMJ pain at 5, average daily pain at 5, jaw function at 5, diet at 8, and disability at 4.  

To palpation she has moderate pain in the temporalis and masseter muscles and mild to moderate pain over the TMJs.  

Based on MRI, history as well as photographs, Anna's diagnoses would be as follows:
Bilateral TMJ articular disc dislocation.
Mandibular hyperplasia (relative).
Headaches.
TMJ pain.
Myofascial pain.
Masticatory dysfunction.
ANNA SOMMERHAUSER			5/5/05					Page 2


Recommended treatment would be as follows:
Presurgical orthodontics.
Place orthodontic appliances on the maxillary teeth and begin to align and level the arch.
Place orthodontic appliances on the mandibular teeth and begin to align and level the arch.

Surgery.
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies with bone screw stabilization. 

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

I answered all the questions that Anna had at this time.  She has some financial questions and insurance questions and I told her to call Eve and discuss those factors with her.  She will do so.  She is to call us if she has any other questions or concerns.  We will prepare a letter of preauthorization for her insurance company.
