original_note:
RYAN MURRAY									03/13/2009

Ryan is 24 years old and is a self-referral from his online research.  Ryan’s TMJ problems began when he was approximately 14 years of age when his joints started to click and pop.  He began noting facial asymmetry around the age of 17 to 18, although of unknown etiology.  He had a bone scan done in 2007 that showed no mandibular growth.  He had orthodontics as a teenager and then orthodontics again from 2006 through 2008.  His bite continues to shift and move and the bite tends to open on the right side.  He has had equilibrations done and has also had some splint therapy.  Other treatment that Ryan has had includes splint therapy x4 and they provide, however, only temporary relief and then problems get worse.  In addition to equilibration, he has also had physical therapy which has been of no benefit.  He has no trouble with earaches, tinnitus, or vertigo.  No other joints bother him.  He is aware of clenching and bruxing at night and currently has an NTI splint to help relieve that.  He has moderate snoring at night, but no daytime somnolence and no sleep apnea symptoms.  He believes that he breathes predominantly through his nose at night. 

He gets daily headaches at about a level of 6 predominantly in the temple areas.  He rates his TMJ pain at 6, average daily pain at 4, jaw function 7, diet at 0, and disability at 6.  

CLINICAL EXAMINATION.  In frontal view, Ryan has increased fullness on the right side of the face compared to the left side.  Upper lip length is about 23 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 2 to 3 mm.  The maxillary midline is shifted almost 2 mm to the left side.  Mandibular dental midline is about 1 mm to the left.  Chin is about 5 to 6 mm to the left.  In profile, the maxilla is retruded about 2 mm and the chin is retruded about 10 to 12 mm.  Chin neckline is short and chin neck angle is moderately well defined.  There is some fullness in the submental area.  Transversely, Ryan has facial asymmetry with the right side of the face being probably 4 to 5 mm wider than the left side in transverse dimension.  Intraorally, Ryan has all of his teeth back to the second molars.  The teeth are fairly well aligned and level.  He has good attached gingiva.  In the upper arch, Ryan has all of his teeth back through the second molars.  Upper lateral incisors appears a little bit small in size.  Ryan has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship on the right side by probably 2 mm, but on the left side it is closer to Class I occlusion.  On the right side, it could be just slightly open in the molar area possibly.  He has some flattening of the teeth in the molar area, particularly on the left side, likely due in part to the equilibrations.  There is a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the maxillary left cuspid being 2 to 2.5 mm higher than the right side.  The mandibular right cuspid is about 3 mm lower than the left cuspid.  There is slightly greater transverse discrepancy in the mandible compared to the maxilla.  Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively normal in architecture and morphology.  Posterior faucial pillars are slightly posteriorly positioned, but not too significant.  Tonsils are normal.  Turbinates appears moderately enlarged anteriorly.  The septum is reasonably straight.  There is probably moderate nasal airway obstruction.  Maximum incisal opening is about 48 mm, and he can get to about 35 mm with reasonable comfort.  Excursion movement to the right was 9 mm, to the left 5 mm.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  I-CAT evaluation shows in the Panorex he is missing all four third molars.  All of the rest of the teeth are present.  He has crowns or large restorations in most of the posterior teeth.  The turbinates appear quite large.  Right condyle appears significantly larger than the left side.  Bony septum appears reasonably straight.  
Axial view shows that the turbinates are moderately enlarged.  
Coronal view shows again that the turbinates are moderately enlarged.  There may be some significantly increased thickness of the sinus membrane in the left sinus.  
Right TMJ coronal view shows condyle that has fairly good width.  There appears to be decrease lateral joint space, although the vertical joint space appears fairly reasonable.  
Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is smaller in size compared to the right side.  Joint space appears a little bit better balanced.  
Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle with elongated head and neck.  The articular eminence is moderately steep.  The condyle is posteriorly positioned in the fossa.  
Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that likewise is posteriorly positioned in the fossa.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  There is increased anterior joint space.  
Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Ryan has a high occlusal plane angle and a skeletal Class II relationship.  He appears to be fairly close to a Class I occlusion.  Oropharyngeal airway, however, measures 6 mm at the soft palate level and probably 8 mm at the base of the tongue (normal dimension 11 mm). 

Basic diagnoses would be as follows:    

Right mandibular condylar hyperplasia versus osteoma. 
AP mandibular hypoplasia and asymmetry. 
Maxillary AP hypoplasia and asymmetry.
Class II end-on occlusion on the right side, Class I on the left.
Slight posterior open bite on the right side.
Transverse yaw in the maxilla and mandible with both jaws being shifted to the right approximately 3 mm. 
Bilateral TMJ articular disc dislocations with reduction.
TMJ pain. 
Headaches.  
Myofascial pain. 
Slight tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton’s difference of approximately 2 mm.
Bilateral turbinate hyperplasia. 

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

Presurgical orthodontics.  
Align and level maxillary arch.  Tip lateral incisors mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. 
Align and level mandibular arch.  
Surgery. 
Right TMJ high condylectomy. 
Bilateral TMJ disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Maxillary osteotomies to level the maxilla and advance it forward.  
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible forward in a counterclockwise direction and level it transversely.
Bilateral partial turbinectomies. 
Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B231
B231 is 51 years old. She has had previous surgery for her retina 5 years ago, gallbladder surgery 5 years ago, hysterectomy 5 years ago, and fibroid surgery 25 years ago.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS:
1. Synthroid 0.137 mg per day.
2. Natural progesterone.
She has an allergy to penicillin. She had papillary carcinoma in her thyroid gland necessitating the previous surgery. She is otherwise in good health except for her problems associated with the head and neck area.

PRIMARY CONCERNS:
1. Breathing problems.
2. Muscle problems particularly affecting her neck and shoulders.
3. Ear ringing.
4. Sleep disorder.
5. Digestive problems.
6. Dental problems.

B231's problems began probably around 20 years ago or longer of unknown etiology. She developed muscle spasms in her neck and shoulders. She even developed numbness in her hands and fingers. She has had cervical workup done. Apparently they did find some problem with her cervical vertebrae but her physical therapist, XXXX, felt it was more related to B231's bite problems. ___-saw B231 in 2003. She was able to give B231 treatments that seemed to help. She then saw Dr. XXXX. He was the first to suggest that she have some jaw surgery to fix her bite relationship that could not be done strictly orthodontically. She saw Dr. XXXX about having the surgery. She subsequently moved to Dallas. She moved into this area 3 years ago. She has seen Dr. XXXX who placed her in braces 10 months ago. Up to this time, B231 has had the following treatments:
1. Physical therapy.
2. Medications.
3. Massages.
4. Five years ago, splint therapy by Dr. XXXX.
5. Orthodontics beginning in 10 months ago by Dr. XXXX.

She has also done regiments of exercises and massages that seemed to have helped her.
She has no problems with earaches but gets tinnitus but no vertigo or hearing loss. No other joints bother her. She is aware of clenching and bruxing of her teeth at night. She really does not have any significant headache problems. She rates her TMJ pain at 1, average daily pain at 4 which involves primarily the neck and shoulder areas. Jaw function and diet are about a level of 1 and disability is at 0.

B231 does feel that she has a posture problem and has a head postured forward type of stature. She does report problems with snoring but does not feel that she has actual sleep apnea problems. She has had a sleep study done. Sleep study showed that she had some snoring problems but the O2 sats were good and her apnea-hypopnea index was 0.6 events per hour. Sleep study impression:
1. Primary snoring disorder versus upper airway resistance syndrome.
2. Sleep maintenance insomnia with endorsement of psychological variables.

The sleep study was done 9 months ago. In frontal view, B231 has fairly good facial symmetry except there is some asymmetry in the nose. Lip length is about 24 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is about 0. Maxillary, mandibular and chin midline are all pretty much on the facial midline. Nasal tip is deviated by at least 2 mm off towards the left side. In profile view, B231 has fairly good balance in the upper lip, cheeks and nose. Chin appears retruded by probably 10 to 12 mm. Chin-neckline is extremely shortened and there is essentially no chin-neck angle. She does have a head postured forward neck position. As B231 postures her mandible forward, it significantly improves her facial balance. She is essentially in an end-on incisor relationship but overall profile is significantly improved although the chin-neck angle is still not well defined. Still somewhat deficient with a little bit of apparent AP maxillary deficiency of perhaps 2 to 3 mm. B231 is currently in braces back through the first molars. Teeth appear to be reasonably aligned and leveled. She has attrition on her lower incisors. In the upper arch she has all of her teeth back through the second molars and has appliances there. She has a slight reverse curve of Spee in the upper arch. She has a Class II end-on occlusion being about at least 5 mm Class II. She has about a 3 mm overjet. She has about a 2.5 mm vertical overbite. As she slides forward, it eliminates the overjet. She still is at least 3 mm Class II. She has a slight cant in the mandibular occlusal plane with the left cuspid being probably 1 to 1.5 mm but maxilla appears relatively level. In the oropharynx the tissues look healthy. Uvula is mild to moderate in size. The soft palate may be slightly elongated but more important is that the faucial pillars show significant constricture with a very narrow space between the posterior faucial pillars. They are positioned quite medially in the oropharynx. Nasally, B231 has constricted nostrils particularly on the left side. The alar feet are wide and are somewhat obstructed particularly on the left side. B231 has an inward collapse of the upper and lower lateral cartilages in the nose which is the primary factor causing the constricture of the nostril and the luminal valve area. Also, she has a wide columella base with the feet extending laterally out into the nares again contributing significantly to occlusion. Using the nasal speculum and just holding open the nares significantly improved her abilities to breathe through both sides of her nose. Even though the nasal septum is deviated somewhat off towards the right side, her major airway obstruction is anterior at the nostril area and luminal valve area and not so much posteriorly in the turbinate and septal area. To palpation, she had no tenderness around the head and neck area. Joint seems to function relatively smoothly although she felt a little tension on the right side in wide opening. Maximal incisal opening is about 49 mm. Excursive movements are about 9 mm in each direction.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
: Panorex shows the absence of all 4 third molars and all the rest of the teeth are present. She has root canals on the maxillary right first bicuspid second molar. Sinuses look relatively clear. Condyles appear to be a little bit flattened anteriorly and there maybe a little bit of sclerosis associated with them. The condyles appear large.

Waters view shows a little bit of cloudiness in the left sinus. Nasal septum has some significant deviation to it in the bony area particularly at the superior aspect. The turbinates appear moderate in size. Left TMJ tomogram shows that there appears to be slight increase in posterior joint space and a little bit decreased anterior joint space. The joint space does appear quite narrow anteriorly. Articular eminence is moderately steep. I do not now if the joint space is compatible with the disc being in position.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears somewhat large with a little flattening on the anterior head. It seems to be postured forward slightly in the mandible which could mean that it was not in central relation at the time of the imaging. Anterior joint space appears quite narrow and perhaps moderately decreased. On opening, there is good translation of the condyles and there appears to be adequate joint space between the condyle and articular eminences. Flattening on the anterior head and there is mild sclerosis as well as anterior beaking can be seen.

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that B231 has a high occlusal plane angle facial morphology Class II position of the mandible. The oropharyngeal airway actually looks quite good and better than anticipated. Airway dimension from soft palate to posterior pharyngeal wall measures about 10 mm and from the base of the tongue the posterior pharyngeal wall measures about 9 mm. Class II occlusion is present. She has a Bolton's difference of 3.75 to 4 mm. She has relatively short crown lengths on the upper anterior teeth with the incisors being only 7 to 8 mm in vertical length. Part of this is due to attrition of the teeth. She did start out as a Class II division II malocclusion.

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
1. AP mandibular deficiency.
2. AP maxillary deficiency.
3. Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency.
4. High occlusal plane angle.
5. Class II occlusion.
6. Rule out TMJ pathology.
7. Significant attrition on the anterior teeth.
8. Significant constricture of the faucial pillars.
9. Significant constricture of the nasal valves.
10. Wide columella base.
11. Constricture of the nostrils.
12. Nasal airway obstruction.
13. Decreased oropharyngeal airway.
14. Tooth size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of approximately 4 mm.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
1. MRI of left and right TMJ.
2. Presurgical orthodontics. a. Continue to align and level maxillary arch. Open up spacing between the upper lateral incisors and cuspids of 1 mm on each side to help correct for the tooth size discrepancy. Tip lateral incisor roots mesially and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies. b. Continue to align and level mandibular arch. Slenderize the lower anterior teeth removing about 2 mm of tooth structure to help correct for the tooth size discrepancy.
3. Surgery. a. Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction. b. Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla and down graft it posteriorly. c. Reconstruct nasal valves with bone grafting (harvest bone from posterior maxillary wall and/or from septum.) d. Reconstruct nasal columella. e. Palatoplasty. f. Bilateral TMJ surgery if indicated, but will depend on MRI findings.
4. Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.
5. Restorative dentistry to rebuild the anterior teeth.

B231
MRI EVALUATION
Age: 51/F
Date of Acquisition: Date of Interpretation: 
Axial view shows the presence of bilateral maxillary sinusitis. The nasal septum is deviated off towards the right side. There is fluid in both sinuses. The condyles have good mediolateral and anteroposterior width. In the left maxillary sinus there may be a mucous retention cyst. Ethmoid and frontal sinuses look relatively clear.

Coronal view shows the presence of fluid in both sinuses. The turbinates appear moderate in size. The nasal septum is deviated a little bit off towards the right side. It appears there is a passable airway. Ethmoid sinuses look relatively clear as do the frontal sinuses.

The left TMJ shows the condyle is a little bit arthritic. There is some sclerosis on the head of the head of the condyle. The lateral rim of the fossa is fairly flat. Vertical joint space looks reasonably well balance.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has an area of sclerosis on top of it. The lateral rim of the fossa appears inferiorly about 2 mm. There appears to be some decreased joint space vertically particularly towards the medial side of the joint.

IMPRESSION:
1. Bilateral TMJ arthritis.
2. Bilateral maxillary sinusitis with fluid levels.
B231
CT EVALUATION

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that is a little arthritic. There appears to be reasonable joint space vertically. The lateral rim of the fossa is fairly flat.

Right TMJ coronal view shows a condyle with fairly good morphology. Some decreased joint space. The lateral rim of the fossa is fairly flat. The nasal septum is deviated a little bit off towards the right side. Turbinates are moderate in size with the left one being larger than the right one. There is a fluid level or thickening of the sinus wall on the left side. On the right side anteriorly it is fairly clear but posteriorly almost the entire sinus is full of material. Axial view shows that the left sinus is full with the floor and the right sinus is full of material more at the posterior aspect of it with anterior part reasonably clear although there is a little bit of thickening of mucosa in some areas. Closer to the orbit the left sinus is fairly clear but the right sinus is fairly full along the posterior aspect. Other sinuses appear relatively clear.

IMPRESSION:
1. Bilateral TMJ arthritis.

B352 
B352 is 29 years old and referred by Dr. __.
Patient History
B352 problems began 4 years ago when she was involved in a motor vehicle accident. She later also was involved in an accident 2 years ago. In accident 4 years ago, she sustained injuries including TMJ, back, and neck. 3 years ago, she underwent bilateral TMJ tie-back procedures and arthroplasties. She did okay for a short time and then began developing problems again. She was in fairly severe pain and was ready to have surgery again when she had the accident 2 years ago but proceeded on with the surgery 2 years ago that included again bilateral TMJ tie-backs and arthroplasties. 2 years ago, she was taken back to surgery and thinks that she probably had at least one of the joints reopened again, and she had what sounds to be bilateral vertical ramus osteotomies. She has been in a splint ever since college to help her with her clenching and bruxism. She has continued to wear that splint. She has had physical therapy for almost five years since her accident. She has had acupuncture, hypnosis, electrical stimulation, radiofrequency treatment, ultrasonic treatment, and HVG (high voltage galvanometer, similar to a TENS device) without improvement.

She gets horrible earaches and has a lot of popping in her ears. She gets some tinnitus and some occasional vertigo. She recently had her hearing testing, and she had evidence of hearing loss. She has no known allergies and no problems with jewelry, although her mother has trouble with cheap earrings. She is very much aware of having nocturnal clenching and bruxism. She rates her headaches at a level of 8 basically through the head area, and these occur daily. TMJ pain is a level of 9, overall pain on an average day is about 7, jaw function at 6, diet at 3, and disability at 5. Other joints that bother B352 include her neck and her back.

Current Medications
Kadian (she was on 100 mg b.i.d. but she switched to currently taking 60 mg t.i.d.)
Thyroid 0.15 mg per day
Celexa 40 mg per day
Zanaflex 8 mg per day
Ansaid 100 mg per day
Advil
Fluori-methane

Patient Concerns
Severe TMJ pain
Severe headaches Pain through the jaws, neck, shoulders, and upper back
Difficulties eating and chewing
Snoring
Terrible earaches and popping in the ears
Clinical Examination

In frontal view, B352 has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 21 mm. Upper tooth to lip relationship is about 2.5 mm. Maxillary midline appear to be pretty much on the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline is shifted about a millimeter off to the right.

In profile, B352 has a little bit of AP deficiency in the maxilla. Chin appears somewhat retruded by approximately 12 to 15 mm.
Intraorally, B352 is missing the lower first bicuspids but has the rest of her teeth. Some mild crowding in the lower anterior arch. Has adequate attached gingiva around the lower anterior teeth. In the upper arch, she is missing the first bicuspid on the left side and on the right side the first bicuspid, first molar, and all four third molars are absent. She is just slightly end-on Class II by maybe 2 mm. Has about a 4 to 5-mm anterior deep-bite relationship. Oropharynx appears clear. Posterior faucial pillars may be a little bit posteriorly postured. Uvula is relatively normal in size.

Nasal septum has several turns and bends in it. Turbinates are relatively normal in size. It appears she has a good nasal airway. To palpation, she has rather severe pain and gets shooting kinds of pains to the TMJs in the styloid areas at about a level of 5 and in the masseter areas some rather mild tenderness. Joints seem to function reasonably smoothly, and she says they have since her last surgery. Maximum incisal opening is 41 mm but only about 26 mm without significant pain and discomfort. Excursive movements are about 4 mm in each direction.

Radiographic Evaluation
Panorex shows the sinuses appear fairly clear. Turbinates appear a little bit large. She is missing maxillary right first bicuspid, first and third molars, maxillary left lateral incisor, first bicuspid, and third molar. The missing left maxillary lateral incisor has been replaced with a bridge. In the lower arch, she is missing bilateral first bicuspids and third molars. Condyles show severe osteoarthritic changes, and the right one has significant anterior beaking. Fossa also appears somewhat deformed.

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle postured posteriorly in the fossa. Articular eminence looks fairly prominent, and it is significantly elongated and just appears as though there is a lot of reaction particularly bony reaction around it. Condyle appears posteriorly positioned in the fossa and appears to be an increased joint space.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle with major anterior beaking present and a lot of sclerosis in the head of the condyle. Articular eminence appears flattened and as though it has been reshaped. May be some reactive bone in the fossa area. Some major arthritic changes are present in both joints. In the open views, both condyles are very limited in their transitory abilities, and there are significant osteoarthritic changes with the left condyle appearing much smaller in size than normal and the right appearing much larger particularly involving the anterior beaking that is present.

Waters view shows that the nasal airway appears reasonably good. Sinuses are all clear.
Cephalometrically, B352 has a skeletal Class II jaw relationship. She has severe constricture of the oropharyngeal airway with an airway measurement of only about 2.5 mm at the soft palate level and only about 3 at the base of the tongue. Lower incisors appear to be overangulated.

Of interest is that B352 is on thyroid medication but has never had any documented thyroid problems. She had reported to her pain doctor that she was tired, and so he said, “Well try this” and put her on thyroid medication. Tooth-size analysis reveals a Bolton's difference of 1 mm. However, the lower incisors are overangulated, and the upper incisors are more vertical in orientation. In addition, the anteroposterior width of the incisal edges is greater than usual, and, therefore, we probably have at least a 3-mm tooth-size discrepancy.

Basic Diagnoses
Bilateral severe TMJ arthritis and articular disc degeneration
Posterior vertical maxillary deficiency
Slight AP maxillary deficiency
AP mandibular deficiency
High occlusal plane angulation
Class II end-on occlusal relationship
Anterior deep-bite of about 4 to 5 mm
Mild crowding, lower anterior arch
Overangulation of lower incisors
Vertical inclination of maxillary incisors
Tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2 to 3 mm
Missing all four first bicuspids, all four third molars, maxillary right first molar, as well as the maxillary left lateral incisor (replaced with a pontic)
Severely decreased oropharyngeal airway of only 2 to 3 mm (normal 11 mm)
Severe earaches
Tinnitus and vertigo
Severe TMJ pain
Myofascial pain

Recommended Treatment
Presurgical orthodontics
Align and level maxillary arch. Maintain first molar extraction space on the right side
Align and level mandibular arch. Strip lower anterior teeth removing 2.5 to 3 mm of tooth structure and retracting
CT scan of TMJs and jaws (TMJ Concepts protocol)

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ condylectomy, debridement, reconstruction, and mandibular advancement with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses
Multiple maxillary osteotomies with bone plate stabilization and hydroxyapatite grafting
Bilateral coronoidectomies
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts (harvested from the abdomen

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion
Restorative dentistry to replace the missing maxillary right first molar
B352  MRI Evaluation 

Date of Acquisition:	
Date of Reading:	
Left TMJ, coronal view, shows a condyle with fairly good mediolateral width. A little depression in the medial superior aspect of the condyle. Fossa appears to be intact. Lateral rim of the fossa is fairly flat and only comes down less than 2 mm. Appears to be decreased joint space over medially between the condylar head and the medial wall of the fossa. Vertically, joint space looks pretty good. Appears to be some kind of fluid or tissue, perhaps maybe Proplast material, in the fossa area. Actually, roof of the fossa appears to be a little bit rough and not too smooth.

Right TMJ, coronal view, shows a condyle with fairly good mediolateral width but a lot of erosion in the condylar head. There is some type of material up in the fossa. Bone in the fossa looks a little bit irregular. There is some soft tissue between the mass in the fossa and the condylar head, and this could be a reactive kind of tissue perhaps. Discal tissue is not readily seen.

Left TMJ, sagittal view, shows a condyle with significant flattening and some superior peaking. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Appears to be a lot of fluid in the articular eminence area, and this may have been the white foreign body type of tissue that was present in the coronal views. Articular disc appears to be out lateral and is positioned anteriorly in the fossa. Bone on the articular eminence appears rough. There is anterior beaking on the condyle.

On opening, there is restricted translation, and whatever discal tissue is there appears to be adhesed to the articular eminence. No particular inflammatory process is seen around the joint, however.

Right TMJ, sagittal view, shows a condyle that is severely deformed. Appears to be heterotopic development off the top of the condyle and large anterior beaking coming forward and flattening on the head of the condyle. Articular eminence is relatively flat except at the most medial aspect where it is extremely steep. There are erosions in the articular eminence itself. May be some discal tissue present but severely deformed and degenerated. There are actually erosions on the articular eminence itself. There is very minimal translation of the condyle.

Impression:
Left TMJ severe arthritis and severe degeneration of the articular disc and adhesions of the disc to the articular eminence
Right TMJ severe arthritis with erosions in the condyle and fossa and eminence.

It appears the patient is a very strong candidate for bilateral total joint prostheses.

SUE IPPOLITO									08/06/2007

Sue is 46 years old, referred by Dr. Pushkar Mehra.  She is in good health except for her current TMJ problems.  She does have some exercise-induced asthma.  

PREVIOUS SURGERIES:
Appendix.
Gallbladder.
Ulna nerve transfer.
Two ACI revisions.
Four knee scopes.
Muscle release of lower right leg.
Two carpal tunnel procedures on the right hand.
Rib removal.
TMJ surgeries x2.

Sue's TMJ problems began in 1983 following an altercation from an ex-husband that struck her in the jaw.  It was presumed that her jaw was fractured.  She was told they could not do anything for her.  Her pain got worse and in 2003 she was treated by Dr. Pushkar Mehra with bilateral meniscectomies.  She did well after surgery until she was involved in a motor vehicle accident in June of 2004.  The pain got significantly worse, so in November of 2005 she underwent bilateral TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses with abdominal fat grafts to the prosthetic areas.  Initially after the procedure she got a staph infection on the left side that was treated with local measures as well as IV antibiotics.  She does have times when she does better than at other times when she does not do so well.  She goes through cycles of where the pain peaks and then settles down and then the cycle reoccurs.  In November of 2005 she had bilateral TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses placed with abdominal fat grafts.  In the mid 2006 she had Botox injections to the TMJs and neck area.  She had a second bout of Botox injections done towards the end of 2006.  She has no problems with earaches, tinnitus or vertigo.  No other joints bother her at this time.  She is not aware of clenching or bruxism.  She feels that she does sleep with her mouth open.  She rates her TMJ pain at 9, average daily pain at 9, jaw function at 3, diet at 4, disability at 6.  

PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Lots of pain around the TMJ areas.
She itches really bad around the face and jaw areas.  The itching was instant after the surgery.  She was doing well prior to the surgery but then after the surgery she stopped the Demerol for awhile and then came back on it.  She has had the itching almost since the onset of surgery. 

She has fairly good left to right facial symmetry.  Intraorally, she has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars present.  She has a severely decayed mandibular left first molar.  First molar on the mandibular right side has a fracture with missing lingual cusps.  In the upper arch she has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  She has a Class I cuspid relationship on the left side and just a slight Class III on the right side by maybe 2 to 3 mm.  She has about a 1 mm overbite relationship.  

SUE IPPOLITO				08/06/2007				Page 2

Sue is unable to wear any type of jewelry except gold and she has a significant reaction.  The fact that she has the itching over the prostheses area is a possible indicator as well.  She was taking Demerol prior to the placement of total joint prostheses and had no itching reaction at that time.  I therefore, believe that the itching is probably due to hypersensitivity.  The itching is directly over the TMJ prosthesis areas as is the pain factors also extend in the temporalis, masseter, SCMs, posterior neck muscles and directly over the TMJs.

BASIC DIAGNOSES:
Constant headaches.
Tinnitus and earaches.
Metal hypersensitivity to the TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses.
Rule out heterotopic bone formation.
Rule out neuromas.
Rule out neuromuscular disorder.
Severe itching.

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
Referral to Environmental Center for testing for metal hypersensitivity to the materials in the prostheses. 
Consider possible placement of metal samples into the forearm if confirmed to be hypersensitive to the injection form of materials.
Consider removing current mandibular prostheses and replacing with appropriate materials to which she is nonreactive.

STACY THREADGILL							08/31/2011

Stacy is 30 years old, birth date 04/02/1981.  Stacy began having clicking and popping in her right jaw joint several years ago.  At the age of 25, she developed a closed lock situation.  In 2006, Dr. Pedro Franco performed a right TMJ disc repositioning procedure with a Mitek anchor and that side has done fairly well.  She then developed some clicking on the left side.  She did not have any clicking in her left jaw joint for about 6 months to a year, but about 3 weeks ago she developed a closed lock situation.  She had previous orthodontics at the age of 14 to 15 for about 8 months.  She had her wisdom teeth extracted at the age of 18.  She has had no other treatment for her jaw situation to date.  

She gets headaches 5 to 7 days a week at about a level predominantly in the frontal and temporal areas bilaterally.  She rates her TMJ pain at 7, but has no pain on the right side.  She rates her average daily pain at 6, jaw function at 8, diet at 9, disability at 5.  She gets occasional earaches, but no tinnitus, vertigo or hearing loss.  Other joints that bother her include her knees and her back (she has had previous surgery on her back).  She is unable to wear cheap earrings.  She is not aware of clenching or bruxing her teeth.

In frontal view, Stacy has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry.  Upper lip length is about 28 mm.  Upper tooth lip relationship is about 3 mm.  The maxillary dental midline is pretty much on the facial midline.  Mandibular dental midline is 1 mm to the left and chin may be off to the left side about 3 mm.  In profile view, Stacy has some maxillary AP discomfort by probably about 4 or 5 mm.  The chin appears retruded about 12 to 15 mm.  The chin neckline is shortened and the chin neckline is not well defined.  Intraorally, Stacy has all of her lower teeth back through the second molars.  Teeth are fairly well aligned and leveled with good attached gingiva.  In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars.  She has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship on the left side being 4 mm Class II and on the left side she is probably 2 mm Class II.  She has anterior deep bite of 1.5 mm.  She has a small upper lateral incisor particularly on the left side.  She has some spacing between the upper left cuspid, first bicuspid.  Oropharyngeal tissues look relatively normal.  Turbinates are moderately enlarged.  The septum appears reasonably straight with just some slight protrusions laterally on both sides of the nose coming in fairly close approximation to the turbinates.  She may have a mild to moderate nasal airway obstruction.  She does tend to have a head postured neck position.  To palpation, she has some mild tenderness over the temporalis muscles and the styloid areas.  Joints seem to function relatively smoothly, but she says prior to getting locked up that she did have some crepitus kind of noises in the right joint.  The maximal opening is 28 mm, excursion movement to the right 8 mm and to the left 10 mm.  The excursion movement to the right is very painful, opening without pain 18 mm.  

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION.  Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars.  Right TMJ demonstrates the presence of a Mitek anchor.  There is arthritis in this joint with somewhat elongation of the head of the condyle and flattening with some anterior beaking.  Left TMJ shows a condyle that has better morphology, but appears posteriorly positioned in the fossa.  

Coronal view shows the septum appears reasonably straight.  Sinuses appear clear.  Turbinates appear moderately enlarged and appear to block a good part of the airway.  

Axial view shows that the sinuses appear clear and turbinates appear moderately enlarged. 

Left TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that has fairly good morphology with some flattening on the anterior head particularly towards the lateral aspect of the joint.  There is decreased posterior joint space and superior joint space.  The articular eminence has a moderate inclination.  Imaging is compatible with an anteriorly displaced disc.

Right TMJ sagittal view shows a condyle that is somewhat arthritic.  There is some anterior beaking present.  The joint space is decreased towards the lateral aspect of the joint.  There is a Mitek anchor present.  The condyle does appear arthritic.  

Left TMJ coronal view shows a condyle that has some flattening around the lateral pole of the condyle and some peaking up of the condyle in the mid condyle portion.  There is decreased superior joint space.  The lateral rim curves inferiorly about 3 or 4 mm and there is decreased joint space there as well.  

Right TMJ coronal view shows the presence of a Mitek anchor in the condyle.  The condyle has some arthritic changes as does the fossa particularly at the lateral aspect of the rim of the fossa.  The lateral rim curves inferiorly probably about 4 mm.  

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows that Stacy has a retruded maxilla and mandible with a moderately high occlusal plane angle.  She has a significantly decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of only about 2 mm at the soft palate level and 4 mm at the base of the tongue.  Stacy has a transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the left side being 1.5 mm higher than the right side.  

Stacy states that she has significant daytime tiredness.  She can sleep 10 to 12 hours a day and still wake up being tired.  Because of this, I will recommend that she get a sleep study. 

Basic diagnoses for Stacy would be as follows:

Left TMJ articular disc dislocation with closed lock situation and mild arthritis.
Right TMJ arthritis and previous placement of Mitek anchor. 
Maxillary AP and posterior vertical hypoplasia and slight anterior vertical hyperplasia.
AP mandibular hyperplasia. 
Class II end-on occlusion being a little bit worse on the left side than the right side.  
Headaches.
TMJ pain.
Small maxillary left lateral incisor.
Transverse cant in the occlusal plane with the left side being 1.5 mm higher than the right side.
Mandibular dental midline shifted 1 mm to the left, chin 3 mm to the left.
Severely decreased oropharyngeal airway with an AP dimension of 2 mm (normal AP dimension is 11 mm).
Significant daytime somnolence.  

Recommended treatment would be as follows:

1.	Sleep study.
2.	MRI of left and right TMJ.
3.	Surgery Stage 1.
	a.	Left TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek 			anchor.
	b.	Left mandibular ramus sagittal split osteotomy to maintain occlusion and 			create space in the joint to accommodate the disc.
	c.	Application of maxillary and mandibular arch bars.
4.	Presurgical Orthodontics for Stage 2 Surgery.
	a.	Align and level maxillary arch.  Open up 1.5 mm of space around the	 		maxillary left lateral incisor.  Keep lateral incisor roots tipped mesially    
                        and cuspid roots distally to make room for interdental osteotomies in case 
                        they are necessary.  
	b.	Align and level mandibular arch.  
5.	Surgery Stage 2.
	a.	Bilateral mandibular ramus sagittal split osteotomies to advance the 			mandible in a counterclockwise direction and level it transversely.
	b.	Multiple maxillary osteotomies to advance the maxilla in a 					counterclockwise direction and transversely level the occlusal plane.
	c.	Anterior mandibular horizontal osteotomy to augment the chin.
6.	Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.  
7.	Bonding to build up the maxillary left lateral incisor.  

Stacy had scoliosis and in 1996 had stainless steel Harrington rods placed in her back.  She is concerned as to whether or not she can have an MRI taken.  I told her that we would check with the MRI facility and see if that presents any problem.  

Stacy is only interested in having her left TMJ issue resolved.  She does not want to pursue the treatment of her sleep apnea at this time, thus the reason for the 2 stage surgery.  If she decides to pursue the treatment of the her sleep apnea, Stage 2 would be implemented including the orthodontic treatment.

original_note:
MELISSA PERSON									01-22-04

Melissa is 20 years old and a sophomore at TCU.

Patient History

Melissa is in good health with no major medical problems. She takes no medications. Melissa has no allergies that she is aware of, no food allergies, and no problems wearing jewelry.

She states that she doesn’t really have any breathing problems through her nose that she is aware of, but she does tend to breathe through her mouth when she sleeps. She has no problems with earaches but gets occasional tinnitus, no vertigo, and no hearing loss. No other joints bother her. She is aware of nocturnal clenching and bruxism, and sometimes during the day she will find herself clenching. She takes no medications except for Advil for her headaches. 

Her TMJ problems began as far back as she can remember but probably in her early teen ears of unknown etiology. She did undergo some preliminary orthodontics and then had interceptive treatment predominantly using a Herbst appliance for one year that was cemented in place. This was followed by full orthodontics for two years. She has had some splint therapy as well. 

She gets headaches fairly often but not particularly every week, and they occur predominantly in the temporal area at about a level of 3. She used to get severe headaches at a level of 10 prior to puberty. After achieving puberty, those severe headaches pretty much disappeared, and she was left with just this ore regular type of headaches. She rates her TMJ pain at 5, average daily pain at 3, jaw function at 7, diet at 4, and disability at 4. 

Patient Concerns

Popping in the jaw joints
Headaches 
Jaw joint pain
Difficulties eating and chewing

Clinical Examination

In frontal view, Melissa has fairly good left-to-right facial symmetry. Upper lip length is 22 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is almost 4 mm. Maxillary, mandibular and chin midline is all pretty much on the facial midline. She does tend to have somewhat decreased vermilion exposed in the upper lip. 

In profile view, she may have just some slight AP maxillary deficiency of maybe 2 to 3 mm. Chin appears to be retruded approximately 12 to 15 mm. Chin-neckline is short, although chin-neck angle is fairly well defined. Some fullness in the submental area. 

Intraorally, Melissa has all of her lower teeth back through her second molars. Teeth are fairly well aligned and leveled. She has adequate attached gingiva around the lower anterior arch. In the upper arch, she has all of her teeth back through the second molars. She has a Class II end-on cuspid relationship by about 3 mm, and she has an overjet of probably about 3 mm. She has about a 3-mm anterior deep bite relationship. Oropharynx appears relatively healthy and clear. 

She does have a little bit of a wide columella base and somewhat constricted nostrils. Intranasally, turbinates are a little bit enlarged particularly on the right side but not so much on the left side. Nasal airway is a little bit greater on the left side as compared to the right side. 

To palpation, Melissa has no tenderness around the head and neck area. 

On opening, she has a definite click in the left TMJ at less than 10 mm of opening and a closing click at probably less than 5 mm. There also appears to be a closing click on the right side, although an opening click is not as noticeable.

Maximal incisal opening for Melissa is 39 mm but only about 19 mm with reasonable comfort. Excursive movements are 7 mm in each direction.

Radiographic Evaluation

Panorex shows that Melissa has all of her teeth except for her third molars. Sinuses look fairly clear. Turbinates are a little bit difficult to evaluate. Condyles appear a little bit flattened anteriorly, and it appears that the lateral poles are inferiorly located relative to the medial poles. 

Lateral cephalometric radiograph shows a skeletal Class II relationship. Probably a mild high occlusal plane angle and some slight anterior vertical maxillary excess. Skeletal Class II relationship is present. Oropharyngeal airway appears good. 

Waters view shows that the sinuses appear clear, and the face appears to have fairly good symmetry. Bony septum appears relatively symmetrical. Right turbinate appears a little bit enlarged compared to the left one, and the nasal airway has better passage on the left side as compared to the right side. 

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is relatively small in anteroposterior dimension. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Condyle is posteriorly positioned in the fossa with a significantly increased anterior joint space indicative of probably anterior displacement of the articular disc. The condylar position on the tomograms is different than the MRI possibly due to anterior posturing during the MRIs compared to these centrically positioned condyles. 

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that is narrow anteroposteriorly. Some flattening on the superior head. Significantly decreased posterior joint space and significantly increased anterior joint space. Articular eminence is moderately steep. Likewise this joint is indicative of probably anteriorly displaced articular disc. 

On opening, there is reasonably good translation of both condyles. Joint space in the open view appears reasonable probably indicative of reduction of the articular discs in the open position. Extreme narrowness anteroposteriorly of the condyles is noted in the open position with the right condyle having more AP narrowness than the left side. 

Basic Diagnoses

Bilateral TMJ arthritis and articular disc dislocation with early reduction
Anterior vertical maxillary excess
AP mandibular deficiency
High occlusal plane angle
Class II end-on occlusion
Accentuated curve of occlusion in the lower arch
Slight reverse curve of occlusion in the upper arch
Slight anterior deep bite of about 3 mm
Headaches 
TMJ pain
Tooth-size discrepancy with a Bolton's difference of 2 mm

Recommended Treatment

Presurgical orthodontics 
Align and level maxillary arch. Open up about 0.5 to 1 mm of space around each upper lateral incisor to help compensate for the tooth-size discrepancy
Align and level mandibular arch trying to decrease the accentuated curve of Spee. Strip lower anterior teeth removing at least a millimeter of tooth structure (She has had previous stripping there, so want to be careful not to remove more than about 1 more millimeter). Include the second molar in the presurgical orthodontics and try to elevate the mesial ridge and lower the distal marginal ridge by bringing the molar roots anteriorly in the mandible

Surgery
Bilateral TMJ articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors 
Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies to advance the mandible in a counterclockwise direction
Multiple maxillary osteotomies to move the anterior aspect superiorly and slightly forward as well as correct any vertical transverse discrepancies that may be present
Augmentation genioplasty

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion

original_note:
DEEANN SCHROYER								5/27/05

Deeann is 50-year-old, referred by Dr. Michael Steffen.  

PRIMARY CONCERNS:
Swelling of left TMJ.
Pain of left TMJ.
Left ear pain.
Swollen glands on the left side.
Pain from talking.
Left salivary gland seems irritated.
Difficulties eating and chewing.
Difficulties sleeping at night because of pain in the jaw area.
Pain worse in the mornings when she wakes up. 
Pain in neck and shoulders.
Headaches. 

Deeann feels that her joint problems may have began back in her teenage years or at least she had headaches since that time of unknown etiology.  She had some significant jaw trauma in 1991 due to a domestic altercation.  Back in the early 90s she switched her dentist and the new dentist realized that as Deeann opened her jaw that the lower jaw shifted off significantly onto one side.  She was referred to Dr. Mike Steffen.  Dr. Steffen performed orthodontic treatment on Deeann from 1994 through 1997 and made a splint type of retainer for her which she wore from 1997 until about 4 months ago when Dr. Steffen told her she no longer needed to wear it.  She has had some physical therapy in the past but apparently no other treatment for her jaw related problem.  She takes Advil for her headaches.  She has earaches on the left side.  They are a shooting kind of pain but not there all the time.  No tinnitus, vertigo or hearing loss.  Other joints that bother her include her left shoulder, left elbow and left hip.  Her pain is worse in the mornings when she wakes up indicative of probable nocturnal clenching.  She is also aware that she clenches during the day.  She likes to sleep on her side but it is difficult for her to do that now because of the pain factors.  Deeann has continuous headaches predominately on the left side that occur in the frontal, temporal, and posterior head area at a level of 5 but can escalate to a level of 8.  She has continuous left TMJ pain at about a level of 7.  Eating exacerbates the TMJ pain.  Her average daily pain is a level of 6, jaw function, diet and disability are a level of 5. 

CLINICAL EVALUATION: 
Frontal view shows that Deeann has fairly good left to right facial symmetry.  Maxillary midline may be just about 1 mm off to the right side.  Mandibular dental midline and chin appear to be pretty much on the facial midline.  Nasal tip may be shifted just a little bit off to the right side as well about 1 mm or 2.  In profile view she may have just some slight AP maxillary deficiency probably about 3 to 4 mm and mandibular AP deficiency probably 6 to 8 mm.  Chin-neck line is short and chin-neck angle not well defined.  Intraorally, Deeann has all of her lower teeth back to the second molars.  There is some mild crowding of the anterior arch.  She has crowns on the left second bicuspid, first second molars.  She also has crowns on the mandibular right second bicuspid and molars.  In the maxilla, she also has crowns on her molars and the left second bicuspid. 
DEEANN SCHROYER			5/27/05				Page 2


In the upper arch she is missing probably second bicuspid on the right side and on the left side she is missing the second molar and it appears that all four third molars are absent.  She is basically a Class I cuspid occlusion on the right side and Class II end-on cuspid on the left side by probably a couple of millimeters.  She has an anterior deep bite of about 3 mm.  Uvula, soft palate and faucial pillars all appear pretty much normal.  Intranasally, the septum looks acceptable.  Left turbinate appears enlarged with a somewhat decreased airway.  The right side turbinate appears a little bit smaller in size at least anteriorly.  Deeann gives no real significant history of snoring.  She is moderately tired during the day particularly after lunch.  She is not aware of any sleep apnea problems.  To palpation, Deeann had a little severe discomfort over the left TMJ at a level of 8, styloid area 3 and SCM 4.  Incisal opening is about 45 mm and she can get to 40 mm without too much pain.  Excursion movements about 4 mm in each direction.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION:  
Panorex shows the absence of all four third molars.  Maxillary left second molar, maxillary right second bicuspid.  She has crowns bilaterally on the second bicuspid and both molars in the upper arch on all the molars and left second bicuspid.  Sinuses appear relatively clear.  

Left condyle shows significant arthritic changes with erosion on the head of the condyle.  Right condyle looks relatively normal. 

Waters view show that the sinuses are clear.  Bony septum is relatively straight.  Turbinates are moderately enlarged.  

Right TMJ tomogram shows some flattening on the anterior head of the condyle and a little bit of anterior beaking.  The condyle is postured forward and not in centric relation and will be redone.  

Left TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that has undergone significant arthritic changes and appears to have lost some vertical dimension.  There is some anterior beaking present. Condyle is also postured forward and will need to be re-shot.  On opening, there is moderately good translation being a little bit better on the right side as compared to the left side.  Decreased joint space vertically on the left side.  Closed tomograms in centric relation in the right joint show very good chondral morphology and good joint space.  Articular eminence is moderately steep.  Left TMJ tomogram in centric relation shows that the joint space actually looks reasonably good in the cuts available but there is significant evidence of arthritis within the joint.  Articular eminence is fairly steep and perhaps slightly steeper than the opposite side.  Slight decreased dimension between the sigmoid notch and the articular eminence on the left side as compared to the right side.  
DEEANN SCHOYER		5/27/05					Page 3


BASIC DIAGNOSES: 
Left TMJ reactive arthritis.
Left TMJ pain.
Left-sided headaches.
Left ear pain.
Myofascial pain to the neck and shoulders.
Difficulties eating and chewing.
Nocturnal and daytime and clenching.  

RECOMMENDED TREATMENT:
MRI of left and right TMJ (to be done 5/27/05.)

CT scan of TMJs and jaws (TMJ Concepts protocol.)

Medications.
Klonopin to control nocturnal clenching and bruxism.
Advil 3 to 4 tablets 3 to 4 times per day as an anti-inflammatory agent and analgesic.
Consider antibiotic (Levaquin, Zithromax etc) to treat reactive arthritis. 

Presurgical orthodontics. 
Align and level maxillary arch.
Align and level mandibular arch.

Surgery.
Left TMJ reconstruction with TMJ Concepts total joint prostheses. 
Left TMJ fat graft (harvest from abdomen.)

Postsurgical orthodontics to refine occlusion.

original_note:
B319 
B319 is almost 13 years old. Her birthday is . She was referred by Dr. __.
Patient History
She appears to be in good health. She takes Zyrtec-D 1 tablet b.i.d. She takes multivitamins and sodium chloride 0.5 mg 1 tablet every day. She also takes Proventil p.r.n. and uses it mainly when she has to run. She has some allergy problems to pollens, grass, cotton, mold, and just to shellfish. Not aware of any problems wearing jewelry. She has nasal congestion that she has been told is related to enlarged inferior turbinates. It has been recommended that she have radiofrequency treatment to reduce the side of the turbinates. She does have exercise-induced asthma, and this has been getting worse for her over the last couple of years. She began orthodontic treatment at the age of 10 and has had bioblock treatment that was just recently finished earlier this year. She had some maxillary expansion initially and then wore the bioblock for about two years approximately. She gets periodic headaches mainly in the temporal area that can escalate to about a level of 8, but these are periodic episodes. In using the bioblock, she is on a regimen where she is to clench her teeth a lot which is necessary to achieve the proper results with the bioblock technique. She has developed some mild masseter hypoplasia as a result of that. She rates her TMJ pain at 0, pain around the head and neck area on an average day at 0, jaw function at 0, diet at 1, and disability at 0.

Clinical Examination
In frontal view, B319 has good facial symmetry and balance. Upper lip length is about 21 mm. Upper tooth-to-lip relationship is 0. Maxillary and mandibular dental midline are fairly coincident with each other, all just a slightly to the left about 1 mm.

In profile view, she has an acute nasolabial angle. Upper lip supralabrale is probably about 5 mm anterior to subnasale. Chin actually has fairly good AP projection with just some very minor deficiency maybe about 3 mm but not too significant. Chin-neck is very well-defined, and chin-neck angle is well-defined. In centric relation, chin may be just slightly AP deficient by maybe 4 to 5 mm.

Intraorally, first molars are erupted in, but second molars are beginning to come in. Second bicuspids are erupting in as well. Some slight spacing around the bicuspid area. Appears to have adequate attached gingiva around the lower anterior teeth with a little less at the centrals.

In the upper arch, she has her teeth erupted but through the first molars. Second molars aren't quite in yet. She has a lot of spacing in the upper arch around the cuspids and first bicuspids and particularly between the cuspids and lateral incisors where she has probably about 4 mm of space on the left side and probably 6 to 7 mm of space on the right side. Has basically a Class I cuspid relationship bilaterally. She has a significant overjet of probably at least 3 mm and 0.5 overbite relationship. In centric relationship, her mandibular and chin midline are pretty much on the facial midline and just the maxillary midline is a little bit off to the left side about a millimeter. This goes along with the significant spacing present in the upper arch. With the retainer in, B319 tends to hold the lower jaw forward quite a ways. Probably holds it forward 3 to 4 mm.

To palpation, she has high moderate discomfort over the temporalis muscles and moderate discomfort over the posterior neck muscles with very minor tenderness over the masseters. No tenderness over the TMJs. TMJs seem to function relatively smoothly from the centric relation position. Incisal opening is 57 mm. It was difficult to get a good reading on the level of translation but to the right seemed to be around 5 mm and to the left it seemed like she may have gotten up to 7 mm. She does get a lot of problems with tenderness in the temporalis muscles, and her mother has to massage those periodically. She is on a regimen of chronic clenching for the bioblock therapy. This probably has resulted in the slight masseteric hyperplasia as well as the soreness in the temporalis muscles.

Oropharynx appears clear, and architecture looks relatively normal.
Intranasally, the right turbinate is quite large today and seems to block a good part of the airway. On the left side, the turbinate is more normal size and appears to be reasonable airway on that side. Could be cyclic enlargements occurring with the turbinates, but at least today the right one is enlarged, left one is not.

Radiographic Evaluation
Radiographic evaluation shows that B319 has the presence of no third molars as they are totally undeveloped. Her dad also did not develop third molars. Second molars are there but haven't erupted in yet. A lot of spacing present between the upper later incisors and cuspids, cuspids and first bicuspids, as well as some of the other posterior teeth. Sinuses look relatively clear. Nasal airway may be a little bit constricted. Condyles appear a little bit flattened on the anterior aspect.

Left TMJ tomogram shows increased vertical and posterior joint space, but this may be because B319 tends to posture her mandible forward. We may want to repeat these with B319 in a centric jaw position without her retainer in. Condyle may be just slightly flattened anteriorly but for the most part looks reasonably good. Articular eminence is moderately steep.

Right TMJ tomogram shows a condyle that appears a little bit flattened on the posterior head. Condyle is postured way forward in the fossa with increased superior and posterior joint space. Articular eminence is moderately steep to a greater extent that the opposite side.

On opening, there is good translation of both condyles.
Waters view shows that the sinuses look relatively clear. Left nasal airway actually looks fairly good, but the right side appears as though the turbinate is enlarged and that airway seems somewhat blocked. Good development of the frontal sinuses. The septum has some minor deviations to it but nothing too significant.

Cephalometrically, the mandible is postured forward. Shows that there is a good airway. Will need to re-shoot a couple of x-rays here so we can better determine jaw alignment and jaw position.

Basic Diagnoses
Patient in active treatment orthodontically and just recently completing the bioblock therapy and in retainers in the upper arch
Essentially Class I cuspid relationship
Slight overangulation of the maxillary incisors
Spacing between the upper laterals/cuspids, cuspids/bicuspids, and then more minimal spacing between the rest of the posterior teeth
Hypertrophy of the right turbinate, although this could be a cyclic phenomena with only the right side being enlarged at the time of the appointment
Mild arthritic changes in the joints bilaterally, but discs may be in position
Absence of all four third molars
Second molars unerupted

Recommended Treatment
Continue on with orthodontic treatment
Continue to monitor TMJs
Considerations for orthognathic surgery only if there are aesthetic concerns following completion of the orthodontic treatment

B319  MRI Scan Evaluation
Date of Acquisition: Date of :
Left TMJ, coronal view, shows a condyle that appears to be small in size and perhaps has lost some vertical height. Condyle has a little bit of an abnormal shape to it. Decreased joint space over medially, but in some other areas it appears to be increased. Discal tissue may be anteriorly positioned. Cortical bone is thin but appears to be intact. Moderate downward curvature of the lateral rim of the fossa being about 3 mm.

Right TMJ, coronal view, shows a condyle that likewise appears to be a little bit small in size. Some increased joint space particularly toward the superior and lateral aspect of the joint and decreased over medially. May be discal tissue anteriorly but difficult to identify. Cortical bone is thin but appears to be intact. Moderate downward curvature of the lateral rim of the fossa being about 3 mm.

Left TMJ, sagittal view, shows a condyle that appears to have a little bit of an abnormal shape to it with a little bit of anterior beaking. Articular eminence is fairly steep. Disc may be in fairly reasonable position; however, there does appear to be increased vertical joint space particularly toward the lateral aspect of the joint. Even toward the medial side, there appears to be increased joint space vertically and posteriorly. Disc may be in fairly reasonable position at this time.

Left open view shows that there is moderately good translation of the condyle where the condyle comes beneath the articular eminence. Disc appears to be positioned in the opened views.

Right TMJ, sagittal view, shows a condyle that appears a little flattened on the anterior aspect, and the cortical bone is hard to see in the very anterior aspect. A little bit of anterior beaking present. Articular disc appears to be vertically oriented in the fossa. Articular eminence is fairly steep. Increased superior and posterior joint space. May be a little inflammation in the bilaminar tissues, and in the closed position it appears that the articular disc is anteriorly displaced on the right side.

On opening, there is moderate translation. Appears that the disc reduces and is in position.
Impression:
Left TMJ mild arthritis. It appears that the disc may be in reasonable position but increased joint space vertically and posteriorly
Right TMJ arthritis and articular disc anteriorly dislocated but with reduction

original_note:
B261 
B261 is currently 60 years old. I performed previous surgery on her 13 years ago, so she is 12 years 10 months postsurgery. Four years ago, she fell and struck the left side of her jaw. She was aware of her bite changing at that time. Last year, she fell and struck her forehead. She has broken some of the teeth with both of these falls. She is aware of nocturnal clenching. She has been diagnosed with ulcerative colitis. She has no problems with headaches, but rates her TMJ pain at an average level of about 5, but it can easily escalate to 8. Average daily pain usually reaches about an 8. Jaw function is at 0, diet at 2, and disability at 0. She takes the following medications:
Levothyroxine.
Detrol LA (for her bladder).
Estrace 0.5 mg per day.
Zestoretic (for blood pressure and diuretic).
Asacol.

CLINICAL EXAMINATION
In frontal view, B261 has fairly good facial symmetry. Upper lip length is about 21 mm. Upper tooth lip relationship is about 1 mm. Maxillary dental midline is fairly close to being on the facial midline. Mandibular dental midline is shifted about 3 mm off to the right. She has a left posterior overbite. She has a Class III end-on occlusion on the left side and a slight Class II cuspid relationship on the right side. B261 is in splint therapy now. She is predominantly on her front teeth and her back teeth do not touch, particularly on the left side. She has about a 2 mm open bite. She has a crossbite that has developed now on the right side and anterior. She has end-on incisor relationship.

RADIOGRAPHIC EVALUATION
Panorex shows the presence and evidence of previous surgery with bilateral Mitek anchors in place and 4 bone screws in each ramus body area. In B261's previous surgeries, we performed the following procedures:
Multiple maxillary osteotomies with HA grafting and bone plates. Bilateral mandibular ramus osteotomies.
Bilateral tempomandibular joint articular disc repositioning and ligament repair with Mitek anchors.
Bilateral tempomandibular joint eminectomies. Arthroplasty of the left mandibular condyle.
Placement of abdominal fat graft into the bilateral TMJ joint areas.
External rhinoplasty.
Excision of lesion off the right neck.

Basic diagnoses for B261 would be as follows:
Bilateral TMJ severe arthritis. Bony tumor growing in the left glenoid fossa area. Mandibular deviated prognathism.
Class III occlusion on the left side, Class II end-on on the right side. Crossbite on the right side.
Posterior open bite on the left side of approximately 2-3 mm. Bilateral TMJ pain.

Recommended treatment would be as follows:
CT scan of jaws and jaw joints (TMJ Concepts protocol). Surgery. Bilateral TMJ condylectomies and meniscectomies. Removal of bony tumor left temporal bone on glenoid fossa area. Bilateral TMJ reconstruction with TMJ Concept total joint prostheses.
Bilateral TMJ fat grafts. Placement of arch bars.

Telephone conversation. She would like to be treated without having total joint prostheses. I told her the only option we would have would be to try and go in and remove the reactive bone that has formed particularly in the left joint, but I did not know how good of an outcome we could get doing that since it would create an arthritic situation in the fossa component at least and may not prevent the redevelopment of heterotopic bone. Also I have no prediction of how it would affect her pain problems. She says her pain has increased significantly since her appointment with me. I discussed at length the prosthesis and also mentioned that we could do implants to check for hypersensitivity to the materials if she would like to do that. She will contact me if she would like to do that. I answered all the questions she had at this time.
